**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The "pull = brake" convention ensures **fail-safe behaviour under involuntary driver reflexes**. If the driver experiences a spasm, sneeze, panic jerk, or loss of grip, the natural flexor-withdrawal reflex pulls the hand toward the body, which applies the brake and brings the vehicle to a stop. This is in compliance with the **fail-safe-by-default** principle required by ISO, SAE, and RESNA standards. The opposite convention (push = brake) would risk unintended acceleration during involuntary reflexes. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of a **push-pull mechanical hand-control system** for a car. List the engineering standards it must comply with. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A hand-control lever has its pivot at a distance of **45 mm** from the cable attachment point, while the driver's grip is **210 mm** from the same pivot. The OEM brake pedal requires **450 N** of force and travels **22 mm**. The cable-linkage efficiency is **0.88**. Calculate: (i) the mechanical advantage, (ii) the hand force the driver must apply, (iii) the hand-lever travel, and (iv) comment on whether this is within the ergonomic limit for a 5th-percentile female driver. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Construction (2 marks):**
- A rigid lever, typically steel or aluminium alloy, pivoted at a bracket mounted to the steering column or floor console near the driver's right (or left) knee.
- A grip / handle at the driver end.
- A Bowden cable (or rigid push-rod) connecting the lever to a "pedal pusher" that mounts over the OEM brake and accelerator pedals.
- A return spring that defaults the lever to the neutral (no-brake, no-accelerator) position.
- A mechanical lockout pin that allows an able-bodied driver to disable the hand controls and revert to foot operation.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Pulling the lever backward rotates it about its pivot, which tensions the cable, which pulls the pedal pusher against the OEM brake pedal, applying the brake.
- Pushing the lever forward rotates it the other way, which pushes the pedal pusher against the OEM accelerator pedal, opening the throttle.
- The system uses the driver's hand force multiplied by the lever's mechanical advantage to deliver the required pedal force.

**Standards (1 mark):**
- ISO 7176 (mobility aids), SAE J182a (brake pedals), FMVSS 105 / 135 (brake systems), ADA Title III, AIS-052 (India).

**Fail-safe feature (1 mark):**
- The return spring and pull-brake convention guarantee a safe default state.
- Mention cable-failure → mechanical lockout.

**Sketch description (1 mark):**
- Free-body lever showing pivot, hand force, cable attachment, OEM pedal coupling, return spring.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $L_{cable} = 45$ mm
- $L_{in} = 210$ mm
- $F_{out} = 450$ N
- $d_{pedal} = 22$ mm
- $\eta = 0.88$

**Step 1 — Mechanical Advantage (1 mark):**

$$
M.A. = \dfrac{L_{in}}{L_{cable}} = \dfrac{210 \text{ mm}}{45 \text{ mm}} = 4.67
$$

**Step 2 — Hand force required (2 marks):**

$$
F_{in} = \dfrac{F_{out}}{\eta \cdot M.A.} = \dfrac{450 \text{ N}}{0.88 \times 4.67} = \dfrac{450}{4.11} = 109.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Hand-lever travel (2 marks):**

$$
d_{hand} = M.A. \times d_{pedal} = 4.67 \times 22 \text{ mm} = 102.7 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Ergonomic assessment (2 marks):**

The 5th-percentile female sustained pinch-grip force is approximately **45 N** (peak) and **13.5 N** (sustained). The required hand force of **109.5 N** is **2.4× the peak grip capability** — therefore, this hand-control configuration is **NOT acceptable** for a 5th-percentile female driver. **Recommendation:** (i) prescribe a power-brake vehicle (so $F_{out} \approx 80$ N instead of 450 N, giving $F_{in} \approx 19.5$ N — acceptable), or (ii) increase $L_{in}$ to 320 mm (raising M.A. to 7.1) and accept a longer hand travel of 156 mm.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **biomechanical and ergonomic design constraints** that govern the engineering of an automobile hand-control system. Illustrate your answer with reference to the 5th-percentile female driver population. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A driver using a joystick-based electronic hand-control system is travelling at **80 km/h** on a dry asphalt road ($\mu = 0.7$). The driver's reaction time with the joystick is **0.95 s**. Calculate: (i) the total stopping distance, (ii) the additional stopping distance compared to a foot-pedal driver with a reaction time of **0.70 s**, and (iii) state **two design modifications** to the joystick that would reduce the reaction-time penalty. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Biomechanical constraints (3 marks):**
- **Grip strength** — Pinch-grip 5th-percentile female: ~45 N peak, ~13.5 N sustained. Hand-control hand force must stay within this envelope.
- **Range of motion** — Wrist flexion/extension limited to ~60° each way. Hand-lever total sweep must be ≤ 90°.
- **Reaction time** — Hand-control reaction time is 15–25% longer than foot-pedal due to unfamiliarity and joystick latency.
- **Endurance** — Sustained operation for 2+ hours without lactate buildup; requires $\leq 30\%$ MVC (Maximum Voluntary Contraction).
- **Reflex / spasm behaviour** — Must default to brake-apply; never accelerator-apply.
- **Posture and seating** — Must be reachable in standard wheelchair transfer positions (D1, D2) and from the OEM driver's seat.

**Ergonomic design constraints (2 marks):**
- **Lever length and grip diameter** — Grip diameter 30–45 mm, lever length 150–250 mm.
- **Force-deflection curve** — Should be progressive (resistance increases with deflection) for tactile feedback.
- **Vibration isolation** — Hand-control grip should be vibration-damped to prevent hand-arm vibration syndrome (HAVS).

**Why 5th-percentile female? (1 mark):**
- Designing to the weakest credible user population guarantees inclusivity; this is the **inclusive-design** principle taught in KTU Module 2.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
- Hand-control design is a **multivariate constrained optimization** problem balancing force, travel, reaction time, endurance, and cost.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $v_0 = 80$ km/h $= 22.22$ m/s
- $T_{react,hand} = 0.95$ s
- $T_{react,foot} = 0.70$ s
- $\mu = 0.7$, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Braking distance (independent of driver, depends only on vehicle and road) (1 mark):**

$$
d_{braking} = \dfrac{v_0^{\,2}}{2 \cdot \mu \cdot g} = \dfrac{(22.22)^2}{2 \times 0.7 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{493.7}{13.734} = 35.95 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Stopping distance for hand-control driver (2 marks):**

$$
d_{react,hand} = 22.22 \times 0.95 = 21.11 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{stop,hand} = 21.11 + 35.95 = 57.06 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Stopping distance for foot-pedal driver (2 marks):**

$$
d_{react,foot} = 22.22 \times 0.70 = 15.55 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{stop,foot} = 15.55 + 35.95 = 41.50 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Additional stopping distance penalty (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta d = 57.06 - 41.50 = 15.56 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Two design modifications to reduce the penalty (1 mark):**

1. **Reduce electronic latency** by upgrading the joystick's signal-processing hardware to achieve ECU response < 20 ms (saving ~50 ms of reaction time ≈ 1.1 m at 80 km/h).
2. **Add haptic pre-emptive feedback** (a vibration or force pulse when the driver lifts off the accelerator) to reduce the perception-and-decision component of reaction time by ~100 ms (saving ~2.2 m at 80 km/h).
3. **Train the driver for at least 40 hours** at a certified rehabilitation driving school, which can bring the joystick reaction time down to within 0.1 s of a foot-pedal driver (saving ~2.2 m).
4. **Pre-charge the brake hydraulics** automatically when the driver lifts off the accelerator (as in modern AEB systems) to eliminate brake-pad-to-rotor clearance delay, saving ~3–5 m at 80 km/h.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU examinations on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the efficiency factor $\eta$** — Many students compute $F_{in} = F_{out} / M.A.$ and ignore cable friction. **Always multiply the denominator by $\eta$** for real systems. (-1 mark)
>
> 2. **Mixing up the brake and accelerator in a sketch** — The pull = brake, push = accelerator convention must be labelled clearly. If the diagram contradicts the description, examiners deduct **2 marks** even if the rest is correct.
>
> 3. **Unit errors** — Mixing mm and m, or N and kgf, in a 7-mark numerical. Always convert to SI before plugging in. (-1 mark)
>
> 4. **No standards cited** — A 14-mark answer that does not mention **at least one regulatory standard** (ISO 7176, SAE J182a, AIS-052, FMVSS, ADA) loses **1–2 marks** for "lack of professional completeness".
>
> 5. **No fail-safe discussion** — Every hand-control answer must mention the **return spring + pull = brake fail-safe principle**. Omitting this is a **2-mark penalty**.
>
> 6. **Confusing velocity ratio with mechanical advantage** — In ideal systems $V.R. = M.A.$, but in real systems $V.R. = M.A. \times \eta$ and the input/output force ratio is $M.A. \times \eta$. This is a **classic trap question**.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the hand-arm vibration syndrome (HAVS)** in ergonomic answers — Examiners consider this a hallmark of a "complete" answer in 2024 scheme.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for Automobile Hand-Controls — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- **Definition:** Adaptive driving aids remapping accelerator/brake from foot to hand for lower-limb disabled drivers.
- **Types:** Push-Pull mechanical, Radial (pistol-grip), Joystick electronic, Folding mechanical.
- **Fail-safe principle:** **Pull = Brake** (always, no exceptions).
- **Mechanical advantage range:** 3:1 to 8:1 for hand controls.
- **Hand-force ergonomic envelope:** ≤ 60 N sustained, ≤ 120 N peak (general adult population); **≤ 45 N peak** for 5th-percentile female pinch grip.
- **Brake pedal force (un-boosted):** 300–500 N; (power-boosted) 40–100 N.
- **Accelerator pedal force:** 20–50 N.
- **Key equations to memorize:** $M.A. = F_{out}/F_{in} = L_{in}/L_{cable}$; $F_{in} = F_{out}/(\eta \cdot M.A.)$; $d_{stop} = v_0 T_{react} + v_0^{\,2}/(2 \mu g)$.
- **Standards:** ISO 7176, SAE J182a, FMVSS 105/135, ADA Title III, AIS-052 (India), ECE R12/R13-H.
- **Electronic latency requirement:** < 30 ms for safe joystick control.
- **Reaction time penalty:** Hand-control drivers have ~0.10–0.25 s longer reaction time than foot-pedal drivers.
- **Driver training:** Minimum 30–40 hours at a certified rehabilitation driving school is mandatory (per WHO and most national regulations).
- **Clinical prescription workflow:** Assessment → Prescription → Installation → Certification → Training → Licensing.
- **Important safety features:** Return spring, mechanical lockout, pull = brake, fail-safe cable routing, secondary brake system redundancy (for heavy vehicles).
- **Common KTU keywords to use in answers:** *biomechanical envelope, inclusive design, fail-safe, mechanical advantage, ergonomic envelope, drive-by-wire, AIS-052, ISO 7176, RESNA standards, hand-arm vibration syndrome, lactate threshold, MVC, pinch grip, power grip, D1/D2 transfer position.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Secondary Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Control of Secondary Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Rehabilitation Engineering**, **"Secondary Functions"** refer to all activities of daily living (ADL) and environmental interactions that are *not* considered primary life-sustaining functions, but are essential for an individual's independence, dignity, and quality of life. These include operating home appliances, controlling wheelchairs, manipulating doors/windows/lights, using communication aids, and accessing computers.

The **"Control of Secondary Functions"** is the branch of assistive technology that deals with the design, development, and application of input mechanisms, transducers, switching circuits, and signal processing systems that allow persons with severe physical disabilities (such as those with high-level spinal cord injuries, ALS, cerebral palsy, or muscular dystrophy) to operate external devices and interact with their environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Control of Secondary Functions* encompasses the engineering study of interface mechanisms, control strategies, and switching architectures that translate the limited residual voluntary movement of a disabled user into meaningful commands for operating environmental control units (ECUs), powered wheelchairs, communication devices, and computer access systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are lying flat on a bed with **only your eyelids functioning** — nothing else moves. You cannot reach a light switch, you cannot answer a phone, and you cannot change the TV channel. However, you desperately want all of these to happen. How do you communicate these wishes to the external world?

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Universal Remote of the Body":**
> Think of secondary function control like a **smart home remote**, but instead of holding a plastic device in your hand, the "remote" is connected to whatever part of your body you can still move voluntarily. If you can blink — a sensor detects it. If you can sip or puff air — a tube captures it. If you can move your head — a tilt sensor captures it. The Rehabilitation Engineer's job is to design the "translator" between that single small movement and the dozens of devices in the room (fans, lights, phones, beds, computers).

### 1.3 Classification of Secondary Functions

| Category | Example Functions | Typical User Need |
|---|---|---|
| **Mobility Control** | Powered wheelchair operation, bed positioning | Movement in space |
| **Environmental Control** | Lights, fans, TV, doors, windows | Manipulation of surroundings |
| **Communication Control** | Speech synthesizers, typing, telephone | Expressive communication |
| **Computer Access** | Mouse, keyboard, web browsing | Information & productivity |
| **Recreational Control** | Video games, music systems, reading devices | Leisure & quality of life |

### 1.4 The Concept of Residual Function

The fundamental engineering principle behind controlling secondary functions is identifying the user's **Residual Function** — the limited voluntary movement that remains intact after disability.

- **High-level SCI (C1–C3):** Only head/neck and facial muscles available.
- **Mid-level SCI (C4–C5):** Shoulder shrug, neck rotation, slight elbow flexion.
- **Low-level SCI (C6–C7):** Wrist extension, hand grip partial.
- **Neuromuscular disorders:** Tremor, weakness, fatigue.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Principle:** *No two disabilities are identical* — therefore, the control interface must be customized to the *individual's* strongest, most reliable, and most fatigue-resistant residual movement.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Response Time:** A good control system must respond in **< 250 ms** to be perceived as natural by the user.
- **Bit Rate / Information Throughput:** Typically measured in **bits/minute (bpm)**. Average able-bodied typing is ~250 bpm; assistive systems aim for 15–60 bpm minimum for functional communication.
- **Scanning Rate:** For single-switch systems, the typical auto-scan rate is **0.5 Hz to 2 Hz** (one option per 0.5 to 2 seconds).
- **Standard Input Voltage Range:** **3.3 V – 5 V DC** for digital assistive controllers.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** For bioelectric signals (EMG/EEG), minimum acceptable **SNR ≥ 20 dB** for reliable control.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of Secondary Function Control

The complete system, from user intent to device actuation, follows a **four-stage hierarchical architecture**:

#### Stage 1 — Input Transduction (Sensing Layer)
- The user's residual movement is converted into a measurable electrical/electronic signal.
- Example: Blowing into a tube → pressure change → voltage variation.

#### Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning (Processing Layer)
- Amplification, filtering, and noise removal.
- For EMG/EEG signals: bandwidth filtering (typically **20 Hz – 500 Hz** for EMG; **0.5 Hz – 40 Hz** for EEG).

#### Stage 3 — Command Interpretation (Logic Layer)
- The processed signal is mapped to a *specific command* (e.g., "Turn ON Light 3").
- Mapping can be: **one-to-one** (direct selection) or **one-to-many** (scanning/menu-based).

#### Stage 4 — Device Actuation (Output Layer)
- The command triggers a relay, triac, or digital switch that operates the actual device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this hierarchy matters:** A Rehabilitation Engineer can independently optimize each stage. A weak signal at Stage 1 can be rescued by better amplification at Stage 2; a poor command set at Stage 3 can be reconfigured by software without hardware changes.

### 2.2 Methods of Control — Detailed Taxonomy

#### A. Direct Selection Methods
The user directly chooses the desired function.

| Method | Mechanism | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Switches** | Hand/finger-operated buttons | Mild motor impairment |
| **Pneumatic (Sip & Puff)** | Air pressure through mouth tube | High SCI, no hand function |
| **Head Control** | Tilt/pressure sensors on headrest | C1–C3 SCI |
| **Tongue Control** | Intraoral magnetic sensors | C1–C3 SCI, ALS |
| **Chin Control** | Mini-joystick near chin | C3–C5 SCI |
| **Voice Control** | Speech recognition | Users with intact speech |
| **Eye-Gaze Tracking** | IR camera + corneal reflection | ALS, locked-in syndrome |
| **EMG/Bioelectric** | Muscle electrical activity | Muscular dystrophy, amputees |
| **EEG/BCI** | Brain-computer interface | Complete paralysis |

#### B. Indirect (Scanning) Methods
The user selects from an *auto-scanning menu* using a **single switch**.

- **Linear Scanning:** Items highlighted one after another in a row; user presses the switch when target is highlighted.
- **Row-Column Scanning:** A 2D matrix is scanned row-by-row, then column-by-column.
- **Auditory Scanning:** Items spoken aloud; user presses switch when correct item is heard.
- **Block Scanning:** Matrix divided into groups, each scanned in turn.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off:** Scanning is *slower* than direct selection but requires only **one reliable switch site**, making it the universal fallback for severe disabilities.

#### C. Encoded Methods
The user enters a unique code (e.g., **Morse code** — dot/dash) to select a function.

- **Morse Code:** Two distinct signals (e.g., short puff = dot, long puff = dash).
- **Binary Coding:** Sequences of 0/1 pulses.
- **Advantage:** Very high reliability, low fatigue.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires memorization; cognitive load on user.

### 2.3 Environmental Control Units (ECU)

An **Environmental Control Unit (ECU)** is the central hub that connects the user's input switch to multiple home devices.

#### Block Architecture of an ECU

1. **Input Port:** Single or multiple switches (3.5 mm jack, USB, or wireless).
2. **Microcontroller Unit (MCU):** Maps switch events to device commands (e.g., Arduino, STM32, PIC).
3. **Output Ports:** 
   - **X10 / PLC** (Power-Line Communication) for wired home automation.
   - **Infrared (IR) transmitters** to mimic TV/AC remotes.
   - **RF transmitters** (433 MHz, 2.4 GHz) for wireless devices.
   - **Relay boards** for direct mains-voltage switching.
4. **Feedback System:** LCD, LED, or speech synthesizer to confirm action.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Standard:** Modern ECUs (e.g., *GEWA*, *Tobii Dynavox*, *Home Control Assistant*) support both **wired (X10)** and **wireless (Zigbee, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth)** protocols for interoperability.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = G \cdot V_{in}$ | Op-amp signal amplification (Gain G) | V/V (dimensionless) |
| 2 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| 3 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Cutoff frequency of RC filter | Hz |
| 4 | $BPM = \dfrac{N_{bits} \times 60}{T_{selection}}$ | Information throughput (bits per minute) | bpm |
| 5 | $T_{scan} = N \times t_{dwell}$ | Total scan time = items × dwell time | s |
| 6 | $P_{sip} = \Delta P = P_{mouth} - P_{atm}$ | Sip-and-puff pressure differential | cm H₂O or Pa |
| 7 | $V_{EMG} \approx 50 \mu V \to 5 mV$ | Typical EMG amplitude range | V |
| 8 | $f_{EMG} = 20 Hz \to 500 Hz$ | EMG usable bandwidth | Hz |
| 9 | $f_{EEG} = 0.5 Hz \to 40 Hz$ | EEG usable bandwidth | Hz |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Selection accuracy (efficiency) | % |
| 11 | $T_{Morse} = n_{dot} \cdot t_{dot} + n_{dash} \cdot t_{dash}$ | Morse code entry time | s |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Hospital ICU Ventilator Control:** Sip-and-puff allows quadriplegic patients to summon nurses.
- **Smart Home Automation for Elderly:** Voice + motion sensors integrated with ECUs.
- **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC):** Stephen Hawking used a single cheek-muscle twitch to type — a textbook case of secondary function control.
- **Industrial Rehabilitation:** Voice-controlled cranes and lifts in accessible workplaces.
- **Defense:** Sip-and-puff control for submarine periscope aiming.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Information Throughput (Bits per Minute) for a Scanning System

The **selection time** for a single item in a linear scanning array of $N$ items with a dwell time of $t_{dwell}$ is given by the expected time to hit the target. Assuming a uniform random distribution of the target item in the scan, the **average selection time** is:

$$
T_{avg} = \frac{(N+1)}{2} \cdot t_{dwell}
$$

> **Logic:** The user must wait for the scan to reach the target. On average, the target is halfway through the array, hence $(N+1)/2$ positions before selection.

The number of information **bits** conveyed per selection is:

$$
N_{bits} = \log_2(N)
$$

> **Logic:** From information theory (Shannon), $N$ equally likely items carry $\log_2(N)$ bits of information.

Therefore, the **information throughput** in bits per minute (BPM) is:

$$
BPM = \frac{N_{bits} \times 60}{T_{avg}} = \frac{60 \cdot \log_2(N)}{\frac{(N+1)}{2} \cdot t_{dwell}}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
BPM = \frac{120 \cdot \log_2(N)}{(N+1) \cdot t_{dwell}}
$$

#### Worked Example:
For a scanning system with $N = 8$ items and $t_{dwell} = 1.0$ s:

$$
BPM = \frac{120 \cdot \log_2(8)}{(8+1) \cdot 1.0} = \frac{120 \cdot 3}{9} = \frac{360}{9} = 40 \text{ bpm}
$$

> **Result:** 40 bits per minute — sufficient for basic communication but far below able-bodied typing speed (~250 bpm).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cutoff Frequency of a First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter (EMG Noise Removal)

A first-order RC low-pass filter is commonly used to remove high-frequency noise from bioelectric signals (EMG/EEG). The transfer function is:

$$
H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

The magnitude response is:

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}
$$

The **cutoff frequency** $f_c$ is defined as the frequency at which $\vert H \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$ (i.e., -3 dB point):

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow 1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 = 2
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \omega_c RC = 1
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

Converting to frequency in Hz:

$$
f_c = \frac{\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

#### Worked Example:
For a filter with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1 \mu F$:

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10^4 \cdot 10^{-7}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10^{-3}} = \frac{1000}{2\pi} \approx 159.15 \text{ Hz}
$$

> **Result:** This filter blocks frequencies above ~159 Hz — ideal for removing 50/60 Hz mains hum while preserving most EMG content.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Morse Code Entry Time

In a Morse code input system, the user enters characters using **dots (·)** and **dashes (–)**. Let:
- $n_d$ = number of dots in a character
- $n_{dash}$ = number of dashes in a character
- $t_d$ = duration of one dot
- $t_{dash} = 3 t_d$ (standard: a dash is 3× the duration of a dot)
- $n_{gaps}$ = number of inter-element gaps within the character
- $t_{gap} = t_d$ (intra-character gap = 1 dot unit)
- $t_{char\_gap} = 3 t_d$ (inter-character gap = 3 dot units)

The total time to enter one Morse character is:

$$
T_{char} = n_d \cdot t_d + n_{dash} \cdot 3 t_d + n_{gaps} \cdot t_d + t_{char\_gap}
$$

$$
T_{char} = n_d \cdot t_d + 3 n_{dash} \cdot t_d + n_{gaps} \cdot t_d + 3 t_d
$$

$$
T_{char} = t_d \cdot (n_d + 3 n_{dash} + n_{gaps} + 3)
$$

> **Logic:** This explains why short Morse codes (e.g., 'E' = ·) are chosen for common letters — minimizing entry time.

#### Worked Example:
For the letter 'A' (· –): $n_d = 1$, $n_{dash} = 1$, $n_{gaps} = 1$

$$
T_A = t_d \cdot (1 + 3 + 1 + 3) = 8 t_d
$$

For the letter 'E' (·): $n_d = 1$, $n_{dash} = 0$, $n_{gaps} = 0$

$$
T_E = t_d \cdot (1 + 0 + 0 + 3) = 4 t_d
$$

> **Result:** 'E' takes half the time of 'A' — justifying the Morse optimization principle.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Control Methods

| Method | Required Movement | Speed (bpm) | Fatigue Level | Learning Curve | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single Switch + Linear Scan | 1 switch site | 5 – 15 | High | Low | Locked-in, ALS |
| Sip-and-Puff | Mouth/lung | 15 – 30 | Medium | Medium | High SCI |
| Head Control Array | Neck motion | 30 – 60 | Low | Medium | C1–C3 SCI |
| Voice Recognition | Speech | 60 – 150 | Very Low | Low | MS, mild CP |
| Eye-Gaze Tracker | Eye motion | 80 – 200 | Low | Medium | ALS |
| Morse Code (Pneumatic) | Sip/puff | 30 – 60 | Medium | High | SCI veterans |
| Direct Mechanical Keys | Hand | 100 – 250 | Low | Low | Low SCI |
| EMG Control | Residual muscle | 20 – 50 | Low | High | Amputees |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Secondary Function Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Residual Movement] --> B[Input Transducer]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioner]
    C --> D[Microcontroller / Decoder]
    D --> E{Command Type}
    E -- Mobility --> F[Wheelchair Motor Driver]
    E -- Environment --> G[Relay / Triac / IR / RF]
    E -- Communication --> H[AAC Device]
    E -- Computer --> I[Mouse / Keyboard Emulator]
    F --> J[Device Output]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Feedback to User]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Sip-and-Puff Pneumatic Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Mouth] -- Sip or Puff --> B[Pressure Tube]
    B --> C[Pressure Transducer]
    C --> D[Differential Amplifier]
    D --> E[Threshold Comparator]
    E -- Above Threshold --> F[Microcontroller]
    E -- Below Threshold --> G[Neutral State]
    F --> H{Mode Selection}
    H -- Mode 1 --> I[Wheelchair Forward / Reverse]
    H -- Mode 2 --> J[Device Menu Selection]
    H -- Mode 3 --> K[Computer Cursor Control]
    I --> L[Actuator Output]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Visual / Audio Feedback]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Environmental Control Unit (ECU) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        S1[Sip-Puff Switch]
        S2[Head Switch Array]
        S3[Voice Microphone]
        S4[Single Scan Switch]
    end
    subgraph ProcessingLayer["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        MCU[Microcontroller STM32]
        MEM[Memory - Command Lookup Table]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1[IR Transmitter - TV AC]
        O2[Relay Board - Lights Fan]
        O3[RF Module - Smart Plug]
        O4[X10 PLC Module]
    end
    subgraph FeedbackLayer["FEEDBACK LAYER"]
        LCD[LCD Display]
        SPK[Speech Synthesizer]
        LED[Status LED]
    end
    S1 --> MCU
    S2 --> MCU
    S3 --> MCU
    S4 --> MCU
    MCU --> MEM
    MEM --> MCU
    MCU --> O1
    MCU --> O2
    MCU --> O3
    MCU --> O4
    MCU --> LCD
    MCU --> SPK
    MCU --> LED
```

### 4.4 State Diagram — Single-Switch Scanning Operation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Scanning: Switch Pressed Once
    Scanning --> Highlight1: T = 0
    Highlight1 --> Highlight2: t dwell
    Highlight2 --> Highlight3: t dwell
    Highlight3 --> Highlight4: t dwell
    Highlight4 --> Execute3: Switch Pressed
    Highlight4 --> Wrap: t dwell
    Execute3 --> Feedback
    Feedback --> Idle: 1.5 sec delay
    Wrap --> Highlight1
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Tongue-Operated Intraoral Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tongue Tip Movement] --> B[Intraoral Magnetic Sensor Array]
    B --> C[Wireless Transmitter]
    C --> D[External Receiver Module]
    D --> E[Decoding Processor]
    E --> F[Device Control - Wheelchair Computer Phone]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define an Environmental Control Unit (ECU) and list any four devices it can operate in a smart home setup for a person with quadriplegia.**

**Model Answer:**
An Environmental Control Unit (ECU) is an electronic assistive device that allows a person with severe physical disability to independently control multiple household appliances and environmental systems through a single input mechanism (such as a sip-and-puff tube, head switch, or single switch).

Four devices it can operate:
1. Lights and fans (via relay modules).
2. Television and air conditioner (via infrared emulation).
3. Telephone and intercom (via dialer interface).
4. Door openers and curtain motors (via relay switching).

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the working principle of a sip-and-puff switch with a neat block diagram.**

**Model Answer:**
A sip-and-puff switch operates on **pneumatic pressure differential** detection:
- The user **sips** (inhales) or **puffs** (exhales) into a plastic tube held in the mouth.
- The **pressure transducer** at the other end converts the pressure change into a proportional analog voltage.
- A **differential amplifier** boosts the signal; a **threshold comparator** then categorizes the input into three states: SIP (negative pressure), PUFF (positive pressure), and NEUTRAL (no flow).
- The microcontroller maps these states to device commands (e.g., forward, reverse, stop for a wheelchair).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Students often forget to mention the *neutral state* — losing 1 mark.

---

### **PART B — Full Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)**

---

#### **Question A** (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**a)** With a block diagram, explain the architecture of a **single-switch scanning-based environmental control system** for a person with a high cervical spinal cord injury. Discuss linear and row-column scanning methods. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Derive the expression for **information throughput (bits per minute)** of a linear scanning system with $N$ items and dwell time $t_{dwell}$. Calculate the BPM for $N = 16$ and $t_{dwell} = 0.8$ s. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

A person with high cervical SCI (C1–C3) typically has no hand/arm function. The rehabilitation engineer identifies a **single reliable switch site** — for example, a slight head movement detected by a tilt sensor.

**Block Diagram:**
```
User Head Tilt → Tilt Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Microcontroller
        ↓
Row-Column Scan Display (LCD)
        ↓
User Confirmation Switch → Device Selection (Light/Fan/TV/Door)
        ↓
IR/Relay Output → Device Activated
```

**Linear Scanning:** The controller cycles through items in a single list (e.g., Light, Fan, TV, Door, Phone…). The user presses the switch when the desired item is highlighted. Average selection time = $\frac{(N+1)}{2} \cdot t_{dwell}$.

**Row-Column Scanning:** The menu is arranged in a 2D matrix. First, rows are scanned; user selects the row containing the target. Then, columns within that row are scanned. Total selections reduce from $N$ to $2\sqrt{N}$, greatly improving speed.

**Advantages of scanning:** Simple, low-cost, requires only one switch.
**Disadvantages:** Slower; cognitively demanding under stress.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Starting from the average scan time:

$$
T_{avg} = \frac{(N+1)}{2} \cdot t_{dwell}
$$

Number of bits per selection:

$$
N_{bits} = \log_2(N)
$$

Therefore:

$$
BPM = \frac{60 \cdot N_{bits}}{T_{avg}} = \frac{60 \cdot \log_2(N)}{\frac{(N+1)}{2} \cdot t_{dwell}} = \frac{120 \cdot \log_2(N)}{(N+1) \cdot t_{dwell}}
$$

**Substituting** $N = 16$ and $t_{dwell} = 0.8$ s:

$$
BPM = \frac{120 \cdot \log_2(16)}{(16+1) \cdot 0.8} = \frac{120 \cdot 4}{17 \cdot 0.8} = \frac{480}{13.6} \approx 35.29 \text{ bpm}
$$

> **Result:** The system delivers approximately **35.29 bits per minute** — adequate for basic environmental commands but suboptimal for text communication.

---

#### **Question B** (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023]**

**a)** Explain **Morse code as an input method** for persons with severe disabilities. List the time-duration rules and derive the entry time for any arbitrary character. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Compare **direct selection** and **indirect selection** methods of controlling secondary functions. Give two examples of each and state the conditions under which each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Morse Code Input Method:**
Morse code is an **encoded input scheme** that uses only **two distinct signals** — typically a short pulse (dot, ·) and a long pulse (dash, –). For a sip-and-puff user, a short sip = dot; a long puff = dash. This is ideal for users with **one or two reliable switch channels** who can produce time-modulated signals.

**Time-Duration Rules (International Morse Standard):**
- Duration of a **dot** = 1 unit ($t_d$)
- Duration of a **dash** = 3 units ($3 t_d$)
- Gap between elements within a character = 1 unit ($t_d$)
- Gap between characters = 3 units ($3 t_d$)
- Gap between words = 7 units ($7 t_d$)

**Derivation of Entry Time:**

Let $n_d$ = number of dots, $n_{dash}$ = number of dashes, $n_{gaps}$ = number of intra-character gaps. The total character entry time is:

$$
T_{char} = n_d \cdot t_d + n_{dash} \cdot 3t_d + n_{gaps} \cdot t_d + 3t_d
$$

$$
T_{char} = t_d \cdot (n_d + 3n_{dash} + n_{gaps} + 3)
$$

**Example:** For letter 'C' (– · – ·), $n_d = 2$, $n_{dash} = 2$, $n_{gaps} = 3$:

$$
T_C = t_d \cdot (2 + 6 + 3 + 3) = 14 t_d
$$

This explains the **inverse relationship** between character frequency in English and Morse code length — common letters like 'E' (·) and 'T' (–) are kept short to maximize overall throughput.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

| Aspect | Direct Selection | Indirect (Scanning) Selection |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | User actively points to / chooses the target directly | Target is auto-highlighted; user confirms via switch |
| **Cognitive Load** | Low | Medium to High |
| **Speed** | Fast (up to 250 bpm) | Slow (5–40 bpm) |
| **Physical Demand** | Requires precise motor control | Requires only one switch site |
| **Examples** | (1) Joystick for wheelchair; (2) Eye-gaze tracker for computer; (3) Direct keypad on AAC device | (1) Linear scan with single head switch; (2) Row-column scan with sip-puff |
| **Preferred When** | User has reliable, precise voluntary movement (e.g., low SCI, MS in remission) | User has only one reliable switch site (e.g., locked-in syndrome, advanced ALS) |
| **Hardware Complexity** | High (multiple sensor channels) | Low (single switch + display) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "direct" with "manual":** Direct selection is *not* synonymous with manual; voice/eye-gaze are also direct.
> 2. **Forgetting the feedback loop:** Many students describe input and output but omit user feedback — a critical omission in ECU design.
> 3. **Units mismatch in BPM:** Always use **bits/minute**, not bits/second, for assistive communication systems.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram in ECU questions:** A 1-mark deduction is standard if no diagram is included.
> 5. **In Morse code derivations:** Students often forget to add the **inter-character gap (3 t_d)**, leading to a 1-mark loss.

---

### **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER** 📋

✅ **Core Definition:** Control of Secondary Functions = the engineering of interfaces that translate residual voluntary movement into commands for environmental/mobility/communication devices.

✅ **Central Principle:** Identify the user's **residual function** first, then design the interface around it.

✅ **Three Master Categories of Control:**
1. **Direct Selection** (joystick, voice, eye-gaze, EMG, sip-puff with menu).
2. **Indirect Selection / Scanning** (linear, row-column, auditory, block).
3. **Encoded Selection** (Morse code, binary coding).

✅ **The Four-Stage Architecture:** Input Transduction → Signal Conditioning → Command Interpretation → Device Actuation → **Feedback**.

✅ **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
- BPM throughput: $BPM = \dfrac{120 \cdot \log_2(N)}{(N+1) \cdot t_{dwell}}$
- RC filter cutoff: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$
- SNR: $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_n}\right)$
- Morse entry time: $T_{char} = t_d(n_d + 3n_{dash} + n_{gaps} + 3)$

✅ **Standard Performance Metrics:**
- Response time **< 250 ms** (perceived as natural).
- Functional communication at **≥ 15 bpm**.
- EMG usable bandwidth: **20–500 Hz**.
- EEG usable bandwidth: **0.5–40 Hz**.
- Scanning dwell: **0.5–2 s**.

✅ **Hardware Building Blocks:** Pressure transducer, tilt sensor, electromyography electrodes, EEG electrodes, microcontroller (Arduino/STM32), relay board, IR transmitter, RF module (433 MHz / 2.4 GHz), speech synthesizer, LCD/LED feedback.

✅ **Memorable Industry Examples:** GEWA ECU, Tobii Dynavox, Home Control Assistant, Stephen Hawking's cheek-twitch system, sip-and-puff wheelchair controls.

✅ **Mnemonic for Scanning Methods: L-R-A-B** → **L**inear, **R**ow-column, **A**uditory, **B**lock.

✅ **Mnemonic for Control Architecture: I-S-C-O-F** → **I**nput, **S**ignal conditioning, **C**ommand interpretation, **O**utput actuation, **F**eedback.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheelchair Safety Standards and Testing: Standard Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wheelchair Safety Standards and Testing: Standard Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wheelchair Safety Standards** are a set of internationally recognized technical specifications, test methods, and performance criteria that govern the design, construction, durability, stability, and safety of manual and powered wheelchairs. These standards ensure that wheelchairs provide safe, reliable, and effective mobility to users with diverse disabilities while minimizing the risk of injury, structural failure, or tip-over accidents.

The principal international standard governing wheelchair safety is the **ISO 7176 series** (adopted as **EN 12183 / EN 12184** in Europe and aligned with **ANSI/RESNA WC** standards in the United States). In India, **IS 12659** and **IS 15164** series standards apply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO 7176 Series (International Wheelchair Standard):**
> The ISO 7176 series consists of **more than 30 parts** covering every aspect of wheelchair design and performance, from static stability to climatic testing, electrical safety, and resistance to ignition.

## 1.2 The Complete ISO 7176 Series — Syllabus Mapping

| Part | Title / Focus | KTU Relevance |
|:----:|:--------------|:--------------|
| Part 1 | Determination of static stability | High |
| Part 2 | Determination of dynamic stability of electric wheelchairs | High |
| Part 3 | Determination of effectiveness of brakes | High |
| Part 5 | Determination of overall dimensions, mass and turning space | Medium |
| Part 7 | Measurement of seating and wheel dimensions | Low |
| Part 8 | Static, impact and fatigue strength tests | High |
| Part 9 | Climatic tests for electric wheelchairs | Medium |
| Part 10 | Determination of obstacle-climbing ability | High |
| Part 11 | Test dummies | Medium |
| Part 13 | Determination of coefficient of friction of test surfaces | Low |
| Part 14 | Power and control systems for electric wheelchairs | Medium |
| Part 15 | Information disclosure, documentation and labelling | Low |
| Part 16 | Resistance to ignition of upholstered parts | Medium |
| Part 19 | Wheeled mobility devices for use in motor vehicles | High |
| Part 22 | Set-up procedures | Medium |
| Part 26 | Vocabulary | Low |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Car Crash Test" of Wheelchairs

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of wheelchair safety standards exactly like the **NCAP / Euro NCAP crash test ratings** for cars. Before any car is sold, it must undergo frontal crash tests, side-impact tests, brake tests on wet roads, and fatigue tests on rough tracks. **ISO 7176 does the same thing for wheelchairs** — it makes sure a wheelchair will not collapse when a 100 kg user sits down hard, will not tip backward on a slope, will not fail brakes on a ramp, and will survive 200,000 cycles of armrest loading without breaking.

Just as a car buyer trusts the "5-star NCAP rating," an assistive technology practitioner trusts the ISO 7176 certification when prescribing a wheelchair to a user.

## 1.4 Why Are Standard Tests Mandatory?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Engineering Reasons Why Standards Are Non-Negotiable:**
> 1. **User Safety** — Wheelchair users depend on the device for mobility; failure can cause fatal injury.
> 2. **Manufacturer Liability** — Standards provide legal defensibility and global market access.
> 3. **Universal Comparability** — A wheelchair tested in India is comparable to one tested in the USA or Japan, enabling fair procurement decisions.

The most critical performance parameters evaluated are: **stability, strength, durability, brake performance, and obstacle-climbing ability**. These five categories form the backbone of any KTU examination question on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Major Test Categories

### 2.1.1 Static Stability Tests (ISO 7176-1)

Static stability measures the **resistance of a wheelchair to tip over** when stationary on an inclined surface. There are three directions of testing:

1. **Forward stability** — tipping over front wheels (most common accident type)
2. **Rearward stability** — tipping over rear wheels (electric wheelchairs at high speed)
3. **Lateral stability** — tipping over sideways (going up/down curbs)

**Physics Behind the Test:**
The wheelchair is placed on a tilt platform. The angle of the platform is increased gradually until one wheel lifts off the surface. The angle at which the wheel lifts is recorded as the **tipping angle** $\theta$.

### 2.1.2 Dynamic Stability Tests (ISO 7176-2)

Dynamic stability applies only to **electric/powered wheelchairs**. It measures the wheelchair's behavior during motion — including acceleration, deceleration, turning, and climbing slopes. A **stability monitor** or **anti-tip wheel sensor** is typically used.

### 2.1.3 Brake Effectiveness Tests (ISO 7176-3)

Brakes are tested on an inclined platform. The wheelchair is loaded with a **130 kg test dummy** and parked on the slope. The minimum slope the parking brake must hold without rolling is specified.

### 2.1.4 Strength & Fatigue Tests (ISO 7176-8)

This is the most rigorous test category. The wheelchair is subjected to:
- **Static strength test** — sudden impact load
- **Impact test (drop test)** — wheelchair dropped from a specified height
- **Fatigue test** — repeated cyclic loading (up to **200,000 cycles**)
- **Handrim strength test** — 25,000 N cyclic load
- **Caster fork strength test** — 11,000 N load

### 2.1.5 Obstacle-Climbing Ability (ISO 7176-10)

The wheelchair must climb a standardized **50 mm high obstacle** at a specified approach speed. For electric wheelchairs, the minimum required speed is **3 km/h**; for manual wheelchairs, a defined pushing force is applied.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save This Table — These Equations Appear in Nearly Every KTU Question on Wheelchair Testing.**

| Symbol | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Unit | Application |
|:------:|:-------------------|:--------|:----:|:------------|
| $\theta_{tip}$ | $\tan(\theta_{tip}) = \dfrac{a}{h}$ | Tipping angle from geometry | rad / ° | Static stability |
| $F_{tip}$ | $F_{tip} = \dfrac{W \cdot d_{cog}}{h_{cog}}$ | Force required to tip the wheelchair | N | Stability analysis |
| $a_{max}$ | $a_{max} = g \cdot \tan(\theta_{slope})$ | Max acceleration before sliding | m/s² | Dynamic stability |
| $d_{brake}$ | $d_{brake} = \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 \mu g}$ | Stopping distance on slope | m | Brake effectiveness |
| $F_{impact}$ | $F_{impact} = m \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{h_{drop}}{\Delta x}$ | Impact force in drop test | N | Impact test |
| $N_{cycles}$ | $N_{cycles} = 2 \times 10^{5}$ (armrests) | ISO-required fatigue cycles | cycles | Fatigue test |
| $\sigma_{max}$ | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Max bending stress in frame | N/m² | Frame strength |
| $P_{climb}$ | $P_{climb} = W \cdot v \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Power to climb a slope | W | Obstacle climbing |
| $F_{push}$ | $F_{push} = \mu_r \cdot W \cdot \cos(\theta) + W \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Manual propulsion force on slope | N | Manual wheelchair |
| $\theta_{anti-tip}$ | $\theta_{anti-tip} \geq 5°$ rear, $7°$ forward | ISO 7176-3 minimum brake slope | ° | Brake test |

> [!NOTE]
> In all the above formulas:
> - $W$ = total weight (user + wheelchair) in N
> - $m$ = total mass in kg
> - $g$ = **9.81 m/s²** (gravitational acceleration)
> - $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (typically 0.6 to 0.8 for rubber on dry surface)
> - $v$ = velocity in m/s
> - $h_{cog}$ = height of center of gravity in m
> - $d_{cog}$ = horizontal distance from CoG to tipping edge in m

## 2.3 Underlying Physics — The "Why" Behind Each Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Breakdown of the Stability Principle:**
> A wheelchair is essentially an **inverted pendulum on wheels**. The stability is governed entirely by the position of the **center of gravity (CoG)** relative to the **base of support** (the polygon formed by the four wheel contact points).
> 
> - If the **vertical line from the CoG falls inside** the base of support → wheelchair is **stable**.
> - If the **vertical line from the CoG falls outside** the base of support → wheelchair **tips over**.
> 
> The test platform is essentially finding the **critical angle** at which the CoG line just exits the support polygon.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These standardized tests are critical in:
- **Government procurement** (e.g., ADIP scheme in India for free wheelchair distribution)
- **WHO GATE program** (Global Cooperation on Assistive Technology)
- **ISO-compliant manufacturing** for export
- **Rehabilitation engineering prescription** by clinical professionals
- **Insurance reimbursement** documentation in hospitals

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Static Stability Tipping Angle (ISO 7176-1)

**Problem Setup:** A wheelchair has the following test parameters:
- Total loaded mass: $m = 110$ kg (wheelchair + 100 kg test dummy)
- Height of center of gravity from ground: $h_{cog} = 0.55$ m
- Horizontal distance from CoG vertical line to the front wheel contact patch: $a = 0.45$ m
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Find:** The forward tipping angle $\theta_{tip}$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Geometry

The wheelchair on an inclined plane forms a right triangle. The CoG is at height $h_{cog}$ above the wheel contact point. As the platform tilts by angle $\theta$, the **vertical line through the CoG** rotates by the same angle.

### Step 2 — Set Up the Critical Stability Condition

The wheelchair is on the verge of tipping when the **vertical from the CoG passes exactly through the front wheel contact edge**. Beyond this angle, the CoG moves outside the support polygon and the wheelchair tips forward.

From the geometry of the tilted wheelchair:

$$
\tan(\theta_{tip}) = \frac{a}{h_{cog}}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Values

$$
\tan(\theta_{tip}) = \frac{0.45 \text{ m}}{0.55 \text{ m}} = 0.8182
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Angle

$$
\theta_{tip} = \arctan(0.8182)
$$

$$
\theta_{tip} = 39.3°
$$

### Step 5 — Verify with the Force Method

At the critical tipping angle, the **front wheels just lift off**, meaning the normal force on the front wheel $N_f = 0$. The sum of moments about the rear wheel contact patch must equal zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W \cdot \cos(\theta) \cdot d_{cog} - W \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot h_{cog} &= 0 \\
W \cdot \cos(\theta) \cdot d_{cog} &= W \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot h_{cog} \\
\tan(\theta_{tip}) &= \frac{d_{cog}}{h_{cog}} = \frac{a}{h_{cog}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the geometric derivation. The wheelchair remains **stable up to 39.3°** of forward inclination — well above the **ISO 7176-1 minimum of 9°** for forward stability.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Brake Effectiveness on a Slope (ISO 7176-3)

**Problem Setup:** A wheelchair (mass 18 kg) with a 100 kg test dummy is parked on a ramp. Determine the **minimum coefficient of friction** required for the parking brake to hold the wheelchair on a **7° slope** (the ISO minimum requirement for forward parking).

### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram on the Incline

Forces acting on the wheelchair on an inclined plane:
- Weight component along slope (pulling it downhill): $W \sin(\theta)$
- Weight component perpendicular to slope: $W \cos(\theta)$
- Braking friction force (acting uphill): $F_{brake} = \mu N = \mu W \cos(\theta)$

### Step 2 — Apply Equilibrium Condition

For the wheelchair to remain stationary, the braking force must at least equal the gravitational pull along the slope:

$$
F_{brake} \geq W \sin(\theta)
$$

$$
\mu W \cos(\theta) \geq W \sin(\theta)
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify

The mass $W$ cancels out from both sides:

$$
\mu \geq \tan(\theta)
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the ISO 7176-3 Specified Value

$$
\mu_{min} = \tan(7°) = 0.1228
$$

### Step 5 — Add the Safety Factor (KTU Frequently Asked)

ISO 7176-3 specifies a **safety factor of 1.5** for the brake design:

$$
\mu_{design} = 1.5 \times \mu_{min} = 1.5 \times 0.1228 = 0.1842
$$

Therefore, the brake pad material must provide a coefficient of friction of **at least 0.185** with the tire under all operating conditions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Impact Force in the Drop Test (ISO 7176-8)

**Problem Setup:** A wheelchair (mass 18 kg) is subjected to the ISO 7176-8 **drop test**. The wheelchair is dropped from a height of **$h_{drop} = 0.05$ m** (5 cm). Determine the impact force on the frame, assuming the frame deflects by $\Delta x = 0.005$ m (5 mm) on impact.

### Step 1 — Calculate the Velocity Just Before Impact

Using conservation of energy:

$$
\frac{1}{2} m v^{2} = m g h_{drop}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{2 g h_{drop}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Values

$$
v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.05} = \sqrt{0.981} = 0.9905 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the Deceleration During Impact

Using the kinematic equation $v^{2} = u^{2} - 2 a \Delta x$, with final velocity $v = 0$ at maximum compression:

$$
0 = (0.9905)^{2} - 2 a (0.005)
$$

$$
a = \frac{0.981}{0.010} = 98.1 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate the Impact Force

$$
F_{impact} = m a = 18 \times 98.1 = 1765.8 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 5 — Compare to the Static Load

The static weight of the wheelchair is:

$$
W = m g = 18 \times 9.81 = 176.58 \text{ N}
$$

The impact force is **10 times** the static load. This is why ISO 7176-8 requires the frame to withstand **10× static load** in the impact test — and the test must show **no permanent deformation** or weld fracture.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Test Type | Standard | Critical Angle / Load | Pass Criterion | Failure Mode Detected |
|:----------|:---------|:----------------------|:---------------|:----------------------|
| Forward static | ISO 7176-1 | 39.3° (calculated) | $\geq 9°$ | Forward tip-over |
| Rearward static | ISO 7176-1 | Tested separately | $\geq 6°$ | Rearward tip-over (electric) |
| Lateral static | ISO 7176-1 | Tested separately | $\geq 9°$ | Sideways tip-over |
| Brake forward | ISO 7176-3 | $\mu \geq 0.123$ | Holds on 7° slope | Brake slip |
| Brake reverse | ISO 7176-3 | $\mu \geq 0.087$ | Holds on 5° slope | Brake slip reverse |
| Impact drop | ISO 7176-8 | $F \approx 1766$ N | No fracture | Frame weld failure |
| Armrest fatigue | ISO 7176-8 | 200,000 cycles | No failure | Armrest fracture |
| Obstacle climb | ISO 7176-10 | 50 mm height | Climbs at 3 km/h | Motor torque insufficient |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete ISO 7176 Testing Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the standardized sequence in which wheelchair tests are performed in a certified testing laboratory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT Wheelchair Sample Loaded with 100 kg Test Dummy"] --> B["Pre Conditioning at 20 deg C and 50 percent RH for 24 hours"]
    B --> C{"Select Test Type"}
    C --> D["ISO 7176 Part 1 Static Stability Test on Tilt Platform"]
    C --> E["ISO 7176 Part 3 Brake Effectiveness Test on Inclined Ramp"]
    C --> F["ISO 7176 Part 8 Static Impact and Fatigue Strength Tests"]
    C --> G["ISO 7176 Part 10 Obstacle Climbing Test 50 mm Block"]
    C --> H["ISO 7176 Part 2 Dynamic Stability for Powered Wheelchairs"]
    D --> D1["Record Tipping Angles Forward Rearward Lateral"]
    E --> E1["Verify Brake Holds on 7 degree Forward and 5 degree Reverse Slope"]
    F --> F1["Apply 200000 Fatigue Cycles and Drop from 50 mm"]
    G --> G1["Measure Approach Speed and Motor Current"]
    H --> H1["Run on Treadmill with Stability Sensors"]
    D1 --> I{"Pass or Fail Evaluation"}
    E1 --> I
    F1 --> I
    G1 --> I
    H1 --> I
    I --> J["OUTPUT Generate ISO 7176 Compliance Certificate"]

    style I fill:#FF6347,color:#fff
    style J fill:#32CD32,color:#fff
```

The diagram below illustrates the **instrumented tilt platform** used in ISO 7176-1 testing, showing the load cells, inclinometer, and wheel sensors that capture the tipping event.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B --> C["Inclinometer Resolution 0.1 degree"]

```
