# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define ISO 7176 and list any six parts of this standard with their focus areas. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

ISO 7176 is the **International Organization for Standardization** standard series that specifies requirements and test methods for manual and powered wheelchairs. The series ensures that wheelchairs meet minimum safety, performance, and durability requirements before being placed in the market.

Six important parts of ISO 7176 are:

1. **Part 1:** Determination of static stability (forward, rearward, lateral tipping angles).
2. **Part 2:** Determination of dynamic stability of electric wheelchairs.
3. **Part 3:** Determination of effectiveness of brakes.
4. **Part 8:** Requirements and test methods for static, impact, and fatigue strength.
5. **Part 10:** Determination of obstacle-climbing ability (50 mm obstacle).
6. **Part 19:** Wheeled mobility devices for use as seats in motor vehicles.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the list with correct focus)*

### Question 2: What is the significance of the drop test in ISO 7176-8? Specify the drop height. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The **drop test (impact test)** in ISO 7176-8 simulates the sudden shock loading that a wheelchair experiences when it falls off a curb, is dropped from a vehicle, or is mishandled during transport. The test ensures that the wheelchair frame, welds, and joints can absorb the impact energy without permanent deformation or fracture.

- **Standard drop height:** $h_{drop} = 50$ mm (0.05 m) as per ISO 7176-8.
- **Acceptance criterion:** No visible fracture, no permanent deformation greater than **2% of frame dimension**, and no failure of any welded joint.

*(3 marks: 1 for significance + 1 for drop height + 1 for acceptance criterion)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1):** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**a)** Explain in detail the **static stability test (ISO 7176-1)** for a manual wheelchair. Describe the test setup, procedure, and the three directions of testing. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A wheelchair with a 100 kg test dummy has a center of gravity located at a height of 0.50 m and 0.40 m horizontally behind the front wheel contact patch. Calculate the **forward tipping angle** and comment on whether it satisfies the ISO 7176-1 minimum requirement of 9°. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Static Stability Test (ISO 7176-1):**

**1. Test Setup:**
- A **hydraulically operated tilt platform** of minimum size 1.5 m × 1.5 m is used.
- The platform is instrumented with an **inclinometer** (resolution 0.1°) and **load cells** at each wheel contact point.
- The wheelchair is loaded with a **100 kg test dummy** (ISO 7176-11) seated in the normal riding posture.
- The test is conducted at a **temperature of 20 ± 5 °C** and **relative humidity of 50 ± 10%**.

**2. Procedure:**
- The wheelchair is placed on the platform with all **four wheels in contact** and **brakes released**.
- The platform is tilted at a rate not exceeding **1°/second**.
- The tilt continues until **any one wheel lifts off** the platform surface.
- The **angle of tilt at the moment of wheel lift** is recorded as the tipping angle.
- The test is repeated **three times in each direction** and the **average value** is reported.

**3. Three Directions of Testing:**

| Direction | Setup | Records | ISO Minimum |
|:----------|:------|:--------|:------------|
| **Forward** | Rear of wheelchair against the tilt axis | Anti-tip devices removed | $\geq 9°$ |
| **Rearward** | Front of wheelchair against the tilt axis | All wheels free | $\geq 6°$ for electric, $\geq 9°$ for manual |
| **Lateral** | Side of wheelchair against the tilt axis | Left and right tested | $\geq 9°$ (both sides) |

**4. Significance:**
A wheelchair with adequate static stability prevents tip-over accidents on slopes, ramps, and uneven terrain — the most common cause of wheelchair-related injuries.

*(7 marks: 2 for setup + 2 for procedure + 3 for three directions with values)*

**Part (b) — Forward Tipping Angle Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 100$ kg (user) + 18 kg (wheelchair) = 118 kg total
- Height of CoG: $h_{cog} = 0.50$ m
- Horizontal distance from CoG vertical to front wheel contact: $a = 0.40$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1:** Apply the tipping condition formula:

$$
\tan(\theta_{tip}) = \frac{a}{h_{cog}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$
\tan(\theta_{tip}) = \frac{0.40}{0.50} = 0.80
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the angle:

$$
\theta_{tip} = \arctan(0.80) = 38.66°
$$

**Step 4:** Compare with ISO 7176-1 minimum:

Since $38.66° \gg 9°$, the wheelchair **easily satisfies** the ISO 7176-1 forward stability requirement with a **large safety margin of 29.66°**.

**Comment:** A high tipping angle indicates that the wheelchair's CoG is well-positioned rearward of the front wheel contact, providing excellent resistance to forward tip-over during normal use, including sudden stops on a downhill slope.

*(7 marks: 2 for formula + 2 for substitution + 1 for calculation + 2 for comparison and comment)*

### **Question B (Choice 2):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**a)** Explain the **brake effectiveness test (ISO 7176-3)** in detail. List the minimum slope requirements for parking brakes. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A wheelchair with a 100 kg test dummy is parked on a slope. Calculate the **minimum coefficient of friction** required between the brake pad and the tire so that the parking brake holds the wheelchair on a **7° forward slope**. Apply a safety factor of 1.5 as required by ISO 7176-3. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Brake Effectiveness Test (ISO 7176-1):**

**1. Purpose:**
To verify that the **parking brake** of a wheelchair can hold the wheelchair (with user) stationary on an inclined surface without rolling or sliding.

**2. Test Setup:**
- An **inclined ramp** with a non-slip surface (coefficient of friction $\mu \geq 0.6$).
- The wheelchair is loaded with a **100 kg ISO test dummy**.
- The brake is applied per the **manufacturer's instructions**.
- An **inclinometer** is used to measure the slope angle.

**3. Procedure:**
- The wheelchair is placed on the ramp facing **forward**, brakes engaged.
- The ramp angle is increased gradually until the **wheelchair begins to roll**, OR the maximum test angle is reached.
- The procedure is repeated facing **reverse**.
- For **electric wheelchairs**, the brake is also tested under **dynamic conditions** (ISO 7176-2).

**4. Minimum Slope Requirements (ISO 7176-3):**

| Test Direction | Minimum Slope | Comment |
|:---------------|:--------------|:--------|
| Forward | $7°$ | Parking brake must hold |
| Reverse | $5°$ | Parking brake must hold |
| Lateral | $9°$ (static) | For overall stability |

**5. Acceptance Criterion:**
The wheelchair must not roll, slide, or tip over at the specified slope angle. **No brake slip is permitted for 5 minutes** at the test angle.

*(7 marks: 2 for purpose/setup + 2 for procedure + 3 for minimum values and acceptance)*

**Part (b) — Minimum Coefficient of Friction Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Required slope angle: $\theta = 7°$
- Safety factor: $SF = 1.5$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s² (not directly needed)

**Step 1:** Apply the equilibrium condition. For the wheelchair to remain stationary, the braking friction must equal the gravitational component along the slope:

$$
\mu W \cos(\theta) \geq W \sin(\theta)
$$

**Step 2:** Simplify by canceling $W$ from both sides:

$$
\mu \geq \tan(\theta)
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\theta = 7°$:

$$
\mu_{min} = \tan(7°) = 0.1228
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the safety factor of 1.5:

$$
\mu_{design} = SF \times \mu_{min} = 1.5 \times 0.1228 = 0.1842
$$

**Step 5:** Round up for material selection:

$$
\mu_{design} \approx 0.19
$$

**Conclusion:** The brake pad material must provide a coefficient of friction of **at least 0.19** with the tire material under all conditions (wet, dry, hot, cold). Common materials that meet this requirement include:
- **Rubber compounds** (dry: 0.6–0.8, wet: 0.4–0.5) — easily passes.
- **Polyurethane** (dry: 0.4–0.6) — easily passes.
- **Sintered metal** (dry: 0.5–0.7) — easily passes.

The **safety factor of 1.5** ensures the brake performs reliably even when the surfaces are wet, contaminated with oil, or worn down by long-term use.

*(7 marks: 2 for equilibrium equation + 1 for cancellation + 1 for substitution + 1 for safety factor application + 2 for conclusion and material selection)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Students Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up ISO 7176 parts.** Part 1 is STATIC stability, Part 2 is DYNAMIC stability. Do not interchange them in long answers. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this confusion.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the 100 kg test dummy.** The standard test always uses a **100 kg dummy (or specified load)**, not 75 kg or 80 kg. This is a frequent error in numerical problems.
> 
> 3. **Omitting the safety factor in brake calculations.** ISO 7176-3 mandates a **safety factor of 1.5**. Writing $\mu \geq \tan(\theta)$ without the safety factor loses **1 mark**.
> 
> 4. **Confusing tipping angle with stability angle.** The "tipping angle" is the angle at which tipping BEGINS. Do not write "the wheelchair tips at 0°" — this is a common conceptual error.
> 
> 5. **Not mentioning units in numerical answers.** Always state units: m, m/s, N, °. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units consistently.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the 50 mm drop test height.** ISO 7176-8 specifies exactly **50 mm** drop height. Writing "drop from a height" without specifying loses marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**
> 
> - **ISO 7176 is the master standard** for wheelchairs globally; IS 12659 is its Indian equivalent.
> - **Part 1 = Static Stability** (tipping angle test on tilt platform).
> - **Part 2 = Dynamic Stability** (electric wheelchairs only, motion-based).
> - **Part 3 = Brake Effectiveness** (7° forward, 5° reverse minimum slope, **safety factor 1.5**).
> - **Part 8 = Strength Tests** (drop test from **50 mm**, fatigue test **200,000 cycles** for armrests).
> - **Part 10 = Obstacle Climbing** (50 mm obstacle, 3 km/h approach speed for electric).
> - **Tipping angle formula:** $\tan(\theta_{tip}) = a / h_{cog}$ — this is the most-tested equation.
> - **Brake condition:** $\mu \geq \tan(\theta)$ for hold, with **SF = 1.5** applied.
> - **Impact force in drop test:** $F = m v / \Delta t$ or equivalently $F = m \sqrt{2 g h} / \Delta t$.
> - **Test dummy mass is 100 kg** as per ISO 7176-11.
> - **Always include units** in numerical answers: N, m, m/s, m/s², °.
> - **Lateral stability** is tested on **both left and right** sides.
> - **Anti-tip devices** must be tested separately if present.
> - **Resistance to ignition** (Part 16) is required for **upholstered seating** materials.
> - **Climate test** (Part 9) for electric wheelchairs: $-20°C$ to $+50°C$ operating range.
> - **Information disclosure** (Part 15) requires manufacturer to publish test results.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Recall Mnemonic for KTU Exam Hall:**
> **"S-B-S-F-O-C"** → **S**tability, **B**rakes, **S**trength, **F**atigue, **O**bstacle, **C**limate
> These are the **6 mandatory test categories** for a wheelchair to receive ISO 7176 certification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normative Values
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Normative Values

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Normative Values** in Rehabilitation Engineering are statistically derived, reference-standard measurements of human physical, sensory, anthropometric, biomechanical, and functional parameters obtained from a healthy reference population. These values serve as the **quantitative baseline** against which an individual with impairment, disability, or functional limitation is compared for clinical assessment, device prescription, ergonomic design, and rehabilitation outcome measurement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination-Ready):**
> *Normative values are the standard reference data representing the typical range, mean, and standard deviation of human physiological, anatomical, and functional parameters in a healthy population, used by rehabilitation engineers as a benchmark for assessment and assistive technology design.*

Formally, if a parameter $X$ is measured in a normal population of size $N$, the normative data is characterized by:

$$X_{norm} = \{\mu_X,\ \sigma_X,\ P_{5}^{X},\ P_{50}^{X},\ P_{95}^{X}\}$$

where $\mu_X$ is the population mean, $\sigma_X$ is the standard deviation, and $P_{k}^{X}$ is the $k$-th percentile.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Normative Values in Rehabilitation

| Category | Parameters Included | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Anthropometric Norms** | Stature, weight, segment lengths, reach, body circumference | Wheelchair sizing, prosthetics |
| **Strength Norms** | Grip strength, joint torques, lifting capacity | Ergonomic load limits, orthotic design |
| **Range of Motion (ROM) Norms** | Joint angles in flexion, extension, abduction | ROM assessment, orthotic alignment |
| **Cardiovascular Norms** | Heart rate, VO₂ max, blood pressure | Exercise prescription, fitness evaluation |
| **Sensory Norms** | Visual acuity, hearing thresholds, tactile sensitivity | Sensory aid design |
| **Functional Mobility Norms** | Gait speed, stair climb time, timed up-and-go (TUG) | Mobility aid prescription |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Class Average" Concept**
>
> Imagine your class has 60 students, and the **average score** in a mathematics test is **65/100**. This average acts as a *normative value*. If a student scores **35/100**, you can immediately say they are **below the norm** and need help. If they score **90/100**, they are above the norm.
>
> In **exactly the same way**, Rehabilitation Engineers maintain a "human database of averages" — if the average grip strength of a healthy 25-year-old male is **47 kg**, and a stroke patient's grip is measured at **8 kg**, the engineer knows the patient has lost roughly **83%** of normal function. This deviation directly drives:
> - How much **assistance** a device must provide
> - What **type of orthosis** to prescribe
> - How to set **ergonomic limits** in workplace design

**Physical constants and reference metrics used in normative data:**

- **Standard deviation multiplier for "normal range":** **2 standard deviations (2σ)** captures 95% of the healthy population.
- **Percentile convention:** $P_{5}$ and $P_{95}$ are commonly used as the **boundaries of normal**.
- **Body proportion ratio (sitting height / stature):** approximately **0.52** for adults.
- **Population sampling minimum (statistical reliability):** **N ≥ 100** for stable normative reference.

---

## 1.4 Why Normative Values are Indispensable in Rehabilitation

A rehabilitation engineer cannot make data-driven decisions without normative values because:

1. **Diagnosis of Impairment Magnitude** — Quantifies *how far* a patient deviates from typical.
2. **Assistive Device Customization** — Wheelchair seat width, prosthetic socket fit, and orthotic joint alignment are all derived from anthropometric norms.
3. **Ergonomic and Accessibility Design** — Ramp slopes, door widths, and grab-bar placements rely on population-based dimensions.
4. **Outcome Measurement** — Pre/post rehabilitation progress is judged by movement *toward* the norm.
5. **Legal and Standards Compliance** — Rehabilitation products must align with standards (e.g., ISO 7250, ISO 16840 for wheelchairs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Normative Data Generation

The creation of normative values follows a **structured six-step process** in rehabilitation research and engineering practice:

- **Step 1 — Define the Parameter:** A specific measurable quantity is selected (e.g., knee extension torque).
- **Step 2 — Stratify the Population:** Subjects are divided by age, sex, ethnicity, and anthropometric class to avoid confounding.
- **Step 3 — Standardize Measurement Protocol:** Use of calibrated instruments, fixed postures, and consistent instructions.
- **Step 4 — Sample Adequately:** Minimum $N \geq 100$ per stratum to satisfy the **Central Limit Theorem** and ensure normality of the sampling distribution.
- **Step 5 — Compute Descriptive Statistics:** Mean, standard deviation, percentiles, and confidence intervals.
- **Step 6 — Validate Cross-Population:** Compare with published datasets to confirm reliability.

---

## 2.2 Statistical Foundation — The Why and How

The **"why"** behind using mean $\pm$ 2SD as the normal range is rooted in the **empirical rule** of the normal (Gaussian) distribution:

- **~68%** of healthy individuals fall within $\mu \pm 1\sigma$.
- **~95%** of healthy individuals fall within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$.
- **~99.7%** of healthy individuals fall within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.

Since rehabilitation engineering works with the "typical person," using $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ as the **normal range** is the industry-accepted convention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Percentile vs. Standard Deviation:**
> - $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ corresponds approximately to $P_{2.5}$ to $P_{97.5}$ when the data is normally distributed.
> - For non-Gaussian data, **percentile-based norms** ($P_{5}$–$P_{95}$) are preferred.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Population Mean | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | $x_i$ = individual measurement |
| 2 | Standard Deviation (sample) | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2}$ | Dimensionless or same as $x$ |
| 3 | Normal Range (Gaussian) | $X_{normal} = \mu \pm 2\sigma$ | Captures ~95% of population |
| 4 | Percentile Definition | $P_k = $ value below which $k\%$ of data lies | Used for skewed data |
| 5 | Z-Score (Standard Score) | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Dimensionless; indicates deviation |
| 6 | Coefficient of Variation | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100\%$ | Percentage; measures relative spread |
| 7 | Confidence Interval (95%) | $CI = \mu \pm 1.96 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Used to estimate population mean |
| 8 | Percentage Deviation from Norm | $\Delta\% = \dfrac{x_{patient} - \mu_{norm}}{\mu_{norm}} \times 100\%$ | Negative = below normal |
| 9 | Body Mass Index (BMI) | $BMI = \dfrac{W}{H^2}$ | $W$ in kg, $H$ in m |
| 10 | Stature-to-Sitting Height Ratio | $R_{SH} = \dfrac{SH}{S}$ | $SH$ = sitting height, $S$ = stature |
| 11 | Joint Torque Norm Index | $T_{rel} = \dfrac{T_{measured}}{T_{normative}}$ | Ratio; 1.0 = at norm |
| 12 | Grip Strength Predictor (males) | $GS \approx 0.65 \cdot BW + 5$ | $BW$ = body weight (kg), $GS$ in kgf |
| 13 | Gait Speed Norm | $v_{gait}^{norm} \approx 1.30 \text{ m/s}$ | Healthy adult, comfortable pace |
| 14 | Timed Up-and-Go (TUG) | $T_{TUG}^{norm} \lt 10 \text{ s}$ | Above 12 s = fall risk |
| 15 | Wheelchair Seat Width | $W_{seat} = Hip_{width} + 2 \cdot (30\text{–}50\text{ mm})$ | Anthropometric design rule |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Normative values drive engineering decisions across multiple rehabilitation domains:

- **Wheelchair Engineering:** Seat depth is set at *buttock-to-popliteal length + 25–50 mm clearance* derived from anthropometric norms.
- **Prosthetics:** Residual limb circumference norms guide socket-liner sizing.
- **Ergonomics:** Lifting capacity is set at the $P_{75}$ strength value to accommodate 75% of the workforce.
- **Pediatric Rehabilitation:** Cerebral palsy gait norms are compared against age-stratified developmental norms to grade severity (GMFCS levels).
- **Accessibility Architecture:** Door widths, ramp gradients, and toilet dimensions are derived from wheelchair and anthropometric norms (e.g., **ADA Standards**, **ISO 21542**).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Construction of a Normative Range from Sample Data

**Problem Statement (Foundation Concept):**
A rehabilitation engineer measures the **knee extension torque** (in N·m) of **120 healthy adult males** aged 25–35. The data yields a sample mean $\bar{x} = 175$ N·m and sample standard deviation $s = 25$ N·m. Construct the normative range using the empirical rule.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters given.**
We are given:
$$N = 120,\quad \bar{x} = 175 \text{ N·m},\quad s = 25 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the empirical (68-95-99.7) rule.**
For a normally distributed parameter, the interval $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ contains approximately 95% of healthy values.

**Step 3 — Substitute values into the formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_{lower} &= \mu - 2\sigma \\
&= 175 - 2(25) \\
&= 175 - 50 \\
&= 125 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
X_{upper} &= \mu + 2\sigma \\
&= 175 + 2(25) \\
&= 175 + 50 \\
&= 225 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Express the final normative range.**

$$X_{norm} = 125 \text{ N·m} \leq X \leq 225 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
Any healthy male aged 25–35 should generate knee extension torque within this range with 95% probability. Torque values below 125 N·m suggest **significant quadriceps weakness**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Z-Score and Percentage Deviation for a Patient

**Problem Statement (Application):**
A 30-year-old male stroke patient produces a knee extension torque of $x_p = 95$ N·m during rehabilitation assessment. Using the normative data above, calculate:
- (a) The Z-score of the patient
- (b) The percentage deviation from the normative mean

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the Z-score formula.**

$$Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the patient value and normative data.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z &= \dfrac{95 - 175}{25} \\
&= \dfrac{-80}{25} \\
&= -3.2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the Z-score.**
A Z-score of $-3.2$ means the patient's torque is **3.2 standard deviations below the mean**, well outside the normal range ($Z = \pm 2$).

**Step 4 — Apply the percentage deviation formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta\% &= \dfrac{x_p - \mu_{norm}}{\mu_{norm}} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{95 - 175}{175} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{-80}{175} \times 100\% \\
&= -45.71\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final engineering conclusion.**
The patient has only **54.29%** of normal knee extension strength and requires a **knee-ankle-foot orthosis (KAFO)** with stance-phase locking, plus aggressive quadriceps strengthening.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wheelchair Seat Width from Anthropometric Norms

**Problem Statement (Design Application):**
A rehabilitation engineer must design a **manual wheelchair** for an adult male patient. Standard anthropometric data shows the **hip breadth** of an adult Indian male has:
$$\mu = 360 \text{ mm},\quad \sigma = 28 \text{ mm}$$

If the design uses the $P_{50}$ (median) value and a clearance of $2 \times 40$ mm, calculate the **nominal seat width**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Use the median hip breadth (assuming near-symmetric distribution, $P_{50} \approx \mu$).**

$$P_{50}^{hip} = 360 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the standard ergonomic clearance rule.**

$$W_{seat} = W_{hip} + 2 \cdot C_{clearance}$$

where $C_{clearance} = 40$ mm.

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{seat} &= 360 + 2(40) \\
&= 360 + 80 \\
&= 440 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Express in standard wheelchair sizing.**
The standard Indian manual wheelchair seat width is **440 mm (17 inches)**, which matches the normative-based design.

**Step 5 — Engineering insight.**
For a **heavier or larger patient** ($P_{95} \approx \mu + 1.645\sigma$):

$$P_{95} = 360 + 1.645(28) \approx 406 \text{ mm}$$
$$W_{seat}^{P95} = 406 + 80 = 486 \text{ mm (19-inch wheelchair)}$$

This demonstrates how normative data enables **scaling of assistive devices across the population**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Normative Values Used in KTU Examinations

| Parameter | Population | Normative Value (Mean) | Clinical Cut-off | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Adult Male Grip Strength | 20–40 yrs | **47 kgf** | $<25$ kgf = impaired | Hand orthosis design |
| Adult Female Grip Strength | 20–40 yrs | **28 kgf** | $<15$ kgf = impaired | Hand orthosis design |
| Normal Gait Speed | Adult | **1.30 m/s** | $<0.8$ m/s = limited community ambulator | Mobility aid selection |
| Timed Up-and-Go (TUG) | Elderly | **8 s** | $\geq 12$ s = fall risk | Walker/cane prescription |
| Normal Stair Climb (10 steps) | Adult | **6.0 s** | $>15$ s = severe limitation | Stair-training program |
| VO₂ Max (males) | 25 yrs | **42 mL/kg/min** | $<20$ = very poor | Aerobic capacity assessment |
| Active Shoulder Flexion ROM | Adult | **180°** | $<90°$ = significant ADL loss | Prosthetic shoulder design |
| Active Elbow Flexion ROM | Adult | **150°** | $<90°$ = feeding difficulty | Elbow orthosis |
| Knee Flexion ROM | Adult | **135°** | $<90°$ = stair difficulty | KAFO design |
| Hip Width (Adult Male) | Indian | **360 mm** | Used in $P_{50}$ | Wheelchair seat width |
| Sitting Height/Stature Ratio | Adult | **0.52** | Used for percentile estimation | Pediatric chair design |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — Normative Data Lifecycle in Rehabilitation Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Healthy Reference Population"] --> B["Stratified Sampling by Age, Sex, Body Type"]
    B --> C["Standardized Measurement Protocol"]
    C --> D["Descriptive Statistics Computation"]
    D --> E["Normative Database Established"]
    E --> F["Patient Functional Assessment"]
    F --> G{"Compare Patient vs Norm"}
    G -->|"Within 2 SD"| H["Normal Function Continue Monitoring"]
    G -->|"Below 2 SD"| I["Identify Impairment Magnitude"]
    I --> J["Prescribe Assistive Device or Therapy"]
    J --> K["Reassess and Compare Progress vs Norm"]
    K --> F
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A normative database is built from healthy population data, then used as a **continuous comparison benchmark** during patient assessment, device prescription, and outcome evaluation.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Normative Value Application Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT DOMAIN"]
        I1["Anthropometric Data"]
        I2["Strength Data"]
        I3["Range of Motion Data"]
        I4["Cardiopulmonary Data"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        P1["Statistical Analysis Mean, SD, Percentiles"]
        P2["Normal Range Definition mu plus minus 2 sigma"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["ENGINEERING OUTPUT"]
        O1["Wheelchair Dimensions"]
        O2["Prosthetic Socket Sizing"]
        O3["Orthotic Joint Settings"]
        O4["Ergonomic Load Limits"]
        O5["Accessibility Standards"]
    end

    subgraph FEEDBACK["OUTCOME LOOP"]
        F1["Patient Progress Tracking"]
        F2["Database Refinement"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> O1
    P2 --> O2
    P2 --> O3
    P2 --> O4
    P2 --> O5
    O1 --> F1
    O2 --> F1
    O3 --> F1
    O4 --> F1
    O5 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> P1
```

> **Architectural insight:** Normative values are not a one-time calculation but a **closed-loop feedback system** where patient outcomes continuously refine the reference database.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting a Normative Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start Need to Select a Normative Value"] --> Q1{"Is Data Normally Distributed?"}
    Q1 -->|"Yes Gaussian"| P1["Use mu plus minus 2 sigma Range"]
    Q1 -->|"No Skewed"| P2["Use P5 to P95 Percentile Range"]
    P1 --> Q2{"Is the Population Stratified?"}
    P2 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|"By Age and Sex"| P3["Apply Age Sex Specific Norm Table"]
    Q2 -->|"Not Stratified"| P4["Apply General Adult Norm with Caution"]
    P3 --> END["Use Norm in Design or Assessment"]
    P4 --> END
```

> **Practical use:** This decision tree is exactly how a KTU examiner expects you to *justify* the choice of a normative reference in an exam answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
**Define normative values. Why are they essential in rehabilitation engineering?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Normative values are statistically derived reference measurements of human anatomical, physiological, biomechanical, and functional parameters obtained from a healthy reference population. They typically include the mean, standard deviation, and percentile ranges of parameters such as strength, range of motion, and anthropometric dimensions.

**Importance (1 mark):**
They are essential because they:
- Provide a **quantitative baseline** to quantify a patient's deviation from normal function
- Guide the **design and sizing of assistive devices** such as wheelchairs, prosthetics, and orthoses
- Enable **outcome measurement** during rehabilitation by tracking movement toward the norm

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Style]**
**Explain the difference between mean ± 2 SD and the $P_{5}$–$P_{95}$ approach in establishing normative ranges. When is each preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Mean ± 2 SD approach (1.5 marks):** This method assumes the data follows a Gaussian (normal) distribution. It defines the normal range as $\mu - 2\sigma$ to $\mu + 2\sigma$, capturing approximately **95% of the healthy population**. It is preferred when the data is symmetric and bell-shaped, such as stature or joint torque in a homogeneous population.
- **Percentile approach (1.5 marks):** The $P_{5}$–$P_{95}$ method defines the normal range by ranking the data and selecting the values at the 5th and 95th percentiles. It is preferred when the data is **skewed or non-Gaussian**, such as grip strength in a mixed population or recovery times after injury.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice, Model Paper]**

**(a) Describe the classification of normative values used in rehabilitation engineering. List the parameters under each category. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Normative values in rehabilitation engineering are classified into **six major categories** based on the type of human parameter they describe:

1. **Anthropometric Norms (1 mark):** Body dimensions such as stature, weight, segment lengths, reach, hip breadth, and sitting height. Used in wheelchair sizing, prosthetic design, and ergonomic workspace design.

2. **Strength Norms (1 mark):** Maximum voluntary force or torque values such as grip strength, knee extension torque, and lifting capacity. Used in orthotic prescription and ergonomic load limits.

3. **Range of Motion (ROM) Norms (1 mark):** Joint angular displacements such as shoulder flexion (180°), elbow flexion (150°), and knee flexion (135°). Used in assessing joint dysfunction and designing ROM-limiting or ROM-assisting orthoses.

4. **Cardiovascular Norms (1 mark):** Parameters such as resting heart rate, maximal oxygen consumption (VO₂ max), and blood pressure. Used in prescribing aerobic exercise programs for cardiac and pulmonary rehabilitation.

5. **Sensory Norms (1 mark):** Thresholds for vision, hearing, and tactile sensation. Used in designing sensory aids such as hearing aids and tactile feedback devices.

6. **Functional Mobility Norms (1 mark):** Performance-based measures such as gait speed, Timed Up-and-Go (TUG), and stair climb time. Used in selecting mobility aids and predicting fall risk.

7. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Each category supports a different aspect of rehabilitation engineering — from device design to outcome assessment — and collectively they form a comprehensive reference system.

---

**(b) A rehabilitation engineer measures the grip strength of 150 healthy adult females aged 25–35 and obtains a mean of 28 kgf and a standard deviation of 5 kgf. A 30-year-old female patient with rheumatoid arthritis has a measured grip strength of 16 kgf. Calculate the Z-score and percentage deviation. Comment on the engineering implications. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given data:**
$$\mu = 28 \text{ kgf},\quad \sigma = 5 \text{ kgf},\quad x_p = 16 \text{ kgf}$$

**Step 1 — Calculate the Z-score (2 marks):**

$$Z = \dfrac{x_p - \mu}{\sigma} = \dfrac{16 - 28}{5} = \dfrac{-12}{5} = -2.4$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the percentage deviation (2 marks):**

$$\Delta\% = \dfrac{x_p - \mu}{\mu} \times 100\% = \dfrac{16 - 28}{28} \times 100\% = -42.86\%$$

**Step 3 — Determine the normative range (1 mark):**

$$X_{normal} = 28 \pm 2(5) = 18 \text{ to } 38 \text{ kgf}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering implications (2 marks):**
- The patient's grip strength (16 kgf) is **below the lower bound of normal (18 kgf)** with a Z-score of $-2.4$, indicating significant impairment.
- The patient has only **57.14%** of normal grip strength, which will impair activities of daily living (ADLs) such as opening jars, lifting utensils, and gripping a wheelchair push-rim.
- **Engineering recommendations:**
  - Prescribe a **hand-finger orthosis** with grip-assist features
  - Recommend **adaptive utensils** with built-up handles
  - Design a **wheelchair with power-assist push-rims** to reduce grip-load demand
  - Plan a progressive **grip-strengthening rehabilitation program** with periodic re-measurement against the normative baseline

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice, Model Paper]**

**(a) With suitable examples, explain how anthropometric normative values are used in designing a manual wheelchair. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Anthropometric normative values form the **primary input** for wheelchair dimensional design. A standard adult manual wheelchair is designed around the following key body dimensions, all obtained from population-based anthropometric studies:

1. **Hip Breadth (Seat Width) (2 marks):**
   - Indian adult male $P_{50}$ hip breadth $\approx 360$ mm
   - Design rule: $W_{seat} = W_{hip} + 2 \times 40$ mm clearance
   - $W_{seat} = 360 + 80 = 440$ mm (standard 17-inch wheelchair)

2. **Popliteal Length + Buttock Depth (Seat Depth) (2 marks):**
   - Adult popliteal-to-buttock length $\approx 430$ mm
   - Design rule: $D_{seat} = L_{pop-buttock} - 25$ to 50 mm to avoid popliteal pressure
   - Typical seat depth $\approx 400$ mm

3. **Popliteal Height (Seat Height) (1.5 marks):**
   - Adult popliteal height $\approx 450$ mm
   - Seat height is matched to this value so the footrest places the foot flat on the ground or pedal for self-propulsion.

4. **Backrest Height (1.5 marks):**
   - Determined by **scapular height** and required trunk support level.
   - For self-propelling users: backrest should be **below inferior scapula** (~250–300 mm above seat).
   - For users needing postural support: full backrest extending to shoulder level.

**Conclusion:** Anthropometric norms transform generic wheelchairs into **patient-specific mobility solutions**, ensuring pressure distribution, postural stability, and propulsive efficiency.

---

**(b) The 6-minute walk test (6MWT) normative distance for healthy adults aged 60–70 is 530 m with SD = 80 m. A 65-year-old patient walks 380 m. Calculate the Z-score and determine whether this patient is within the normal range. Suggest appropriate rehabilitation interventions. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given data:**
$$\mu = 530 \text{ m},\quad \sigma = 80 \text{ m},\quad x_p = 380 \text{ m}$$

**Step 1 — Z-score calculation (2 marks):**

$$Z = \dfrac{380 - 530}{80} = \dfrac{-150}{80} = -1.875$$

**Step 2 — Normal range determination (1 mark):**

$$X_{normal} = 530 \pm 2(80) = 370 \text{ to } 690 \text{ m}$$

The patient's value (380 m) lies **just within the lower boundary** of the normal range but very close to the cutoff.

**Step 3 — Percentage deviation (1 mark):**

$$\Delta\% = \dfrac{380 - 530}{530} \times 100\% = -28.30\%$$

**Step 4 — Rehabilitation interventions (3 marks):**
- The patient is **borderline impaired** with only 71.7% of expected walking capacity, suggesting reduced cardiopulmonary endurance.
- Recommended interventions:
  1. **Aerobic conditioning** — supervised treadmill walking, 20–30 min, 3–5 times/week at moderate intensity.
  2. **Strength training** — lower-limb resistance exercises (sit-to-stand, leg press) to improve propulsive capacity.
  3. **Use of assistive device if needed** — a single-point cane may improve endurance by off-loading 15–20% of body weight.
  4. **Periodic reassessment** — repeat 6MWT every 4–6 weeks and compare against the same normative reference to track recovery toward $\mu = 530$ m.
  5. **Lifestyle and home modification** — pacing strategies, energy conservation techniques, and removal of environmental barriers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Where You Lose Marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing percentile with percentage deviation** — $P_{5}$ is a *ranking*, not a "5% deviation." Mark loss: 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $N-1$ Bessel correction** in sample standard deviation. For normative data with finite samples, always use:
>    $$s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{N-1}}$$
> 3. **Applying mean ± 2 SD to non-Gaussian data** — strength data in mixed populations is often skewed. Use percentiles instead.
> 4. **Wrong unit consistency** — grip strength may be reported in **kgf, N, or lb**. Always state units and convert correctly.
> 5. **Missing the design-application link** — A question asking "engineering implications" requires a *device or intervention recommendation*, not just a numerical answer.
> 6. **Forgetting to state assumptions** — Always mention: "Assuming the data is normally distributed" before using mean ± 2 SD.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Normative Values (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Reference data from a healthy population, expressed as mean, SD, and percentiles.
- **Normal Range Rule:** $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ for Gaussian data; $P_{5}$–$P_{95}$ for non-Gaussian.
- **Z-Score Formula:** $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ — measures deviation in standard-deviation units.
- **Percentage Deviation:** $\Delta\% = \dfrac{x_{patient} - \mu_{norm}}{\mu_{norm}} \times 100\%$
- **Six Normative Categories:** Anthropometric, Strength, ROM, Cardiovascular, Sensory, Functional Mobility.
- **Key Adult Norms to Memorize:**
  - Grip strength — Male: **47 kgf**, Female: **28 kgf**
  - Gait speed — **1.30 m/s**
  - TUG — Normal **< 10 s**, fall risk $\geq$ **12 s**
  - Hip breadth (Indian male) — **360 mm**
  - Sitting height / stature — **0.52**
  - VO₂ max (young adult male) — **42 mL/kg/min**
- **Wheelchair Design Rule:** $W_{seat} = W_{hip} + 2 \times (30\text{–}50 \text{ mm})$
- **Standard Sampling Requirement:** $N \geq 100$ per stratum for stable norms.
- **Engineering Utility:** Device sizing, ergonomic limits, accessibility standards, outcome tracking.
- **Exam Tip:** Always (i) state assumptions, (ii) show full calculation, and (iii) link numerical result to a rehabilitation-engineering decision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Static Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Stability (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Static stability* of an assistive device (wheelchair, walker, prosthesis, or orthosis) is defined as the **ability of the device to remain in a stable, upright equilibrium position when subjected to a static (non-accelerating) load or a slow, quasi-static disturbance**, without tipping over, sliding, or collapsing, when the device is in a stationary configuration (i.e., velocity and acceleration are zero).

In simpler KTU-board terms: **Static stability = the resistance of a stationary rehabilitation device to tipping or loss of balance when forces are slowly applied to it.**

Static stability is fundamentally different from *dynamic stability*, which concerns the device in motion (e.g., a wheelchair rolling over uneven terrain, a prosthetic limb during gait). For the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2), the focus is on the **static** condition.

### 1.1.1 Two Sub-Categories of Static Stability

| Sub-Category | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward Static Stability** | Resistance to forward tipping (nose-dive) | Critical for wheelchairs on downhill slopes |
| **Rearward Static Stability** | Resistance to backward tipping (back-over) | Critical when a wheelchair is pushed up a curb |
| **Lateral (Sideways) Static Stability** | Resistance to sideways tipping | Critical on lateral inclines; affected by wheel-track width |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Examiners frequently test the three directions of static stability using a free-body diagram of a wheelchair on an inclined plane. Memorize the **tipping axis concept**.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Static Stability

Any rehabilitation device in static equilibrium derives its stability from three interlinked mechanical concepts:

1. **Center of Gravity (CoG / G):** The point where the entire weight $W$ of the device-plus-user system is considered to act vertically downward.
2. **Base of Support (BoS):** The polygonal area enclosed by the points of contact of the device with the ground (e.g., the rectangle formed by the four wheel contact points of a wheelchair).
3. **Line of Gravity (LoG):** The imaginary vertical line passing downward through the CoG.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Static Stability (Universally tested in KTU):**
> A body is in *stable static equilibrium* if and only if the **Line of Gravity (LoG) falls inside the Base of Support (BoS)**. If the LoG exits the BoS, the device **tips over** the boundary edge of the BoS, called the **tipping axis**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pencil Test"

Imagine balancing a sharpened pencil on its tip versus laying it flat on a table:

- **Pencil on tip:** The CoG is high, and the BoS is a single point (the tip). The LoG is *barely* inside the BoS. Even a tiny breath of wind pushes the LoG outside → **unstable**.
- **Pencil lying flat:** The CoG is low, and the BoS is the long flat side. The LoG is comfortably inside the BoS → **highly stable**.

**Application to wheelchairs:** A wheelchair with its occupant leaning far forward has the CoG shifted anteriorly, pushing the LoG toward the front wheel axis → reduced forward stability. Rearranging the rear wheels **forward** shortens the wheelbase and **decreases** rearward stability but improves maneuverability.

## 1.4 Why Static Stability Matters in Rehabilitation Engineering

- **Safety:** Wheelchair tip-overs are a leading cause of injury among spinal-cord-injured users.
- **Standardization:** International standards (ISO 7176-1, RESNA WC-1) prescribe static stability tests for manual and powered wheelchairs.
- **Prescription:** A clinician must match the wheelchair's static stability to the user's cognition, postural control, and environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (KTU examiners love these):**
> - Typical **wheelchair tip angle on flat ground**: 20°–30° (forward), 15°–25° (rearward), 18°–24° (lateral).
> - **User CoG height** (seated, adult): approximately **0.9 m to 1.0 m** above the seat pan.
> - **Stability margin** is measured in **Newton-metres (N·m)** as the *restoring moment* about the tipping axis.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanical Model of a Wheelchair in Static Equilibrium

For KTU 2024 analytical problems, model the wheelchair-plus-user as a **single rigid body** with:

- Total weight $W$ (in Newtons) acting at the combined CoG, point $G$.
- A rectangular base of support with four contact points: front-left caster, front-right caster, rear-left wheel, rear-right wheel.
- The CoG is located at height $h$ above the ground and at horizontal position $x_G$ measured from a reference axis (usually the rear axle).

### 2.1.1 The Tipping Axis Concept

A *tipping axis* is a straight line on the ground about which the device would rotate if it began to tip. The three classical tipping axes for a wheelchair are:

1. **Front tipping axis:** The line connecting the two front caster contact points.
2. **Rear tipping axis:** The line connecting the two rear wheel contact points.
3. **Lateral tipping axis (left or right):** A line connecting the front and rear contact points on the same side of the chair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The LoG-Tipping-Axis Rule:**
> The wheelchair will begin to tip about a particular axis when the **Line of Gravity** passes *exactly through* that axis. If the LoG moves past the axis (i.e., outside the BoS bounded by that axis), the device **tips**.

## 2.2 Static Stability on a Flat Horizontal Surface

On a flat surface, static stability is governed purely by the **horizontal position of the CoG** relative to the tipping axis.

### 2.2.1 Forward Static Stability (Anti-Nose-Dive)

The **forward restoring moment** about the front wheel axis is:

$$M_{F} = W \cdot d_{F}$$

where $d_{F}$ is the **horizontal distance from the front tipping axis to the vertically-projected CoG** (i.e., the perpendicular distance from the front axis to the line of gravity).

- $M_{F} > 0$ → Stable (the front wheel contact prevents forward tipping).
- $M_{F} = 0$ → On the verge of forward tipping.
- $M_{F} < 0$ → The wheelchair tips forward.

### 2.2.2 Rearward Static Stability (Anti-Back-Over)

$$M_{R} = W \cdot d_{R}$$

where $d_{R}$ is the horizontal distance from the **rear tipping axis** to the LoG.

- $M_{R} > 0$ → Stable rearward.
- $M_{R} = 0$ → Verge of rearward tip.

### 2.2.3 Lateral Static Stability

$$M_{L} = W \cdot d_{L}$$

where $d_{L}$ is the horizontal distance from the **lateral tipping axis** to the LoG (measured sideways, half the wheel-track).

## 2.3 Static Stability on an Inclined Surface — The Critical Tip Angle

This is the **most heavily tested derivation** in KTU ESE for Module 2. The wheelchair is placed on a slope inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. The slope tilts the LoG relative to the ground.

The **critical tip angle** $\theta_{c}$ is the slope angle at which the LoG just passes through the tipping axis and the device is on the verge of tipping.

### 2.3.1 Forward Critical Tip Angle

For tipping about the front wheel axis on an upward slope:

$$\tan(\theta_{c,F}) = \frac{d_{F}}{h}$$

where:
- $d_{F}$ = horizontal distance from front axis to CoG projection (on flat ground).
- $h$ = height of CoG above the ground.

### 2.3.2 Rearward Critical Tip Angle

For tipping about the rear wheel axis on an upward slope (i.e., the chair is facing downhill):

$$\tan(\theta_{c,R}) = \frac{d_{R}}{h}$$

### 2.3.3 Lateral Critical Tip Angle

$$\tan(\theta_{c,L}) = \frac{d_{L}}{h} = \frac{T/2}{h}$$

where $T$ is the **wheel track** (lateral distance between the two wheels on the same axle line).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> Notice that $\theta_{c}$ is **inversely proportional to $h$** and **directly proportional to the base distance**. Thus:
> - Lowering the user's CoG (e.g., reclining the back, using a lower cushion) **increases** all three tip angles → safer chair.
> - Widening the wheelbase or wheel track → **increases** tip angles.

## 2.4 Stability Margin (Indoor vs. Outdoor)

The **stability margin** is defined as the *additional moment* the wheelchair can resist before tipping, often expressed as a percentage of the total weight moment or as an extra angle.

$$\text{Stability Margin (angle)} = \theta_{c} - \theta_{operating}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = W \cdot d$ | Restoring moment about tipping axis | $W$=weight, $d$=horiz. distance from axis to LoG | N·m |
| 2 | $\tan(\theta_{c}) = d/h$ | Critical tip angle (any direction) | $d$=horizontal base distance, $h$=CoG height | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\theta_{c,L} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{T/2}{h}\right)$ | Lateral tip angle | $T$=wheel track, $h$=CoG height | radians or degrees |
| 4 | $W_{max} = M_{critical}/d$ | Max load before tip | — | N |
| 5 | $F_{anti\text{-}tip} = W \cdot \dfrac{d_{F}}{L_{anti}}$ | Force in anti-tip lever | $L_{anti}$=lever arm to anti-tip wheel | N |
| 6 | $h_{eff} = h - \Delta h \cos\phi$ | Effective CoG height after recline $\phi$ | $\phi$=recline angle | m |
| 7 | $\theta_{c,combined} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{d_F^{2} + d_L^{2}}}{h}\right)$ | Combined diagonal tip angle | $d_F$, $d_L$ | degrees |

> **Notation Note:** $\arctan$ is also denoted $\tan^{-1}$. All angles in the formulas are in **radians** when used in calculus, but KTU typically expects **degrees** in final numerical answers.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Wheelchair Prescription:** A clinician prescribing a chair for an active user (e.g., a young paraplegic) prioritizes **rearward stability** (to prevent back-overs during quick push-strokes), possibly at the cost of maneuverability.
2. **Anti-Tip Devices:** Small rear caster wheels (anti-tippers) that deploy behind the rear wheels *artificially extend the BoS rearward*, increasing $d_R$ and thus $\theta_{c,R}$.
3. **Prosthetic Standing Stability:** For above-knee prostheses with a locked knee, the CoG must be positioned *anterior* to the knee axis for static standing stability.
4. **Walker Design:** Standard vs. rollator walkers have different static stability profiles; rollators often include parking brakes to lock the wheels during static standing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Forward Tip Angle on an Inclined Plane

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
A wheelchair with combined CoG located at height $h$ above the ground and horizontal distance $d_F$ from the front wheel axis (measured rearward). The chair is placed on a slope of angle $\theta$ inclined upward in the forward direction. Derive the critical tip angle $\theta_{c,F}$.

### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram
Draw the wheelchair as a rigid body. The only external forces are:
- Weight $W$ acting vertically downward at $G$.
- Normal reactions at all four wheels (only the front wheels are critical just before tipping).

### Step 2 — Geometry of the LoG
On an inclined plane, the ground plane is tilted by angle $\theta$ with respect to the horizontal. The LoG, however, remains **vertical** (gravity always acts vertically). Thus the LoG makes an angle $\theta$ with the *normal* to the inclined ground.

In the body-fixed frame (rotated with the slope), the LoG can be decomposed. The horizontal distance (along the slope) from the CoG to the front axis is $d_F$, and the perpendicular height of $G$ above the slope is $h$.

### Step 3 — Take Moments About the Front Tipping Axis
At the verge of tipping, the rear wheels lose contact, and the entire weight $W$ is supported at the front axis. The moment of $W$ about the front axis determines stability.

The perpendicular distance from the front axis to the LoG in the inclined configuration is:

$$d_{perp} = d_F \cos\theta - h \sin\theta$$

> **Sign convention:** Positive when the LoG is rearward of the front axis (stable); negative when the LoG is forward of the front axis (tipping).

### Step 4 — Set the Critical Condition
At the critical tip angle, the perpendicular distance becomes zero (the LoG passes through the front axis):

$$d_F \cos\theta_c - h \sin\theta_c = 0$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $\theta_c$

$$d_F \cos\theta_c = h \sin\theta_c$$

$$\frac{\sin\theta_c}{\cos\theta_c} = \frac{d_F}{h}$$

$$\tan\theta_c = \frac{d_F}{h}$$

### Step 6 — Final Expression

$$\boxed{\theta_{c,F} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{d_F}{h}\right)}$$

**KTU Numerical Example:**

A wheelchair user has CoG at $h = 0.95$ m and $d_F = 0.40$ m. Find the critical forward tip angle.

$$\theta_{c,F} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.40}{0.95}\right) = \arctan(0.4211) = 22.85°$$

The chair is safe on slopes up to ~22.85°.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lateral Critical Tip Angle and the Role of Wheel Track

**Problem Setup:**
A wheelchair has wheel track $T = 0.60$ m (distance between the centers of the two rear wheels). Combined CoG height $h = 1.00$ m. The CoG is centered laterally. Find the critical lateral tip angle.

### Step 1 — Geometry
For a laterally symmetric chair, the LoG passes through the centerline of the chair. The lateral tipping axis is the line connecting the right-side front and rear wheel contact points. The horizontal distance from this axis to the LoG is $d_L = T/2 = 0.30$ m.

### Step 2 — Apply the General Tip-Angle Formula

$$\theta_{c,L} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{d_L}{h}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{T/2}{h}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Values

$$\theta_{c,L} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.30}{1.00}\right) = \arctan(0.3000) = 16.70°$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion
The chair will tip laterally on a side-slope steeper than **16.7°**. This is the most vulnerable direction in many wheelchairs, often lower than the forward or rearward tip angles.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effect of Rear-Wheel Axle Position on Rearward Stability

**Problem Setup (a classic KTU 14-mark question):**
A standard adult manual wheelchair has a wheelbase $L$ (distance between front and rear axles). The rear wheels can be mounted in two positions:
- **Position A (rearward):** Rear axle at the very back of the frame. $d_R = 0.15$ m.
- **Position B (forward):** Rear axle moved forward by 0.10 m, so $d_R = 0.05$ m.

The CoG height is $h = 1.00$ m. Compare the rearward critical tip angles for both positions.

### Step 1 — Apply the Rearward Tip Formula

$$\theta_{c,R} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{d_R}{h}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Compute for Position A

$$\theta_{c,R,A} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.15}{1.00}\right) = \arctan(0.15) = 8.53°$$

### Step 3 — Compute for Position B

$$\theta_{c,R,B} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.05}{1.00}\right) = \arctan(0.05) = 2.86°$$

### Step 4 — Comparison

$$\Delta\theta = \theta_{c,R,A} - \theta_{c,R,B} = 8.53° - 2.86° = 5.67°$$

**Engineering Insight (write this in the KTU exam for full marks):**
- Position B (forward axle) **decreases rearward stability** by ~5.7°, making back-overs much more likely.
- However, Position B **improves maneuverability** (smaller turning circle) and **lightens the front** for the user.
- For active users, this is a deliberate **stability-maneuverability trade-off**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Anti-Tip Caster Effect on Rearward Stability

**Problem Setup:**
Add an anti-tip wheel behind the rear axle, at distance $L_{anti} = 0.12$ m rearward. The chair is in static equilibrium on flat ground. The anti-tip wheel carries a normal reaction $N_{anti}$. Derive the relation between $N_{anti}$ and the CoG position.

### Step 1 — Free Body
- Total weight $W$ acts at $G$, distance $d_R$ from the rear axle (i.e., $d_R$ forward of the rear axle, since the LoG falls inside the BoS).
- Normal reaction at rear axle: $N_{rear}$ (upward).
- Normal reaction at front wheels: $N_{front}$ (upward).
- Normal reaction at anti-tip wheel: $N_{anti}$ (upward).

### Step 2 — Vertical Force Equilibrium

$$N_{front} + N_{rear} + N_{anti} = W$$

### Step 3 — Moment Equilibrium About the Rear Axle
Taking counterclockwise as positive, with the front wheels at distance $L$ forward and the anti-tip wheel at distance $L_{anti}$ rearward:

$$N_{front} \cdot L - W \cdot d_R - N_{anti} \cdot L_{anti} = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $N_{anti}$

$$N_{anti} = \frac{N_{front} \cdot L - W \cdot d_R}{L_{anti}}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** When the user shifts rearward (e.g., reclining), $d_R$ increases, $N_{front} \cdot L$ may decrease, and the anti-tip wheel must **carry more load** to prevent rearward tipping. If $N_{anti}$ becomes zero, the chair is at the verge of rearward tip.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Direction-wise Summary

| Direction | Tipping Axis | LoG Distance $d$ | Critical Angle $\theta_c$ | Risk Aggravators |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward** | Front wheel contact line | $d_F$ (CoG behind front axis) | $\arctan(d_F/h)$ | Forward-leaning user, downhill slope, heavy backpack on knees |
| **Rearward** | Rear wheel contact line | $d_R$ (CoG in front of rear axis) | $\arctan(d_R/h)$ | Reclined user, uphill slope, rearward axle position, hard push on handles |
| **Lateral (Left)** | Left-side wheel contact line | $T/2$ | $\arctan((T/2)/h)$ | Side-slope, narrow wheel track, tall user, sudden lateral lean |
| **Lateral (Right)** | Right-side wheel contact line | $T/2$ | $\arctan((T/2)/h)$ | Same as above (mirrored) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Static Stability Analysis Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram maps the logical workflow a KTU student should follow when solving a static stability problem on a wheelchair.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Wheelchair Configuration] --> B[Define Coordinate System]
    B --> C[Locate Combined CoG Point G]
    C --> D[Determine Base of Support BoS]
    D --> E{Identify Tipping Axis of Interest}
    E --> F1[Forward Tipping Axis]
    E --> F2[Rearward Tipping Axis]
    E --> F3[Lateral Tipping Axis]
    F1 --> G1[Measure dF and h]
    F2 --> G2[Measure dR and h]
    F3 --> G3[Measure T/2 and h]
    G1 --> H1[Compute theta cF using arctan dF over h]
    G2 --> H2[Compute theta cR using arctan dR over h]
    G3 --> H3[Compute theta cL using arctan T over 2h]
    H1 --> I[Compare theta c with operating slope theta]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J{Is theta less than theta c}
    J -->|Yes| K[STABLE - Return Stability Margin]
    J -->|No| L[UNSTABLE - Recommend Modifications]
    L --> M1[Lower CoG]
    L --> M2[Widen Wheelbase]
    L --> M3[Add Anti-Tip Casters]
    L --> M4[Shift Axle Position]
```

## 4.2 Free-Body Schematic: Wheelchair on a Side-Slope (Lateral Static Stability)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Slope["Inclined Ground - Tilted by theta"]
        FL[Front-Left Wheel Contact]
        FR[Front-Right Wheel Contact]
        RL[Rear-Left Wheel Contact]
        RR[Rear-Right Wheel Contact]
        AT[Lateral Tipping Axis Left Side]
    end
    G[CoG - Point G at height h]
    LOG[Line of Gravity - Vertical through G]
    W[Weight W acts downward at G]
    G --> W
    G --> LOG
    LOG -.->|Perpendicular distance dL| AT
    AT --> Result{LoG inside BoS}
    Result -->|Stable| SM[Stability Margin: theta cL minus theta]
    Result -->|Tipping| T[TIP OVER - Restore Balance]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix Diagram: Factors Influencing Static Stability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Static Stability of Wheelchair]
    Root --> P1[User Posture]
    Root --> P2[Wheelchair Geometry]
    Root --> P3[Environmental Slope]
    Root --> P4[Anti-Tip Devices]

    P1 --> P1a[Upright: Standard CoG Height]
    P1 --> P1b[Reclined: Lower CoG, Shifted Rearward]
    P1 --> P1c[Forward Lean: Higher Effective CoG, Forward Shifted]

    P2 --> P2a[Wheelbase Length L]
    P2 --> P2b[Wheel Track T]
    P2 --> P2c[Rear Axle Position]

    P3 --> P3a[Uphill Slope]
    P3 --> P3b[Downhill Slope]
    P3 --> P3c[Lateral Side-Slope]

    P4 --> P4a[Rear Anti-Tip Casters]
    P4 --> P4b[Front Anti-Tip Bumper]
    P4 --> P4c[Wheel Locks / Parking Brakes]
```

## 4.4 Geometry Diagram (Textual Schematic) — Inclined Plane Analysis

```
                     (Vertical LoG)
                          |
                          |
                          G ......... CoG at height h above ground
                         /|
                        / |
                       /  |
                      /   |  h (CoG height)
                     /    |
                    /     |
                   /θ_c   |
                  /       |
        (Slope) /_________|________
                |    d_F  |        |
        ----[FRONT AXIS]--|-------|
                          |
                  Ground tilted by θ
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The slope is tilted by $\theta$ to the horizontal.
- The LoG is *vertical* (gravity is always vertical).
- At the critical tip angle $\theta_c$, the LoG just grazes the front axis.
- The geometry yields $\tan\theta_c = d_F / h$.

---
