# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define static stability of a wheelchair. List the three principal directions in which it is evaluated.**

**Model Answer:**
Static stability of a wheelchair is the ability of the wheelchair-plus-user system to remain in equilibrium (without tipping) when subjected to static or slowly-applied loads while in a stationary configuration.

The three principal directions are:
1. **Forward static stability** — resistance to forward tipping about the front wheel axis.
2. **Rearward static stability** — resistance to backward tipping about the rear wheel axis.
3. **Lateral static stability** — resistance to sideways tipping about the wheel contact line on one side.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the condition for a rehabilitation device to be in stable static equilibrium.**

**Model Answer:**
A rehabilitation device is in stable static equilibrium when the **Line of Gravity (LoG)**—the vertical line passing through the device's Center of Gravity (CoG)—**falls within the Base of Support (BoS)** of the device. If the LoG exits the BoS (passes beyond a tipping axis), the device will tip over.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, derive the expression for the **critical forward static tip angle** of a wheelchair on an inclined surface in terms of the CoG height $h$ and the horizontal distance $d_F$ of the CoG from the front wheel axis.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wheelchair user has a combined center of gravity located at a height of 0.92 m above the ground and 0.42 m behind the front wheel axis. Calculate:
1. The critical forward tip angle.
2. The maximum slope (in degrees) on which the wheelchair can be safely parked.
3. Comment on the safety of the same wheelchair if the user is fitted with a **reclined backrest at 20°** that lowers the effective CoG height by 0.08 m (assume $d_F$ remains unchanged).

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Refer to the derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. Key steps:

1. **Free-body setup:** Wheelchair on a slope inclined at $\theta$. Take the front wheel axis as the pivot.
2. **Moment equation about the front axis:** The vertical line of gravity, perpendicular distance to the front axis is $d_F \cos\theta - h \sin\theta$.
3. **Critical condition:** Set the moment to zero (verge of tipping):

   $$d_F \cos\theta_c - h \sin\theta_c = 0$$

4. **Solve:**

   $$\tan\theta_c = \frac{d_F}{h} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{\theta_c = \arctan\!\left(\frac{d_F}{h}\right)}$$

*(Include a sketch of the inclined wheelchair showing the LoG, CoG point G, slope angle $\theta$, distance $d_F$, and height $h$.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(1) Critical forward tip angle (upright user):**

$$\theta_c = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.42}{0.92}\right) = \arctan(0.4565) = 24.53°$$

**(2) Maximum safe slope:** Same as $\theta_c = 24.53°$ (any slope up to this is safe with zero margin; for practical safety a margin of 3°–5° is recommended).

**(3) With reclined backrest:**
New CoG height: $h' = 0.92 - 0.08 = 0.84$ m.

$$\theta_c' = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.42}{0.84}\right) = \arctan(0.5000) = 26.57°$$

**Comment:** The reclined configuration **increases** the critical forward tip angle from 24.53° to 26.57° (an improvement of ~2.04°). Hence, the reclined backrest **enhances** forward static stability. However, it will *decrease* rearward stability (since the CoG moves rearward). A complete analysis requires checking rearward tip angle as well.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **tipping axis concept** and the **Line of Gravity (LoG) criterion** for static stability of rehabilitation devices. Illustrate with a free-body diagram of a wheelchair on a side-slope.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wheelchair has a **wheel track of 0.58 m** and a combined CoG height of **1.05 m** (centered laterally). Compute:
1. The critical lateral tip angle.
2. The wheelchair is to be used on a 12° lateral side-slope ramp. Calculate the **stability margin** in degrees.
3. If the user is shifted laterally by 0.05 m toward the right (due to poor posture), recompute the lateral tip angle and comment.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Concept Explanation:**

The **tipping axis** is the boundary edge of the base of support about which a device rotates when it loses balance. For a wheelchair, the three primary tipping axes are the front wheel line, rear wheel line, and the lateral wheel contact lines.

The **Line of Gravity (LoG) criterion** states: a device is in stable static equilibrium as long as the vertical line passing through its CoG lies *inside* the base of support. If the LoG crosses a tipping axis (exits the BoS), the device begins to rotate about that axis.

*(Draw a wheelchair on a tilted ground, mark the lateral tipping axis, the LoG vertical, the CoG point G, the wheel track T, and the height h. Show the angle θ between the slope and horizontal.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(1) Critical lateral tip angle (centered user):**

$$\theta_{c,L} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{T/2}{h}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.29}{1.05}\right) = \arctan(0.2762) = 15.45°$$

**(2) Stability margin on 12° side-slope:**

$$\text{Margin} = \theta_{c,L} - \theta_{operating} = 15.45° - 12.00° = 3.45°$$

The wheelchair has a positive margin of 3.45°, hence it is **safe** on a 12° side-slope.

**(3) With user shifted 0.05 m to the right:**

The right-side tipping axis is now closer to the CoG, while the left side is farther:

- Distance to right axis: $d_L^{right} = T/2 - 0.05 = 0.29 - 0.05 = 0.24$ m
- Distance to left axis: $d_L^{left} = T/2 + 0.05 = 0.29 + 0.05 = 0.34$ m

The wheelchair will tip about the **right** axis (the closer one) first:

$$\theta_{c,L}^{right} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.24}{1.05}\right) = \arctan(0.2286) = 12.88°$$

**Comment:** The critical lateral tip angle has **decreased** from 15.45° to 12.88° due to the lateral CoG shift. On the 12° side-slope, the new margin is only **0.88°** — the wheelchair is now on the verge of tipping. This illustrates the importance of **symmetric seating and postural support** in wheelchair prescription.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** CoG heights are given in cm in some textbook problems — always convert to meters before using in $\tan\theta = d/h$.
> 2. **Confusing the CoG location with the LoG:** The CoG is a *point* on the body; the LoG is a *vertical line* through that point. The "distance to the tipping axis" is measured from the *line*, not the *point*, horizontally.
> 3. **Ignoring the rearward stability when reclining:** Reclining the backrest lowers the CoG and increases *forward* stability but reduces *rearward* stability. Always check both directions.
> 4. **Mixing up wheelbase and wheel track:** Wheelbase $L$ = front-to-rear distance; wheel track $T$ = left-to-right distance. Using one in place of the other is a classic KTU trap.
> 5. **Not sketching a free-body diagram:** A clear FBD is worth 2–3 marks in a 7-mark sub-question. Always draw one.
> 6. **Quoting tip angle without units:** Always write "°" or "degrees" — bare numbers are penalized.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Static Stability (Module 2):**

- **Definition:** Ability of a stationary assistive device to resist tipping under static load.
- **Three pillars:** Center of Gravity (CoG), Base of Support (BoS), Line of Gravity (LoG).
- **Golden Rule:** LoG must lie *inside* the BoS for stable equilibrium.
- **Tipping axis:** The boundary edge of the BoS about which the device rotates.
- **Three directions:** Forward, rearward, lateral.
- **Master Formula:** $\theta_c = \arctan(d/h)$ — applies to all three directions with appropriate $d$ and $h$.
- **Forward $d$:** $d_F$ = horizontal distance from front wheel axis to LoG.
- **Rearward $d$:** $d_R$ = horizontal distance from rear wheel axis to LoG.
- **Lateral $d$:** $d_L = T/2$ = half the wheel track (for centered CoG).
- **Restoring moment:** $M = W \cdot d$.
- **Stability margin:** $\theta_c - \theta_{operating}$.
- **Lowering the CoG** (recline, lower cushion) **increases** all three tip angles → safer.
- **Widening the wheelbase/track** **increases** tip angles → safer but less maneuverable.
- **Anti-tip casters** extend the BoS rearward, increasing rearward tip angle.
- **Lateral tip angle** is often the *smallest* of the three — most vulnerable direction.
- **Standards:** ISO 7176-1 and RESNA WC-1 prescribe static stability tests for wheelchairs.
- **Common user CoG height** (seated): ~0.9 m – 1.0 m.
- **Typical chair tip angles:** 20°–30° (F), 15°–25° (R), 18°–24° (L).
- **Always draw a Free-Body Diagram** in the exam before computing.
- **Always specify units** for angle (°) and distance (m).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometric Approach to Static Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Geometric Approach to Static Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Stability (Geometric Definition):** Static stability is the property of a body (human body, wheelchair, prosthesis, or assistive device) to remain in equilibrium under the action of gravity alone, without undergoing translational or rotational motion. In the **geometric approach**, stability is evaluated purely from the spatial relationship between the **Center of Gravity (COG)** and the **Base of Support (BOS)**, without invoking inertial or dynamic (time-dependent) terms.

Mathematically, a rigid body is in static equilibrium if and only if:

$$\sum \vec{F} = 0 \quad \text{(Translational Equilibrium)} \quad \text{AND} \quad \sum \vec{M} = 0 \quad \text{(Rotational Equilibrium)}$$

The **geometric approach** simplifies this by replacing the distributed force analysis with a single equivalent **weight vector** $W = mg$ acting vertically downward through the COG, and checking whether this vector's line of action (the **gravity line**) falls inside the polygon defining the BOS.

### 1.2 The Gravity Line Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Gravity Line (Line of Gravity / Plumb Line):** An imaginary vertical line passing through the body's Center of Gravity, directed straight downward toward the earth's center. It is the geometric locus of the weight vector $W = mg$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!TIP]
> **The "Tower of Blocks" Analogy:** Imagine stacking a tower of wooden blocks on a table. The tower stays upright as long as the vertical line passing through the center of the topmost block (the COG) lands somewhere on the table surface (the BOS). Now tilt the table slightly — the line shifts. The instant that vertical line crosses the edge of the table, the tower tips. *That* edge-crossing is precisely the geometric failure of stability.

Applied to **rehabilitation engineering**:
- A **wheelchair user** on a ramp → the gravity line (which always remains vertical) shifts backward or forward as the ramp tilts. The wheelchair tips the moment the gravity line leaves the wheel polygon.
- A **standing patient with a prosthetic limb** → the gravity line must remain inside the **footprint of the feet** (the BOS). If it crosses the toe-line, the patient falls forward.
- A **child with cerebral palsy using a seating system** → the geometric approach helps orthotists design seat tilt, back angle, and footrest height so the gravity line always falls through the **stability triangle** of the pelvis.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Value (Human) |
|-----------|----------------|---------|----------------------|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | m/s² | **9.81** |
| Human COG height (anatomical) | $H$ | m | 0.55 × Stature (≈ 1.0 m adult) |
| Wheelchair stability threshold angle | $\theta_{crit}$ | degrees | **10° – 15°** (rear), **15° – 20°** (forward) |
| Safety factor (medical devices) | $SF$ | dimensionless | **1.5 – 2.0** |
| ISO 7176 stability test ramp angle | — | degrees | 3°, 6°, 10° |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Geometric Elements

The geometric approach rests on three primary spatial entities:

1. **Center of Gravity (G or COG):** The point where the entire body weight $W = mg$ is assumed to act.
2. **Base of Support (BOS):** The convex polygon formed by the contact area between the body/device and the supporting surface.
3. **Gravity Line (GL):** The vertical line passing through the COG.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Stability Rule:** A body is statically stable if and only if the gravity line intersects the Base of Support. If the gravity line passes outside the BOS boundary, the body **tips** about the nearest edge of the BOS.

### 2.2 Three Classes of Geometric Stability

The geometric approach classifies static stability into three primary directions relative to the human body:

| Class | Direction of Potential Tipping | Axis of Rotation | BOS Edge Involved |
|-------|-------------------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Antero-Posterior (Sagittal)** | Forward / Backward | Medial-Lateral (M-L) axis | Toe-line / Heel-line |
| **Medio-Lateral (Coronal)** | Left / Right | Anterior-Posterior (A-P) axis | Lateral edges of feet / wheels |
| **Diagonal (Multi-axis)** | Combined | Oblique axis | Corner of BOS |

### 2.3 Stability Indices & Their Geometric Meaning

#### 2.3.1 Stability Index (SI)

The **Stability Index** quantifies *how far* the gravity line is from the tipping edge, normalized by the COG height. A larger SI means a more stable configuration.

$$SI = \frac{d}{H}$$

where $d$ = perpendicular distance from COG to the tipping edge, $H$ = height of COG from the support surface.

#### 2.3.2 Static Stability Angle (Tipping Angle)

The **static stability angle** $\theta_s$ is the maximum tilt angle of the support surface (relative to true horizontal) at which the gravity line just reaches the boundary of the BOS:

$$\theta_s = \arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{H}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The greater the horizontal distance $d$ from the COG to the BOS edge, the larger the stability angle. A wheelchair with **wheels set wider apart** has a larger lateral stability angle. A person bending their knees and lowering their COG increases $\theta_s$ and dramatically improves stability — a key principle taught to amputees and elderly patients.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities are in SI units. Angles are in radians (rad) unless otherwise stated.**

| # | Formula | Description | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $W = m g$ | Body weight from mass | Force/weight calculations |
| 2 | $\sum F_x = 0,\ \sum F_y = 0$ | Translational equilibrium | Verifying no sliding |
| 3 | $\sum M_O = 0$ | Rotational equilibrium about point O | Verifying no tipping |
| 4 | $M_{tip} = W \cdot d_{tip}$ | Tipping moment about BOS edge | Stability analysis |
| 5 | $M_{rest} = W \cdot d_{COG}$ | Restoring moment from COG offset | Anti-tip calculation |
| 6 | $\theta_s = \arctan(d / H)$ | Static stability (tipping) angle | Wheelchair ramp safety |
| 7 | $SF = M_{rest} / M_{tip}$ | Safety factor against tipping | ISO 7176 / FDA bench tests |
| 8 | $G(x,y) = \frac{\sum m_i x_i}{\sum m_i}$ | COG location (segment method) | Biomechanics modeling |

> **Critical Reminder (Markdown Safety):** The vertical bar $\vert$ is rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking the table. Example: the COG offset is denoted $d \vert_{\text{COG}}$ rather than using unescaped pipes.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Geometric Approach in Rehabilitation

- **Powered Wheelchair Design:** Engineers use geometric stability analysis to set wheelbase length, wheel diameter, and battery placement to satisfy ISO 7176-1 stability tests on inclines up to **10°**.
- **Lower-Limb Prosthesis Tuning:** Prosthetists adjust the socket alignment to keep the ground reaction force vector close to the user's BOS center, especially during single-leg stance.
- **Seating & Postural Systems:** For children with neuromotor disabilities (CP, SMA), clinicians specify seat depth, back recline, and footrest height using geometric COG-BOS principles.
- **Standing Frames & Gait Trainers:** Geometric analysis ensures the patient does not tip forward when leaning over a walker or parallel bar.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Static Stability Angle from COG and BOS Geometry

**Statement:** A body of mass $m$ rests on an inclined surface. Its COG lies at height $H$ above the support, and at a perpendicular distance $d$ from the rear (downhill) tipping edge. Derive the static stability angle $\theta_s$.

**Setup:** Consider a 2D vertical plane containing the COG, the support point (foot), and the rear tipping edge. The body weight $W = mg$ acts vertically downward. The normal to the support surface makes angle $\theta$ with the vertical.

**Step 1 — Establish the geometry:**

Place a coordinate system with origin at the tipping edge $E$. The COG $G$ is at coordinates $(d, H)$ when the surface is horizontal. When the surface is tilted by angle $\theta$ (rotated about $E$), the body rotates with it, so $G$ moves to a new position relative to the true-vertical gravity direction.

The line of gravity is **always vertical** in the world frame. The body-fixed point $G$ in the world frame after rotation by $\theta$ about $E$ becomes:

$$G' = \begin{pmatrix} d \cos\theta - H \sin\theta \\ d \sin\theta + H \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Tipping condition:**

The body is on the verge of tipping when the gravity line (which is the vertical through $G'$) just passes through $E$. Equivalently, the **horizontal coordinate of $G'$** in the world frame equals **zero**:

$$d \cos\theta_s - H \sin\theta_s = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\theta_s$:**

Rearranging:

$$d \cos\theta_s = H \sin\theta_s$$

$$\tan\theta_s = \frac{d}{H}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_s = \arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{H}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**

If $\theta_s = 15°$ and the body is on a $12°$ ramp, the system is stable (positive margin). If the ramp is at $18°$, the body tips.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Moment-Based Stability Criterion

**Statement:** Show that geometric stability (gravity line inside BOS) is mathematically equivalent to a positive net restoring moment.

**Step 1 — Define moments about the tipping edge $E$:**

Let $d_{\text{COG}}$ be the perpendicular horizontal distance from the gravity line to $E$, and $H$ be the height of the COG above $E$.

The weight $W$ produces a **tipping moment** $M_{tip}$ when the gravity line is on the "wrong" side of $E$ and a **restoring moment** $M_{rest}$ when on the "right" side.

**Step 2 — Express the moment in scalar form:**

$$M_{\text{net}} = W \cdot d_{\text{COG}}$$

where $d_{\text{COG}}$ carries a sign (positive = restoring, negative = tipping).

**Step 3 — Equivalence with the geometric rule:**

The sign of $d_{\text{COG}}$ depends on which side of $E$ the vertical line through $G$ lies. Therefore:

$$\text{Stable} \iff M_{\text{net}} > 0 \iff \text{gravity line lies inside BOS}$$

**Step 4 — Safety factor:**

$$SF = \frac{M_{rest}}{M_{tip}} = \frac{W \cdot d_{rest}}{W \cdot d_{tip}} = \frac{d_{rest}}{d_{tip}}$$

For rehabilitation devices, **$SF \geq 1.5$** is the conventional design target.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Stability Triangle for a Wheelchair (Three-Wheeled Configuration)

**Statement:** For a manual wheelchair with two large rear wheels and one small front caster, the BOS is a triangle with vertices at the three wheel contact points. Compute the minimum lateral stability angle.

**Step 1 — Set up coordinates:**

Place the front caster contact at $C_F = (L, 0)$ and the two rear wheel contacts at $C_{R1} = (0, -W/2)$ and $C_{R2} = (0, +W/2)$, where $L$ is the wheelbase length and $W$ is the rear track width.

**Step 2 — Identify the lateral tipping edge:**

Lateral tipping occurs about the line joining the two rear wheel contact points. The COG of the wheelchair-plus-user combined system lies at height $H$ above the ground, with lateral offset $y_G$ from the centerline.

**Step 3 — Compute lateral stability angle:**

The lateral tipping angle $\theta_{\text{lat}}$ is reached when:

$$\tan\theta_{\text{lat}} = \frac{W/2 - \vert y_G \vert}{H}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{\text{lat}} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{W/2 - \vert y_G \vert}{H}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU practice):**

Let $W = 0.60$ m, $H = 0.95$ m, $y_G = 0.05$ m (slight offset toward one side due to asymmetric user posture).

$$\theta_{\text{lat}} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.30 - 0.05}{0.95}\right) = \arctan(0.2632) \approx 14.74°$$

This satisfies the ISO 7176 minimum of **10°**, so the design is **acceptable**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stability by Direction

| Direction of Tipping | Tipping Edge | Critical Angle Formula | Typical Adult Value | Clinical Concern |
|---------------------|--------------|------------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Forward** (Sagittal) | Toe-line | $\theta_F = \arctan(d_F / H)$ | 14° – 18° | Knee buckling, CP patients |
| **Backward** (Sagittal) | Heel-line | $\theta_R = \arctan(d_R / H)$ | 8° – 12° | Amputees, Parkinsonism |
| **Lateral** (Coronal) | Foot lateral edge | $\theta_L = \arctan((W/2 - y_G)/H)$ | 10° – 16° | Stroke patients, hemiplegia |
| **Wheelchair Forward** | Front-caster line | $\theta_{WF} = \arctan(L_F / H_{wc})$ | 15° – 25° | Anti-tip wheel design |
| **Wheelchair Rearward** | Rear-wheel line | $\theta_{WR} = \arctan(L_R / H_{wc})$ | 10° – 15° | Powered chair braking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Low COG" Principle (Rehabilitation Rule of Thumb):** Lowering the COG by **1 cm** in a standing patient increases the static stability angle by approximately **0.6° – 1.0°**, depending on stance width. This is why cane users, prosthetic users, and wheelchair users are taught to lean slightly forward and keep loads low.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Geometric Stability Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Body on Support Surface] --> B[Locate Center of Gravity G]
    B --> C[Define Base of Support BOS Polygon]
    C --> D[Draw Vertical Gravity Line through G]
    D --> E{Does Gravity Line intersect BOS?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Body is Statically Stable]
    E -- No --> G[Body Tends to Tip about Nearest BOS Edge]
    F --> H[Compute Stability Index SI = d / H]
    H --> I[Compute Static Stability Angle theta_s = arctan d over H]
    I --> J[Check SF greater than or equal to 1.5]
    J -- Yes --> K[Design Acceptable per ISO 7176]
    J -- No --> L[Redesign: Widen BOS or Lower COG]
    G --> M[Add Anti-Tip Wheels or Restraints]
    M --> N[Re-evaluate Stability]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Three-Directional Stability Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sagittal[Antero-Posterior Plane]
        A1[Forward Tipping] --> A2[Tipping Edge: Toes]
        A3[Backward Tipping] --> A4[Tipping Edge: Heels]
    end
    subgraph Coronal[Medio-Lateral Plane]
        B1[Left Tipping] --> B2[Tipping Edge: Right Foot Lateral Border]
        B3[Right Tipping] --> B4[Tipping Edge: Left Foot Lateral Border]
    end
    subgraph Multiaxial[Diagonal Tipping]
        C1[Combined Tipping] --> C2[Tipping Edge: BOS Corner]
    end
    A2 --> D1[Stability Angle theta_F]
    A4 --> D2[Stability Angle theta_R]
    B2 --> D3[Stability Angle theta_L]
    B4 --> D3
    C2 --> D4[Diagonal Stability Angle theta_D]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Wheelchair Stability Geometry (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UserBody[User Plus Wheelchair System]
        U1[COG of User G_user]
        U2[COG of Wheelchair G_chair]
        U3[Combined COG G_combined]
        U1 --> U3
        U2 --> U3
    end
    subgraph BOS[Base of Support Polygon]
        B1[Rear Left Wheel Contact]
        B2[Rear Right Wheel Contact]
        B3[Front Caster Contact]
        B1 --> B2
        B2 --> B3
        B3 --> B1
    end
    U3 -->|Gravity Line| GL[Vertical Gravity Line]
    GL --> Q{Intersection inside BOS triangle?}
    Q -- Yes --> STABLE[Stable Configuration]
    Q -- No --> UNSTABLE[Tipping Initiated about Nearest Edge]
    UNSTABLE --> FIX[Engage Anti-Tip Wheel or Restraint]
```

### 4.4 Force & Geometry Vector Diagram (Textual Schematic)

> [!NOTE]
> **2D Sagittal-Plane Schematic (textual — for student sketch):**
>
> ```
>           G  (COG)
>           |\
>           | \   H
>           |  \
>           |   \         ← gravity line (always vertical)
>           |    \
>           |_____\
>           BOS edge (toe-line)
>           ←─ d ─→
> ```
> *The angle between the gravity line and the line $G$-toe is $\theta_s = \arctan(d/H)$.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define static stability. State the geometric condition for a body to be in static equilibrium.

**Model Answer:**
Static stability is the ability of a body to remain at rest under the action of gravity without undergoing rotational or translational motion. The geometric condition is that the **line of gravity (vertical line through the COG) must fall within the base of support (BOS)**. If the gravity line exits the BOS boundary, a tipping moment develops about the nearest BOS edge, and the body will topple.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is a "stability triangle" in the context of a wheelchair? Why is it important?

**Model Answer:**
The **stability triangle** of a wheelchair is the triangular polygon formed by joining the three wheel contact points on the ground (two rear wheels and one front caster, or two rear and two front casters, etc.). It is important because, for the wheelchair-user system to be statically stable, the **combined COG of the user and chair must project vertically onto a point inside this triangle**. Designing the chair's wheelbase, track width, and component placement (battery, motors) ensures the COG projection remains inside the triangle on level ground, ramps, and during braking.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the static stability angle $\theta_s$ of a body standing on a horizontal surface, in terms of the COG height $H$ and the horizontal distance $d$ from the gravity line to the nearest tipping edge. State two practical measures to increase $\theta_s$ in a standing patient fitted with a below-knee prosthesis.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wheelchair user of total mass (user + chair) $m = 80$ kg has a combined COG located at height $H = 0.95$ m above the ground and at a longitudinal distance $d_F = 0.40$ m from the front-caster tipping edge. Calculate:
1. The forward static stability angle $\theta_F$.
2. The forward tipping moment when the wheelchair is on a $10°$ ramp (use $g = 9.81$ m/s²).
3. The minimum rearward wheel offset $L_R$ required to provide a safety factor of $SF = 1.5$ against rearward tipping on a $10°}$ ramp.

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student is expected to:
1. State the geometric setup with COG at $(d, H)$ above the support.
2. Apply the rotation transformation about the tipping edge.
3. Set the horizontal coordinate of the rotated COG to zero at the verge of tipping.
4. Obtain $\tan\theta_s = d/H$ and hence $\theta_s = \arctan(d/H)$.

**Practical measures to increase $\theta_s$:**
1. **Lower the COG** by flexing the knees, bending forward at the hips, or carrying loads close to the body. A lower $H$ increases $\theta_s$.
2. **Widen the BOS** by spreading the feet apart, using a cane/walker, or adjusting the prosthetic foot position outward. A larger $d$ increases $\theta_s$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Forward static stability angle:**

$$\theta_F = \arctan\!\left(\frac{d_F}{H}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.40}{0.95}\right) = \arctan(0.4211)$$

$$\boxed{\theta_F \approx 22.83°}$$

**Step 2 — Forward tipping moment on a 10° ramp:**

The component of weight creating a tipping moment about the front-caster edge on a ramp of angle $\theta$ is found from the horizontal offset of the gravity line from the edge.

When the chair is on a $10°$ ramp and the COG is at $(d_F, H)$ relative to the front-caster edge in the body frame, the horizontal offset in the world frame is:

$$x_G = d_F \cos(10°) - H \sin(10°)$$

$$x_G = 0.40 \times 0.9848 - 0.95 \times 0.1736 = 0.3939 - 0.1649 = 0.2290 \text{ m}$$

This is **positive** (gravity line still inside BOS), so the chair is stable and there is no tipping moment in the conventional sense; instead there is a restoring moment:

$$M_{rest} = W \cdot x_G = (80 \times 9.81) \times 0.2290$$

$$M_{rest} = 784.8 \times 0.2290 \approx \boxed{179.7 \text{ N·m (restoring)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students blindly compute $M = W \cdot d_F \cos\theta$ without checking the sign of the horizontal offset. On a moderate ramp the chair is still stable, so the moment is a **restoring** moment, not a **tipping** moment. Sign convention is critical for full marks.

**Step 3 — Minimum rearward offset for $SF = 1.5$:**

On a $10°$ ramp, the rearward tipping edge is the rear-wheel contact line. To prevent rearward tipping, the COG must remain behind the rearward edge by a margin. For a safety factor of 1.5, the effective restoring distance $d_R^{\text{eff}}$ must satisfy:

$$\frac{d_R^{\text{eff}}}{d_R^{\text{crit}}} = 1.5$$

The critical distance (where tipping just begins) satisfies:

$$d_R^{\text{crit}} = H \tan(10°) = 0.95 \times 0.1763 = 0.1675 \text{ m}$$

For $SF = 1.5$:

$$d_R^{\text{required}} = 1.5 \times 0.1675 = 0.2513 \text{ m}$$

Including the existing rearward offset of the COG (assuming centered) plus the wheelbase projection, the minimum **rearward wheel offset $L_R$ measured from the COG vertical projection** is:

$$\boxed{L_R \geq 0.251 \text{ m} \approx 25.1 \text{ cm}}$$

This is the minimum longitudinal distance from the COG vertical projection to the rear wheel contact line.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – Sample]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **geometric approach to static stability** as applied to (i) a standing human, and (ii) a wheelchair user. Use neat sketches and identify the COG, gravity line, and BOS in each case.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A patient of mass 70 kg stands with feet 0.30 m apart. The combined COG is at height 0.98 m above the ground and displaced 0.04 m laterally from the centerline. Compute:
1. The lateral stability angle.
2. Whether the patient can safely stand on a surface inclined laterally by $8°$.
3. The minimum cane offset required on the patient's weaker side to raise the lateral stability angle to $20°$.

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Qualitative Explanation [7 Marks]:**

**Standing human:** The BOS is the convex polygon bounded by the outline of the feet and the area between them (if separated). The COG is approximately at the level of the second sacral vertebra, about 0.55 × body height. The gravity line is vertical. Stability requires this vertical line to fall within the foot-polygon. Anteroposterior tipping is about the toe-line or heel-line; mediolateral tipping is about the lateral edge of the foot.

**Wheelchair user:** The BOS is the stability triangle (or quadrilateral) formed by the wheel contact points. The COG of the *combined* user-plus-chair system lies higher than the chair's geometric center because the human body mass is concentrated in the torso. The gravity line must project inside the wheel polygon. Lateral stability is governed by the rear track width; longitudinal stability is governed by the wheelbase length.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Lateral stability angle on level ground:**

The lateral tipping edge is at distance $(W/2 - \vert y_G \vert)$ from the COG vertical projection:

$$d_{lat} = \frac{0.30}{2} - 0.04 = 0.15 - 0.04 = 0.11 \text{ m}$$

$$\theta_{lat} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.11}{0.98}\right) = \arctan(0.1122) \approx 6.40°$$

**Step 2 — Safety on an $8°$ lateral incline:**

Since $8° > 6.40°$, the patient **cannot** safely stand on the laterally inclined surface without external support. The gravity line would lie outside the BOS.

**Step 3 — Cane offset for $\theta_{lat} = 20°$:**

For a stability angle of $20°$ with $H = 0.98$ m, the required $d_{lat}$ is:

$$d_{lat}^{\text{new}} = H \tan(20°) = 0.98 \times 0.3640 = 0.3567 \text{ m}$$

The current $d_{lat}$ is $0.11$ m. The cane must extend the BOS laterally by:

$$\Delta d = 0.3567 - 0.11 = 0.2467 \text{ m}$$

A standard cane touches the ground at a point offset horizontally by some distance $\delta$ from the foot's lateral edge. For a typical cane angled at $15°$ from vertical with length $1.0$ m, the ground contact offset is:

$$\delta = L \sin(15°) = 1.0 \times 0.2588 = 0.259 \text{ m}$$

Since $\delta \approx 0.259$ m $> 0.247$ m, **a single standard cane is sufficient** to raise the lateral stability angle to approximately $20°$.

$$\boxed{\text{Cane ground-offset} \approx 0.25 \text{ m, which is adequate.}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas (Examiner Observations):**
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder:** Students write $M_{tip} = Wd$ without specifying whether $d$ is the tipping-side or restoring-side lever arm. Always state the convention.
> 2. **Units of Angle:** Using degrees in $\tan$ or $\sin$ arguments is acceptable numerically, but explicitly state "$\theta$ in degrees" or convert to radians. Examiners deduct for ambiguity.
> 3. **Confusing COG and Center of Mass (COM):** Under uniform gravity, COG = COM. The student should not mix up the formulas, but should not over-complicate by invoking $g$ variations (irrelevant in KTU problems).
> 4. **Forgetting the Safety Factor:** ISO 7176 and FDA guidelines require $SF \geq 1.5$ for assistive devices. A design with $SF = 1.05$ is technically stable but **not manufacturable / certifiable**.
> 5. **Single-axis Assumption:** Real patients tip diagonally, not purely forward/laterally. Students who compute only anteroposterior stability miss lateral components in stroke and hemiplegia patients.
> 6. **Static vs Dynamic Stability Confusion:** KTU Module 2 is *static* stability only. If a question asks about a wheelchair going over a curb or a patient tripping, the answer must invoke **dynamic** concepts (angular momentum, base reaction acceleration) — not the geometric approach.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Static stability = gravity line inside BOS. Geometric approach = no inertia, no acceleration.
- **Three Pillars:** COG, BOS, Gravity Line. Memorize their symbols ($G$, BOS, GL).
- **Core Formula:** $\theta_s = \arctan(d / H)$. The two variables are the *only* design handles: increase $d$ (widen BOS) or decrease $H$ (lower COG).
- **Directional Stability:** Forward, rearward, lateral — each has a different tipping edge and a different critical angle.
- **Wheelchair Rule:** Stability triangle of wheel contact points. Combined COG must project inside.
- **Safety Factor:** $SF \geq 1.5$ for assistive devices per ISO 7176.
- **Standing Patient Rule:** Foot separation $W$ sets lateral stability; foot length sets anteroposterior stability.
- **Clinical Use:** Prosthetic alignment, seating systems, standing frames, gait trainers, cane prescription.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Sign of moment, units, confusing static vs dynamic, single-axis oversimplification.
- **Numericals:** Always draw a 2D sagittal or coronal schematic; label $G$, $H$, $d$, $\theta_s$, and the tipping edge explicitly.
- **Past Paper Frequency:** This topic appears in **every** KTU December and May session of PEBRT752 since 2019, typically as a 7-mark sub-part of a 14-mark question.

> **End of Module 2 — Geometric Approach to Static Stability | KTU 2024 Scheme | PEBRT752**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stability with Road Crown and Inclination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Road Crown (Camber / Cross-Slope):** The transverse slope provided across the roadway, rising from the edges toward the centre line, primarily to drain off surface water. Quantitatively, it is expressed as a ratio of vertical rise to half the carriageway width (e.g., **1 in 30** or **2 %**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Road Inclination (Longitudinal Gradient):** The rate of rise or fall of the road along its centre line in the direction of traffic, expressed as a percentage or as "1 vertical to *n* horizontal."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability (in the context of this module):** The condition of equilibrium of a vehicle — or, in rehabilitation engineering, a wheeled mobility device (wheelchair, walker, scooter) — against **overturning** and **skidding/sliding** when the road surface is simultaneously cambered and graded.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme rehabilitation context, the analysis is **dual-purpose**:
1. The classical highway-engineering treatment of **four-wheeled vehicles**.
2. The accessibility treatment of **manual/powered wheelchairs and assistive rolling aids** on sloped urban pavements.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Tilted Tabletop" Analogy:** Imagine placing a coin on a flat table — it sits safely. Now tilt the table slightly side-to-side (this is the **crown**): the coin slowly slides. Now also tip the table forward/backward (this is the **inclination/grade**): the coin's slide direction tilts toward the downhill corner. The coin's *resultant* tendency to slip is set by the **vector sum** of both tilts. The same physics governs a wheelchair on a crowned kerb ramp or a car on a superelevated curve.

**Real-world examples encountered by KTU graduates:**

- A two-wheeler negotiating a **superelevated curve on a gradient** (e.g., a ghat road hairpin bend).
- A **manual wheelchair user** descending a 1:12 ramp (≈ 8.33 %) that is also cambered for drainage.
- A **crutch or walker user** on a wet footpath that slopes both ways.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**.
- Standard urban camber for bituminous roads: **2 % to 2.5 %** (KTU/IRC:SP:73).
- Maximum longitudinal grade in urban areas: **6 % to 8 %** (IRC:73 for plain terrain).
- Wheelchair ramp preferred grade (RPwD Act 2016 / KTU accessibility guidelines): **1:12 (≈ 8.33 %)** maximum, **1:20 (5 %)** preferred.
- Coefficient of lateral friction (dry pavement): **μ ≈ 0.35 to 0.50**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Free-Body Setup

Consider a rigid vehicle (or wheelchair) of weight **W** = mg acting at the Centre of Gravity **G**, located at height **h** above the road and at the centre of the wheelbase **b** (track width). The road is inclined by:
- Longitudinal angle **α** (gradient, in the direction of travel).
- Transverse angle **β** (crown/camber, across the road).

The **resultant road slope** to the horizontal is:

$$
\theta_R = \sqrt{\alpha^{2} + \beta^{2}}
$$

The **resultant direction** of slope lies in a plane whose horizontal projection makes an angle **φ** with the road centre line:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}
$$

## 2.2 Forces Acting on the Vehicle

| Force | Symbol | Direction | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| Weight | **W** | Vertically downward | **mg** |
| Normal reaction (total) | **N** | Perpendicular to road surface | **W cos θ_R** |
| Driving component | **F_d** | Down the resultant slope | **W sin θ_R** |
| Frictional resistance | **F_f** | Opposes motion | **μ N = μ W cos θ_R** |

## 2.3 Two Failure Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode 1 — Overturning (Tipping):** The vehicle rotates about the downhill wheel. Occurs when the line of action of **W** falls **outside** the wheelbase.
>
> **Mode 2 — Skidding (Sliding):** The vehicle translates down the slope. Occurs when the gravitational pull along the slope **exceeds** the available friction.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Condition | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resultant road slope angle | $\theta_R = \sqrt{\alpha^{2} + \beta^{2}}$ | radians | General combined slope |
| 2 | Direction of resultant slope | $\tan\phi = \beta / \alpha$ | radians | Orientation w.r.t. centre line |
| 3 | Overturning condition (pure crown β) | $\tan\beta_{OT} = b / (2h)$ | dimensionless | Vehicle tipping sideways |
| 4 | Skidding condition (pure crown β) | $\tan\beta_{SK} = \mu$ | dimensionless | Vehicle sliding sideways |
| 5 | Overturning condition (pure grade α) | $\tan\alpha_{OT} = b / h$ | dimensionless | Vehicle tipping longitudinally |
| 6 | Skidding condition (pure grade α) | $\tan\alpha_{SK} = \mu$ | dimensionless | Vehicle sliding longitudinally |
| 7 | Combined overturning (square of critical slope) | $\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{b/h}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\beta}{b/(2h)}\right)^{2} = 1$ | dimensionless | Interaction ellipse |
| 8 | Combined skidding | $\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{\mu}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\beta}{\mu}\right)^{2} = 1$ | dimensionless | Interaction circle |
| 9 | Safe design: skidding avoided if | $\alpha^{2} + \beta^{2} \le \mu^{2}$ | dimensionless | Design check |
| 10 | Safe design: overturning avoided if | $\alpha^{2} \le (b/h)^{2},\;\; \beta^{2} \le (b/2h)^{2}$ | dimensionless | Design check |

> [!NOTE]
> In the cheat sheet above, **b** is the wheelbase (longitudinal) and **b/2** is the half-track (transverse). For a wheelchair, use the distance between the rear-wheel contact patch and the front-caster contact patch; for **h** use the seated Centre of Gravity height of the occupant.

## 2.5 The Two Critical Ellipses Explained

- **Overturning envelope** is an **ellipse** in the (α, β) plane because the resisting moment arms are different in the longitudinal and transverse directions (full wheelbase *vs* half-track).
- **Skidding envelope** is a **circle** in the (α, β) plane because friction is direction-independent (isotropic Coulomb friction).
- The vehicle is **safe** only when the operating point (α_operating, β_operating) lies **inside both** the overturning ellipse and the skidding circle.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highway design:** Determines the maximum superelevation combined with grade that a car can safely negotiate on mountainous hairpin bends.
- **Barrier / kerb design:** Wheelchair stoppers at pedestrian crossings must overcome the skidding tendency on a cambered, ramped footpath.
- **Pavement rehabilitation:** When overlaying a road, the camber must be preserved so that combined slopes do not push the rehabilitated surface into the overturning regime for two-wheelers.
- **Universal access design (Rehab):** Wheelchair ramps on cambered footpaths have a *reduced* effective grade along the line of fall; designers must verify both α and β independently against the RPwD Act limit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overturning About the Downhill Wheels (Crown Only)

**Setup:** A vehicle of weight W at height h sits on a road cambered at angle β. Track width (centre-to-centre of wheels) = b.

**Step 1 — Resolve weight perpendicular and parallel to the cambered surface.**

The road surface makes angle β with the horizontal. Rotate the reference frame by β so the surface becomes the new "horizontal."

In this rotated frame, the weight **W** decomposes as:
- Component normal to the surface (pressing into the road): N = W cos β.
- Component parallel to the surface, pointing downhill: F = W sin β.

**Step 2 — Take moments about the downhill wheel edge (the pivot point for tipping).**

Let the two contact points be A (uphill) and B (downhill), separated by distance **b**. The CG is at the mid-width at height **h**.

The overturning moment (about B) is produced by the parallel component F acting through the CG horizontally. Its moment arm, measured **perpendicular to F** in the rotated plane, is the horizontal offset of G from B, which equals **b/2**.

The restoring moment is produced by the normal component N acting at the CG. Its moment arm is the height offset **h** above the road.

$$
\text{Overturning moment} = F \cdot \frac{b}{2} = (W \sin\beta)\,\frac{b}{2}
$$

$$
\text{Restoring moment} = N \cdot h = (W \cos\beta)\,h
$$

**Step 3 — Set overturning moment equal to restoring moment (critical condition).**

$$
(W \sin\beta)\,\frac{b}{2} = (W \cos\beta)\,h
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel W and rearrange.**

$$
\sin\beta \cdot \frac{b}{2} = \cos\beta \cdot h
$$

$$
\frac{\sin\beta}{\cos\beta} = \frac{h}{b/2} = \frac{2h}{b}
$$

**Step 5 — Final overturning condition.**

$$
\boxed{\;\tan\beta_{OT} = \dfrac{2h}{b}\;}
$$

The vehicle **overturns** when the camber angle β **exceeds** arctan(2h/b).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Skidding (Sliding) on a Cambered Surface

**Step 1 — Same setup, but now ask when the parallel force overcomes static friction.**

The available frictional resistance is:

$$
F_f = \mu N = \mu\,W\cos\beta
$$

The driving force (down the slope) is:

$$
F_d = W\sin\beta
$$

**Step 2 — Critical condition: F_d = F_f.**

$$
W\sin\beta = \mu\,W\cos\beta
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel W and rearrange.**

$$
\tan\beta = \mu
$$

**Step 4 — Final skidding condition.**

$$
\boxed{\;\tan\beta_{SK} = \mu\;}
$$

The vehicle **skids** when β **exceeds** arctan(μ).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical insight (KTU favourite):** Because 2h/b is usually **less than 1** (a car has a wide track and a low CG), but μ is also typically less than 1, the comparison of μ and 2h/b determines whether a vehicle **overturns first or skids first**:
> - If μ < 2h/b → **skidding occurs first** (safer — the car slides before it tips).
> - If μ > 2h/b → **overturning occurs first** (more dangerous).
> - For typical cars, μ ≈ 0.35–0.50 < 2h/b ≈ 0.8–1.2, so cars **skid before they overturn** — the safe failure mode.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined Crown (β) and Inclination (α)

**Step 1 — Set up the resultant slope.**

Let the road have longitudinal grade α and transverse camber β. The road-surface normal **n̂** and the down-slope direction **d̂** lie in a plane containing the vertical. Define the local road coordinate system:
- x̂ — along centre line (climb direction).
- ŷ — transverse, from downhill edge toward uphill edge.
- ẑ — vertically upward (true vertical, not road normal).

The **true vertical** in road coordinates has components:
- Component along the road surface in the x̂ direction: **sin α** (pointing downhill if α is a rise in the +x̂ direction).
- Component along the road surface in the ŷ direction: **sin β** (pointing toward the lower edge if β rises toward the centre).
- Component along the road normal n̂: **cos θ_R**, where

$$
\cos\theta_R = \cos\alpha\,\cos\beta
$$

(Exact composition, not a small-angle approximation.)

**Step 2 — Resultant in-slope force components.**

The down-slope driving force in the road surface plane has components:

$$
F_x = W\sin\alpha\cos\beta \quad\text{(longitudinal)}$$
$$
F_y = W\cos\alpha\sin\beta \quad\text{(transverse)}
$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of resultant driving force.**

$$
F_R = W\sqrt{(\sin\alpha\cos\beta)^{2} + (\cos\alpha\sin\beta)^{2}}
$$

For small α, β (≤ ~10°), cos α ≈ cos β ≈ 1, giving the engineering approximation:

$$
F_R \approx W\sqrt{\sin^{2}\alpha + \sin^{2}\beta} \approx W\sqrt{\alpha^{2} + \beta^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Overturning condition in the combined case.**

The vehicle tips about the **diagonal** wheel pair (front-downhill and rear-uphill, or rear-downhill and front-uphill, depending on sign of α). The tipping occurs about an axis in the road plane perpendicular to the projection of the line of action of W onto the road surface.

Taking moments about the downhill-corner wheel contact point:
- Distance from CG to that corner in the road plane = half the diagonal wheelbase.
- For a rectangular wheelbase (longitudinal b_L, transverse b_T):

$$
\text{Tipping moment} = F_R \cdot \frac{\sqrt{b_L^{2} + b_T^{2}}}{2}
$$

$$
\text{Restoring moment} = N \cdot h = W\cos\theta_R \cdot h
$$

Setting them equal at the verge of tipping and using the exact small-angle relation $F_R \approx W\sqrt{\alpha^{2} + \beta^{2}}$ and $\cos\theta_R \approx 1$:

$$
W\sqrt{\alpha^{2} + \beta^{2}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{b_L^{2} + b_T^{2}}}{2} = W \cdot h
$$

Squaring and rearranging into ellipse form:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\alpha^{2}}{(2h/b_L)^{2}} + \frac{\beta^{2}}{(2h/b_T)^{2}} = 1\;}
$$

This is the **overturning ellipse**. With $b_T = b/2$ as the full track (so half-track = b/4) we recover the form in the cheat sheet, depending on notation.

**Step 5 — Skidding condition in the combined case.**

For skidding, the resultant driving force must exceed friction in the direction of sliding:

$$
W\sqrt{\alpha^{2} + \beta^{2}} = \mu\,W
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha^{2} + \beta^{2} = \mu^{2}\;}
$$

This is the **skidding circle** in the (α, β) plane.

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style)

> **Problem:** A car has wheelbase **b_L = 2.5 m**, track width **b_T = 1.5 m**, and CG height **h = 0.6 m**. It negotiates a road with longitudinal grade **α = 4 %** and camber **β = 3 %**. Take μ = 0.35. Check whether the car will skid or overturn.

**Solution:**

Convert percentages to angles in radians (small angle: tan ≈ sin ≈ angle).
- α = 0.04 rad.
- β = 0.03 rad.

**Check skidding (combined):**

$$
\alpha^{2} + \beta^{2} = 0.04^{2} + 0.03^{2} = 0.0016 + 0.0009 = 0.0025
$$

$$
\mu^{2} = 0.35^{2} = 0.1225
$$

Since 0.0025 ≪ 0.1225, **skidding does not occur**. ✓

**Check overturning (combined):**

Critical ellipse parameters:
- $2h/b_L = 2(0.6)/2.5 = 0.48$.
- $2h/b_T = 2(0.6)/1.5 = 0.80$.

Compute:

$$
\frac{\alpha^{2}}{(2h/b_L)^{2}} + \frac{\beta^{2}}{(2h/b_T)^{2}} = \frac{0.0016}{0.48^{2}} + \frac{0.0009}{0.80^{2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.0016}{0.2304} + \frac{0.0009}{0.6400} = 0.00694 + 0.00141 = 0.00835
$$

Since 0.00835 ≪ 1, the operating point is well **inside the overturning ellipse**. **No overturning.** ✓

> **Conclusion:** The car is stable in both modes.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Failure Hierarchy

| Vehicle | μ | 2h/b_T (lateral tipping ratio) | Critical Mode | Why |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Slow truck | 0.30 | 0.25 | Skids first | Low CG, high friction (loaded) |
| Passenger car | 0.40 | 0.80 | Skids first | μ < 2h/b_T |
| Tall van | 0.40 | 1.20 | Overturns first | High CG, narrow track |
| Two-wheeler | 0.35 | 1.50 | Overturns first | Very narrow track, high CG |
| Loaded wheelchair (occupant) | 0.20 | 0.60 | Skids first | Low CG of seated user |
| Unloaded manual wheelchair | 0.15 | 0.30 | Skids first | Very light, low friction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free-Body Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Vehicle Wheelchair or Car of Weight W] --> B[Centre of Gravity G at height h]
    B --> C[Resolve W into road-aligned axes]
    C --> D[Normal Reaction N perpendicular to road surface]
    C --> E[Down-slope Driving Force F parallel to road surface]
    E --> E1[Longitudinal component F_x from grade alpha]
    E --> E2[Transverse component F_y from crown beta]
    E1 --> F[Resultant F_R = W sqrt of alpha squared + beta squared]
    E2 --> F
    D --> G[Frictional Resistance F_f = mu times N]
    F --> H{F_R greater than F_f ?}
    G --> H
    H -->|Yes| I[FAILURE MODE 1: SKIDDING]
    H -->|No| J[Resultant moment about downhill wheel edge]
    J --> K{Tipping moment greater than restoring moment ?}
    K -->|Yes| L[FAILURE MODE 2: OVERTURNING]
    K -->|No| M[SAFE EQUILIBRIUM]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Designer/Examiner Use

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Input alpha grade and beta camber and vehicle data b h mu] --> Q[Convert percentages to radians]
    Q --> R[Compute S = alpha squared + beta squared]
    R --> S{S less than mu squared ?}
    S -->|No| T1[Vehicle SKIDS first]
    S -->|Yes| U[Compute overturning index OI]
    U --> V[OI = alpha over 2h by bL squared + beta over 2h by bT squared]
    V --> W{OI less than 1 ?}
    W -->|No| T2[Vehicle OVERTURNS first]
    W -->|Yes| X[Vehicle is STABLE]
    T1 --> Y[Design action: reduce mu dependency, add texture, reduce crown]
    T2 --> Z[Design action: lower CG, widen track, reduce grade]
    X --> AA[Proceed with design]
```

## 4.3 Stability Envelope Schematic (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AXES[Alpha Beta Stability Plane]
        XAX[Horizontal axis: alpha grade]
        YAX[Vertical axis: beta camber]
    end
    subgraph SKC[Skidding Circle]
        SK1[Radius mu on both axes]
    end
    subgraph OTE[Overturning Ellipse]
        OT1[Semi major axis 2h by bL along alpha]
        OT2[Semi minor axis 2h by bT along beta]
    end
    subgraph OP[Operating Point P of the vehicle on the road]
        OP1[Coordinates alpha_op and beta_op]
    end
    SKC --- OTE
    OTE --- OP
    OP --> SAFE{P lies inside both boundaries ?}
    SAFE -->|Yes| OK[DESIGN ACCEPTABLE]
    SAFE -->|No| NG[REDESIGN REQUIRED]
```

## 4.4 Notes on the Diagrams

- These are **block-level functional architecture flows**, not physical drawings, in line with the topic being primarily analytical.
- In an examination, students should sketch a **2-D cross-section** showing W, N, F, the camber angle β, and label h and b explicitly.
