# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Define road crown. State its typical value for an urban bituminous carriageway as per KTU/IRC guidelines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Road crown (camber) is the transverse slope provided across the road surface, rising from the edges toward the centre, to drain off surface water and prevent accumulation that could cause skidding or seepage damage.
- **Typical value (1 mark):** For urban bituminous roads, the camber is **2 % to 2.5 %** (i.e., 1 in 40 to 1 in 50), as recommended by **IRC:73 and IRC:SP:73**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**State the two modes of failure of a vehicle on a sloped road surface. Write the critical condition for each mode considering pure camber β.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Mode 1 — Overturning (1 mark):** Vehicle tips about the downhill wheel. Critical condition: **tan β_OT = 2h / b**, where b is track width and h is CG height.
- **Mode 2 — Skidding (1 mark):** Vehicle slides down the slope. Critical condition: **tan β_SK = μ**, where μ is the lateral coefficient of friction.
- **Hierarchy (1 mark):** If μ < 2h/b, skidding occurs first (safe failure); if μ > 2h/b, overturning occurs first (unsafe failure).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model — PEBRT752 Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** Derive, with a clear free-body diagram, the critical condition for **overturning** of a four-wheeled vehicle on a road with camber β. State the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **manual wheelchair** carrying an occupant has the following data:
- Wheelbase (rear-wheel to front-caster contact) b_L = 0.6 m.
- Track width b_T = 0.55 m.
- CG height of occupant h = 0.95 m (seated, above ground).
- Coefficient of rolling friction on wet paving μ = 0.15.

The wheelchair is on a footpath ramp with longitudinal grade **α = 5 %** and camber **β = 2.5 %**. Determine whether the wheelchair will **skid or overturn** under static (no-brake) conditions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*(Steps identical to Derivation 1 in Section 3.1.)*
- Free-body: W at height h, two contact points A (uphill) and B (downhill) separated by track width b.
- Resolved components (1 mark): N = W cos β perpendicular to surface; F = W sin β along surface (downhill).
- Overturning moment about B: F × (b/2) = W sin β × (b/2) (2 marks).
- Restoring moment about B: N × h = W cos β × h (1 mark).
- Equate, cancel W, rearrange: tan β = 2h/b (2 marks).
- **Assumptions (1 mark):** Rigid body, level surface transverse profile, CG on centreline, slow/quasi-static motion, Coulomb friction, no dynamic effects.

**Final answer:**

$$
\tan\beta_{OT} = \dfrac{2h}{b}
$$

**(b) Numerical check (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert to radians (1 mark):**
- α = 0.05 rad.
- β = 0.025 rad.

**Step 2 — Skidding check (3 marks):**

$$
\alpha^{2} + \beta^{2} = 0.05^{2} + 0.025^{2} = 0.0025 + 0.000625 = 0.003125
$$

$$
\mu^{2} = 0.15^{2} = 0.0225
$$

Since 0.003125 < 0.0225, **no skidding**.

**Step 3 — Overturning check (3 marks):**

Compute overturning index OI:

$$
\text{OI} = \left(\frac{\alpha}{2h/b_L}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\beta}{2h/b_T}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
\frac{2h}{b_L} = \frac{2 \times 0.95}{0.6} = 3.167
$$

$$
\frac{2h}{b_T} = \frac{2 \times 0.95}{0.55} = 3.455
$$

$$
\text{OI} = \left(\frac{0.05}{3.167}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{0.025}{3.455}\right)^{2} = (0.01579)^{2} + (0.007236)^{2}
$$

$$
\text{OI} = 0.000249 + 0.0000524 = 0.000301
$$

Since OI = 0.000301 ≪ 1, **no overturning**.

**Final conclusion:** The wheelchair is **safe** in both modes on the given ramp. Designers may still add a **kerb rail** for fall protection because real-world conditions (wet surface, dynamic lurch) reduce the effective safety margin.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model — PEBRT752 Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain how the **combined effect** of road crown β and longitudinal inclination α modifies the overturning and skidding conditions of a vehicle. Show that the **skidding boundary is a circle** and the **overturning boundary is an ellipse** in the (α, β) plane. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-wheeler has:
- Wheelbase b_L = 1.3 m.
- Track width b_T = 0.45 m.
- CG height h = 0.85 m.
- μ = 0.35.

A road section has a **superelevation equivalent camber β = 6 %** combined with a **downhill grade α = 4 %**. Determine if the two-wheeler is safe. If unsafe, suggest **two rehabilitation/accessibility-relevant design modifications** to bring it to a safe state. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

- Free-body with both tilts (1 mark).
- Resultant slope direction set by tan φ = β/α, magnitude θ_R = √(α² + β²) (1 mark).
- **Skidding:** F_R = W√(α² + β²); friction = μW. Equate: α² + β² = μ² — a **circle** of radius μ in the (α, β) plane (2 marks).
- **Overturning:** Different moment arms in longitudinal (full b_L) and transverse (full b_T) directions give an **ellipse** with semi-axes 2h/b_L and 2h/b_T respectively (2 marks).
- Stability requires the operating point to lie **inside both** the ellipse and the circle (1 mark).

**Final equations:**

$$
\text{Skidding:}\quad \alpha^{2} + \beta^{2} = \mu^{2}
$$

$$
\text{Overturning:}\quad \frac{\alpha^{2}}{(2h/b_L)^{2}} + \frac{\beta^{2}}{(2h/b_T)^{2}} = 1
$$

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert (1 mark):** α = 0.04, β = 0.06 rad.

**Step 2 — Skidding check (2 marks):**

$$
\alpha^{2} + \beta^{2} = 0.0016 + 0.0036 = 0.0052
$$

$$
\mu^{2} = 0.1225
$$

0.0052 < 0.1225 → **no skidding**.

**Step 3 — Overturning check (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{2h}{b_L} = \frac{1.70}{1.3} = 1.308
$$

$$
\frac{2h}{b_T} = \frac{1.70}{0.45} = 3.778
$$

$$
\text{OI} = \left(\frac{0.04}{1.308}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{0.06}{3.778}\right)^{2} = 0.000935 + 0.000252 = 0.00119
$$

OI ≪ 1 → **no overturning** under quasi-static conditions.

**Step 4 — Conclusion and rehabilitation-relevant suggestions (2 marks):**

**Conclusion:** The two-wheeler is **statically safe**, but the **safety margin is small on the longitudinal axis** (OI dominated by α, since b_L is small and 2h/b_L is comparable to β). At higher speeds or with a heavier rider, dynamic amplification can push OI past 1.

**Two design modifications:**
1. **Reduce superelevation to 4 %** so that the combined operating point moves toward the centre of the safe envelope — improves safety for two-wheelers and assists a side-foot-rest user of a tricycle/wheelchair.
2. **Add high-friction surface texture (anti-skid dressing)** on the camber zone, increasing effective μ to 0.50+, pushing the skidding circle outward and providing a wider safety envelope — also a major accessibility upgrade for crutch and prosthetic-foot users.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Forgetting to convert % to radians** before plugging into tan⁻¹: Common 1-mark loss. Always convert grades to decimal angle (e.g., 4 % → 0.04 rad for small angles, or use arctan(0.04) for precise work).
> - **Using wheelbase b in BOTH overturning equations**: Students often write tan β = b/(2h) instead of b being the **track** in the camber equation and the **wheelbase** in the grade equation. The KTU answer key explicitly distinguishes them. Lose 2 marks per instance.
> - **Skipping the assumption list** in derivations: A 1-mark deduction is routine.
> - **For combined slopes, students sometimes ADD the angles**: This is wrong. The vector composition is via the **Pythagorean** √(α² + β²), not α + β. Wrong-answer loss is up to 3 marks.
> - **Confusing μ and 2h/b hierarchy**: This is a KTU favourite for 2-mark conceptual sub-parts.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Crown / camber β** is transverse; **inclination / grade α** is longitudinal; both small angles (≤ 10 %).
- **Two failure modes only:** Skidding and Overturning. Always check both.
- **Skidding condition (pure):** tan β = μ (or tan α = μ).
- **Overturning condition (pure):** tan β = 2h/b_T (camber) or tan α = 2h/b_L (grade).
- **Critical ratio:** Compare μ with 2h/b — whichever is smaller defines the **first failure**.
- **Combined slopes** decompose the driving force vectorially:
  - **Skidding envelope = circle** of radius μ in the (α, β) plane.
  - **Overturning envelope = ellipse** with semi-axes (2h/b_L) and (2h/b_T).
- **Rehab relevance:** Manual/powered wheelchairs have **low μ (0.10–0.20)** on wet pavement → skidding is the dominant failure; design ramps with anti-skid strips and kerb rails.
- **Standard urban camber:** 2 %–2.5 %; **max urban grade:** 6 %–8 %; **wheelchair ramp preferred grade:** 1:20 (5 %).
- **Free-body must include:** W, N, F (driving), F_f (friction), with h and b clearly labelled.
- **Small-angle approximation:** sin θ ≈ tan θ ≈ θ (in radians) is valid for grades up to ~10 %.
- **Resultant slope magnitude:** $\theta_R = \sqrt{\alpha^{2} + \beta^{2}}$; **direction:** $\tan\phi = \beta/\alpha$.
- **Design safety rule of thumb:** Operating point must lie **inside both** boundaries, with a margin ≥ 30 % for dynamic effects.
- **Examiner hot-words to use:** *free-body diagram, normal reaction, component of weight, critical angle, verge of overturning, factor of safety, interaction ellipse, interaction circle*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact Strength Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Impact Strength Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Impact Strength** of a material is defined as its ability to absorb energy and undergo **plastic deformation** under a suddenly applied (high-velocity) load or shock before fracture occurs. Mathematically, it is expressed as the ratio of the **impact energy absorbed** at fracture to the original cross-sectional area of the specimen at the notch.

In the context of **Rehabilitation Engineering**, impact strength is a critical mechanical property because assistive devices (prosthetic sockets, orthotic braces, crutches, wheelchair frames, grab-bars, and walking aids) are routinely subjected to sudden accidental loads such as stumbles, falls, or collisions. A material with poor impact strength may shatter catastrophically, endangering the user; hence, impact testing is a statutory requirement under **ISO 10328** (Prosthetics — Testing of structural components) and **ISO 7176** (Wheelchair strength and durability).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must be able to *define* impact strength, *classify* impact tests, *derive* the energy equation, and *evaluate* the suitability of a material for a given rehabilitation device. Questions on Izod vs. Charpy comparison and the Ductile-to-Brittle Transition (DBT) curve are **frequently repeated** in KTU university examinations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are using a **wooden crutch** and accidentally hit the floor hard. The wood absorbs the shock, bends slightly, and you feel a soft thud. Now replace the wooden shaft with a **glass-reinforced plastic rod** of the same thickness. The same hit causes it to crack and splinter. The wooden crutch has **high impact strength**; the GRP rod has **low impact strength**.

In engineering terms, the wooden material is **tough** (energy-absorbing + ductile), while the GRP is **brittle** (low energy absorption). Impact tests quantify this "thud versus crack" behaviour numerically using a swinging pendulum or a falling weight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Impact strength is **not** the same as tensile strength. A material can be very strong in static tension (high ultimate tensile strength) but extremely weak under impact (e.g., cast iron, ceramics). In rehabilitation, toughness matters more than raw strength because human motion is dynamic.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Units

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit (SI) | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Impact Energy | $A$ or $W$ | **Joule (J)** | Total energy absorbed at fracture |
| Impact Strength (Notch) | $a_{k}$ or $KCV/KCU$ | **J/cm²** or **J/mm²** | Energy per unit notch area |
| Notch Radius | $r$ | **mm** | Typically 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 |
| Striking Velocity | $v$ | **m/s** | Usually 3.8, 4.0, 5.0, 5.5 m/s |
| Test Temperature | $T$ | **°C / K** | Critical for DBT analysis |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Impact Tests

Impact tests are broadly classified by the **mode of loading** and the **type of specimen**. The KTU syllabus emphasises four primary categories:

1. **Pendulum-type (Swinging Hammer) Tests**
   - **Izod Impact Test** (Cantilever beam, notch facing striker)
   - **Charpy Impact Test** (Simply-supported beam, notch on tension side)
2. **Falling-Weight Tests**
   - **Drop-Weight Test (DWT)** — for thick plates, used to find Nil-Ductility Transition (NDT)
   - **Falling-Dart Test** — for plastics, films, and laminates
3. **Instrumented Impact Test** — modern variant with load-cell and oscilloscope for force–time data
4. **High-Strain-Rate Tests** — Split-Hopkinson Pressure Bar (SHPB) for very high strain rates

## 2.2 Principle of the Pendulum Test (Universal)

A pendulum of known mass $m$ and length $L$ is raised to a height corresponding to an initial angle $\alpha$ from the vertical. It is then released, swings down, and strikes the specimen. After breaking the specimen, the pendulum rises to a final (recovery) angle $\beta$ on the opposite side.

The energy absorbed by the specimen is the **loss of kinetic energy** of the pendulum, which equals the **loss of potential energy** between the two extreme positions.

The initial potential energy (relative to the lowest point) is:

$$U_{i} = mgL(1 - \cos\alpha)$$

The final potential energy after fracture is:

$$U_{f} = mgL(1 - \cos\beta)$$

Therefore, the **impact energy absorbed** by the specimen is:

$$\boxed{A = mgL(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the pendulum **does not break** the specimen (energy capacity too high), the test is **invalid** and a heavier pendulum or a sharper notch must be used. The reading on the dial is *directly* the energy in **Joules**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Print & Memorise)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = mgL(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$ | Energy absorbed by specimen | **J** |
| 2 | $a_{k} = \dfrac{A}{A_{0}}$ | Impact strength (notched) = Energy ÷ Cross-sectional area at notch | **J/m²** or **J/cm²** |
| 3 | $v = \sqrt{2gL(1 - \cos\alpha)}$ | Striking velocity of hammer | **m/s** |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{A}{A_{\text{max}}} \times 100\%$ | Energy efficiency of test (how much of available energy was used) | **%** |
| 5 | $KCV = \dfrac{KU}{A_{0}}$ | Charpy V-notch impact strength (ISO notation) | **J/cm²** |
| 6 | $KCU = \dfrac{KU}{A_{0}}$ | Charpy U-notch impact strength (ISO notation) | **J/cm²** |
| 7 | $\sigma_{d} = \dfrac{F}{A_{0}}$ | Dynamic stress at impact (from instrumented test) | **MPa** |

> [!NOTE]
> In ISO notation, **K** = impact energy in Joules, **C** = Charpy, **U** = U-notch, **V** = V-notch. So $KV$ means Charpy V-notch energy, and $KCV$ is the corresponding strength (KV ÷ area).

## 2.4 Relevance in Rehabilitation Engineering

- **Lower-limb prosthetics (e.g., transtibial socket):** Subjected to impact loads during heel-strike (≈ 1.2× body weight within 25 ms). Materials must pass impact tests per **ISO 10328**.
- **Wheelchair frames (aluminium, titanium, composites):** Tested for impact during curb-drop and crash scenarios per **ISO 7176-8**.
- **Crutches and walking sticks:** Must withstand sudden axial and lateral impacts.
- **Orthotic knee joints:** Tested for fatigue + impact combined loading.
- **Helmets and protective gear:** Use **falling-dart** equivalent tests to simulate falling objects.

## 2.5 Comparison of Test Methods (Quick-Reference)

| Parameter | Izod | Charpy | Drop-Weight | Falling-Dart |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Specimen orientation | Vertical (cantilever) | Horizontal (simply supported) | Horizontal plate | Horizontal sheet/film |
| Notch position | At the struck end, facing striker | Centre, opposite to striker | Usually no notch (or pressed notch) | Usually no notch |
| Striking direction | Lateral | Lateral | Vertical drop | Vertical drop |
| Result reported as | Energy in J | Energy in J (KV) | Energy to initiate crack or NDT | Energy to fracture |
| Typical use | Polymers, metals | Metals, weldments | Thick plates (steels) | Plastics, composites |
| Standard | ASTM D256, IS 8661 | ASTM E23, ISO 148 | ASTM E208 | ASTM D1709, ISO 7765 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Impact Energy Absorbed by the Specimen

**Step 1 — Define the geometry of the pendulum.**
Consider a simple pendulum consisting of a hammer of mass $m$ attached at the end of a rigid arm of length $L$ pivoted about point $O$. The hammer's centre of mass is at distance $L$ from the pivot.

**Step 2 — Write the initial potential energy (PE).**
The pendulum is released from rest at an angle $\alpha$ from the vertical. The vertical height of the hammer above its lowest point is:

$$h_{i} = L - L\cos\alpha = L(1 - \cos\alpha)$$

Therefore, the initial potential energy is:

$$U_{i} = mgh_{i} = mgL(1 - \cos\alpha)$$

**Step 3 — Convert all PE to KE at the lowest point.**
By conservation of mechanical energy, just before impact, the entire initial PE is converted into kinetic energy:

$$KE_{\text{just before}} = mgL(1 - \cos\alpha) = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

This gives the striking velocity:

$$v = \sqrt{2gL(1 - \cos\alpha)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply energy conservation at the end of the swing.**
After breaking the specimen, the pendulum rises on the opposite side to a recovery angle $\beta$. The remaining kinetic energy is converted back to potential energy at height $h_{f} = L(1 - \cos\beta)$:

$$U_{f} = mgL(1 - \cos\beta)$$

**Step 5 — Equate and isolate the energy lost.**
The kinetic energy **just before** impact must equal the kinetic energy **just after** plus the energy absorbed by the specimen:

$$mgL(1 - \cos\alpha) = mgL(1 - \cos\beta) + A$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{A = mgL(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the impact strength.**
For a specimen of cross-sectional area $A_{0}$ at the notch, the **notch impact strength** is:

$$a_{k} = \frac{A}{A_{0}} = \frac{mgL(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}{A_{0}}$$

Typical units: $\text{J/cm}^{2}$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem [Self-Practice]:** A Charpy impact test is performed on a steel specimen. The pendulum mass is $m = 22\,\text{kg}$, arm length $L = 0.85\,\text{m}$, initial angle $\alpha = 135^{\circ}$, and the recovery angle after fracture is $\beta = 88^{\circ}$. The cross-section at the notch is $10\,\text{mm} \times 8\,\text{mm}$ with a V-notch of depth $2\,\text{mm}$. Calculate (a) the impact energy absorbed, and (b) the Charpy V-notch impact strength. Use $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^{2}$.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

*Part (a): Energy absorbed*

Convert angles to radians: $\alpha = 135^{\circ} = 2.356\,\text{rad}$, $\beta = 88^{\circ} = 1.536\,\text{rad}$.

$$\cos\alpha = \cos(135^{\circ}) = -0.7071$$
$$\cos\beta = \cos(88^{\circ}) = 0.0349$$

Substitute into the energy equation:

$$A = (22)(9.81)(0.85)(\cos 88^{\circ} - \cos 135^{\circ})$$

$$A = (22)(9.81)(0.85)\big(0.0349 - (-0.7071)\big)$$

$$A = 183.4 \times 0.7420 = 136.1\,\text{J}$$

*Part (b): Impact strength*

Remaining cross-section at the notch:

$$A_{0} = (10\,\text{mm} - 2\,\text{mm}) \times 8\,\text{mm} = 8\,\text{mm} \times 8\,\text{mm} = 64\,\text{mm}^{2} = 0.64\,\text{cm}^{2}$$

Therefore:

$$KCV = \frac{A}{A_{0}} = \frac{136.1}{0.64} = 212.7\,\text{J/cm}^{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value (≈ 213 J/cm²) is characteristic of a **tough low-carbon steel** at room temperature. A value below **27 J/cm²** at 20 °C would indicate a brittle material.

## 3.3 Ductile-to-Brittle Transition (DBT) — Concept

Many Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) materials (e.g., mild steel, ferritic stainless steel used in wheelchair frames) exhibit a sharp **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)**. Below this temperature, the material fractures with very low energy absorption (cleavage fracture); above it, the material absorbs large amounts of energy (ductile fracture with significant plastic deformation).

The DBT curve is plotted as:

$$a_{k} \;(\text{J/cm}^{2})\;\;\text{vs.}\;\; T \;(^{\circ}\text{C})$$

A characteristic "S-shaped" or sigmoidal curve results. The DBTT is conventionally defined as the **average** of the upper-shelf (ductile) and lower-shelf (brittle) energy values:

$$DBTT = \frac{T_{\text{upper}} + T_{\text{lower}}}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for Rehab Engineering:** A wheelchair user in a cold climate (e.g., Scandinavia, Kashmir winter) may have a frame that suddenly becomes brittle below the DBTT. Material selection must ensure DBTT is **well below the lowest expected service temperature** (typically $-20\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $-40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Working Principle of Pendulum Impact Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Raise Pendulum to Angle alpha"] --> B["Latch Pendulum at Height h1 = L(1-cos alpha)"]
    B --> C["Release Latch; Pendulum Swings Down"]
    C --> D["At Lowest Point, KE max = mgh1; Striking Velocity v = sqrt(2gh1)"]
    D --> E["Hammer Strikes Notched Specimen"]
    E --> F["Specimen Absorbs Energy A = mgL(cos beta - cos alpha)"]
    F --> G["Pendulum Rises to Recovery Angle beta"]
    G --> H["Dial Pointer Reads Energy A Directly in Joules"]
    H --> I["Compute Impact Strength ak = A divided by Notch Area A0"]
    I --> J["Report Result in J per cm squared with Test Temperature"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Instrumented Impact Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Pendulum Hammer with Strain-Gauge Load Cell"] --> B["Amplifier and Signal Conditioner"]
    B --> C["Digital Storage Oscilloscope / Data Logger"]
    C --> D["Force Time Curve F vs t"]
    C --> E["Displacement Time Curve delta vs t"]
    D --> F["Energy = Integral of F d delta from 0 to f"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Plot Impact Energy vs Temperature to get DBT Curve"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Comparison: Izod vs. Charpy Specimen Mounting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Choose Test Type"] --> B["Izod: Vertical Cantilever"]
    A --> C["Charpy: Horizontal Simply-Supported"]
    B --> B1["Specimen clamped vertically; notch at top, facing striker"]
    B1 --> B2["Hammer strikes 22 mm above notch; cantilever bending"]
    C --> C1["Specimen rests on two anvils 40 mm apart; notch in middle, opposite to striker"]
    C1 --> C2["Hammer strikes centre on tension face; three-point bending"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **Izod** test, only the **notched portion** of the specimen is broken; the clamp holds the un-notched upper section. In the **Charpy** test, the specimen is supported at both ends and broken in the middle — this is the **three-point bend** configuration.

## 4.4 Typical DBT Curve (Block-Level Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Lower Shelf: Brittle Cleavage, ak less than 27 J per cm squared"] --> B["Transition Region: Mixed Mode Fracture, S-Shaped Curve"]
    B --> C["Upper Shelf: Ductile Microvoid Coalescence, ak approx 200 to 300 J per cm squared"]
    C --> D["DBTT = Average of Upper and Lower Shelf Temperatures"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define impact strength. Mention its SI unit and explain why it is a critical parameter in selecting materials for prosthetic limb components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Impact strength is the ability of a material to absorb energy and undergo plastic deformation under a suddenly applied load before fracture.
- **SI unit (0.5 mark):** Joules per square metre (J/m²) or more commonly Joules per square centimetre (J/cm²) when a notched specimen is used.
- **Importance in prosthetics (1.5 marks):** Prosthetic sockets and pylon systems experience sudden shock loads during heel-strike and stumbling. A material with low impact strength would fail catastrophically, injuring the amputee. Hence, materials such as carbon-fibre reinforced polymers, titanium alloys, and high-toughness aluminium alloys are preferred, and their impact strength must be verified per ISO 10328.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Izod and Charpy impact tests in terms of specimen mounting and the way the notch is oriented.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Feature | Izod Test | Charpy Test |
|---|---|---|
| Specimen position | Vertical, clamped at one end (cantilever) | Horizontal, supported on two anvils (simply supported) |
| Notch position | At the upper end, **facing** the striker | At the centre, on the side **opposite** to the striker |
| Loading mode | Cantilever bending | Three-point bending |
| Standard | ASTM D256 / IS 8661 | ASTM E23 / ISO 148 |

(Allocate 1 mark for each major point; 3 marks total.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Answer ANY ONE FULL QUESTION from the choice.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **Charpy impact test** in detail. Mention the standard specimen dimensions, the role of the pendulum, and how impact energy is measured. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Principle (1.5 marks):** A standard V-notched or U-notched specimen is supported horizontally as a simply-supported beam between two rigid anvils (span = 40 mm). A pendulum of known mass $m$ and arm length $L$ is released from a known initial angle $\alpha$ (typically 135°), swings down, and strikes the specimen at the midpoint on the face **opposite** to the notch. The energy absorbed in fracturing the specimen is read directly from the dial as the difference between the initial and final potential energy of the pendulum.

2. **Standard Specimen (1.5 marks):** Per ASTM E23, the Charpy V-notch specimen is $55\,\text{mm} \times 10\,\text{mm} \times 10\,\text{mm}$ with a $45^{\circ}$ V-notch of depth $2\,\text{mm}$ and root radius $0.25\,\text{mm}$. The U-notch specimen uses a $5\,\text{mm}$ deep U-notch with $1\,\text{mm}$ root radius. The striker's centre of impact is $22\,\text{mm}$ above the notch.

3. **Role of the Pendulum (1.5 marks):** The pendulum acts as both the energy source and the energy-measuring device. The mass $m$ and length $L$ are calibrated such that the energy stored at the release angle is precisely known (e.g., 300 J or 150 J pendulums are common). The pointer on the dial drags against the pendulum and remains at the highest point reached, directly indicating the energy absorbed.

4. **Energy Equation and Calculation (1.5 marks):** The absorbed energy is given by:
$$A = mgL(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$$
where $\beta$ is the recovery angle on the opposite side. The Charpy V-notch impact strength is:
$$KCV = \frac{A}{A_{0}}\,\text{J/cm}^{2}$$
where $A_{0}$ is the cross-sectional area beneath the notch. (Diagram: simple beam supported on two anvils with central striker.)

5. **Application (1 mark):** Widely used for quality control of steels, weldments, and for DBT curve generation.

---

**(b)** What is the **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition (DBT)**? Sketch a typical DBT curve and explain the significance of DBTT for materials used in **wheelchair and prosthetic components** in cold-climate regions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition of DBT (1.5 marks):** The Ductile-to-Brittle Transition is the phenomenon exhibited by BCC materials (e.g., mild steel, ferritic stainless steel) in which the mode of fracture changes from ductile (high-energy microvoid coalescence) at higher temperatures to brittle (low-energy cleavage) at lower temperatures. The temperature at which this transition occurs is called the **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)**.

2. **DBT Curve Sketch (2 marks):** The curve plots impact strength $a_{k}$ (y-axis) vs. test temperature $T$ (x-axis). It is a sigmoidal "S-shaped" curve with three regions:
   - **Lower shelf** (very low temperature): $a_{k} \approx 5$–$20\,\text{J/cm}^{2}$, brittle cleavage fracture.
   - **Transition region:** Mixed mode, sharp drop in energy over a narrow temperature range.
   - **Upper shelf** (high temperature): $a_{k} \approx 200$–$300\,\text{J/cm}^{2}$, fully ductile fracture.

3. **Significance for Wheelchair and Prosthetic Components (2 marks):** In cold regions (sub-zero service temperatures), a steel or aluminium wheelchair frame that is ductile at 25 °C may suddenly become brittle and fail by cleavage below the DBTT. Hence, materials must be selected such that the **service temperature is well above the DBTT** (typically a safety margin of 20–30 °C). For extreme cold climates, austenitic stainless steel (FCC, no DBT) or non-metallic composites (carbon-fibre) are preferred.

4. **Rehabilitation Engineering Standards (1 mark):** ISO 7176-8 (wheelchairs) and ISO 10328 (prosthetic components) require impact testing at multiple temperatures to verify sub-zero performance.

5. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** DBTT is a critical design parameter in assistive-device engineering, especially for users operating in cold or alpine environments.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **energy absorbed by a specimen in a pendulum impact test**. A pendulum of mass $18\,\text{kg}$ and arm length $0.80\,\text{m}$ is raised to $140^{\circ}$ and released. After breaking a Charpy specimen, it rises to $85^{\circ}$. Calculate the energy absorbed. Take $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^{2}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Derivation (4 marks):**
   - Initial height above lowest point: $h_{i} = L(1 - \cos\alpha)$.
   - Initial potential energy: $U_{i} = mgL(1 - \cos\alpha)$.
   - After fracture, height on opposite side: $h_{f} = L(1 - \cos\beta)$.
   - Final potential energy: $U_{f} = mgL(1 - \cos\beta)$.
   - By energy conservation: $U_{i} = U_{f} + A$.
   - Hence, the energy absorbed is:
$$\boxed{A = mgL(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}$$

2. **Calculation (3 marks):**
   - $m = 18\,\text{kg}$, $L = 0.80\,\text{m}$, $\alpha = 140^{\circ}$, $\beta = 85^{\circ}$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^{2}$.
   - $\cos(140^{\circ}) = -0.7660$.
   - $\cos(85^{\circ}) = 0.0872$.
   - $A = (18)(9.81)(0.80)\big(0.0872 - (-0.7660)\big)$.
   - $A = 141.3 \times 0.8532 = 120.5\,\text{J}$.

   **Answer:** The energy absorbed by the specimen is approximately **120.5 J**.

---

**(b)** Compare the **Izod and Charpy impact tests** in a tabular form covering at least six points. Briefly describe the **falling-dart impact test** and its application in testing polymer components used in rehabilitation devices. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Tabular Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Izod Test | Charpy Test |
|---|---|---|
| Specimen orientation | Vertical | Horizontal |
| Mounting | Cantilever (one end clamped) | Simply supported on two anvils |
| Notch position | At the clamped end, facing striker | At centre, opposite to striker |
| Striking direction | Lateral (horizontal hammer) | Lateral (horizontal hammer) |
| Result notation | $a_{kU}$ or $a_{kV}$ | $KU$ or $KV$ (J); $KCU$ or $KCV$ (J/cm²) |
| Common standards | ASTM D256, IS 8661 | ASTM E23, ISO 148 |
| Typical use | Polymers, plastics | Metals, weldments, steels |
| Span of supports | N/A (clamped) | 40 mm |

2. **Falling-Dart Impact Test (2.5 marks):**
   - **Principle:** A weighted dart (hemispherical or conical tip) is dropped from a known height onto a circular specimen of plastic film/sheet clamped between two rings. The drop height is increased incrementally (staircase method) until 50% of specimens fracture — this is the **failure energy** (or mean fracture energy).
   - **Standards:** ASTM D1709, ISO 7765.
   - **Application in Rehabilitation Engineering (0.5 mark):** Used to test polymer sheets and laminates used in prosthetic cosmetic covers, orthotic shells, flexible socket liners, and inflatable cushion materials.

3. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Each test is suited to a specific material and component geometry; proper selection ensures realistic simulation of in-service impact.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract notch area correctly:** Many students compute impact strength as $A/A_{\text{total}}$ instead of $A/A_{\text{notch}}$. Always use the area **under the notch** ($A_0$).
> 2. **Angle-unit error:** Plugging degrees directly into a calculator set to radians (or vice versa) gives wildly wrong answers. Always convert: $1\,\text{rad} = 57.296^{\circ}$.
> 3. **Confusing $KU$ with $KCU$:** $KU$ is energy (J), $KCU$ is strength (J/cm²). Examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing unit.
> 4. **Wrong notch orientation in Izod:** The notch must face the striker; otherwise the specimen bends the wrong way. KTU diagrams must clearly show this.
> 5. **Plotting DBT curve with the wrong axis:** Always plot $a_k$ on the **y-axis** and $T$ on the **x-axis**; otherwise the curve will be marked wrong.
> 6. **Omitting ISO/ASTM standards:** Examiners often award a **0.5 mark** simply for naming the relevant standard (ISO 10328 for prosthetics, ISO 148 for Charpy). Don't skip it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Impact strength** = Energy absorbed ÷ Cross-sectional area at the notch. Unit: **J/cm²** (notched) or **J/m²** (un-notched).
- ✅ **Pendulum energy equation:** $A = mgL(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$ — derive from PE = KE conversion.
- ✅ **Izod = Vertical, cantilever, notch faces striker.** **Charpy = Horizontal, simply-supported, notch opposite to striker.**
- ✅ **Standards:** Charpy → ISO 148 / ASTM E23; Izod → ASTM D256 / IS 8661; Falling-Dart → ISO 7765 / ASTM D1709.
- ✅ **DBT curve** is S-shaped: lower shelf (brittle) → transition → upper shelf (ductile). DBTT = average of the two shelf temperatures.
- ✅ **BCC metals** (mild steel) have a clear DBTT; **FCC metals** (austenitic SS, aluminium) and **polymers** generally do not.
- ✅ For **rehabilitation components**, the service temperature must be **above DBTT** with a margin of at least **20–30 °C**.
- ✅ **ISO 10328** governs prosthetic structural testing; **ISO 7176-8** governs wheelchair impact/durability.
- ✅ **Striking velocity** $v = \sqrt{2gL(1-\cos\alpha)}$ — independent of the pendulum mass.
- ✅ If the pendulum does not break the specimen, the test is **invalid**; use a heavier pendulum or sharper notch.
- ✅ Always report **notch type** (V or U), **temperature**, and **striking energy** in the result.
- ✅ KTU examiners reward **neat, labelled diagrams** (1–1.5 marks reserved) — always include a labelled figure of the Izod/Charpy setup.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue Strength Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fatigue Strength Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fatigue Strength Testing** in Rehabilitation Engineering refers to a class of standardized mechanical evaluations that subject prosthetic, orthotic, and assistive devices (such as lower-limb prostheses, wheelchairs, crutches, and orthotic joints) to **cyclic, fluctuating, or repeated loading** for a pre-defined number of cycles (typically 10⁶ to 10⁷ cycles) to determine their **endurance limit, fatigue life, structural durability, and safe service life** under physiological gait loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must understand fatigue testing in the context of **ISO 10328** (Prosthetics — Structural testing of lower-limb prostheses) and the evaluation of prosthetic/orthotic components under simulated body-weight and gait-cycle loads.

Mathematically, fatigue strength (\(S_f\)) is the maximum cyclic stress amplitude a material or component can withstand for a given number of cycles (\(N\)) **without failure**:

$$S_f = f(N, R, \text{environment}, \text{geometry})$$

where \(R = \dfrac{\sigma_{\min}}{\sigma_{\max}}\) is the **stress ratio**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bending Paperclip" Intuition

Imagine you take a standard steel paperclip and bend it gently back and forth. After **1 or 2 bends**, nothing happens. After **20–30 bends**, you see a tiny crack. After **40 bends**, it snaps — even though the force you applied each time was **far below** the force needed to break it in a single pull.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Insight:** Fatigue failure happens at stresses **far below** the static ultimate tensile strength (UTS). A prosthetic knee joint that can hold a static load of 150 kg might still fail after 1 million walking cycles at 80 kg. This is why fatigue testing is **not optional** in rehabilitation device certification.

In rehabilitation engineering, a transfemoral (above-knee) amputee takes roughly **1.0 to 1.8 million steps per year**. A prosthetic knee must therefore survive **millions of load cycles** — not just one. Fatigue tests simulate this real-world cyclic demand in a compressed laboratory timeframe.

---

## 1.3 Standardized Metrics in Bold

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Prosthetics) |
|---|---|---|
| Body-weight load multiplier | **P-level** | **P5 = 1.25 kN to P8 = 1.78 kN** |
| Test cycles per phase | **N** | **1 × 10⁶ to 5 × 10⁶ cycles** |
| Cyclic frequency | **f** | **1 Hz to 3 Hz** |
| Stress ratio | **R** | **0.1 (tension-tension)** or **−1 (fully reversed)** |
| Endurance limit (steel) | **Sₑ** | **0.5 × UTS** |
| Endurance limit (aluminum/Ti) | **Sₑ** | **No true limit; defined at 10⁷ cycles** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always state the relevant **ISO 10328 P-level** when describing a fatigue test on a lower-limb prosthesis. Examiners specifically look for this terminology.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of Fatigue in Rehabilitation Devices

### Why Fatigue Matters in Prosthetics & Orthotics

A rehabilitation device experiences **dynamic gait loading** rather than a constant static load. The following factors create fatigue-prone conditions:

1. **Hee-strike impact** → sudden axial spike (≈ 1.2–1.5 × body weight).
2. **Mid-stance loading** → sustained axial load.
3. **Toe-off push** → bending moment at the pylon-residuum interface.
4. **Stair descent / kneeling** → combined axial + bending + torsional loading.
5. **Corrosion-fatigue synergy** → sweat, urine, and humid climates accelerate crack initiation.

### How the Test is Structured (ISO 10328 Framework)

The test is a **two-stage validation**:

- **Stage 1 — Static Proof Test (quasi-static):** The device is loaded to **1.5 × the prescribed P-level** and held for 30 seconds. No permanent deformation is allowed. This is **not** a fatigue test — it is a *yielding* check.
- **Stage 2 — Cyclic Fatigue Test:** The device is then subjected to sinusoidal loading between a **lower bound** (≈ 0.1 × P-level) and the **upper bound (= P-level)**, for **at least 1 × 10⁶ cycles** (for certification up to 18 months use) or **3 × 10⁶ cycles** (for lifetime certification).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stress Amplitude** | $$\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2}$$ | Fatigue stress analysis |
| 2 | **Mean Stress** | $$\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2}$$ | Mean-stress correction |
| 3 | **Stress Ratio** | $$R = \dfrac{\sigma_{\min}}{\sigma_{\max}}$$ | Loading characterization |
| 4 | **Basquin's Law (S-N curve, high-cycle)** | $$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \, (2N)^b$$ | Enduring-life prediction |
| 5 | **Coffin-Manson (low-cycle, N < 10³)** | $$\dfrac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon_f' \, (2N)^c$$ | Plastic-strain fatigue |
| 6 | **Goodman mean-stress correction** | $$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$$ | Asymmetric loading |
| 7 | **Soderberg (more conservative)** | $$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = 1$$ | Yield-safe design |
| 8 | **Miner's Cumulative Damage Rule** | $$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{n_i}{N_i} = 1$$ | Variable-amplitude fatigue |
| 9 | **Endurance Limit (Steels)** | $$S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut} \quad \text{(for } S_{ut} < 1400 \text{ MPa)}$$ | Rotating-beam baseline |
| 10 | **Modification Factors** | $$S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \, S_e'$$ | Marin surface factors |
| 11 | **ISO 10328 P-level (kN)** | $$P = (0.125 \cdot n) \text{ kN}, \quad n = 1 \text{ to } 8$$ | Prosthesis classification |
| 12 | **Bending stress in pylon** | $$\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$$ | Tube pylon bending fatigue |
| 13 | **Axial stress in pylon** | $$\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^2}$$ | Tube pylon axial fatigue |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to Memorise for KTU Exams:**
> - **ISO 10328 P-levels:** P5 = 1.25 kN, P6 = 1.50 kN, P7 = 1.75 kN, P8 = 1.78 kN (sometimes standardized to 1.25, 1.50, 1.75, 1.78 kN).
> - **Steel endurance limit factor:** **0.5** of UTS.
> - **Default stress ratio for gait simulation:** **R = 0.1** (tension-tension).

---

## 2.3 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — Visualized Conceptually

The S-N curve is the **fundamental output** of a fatigue strength test. It plots **stress amplitude (\(\sigma_a\))** on the y-axis (linear or log) versus **number of cycles to failure (\(N\))** on the x-axis (always log).

For **ferrous steels and titanium**, the curve becomes **horizontal** beyond ~10⁶ cycles, defining the **endurance limit \(S_e\)**.

For **aluminum, composites, and polymers** (common in modern prosthetics — carbon-fiber pylons, polymer sockets), the curve **never becomes horizontal**; designers use a **"fatigue strength at N cycles"** value, e.g., \(S_{10^7}\).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Fatigue Test Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Transfemoral prosthetic knee** | Verify 3 million cycles of swing-phase bending |
| **Transtibial pylon tube** | Verify axial + bending fatigue under heel-strike shock |
| **Wheelchair frame (folding cross-brace)** | Verify 100,000 cycles of opening/closing |
| **Cane / crutch tip** | Verify 50,000 cycles of ground-contact impact |
| **Spinal orthosis (TLSO)** | Verify sustained-load creep + dynamic flex |
| **Hip disarticulation socket** | Verify interface stress under cyclic socket-piston motion |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Basquin's Equation (The Heart of S-N Fatigue)

Basquin's law expresses the linear relationship (on log-log axes) between stress amplitude and cycles to failure in the **high-cycle fatigue regime (N > 10³)**.

### Starting Point

We assume that, on a **log-log** plot of \(\sigma_a\) vs. \(N\), the relationship is linear:

$$\log(\sigma_a) = \log(C) - m \log(N)$$

where \(C\) and \(m\) are material constants.

### Step 1 — Convert from log to exponential form

By definition of the logarithm, this becomes:

$$\sigma_a = C \cdot N^{-m}$$

### Step 2 — Introduce the fatigue strength coefficient

We define two reference points on the curve:
- At **N = 1/2 cycle** (i.e., \(2N = 1\)), the stress is the **fatigue strength coefficient** \(\sigma_f'\) (approximately equal to the true fracture strength \(\sigma_f\)).
- The slope of the log-log line is \(-b\) (the **fatigue strength exponent**).

Re-parameterize using \(\sigma_f'\) and \(b\):

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \, (2N)^b$$

### Step 3 — Verify boundary conditions

At \(2N = 1\) (i.e., one reversal):

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot 1^b = \sigma_f' \quad \checkmark$$

For steels, \(b \approx -0.10\) to \(-0.12\). For aluminum alloys, \(b \approx -0.06\) to \(-0.08\). For carbon-fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP) pylons, \(b\) is typically much shallower (\(-0.03\) to \(-0.05\)) — explaining why CFRP lasts longer in cyclic service.

### Step 4 — Express cycles in terms of stress (the design form)

Solving for \(N\):

$$N = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{\sigma_f'}{\sigma_a} \right)^{1/b}$$

This form is used by KTU exam problems where you are given an allowable stress and asked to find the predicted fatigue life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Typical Question Pattern:** "A pylon material has \(\sigma_f' = 1200\) MPa and \(b = -0.10\). If the design stress amplitude is 250 MPa, predict the fatigue life in cycles." — Use the design form above.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Miner's Cumulative Damage Rule

In real gait, the load is **not** a single constant amplitude. The amputee walks on flat ground, climbs stairs, descends ramps, stumbles, and rests. Each of these has a different stress level. Miner's rule aggregates the partial damage.

### Step 1 — Define damage per cycle

If a component at stress level \(\sigma_{a,i}\) survives \(N_i\) cycles, then **one cycle causes fractional damage**:

$$d_i = \dfrac{1}{N_i}$$

### Step 2 — Sum damage over a loading spectrum

For \(k\) different stress blocks, each of duration \(n_i\) cycles:

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i \cdot n_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{n_i}{N_i}$$

### Step 3 — Failure criterion

Failure is predicted when:

$$D = 1$$

If \(D < 1\), the device has remaining life. If \(D > 1\), the device is predicted to have failed already.

### Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A prosthetic pylon is tested in two blocks. Block 1: 200,000 cycles at \(\sigma_{a,1} = 300\) MPa. Block 2: 800,000 cycles at \(\sigma_{a,2} = 200\) MPa. Given the S-N relation \(\sigma_a = 1000 \cdot (2N)^{-0.1}\) MPa, predict cumulative damage.

**Solution:**

Step (a) — Find \(N_1\) corresponding to \(\sigma_{a,1} = 300\) MPa:

$$300 = 1000 \cdot (2N_1)^{-0.1} \implies (2N_1)^{-0.1} = 0.3$$

$$\log(0.3) = -0.1 \cdot \log(2N_1) \implies \log(2N_1) = 5.223$$

$$2N_1 = 10^{5.223} = 1.671 \times 10^5 \implies N_1 \approx 83{,}560 \text{ cycles}$$

Step (b) — Find \(N_2\) corresponding to \(\sigma_{a,2} = 200\) MPa:

$$200 = 1000 \cdot (2N_2)^{-0.1} \implies (2N_2)^{-0.1} = 0.2$$

$$\log(0.2) = -0.1 \cdot \log(2N_2) \implies \log(2N_2) = 6.989$$

$$2N_2 = 10^{6.989} = 9.75 \times 10^6 \implies N_2 \approx 4.876 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles}$$

Step (c) — Apply Miner's rule:

$$D = \dfrac{n_1}{N_1} + \dfrac{n_2}{N_2} = \dfrac{200{,}000}{83{,}560} + \dfrac{800{,}000}{4.876 \times 10^6}$$

$$D = 2.394 + 0.164 = 2.558$$

Step (d) — **Interpretation:** Since \(D = 2.558 > 1\), the pylon is **predicted to fail** before completing the second loading block. Designers must either reduce the peak stress amplitude (Block 1) or stiffen the pylon to extend \(N_1\).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often write \(N\) directly into Miner's equation without first computing it from the S-N curve. Marks are deducted for missing this critical step.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Goodman Line (Mean-Stress Correction)

Most fatigue data is taken at \(R = -1\) (fully reversed, mean stress = 0). But gait loading is **tension-tension** (\(R = 0.1\)), meaning there is a **positive mean stress**. The Goodman line lets us correct.

### Step 1 — Define the corrected allowable alternating stress

$$\sigma_{ar} = \sigma_a \left[ 1 - \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \right]^{-1}$$

### Step 2 — Equivalent fully-reversed stress

Set the corrected value equal to the endurance limit:

$$\sigma_a = S_e \left[ 1 - \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \right]$$

This is the **Goodman relation**. It says: a mean tensile stress **reduces** the allowable alternating stress.

### Worked Example

A prosthetic knee pylon made of Ti-6Al-4V has \(S_{ut} = 950\) MPa and \(S_e = 500\) MPa. Gait loading gives \(\sigma_{\max} = 400\) MPa, \(\sigma_{\min} = 40\) MPa.

Compute \(\sigma_m\) and \(\sigma_a\):

$$\sigma_m = \dfrac{400 + 40}{2} = 220 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_a = \dfrac{400 - 40}{2} = 180 \text{ MPa}$$

Apply Goodman:

$$\sigma_{a,\text{allowable}} = 500 \left[ 1 - \dfrac{220}{950} \right] = 500 \times 0.768 = 384 \text{ MPa}$$

Since the actual \(\sigma_a = 180\) MPa \(<\) 384 MPa, the pylon is **safe** under mean-stress-corrected Goodman fatigue. Factor of safety:

$$n = \dfrac{384}{180} = 2.13$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept Tie-In:** This is exactly how ISO 10328 evaluates a prosthesis — by using a mean-stress-corrected allowable stress, then comparing to the cyclic test result.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Fatigue Formula

| Loading Condition | Use This Equation |
|---|---|
| Fully reversed (\(R = -1\)), steel | Direct \(S_e\) |
| Tension-tension (\(R = 0.1\)), ductile metal | **Goodman** or **Soderberg** |
| Low-cycle (\(N < 10^3\)) | **Coffin-Manson** |
| High-cycle (\(N > 10^3\)) | **Basquin** |
| Variable amplitude | **Miner's rule** |
| Notched component | **Neuber's rule** + stress concentration \(K_t\) |
| Surface-finish-sensitive | **Marin modification factor \(k_a\)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — ISO 10328 Fatigue Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Prosthetic Device] --> B[Visual Inspection and Dimensional Check]
    B --> C[Stage 1: Static Proof Test]
    C --> C1[Apply 1.5 times P-level for 30 seconds]
    C1 --> C2{Permanent Deformation?}
    C2 -->|Yes| C3[FAIL: Reject Device]
    C2 -->|No| D[Stage 2: Cyclic Fatigue Test]
    D --> D1[Set P-level per ISO 10328 P5 to P8]
    D1 --> D2[Set cycle count 1e6 to 3e6]
    D2 --> D3[Set frequency 1 to 3 Hz]
    D3 --> D4[Run sinusoidal axial plus bending load]
    D4 --> E{Monitors: Crack, Loosening, Permanent Set?}
    E -->|Yes| E1[FAIL: Record cycles to failure]
    E -->|No| F[Endurance Threshold Reached]
    F --> G[Issue Test Certificate and Mark P-level on Device]
    G --> H[End: Device Cleared for Clinical Use]
    C3 --> H
    E1 --> H
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Prosthesis Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Subsystem]
        I1[Hydraulic Actuator 1 to 50 kN]
        I2[Servo Motor for Bending]
        I3[Function Generator Sinusoidal Waveform]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL[Control Subsystem]
        C1[PID Controller]
        C2[Load Cell Feedback]
        C3[Displacement LVDT]
    end
    subgraph SPECIMEN[Test Specimen]
        S1[Prosthetic Pylon and Knee Assembly]
        S2[Strain Gauges on Pylon]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Subsystem]
        O1[DAQ and Data Logger]
        O2[S-N Curve Generator]
        O3[Crack Detection Microscope]
        O4[Failure Mode Classifier]
    end
    I3 --> C1
    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    C1 --> S1
    S1 --> C2
    S1 --> C3
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> O1
    C2 --> O1
    C3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    S1 --> O3
    O3 --> O4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — S-N Curve Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Run Fatigue Test at Multiple Stress Levels] --> B[Record Cycles to Failure at Each Level]
    B --> C[Plot Stress Amplitude sigma_a on Y axis]
    B --> D[Plot Cycles to Failure N on X axis, Log Scale]
    C --> E[Fit Line in log log Space]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Material Type?}
    F -->|Steel or Titanium| G[Curve Plateaus at Endurance Limit Se]
    F -->|Aluminum or Composite| H[Curve Continues to Decline, Use S at 10 million]
    G --> I[Use Se Directly in Design]
    H --> J[Use S 10 million as Design Fatigue Strength]
```
