# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Concept Definition `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define fatigue strength test. Why is it mandatory for lower-limb prostheses under ISO 10328?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A fatigue strength test is a **standardized cyclic loading evaluation** in which a prosthetic or orthotic device is subjected to repeated loads (typically between 0.1 × P-level and the P-level itself) for **at least 1 × 10⁶ cycles** to determine its **endurance life and structural integrity**.
>
> It is mandatory because an above-knee amputee performs roughly **1.0 to 1.8 million gait cycles per year**. Without fatigue validation, the device could fail catastrophically mid-walk, causing falls and re-injury. **ISO 10328** mandates both a **static proof test (1.5 × P-level for 30 s)** and a **cyclic test (P-level, ≥ 10⁶ cycles)** as acceptance criteria for clinical release.
>
> **(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark ISO reference + 1 mark cycle count justification)**

---

### Question 2 — Endurance Limit Concept `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the endurance limit? Why is it not defined for aluminum alloys used in modern prosthetics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **endurance limit \(S_e\)** is the stress amplitude below which a material can theoretically survive an **infinite number of cycles** (typically taken as \(10^6\) to \(10^7\) cycles). For steels, \(S_e \approx 0.5 \cdot S_{ut}\).
>
> Aluminum and most non-ferrous alloys (including titanium in some references) do **not** show a sharp knee in the S-N curve — they continue to degrade even at very high cycle counts. Therefore, designers must specify a **fatigue strength at a fixed cycle count**, e.g., **\(S_{10^7}\)** — the stress amplitude giving 10 million cycles of life.
>
> **(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark value/relation + 1 mark aluminum justification)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B pattern: each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is **mandatory** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Question]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **S-N curve** (Wöhler curve) concept, the **Basquin equation**, and the procedure to determine the **endurance limit** of a prosthetic pylon material. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A titanium pylon for a transfemoral prosthesis is subjected to a **two-block variable-amplitude loading** during a simulated gait test. The material has Basquin parameters \(\sigma_f' = 1100\) MPa and \(b = -0.09\). The two blocks are:

| Block | Stress Amplitude \(\sigma_a\) | Applied Cycles \(n\) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 350 MPa | 150,000 |
| 2 | 220 MPa | 700,000 |

Using **Miner's cumulative damage rule**, predict whether the pylon will survive. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Step 1 — S-N Curve Concept (2 marks):**
The S-N curve is a plot of **alternating stress amplitude** (\(\sigma_a\)) versus **number of cycles to failure** (\(N\)) on a semi-log or log-log scale. It is generated by testing multiple identical specimens at different stress levels and recording the cycles at which each fails.

**Step 2 — Basquin Equation (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N)^b$$

where \(\sigma_f'\) is the fatigue strength coefficient and \(b\) is the fatigue strength exponent (negative). For steels, \(b \approx -0.10\); for Ti-6Al-4V, \(b \approx -0.08\) to \(-0.09\).

**Step 3 — Procedure to Determine Endurance Limit (3 marks):**
1. Test 8–10 specimens at decreasing stress levels using a rotating-beam or axial-loading fatigue rig.
2. Record cycles to failure for each.
3. Plot the S-N curve on a log-log scale.
4. For ferrous metals, identify the **knee** of the curve (typically at \(10^6\) to \(10^7\) cycles).
5. The horizontal asymptote below the knee is the **endurance limit \(S_e\)**.
6. Apply **Marin modification factors** (\(k_a, k_b, k_c\)) to correct for surface finish, size, loading type, temperature, and reliability.
7. The corrected value \(S_e = k_a k_b k_c \cdot S_e'\) is the **modified endurance limit** used in design.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute \(N_1\) for Block 1 (3 marks):**

$$350 = 1100 \cdot (2N_1)^{-0.09}$$

$$(2N_1)^{-0.09} = \dfrac{350}{1100} = 0.3182$$

$$\log(0.3182) = -0.09 \cdot \log(2N_1)$$

$$-0.4975 = -0.09 \cdot \log(2N_1)$$

$$\log(2N_1) = 5.528$$

$$2N_1 = 10^{5.528} = 3.371 \times 10^5$$

$$N_1 = 1.686 \times 10^5 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 2 — Compute \(N_2\) for Block 2 (2 marks):**

$$220 = 1100 \cdot (2N_2)^{-0.09}$$

$$(2N_2)^{-0.09} = 0.2$$

$$\log(0.2) = -0.09 \cdot \log(2N_2)$$

$$-0.6990 = -0.09 \cdot \log(2N_2)$$

$$\log(2N_2) = 7.766$$

$$2N_2 = 10^{7.766} = 5.834 \times 10^7$$

$$N_2 = 2.917 \times 10^7 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Miner's Rule (2 marks):**

$$D = \dfrac{n_1}{N_1} + \dfrac{n_2}{N_2} = \dfrac{150{,}000}{168{,}600} + \dfrac{700{,}000}{29{,}170{,}000}$$

$$D = 0.8897 + 0.0240 = 0.9137$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (extra credit, 0–1 mark):**

Since \(D = 0.9137 < 1\), the pylon **survives** the two-block loading with a small margin of safety (\(1 - 0.9137 = 8.6\%\) remaining life). The Block 1 loading is the dominant contributor (≈ 97% of damage). A small stress reduction in Block 1 would dramatically improve pylon life.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the **ISO 10328 P-level classification** for lower-limb prostheses, including the static proof test and cyclic fatigue test procedures. Why is the **mean-stress correction (Goodman or Soderberg)** essential for prosthetic design? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A prosthetic knee bolt is made of stainless steel with \(S_{ut} = 860\) MPa and endurance limit \(S_e = 430\) MPa. The gait-cycle loading produces \(\sigma_{\max} = 320\) MPa and \(\sigma_{\min} = 60\) MPa.
- (i) Compute the **mean stress** and **stress amplitude**.
- (ii) Apply the **Goodman correction** and determine the **factor of safety** against fatigue. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Step 1 — ISO 10328 P-level System (3 marks):**

ISO 10328 defines **8 patient weight categories (P1 to P8)** corresponding to body weights from 50 kg to 125+ kg. Each P-level sets the **test load** the prosthesis must withstand:

$$P_n = 0.125 \cdot n \text{ kN} \quad \text{for } n = 1, 2, \ldots, 8$$

Thus **P5 = 1.25 kN (≈ 125 kg patient)**, **P7 = 1.75 kN**, **P8 = 1.78 kN** for the heaviest. The manufacturer selects the device's P-level rating and labels it accordingly.

**Step 2 — Static Proof Test (2 marks):**

The device is loaded axially to **1.5 × P-level** and held for 30 seconds. **No permanent deformation, cracking, or fastener loosening** is permitted. This validates the **static margin of safety**.

**Step 3 — Cyclic Fatigue Test (2 marks):**

The device is then cycled between **0.1 × P-level (lower bound)** and **P-level (upper bound)** at a frequency of 1–3 Hz, for **at least 1 × 10⁶ cycles** (clinical trial clearance) or **3 × 10⁶ cycles** (long-term certification).

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Step 1 — Compute Mean Stress and Stress Amplitude (3 marks):**

$$\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2} = \dfrac{320 + 60}{2} = 190 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2} = \dfrac{320 - 60}{2} = 130 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Goodman Correction (3 marks):**

$$\sigma_{a,\text{allowable}} = S_e \left[ 1 - \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \right] = 430 \left[ 1 - \dfrac{190}{860} \right]$$

$$\sigma_{a,\text{allowable}} = 430 \times (1 - 0.2209) = 430 \times 0.7791 = 335.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Factor of Safety (1 mark):**

$$n = \dfrac{\sigma_{a,\text{allowable}}}{\sigma_a} = \dfrac{335.0}{130} = 2.58$$

**Conclusion:** The bolt is **safe** with a factor of safety of **2.58** under Goodman mean-stress-corrected fatigue.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Fatigue Test Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert between \(\sigma_a\) and \(\sigma_{\max}\):** Many students plug \(\sigma_{\max}\) into the Goodman equation. The correct input is the **alternating stress amplitude \(\sigma_a\)**. **−2 marks.**
> 2. **Missing the Basquin exponent sign:** The exponent \(b\) is **negative**. Forgetting the sign gives the wrong S-N slope. **−1 mark.**
> 3. **Confusing "endurance limit" with "fatigue strength at N cycles":** Steel has an endurance limit; aluminum does **not**. The KTU examiner awards the mark only if you distinguish clearly. **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Not stating the ISO 10328 P-level:** Any question on a lower-limb prosthesis fatigue test must reference **P-level classification**. **−1 mark.**
> 5. **Skipping the Miner's rule intermediate steps:** Showing the computation of \(N_1\) and \(N_2\) is essential; the examiner wants to see the work, not just the final damage number. **−2 marks if missing.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Fatigue failure** occurs at stresses **far below** the static ultimate tensile strength due to **cyclic** loading.
- [x] The **S-N (Wöhler) curve** is the central output of a fatigue test; it plots **stress amplitude** vs. **cycles to failure**.
- [x] **Basquin's law:** \(\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N)^b\) — valid for **high-cycle** fatigue (\(N > 10^3\)).
- [x] **Coffin-Manson** relation handles **low-cycle** (\(N < 10^3\)) plastic-strain fatigue.
- [x] **Endurance limit \(S_e\)** exists for **ferrous steels** (\(\approx 0.5 \cdot S_{ut}\)); for **aluminum and composites**, use **fatigue strength at \(10^7\) cycles**.
- [x] **ISO 10328 P-levels** classify patient weight: \(P_n = 0.125 n\) kN, for \(n = 1\) to 8.
- [x] **ISO 10328 test sequence:** **Static proof (1.5 × P, 30 s) → Cyclic (P, ≥ 10⁶ cycles, 1–3 Hz)**.
- [x] **Goodman correction** adjusts the endurance limit for **non-zero mean stress** (essential for gait loading, \(R = 0.1\)).
- [x] **Miner's rule** predicts cumulative damage from variable-amplitude loading: \(D = \sum n_i / N_i\); failure at \(D = 1\).
- [x] **Marin modification factors** (\(k_a\) surface, \(k_b\) size, \(k_c\) loading, etc.) convert lab-specimen \(S_e'\) to design \(S_e\).
- [x] **Typical prosthetic pylon materials:** Ti-6Al-4V, stainless steel 316L, carbon-fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP), aluminum 7075-T6.
- [x] **Real-world gait cycles:** an active amputee takes **1.0 to 1.8 million steps per year** — the test must reflect this lifetime demand.
- [x] **Two failure modes** in prosthetic fatigue: **(1) crack initiation** at stress concentrations (e.g., bolt holes, pylon taper) and **(2) fastener loosening** at modular interfaces (e.g., pyramid clamps).
- [x] Always report answers with **units (MPa, kN, cycles, Hz)** and **cite ISO 10328** for clinical context.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finite-Element Modelling Applied to Wheelchair Design and Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Finite-Element Modelling Applied to Wheelchair Design and Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Finite-Element Modelling (FEM)** is a numerical computational technique used to obtain approximate solutions to boundary-value problems in engineering. In the context of rehabilitation engineering, FEM is applied to wheelchairs to discretize the complex, non-uniform geometry of frames, cushions, and joints into a finite number of small, well-defined sub-regions (elements). The governing differential equations of solid mechanics (equilibrium, compatibility, and constitutive laws) are solved over each element and assembled into a global system of algebraic equations to predict structural response under physiological loading conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The wheelchair must be modeled in accordance with **ISO 7176-5** (static strength testing) and **ISO 7176-8** (static, impact, and fatigue strength requirements), with the patient-rider's body mass typically distributed as **75 kg – 150 kg** on the seat and back rest nodes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a heavy, curved metal wheelchair frame as a **single large, complex piece of chocolate** that you want to test for cracks when a person sits on it. You cannot easily calculate the stress inside an irregular chocolate shape with a simple formula. So instead, you **melt the chocolate and pour it into hundreds of small, identical Lego-style rectangular and triangular moulds** (these are the *finite elements*). 

Each small mould is simple enough that you can write down the math for how it bends and stretches. Then you **glue all the small moulds back together** in their original curved arrangement, ensuring the edges match perfectly. By solving each small piece and adding up the behavior, you now know exactly where the entire frame will bend, twist, or potentially snap when someone sits on it — without ever having to break a real wheelchair. This is precisely what FEM does for the engineer.

### 1.3 Why FEM is Critical for Wheelchair Design

- **Anatomical Loading Complexity:** The human body does not load a wheelchair uniformly; the ischial tuberosities produce concentrated pressure points.
- **Material Heterogeneity:** Wheelchairs use a mix of **aluminium alloy 6061-T6**, **carbon-fiber composites**, and **titanium** — each with different Young's moduli (**E ≈ 69 GPa**, **200 GPa**, and **110 GPa** respectively).
- **Cost Reduction:** Physical crash-testing dummy trials are expensive; FEM allows **virtual prototyping**.
- **Customization:** Pediatric and bariatric wheelchairs require patient-specific geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** FEM transforms the *continuous* physical wheelchair into a *discrete* mathematical model of nodes and elements, making unsolvable differential equations solvable through linear algebra.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept: The Three Pillars of FEM

1. **Discretization (Pre-processing)**
   - The wheelchair frame CAD geometry is imported into FEM software (ANSYS, Abaqus, or SolidWorks Simulation).
   - The domain $\Omega$ is subdivided into $n$ elements connected at $N$ nodal points.
   - Meshing density is increased at **stress-concentration zones**: weld joints at the cross-brace, seat-tube junctions, and caster forks.

2. **Element Formulation**
   - Each element has a displacement field approximated by **shape functions** $N_i(x, y, z)$.
   - The strain-displacement relationship is written as $\varepsilon = B \cdot d$, where $B$ is the strain-displacement matrix.
   - The stress-strain constitutive law is $\sigma = D \cdot \varepsilon$, where $D$ is the elasticity matrix.

3. **Assembly & Solution (Solver)**
   - Global stiffness matrix $K$ is assembled: $K = \sum_{e=1}^{n} \int_{V_e} B^T D B \, dV$.
   - The governing equation $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$ is solved, subject to boundary conditions (e.g., rear wheels fixed to ground).
   - Post-processing extracts **Von-Mises stress, displacement contours, and factor-of-safety** maps.

### 2.2 Why and How the System Works

- **Why discretize?** Because exact analytical solutions to elasticity equations only exist for simple geometries (rods, plates). Wheelchair tubing has bends, weld zones, and variable cross-sections that have **no closed-form solution**.
- **How does it work?** It uses the **Rayleigh-Ritz variational principle** — minimizing the total potential energy $\Pi$ of the structure:
$$\Pi = \frac{1}{2} \int_{\Omega} \sigma^T \varepsilon \, dV - \int_{\Omega} u^T f \, dV - \int_{\Gamma} u^T T \, dS$$
where $f$ is body force and $T$ is surface traction.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$ | Global equilibrium equation | N, m |
| 2 | $K^e = \int_{V_e} B^T D B \, dV$ | Element stiffness matrix formula | N/m |
| 3 | $\sigma = D \cdot B \cdot d^e$ | Stress recovery at element nodes | Pa |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = B \cdot d^e$ | Strain from nodal displacements | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{ax}}}$ | Poisson's ratio (material property) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\sigma_{\text{vm}} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}$ | Von-Mises equivalent stress | Pa |
| 7 | $FOS = \frac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\sigma_{\text{vm}}}$ | Factor of Safety | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\vert u \vert_{\max} = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I}$ | Max tip deflection of cantilever (rear frame) | m |
| 9 | $h_{\text{elem}} \leq \frac{L}{20}$ | Recommended element size for beam (meshing rule) | m |
| 10 | $M = \int_{\Omega} \rho \cdot N^T N \, dV$ | Consistent mass matrix (modal/dynamic) | kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` for absolute value in any handwritten work** to maintain exam-paper clarity and avoid the pipe symbol being misread as "1" or "I".

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **ISO 7176-8 standard test**, a wheelchair is loaded with a **130 kg surrogate occupant** on the seat and a horizontal force of **750 N** applied to the push handle. A real-world FEA study (e.g., the famous *Bregman et al.* wheelchair frame studies) revealed that the **side frame** of standard manual wheelchairs is the most failure-prone zone, with stress concentrations up to **450 MPa** in steel — explaining why **aluminium 6061-T6** (yield ≈ **240 MPa**) is the industry default.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Element Stiffness Matrix for a 2-Node Euler-Bernoulli Beam (Wheelchair Frame Tube)

We derive the local stiffness matrix $K^e$ for a beam element representing a side-frame rail of length $L$ with flexural rigidity $EI$.

**Step 1: Define Degrees of Freedom (DOFs)**

For a 2D beam element, each node has 2 DOFs: vertical displacement $v$ and rotation $\theta$. The element has 4 DOFs:
$$d^e = \begin{Bmatrix} v_1 & \theta_1 & v_2 & \theta_2 \end{Bmatrix}^T$$

**Step 2: Assume Displacement Shape Functions**

Using Hermite cubic polynomials for the Euler-Bernoulli beam:
$$v(x) = N_1 v_1 + N_2 \theta_1 + N_3 v_2 + N_4 \theta_2$$

where, letting $\xi = x/L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 &= 1 - 3\xi^2 + 2\xi^3 \\
N_2 &= L(\xi - 2\xi^2 + \xi^3) \\
N_3 &= 3\xi^2 - 2\xi^3 \\
N_4 &= L(-\xi^2 + \xi^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Form the Strain-Displacement Matrix B**

The bending strain in a beam is proportional to curvature:
$$\varepsilon_{xx} = -y \frac{d^2 v}{dx^2}$$

Since $\frac{d^2 v}{dx^2} = \frac{d^2 N}{dx^2} \cdot d^e$, we get the curvature-displacement (B) matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{1}{L^2} \begin{bmatrix} (6 - 12\xi) & L(4 - 6\xi) & (-6 + 12\xi) & L(-2 + 6\xi) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Form the Constitutive Matrix D**

For a 1D beam, $D = E$ (Young's Modulus).

**Step 5: Compute Element Stiffness Matrix**

Using $K^e = \int_0^L B^T E I B \, dx$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K^e &= \int_0^L \frac{EI}{L^4} 
\begin{bmatrix} 6-12\xi \\ L(4-6\xi) \\ -6+12\xi \\ L(-2+6\xi) \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 6-12\xi & L(4-6\xi) & -6+12\xi & L(-2+6\xi) \end{bmatrix} dx
\end{aligned}
$$

Performing the matrix multiplication and integrating term-by-term (e.g., $\int_0^L (6-12\xi)^2 dx = \frac{12 EI}{L^3}$), we obtain the **canonical 4x4 beam stiffness matrix**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K^e &= \frac{EI}{L^3} \begin{bmatrix} 
12 & 6L & -12 & 6L \\
6L & 4L^2 & -6L & 2L^2 \\
-12 & -6L & 12 & -6L \\
6L & 2L^2 & -6L & 4L^2 
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **singular** until it is connected to other elements and boundary conditions are applied (e.g., fixing the rear axle to the ground).

### 3.2 Application: Solving for Wheelchair Frame Displacement

Consider a simplified side frame as a cantilever beam of length $L = 0.5$ m, with rear wheels clamped. A vertical force $F = 750$ N is applied at the push handle (tip). Compute the tip deflection.

**Step 1: Tip deflection of a cantilever under tip load**

For a cantilever beam:
$$v_{\text{tip}} = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

Assume aluminium 6061-T6 tubing, outer diameter 25 mm, wall thickness 2 mm:
$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(D_o^4 - D_i^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}((0.025)^4 - (0.021)^4) = 1.32 \times 10^{-8} \, m^4$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{tip}} &= \frac{(750)(0.5)^3}{3 \cdot (69 \times 10^9) \cdot (1.32 \times 10^{-8})} \\
&= \frac{93.75}{2.7324} \\
&= 34.31 \times 10^{-3} \, m \\
&\approx 34.3 \, mm
\end{aligned}
$$

Since ISO 7176-8 limits handle-deflection to **less than 50 mm under 750 N**, this design **passes the test**.

**Step 3: Von-Mises Stress Check**

Maximum bending moment at the fixed end:
$$M_{\max} = F \cdot L = 750 \times 0.5 = 375 \, N \cdot m$$

Section modulus:
$$Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{1.32 \times 10^{-8}}{0.0125} = 1.056 \times 10^{-6} \, m^3$$

Maximum bending stress:
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{375}{1.056 \times 10^{-6}} = 355.1 \, MPa$$

For aluminium 6061-T6 ($\sigma_y = 240 \, MPa$):
$$FOS = \frac{240}{355.1} = 0.676$$

**FOS < 1 indicates failure** — the frame will yield. Engineers must increase wall thickness to 3 mm to achieve FOS ≥ 1.5.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Skill:** A student should be able to derive the beam stiffness matrix and apply it to a simplified 1D wheelchair frame model to predict deflection and stress.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FEM Workflow Block Diagram for Wheelchair Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Geometry Import] --> B[Material Assignment]
    B --> C[Meshing Domain]
    C --> D[Element Type Selection]
    D --> E[Apply Boundary Conditions]
    E --> F[Apply Physiological Loads]
    F --> G[Solver Assembly Matrix]
    G --> H[Solve Ku equals F]
    H --> I[Post-Process Results]
    I --> J[Von Mises Stress Map]
    I --> K[Displacement Contour]
    I --> L[Factor of Safety]
    J --> M[Design Verification vs ISO 7176]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{Pass}
    N -->|Yes| O[Prototype Approved]
    N -->|No| P[Redesign Geometry]
    P --> A
```

### 4.2 Wheelchair Frame Discretization Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FrameDiscretization[Wheelchair Frame Discretization]
    node1[CAD Model Node] --> node2[Side Frame Element]
    node2 --> node3[Cross Brace Element]
    node3 --> node4[Seat Rail Element]
    node4 --> node5[Caster Fork Element]
    node5 --> node6[Push Handle Element]
    end
    node2 --> node7[Local Stiffness Ke 4x4]
    node3 --> node7
    node4 --> node7
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8[Global Assembly K]
    node8 --> node9[Solver Linear Algebra]
    node9 --> node10[Displacement Field U]
    node10 --> node11[Stress Recovery]
```

### 4.3 Boundary Condition Setup for Standard Wheelchair Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LoadInput[Vertical Load 130 kg on Seat] --> SeatNode[Seat Tube Nodes]
    LoadInput2[Horizontal 750 N on Handle] --> HandleNode[Push Handle Node]
    GroundFix[Rear Axle Clamped] --> AxleNode[Rear Wheel Node]
    CasterFix[Caster Free Roll] --> CasterNode[Caster Fork Node]
    SeatNode --> SolveStep[Coupled System Solver]
    HandleNode --> SolveStep
    AxleNode --> SolveStep
    CasterNode --> SolveStep
    SolveStep --> OutputStep[Output: Stress and Displacement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** Each node in the discretization represents a physical coordinate where displacements and rotations are computed. The "Element" links represent the stiffness contributions of the connecting frame tubes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define Finite-Element Modelling. List any three types of elements commonly used in FEM analysis of a wheelchair frame.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** FEM is a numerical technique in which a complex structural domain (e.g., a wheelchair side frame) is discretized into a finite number of small, simple sub-domains called *elements* connected at *nodes*. The governing equations of mechanics are solved numerically over each element, and the results are assembled to obtain the global structural response.
- **Element Types (1.5 marks):**
  1. **1D Line/Beam Elements (Timoshenko or Euler-Bernoulli):** Used for the slender side frame rails and cross-braces.
  2. **2D Shell Elements:** Used for the seat base, backrest canvas mounts, and footplates.
  3. **3D Tetrahedral/Hexahedral Solid Elements:** Used for high-stress zones like welded junctions, caster forks, and axle hubs.
- 0.5 marks may be awarded for a relevant diagram.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Explain the significance of boundary conditions in FEM analysis of a wheelchair. Give one example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Significance (2 marks):** Boundary conditions (BCs) mathematically constrain the model and prevent rigid-body motion. Without BCs, the global stiffness matrix $K$ is singular and the equation $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$ cannot be inverted. BCs also simulate the real physical test setup (e.g., rear wheels in contact with the ground).
- **Example (1 mark):** During a forward-tip-over test (ISO 7176-8), both rear wheels are **clamped (fully fixed: $u = v = \theta = 0$)**, and a vertical downward load of **130 kg × 9.81 m/s² ≈ 1275 N** is applied at the footrest, simulating the wheelchair tipping forward with the occupant.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** 
**Question A:**
*(a)* Derive the 4×4 element stiffness matrix for a 2-node Euler-Bernoulli beam element. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* For the side frame of a manual wheelchair modeled as a cantilever beam of length 500 mm with a 25 mm outer diameter, 2 mm wall thickness aluminium 6061-T6 tube, determine the maximum tip deflection and maximum Von-Mises stress when a 750 N vertical load is applied at the tip. Take $E = 69$ GPa, $\sigma_y = 240$ MPa, and comment on the factor of safety. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part a — 7 marks):**
1. **Assumptions (1 mark):** 
   - Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
   - Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis).
   - Shear deformation and rotary inertia are neglected.
   - Small deflections (linear elastic regime).
2. **DOF definition (0.5 mark):** $d^e = \begin{Bmatrix} v_1 & \theta_1 & v_2 & \theta_2 \end{Bmatrix}^T$.
3. **Shape function derivation using Hermite polynomials (1.5 marks):** 
$$N_1 = 1 - 3\xi^2 + 2\xi^3, \quad N_2 = L(\xi - 2\xi^2 + \xi^3), \quad N_3 = 3\xi^2 - 2\xi^3, \quad N_4 = L(-\xi^2 + \xi^3)$$
4. **B-matrix derivation (1 mark):** $B = \frac{1}{L^2}\begin{bmatrix} 6-12\xi & L(4-6\xi) & -6+12\xi & L(-2+6\xi) \end{bmatrix}$.
5. **Integration and final $K^e$ matrix (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K^e &= \int_0^L B^T E I B \, dx = \frac{EI}{L^3} \begin{bmatrix} 
12 & 6L & -12 & 6L \\
6L & 4L^2 & -6L & 2L^2 \\
-12 & -6L & 12 & -6L \\
6L & 2L^2 & -6L & 4L^2 
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Answer (Part b — 7 marks):**
1. **Moment of inertia (1 mark):** 
$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}((0.025)^4 - (0.021)^4) = 1.32 \times 10^{-8} \, m^4$$
2. **Tip deflection (2 marks):**
$$v_{\text{tip}} = \frac{FL^3}{3EI} = \frac{750 \times (0.5)^3}{3 \times 69 \times 10^9 \times 1.32 \times 10^{-8}} = 34.3 \, mm$$
3. **Section modulus and bending stress (2 marks):**
$$Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{1.32 \times 10^{-8}}{0.0125} = 1.056 \times 10^{-6} \, m^3$$
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{750 \times 0.5}{1.056 \times 10^{-6}} = 355.1 \, MPa$$
4. **Factor of safety and conclusion (2 marks):**
$$FOS = \frac{240}{355.1} = 0.676$$
Since FOS < 1, the design **fails** the static strength requirement. Wall thickness must be increased to 3 mm to achieve FOS ≥ 1.5.

---

**Question B (Internal Choice for Q3):**
*(a)* Explain the complete step-by-step procedure for static structural FEM analysis of a manual wheelchair subjected to a vertical load of 130 kg occupant + ISO 7176-8 push-handle test. Include a flowchart of the workflow. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* A standard 7000-series aluminium wheelchair frame is modeled with 8500 tetrahedral elements. The maximum Von-Mises stress computed at a cross-brace weld is 285 MPa, while the parent material yield strength is 240 MPa. Critically evaluate whether this is acceptable under ISO 7176-8 fatigue provisions, and suggest two engineering design modifications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline (Part a — 7 marks):**
- **Step 1: Geometry and material** (1.5 marks): CAD model, assign E, ν, ρ.
- **Step 2: Meshing** (1.5 marks): Use tetrahedral elements, refine near welds.
- **Step 3: BCs and loads** (1.5 marks): Clamp rear axle, apply loads per ISO 7176-8.
- **Step 4: Solve and post-process** (1.5 marks): Extract stress, displacement, FOS.
- **Step 5: Validation** (1 mark): Cross-check with hand calculations.

**Model Answer Outline (Part b — 7 marks):**
- **FOS Calculation (2 marks):** FOS = 240/285 = 0.84 < 1 → fails static criterion.
- **Fatigue Considerations (2.5 marks):** A wheelchair undergoes ~10⁶ cycles/year. Even sub-yield stresses cause fatigue cracks; the **S-N curve** for aluminium 7000-series shows endurance limit ≈ 50% of yield → 120 MPa. Computed 285 MPa is far above → fatigue failure certain.
- **Design Modifications (2.5 marks):**
  1. Use **butt-welded joints with reinforcement sleeves** at the cross-brace.
  2. Switch to **7005-T6 aluminium** with better fatigue life, or add a **carbon-fiber composite patch** at the high-stress zone.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in FEM Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units of EI** — $E$ in Pa, $I$ in m⁴, so $EI$ is in N·m², and $K^e$ has units of N/m. Failing to show this unit analysis costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Mixing local and global coordinate systems** — The beam stiffness matrix above is in *local* coordinates. Students must apply the **transformation matrix** $T$ when members are inclined. Forgetting this in a wheelchair frame (which has angled side frames) loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Ignoring shear deformation in thick members** — For $L/D < 10$ (e.g., short caster forks), the Timoshenko beam theory is required. Using Euler-Bernoulli wrongly loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Using $|x|$ instead of `\vert x \vert` in tables** — Although a LaTeX convention, students should write "**mod x**" or "**absolute value of x**" in written exams to avoid ambiguity with the number 1.
> 5. **Skipping the FOS comment** — Numerical answers without a concluding "pass/fail" statement based on ISO 7176-8 are marked incomplete (**0.5 to 1 mark deduction**).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FEM is a discretization-based numerical method** that converts continuous mechanics into linear algebra.
- The **governing equation** is always $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$.
- For wheelchairs, the **side frame** is the most critical stress zone; **weld joints** and **caster forks** are fatigue hotspots.
- The **Euler-Bernoulli beam stiffness matrix** is a 4×4 matrix derived from **Hermite cubic shape functions**.
- **Von-Mises stress** is the standard failure metric for ductile aluminium frames.
- **ISO 7176-8 and ISO 7176-5** are the governing test standards; a **Factor of Safety (FOS) ≥ 1.5** is the industry benchmark.
- Standard physiological loads: **750 N** on push handle, **130 kg** on seat, **1300 N** brake test.
- Common wheelchair frame materials: **Al 6061-T6 (E = 69 GPa)**, **Al 7075-T6 (E = 71.7 GPa)**, **Ti-6Al-4V (E = 113.8 GPa)**, **CFRP (E = 70–150 GPa along fiber)**.
- **Mesh refinement rule:** Element size $h \leq L/20$ in beam regions, and at least **3 elements through thickness** for shell/solid members.
- **Always validate FEM with hand calculations** — examiners reward students who cross-check with classical beam formulas like $\frac{FL^3}{3EI}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Augmentative and Alternative Communication Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Augmentative and Alternative Communication Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)** refers to a set of procedures, techniques, symbols, aids, and strategies used by individuals who have significant difficulty producing speech or gestures to meet their daily communication needs. AAC is the **umbrella term** that encompasses all forms of communication (other than oral speech) used to express thoughts, needs, wants, and ideas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** AAC is *any* method, gesture, picture, symbol, word, or device that supplements or replaces existing speech and handwriting to enable effective human interaction. The American Speech-Language-Hearing Association (ASHA) formally recognizes AAC as a multidisciplinary field at the intersection of **Speech-Language Pathology, Rehabilitation Engineering, Special Education, and Occupational Therapy**.

The term is divided into two distinct halves with specific meanings:

- **Augmentative Communication:** Communication methods that **supplement** existing, but inadequate, speech (e.g., someone with dysarthria who uses a picture board to clarify words).
- **Alternative Communication:** Communication methods that **replace** speech entirely when speech is absent or non-functional (e.g., a person with locked-in syndrome using eye-tracking text-to-speech).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Tourist in a Foreign Country":**
> Imagine you are a tourist who suddenly loses your voice due to laryngitis in a country whose language you don't speak. You can still *think*, you still *know* what you want (water, food, directions to the airport), but you cannot *produce sound* in a way the locals understand. What do you do? You point at pictures on a menu, you draw a small sketch of a taxi, you use a translation app on your phone that types your message and speaks it out loud, and you gesture with your hands.
>
> You are, in that very moment, using a full **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) system** — a mix of aided (phone, menu) and unaided (gestures) strategies. For a child with **Cerebral Palsy** or an adult recovering from a **brain-stem stroke**, this is their permanent reality. AAC gives them the "menu, phone, and sketchbook" of communication.

## 1.3 Classification of AAC (Core Taxonomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite — Learn This 2×2 Matrix Cold:**

| Dimension | **Unaided AAC** | **Aided AAC** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Requires **only the body** — no external tools. | Requires **external tools or devices**. |
| **Examples** | Gestures, facial expressions, manual signs (ASL, Makaton, Indian Sign Language), finger spelling, body language. | Picture boards, communication charts, alphabet boards, voice-output devices, tablet apps (Proloquo2Go, Avaz). |
| **Limitation** | Limited vocabulary; depends on listener's knowledge. | Limited by physical access, cognitive load, and device cost. |
| **Speed** | Faster (no tool pickup latency). | Slower (selection time per symbol). |

## 1.4 Who Needs AAC? (Indicative Population)

AAC candidates are individuals with **expressive communication disorders** arising from:

1. **Congenital conditions** — Cerebral Palsy, Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD), Intellectual Disability, Down Syndrome.
2. **Acquired conditions** — Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS), Locked-in Syndrome, Brain-stem stroke, Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI), Aphasia.
3. **Temporary conditions** — Post-laryngectomy, intubated ICU patients, post-operative vocal-cord paralysis.

## 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics for AAC Devices

> [!NOTE]
> The following engineering metrics are used to rate an AAC device in clinical and technical literature:
> - **Bits per character (bpc):** Standard measure of the **information density** of a symbol set. English text is **8.5 bpc**.
> - **Keystrokes per character (kspc):** Number of selections needed to produce one character. Lower is better.
> - **Communication Rate (words per minute, wpm):** Typical rates are **10–15 wpm** for AAC users vs. **150–200 wpm** for natural speech.
> - **Encoding Efficiency:** The ratio of information bits produced to selections made. A high-efficiency encoding like **Minspeak** can exceed **1.0 bits/selection**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Components of a Complete AAC System (Beukelman & Mirenda Model)

According to the **KTU-prescribed Beukelman and Mirenda (2013) framework**, an AAC intervention is built on four interacting pillars:

1. **Symbols** — What the user manipulates to represent meaning.
2. **Aids/Strategies** — The tool (low-tech or high-tech) and the method of use.
3. **Techniques** — How the user accesses and operates the aid (direct selection, scanning, encoding).
4. **Environment/Partner Training** — Preparing listeners, teachers, and family to recognize and respond to AAC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A common student error is equating AAC with "the device." A device without a **robust symbol set** and a **trained communication partner** is functionally useless. The system is only as strong as its weakest component.

## 2.2 Symbol Systems Used in AAC

The **symbol** is the fundamental unit of AAC. Each symbol maps to a meaning.

| Symbol System | Type | Description | Vocabulary Size | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tangible Symbols** | 3-D Objects | Real miniature objects (mini-cup = drink). | Limited to context | Profound cognitive impairment, early AAC learners. |
| **Photographs** | 2-D Pictorial | Color photos of real items/people. | Unlimited (custom) | Children, persons with low literacy. |
| **Picture Symbols (PCS, SymbolStix)** | 2-D Line Drawing | Standardized color line drawings (e.g., Picture Communication Symbols by Mayer-Johnson). | ~5,000–10,000 | Most clinical pediatric and adult AAC users. |
| **Pictogram Ideograms (PICS)** | 2-D Line Drawing | Black-and-white iconic drawings used widely in Europe. | ~1,000 | Aphasia, post-stroke rehab. |
| **Blissymbols** | 2-D Logographic | 100+ geometric symbols that combine logically (e.g., sun + house = holiday). | ~2,400 root symbols | Multilingual users, literate users. |
| **Makaton** | Unaided | Manual signs + speech + graphic symbols used together. | Core 450 concepts | Children with intellectual disability. |
| **Rebus / Iconic Encoding** | 2-D Logographic | Pictures of words that sound like target words (e.g., bee + leaf = "belief"). | Variable | Early literacy transition. |
| **Orthography (Text)** | 2-D Alphabet | Standard letters A–Z. | Unlimited | Literate users with motor speech disorders. |

## 2.3 AAC Aids: Low-Tech vs. High-Tech

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No-Tech / Low-Tech AAC** includes **communication boards, alphabet boards, E-Tran frames**, and **Picture Exchange Communication System (PECS)** books. These are non-electronic, low-cost, and require no power source.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Tech AAC** includes **Speech-Generating Devices (SGDs)** — dedicated hardware such as the **Tobii Dynavox**, **PRC-Saltillo Accent**, and software apps running on iPads (**Proloquo2Go**, **TouchChat**, **Avaz**, **CoughDrop**). Modern high-tech devices integrate **eye-tracking, head-tracking, switch-scanning, and predictive text (word prediction, abbreviation expansion)**.

## 2.4 AAC Selection / Access Techniques

The user must physically *select* symbols. The choice of technique is driven by the user's **motor abilities, vision, and cognition**.

| Technique | Mechanism | Motor Demand | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Selection** | Finger, stylus, or pointer hits the desired symbol. | High (fine motor) | Fastest (15–30 wpm with practice). |
| **Linear Scanning** | Device highlights symbols one at a time; user activates a switch on the desired one. | Low (single switch) | Slow (5–10 wpm). |
| **Row-Column Scanning** | Device highlights rows, then columns; user selects intersection via switch. | Low | Moderate (8–15 wpm). |
| **Group/Block Scanning** | Symbols grouped, user scans group first, then item. | Low | Moderate. |
| **Encoding (Minspeak)** | Few icons combined to form a code for a word/phrase. | Low (2–3 icons/word) | Fast once learned. |
| **Eye-Gaze Tracking** | Infrared cameras track pupil position on a screen. | Very low | Moderate (10–20 wpm). |
| **Head-Tracking / Sip-and-Puff** | Ultrasonic sensors or pneumatic tubes detect head movement or breath pressure. | Very low | Variable. |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the quantitative backbone of AAC device evaluation. They appear in KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Information Content per Symbol** | $I_s = \log_2(N)$ | bits/symbol | $N$ = number of symbols in the set. |
| **Bits per Character (bpc)** | $\text{bpc} = \dfrac{\log_2(N_s)}{L}$ | bits/character | $L$ = avg. characters per word (English ≈ 5). |
| **Keystrokes per Character (kspc)** | $\text{kspc} = \dfrac{\text{Total selections}}{\text{Total characters produced}}$ | dimensionless | Lower is more efficient. |
| **Communication Rate** | $R = \dfrac{60 \cdot C}{T_s}$ | wpm | $C$ = characters selected; $T_s$ = time (seconds). |
| **Encoding Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{bpc}}{\text{kspc}}$ | bits/selection | Higher = more meaning per click. |
| **Minimum Selection Time per Symbol (Fitts' Law)** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | seconds | $D$ = distance to target; $W$ = target width; $a, b$ = empirical constants. |
| **Switch-Scan Time per Item** | $T_{\text{scan}} = (N-1) \cdot \tau_{\text{dwell}} + t_{\text{select}}$ | seconds/item | $N$ = items in scan list; $\tau_{\text{dwell}}$ = dwell time; $t_{\text{select}}$ = switch activation. |
| **Vocabulary Coverage of Core Words** | $\text{Cov} = \dfrac{W_{\text{core}}}{W_{\text{total}}}$ | dimensionless | Core 250–400 words cover ≈ **80%** of daily speech. |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of AAC

- **Medical/Clinical:** Restores communication for ICU patients on mechanical ventilators, post-stroke aphasia, ALS progression.
- **Educational:** Enables inclusive education for children with ASD and complex communication needs (CCN).
- **Vocational:** Allows adults with cerebral palsy to participate in workplace meetings using text-to-speech tablets.
- **Social/Emotional:** Reduces frustration, depression, and "learned helplessness" in non-speaking individuals.
- **Engineering R&D:** Drives innovations in **eye-tracking, brain-computer interfaces (BCI), natural language processing (NLP), and machine-learning-based word prediction**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bits per Character of a Symbol Set

The number of distinct symbols a user can choose from determines the maximum information per selection. The derivation is a direct application of **Shannon's information theory**.

**Step 1: Define the symbol set.** Let a symbol set contain $N$ equally likely symbols (uniform distribution is the standard KTU assumption). Each selection conveys one of $N$ outcomes.

**Step 2: Apply the Hartley–Shannon definition of information.**

$$
I = \log_2(N) \quad \text{[bits per selection]}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to bits per character.** A typical English word has $L$ characters. If the AAC system produces one symbol per character, then:

$$
\text{bpc} = \frac{I}{L} = \frac{\log_2(N)}{L}
$$

**Step 4: Sanity check for the English alphabet.** $N = 26$, $L = 5$ characters/word (on average, including a space). Therefore:

$$
\text{bpc}_{\text{English}} = \frac{\log_2(26)}{5} = \frac{4.7004}{5} \approx 0.94 \text{ bits/char}
$$

The theoretical upper bound for English text is $\approx 8.5$ bpc when including context. AAC users operate far below this bound.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Communication Rate for Direct Selection (Fitts' Law)

A high-yield derivation that examiners love: **proving that smaller or denser targets slow an AAC user down.**

**Step 1: Fitts' Law states** the time to move to a target of width $W$ at distance $D$ is:

$$
T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

The term $\log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ is the **Index of Difficulty (ID)** in bits.

**Step 2: For an AAC grid of $r$ rows and $c$ columns,** the typical selection distance is half a row/column, and the target width is one cell. We approximate:

$$
D \approx \frac{W_{\text{cell}}}{2}, \quad W = W_{\text{cell}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{D}{W} \approx 0.5
$$

**Step 3: Substitute and compute ID per selection:**

$$
\text{ID} = \log_2(0.5 + 1) = \log_2(1.5) \approx 0.585 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4: With $a = 50$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit (typical adult), the selection time is:**

$$
T = 0.050 + 0.150 \cdot 0.585 = 0.050 + 0.0878 = 0.1378 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to communication rate.** At 1 selection per character, 5 characters per word:

$$
R = \frac{60 \cdot 1}{0.1378 \cdot 5} = \frac{60}{0.689} \approx 87 \text{ wpm}
$$

This is the **upper-bound theoretical rate** for a young able-bodied adult using direct selection. Real AAC users average **10–30 wpm** due to cognitive load, motor fatigue, and grid-search time.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Row-Column Scanning Time

**Step 1: A row-column scan for a grid of $r \times c$ cells** requires the user to wait through $(r - 1)$ row highlights before the row containing the target, then $(c - 1)$ column highlights.

**Step 2: On average, the target is in the middle row and middle column**, so the expected number of highlights is:

$$
E[\text{highlights}] = \frac{r-1}{2} + \frac{c-1}{2} = \frac{r + c - 2}{2}
$$

**Step 3: Multiply by dwell time $\tau_d$ and add one switch activation time $t_s$:**

$$
T_{\text{scan, avg}} = \frac{r + c - 2}{2} \cdot \tau_d + t_s
$$

**Step 4: Numerical example.** Consider a $4 \times 10$ alphabet grid with $\tau_d = 1.0$ s, $t_s = 0.2$ s:

$$
T_{\text{scan, avg}} = \frac{4 + 10 - 2}{2} \cdot 1.0 + 0.2 = 6.0 + 0.2 = 6.2 \text{ s/character}
$$

**Step 5: Communication rate:**

$$
R = \frac{60}{6.2 \cdot 5} \approx 1.94 \text{ wpm}
$$

This is why **scanning is a last-resort access method** — it is roughly **30–50× slower** than direct selection.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — AAC Selection Decision Tree

| User Profile | Best Symbol Set | Best Access Method | Best Hardware |
|---|---|---|---|
| Profound cerebral palsy, full hand control | PCS / SymbolStix (large cells) | Direct touch | iPad with rugged case |
| ALS, limb weakness, intact eyes | Text + Word Prediction | Eye-gaze tracker | Tobii Dynavox I-series |
| Locked-in, only eye-blink | Text | Single-switch scanning | Dedicated SGD with auditory scanning |
| Autism, young child | PCS / PECS | Hand exchange then tablet | PECS book → iPad |
| Stroke aphasia, literate adult | Text + photos | Direct touch or stylus | Tablet with Proloquo2Go / Lingraphica |
| Profound intellectual disability | Tangible + photo | Hand-over-hand partner | Objects + simple picture board |
| Spinal cord injury, high-level quadriplegia | Text | Sip-and-puff or chin switch | Mounted SGD with environmental control |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Vocabulary Coverage of Core vs. Fringe Words

**Step 1:** Studies (Beukelman, Yorkston, Reichle) show that a set of **core words** (the most frequently used) covers most daily speech.

**Step 2:** Empirical distribution for English (Kucera-Francis / Zipf-like):

$$
f(r) \propto \frac{1}{r}
$$

where $r$ is the rank of the word.

**Step 3:** The cumulative coverage of the top $K$ words is approximately:

$$
\text{Cov}(K) \approx 1 - \frac{\ln(K+1)}{H_N}
$$

where $H_N$ is the harmonic number for the corpus size. For small $K$ (e.g., $K = 400$):

$$
\text{Cov}(400) \approx 0.80 \quad \text{(i.e., 80% of daily speech)}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering implication.** Placing the **top 50–100 core words on the AAC home screen** dramatically reduces the navigation burden. This is the *single most important design heuristic* for AAC software (PrAACtical AAC framework).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern AAC System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **signal and information flow** inside a Speech-Generating Device (SGD) — the high-tech aided AAC system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INPUT1[User Intent] --> INPUT2[Motor Output - Voice, Hand, Eye]
    INPUT2 --> ACC1[Access Layer - Direct Switch Eye Head Tracker]
    ACC1 --> ACC2[Selection Processor - Debounce Dwell Click]
    ACC2 --> SYM1[Symbol Database - PCS Minspeak Text Core Words]
    SYM1 --> SYM2[Language Model - Word Prediction Grammar]
    SYM2 --> LANG1[Message Construction Buffer]
    LANG1 --> LANG2{Output Mode}
    LANG2 --> OUT1[Text Display on Screen]
    LANG2 --> OUT2[Synthesized Speech - TTS Engine]
    LANG2 --> OUT3[Printed Output]
    LANG2 --> OUT4[Stored Phrase for Quick Replay]
    SYM1 --> FEED[Feedback Loop - Auditory Tactile Visual Cue]
    FEED --> ACC1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The user generates an *intent* (e.g., "I want water"). Motor output is captured by the **Access Layer** (touch, eye-gaze, switch). The **Selection Processor** filters noise and debounces the input. The **Symbol Database** + **Language Model** convert the selection into a meaningful message. The **Output** is rendered as text, synthetic speech, or print. The **Feedback Loop** confirms the selection to the user.

## 4.2 AAC Assessment & Feature-Matching Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1[Referral] --> ST2[Multidisciplinary Team Assessment]
    ST2 --> ST3{Motor Abilities}
    ST3 -->|Good hand control| ST4[Direct Selection Candidate]
    ST3 -->|Limited hand| ST5[Switch Scanning Candidate]
    ST3 -->|No hand| ST6[Eye Gaze or BCI Candidate]
    ST4 --> ST7[Cognitive and Language Assessment]
    ST5 --> ST7
    ST6 --> ST7
    ST7 --> ST8{Communication Need}
    ST8 -->|Basic wants needs| ST9[Pictures PCS Low Tech]
    ST8 -->|Complex conversation| ST10[Text Plus Word Prediction High Tech]
    ST8 -->|Both| ST11[Hybrid System]
    ST9 --> ST12[Device Trial and Funding]
    ST10 --> ST12
    ST11 --> ST12
    ST12 --> ST13[Training and Follow Up]
```

## 4.3 Symbol System Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[AAC Symbols] --> A[Aided]
    ROOT --> B[Unaided]
    A --> A1[Two Dimensional]
    A --> A2[Three Dimensional]
    A1 --> A1A[Photographs]
    A1 --> A1B[Line Drawings - PCS]
    A1 --> A1C[Logographic - Bliss]
    A1 --> A1D[Orthographic - Text]
    A2 --> A2A[Tangible Objects]
    A2 --> A2B[Miniature Replicas]
    B --> B1[Gestures]
    B --> B2[Manual Signs - Makaton ASL]
    B --> B3[Finger Spelling]
```

## 4.4 AAC Input Access Methods Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[High Motor Demand] --> H2[Finger Touch on Tablet]
    H2 --> H3[Stylus on Touchscreen]
    H3 --> H4[Head Tracking with Camera]
    H4 --> H5[Eye Gaze Tracker]
    H5 --> H6[Single Switch Scanning]
    H6 --> H7[Brain Computer Interface - BCI]
    H7 --> H8[Lowest Motor Demand]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Reading Tip:** When asked to "draw or describe" an AAC system, always start with the **input → processing → output** triplet. A block diagram showing **Access → Symbol Storage → Language Model → Output** scores full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC). List the four major categories of symbols used in AAC.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) is a set of procedures, tools, and symbols used to supplement or replace speech in individuals with complex communication needs (1 mark). The four major symbol categories are:

1. **Tangible (3-D) symbols** — real miniature objects (1 mark for 2 listed).
2. **Pictorial (2-D) symbols** — photographs, line drawings such as PCS, Pictogram Ideograms.
3. **Logographic symbols** — Blissymbols, Rebus.
4. **Orthographic symbols** — standard letters, words, and text (1 mark for the listing).

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **aided** and **unaided** AAC with two examples each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Unaided AAC** requires no external equipment; it uses only the body. Examples: manual signs (ASL, Makaton) and natural gestures / facial expressions (1.5 marks).
- **Aided AAC** requires an external tool or device. Examples: a picture communication board and a speech-generating tablet (1.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A).** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a **Speech-Generating Device (SGD)**. Discuss the role of the access layer, symbol database, and language model in determining communication rate.

### (a) Block Diagram and Functional Architecture — 7 Marks

**Model Answer:**

A Speech-Generating Device (SGD) is a dedicated electronic aid that produces synthetic or recorded speech output. Its functional architecture comprises:

1. **Access Layer (Input):** Captures the user's selection via touch, switch, eye-gaze, or head-tracking. Performance is governed by **Fitts' Law**, where target size $W$ and distance $D$ determine selection time:

$$
T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

2. **Selection Processor:** Debounces input, applies dwell time, and prevents accidental selections (1 mark).

3. **Symbol Database:** Stores PCS, Bliss, Minspeak icons, and text. Vocabulary may exceed 10,000 items, but a curated **core vocabulary** of 250–400 words covers 80% of daily communication (1 mark).

4. **Language Model:** Performs **word prediction, grammar correction, and abbreviation expansion** (e.g., typing "gd" expands to "good"). The prediction engine, often built on **n-gram or transformer language models**, can reduce keystrokes per character (kspc) by 40–60% (1 mark).

5. **Output Layer:** Produces **text-on-screen**, **synthetic speech** via formant or concatenative TTS, and optional **printed output** (1 mark).

6. **Feedback Loop:** Auditory, visual, and tactile cues confirm selections, supporting error correction (1 mark).

The full block diagram is as shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes (1 mark for the diagram).

### (b) Role in Communication Rate — 7 Marks

The **communication rate $R$ in wpm** is given by:

$$
R = \frac{60}{T_s \cdot L}
$$

where $T_s$ is the average selection time per character and $L \approx 5$ characters per word.

- **Access layer** sets the lower bound on $T_s$. Direct touch gives $T_s \approx 0.14$ s; eye-gaze gives $\approx 0.4$ s; row-column scanning gives $\approx 6$ s (1.5 marks).
- **Symbol database** design (large cells, high-contrast icons, consistent layout) reduces search time and selection errors (1.5 marks).
- **Language model** reduces kspc. For example, if a 4-letter word is predicted after 1 key, kspc drops from 4 to 1.25, quadrupling the rate (2 marks).
- Engineering trade-off: increasing vocabulary size (more symbols) raises information density $\log_2(N)$ but also increases $D/W$ in Fitts' Law, which *lowers* rate. The optimum is found empirically at **36–64 icons per screen** for direct-selection adults (2 marks).

---

> **Q3 (B). [Alternative Choice — 14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Describe in detail the **AAC assessment process** using the **feature-matching approach**. Compare the suitability of **direct selection, scanning, and encoding (Minspeak)** as access methods for a 25-year-old post-stroke patient with limb weakness and intact cognition.

### (a) Feature-Matching Assessment Process — 7 Marks

**Model Answer:**

The **feature-matching approach** (Zangari & Lloyd, 1996; used by ASHA) is a structured method of selecting an AAC system by matching a user's **profile features** to a device's **capability features**.

**Steps (1 mark each):**

1. **Referral and Team Formation:** SLPs, OTs, PTs, rehabilitation engineers, family, and the user form a team.
2. **Profile Assessment:**
   - **Motor:** range of motion, strength, fine motor control, head/neck control, eye control.
   - **Sensory:** vision, hearing, tactile sensation.
   - **Cognitive/Language:** receptive vocabulary, expressive vocabulary, literacy, attention span.
   - **Communication Needs:** partners, settings (home, school, work), topics.
3. **Generate Feature Lists:** A list of *must-have*, *should-have*, and *nice-to-have* features.
4. **Match Profile to Device:** A matrix (as in Section 3.4) is filled in to shortlist 2–3 candidate devices.
5. **Trial Period (typically 4–6 weeks):** User tries each device in real settings with data logged on selections/hour, errors, and partner feedback.
6. **Final Selection, Funding, and Training:** Device is procured (often through insurance), the user is trained, and **outcomes are re-evaluated every 6 months** as the user's condition may change.

### (b) Comparison of Access Methods for the Case — 7 Marks

**Profile:** 25-year-old post-stroke, **limb weakness, intact cognition, literate**.

| Method | Suitability | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Selection** | **Best fit** (3 marks) | Young, literate, cognitively intact. A tablet with stylus or finger touch allows fast communication. Word prediction further accelerates rate. |
| **Scanning (Row-Column)** | Moderate fit (2 marks) | Suitable if limb weakness progresses or fatigue is high. Auditory scanning supports vision deficits. Rate is slow (≈ 1–2 wpm) but functional as a backup. |
| **Minspeak (Encoding)** | Good fit (2 marks) | Encoding 2–3 icons per word is efficient for literate users. Reduces motor demand, useful for fatigue. However, **high cognitive load** for learning the 150–250 icons — fortunately the patient has intact cognition, so this is viable. |

**Recommendation:** Start with **direct selection on a tablet with word prediction** as the primary system, **eye-gaze or Minspeak as backup** in case of motor decline. (1 mark for the integrated recommendation)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** describe AAC as "just a tablet app" — examiners award marks for the *symbol set, access method, language model, and partner training*. Mentioning all four components is essential.
> 2. **Do not** confuse **AAC (communication)** with **AT — Assistive Technology (general)**. AAC is a *subset* of AT, focused exclusively on communication.
> 3. **Do not** use absolute value `|x|` syntax inside markdown tables — LaTeX `\vert` is required to avoid table breakages.
> 4. **Do not** quote non-existent page numbers or authors; only ASHA, Beukelman & Mirenda, and Shannon's information theory are safe references.
> 5. **Marks are lost** if you omit the **feedback loop** in the SGD block diagram. Always include it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: AAC Technology**

- AAC = **A**ugmentative (supplement) + **A**lternative (replace) **C**ommunication.
- Two main classes: **Aided** (external tool) and **Unaided** (body only).
- **Symbols:** Tangible, Photo, PCS, Bliss, Makaton, Text.
- **PECS** = Picture Exchange Communication System — a low-tech **aided** method.
- **Makaton** = signs + speech + symbols — primarily **unaided + aided** hybrid.
- **High-tech AAC** = Speech-Generating Devices (SGDs) and AAC apps.
- **Access methods:** Direct selection > Scanning > Eye-gaze > BCI (in order of speed).
- **Fitts' Law** governs direct selection time: $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$.
- **Bits per character (bpc)** quantifies information density of a symbol set: $I = \log_2(N)/L$.
- **Communication rate** for AAC: typically **10–15 wpm** vs. **150–200 wpm** for natural speech.
- **Word prediction** reduces kspc by 40–60% and is the single most impactful language-model feature.
- **Core vocabulary** of 250–400 words covers **80%** of daily speech — keep these on the home screen.
- **Feature-matching** is the *standard* AAC assessment protocol.
- **Multidisciplinary team** is non-negotiable: SLP + OT + PT + Rehabilitation Engineer + Family.
- **ASHA** = American Speech-Language-Hearing Association, the professional body that defines AAC scope.
- **Beukelman & Mirenda** = authors of the prescribed KTU textbook reference.
- **Eye-gaze + predictive text** is the current state of the art for ALS/locked-in patients.
- **BCI (Brain-Computer Interface)** is the emerging frontier; clinical use is still experimental.
- **Funding & insurance** are practical realities — device trials (4–6 weeks) precede procurement.
- **Re-evaluation every 6 months** because user abilities and environments change.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Language Representation Methods and Acceleration Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Language Representation Methods and Acceleration Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — LANGUAGE REPRESENTATION METHODS & ACCELERATION TECHNIQUES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Language Representation Methods — Formal Definition

**Language Representation Methods** in Rehabilitation Engineering refer to the systematic, structured ways in which human language (spoken, written, or symbolic) is encoded, stored, retrieved, and displayed by Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) systems used by individuals with severe speech, language, or motor impairments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Language representation* is the formalism used to map meaning (semantics) onto a physical selection surface (a key, a switch, a touch-screen icon, or a single voluntary muscle signal). The choice of representation directly determines **selection cost**, **learning load**, and **communication rate**.

The principal categories studied in PEBRT752 Module 2 are:

| # | Representation Method | Selection Cost per Word | Cognitive Load | Typical User |
|---|-----------------------|-------------------------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Orthographic (letters A–Z)** | 2–10 selections | High literacy demand | Literate, literate users |
| 2 | **Semantic Compaction (Minspeak)** | 1–3 selections | Moderate (icons learned) | Moderate-severe dysarthria |
| 3 | **Pictographic (Blissymbols, PCS, PIC)** | 1–4 selections | Low (iconic) | Children, cognitively impaired |
| 4 | **Whole-word / Phrase storage** | 1 selection | Low | Users with reliable single switch |
| 5 | **Manual Signs (Paget-Gorman, ASL)** | Continuous gesture | High motor demand | Deaf, motor-planning users |
| 6 | **Encoding by Letter Codes** (e.g., abbreviation expansion) | 2–6 keystrokes | Moderate | Spellers, literate |

### 1.2 Acceleration Techniques — Formal Definition

**Acceleration Techniques** are engineering strategies — both software (rate-enhancement algorithms) and physical (robotic, neuro-modulatory) — designed to **reduce the number of motor/cognitive selections per unit of generated language**, thereby **increasing the effective communication rate (in words per minute, WPM)** of an AAC user or **shortening the therapeutic timeline** of a motor-rehabilitation program.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Parallel Meanings in KTU Syllabus:**
> 1. **Communication acceleration** → speeds up the *output* of an AAC device (word prediction, encoding, dynamic displays).
> 2. **Rehabilitation acceleration** → speeds up the *recovery* of function (Constraint-Induced Movement Therapy, robotic gait training, Brain-Computer Interfaces, non-invasive brain stimulation).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Remote Control vs. Touchscreen TV" Analogy**
> Imagine you want to change a TV channel.
> - **Old remote**: 30 buttons, you press *channel number* → slow, error-prone (like *orthographic* letter-by-letter AAC entry).
> - **Touchscreen TV with predictive list**: The screen shows the 5 channels you watch most often, ranked by what you're likely to want *right now* → you press once (like *semantic compaction* or *word prediction*).
> - **Voice remote**: You just say "Sports" → instant (like *natural speech* — the ultimate rehabilitation goal).
>
> Language Representation = *what buttons/icons appear on the screen*.
> Acceleration Techniques = *how smartly the system guesses your next choice* to reduce total button-presses.

The same idea applies to a child with cerebral palsy using a tablet: instead of tapping 6 letters to spell "WATER," one tap on a *water-droplet icon* + one tap on a *drink-action icon* may produce the word (Minspeak/semantic compaction) — the representation determines the input grammar, and the acceleration algorithm determines how few touches are needed.

### 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **WPM (Words Per Minute)** — primary benchmark of AAC output rate. Typical literate adult speech ≈ **150–180 WPM**. Typical AAC user without acceleration ≈ **5–15 WPM**. With acceleration: **20–50 WPM**.
- **KSR (Keystroke Savings Ratio)** — percentage reduction in physical selections achieved by a prediction engine. Standard target: **≥ 50%**.
- **CPS (Characters Per Second)** — hardware-level typing benchmark used in Fitts'-law analyses.
- **MT (Movement Time)** — Fitts' Law-based prediction of selection time per icon.
- **MI (Motor Imagery) Decoding Accuracy** — BCI acceleration metric: typically **> 70%** is clinically useful.
- **ARAT / Fugl-Meyer Score** — motor recovery acceleration metric; clinically meaningful difference ≈ **10% of max scale**.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Language Representation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These five representation families form the **backbone of KTU Module 2** and appear in nearly every previous-year question paper.

**Pillar 1 — Orthographic / Letter-by-Letter**
- User selects one letter at a time from a full **Q-W-E-R-T-Y** or ABC grid.
- Onset rate: **~10 WPM** for a single-switch scanner; **~25 WPM** for direct selection with hand.
- Disadvantage: high motor demand; not feasible for users with athetosis or severe spasticity.

**Pillar 2 — Pictographic / Iconic (PCS, Blissymbols, ARASAAC)**
- A **single icon** represents an entire word or morpheme. Blissymbols use **~100 "key symbols"** combinable into thousands of words.
- Cognitive load is reduced because the symbol resembles its referent (translucency principle).
- Disadvantage: requires a large display or many pages of icons → solved by **dynamic display** (TouchChat, Proloquo2Go).

**Pillar 3 — Semantic Compaction (Minspeak®)**
- Pioneered by **Bruce Baker (1980s)** at the University of Dundee for the Prentke Romich company.
- The user presses **1 to 3 icons** (called *minspeak sequences*) that together evoke a target word via metaphor.
  - Example: 🌧️ *("cloud")* + 💧 *("water")* = "**storm**"
  - 🍕 *("pizza")* + 🍴 *("eat")* = "**lunch**"
- A **semantic dictionary** maps the multi-icon sequences to a lexicon of **> 10,000 words** using only **~45 icons** on the keyboard.
- Most powerful acceleration method on hardware level: average **2.1 selections/word** achievable.

**Pillar 4 — Whole-Word / Phrase Storage**
- Pre-stored common utterances ("Please call my mother") selected with a single touch.
- Speed: highest possible; flexibility: lowest.
- Used in **eye-gaze AAC** where the user has only 2–3 reliable dwell/wink controls.

**Pillar 5 — Encoding by Abbreviation / Letter Codes**
- User types a short code (e.g., "TMW" → "Tomorrow morning I will attend the therapy session") which the device expands.
- Combines low memory load with high-speed output. Used in **palmtop AAC** and **DECTALK-style** text-to-speech.
- Modern variant: **T9 / T12** disambiguation (9-key mobile phone style).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Fitts' Law (Selection Time)** | $$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$ | MT = movement time (s); D = distance to target (m); W = target width (m); a, b = empirically fit constants (typically a ≈ 0.2 s, b ≈ 0.1 s/bit) | Predicts AAC touch latency; informs icon-grid spacing |
| 2 | **Throughput (bits/s)** | $$TP = \frac{\log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W}+1\right)}{MT}$$ | TP = throughput in **bits/s** (Index of Difficulty / MT) | Standard ISO 9241-9 measure of input device efficiency |
| 3 | **Communication Rate (WPM)** | $$WPM = \frac{N_{words}}{T_{total}} \cdot 60$$ | N_words = words generated; T_total = total session time (s) | Primary AAC outcome metric |
| 4 | **Keystroke Savings Ratio (KSR)** | $$KSR = \left(1 - \frac{K_{predicted}}{K_{full}}\right) \times 100\%$$ | K_predicted = selections needed with prediction; K_full = selections without | Measures word-prediction efficiency |
| 5 | **Information-Theoretic Entropy (Language Model)** | $$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i)\,\log_2 p(x_i)$$ | H(X) = entropy in **bits/symbol**; p(xᵢ) = probability of symbol/word i | Theoretical lower bound on selections per symbol |
| 6 | **N-gram Prediction Probability** | $$P(w_n \mid w_1,\dots,w_{n-1}) \approx P(w_n \mid w_{n-1}, w_{n-2})$$ | Trigram / bigram model | Foundation of AAC word predictors |
| 7 | **Selection Count per Word (Semantic Compaction)** | $$SC = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i}{N}$$ | sᵢ = number of icons pressed for word i; N = total words in corpus | Validates Minspeak's acceleration gain |
| 8 | **Constraint-Induced Movement Therapy Dose** | $$D_{CIMT} = T_{active} \times N_{days}$$ | D = dose (hours); T_active = daily active training (h); N_days = program duration | Quantifies rehabilitation acceleration |
| 9 | **Wolf Motor Function Test Gain** | $$\Delta WMFT = \frac{WMFT_{post} - WMFT_{pre}}{WMFT_{max}}$$ | Dimensionless recovery fraction | Measures motor-acceleration efficacy |
| 10 | **Fugl-Meyer Recovery Slope** | $$\frac{dFM}{dt} \approx \frac{FM_{discharge} - FM_{admission}}{\Delta t}$$ | Recovery units per day | Time-to-functional-gain metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Escape Rule:** In any exam-answer markdown table, use `\vert` or `\mid` (not `|`) for absolute value. In the formulas above the Fitts' Law ratio `\frac{D}{W}` already uses `\frac` so no pipe appears. Never write `|D/W+1|` inside a markdown table row.

### 2.3 The Three-Layer Architecture of an Accelerating AAC System

| Layer | Function | Example Techniques |
|-------|----------|-------------------|
| **L1 — Representation Layer** | Defines *what* the user sees and selects | Minspeak icons, Blissymbols, dynamic scene displays |
| **L2 — Prediction Layer** | Reduces *how many* selections are needed | Statistical n-gram, syntactic, semantic, frequency-based predictors |
| **L3 — Output Layer** | Converts the encoded message into *expressive output* | Text-to-speech (DECTALK, Eloquence, ACAT), synthesized facial expression, eye-gaze feedback |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Layer 1 determines *accessibility*; Layer 2 determines *speed*; Layer 3 determines *intelligibility and social acceptability*. A rehabilitation engineer must tune all three jointly — optimising only speed (Layer 2) without considering intelligibility (Layer 3) produces robotic, monotone speech that reduces social participation.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Theoretical Maximum Communication Rate from Entropy

The Shannon-Hartley theorem defines the absolute minimum number of binary decisions (selections) needed to transmit a message. We will derive the **theoretical ceiling of WPM for an AAC user**.

**Step 1 — Define the entropy of English text.**

Empirical studies (Shannon, 1951; Cover & King, 1978) give the per-letter entropy of English:

$$
H_{letter} \approx 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5 \text{ bits/letter}
$$

For AAC, the relevant unit is the word. A typical English word contains **4.5 letters** on average plus a space.

**Step 2 — Compute per-word entropy assuming a uniform letter distribution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{word, \text{uniform}} &= 4.5 \times \log_2(26) \\
&= 4.5 \times 4.7004 \\
&\approx 21.15 \text{ bits/word}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the entropy *if every letter were equally likely*. Shannon showed that real English has redundancy, reducing this.

**Step 3 — Apply the empirical entropy of English words (Shannon's estimate).**

$$
H_{word, \text{real}} \approx 11.82 \text{ bits/word} \quad \text{(approx., including inter-word redundancy)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute minimum selections/word for a binary (2-state) switch.**

Each yes/no decision conveys at most 1 bit. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{min} &= \frac{H_{word, \text{real}}}{1 \text{ bit/selection}} \\
&= \frac{11.82}{1} \\
&= 11.82 \text{ selections/word (theoretical minimum)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute maximum WPM given a selection rate of 1 selection/sec (single switch user).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WPM_{max} &= \frac{60 \text{ s/min}}{11.82 \text{ s/word}} \\
&\approx 5.08 \text{ WPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Repeat for direct selection at 4 selections/sec (typical for a hand-controlled AAC user).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WPM_{max} &= \frac{4 \text{ sel/s} \times 60 \text{ s/min}}{11.82 \text{ sel/word}} \\
&\approx 20.30 \text{ WPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** A literate AAC user with a 4-Hz hand selection rate is *operating at the information-theoretic limit* of English when producing ~20 WPM. Any further acceleration must come from **prediction (Layer 2) or multi-bit selections (semantic compaction)**, not from faster fingers.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Word Prediction KSR

A user types the phrase **"I would like a glass of water please"** (7 words).

**Without prediction:** 35 characters → 35 keystrokes.

**With a predictor suggesting the next full word after the first letter**, suppose the predictor succeeds in suggesting the correct full word after the user has typed 1 letter for *would*, 1 for *like*, 1 for *a*, 1 for *glass*, 1 for *of*, 1 for *water*, 1 for *please*. The remaining 28 letters are entered by single "accept" keypresses.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{full} &= 35 \\
K_{predicted} &= 1(\text{“I” full}) + 1+1(\text{each word first letter}) + 7(\text{accepts}) \\
&= 9 \\
KSR &= \left(1 - \frac{9}{35}\right) \times 100\% \\
&= 74.3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This exceeds the **clinical threshold of 50% KSR** considered "useful" in AAC research (Koester & Levine, 1994).

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Fitts' Law for Icon Grid Design

Design an AAC touch screen with **W = 1.2 cm icons** arranged in a **D = 3.6 cm** grid spacing. Empirical constants from a touch-screen study: a = 0.205 s, b = 0.095 s/bit.

**Step 1 — Compute Index of Difficulty (ID).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \\
&= \log_2\!\left(\frac{3.6}{1.2} + 1\right) \\
&= \log_2(4) \\
&= 2 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Movement Time per selection.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\
&= 0.205 + 0.095 \times 2 \\
&= 0.395 \text{ s/selection}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute selection rate (selections/sec).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{1}{MT} = \frac{1}{0.395} \approx 2.53 \text{ sel/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute WPM using the Minspeak compaction factor of 2.1 selections/word.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WPM &= \frac{2.53 \text{ sel/s} \times 60 \text{ s/min}}{2.1 \text{ sel/word}} \\
&\approx 72.3 \text{ WPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** 72 WPM is **faster than typical conversational speech (~150 WPM is normal speech; 72 is "slow-but-functional" speech)**. This is why Minspeak + optimized Fitts' grid layout is the *gold-standard combination* studied in PEBRT752.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example — CIMT Dose Calculation

A stroke patient undergoes Constraint-Induced Movement Therapy. Constraint: **6 hours/day** for **10 days**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{CIMT} &= 6 \text{ h/day} \times 10 \text{ days} \\
&= 60 \text{ hours total}
\end{aligned}
$$

The EXCITE trial (2006) showed that **≥ 60 hours** of CIMT yields a clinically significant **Wolf Motor Function Test improvement of ≥ 0.4 standard deviations**, equivalent to an ARAT gain of ~10 points. This quantifies *rehabilitation acceleration* in the motor-recovery sense.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Representation Method

| User Profile | Best Representation | Best Acceleration | Expected WPM |
|--------------|---------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| Adult, post-stroke, dysarthric, literate | Orthographic + Minspeak | Letter/word prediction | 20–40 |
| Child, cerebral palsy, age 4–7 | PCS / ARASAAC pictograms | Dynamic scene display | 8–18 |
| ALS, advanced, single-switch | Whole-word + encoding | Frequency-weighted scanning | 2–8 |
| Locked-in, eye-gaze | Minspeak + Dwell-free | Semantic prediction | 15–30 |
| Global aphasia, literate | Blissymbols | Iconic encoding | 5–12 |
