## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define "Semantic Compaction" as used in AAC systems. List two of its advantages over letter-by-letter input.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Semantic compaction is a language representation technique in which the user presses a *sequence of one to three multi-meaning icons* (e.g., the Minspeak system), and the AAC device interprets the sequence through a semantic dictionary to produce a full word or phrase.
**Advantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Drastically reduced selection count** — average 2.1 selections per word vs. 5–8 for letter-by-letter, raising WPM from ~15 to ~40–70.
2. **Lower motor and cognitive load** — the icon keyboard uses only 45–128 icons, well within the working memory span, making it suitable for users with cerebral palsy, ALS, or stroke.
3. **Predictive and learnable** — the same icons recombine to express thousands of meanings.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*State Fitts' Law and explain its relevance in designing icon grids for AAC touch screens.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fitts' Law (1954) states that the time to move to and select a target is a logarithmic function of the distance to and width of the target:

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

where MT = movement time, D = distance, W = target width, and a, b are empirical constants (1 mark).
**Relevance to AAC (2 marks):**
- Larger icons and shorter inter-icon distances **reduce selection time** and **increase WPM**.
- For dysarthric users with motor impairment, designers must balance W (avoiding mis-taps) against D (avoiding too large a screen).
- Fitts' throughput (bits/s) is the ISO 9241-9 standard metric for evaluating AAC input methods.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, PEBRT752 Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the *five principal language representation methods* used in AAC devices. Compare their selection cost, learning load, and user suitability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the *three-layer architecture of an accelerating AAC system*. Compute the theoretical **maximum WPM** for a user with a **3-Hz selection rate** assuming the **empirical entropy of English is 11.82 bits/word**. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Five language representation methods (7 marks):**

| # | Method | Selection Cost | Learning Load | Best User |
|---|--------|----------------|---------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Orthographic letter grid** | 2–10 per word | High (literacy) | Literate adults with hand control |
| 2 | **Pictographic** (PCS, Blissymbols) | 1–4 per word | Moderate (iconic, transparent) | Children, cognitively impaired |
| 3 | **Semantic Compaction (Minspeak)** | 1–3 per word | Moderate (must learn sequences) | Moderate-severe dysarthria |
| 4 | **Whole-word / Phrase pages** | 1 per utterance | Low (memorization) | Eye-gaze, single-switch users |
| 5 | **Encoding (abbreviation / T9)** | 2–6 per word | Moderate (codes) | Spellers, mobile users |

> For each method: 1 mark for naming, 0.4 marks each for cost/load/suitability, 0.2 marks for example. Total 7 marks.

**(b) Three-layer architecture and WPM calculation (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** A clear 3-layer stack as in Section 4.1 with L1 (Representation), L2 (Prediction), L3 (Output) properly labelled and one technique listed per layer.

**WPM calculation (5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Selection rate} &= 3 \text{ sel/s} = 180 \text{ sel/min} \\
\text{Selections per word (entropy-limited)} &= 11.82 \text{ sel/word} \\
WPM_{max} &= \frac{180}{11.82} \\
&\approx 15.23 \text{ WPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** A 3-Hz direct-selection user can theoretically generate ~15 WPM at the information-theoretic limit. With Minspeak (2.1 sel/word) the same user reaches ~85 WPM, demonstrating the **5×–6× acceleration** achieved by language-representation engineering.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, PEBRT752]**

**(a)** Describe **Constraint-Induced Movement Therapy (CIMT)** as a *rehabilitation acceleration technique*. Compute the total dose for a 3-week program of 5 hours/day, 5 days/week. Discuss two mechanisms by which CIMT accelerates motor recovery. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to **Fitts' Law**, derive the expected **movement time per selection** for an AAC device with icons of width **W = 1.5 cm** and inter-icon distance **D = 4.5 cm**. Use empirical constants **a = 0.205 s** and **b = 0.095 s/bit**. Then compute the **WPM achievable** if the user employs Minspeak with an average of **2.1 selections/word**. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) CIMT (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** CIMT is a rehabilitation technique in which the *unaffected* limb is restrained (typically with a mitt, ~6 hours/day) while the *affected* limb is forced to perform intensive, **shaped**, supervised task practice. It exploits *use-dependent cortical reorganization* and *learned non-use reversal*.

**Dose calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Days} &= 3 \text{ weeks} \times 5 \text{ days/week} = 15 \text{ days} \\
\text{Hours/day} &= 5 \\
D_{CIMT} &= 5 \times 15 = 75 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **exceeds the 60-hour EXCITE-trial threshold** for clinically significant recovery.

**Two acceleration mechanisms (3 marks, 1.5 each):**
1. **Reversal of learned non-use** — by forcing use, the patient overcomes the *behavioral suppression* of the affected limb, allowing rapid re-engagement of existing neural pathways.
2. **Cortical map expansion (neuroplasticity)** — massed practice drives *long-term potentiation* in the contralesional motor cortex and *dendritic arborization*, accelerating functional map reorganization observed in fMRI.

**(b) Fitts' Law derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Index of Difficulty (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \\
&= \log_2\!\left(\frac{4.5}{1.5} + 1\right) \\
&= \log_2(4) \\
&= 2 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Movement Time (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\
&= 0.205 + 0.095 \times 2 \\
&= 0.395 \text{ s/selection}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Selection rate (1 mark):**

$$
R = \frac{1}{0.395} \approx 2.53 \text{ sel/s}
$$

**Step 4 — WPM calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WPM &= \frac{R \times 60}{SC} \\
&= \frac{2.53 \times 60}{2.1} \\
&\approx 72.3 \text{ WPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** ~72 WPM is competitive with normal conversational pace, validating the combined Fitts'-optimized layout + Minspeak as the *engineering gold standard* for high-performance AAC.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 2:**
> 1. **Confusing "representation" with "prediction"** — representation is *what the user selects from*; prediction is *how the device guesses the next selection*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for conflating the two.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — Fitts' Law throughput must be in **bits/s**, not "sel/s". WPM must be reported with the **2.1 sel/word** assumption stated.
> 3. **Misquoting CIMT** — the constraint is on the *unaffected* limb, not the affected limb. A common error loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the entropy derivation** — in 14-mark questions, examiners expect the **Shannon entropy → minimum selections/word → WPM** chain to be fully visible. Skipping any step costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Treating acceleration as a single thing** — always clarify *which* acceleration (communication rate vs. motor recovery vs. neural plasticity). Module 2 expects both senses.
> 6. **Using `|` for absolute value inside a markdown table** — this breaks the table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` only.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Language Representation** = the *symbol system* used to encode meaning on an AAC device.
- ✅ Five main methods: **Orthographic, Pictographic, Semantic Compaction (Minspeak), Whole-word, Encoding/Abbreviation**.
- ✅ **Semantic compaction** (Baker, 1980s, Prentke Romich) is the *most powerful representation*, achieving ~2.1 selections/word using ~45–128 icons.
- ✅ **Acceleration Techniques** have **two meanings** in KTU Module 2: (1) **AAC rate enhancement** (prediction, encoding, Fitts' optimization) and (2) **motor-recovery acceleration** (CIMT, robotics, BCI, brain stimulation).
- ✅ **Fitts' Law**: $MT = a + b \log_2(D/W + 1)$ — fundamental to AAC icon-grid design.
- ✅ **Shannon entropy of English**: ~**11.82 bits/word** → sets the *theoretical* minimum selections/word.
- ✅ **Keystroke Savings Ratio (KSR)**: clinical threshold = **≥ 50%**.
- ✅ **Typical WPM benchmarks**: normal speech 150–180; literate AAC 5–15; with Minspeak 40–75; with abbreviation encoding 50–90.
- ✅ **CIMT dose threshold** (EXCITE trial, 2006): **≥ 60 hours** → clinically meaningful Wolf Motor Function Test gain.
- ✅ **Three-layer AAC architecture**: L1 Representation, L2 Prediction, L3 Output.
- ✅ **Prediction algorithms** rely on **n-gram language models** (unigram, bigram, trigram); modern systems add **semantic** and **user-frequency** caching.
- ✅ **Mermaid diagrams** in your answer scripts should follow the **flowchart** or **sequenceDiagram** patterns, never use `end` as a node ID, and avoid markdown bold (`**`) inside node labels.
- ✅ **Minspeak sequence examples** to memorize: 🌧️+💧 = "storm", 🍕+🍴 = "lunch", ❤️+🔥 = "love", 👁️+📖 = "read".
- ✅ **B Blissymbol "key symbols"** are combinable into thousands of words; the system is **translucent** (icon resembles meaning).
- ✅ **Eye-gaze + Minspeak** is the current best-practice for **late-stage ALS** communication, achieving 15–35 WPM.
- ✅ **CIMT accelerates recovery** by reversing *learned non-use* and driving *cortical reorganization*; robotic therapy accelerates via **high-repetition dosing** (>1000 reps/session).
- ✅ **BCI + Motor Imagery** is a *last-resort* acceleration pathway; clinically useful **decoding accuracy > 70%**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Applications in Rehabilitation Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computer Applications in Rehabilitation Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer Applications in Rehabilitation Engineering** refers to the systematic integration of computational hardware, software algorithms, and digital information systems into the assessment, design, manufacture, prescription, and monitoring of rehabilitative interventions for persons with disabilities.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Outcome mapping **CO2 – Module 2**), this discipline encompasses the deployment of:

- **Computer-Aided Design / Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAD/CAM)** in prosthetics and orthotics
- **Gait Analysis & Motion Capture Software** for quantitative biomechanical assessment
- **Database Management Systems (DBMS)** for patient records, outcome tracking, and clinical decision support
- **Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR)** for therapeutic intervention
- **Tele-rehabilitation Platforms** for remote service delivery
- **Signal Processing Algorithms** for EMG, EEG, and biofeedback applications
- **Electronic Aids to Daily Living (EADL)** with environmental control units
- **Robotic and Mechatronic Systems** with embedded microcontrollers
- **Expert Systems and Artificial Intelligence (AI)** for prescription support

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a computer in rehabilitation the same way a **central nervous system (CNS)** works in the human body. The CNS receives signals from sensors (eyes, skin, proprioceptors), processes them, and sends motor commands to muscles. Similarly, the rehabilitation computer *receives* data from sensors (force plates, EMG electrodes, motion cameras), *processes* it using algorithms, and *commands* actuators (motors in orthoses, environmental controls, screen displays). Without the computer acting as this "translator" and "decision-maker," the modern rehabilitation clinic would be reduced to guesswork, manual measurement, and one-size-fits-all prescriptions.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Computational Metrics

The following are the **standard quantitative metrics** universally adopted in this domain (must be memorized for KTU ESE):

- **Sampling Frequency for EMG:** typically **1 kHz to 2 kHz** (Nyquist criterion for surface EMG bandwidth of 0–500 Hz)
- **Frame Rate for 3D Motion Capture:** typically **100 Hz to 200 Hz** (Vicon, Qualisys, OptiTrack systems)
- **Digital Resolution of Pressure Sensors:** **12-bit to 16-bit ADC** (resolves 1 part in **4096** to **65536**)
- **CAD Geometric Tolerance:** typically **±0.1 mm** for custom socket fabrication
- **Tele-rehabilitation Latency Threshold:** must be **< 300 ms** for real-time visual feedback
- **Standard Gait Velocity Norm:** **1.30–1.46 m/s** for healthy adults (Perry & Burnfield, 2010)
- **Fitts' Law Index of Performance (IP):** measured in **bits per second (bps)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024, the most heavily examined sub-topics are (a) **CAD/CAM in Prosthetics & Orthotics**, (b) **Gait Analysis Software**, and (c) **Virtual Reality in Rehabilitation Therapy**. Allocate at least **60%** of your preparation time to these three areas.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Five Major Computer Application Domains

#### A. Computer-Aided Design & Manufacturing (CAD/CAM) in Prosthetics/Orthotics

- **Why?** Manual socket fabrication is time-consuming (4–6 hours), operator-dependent, and produces inconsistent results.
- **How?** The patient's residual limb is digitized (3D scanner or MRI/CT segmentation). Software (e.g., **Canfit, Omega Tracer, BioSculptor, Meshmixer**) generates a positive model, which is then modified (relief, build-ups, trim lines) virtually before numerical-control (NC) code drives a **carver (subtractive)** or a **3D printer (additive)**.
- **Why does this matter in engineering practice?** A **CAD-derived socket** fits within **±2 mm** volumetric accuracy, drastically reducing ulceration risk in diabetic amputees.

#### B. Computerized Gait Analysis Systems

- **Why?** Observational gait analysis has an inter-rater reliability of only **0.4–0.6 (Cohen's κ)**. Instrumented analysis improves this to **0.85–0.95**.
- **How?** Multi-camera optoelectronic systems track **retroreflective markers** placed on anatomical landmarks. Force plates measure **Ground Reaction Force (GRF)**. EMG records muscle activity. All data is synchronized and processed to compute joint kinematics, kinetics, and dynamic electromyography.

#### C. Virtual Reality (VR) / Augmented Reality (AR) Rehabilitation

- **Why?** Traditional therapy suffers from low patient motivation and poor adherence (**only 30–50%** adherence rate).
- **How?** Head-Mounted Displays (HMDs) like **Oculus Quest, HTC Vive, Microsoft HoloLens** render interactive environments where patients perform task-specific training (reaching, grasping, balancing). Performance metrics (reaction time, accuracy, range) are logged automatically.

#### D. Database Management in Rehabilitation

- **Why?** A multidisciplinary rehabilitation team (physiatrist, physiotherapist, prosthetist, occupational therapist, social worker) requires **shared, structured, queryable patient data**.
- **How?** Relational databases (SQL) or specialized **Electronic Medical Records (EMR)** like **Epic, Cerner, or custom Rehab-specific systems (e.g., TherapySource, WebPT)** store demographics, diagnosis (ICD-10 codes), interventions (CPT codes), and outcome measures (FIM, Barthel Index).

#### E. Tele-rehabilitation & Web-based Platforms

- **Why?** Geographic, economic, and mobility barriers prevent **~80%** of rural patients from accessing specialist rehabilitation.
- **How?** Synchronous (real-time video conferencing with therapist) and asynchronous (upload of exercise videos, sensor data) platforms using **HL7, DICOM, and FHIR interoperability standards** enable remote assessment and prescription adjustments.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule for Tables:** All mathematical delimiters are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Memorize every entry below.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Standard Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---------------------|----------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Gait Velocity | $V \;=\; \dfrac{L_{stride}}{T_{stride}} \;=\; L_{step} \times f_{cadence}$ | **m/s** | Quantifies walking speed |
| 2 | Cadence | $f_{cadence} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{steps}}{\text{time}}$ | **steps/min** | Steps per minute |
| 3 | Stride Length | $L_{stride} \;=\; 2 \times L_{step}$ | **m** | Distance per full gait cycle |
| 4 | Stance / Swing Ratio | $R_{ss} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{stance}}{T_{swing}}$ | dimensionless | Normal $\approx 60/40$ |
| 5 | Fitts' Law (Movement Time) | $MT \;=\; a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(2 \cdot \dfrac{A}{W}\right)$ | **s** | Predicts pointing/selection time |
| 6 | Index of Performance (Fitts) | $IP \;=\; \dfrac{\log_{2}\!\left(2A/W\right)}{MT}$ | **bits/s** | Assesses assistive input device speed |
| 7 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_{s} \;\geq\; 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | **Hz** | Minimum sampling for EMG |
| 8 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) | $SNR_{dB} \;=\; 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | **dB** | EMG/EEG amplifier quality |
| 9 | Joint Moment (Inverse Dynamics) | $M_{joint} \;=\; I \cdot \alpha + F \cdot r$ | **N·m** | Kinetics from force-plate data |
| 10 | 3D Point Reconstruction (Camera Pair) | $P_{3D} \;=\; f(T_{1}, T_{2}, u_{1}, v_{1}, u_{2}, v_{2})$ | **mm** | DLT or stereo-triangulation |
| 11 | Power Output (Wheelchair) | $P \;=\; F \cdot V$ | **W** | Energy cost of propulsion |
| 12 | Energy Expenditure (Walking) | $E \;=\; \int_{0}^{T} P(t)\, dt$ | **J** | Metabolic cost of gait |
| 13 | Harris-Benedict BMR (Males) | $BMR \;=\; 66 + 13.7W + 5H - 6.8A$ | **kcal/day** | Caloric need assessment |
| 14 | Hounsfield Unit Conversion | $HU \;=\; 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}$ | dimensionless | CT bone-soft tissue segmentation |
| 15 | CAD Mesh Resolution | $\Delta x_{mesh} \;\geq\; 2 \cdot f_{scan\,res}$ | **mm** | STL file generation quality |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Biomedical Industry:** Software-driven prosthetic socket design reduces clinician time by **~70%**.
- **Automotive (Adapted Vehicles):** CAD-based hand-control geometry is validated through Finite Element Analysis (FEA).
- **Aerospace:** Motion-capture technology developed for pilot training has been adapted for balance assessment in vestibular disorders.
- **Sports Engineering:** VR-based neuro-rehabilitation protocols originate from sports performance simulation.
- **Assistive Robotics:** Algorithms (e.g., **Impedance Control, Adaptive Control**) for powered exoskeletons are validated first in computer simulation (MATLAB/Simulink, ROS/Gazebo) before hardware deployment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gait Velocity from Stride Length and Cadence

**Starting Premise (Given):**
A patient's gait is recorded. Two complete strides occur in a measured time $T_{total} = 2.4$ seconds. The distance covered in this interval (measured heel-strike to heel-strike of the same foot) is $L_{stride} = 2.88$ m. The patient takes $N = 4$ steps in this interval.

**Step 1 — Define Stride Time**

$$
T_{stride} \;=\; \frac{T_{total}}{N_{strides}}
$$

We had **2 strides** in 2.4 s.

$$
T_{stride} \;=\; \frac{2.4 \,\text{s}}{2} \;=\; 1.2 \,\text{s}
$$

*Explanation:* Stride time is the duration of one complete gait cycle (one heel-strike of one foot to the next heel-strike of the *same* foot).

**Step 2 — Compute Cadence**

Cadence is the number of steps per minute. We had 4 steps in 2.4 s.

$$
f_{cadence} \;=\; \frac{N_{steps}}{T_{total}} \;=\; \frac{4 \,\text{steps}}{2.4 \,\text{s}} \;=\; 1.667 \,\text{steps/s}
$$

Convert to steps/min:

$$
f_{cadence} \;=\; 1.667 \times 60 \;=\; 100 \,\text{steps/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Gait Velocity (Method A — Stride-based)**

$$
V \;=\; \frac{L_{stride}}{T_{stride}} \;=\; \frac{2.88 \,\text{m}}{1.2 \,\text{s}} \;=\; 2.4 \,\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Gait Velocity (Method B — Step-based, verification)**

First, step length:
$$
L_{step} \;=\; \frac{L_{stride}}{2} \;=\; 1.44 \,\text{m}
$$

Then:
$$
V \;=\; L_{step} \times f_{cadence} \;=\; 1.44 \,\text{m} \times 1.667 \,\text{steps/s} \;=\; 2.4 \,\text{m/s} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Clinical Interpretation**

> [!NOTE]
> The computed velocity of **2.4 m/s** is **above** the healthy adult norm of **1.30–1.46 m/s** (Perry & Burnfield). This unusually high value, combined with a stride length of 2.88 m, suggests either a **running gait** (where double-support vanishes) or a measurement error. The clinician must verify the trial.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fitts' Law for an Assistive Pointing Device (EADL Evaluation)

**Context:** A head-tracking pointer used by a person with quadriplegia must be evaluated. The user performs pointing tasks where the **distance from start to target** is $A$ (Amplitude) and the **target width** is $W$ (Width).

**Step 1 — Index of Difficulty**

$$
ID \;=\; \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right) \quad \text{[bits]}
$$

**Step 2 — Movement Time Prediction**

$$
MT \;=\; a + b \cdot ID
$$

where $a, b$ are device-specific empirically determined constants. For a head-tracking cursor, typical values are $a = 0.32$ s, $b = 0.18$ s/bit.

**Step 3 — Worked Example**

Given: $A = 200$ px, $W = 40$ px.

$$
ID \;=\; \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2 \times 200}{40}\right) \;=\; \log_{2}(10) \;\approx\; 3.322 \,\text{bits}
$$

Then:

$$
MT \;=\; 0.32 + 0.18 \times 3.322 \;\approx\; 0.918 \,\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Index of Performance**

$$
IP \;=\; \frac{ID}{MT} \;=\; \frac{3.322}{0.918} \;\approx\; 3.62 \,\text{bits/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Typical head-tracking IP for assistive use is **2–5 bits/s**, compared to a normal mouse IP of **8–10 bits/s**. The reduction is the engineering cost of accessibility — designers must compensate via **larger targets, dwell-click, or sticky-target** strategies.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — EMG Sampling Rate via Nyquist Criterion

**Context:** Surface EMG (sEMG) signal energy is concentrated in $0$–$500$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Identify the Maximum Useful Frequency**

$$
f_{max} \;=\; 500 \,\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Nyquist-Shannon Theorem**

$$
f_{s,\min} \;=\; 2 \times f_{max} \;=\; 1000 \,\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Engineering Safety Factor**

In practice, a factor of **2× to 4×** is used to preserve waveform fidelity and reduce aliasing in the presence of sharp motor-unit action potentials.

$$
f_{s,\text{typical}} \;=\; 2 \text{ to } 4 \times 1000 \;=\; 2000 \text{ to } 4000 \,\text{Hz}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students must remember that **sEMG = 1 kHz minimum (typically 2 kHz)** and **EEG = 256 Hz minimum** (since EEG band is 0–100 Hz). Mentioning **anti-aliasing filters** before the ADC is a common **valuation plus point**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Computer Tool vs. Clinical Task

| Clinical Task | Computer Tool Used | Software Examples | Output |
|---------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------|
| Custom socket design | 3D scanner + CAD | Canfit, BioSculptor, Meshmixer | STL mesh for carving/3D printing |
| Walking pattern assessment | Motion capture + force plates | Vicon Nexus, Qualisys, BTS Smart | Kinematic + kinetic graphs |
| Muscle activation study | sEMG system + DAQ | Delsys Trigno, Noraxon MR3 | Time-normalized EMG envelopes |
| Upper-limb therapy | VR/AR headset + haptics | Oculus Quest + bespoke Unity | Engagement metrics, range |
| Patient record management | Relational DB / EMR | Epic, TherapySource, WebPT | ICD-10, CPT, FIM, Barthel scores |
| Remote therapy delivery | Tele-rehab platform | Physitrack, PhysiApp, Zoom Healthcare | Adherence logs, video feedback |
| Cognitive assessment | Computerized test batteries | MoCA-digital, Cambridge Neuropsychological | Standardized z-scores |
| Powered wheelchair control | Embedded microcontroller | Arduino, STM32, ROS-based modules | PWM motor commands |
| Speech/communication | AAC device | Tobii Dynavox, Proloquo2Go, Grid 3 | Synthesized speech output |
| Pressure ulcer risk | Smart insole sensors | Tekscan F-Scan, XSensor | Pressure maps (kPa) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — CAD/CAM Workflow for a Trans-tibial Prosthetic Socket

This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing how the patient's residual limb geometry becomes a manufactured socket.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Patient Residual Limb] --> B1[3D Laser Scanner or CT Segmentation]
    B1 --> C1[Point Cloud or Volumetric Data]
    C1 --> D1[CAD Software: Canfit or Meshmixer]
    D1 --> E1[Virtual Socket Modification: Relief, Build-ups, Trim Lines]
    E1 --> F1[STL Mesh Export]
    F1 --> G1{Manufacturing Route}
    G1 --> H1[Subtractive: 5-axis CNC Carver]
    G1 --> H2[Additive: FDM or SLS 3D Printer]
    H1 --> I1[Positive Foam Model]
    H2 --> I2[Direct Printed Socket]
    I1 --> J1[Lamination or Thermoforming]
    I2 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Finished Custom Socket]
    K1 --> L1[Fitting on Patient]
    L1 --> M1[Clinical Feedback Loop]
    M1 --> D1
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Block A1–C1** = data acquisition
- **Block D1–F1** = virtual design (CAD)
- **Block G1** = manufacturing decision (CAM)
- **Block H1–J1** = fabrication
- **Block M1** = iterative refinement — a hallmark of computer-integrated design

---

### 4.2 Diagram B — Tele-rehabilitation System Architecture

This is a **functional client-server architecture** showing how a remote therapist interacts with a home-based patient.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Patient at Home] --> P2[Webcam + Wearable Sensors]
    P2 --> P3[Patient Tablet or Laptop]
    P3 --> P4[Internet / 4G-5G]
    P4 --> S1[Cloud Server HL7-FHIR Compliant]
    S1 --> S2[Therapist Dashboard]
    S2 --> T1[Remote Therapist]
    T1 --> S2
    S2 --> P4
    P4 --> P3
    P3 --> P5[Real-time Video + Exercise Cues]
    P5 --> P1
    S1 --> DB[(Patient EMR Database)]
    S1 --> AI[AI Exercise Classifier]
    AI --> S2
```

**Key Architectural Points:**
- **HL7 / FHIR** standards ensure interoperability between devices.
- **AI classifier** auto-tags exercise quality without manual review.
- **EMR database** maintains longitudinal outcome records.

---

### 4.3 Diagram C — Computerized Gait Analysis Laboratory Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Lab[Instrumented Gait Laboratory]
        M1[Marker Vicon Camera 1] --> PC[Central Synchronization PC]
        M2[Marker Vicon Camera 2] --> PC
        M3[Marker Vicon Camera 3] --> PC
        M4[Marker Vicon Camera 4] --> PC
        FP1[Force Plate 1] --> PC
        FP2[Force Plate 2] --> PC
        EMG1[sEMG Electrode Array] --> PC
        EMG2[sEMG Electrode Array] --> PC
        PC --> SW[Processing Software Nexus, Polygon]
        SW --> OP[Operator Workstation]
        SW --> REP[Clinical Report PDF]
    end
    PT[Walking Patient] --> M1
    PT --> M2
    PT --> M3
    PT --> M4
    PT --> FP1
    PT --> FP2
    PT --> EMG1
    PT --> EMG2
```

**Engineering Note:** All subsystems must be **hardware-synchronized** through a common clock (typically a **TTL trigger**) so that marker trajectories, ground reaction forces, and EMG events are aligned in time with **sub-millisecond jitter**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A expects concise, definition-oriented answers of **~80–100 words**. Avoid long derivations in Part A. Use bullet points where possible.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBRT752]**
**Define CAD/CAM. List any four advantages of using CAD/CAM in the design and fabrication of prosthetic sockets over conventional hand-crafting methods. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**CAD (Computer-Aided Design)** is the use of computer software to create, modify, and analyze 2D/3D models of a product. **CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing)** is the use of computer-controlled machines to fabricate the designed product from its digital model.

**Advantages in prosthetics:**
- **Precision** — geometric accuracy of **±0.1 mm** versus hand-crafted variability
- **Reproducibility** — identical copies from the same digital file
- **Time saving** — design time reduced from hours to minutes
- **Patient database** — digital models archived for future refabrication
- **Remote design** — clinician and technician can collaborate across distances

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBRT752]**
**What is a "Virtual Reality (VR) Rehabilitation System"? List any three clinical applications and two limitations. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **VR Rehabilitation System** is a computer-generated interactive 3D environment, displayed through a Head-Mounted Display (HMD) or projection screen, used to deliver task-specific therapeutic exercises to patients with neurological or musculoskeletal impairments.

**Clinical Applications:**
- Post-stroke upper-limb motor recovery (reaching, grasping)
- Balance and vestibular training in elderly fall-risk patients
- Phantom-limb pain reduction using mirror-VR therapy
- Cognitive rehabilitation in traumatic brain injury
- Pediatric developmental therapy (autism, cerebral palsy)

**Limitations:**
- **Cybersickness** (nausea, disorientation) in some users
- **High cost** of HMDs and haptic interfaces
- **Limited haptic realism** — most systems lack true force feedback
- Risk of **over-reliance on technology** at the cost of functional real-world training

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. In Part B, students often **write generic essay-style introductions** that do not address the specific sub-part asked. **Waste of 2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Diagrams are MANDATORY** for full marks. A textual answer without a labeled block diagram in a "Computer Applications" question will lose **at least 2–3 marks**.
> 3. When asked to "explain" or "describe," do **not** write a one-line definition. KTU examiners expect at least **3–4 bullet points** with engineering specifics (e.g., resolution, frequency, units).
> 4. In numerical sub-parts, always **state the formula, substitute the values, show the result with units, and interpret clinically**. Marks are split across these four sub-steps.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEBRT752, Module 2]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete **CAD/CAM workflow** for the design and fabrication of a custom trans-tibial prosthetic socket. List any four advantages of this digital workflow over conventional hand-crafting. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A stroke patient is undergoing VR-based upper-limb rehabilitation. The patient performs a pointing task in which the **target distance** is $A = 300$ px and the **target width** is $W = 50$ px. For the head-tracking system used, Fitts' law constants are $a = 0.25$ s and $b = 0.20$ s/bit.
   (i) Calculate the **Index of Difficulty (ID)** in bits.
   (ii) Calculate the **predicted Movement Time (MT)**.
   (iii) Calculate the **Index of Performance (IP)** in bits/s and comment on whether the device is acceptable for the patient. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**
*[Draw a block diagram with the following sequential blocks — Patient Residual Limb → 3D Scanner / CT → Point Cloud → CAD Software (Canfit) → Virtual Modification (relief, build-up, trim lines) → STL Export → CAM (CNC Carving / 3D Printing) → Lamination → Finished Socket → Fitting on Patient → Feedback Loop to CAD]*

**Explanation in 4 bullet points (2 marks):**
- The patient's residual limb is **digitized** using a hand-held 3D laser scanner or, for complex cases, **CT/MRI segmentation** producing DICOM data.
- The point cloud is imported into **CAD software** (e.g., Canfit, Meshmixer, or BioSculptor), where the prosthetist applies **reliefs** (over bony prominences), **build-ups** (over soft tissue), and **trim lines** (defining the socket brim) — all virtually.
- The modified 3D mesh is exported as an **STL file** and sent to either a **multi-axis CNC carver** (subtractive) or a **3D printer** (additive) to produce a positive model or the socket directly.
- The patient is fit-tested; any discomfort is **fed back to the CAD stage** for digital iteration, eliminating re-casting of the limb.

**Four Advantages (1 mark — 0.25 each):**
- Geometric accuracy within **±0.1 mm** vs. hand-crafted
- Fabrication time reduced from 4–6 h to 30–60 min
- Digital archive for **rapid refabrication** if socket is lost/damaged
- Enables **remote tele-fitting** between clinician and technician

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Index of Difficulty (2 marks):**
$$
ID \;=\; \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right) \;=\; \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2 \times 300}{50}\right) \;=\; \log_{2}(12) \;\approx\; 3.585 \,\text{bits}
$$

**(ii) Movement Time (2 marks):**
$$
MT \;=\; a + b \cdot ID \;=\; 0.25 + 0.20 \times 3.585 \;=\; 0.25 + 0.717 \;=\; 0.967 \,\text{s}
$$

**(iii) Index of Performance (2 marks):**
$$
IP \;=\; \frac{ID}{MT} \;=\; \frac{3.585}{0.967} \;\approx\; 3.71 \,\text{bits/s}
$$

**Clinical Comment (1 mark):**
> [!NOTE]
> A typical head-tracking assistive device has an **IP of 2–5 bits/s**. Our value of **3.71 bits/s** falls in the **acceptable range**, confirming that the device is suitable for the patient. To improve performance, the designer can **increase target width** (e.g., use larger buttons in the UI), which reduces ID and thus reduces MT.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, PEBRT752, Module 2]**
**(a)** What is **computerized gait analysis**? With a block diagram, describe the major subsystems of an instrumented 3D gait laboratory. List any four clinical indications where such analysis is essential for decision-making. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A paraplegic patient propels a manual wheelchair. During a 30-second test on a roller, the average **propulsion force** is $F = 35$ N at an **average velocity** of $V = 1.2$ m/s. The patient takes **45 strokes** in the 30 s. Calculate:
   (i) The **average power output**.
   (ii) The **cadence** in strokes per minute.
   (iii) The **work done per stroke**.
   (iv) Comment on whether the patient's energy expenditure is within sustainable limits for community ambulation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 marks):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computerized Gait Analysis** is the quantitative, instrumented measurement of human walking using synchronized motion-capture cameras, force platforms, surface electromyography, and dedicated software to produce objective data on **kinematics** (joint angles), **kinetics** (joint moments and powers), and **dynamic EMG** (muscle activity).

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
*[A 3D gait laboratory has four major subsystems: (1) Motion Capture Subsystem (6–12 IR cameras, retroreflective markers, central sync unit), (2) Force Measurement Subsystem (force plates embedded in walkway), (3) EMG Subsystem (surface electrodes + wireless transmitter), (4) Data Processing Subsystem (PC + Vicon Nexus/Polygon software + database). All subsystems synchronized via a TTL trigger.]*

**Four Clinical Indications (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
- **Cerebral palsy** — pre- and post-operative evaluation of gait deviation
- **Post-stroke** hemiplegia — distinguishing compensations from primary deficits
- **Prosthetic alignment** of a new lower-limb prosthesis
- **Pre-surgical planning** for tendon lengthening or osteotomy
- **Sports injury** — running gait retraining

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Average Power Output (2 marks):**
$$
P_{avg} \;=\; F \times V \;=\; 35 \,\text{N} \times 1.2 \,\text{m/s} \;=\; 42 \,\text{W}
$$

**(ii) Cadence (1.5 marks):**
$$
f_{cadence} \;=\; \frac{45 \,\text{strokes}}{30 \,\text{s}} \times 60 \;=\; 90 \,\text{strokes/min}
$$

**(iii) Work Done Per Stroke (2 marks):**

Total work in 30 s:
$$
W_{total} \;=\; P_{avg} \times t \;=\; 42 \,\text{W} \times 30 \,\text{s} \;=\; 1260 \,\text{J}
$$

Work per stroke:
$$
W_{stroke} \;=\; \frac{1260 \,\text{J}}{45} \;=\; 28 \,\text{J/stroke}
$$

**(iv) Clinical Comment (1.5 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> The literature reports that sustainable **manual wheelchair propulsion power for community ambulation** is **≤ 35 W** with cadence of **60–80 strokes/min**. Our patient shows **42 W** (slightly above the limit) and a cadence of **90 strokes/min** (above the recommended range). This indicates **inefficient propulsion technique** — the patient is likely using **short, rapid strokes** instead of long, semi-circular pushes. **Engineering recommendation:** provide a **gear-ratio adjustable hand-rim** (e.g., MagicWheels or natural-Fit) to reduce cadence demand and improve efficiency. Re-test after 4 weeks of training.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Memorize):**

- **CAD/CAM Prosthetic Workflow:** Residual Limb → 3D Scan / CT → CAD Modification (relief, build-up, trim line) → STL → CAM (Carve or Print) → Fit → Feedback.
- **CAD Tolerance:** ±0.1 mm. **CAM Methods:** Subtractive (CNC) and Additive (3D printing — FDM, SLS, SLA).
- **Gait Norms:** Velocity 1.3–1.46 m/s, Cadence ~110 steps/min, Stance/Swing ≈ 60/40.
- **Stride = 2 × Step.** Stride Time = time for one full cycle.
- **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(2A/W)$. $IP = ID / MT$. Normal mouse IP ≈ 8–10 bits/s; head-tracker IP ≈ 2–5 bits/s.
- **Nyquist:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. sEMG → 1–2 kHz, EEG → 256 Hz.
- **Force Plate:** measures Ground Reaction Force (GRF); inverse dynamics computes joint moments.
- **Inverse Dynamics Equation:** $M_{joint} = I \alpha + F \cdot r$.
- **VR Rehabilitation Indications:** stroke, cerebral palsy, balance disorders, phantom-limb pain, TBI.
- **Tele-rehab Latency:** must be < 300 ms for real-time feedback; standards: **HL7, DICOM, FHIR**.
- **EMR Codes:** **ICD-10** for diagnosis, **CPT** for procedure, **FIM / Barthel Index** for outcome.
- **EMG SNR:** measured in dB; $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$. Typical sEMG SNR: **20–40 dB**.
- **Hounsfield Unit (HU):** bone ≈ +1000, water = 0, air = −1000. Used in CT-based orthotic design.
- **Wheelchair Sustainable Power:** ≤ 35 W for community ambulation; cadence 60–80 strokes/min.
- **Standard Frame Rate for 3D Motion Capture:** 100–200 Hz.
- **Anti-aliasing filter** is a MUST before ADC in sEMG acquisition (mention for valuation plus point).
- **Cybersickness** is the principal limitation of HMD-based VR.
- **CAD Software Names to Remember:** Canfit, BioSculptor, Meshmixer, Omega Tracer.
- **Gait Lab Software Names to Remember:** Vicon Nexus, Qualisys, BTS Smart, Polygon.
- **Tele-rehab Platform Examples:** Physitrack, PhysiApp, Zoom Healthcare.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Valuation Tip:** In KTU 2024 ESE, **always include a labeled block diagram** in any 14-mark question related to this module. A textual answer without a diagram is treated as incomplete and typically loses **2–3 marks** even if the content is otherwise correct.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Telecommunications and Web Accessibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Telecommunications and Web Accessibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Telecommunications Accessibility

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Telecommunications Accessibility** is the design, development, and deployment of communication systems, devices, and services that enable persons with disabilities (especially hearing, speech, visual, and motor impairments) to access, transmit, and receive information on an equal basis with others. It encompasses both hardware (phones, TTYs, amplifiers) and services (relay services, captioned telephony, video relay).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Rehabilitation Engineering Act and modern disability frameworks (UNCRPD, RPwD Act 2016 India) treat telecommunications accessibility as a *fundamental right*, not a luxury. Engineers must design with Universal Design (UD) principles.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a staircase-only entrance to a building. A wheelchair user cannot enter. Similarly, an audio-only phone line is a "staircase" for a deaf person. **Telecommunications accessibility is the ramp, the elevator, and the automatic door** — it modifies the communication medium so that the message can flow regardless of the user's sensory or motor abilities.

Think of a **TT machine as a "digital scribe"** — it converts typed characters into audible tones (and vice versa) so two people on regular phone lines can communicate.

### Core Sub-Topics Covered Under Telecommunications Accessibility
1. **TTY / TDD (TeleTYpewriter / Telecommunications Device for the Deaf)**
2. **Text Telephone (TT)**
3. **Relay Services** (Voice / Video / IP Relay)
4. **Hearing Aid Compatibility (HAC)**
5. **Amplified Telephones**
6. **Captioned Telephones (CapTel)**
7. **Mobile Accessibility (Smartphones, Apps)**
8. **Emergency Services Access (e.g., 911 / 112 text)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Frequency:** TTY devices traditionally communicate using **Baudot code at 45.45 baud (carriers at 1275 Hz and 2225 Hz)**. This is a high-yield KTU fact.

---

## 1.2 Web Accessibility

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Web Accessibility** means that websites, web applications, digital documents, and multimedia content are designed and developed so that people with disabilities can perceive, understand, navigate, interact with, and contribute to the Web. It is governed internationally by the **WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines)**, currently at version **2.2 (published October 2023)**, authored by the W3C.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a web page as a **physical museum**. A ramp + tactile floor strips + audio guides + Braille labels = an accessible museum. Web accessibility does the equivalent for digital content — providing **alt text** (audio guide), **keyboard navigation** (ramp), **captions** (Braille labels), and **ARIA landmarks** (tactile floor strips) so that every user, regardless of disability, gets an equivalent experience.

### The Four Pillars of Web Accessibility (POUR)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **POUR** is the cornerstone of WCAG and is a guaranteed KTU 2024 exam question.

| Pillar | Meaning | Example |
|--------|---------|---------|
| **P**erceivable | Information must be presentable to users in ways they can perceive. | Alt text for images, captions for video. |
| **O**perable | Interface components and navigation must be operable. | Keyboard-only navigation, no seizure-inducing flashes. |
| **U**nderstandable | Information and UI operation must be understandable. | Plain language, consistent navigation. |
| **R**obust | Content must be robust enough to be interpreted by current and future assistive tech. | Valid HTML, ARIA roles. |

---

## 1.3 Cross-Modal Mapping Between the Two Domains

| User Impairment | Telecommunications Solution | Web Accessibility Solution |
|-----------------|------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Hearing loss | TTY, CapTel, Relay Services | Closed captions, transcripts |
| Visual impairment | Voice-activated dialing, screen-reader-compatible phone | Screen readers (JAWS, NVDA), alt text |
| Speech impairment | Voice Prosthesis, AAC over VoIP, VRS | Text-to-Speech (TTS) output |
| Motor impairment | Hands-free/Bluetooth, voice control | Eye-tracking, switch input, voice nav |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 TTY / TDD — Operational Architecture

A **TDD (Telecommunications Device for the Deaf)** is essentially a **keyboard + small display + acoustic coupler/modem** that converts typed characters into frequency-shift-keyed (FSK) audio tones transmitted over the regular voice telephone network.

### Operational Logic Steps
1. **User A** types a message on the TDD keyboard.
2. Characters are encoded into **Baudot code (5-bit)** at **45.45 baud**.
3. The encoded signal modulates a carrier:
   - **Mark (1 bit)** → **1275 Hz** tone
   - **Space (0 bit)** → **2225 Hz** tone
4. The tone travels over the PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network) just like speech.
5. The receiving TDD demodulates the FSK tones back into Baudot, then into ASCII for display.
6. The receiver reads the message on an LCD/LED display.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin:** TDD was invented by **Robert H. Weitbrecht** in 1964 (a deaf physicist), enabling deaf users to communicate over standard phone lines — a landmark moment in rehabilitation engineering.

---

## 2.2 Relay Services — The Communication Bridge

A **Relay Service** is a third-party intermediary service that connects a standard phone user with a TTY/text user.

### Types of Relay Services

| Type | Description | Use Case |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **TTY Relay** | Operator (CA – Communications Assistant) reads TTY text aloud to voice caller and types voice caller's response back to TTY user. | Legacy TTY-to-voice calls. |
| **Voice Carry Over (VCO)** | Deaf user speaks for themselves; operator transcribes voice caller's reply. | Users with good speech but hearing loss. |
| **Hearing Carry Over (HCO)** | Hard-of-hearing user listens; operator speaks their typed text. | Speech-impaired but hearing-capable users. |
| **Video Relay Service (VRS)** | Sign-language interpreter via video call. | ASL users with broadband. |
| **IP Relay** | Text typed on a web/app interface; relayed by operator via voice. | Modern smartphone-based text calling. |
| **Speech-to-Speech (STS)** | Trained operator repeats/rephrases unclear speech. | Users with speech impediments. |

---

## 2.3 Hearing Aid Compatibility (HAC)

The **HAC rating** quantifies how well a phone works with a hearing aid set to "T-coil" (Telecoil) mode. The FCC mandates two metrics:

### KTU Formula Sheet — Telecommunications

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit | Notes |
|-----------|----------------------|------|-------|
| **Baud rate of TTY** | $B = 45.45$ | baud | Fixed by ITU-T for TDD |
| **FSK frequencies** | $f_{mark} = 1275$ Hz, $f_{space} = 2225$ Hz | Hz | Originate/Answer tones vary internationally |
| **Baudot code word length** | $n = 5$ | bits | Per character (Letters/Figures shift) |
| **HAC Rating (M-rating)** | $M = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ | dimensionless | Microphone coupling immunity |
| **HAC Rating (T-rating)** | $T = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ | dimensionless | Telecoil coupling strength |
| **Information rate (Baudot)** | $R = B \times \log_2(2) = 45.45$ | bps | Binary FSK; 1 bit per baud |
| **WCAG Conformance Levels** | A, AA, AAA | — | A=minimum, AA=legal standard, AAA=gold |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "**M**icrophone for **M**, **T**elecoil for **T**" — both rated 1 (poor) to 5 (excellent). A rating of **M3/T3** is the FCC minimum for "HAC-rated" phones.

---

## 2.4 Web Accessibility — WCAG 2.2 Success Criteria (High-Yield)

WCAG 2.2 contains **9 new success criteria** added to 2.0/2.1, and a total of **86 success criteria** organized under the four POUR principles.

### Perceivable (Example Criteria)
- **1.1.1 Non-text Content (A):** All images must have `alt` text.
- **1.2.2 Captions (Prerecorded) (A):** Pre-recorded video must have captions.
- **1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) (AA):** Text-to-background contrast ratio ≥ **4.5:1** (normal text), **3:1** (large text).

### Operable
- **2.1.1 Keyboard (A):** All functionality must be available via keyboard.
- **2.2.1 Timing Adjustable (A):** Users must be able to extend/disable time limits.
- **2.4.7 Focus Visible (AA):** Keyboard focus indicator must be visible.
- **2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum) (AA) — *New in 2.2*:** Minimum touch target **24×24 CSS pixels**.

### Understandable
- **3.1.1 Language of Page (A):** `<html lang="en">` must be set.
- **3.3.2 Labels or Instructions (A):** Form fields must have visible labels.

### Robust
- **4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A):** Programmatic labels for all UI components (ARIA).

### Contrast Ratio Formula (KTU High-Yield)

The **WCAG contrast ratio** between two relative luminance values $L_1$ and $L_2$ (where $L_1 \geq L_2$) is:

$$
CR = \frac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05}
$$

Where **relative luminance** is:

$$
L = 0.2126 \cdot R + 0.7152 \cdot G + 0.0722 \cdot B
$$

And each channel $R, G, B$ is linearized from sRGB:

$$
C_{linear} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{C_{sRGB}}{12.92}, & C_{sRGB} \leq 0.03928 \\[6pt] \left(\dfrac{C_{sRGB} + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4}, & C_{sRGB} > 0.03928 \end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold values for WCAG AA:**
> - Normal text: $CR \geq 4.5:1$
> - Large text (≥18 pt or 14 pt bold): $CR \geq 3:1$
>
> **For WCAG AAA:** Normal text $CR \geq 7:1$, Large text $CR \geq 4.5:1$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecom Industry:** FCC Section 508 (US), ITU-T E.121, EN 301 549 (EU) — all mandate accessibility features. Engineers must design compliant hardware.
- **Software Industry:** WCAG 2.2 AA is the **de-facto legal standard** (e.g., ADA lawsuits in US, RPwD 2016 in India, EAA 2025 in EU).
- **Procurement:** Government contracts require VPAT (Voluntary Product Accessibility Template) / ACR documentation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: WCAG Contrast Ratio — Worked Example

**Problem (KTU Numerical Type):** Compute the contrast ratio of foreground color **#555555** against background color **#FFFFFF**. State whether it passes WCAG AA for normal text.

### Step 1 — Convert Hex to sRGB
Hex `#555555` → decimal: 0x55 = 85. So $C_{sRGB} = 85/255 = 0.3333$ for all three channels (R, G, B are equal).

Hex `#FFFFFF` → $C_{sRGB} = 1.0$ for all channels.

### Step 2 — Linearize sRGB → Linear
For `#555555`: $C_{sRGB} = 0.3333 > 0.03928$, so we use the exponent rule:

$$
C_{linear} = \left( \frac{0.3333 + 0.055}{1.055} \right)^{2.4}
$$

$$
C_{linear} = \left( \frac{0.3883}{1.055} \right)^{2.4} = (0.3681)^{2.4}
$$

$$
\ln(0.3681) = -1.0003
$$

$$
C_{linear} = e^{-1.0003 \times 2.4} = e^{-2.4007} = 0.0907
$$

For `#FFFFFF`: $C_{sRGB} = 1.0$, so:

$$
C_{linear} = \left( \frac{1.0 + 0.055}{1.055} \right)^{2.4} = 1.0^{2.4} = 1.0
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Relative Luminance $L$

For `#555555` (R = G = B = 0.0907):

$$
L_1 = 0.2126(0.0907) + 0.7152(0.0907) + 0.0722(0.0907)
$$

$$
L_1 = 0.0907 \times (0.2126 + 0.7152 + 0.0722)
$$

$$
L_1 = 0.0907 \times 1.0 = 0.0907
$$

For `#FFFFFF`: $L_2 = 1.0$ (since coefficients sum to 1 with full linear values).

### Step 4 — Apply the Contrast Ratio Formula

$$
CR = \frac{L_2 + 0.05}{L_1 + 0.05} = \frac{1.0 + 0.05}{0.0907 + 0.05}
$$

$$
CR = \frac{1.05}{0.1407}
$$

$$
CR = 7.46 : 1
$$

### Step 5 — Conformance Check

$$
CR = 7.46 \geq 4.5 \quad \text{(WCAG AA — Normal Text ✅)}
$$

$$
CR = 7.46 \geq 7.0 \quad \text{(WCAG AAA — Normal Text ✅)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verdict:** The pair `#555555` on `#FFFFFF` passes **WCAG AA AND AAA** for normal text. This is why the famous Bootstrap 4 default body color (`#212529` on `#FFFFFF`, ratio ≈ 16.1) is the gold standard.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: TTY Information Throughput

**Problem:** Calculate the effective character rate of a Baudot-coded TTY operating at 45.45 baud, where each character requires 1 start bit, 5 data bits, and 1.5 stop bits.

### Step 1 — Bits per Character

$$
N_{bits} = N_{start} + N_{data} + N_{stop} = 1 + 5 + 1.5 = 7.5 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 2 — Time per Bit

$$
t_{bit} = \frac{1}{B} = \frac{1}{45.45} = 0.0220 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 3 — Time per Character

$$
t_{char} = N_{bits} \times t_{bit} = 7.5 \times 0.0220 = 0.1650 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 4 — Characters per Second (CPS)

$$
CPS = \frac{1}{t_{char}} = \frac{1}{0.1650} = 6.06 \text{ cps}
$$

### Step 5 — Characters per Minute (WPM-equivalent)

$$
CPM = 6.06 \times 60 = 363.6 \text{ chars/min}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For context, average typing speed is ~40 wpm (≈ 200 chars/min). TTY is therefore **slower than speech** but **faster than handwriting**, justifying the need for modern alternatives like IP-Relay and VRS.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Accessibility Technologies

| Feature | TTY | VRS | IP-Relay | CapTel | Smartphone (iOS/Android) |
|---------|-----|-----|----------|--------|-------------------------|
| Bandwidth | Voice line | Video (≥128 kbps) | Low data | Voice + text | Variable |
| Operator needed | Yes (legacy) | Yes (interpreter) | Yes | Yes (auto) | No (built-in) |
| Real-time | No (turn-taking) | Yes | No | Yes | Yes |
| Sign language support | No | Yes | No | No | No (live captions only) |
| Cost to user | Low (often free) | Free (US) | Free | Free (US) | Free |
| KTU exam weight | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TTY Communication Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Deaf User 1] -->|Types on keyboard| B[TTY Device 1]
    B -->|Baudot FSK 1275/2225 Hz| C[PSTN Phone Network]
    C -->|Audio tones| D[TTY Device 2]
    D -->|Displays text on LCD| E[Deaf User 2]
    E -->|Types reply| D
    D -->|FSK tones| C
    C -->|Audio tones| B
    B -->|Displays text| A
```

## 4.2 Relay Service Architecture (Voice-to-TTY Call)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V[Voice User Standard Phone] -->|Dials relay number| O[Communications Assistant CA]
    O -->|Speaks voice users words| T[Reads aloud to TTY User]
    TTY[TTY User Types Reply] -->|Baudot FSK| O
    O -->|Voices typed text| V
    O -->|Maintains neutral facilitation| N[Neutrality and Confidentiality]
```

## 4.3 WCAG POUR Pillars with Associated Technologies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P[Perceivable]
        P1[Alt Text for Images]
        P2[Closed Captions and Transcripts]
        P3[Sufficient Color Contrast 4.5:1]
    end
    subgraph O[Operable]
        O1[Keyboard Navigation]
        O2[No Seizure Inducing Flash]
        O3[Visible Focus Indicator]
    end
    subgraph U[Understandable]
        U1[Plain Language]
        U2[Consistent Navigation]
        U3[Form Labels and Error Messages]
    end
    subgraph R[Robust]
        R1[Valid HTML]
        R2[ARIA Roles and Attributes]
        R3[Compatible with Assistive Tech]
    end
    P --> O
    O --> U
    U --> R
    R --> P
```

## 4.4 Assistive Technology Ecosystem (Telecom + Web)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    User[User with Disability] --> Impairment{Type of Impairment}
    Impairment -->|Visual| ScreenReader[Screen Reader JAWS NVDA]
    Impairment -->|Hearing| CapTel[Captioned Telephone]
    Impairment -->|Motor| Switch[Switch Input or Voice Control]
    Impairment -->|Speech| AAC[AAC Device or TTS]
    ScreenReader --> Browser[Web Browser]
    CapTel --> PSTN[PSTN Network]
    Switch --> Browser
    AAC --> Browser
    Browser --> WCAG[WCAG 2.2 Compliant Content]
    PSTN --> Relay[Relay Service]
    Relay --> WCAG
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Diagram Strategy Tip:** Block diagrams like the above (using Mermaid `flowchart`) are accepted in answer sheets. You earn full marks if you label each block clearly and use arrows to show data/signal flow direction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define TTY. Explain its working principle with reference to Baudot code and FSK frequencies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TTY stands for **TeleTYpewriter**, also called **TDD (Telecommunications Device for the Deaf)**. It is an electromechanical or electronic device that allows deaf, hard-of-hearing, or speech-impaired individuals to communicate over standard telephone lines by typing messages instead of speaking.

**Working Principle:**
1. The sender types a message on a keyboard.
2. Characters are encoded using the **Baudot code (5-bit ITA2)** at a transmission rate of **45.45 baud**.
3. The encoded bits modulate a carrier wave using **Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)**:
   - **Mark (binary 1)** → **1275 Hz**
   - **Space (binary 0)** → **2225 Hz**
4. These audio tones travel over the **PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network)**.
5. The receiving TDD demodulates the FSK tones, decodes the Baudot signal, and displays the text on an LCD/LED screen.

*Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for Baudot/baud, 1 mark for FSK frequencies.*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**State and explain the four POUR principles of WCAG 2.2 with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
WCAG 2.2 (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) is built on four foundational principles, abbreviated as **POUR**:

1. **Perceivable** — Information and UI components must be presentable in ways users can perceive.  
   *Example:* Providing `alt` text for images allows blind screen-reader users to "perceive" the image content.

2. **Operable** — Interface components and navigation must be operable.  
   *Example:* All functionality available via keyboard enables motor-impaired users who cannot use a mouse.

3. **Understandable** — Information and UI operation must be understandable.  
   *Example:* Plain-language content and consistent navigation help users with cognitive disabilities.

4. **Robust** — Content must be robust enough to be interpreted reliably by current and future assistive technologies.  
   *Example:* Valid HTML with ARIA roles ensures screen-reader compatibility across browsers.

*Mark distribution: 0.5 per principle name + 0.25 for example = 3 marks total.*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** *ESE Module — Internal Choice.* Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions carry equal marks.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** Describe the architecture and working of a **TTY/TDD system** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of Baudot code, FSK modulation, and the PSTN in enabling deaf-to-deaf communication. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the operation of a **Video Relay Service (VRS)**. Discuss its advantages over traditional TTY relay. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **Telecommunications Device for the Deaf (TDD)** or **TTY** is a device that enables persons with hearing or speech impairment to communicate via the standard telephone network using typed text. It was invented in **1964 by Robert H. Weitbrecht**.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    KB[Keyboard Input] --> ENC[Character Encoder ITA2 Baudot]
    ENC --> MOD[FSK Modulator 1275/2225 Hz]
    MOD --> AC[Acoustic Coupler or Direct Connect]
    AC --> PSTN[PSTN Telephone Line]
    PSTN --> AC2[Receiving Acoustic Coupler]
    AC2 --> DEMOD[FSK Demodulator]
    DEMOD --> DEC[Character Decoder]
    DEC --> DISP[LCD Display]
```

**Working Explanation (3 marks):**
1. The user types characters on the **QWERTY / ABC keyboard**. Letters, numbers, and punctuation are encoded using the **5-bit Baudot (ITA2) code** with a **Letters/Figures shift** mechanism.
2. The encoded serial bitstream is transmitted at **45.45 baud**, with each character framed by **1 start bit, 5 data bits, and 1.5 stop bits** (total 7.5 bits per character).
3. **FSK modulation** converts the binary data into audio tones:
   - Bit **1 (Mark)** → **1275 Hz** carrier
   - Bit **0 (Space)** → **2225 Hz** carrier
4. The audio signal is coupled into the telephone line either via an **acoustic coupler** (molded rubber cups over the handset) or a **direct-connect** cable.
5. The signal traverses the **PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network)** as ordinary voice-band audio.
6. At the receiver, the FSK tones are demodulated, decoded back into ITA2, converted to ASCII, and displayed on a **scrolling LCD screen** (commonly 20 or 24 characters wide).

**PSTN Role (1 mark):**
The PSTN's **voice-band channel (300–3400 Hz)** is wide enough to pass the FSK frequencies (1275/2225 Hz) without attenuation, allowing TTYs to interoperate with **any phone, anywhere in the world**, without special infrastructure.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**VRS Definition (1 mark):**
A **Video Relay Service (VRS)** is an internet-based video telecommunications service that allows deaf and hard-of-hearing users who use **American Sign Language (ASL)** or other sign languages to communicate with voice telephone users through a **live, certified sign-language interpreter** (the *Video Interpreter* or *VI*).

**Architecture Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D[Deaf User with Webcam ASL] -->|Video stream over IP| VI[Certified Sign Language Interpreter]
    VI -->|Voice over VoIP| V[Voice User Standard Phone]
    V -->|Voice reply| VI
    VI -->|Sign Language via video| D
    VI -->|Maintains neutrality and confidentiality| N[Standards FCC 499A etc]
```

**Working (2 marks):**
1. The deaf user initiates a VRS call from a videophone, smartphone app, or computer.
2. A **trained, certified Video Interpreter (VI)** answers the call on a video workstation.
3. The deaf user signs in ASL; the VI **interprets** the signs into spoken English, transmitting voice to the called party over a regular phone line (or VoIP).
4. The voice party's reply is interpreted by the VI back into ASL signs for the deaf user.
5. The VI facilitates the call **transparently**, similar to how a telephone operator would have worked in the 1900s.

**Advantages of VRS over TTY Relay (2 marks):**
1. **Natural communication:** Sign language is the deaf user's native language; VRS preserves facial expression, body language, and tone, which TTY cannot.
2. **Speed:** VRS is **near real-time**, while TTY is **turn-based** and limited to ~6 characters/second.
3. **Identity and emotional nuance:** Identity is visible; emotion is conveyed via facial expression.
4. **Modern integration:** VRS works on smartphones/tablets, whereas TTY requires dedicated hardware (though TTY apps exist, the network is becoming legacy).

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **WCAG 2.2 framework** in detail. List the four POUR principles and at least three success criteria under each, indicating their conformance level (A / AA / AAA). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A web page uses **#777777** text on a **#FFFFFF** background. Calculate the **contrast ratio** and determine whether the design meets WCAG 2.2 **AA** conformance for normal text. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2**, published by the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** in **October 2023**, is the international standard for web accessibility. It defines how to make web content more accessible to people with disabilities. WCAG is built on four guiding principles abbreviated as **POUR**.

**Perceivable (1.5 marks):**
| SC Number | Criterion | Level |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| 1.1.1 | Non-text Content (alt text) | A |
| 1.2.2 | Captions (Prerecorded) | A |
| 1.4.3 | Contrast (Minimum) ≥ 4.5:1 | AA |
| 1.4.6 | Contrast (Enhanced) ≥ 7:1 | AAA |

**Operable (1.5 marks):**
| SC Number | Criterion | Level |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| 2.1.1 | Keyboard | A |
| 2.2.1 | Timing Adjustable | A |
| 2.4.7 | Focus Visible | AA |
| 2.5.8 | Target Size (Minimum) 24×24 CSS px | AA (new in 2.2) |

**Understandable (1.5 marks):**
| SC Number | Criterion | Level |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| 3.1.1 | Language of Page (`<html lang>`) | A |
| 3.1.5 | Reading Level (lower secondary education) | AAA |
| 3.3.2 | Labels or Instructions (form fields) | A |

**Robust (1.5 marks):**
| SC Number | Criterion | Level |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| 4.1.1 | Parsing (no duplicate IDs) — *obsolete in 2.2* | A |
| 4.1.2 | Name, Role, Value (programmatic labels) | A |
| 4.1.3 | Status Messages (live regions) | AA |

> **Conformance levels:** **A** (minimum / essential), **AA** (legal / industry standard), **AAA** (gold standard / aspirational).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Problem:** Compute contrast ratio of **#777777** text on **#FFFFFF** background and verify WCAG 2.2 AA compliance for normal text.

**Step 1 — Hex → sRGB (1 mark):**
- `#777777`: each channel = 0x77 = 119. $C_{sRGB} = 119/255 = 0.4667$
- `#FFFFFF`: $C_{sRGB} = 1.0$

**Step 2 — Linearize `#777777` (2 marks):**
Since $0.4667 > 0.03928$, use the exponent rule:

$$
C_{linear} = \left( \frac{0.4667 + 0.055}{1.055} \right)^{2.4} = \left( \frac{0.5217}{1.055} \right)^{2.4} = (0.4945)^{2.4}
$$

$$
\ln(0.4945) = -0.7038
$$

$$
C_{linear} = e^{-0.7038 \times 2.4} = e^{-1.6891} = 0.1846
$$

For `#FFFFFF`: $C_{linear} = 1.0$.

**Step 3 — Relative Luminance (1.5 marks):**

For `#777777` (R = G = B = 0.1846):

$$
L_1 = 0.1846 \times (0.2126 + 0.7152 + 0.0722) = 0.1846 \times 1.0 = 0.1846
$$

For `#FFFFFF`: $L_2 = 1.0$.

**Step 4 — Contrast Ratio (1.5 marks):**

$$
CR = \frac{1.0 + 0.05}{0.1846 + 0.05} = \frac{1.05}{0.2346}
$$

$$
CR = 4.476 : 1 \approx 4.48 : 1
$$

**Step 5 — Conformance Verdict (1 mark):**

$$
CR = 4.48 < 4.5 \quad \text{(WCAG AA — Normal Text ❌ FAIL)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often skip the linearization step and use $C_{sRGB}$ directly as luminance. This gives an **incorrect** answer.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Students compute only the foreground luminance and forget the **+0.05 offset** in both numerator and denominator.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Students confuse the order — the **lighter** color goes in the **numerator**.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting that large text (≥18 pt regular or 14 pt bold) only requires **3:1**, not 4.5:1.
> - **Always** state the **threshold**, the **actual ratio**, and the **comparison** in one line for easy valuation marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Highest Yield First):**

- 🔴 **POUR = Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust** — guaranteed 3-mark question.
- 🔴 **WCAG 2.2** is the current standard (published Oct 2023, W3C).
- 🔴 **TTY operates at 45.45 baud using Baudot (ITA2) 5-bit code.**
- 🔴 **FSK frequencies: Mark = 1275 Hz, Space = 2225 Hz.**
- 🔴 **WCAG AA contrast: 4.5:1 normal text, 3:1 large text.**
- 🟡 **Contrast ratio formula:** $CR = (L_1 + 0.05)/(L_2 + 0.05)$ with linearized sRGB.
- 🟡 **Linearization threshold:** $0.03928$ separates the linear vs. power rules.
- 🟡 **TTY = TDD = Text Telephone** — all refer to the same device family.
- 🟡 **VRS = Video Relay Service** — for ASL users; uses certified sign-language interpreters.
- 🟡 **HAC rating M3/T3** is the FCC minimum for hearing-aid-compatible phones.
- 🟢 **TDD was invented in 1964 by Robert H. Weitbrecht**, a deaf physicist.
- 🟢 **Relay services** include TTY relay, VCO, HCO, VRS, IP-Relay, STS.
- 🟢 **UNCRPD and India's RPwD Act 2016** mandate accessible telecom & ICT.
- 🟢 **WCAG 2.2 added 9 new criteria**, including **2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured (AA)** and **2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum) 24×24 CSS px (AA)**.
- 🟢 **Relative luminance** coefficients: **0.2126 R, 0.7152 G, 0.0722 B** — memorized by the phrase "**GR**ee**n** is **most**."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Introduction: Prosthetics, Orthotics & Rehabilitation Aids

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Rehabilitation Engineering** is the systematic application of engineering principles, biomedical sciences, and assistive technology to design, develop, and deliver devices, systems, and environments that assist individuals with physical, sensory, cognitive, or neurological impairments in regaining, restoring, or substituting lost functional abilities and improving their quality of life.

Module 3 specifically introduces the engineering framework for **Restorative and Substitutional Devices**, broadly classified into three pillars:

1. **Prosthetics** — Artificial replacements for missing body parts (limbs, organs).
2. **Orthotics** — External devices that support, align, prevent, or correct deformities of existing body parts.
3. **Assistive Devices / Rehabilitation Aids** — Tools, equipment, and systems that aid in performing activities of daily living (ADL), mobility, communication, and sensory substitution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The distinction between *restoration* (regaining lost function), *substitution* (replacing lost function via alternate means), and *compensation* (using remaining capability efficiently) is a **frequently tested concept** in Part A questions (3-mark category).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy:** Think of a person who has lost a leg as a **building missing one of its pillars**. The building still stands but is unstable. A **prosthesis (artificial leg)** is like installing a new, engineered pillar to restore structural integrity. An **orthosis (brace/splint)** is like a steel reinforcement added to an *existing* weak pillar to prevent it from collapsing. An **assistive device (wheelchair/crutch)** is like building a **wheeled support platform** under the building so it can still move and function even if the pillar is missing.

### 1.3 Why is this Module Important?

- Globally, over **1 billion people** (15% of world population) live with some form of disability (WHO estimate).
- In India alone, approximately **2.68 crore** persons are recorded with disabilities (Census 2011, with significant under-counting).
- The demand for engineered rehabilitation solutions is rising due to **aging populations, road-traffic accidents, diabetes-related amputations**, and **veteran/war casualties**.
- Standard metrics that define the field:
  - **ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health)** — WHO framework
  - **ADL (Activities of Daily Living)** index — Barthel Index scale (**0–100**)
  - **QOL (Quality of Life)** — measured via SF-36, WHOQOL-BREF

### 1.4 Key Terminology (Foundational Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Impairment** | Loss or abnormality of body structure/function (physiological/psychological). |
| **Disability** | Restriction or inability to perform an activity within normal range. |
| **Handicap** | Disadvantage resulting from impairment/disability that limits role performance. |
| **Rehabilitation** | Combined and coordinated use of medical, social, educational, and vocational measures for retraining. |
| **Prosthesis** | Artificial device that replaces a missing body part. |
| **Orthosis** | External device applied to body to support, align, or correct deformity. |
| **Assistive Device** | Any device that helps a person perform a task they would otherwise find difficult. |
| **ADL** | Activities of Daily Living — self-care tasks (eating, bathing, dressing). |
| **IADL** | Instrumental ADL — complex tasks (cooking, shopping, managing finances). |
| **ICF** | International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (WHO, 2001). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Old vs New Terminology (KTU Frequently Asked):** The terms *impairment, disability, handicap* follow the older **ICIDH (1980)** classification. The current WHO standard is **ICF (2001)**, which uses neutral, positive terminology — *body function/structure, activity, participation*. Both terminologies are expected knowledge.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Rehabilitation Engineering — Conceptual Breakdown

### Pillar 1: Prosthetics (Substitution Devices)

**Definition Recap:** A *prosthesis* is an artificial substitute for a **missing** body part.

**Engineering Logic — Step-by-step:**

- **Step 1 — Identify the missing limb/segment** (e.g., transfemoral amputation above knee).
- **Step 2 — Quantify residual limb characteristics** (length, girth, muscle strength, skin condition, range of motion of remaining joints).
- **Step 3 — Determine functional goals** (cosmetic only / light daily use / heavy labor / athletic use).
- **Step 4 — Select socket design** (interface between residual limb and prosthesis — the most critical component).
- **Step 5 — Select components**: pylon (shank), foot/terminal device, knee unit (for above-knee), suspension system.
- **Step 6 — Alignment & fitting** (bench, static, dynamic alignment).
- **Step 7 — Gait training & follow-up**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the socket the "heart" of a prosthesis?** Because 60–70% of patient comfort and prosthetic function is determined by socket fit. A poorly designed socket can cause skin breakdown, pain, and rejection of the device — even if all other components are premium.

### Pillar 2: Orthotics (Supportive/Corrective Devices)

**Definition Recap:** An *orthosis* is an external device applied to a body segment to provide **support, alignment, protection, prevention, or correction** of deformities.

**Engineering Logic — Step-by-step:**

- **Step 1 — Identify the body segment requiring support** (e.g., ankle-foot, knee-ankle-foot, wrist-hand).
- **Step 2 — Determine the biomechanical goal**:
  - **Restrict** unwanted motion (immobilization)
  - **Assist** weak motion (assistance)
  - **Correct** deformity (three-point pressure system)
  - **Protect** healing tissue
- **Step 3 — Select orthosis class** using the standardized nomenclature (see Section 2.3).
- **Step 4 — Choose materials** (thermoplastics, carbon fiber, metal-leather, foams).
- **Step 5 — Custom fabrication or modular selection**.
- **Step 6 — Fitting, training, and periodic review**.

### Pillar 3: Assistive Devices / Rehabilitation Aids

**Definition Recap:** Devices that **do not replace or support a body part directly**, but enable a person to perform functional tasks in the environment.

**Categories Include:**

- Mobility aids: **wheelchairs, walkers, crutches, canes**
- Communication aids: **AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) devices, speech synthesizers**
- Sensory aids: **hearing aids, Braille displays, screen readers, canes for the blind**
- Cognitive aids: **memory devices, electronic organizers**
- Daily living aids: **reachers, adapted cutlery, dressing aids, bath benches**

## 2.2 Classification Frameworks (High-Yield for KTU)

### A) Classification by Functional Outcome

| Category | Restores | Substitutes | Compensates |
|---|---|---|---|
| Example | Gait training, strength rehab | Prosthetic limb, cochlear implant | Wheelchair (uses arms to compensate for legs) |
| Engineering Focus | Biomechanics, exercise science | Replacement mechanics, neural interfaces | Ergonomics, mobility design |

### B) Classification by Body System Addressed

- **Musculoskeletal** — prosthetics, orthotics, wheelchairs
- **Neurological** — FES, neuroprosthetics, brain-computer interfaces
- **Sensory** — visual, auditory, tactile aids
- **Cardiopulmonary** — ventilators, oxygen concentrators (referenced for context)

## 2.3 Standardized Nomenclature (KTU 2024 — Must Memorize)

The **International Standards Organization (ISO)** nomenclature for orthoses is built on **joints spanned**:

| Acronym | Orthosis Name | Joints Spanned |
|---|---|---|
| FO | Foot Orthosis | Foot only |
| AFO | Ankle-Foot Orthosis | Ankle + Foot |
| KAFO | Knee-Ankle-Foot Orthosis | Knee + Ankle + Foot |
| HKAFO | Hip-KAFO | Hip + Knee + Ankle + Foot |
| WHO | Wrist-Hand Orthosis | Wrist + Hand |
| EO | Elbow Orthosis | Elbow |
| SEWHO | Shoulder-Elbow-Wrist-Hand Orthosis | Shoulder + Elbow + Wrist + Hand |
| CO | Cervical Orthosis | Cervical spine |
| CTO | Cervico-Thoracic Orthosis | Cervical + Thoracic |
| TLSO | Thoraco-Lumbo-Sacral Orthosis | Thoracic + Lumbar + Sacral |
| LSO | Lumbo-Sacral Orthosis | Lumbar + Sacral |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students often write "AFO" for "Above Knee" — incorrect. **AFO = Ankle-Foot Orthosis**. For an above-knee amputation, the prosthesis is **transfemoral**, not "AK prosthesis" in formal terminology (though "AK" / "BK" — above-knee / below-knee — is accepted clinical shorthand for amputation level).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Body Mass Index (BMI) for prescription | $BMI = \dfrac{W}{H^2}$ | kg / m² | Used in prosthetic weight-bearing calculations |
| Normal Gait Cadence | $C = \dfrac{120 \text{ steps}}{\min}$ | steps/min | Benchmark for prosthetic gait training |
| Stance:Swing Ratio | $60:40$ (normal) | % of gait cycle | Used to evaluate gait deviation |
| Energy Cost of Walking (amputee) | $\approx 25{-}60\%$ higher than able-bodied | % increase | High above-knee amputee with prosthesis |
| Walking Speed (normal) | $v = 1.3 \text{ m/s}$ | m/s | Reference for mobility-aid prescription |
| Wheelchair Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful work output}}{\text{Energy input}}$ | dimensionless | Evaluates push-rim wheel design |
| Mechanical Advantage (lever) | $MA = \dfrac{\text{Effort arm}}{\text{Load arm}}$ | dimensionless | Crutch/cane design |
| Static Equilibrium (standing) | $\sum F = 0, \sum M = 0$ | N, N·m | Prosthetic alignment |
| Three-Point Pressure Rule | $P_1 \cdot d_1 = P_2 \cdot d_2 + P_3 \cdot d_3$ | N·m | Orthotic correction of deformity |
| Cosmesis Rating | Subjective scale 1–5 | ordinal | Patient satisfaction metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The three-point pressure system is the foundational biomechanical principle behind nearly all orthotic deformity correction (e.g., scoliosis braces — Boston, Milwaukee, Charleston). Engineers must understand the **lever-arm balance** to design braces that apply corrective force without causing skin damage.

## 2.5 The Rehabilitation Engineering Workflow

```
Patient Assessment → Goal Setting → Device Selection → Design/Fabrication 
        ↓
Fitting → Training → Outcome Measurement → Follow-up & Modification
```
