This **cyclic, multidisciplinary workflow** is a recurring KTU 14-mark question topic, where students are asked to describe the **role of the rehabilitation team** in each stage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Three-Point Pressure System in Orthotic Correction

The **three-point pressure system** is the foundational biomechanical model for orthotic correction of angular deformities (e.g., genu varum, scoliosis, clubfoot).

### Setup

Consider a long bone with an angular deformity at the joint. An orthosis applies **three forces** along the limb:

- **Force P₁** at the apex of the deformity (corrective force, pushing the deformity toward alignment)
- **Forces P₂ and P₃** at the proximal and distal ends (counter-forces, equal and opposite)

For **static rotational equilibrium** about the apex point:

### Step 1 — Sum of moments about apex = 0

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum M_{\text{apex}} &= 0 \\
P_2 \cdot d_2 + P_3 \cdot d_3 - P_1 \cdot d_1 &= 0 \\
P_1 \cdot d_1 &= P_2 \cdot d_2 + P_3 \cdot d_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The corrective moment (P₁ × d₁) generated at the apex must equal the sum of the counter-moments generated by the proximal (P₂ × d₂) and distal (P₃ × d₃) forces for the limb to be in rotational equilibrium during correction.

### Step 2 — Simplification (symmetric case)

In a **symmetric orthosis** with P₂ = P₃ = P_c (counter-force) and d₂ = d₃ = d_c (counter-lever arm):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 \cdot d_1 &= 2 \cdot P_c \cdot d_c \\
P_1 &= \dfrac{2 \cdot P_c \cdot d_c}{d_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** This shows the **mechanical disadvantage** of the orthosis — the corrective force at the apex must be large because the counter-lever arms are long. To reduce skin pressure (and prevent pressure ulcers), the lever arms d_c should be **maximized** in the orthosis design.

### Step 3 — Practical design implication

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a given corrective force P₁, doubling the counter-lever arm d_c **halves** the required counter-pressure P_c on the skin. This is why long-leg orthoses (KAFO) extend well above and below the deformity site — to **distribute pressure over a larger area** and minimize soft-tissue damage.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Prosthesis vs Orthosis

| Parameter | Prosthesis | Orthosis |
|---|---|---|
| Body part status | **Missing** (amputated/absent) | **Present** but impaired/weak |
| Primary purpose | Replace missing segment | Support, align, correct, protect |
| Force application | External to residual limb (via socket) | External to body segment (via shell) |
| Tissue interface | Stump-socket interface | Skin-pad interface |
| Weight-bearing | Through residual limb | Through intact limb + orthosis |
| Key engineering challenge | Socket design & suspension | Three-point pressure balance |
| Material trend | Carbon fiber, titanium, silicone | Thermoplastics, composites, 3D-printed polymers |
| Cosmesis priority | High (social reintegration) | Moderate (function over form usually) |
| Example | Below-knee prosthesis | Boston scoliosis brace |

## 3.3 Energy Cost Analysis (Numerical Illustration)

For a normal able-bodied adult:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy cost of walking} &\approx 0.8 \text{ kcal/kg/km} \\
\text{Oxygen rate} &\approx 12 \text{ ml/kg/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **transfemoral (above-knee) amputee** walking with a prosthesis:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy cost} &\approx 1.2 \text{ to } 1.5 \text{ kcal/kg/km} \\
\text{Increase} &\approx 50{-}90\% \text{ higher than able-bodied}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For a unilateral below-knee amputee:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy cost} &\approx 0.9 \text{ to } 1.1 \text{ kcal/kg/km} \\
\text{Increase} &\approx 10{-}40\% \text{ higher}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This energy-cost data is critical for **prescription decisions**. A frail or elderly transfemoral amputee may be **better served by a wheelchair** than a high-functioning prosthesis, because the energy demand of prosthetic walking exceeds their cardiopulmonary capacity. This is a classic **KTU viva question** testing clinical engineering judgment.

## 3.4 The Barthel Index — Functional Outcome Metric

The **Barthel Index** scores 10 ADL items on a 0–100 scale:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Barthel Score} &= \sum_{i=1}^{10} S_i \\
\text{where } S_i &\in \{0, 5, 10, 15\} \text{ depending on item and independence level}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Score Range | Functional Category | Typical Assistive Device Need |
|---|---|---|
| 0 – 20 | Total dependence | Full care, powered wheelchair |
| 21 – 60 | Severe dependence | Manual wheelchair, maximum assistance |
| 61 – 90 | Moderate dependence | Walking aids, partial assistance |
| 91 – 100 | Slight dependence | Independent with minor aids |

This is an **outcome measure** used to evaluate the effectiveness of any rehabilitation intervention, including prosthetics and orthotics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Rehabilitation Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rehabilitation Devices] --> B[Prosthetic Devices]
    A --> C[Orthotic Devices]
    A --> D[Assistive Devices]
    A --> E[Therapeutic Devices]

    B --> B1[Limb Prosthesis]
    B --> B2[Cosmetic Prosthesis]
    B --> B3[Functional Prosthesis]

    B1 --> B1a[Transfemoral AK]
    B1 --> B1b[Transtibial BK]
    B1 --> B1c[Transhumeral AE]
    B1 --> B1d[Transradial BE]

    C --> C1[Lower Extremity Orthosis]
    C --> C2[Upper Extremity Orthosis]
    C --> C3[Spinal Orthosis]

    C1 --> C1a[FO]
    C1 --> C1b[AFO]
    C1 --> C1c[KAFO]
    C1 --> C1d[HKAFO]

    C3 --> C3a[CO]
    C3 --> C3b[CTO]
    C3 --> C3c[TLSO]
    C3 --> C4d[LSO]

    D --> D1[Mobility Aids]
    D --> D2[Sensory Aids]
    D --> D3[Communication Aids]
    D --> D4[Cognitive Aids]
    D --> D5[ADL Aids]

    D1 --> D1a[Wheelchair Manual]
    D1 --> D1b[Wheelchair Powered]
    D1 --> D1c[Walker]
    D1 --> D1d[Crutch]
    D1 --> D1e[Cane]

    D2 --> D2a[Hearing Aid]
    D2 --> D2b[Visual Aid Braille]
    D2 --> D2c[White Cane]

    E --> E1[FES Functional Electrical Stimulation]
    E --> E2[Rehabilitation Robotics]
    E --> E3[Exercise Therapy Units]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Rehabilitation Engineering Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Patient Assessment] --> P2[Clinical Evaluation by Physiatrist]
    P2 --> P3[Impairment and Functional Deficit Analysis]
    P3 --> P4[Goal Setting with Patient Input]
    P4 --> P5[Device Selection and Prescription]
    P5 --> P6[Engineering Design and Fabrication]
    P6 --> P7[Fitting and Alignment]
    P7 --> P8[Gait Training and User Education]
    P8 --> P9[Outcome Measurement Barthel Index]
    P9 --> P10{Outcome Achieved?}
    P10 -- Yes --> P11[Discharge and Follow Up]
    P10 -- No --> P3

    subgraph Team
        T1[Rehabilitation Engineer]
        T2[Physiatrist]
        T3[Prosthetist Orthotist]
        T4[Physical Therapist]
        T5[Occupational Therapist]
        T6[Social Worker]
        T7[Patient and Family]
    end

    Team --> P2
    Team --> P4
    Team --> P7
    Team --> P8
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Prosthetic Limb System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Residual Limb Stump] --> S[Socket Custom Fitted Interface]
    S --> SH[Shank Pylon]
    SH --> K{Knee Unit}
    K --> SH2[Lower Shank]
    SH2 --> FT[Foot and Ankle Assembly]
    FT --> G[Ground Reaction Force]

    S --> SUS[Suspension System]
    SUS --> R

    subgraph Control
        C1[Microprocessor Controlled Knee] --> K
        C2[Myoelectric Sensors] --> C1
        C3[Battery Pack] --> C1
    end

    subgraph Interface
        I1[Silicone Liner]
        I2[Pin Lock Suspension]
        I3[Suction Socket]
        I4[Vacuum Assisted Suspension]
    end

    I1 --> S
    I2 --> SUS
    I3 --> SUS
    I4 --> SUS
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix Flowchart: Selection Between Prosthesis, Orthosis, and Wheelchair

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient with Mobility Limitation] --> B{Is body part MISSING?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Consider PROSTHESIS]
    B -- No --> D{Is existing part WEAK or MISALIGNED?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Consider ORTHOSIS]
    D -- No --> F{Is energy demand too HIGH for prosthetic walking?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Consider WHEELCHAIR]
    F -- No --> H[Consider WALKING AIDS Crutch Cane Walker]
    C --> I{Energy Capacity Sufficient?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Prescribe Prosthesis with Training]
    I -- No --> G
    E --> K{Goal is Correct Support or Immobilize?}
    K -- Correct --> L[Prescribe Corrective Orthosis]
    K -- Support --> M[Prescribe Supportive Orthosis]
    K -- Immobilize --> N[Prescribe Immobilizing Orthosis]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a prosthesis. How is it different from an orthosis? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **prosthesis** is an artificial device that **replaces a missing body part** lost due to trauma, disease, or congenital absence. Example: a below-knee (transtibial) prosthetic leg.

An **orthosis** is an external device applied to an **existing** body segment to **support, align, prevent, or correct** deformity, or to improve function. Example: Ankle-Foot Orthosis (AFO) for foot drop.

| Aspect | Prosthesis | Orthosis |
|---|---|---|
| Body part | Missing | Present but impaired |
| Function | Replaces | Supports/aligns |

**[3 marks: 1 definition + 1 difference + 1 example each]**

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the members of the rehabilitation team and state the role of the rehabilitation engineer.**

**Model Answer:**

**Rehabilitation Team Members:**
1. Physiatrist (Rehabilitation Medicine Physician)
2. Rehabilitation Engineer
3. Prosthetist & Orthotist (P&O)
4. Physical Therapist (Physiotherapist)
5. Occupational Therapist
6. Speech-Language Pathologist
7. Rehabilitation Nurse
8. Social Worker / Vocational Counselor
9. Patient and Family

**Role of the Rehabilitation Engineer:**
The rehabilitation engineer **applies engineering principles (biomechanics, materials science, electronics, control systems)** to **design, develop, customize, and evaluate** assistive devices, prosthetics, orthoses, and accessibility solutions. They also conduct **research, training, and outcome measurement** to ensure devices meet user needs safely and effectively.

**[3 marks: 6 team members + 3 lines on role]**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** framework proposed by WHO. Compare it with the older ICIDH model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **engineering considerations in the design of a lower-limb prosthesis**, with a neat block diagram of its components. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health)** was adopted by the WHO in **2001** as a unified framework for describing health and disability. It replaces the older **ICIDH (1980)**.

**ICF Components:**

1. **Body Functions and Structures** — physiological and anatomical parts (e.g., range of motion, muscle strength).
2. **Activities** — execution of tasks by an individual (e.g., walking, dressing).
3. **Participation** — involvement in life situations (e.g., employment, social life).
4. **Contextual Factors**:
   - **Environmental factors** (physical, social, attitudinal environment)
   - **Personal factors** (age, gender, coping style)

**Comparison with ICIDH:**

| Aspect | ICIDH (1980) | ICF (2001) |
|---|---|---|
| Terminology | Impairment, Disability, Handicap (negative) | Body function, Activity, Participation (neutral) |
| View of disability | Consequence of disease (medical model) | Result of interaction between person and environment (biopsychosocial model) |
| Contextual factors | Not included | Included (environmental + personal) |
| Universality | Disability-focused | Applicable to **all people**, not just disabled |
| Positive framing | No | Yes (focus on functioning) |

**Conclusion:** The ICF provides a **more holistic, inclusive, and positive** framework, enabling engineers to design devices that address **activity limitations and participation restrictions**, not just impairments.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Engineering Considerations in Lower-Limb Prosthetic Design:**

1. **Residual Limb Assessment** — length, girth, distal end bearing capacity, skin condition, ROM of proximal joint.
2. **Socket Design** — total surface bearing (TSB) vs patellar tendon bearing (PTB); materials (thermoplastic, carbon, silicone liner); intimate fit to distribute pressure uniformly.
3. **Suspension System** — pin-lock, lanyard, suction, vacuum-assisted; chosen based on activity level and residual limb condition.
4. **Shank/Pylon** — endoskeletal (modular, cosmetic cover) vs exoskeletal (crustacean, durable).
5. **Knee Unit (for transfemoral)** — single-axis, polycentric, hydraulic, pneumatic, microprocessor-controlled (e.g., C-Leg, Genium).
6. **Foot/Ankle Assembly** — SACH (Solid Ankle Cushioned Heel), single-axis, multi-axial, dynamic-response (energy-storing carbon feet).
7. **Alignment** — bench alignment → static alignment → dynamic alignment during gait training.
8. **Material Selection** — strength-to-weight ratio, fatigue resistance, corrosion resistance (titanium, aluminum, carbon fiber composites).
9. **Cosmesis and Weight** — should match the contralateral limb in appearance and approximate weight.
10. **User Factors** — age, weight, activity level (K-level classification K0–K4), vocational needs.

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.3 in notes).**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]

**Q. (a)** With neat sketches, explain the **three-point pressure system** used in orthotic correction of deformities. Derive the equilibrium equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify **rehabilitation aids** with suitable examples. Explain the **functional differences between manual and powered wheelchairs**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **three-point pressure system** is the fundamental biomechanical principle used in orthotic correction of angular deformities (e.g., scoliosis, clubfoot, genu varum, contractures).

**Principle:** Three forces are applied to a body segment:
- **P₁** — corrective force at the **apex** of the deformity
- **P₂** — counter-force **proximal** to the apex
- **P₃** — counter-force **distal** to the apex

For rotational equilibrium about the apex:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum M_{\text{apex}} &= 0 \\
P_1 \cdot d_1 - P_2 \cdot d_2 - P_3 \cdot d_3 &= 0 \\
P_1 \cdot d_1 &= P_2 \cdot d_2 + P_3 \cdot d_3
\end{aligned}
$$

For the symmetric case (P₂ = P₃ = P_c, d₂ = d₃ = d_c):

$$
P_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P_c \cdot d_c}{d_1}
$$

**Design Implication:** To **minimize skin pressure (P_c)**, the counter-lever arms (d_c) must be **maximized** in orthosis design. This is why orthoses extend well beyond the deformity zone.

**Example Application:** A **Boston Brace** for scoliosis applies corrective pressure at the curve apex with counter-forces on the opposite side of the trunk.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Classification of Rehabilitation Aids:**

| Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| Mobility aids | Wheelchair, walker, crutch, cane |
| Sensory aids (visual) | White cane, Braille display, screen reader |
| Sensory aids (auditory) | Hearing aid, cochlear implant, alerting devices |
| Communication aids | AAC devices, speech synthesizers, picture boards |
| Cognitive aids | Memory aids, electronic reminders, calendars |
| ADL aids | Reachers, adapted cutlery, dressing sticks, bath benches |
| Environmental control | Smart-home hubs, voice-activated switches |

**Manual vs Powered Wheelchair:**

| Parameter | Manual Wheelchair | Powered Wheelchair |
|---|---|---|
| Propulsion | User-driven via push-rims (arms) | Battery + motors (joystick, sip-and-puff, head control) |
| Energy source | User's upper-body strength | Rechargeable battery (typically 24V DC) |
| User population | Adequate upper-body strength, cardiopulmonary capacity | Weak upper limbs, cardiac limitations, high-level SCI |
| Weight | 10–20 kg | 50–150 kg |
| Range per charge / use | Unlimited (user-dependent) | 15–40 km per charge |
| Cost | Low (₹5,000–₹30,000) | High (₹50,000–₹5,00,000+) |
| Maintenance | Low | Moderate (electronics, batteries) |
| Customization | Limited (seat, cushion, footrest) | Extensive (tilt, recline, standing, controls) |
| Energy efficiency | High (if user fit) | Moderate |
| Examples | Standard folding, rigid ultralight, sports | Mid-wheel drive, rear-wheel drive, power-assist add-ons |

**Selection Criteria:** K-level (functional level), upper limb strength, cognitive ability, environment (indoor/outdoor), funding.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing AFOs and amputations** — AFO is *Ankle-Foot Orthosis*, not "Above-knee" anything. (Lose 1 mark)
> 2. **Using ICIDH terminology in ICF questions** — Examiners expect "body function, activity, participation" in current WHO framework. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Skipping the derivation steps in three-point pressure** — Must show the equilibrium equation clearly. (Lose 2 marks)
> 4. **Forgetting to mention the rehabilitation team** — A 14-mark question on rehabilitation engineering that omits the multidisciplinary team is considered incomplete. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 5. **No units in formulas** — Energy in kcal, force in N, lever arm in m. (Lose 0.5–1 mark per formula)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU Module 3 — Introduction Cheat Sheet:**

- **Prosthesis = replaces MISSING** body part; **Orthosis = supports EXISTING** body part.
- **ICF (2001)** is the current WHO framework: *Body Function → Activity → Participation → Contextual Factors*.
- **ICIDH (1980)** is outdated terminology: *Impairment → Disability → Handicap*.
- **Rehabilitation team** is multidisciplinary: Physiatrist, Engineer, P&O, PT, OT, SLP, Nurse, Social Worker, **Patient**.
- **Orthosis nomenclature** is based on **joints spanned**: FO, AFO, KAFO, HKAFO, WHO, CO, CTO, TLSO, LSO.
- **Three-point pressure system** governs orthotic correction: P₁·d₁ = P₂·d₂ + P₃·d₃.
- **Symmetric case:** P₁ = 2·P_c·d_c / d₁ — orthosis must extend well beyond the deformity.
- **Energy cost of walking** is 25–90% higher in amputees than able-bodied.
- **Barthel Index** (0–100) is a standard ADL outcome measure.
- **Wheelchair selection** depends on K-level, upper-limb strength, cognition, environment.
- **Microprocessor-controlled knees** (e.g., C-Leg) use sensors + actuators for swing/stance phase control.
- **Socket** is the most critical component of a prosthesis (60–70% of fit/comfort).
- **Suspension systems** include pin-lock, lanyard, suction, vacuum-assisted.
- **Materials trend:** carbon fiber, titanium, 3D-printed polymers.
- **FES (Functional Electrical Stimulation)** uses electrical pulses to activate paralyzed muscles.
- **ADL vs IADL:** Basic self-care vs complex household tasks.
- **Cosmesis** is rated subjectively and is critical for social reintegration.
- **K-levels (K0–K4)** classify amputee functional mobility potential for prosthetic prescription.
- **Endoskeletal vs exoskeletal:** Modular with cosmetic cover vs crustacean-type durable shell.
- **SACH foot** is the simplest prosthetic foot (Solid Ankle Cushioned Heel); **dynamic-response feet** store and release energy.
- **Rehabilitation workflow is cyclic:** Assessment → Goals → Prescription → Fabrication → Fitting → Training → Outcome → Follow-up.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classes of Manual Wheelchairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classes of Manual Wheelchairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **manual wheelchair** is a manually propelled, occupant-seated mobility device that relies on human power — either from the user or an attendant — for locomotion, and which is **not** powered by any external electrical, battery, or combustion source. The **classes of manual wheelchairs** refer to the systematic grouping of these devices based on **frame construction, mass, adjustability, propulsion mechanics, clinical indication, and load-bearing capacity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Terminology)**
> Manual wheelchairs are classified by **functional performance parameters** — namely, weight category (Standard, Lightweight, Ultra-lightweight), **propulsion type** (self-propelled vs. attendant-propelled), **frame geometry** (folding, rigid, tilt-in-space, reclining), and **clinical specialization** (pediatric, bariatric, hemi, sports, and positioning chairs). The classification aligns broadly with the **U.S. Medicare K-Code system (K0001–K0007)** and **ISO 7176 series** standards adopted in rehabilitation engineering practice.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Family Car Line-up"**
> Imagine walking into a car dealership. You do not see just *one* car. You see hatchbacks, sedans, SUVs, sports cars, and pickup trucks — each designed for a specific lifestyle. A hatchback is light and easy to park (like a **lightweight wheelchair**), an SUV carries heavy loads and rough terrain (like a **bariatric wheelchair**), a sports car is performance-tuned (like an **ultra-lightweight rigid sports chair**), and a taxi takes you where you want to go when you cannot drive yourself (like a **transport / attendant-propelled chair**).
>
> Similarly, manual wheelchairs are **not "one-size-fits-all"**. The class of wheelchair prescribed depends on the user's **strength, endurance, body size, posture needs, environment, and lifestyle**.

## 1.3 Key Classification Metrics (Bolded for High Recall)

- **Weight (Mass) of frame**: Typically in **kilograms (kg)**, ranging from **>15 kg (Standard)** down to **<7 kg (Ultra-lightweight rigid)**
- **Seat width**: Standard **16, 18, 20 inches**, with bariatric up to **24–30 inches**
- **Maximum user weight (load capacity)**: **113 kg (standard)**, up to **>180 kg (extra heavy-duty)**
- **Wheel diameter (rear)**: Typically **24 inches (61 cm)** for self-propelled; **12 inches (30 cm)** for transport chairs
- **Adjustability indices**: Seat-to-floor height, back angle, camber angle, center-of-gravity (COG) position

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why Classification Matters in KTU Exam**
> Examiners frequently test whether the student can **match a clinical condition to the correct wheelchair class**. Memorize the table mapping: *User strength + environment → class of chair*. This is a **3-mark direct short-answer** favorite.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why Are Wheelchairs Classified?

The classification is grounded in three rehabilitation-engineering principles:

1. **Biomechanical Efficiency**: Rolling resistance $F_r$ on a wheelchair is given by:
   
$$
F_r = \mu_r \cdot W
$$

   where $\mu_r$ is the **rolling resistance coefficient** (≈ 0.008–0.015 on hard surfaces for pneumatic tires) and $W$ is the **static load on the wheel**. Heavier chairs raise $W$ and thus the force the user must produce.

2. **Energy Expenditure Minimization**: A lighter frame reduces the metabolic cost $E$ (in joules or kcal) of self-propulsion. Ultra-lightweight chairs can reduce energy cost by up to **25–30 %** compared to standard chairs.

3. **Clinical Appropriateness & Postural Support**: For users with poor trunk control, **tilt-in-space** or **reclining** classes are mandatory; for bariatric users, **reinforced cross-bracing** is mandatory.

## 2.2 The Seven Major Classes of Manual Wheelchairs

> [!NOTE]
> The following classification is the **canonical K0001–K0007 framework** widely adopted in rehabilitation-engineering textbooks and clinical practice.

### **Class 1 — Standard Manual Wheelchair (K0001)**
- **Mass**: > **15 kg** (typically 17–22 kg)
- **Frame**: Folding, cross-braced (X-frame)
- **Adjustability**: Minimal
- **Indication**: Short-term / institutional use, users with limited self-propulsion
- **Disadvantage**: Heavy, difficult to load into vehicles, low durability under heavy daily use

### **Class 2 — Standard Hemi Wheelchair (K0002)**
- **Mass**: ≈ **15 kg**
- **Distinct feature**: **Lower seat-to-floor height** (≈ 43–48 cm vs. standard 50 cm)
- **Indication**: Users who can **propel with one foot** (hemiplegia — e.g., post-stroke)
- **Clinical note**: A hemi-chair is *not* for everyone; the lower seat can be ergonomically harmful for full-time upper-limb propellers.

### **Class 3 — Lightweight Manual Wheelchair (K0003)**
- **Mass**: **12–15 kg**
- **Frame**: Aluminum alloy, folding or rigid
- **Indication**: Active users with moderate upper-limb strength
- **Advantage**: Improved portability over standard

### **Class 4 — High-Strength Lightweight Wheelchair (K0004)**
- **Mass**: **10–13 kg**
- **Frame**: Reinforced aluminum or titanium, folding
- **Indication**: Long-term daily users; **higher durability** (≥ 3 years of heavy use)
- **Distinguishing feature**: Reinforced axles, upgraded upholstery

### **Class 5 — Ultra-Lightweight Manual Wheelchair (K0005)**
- **Mass**: **< 10 kg**, often **6–9 kg**
- **Frame**: Rigid (most common) or folding; titanium or carbon-fiber options
- **Adjustability**: Extensive — adjustable COG, back angle, camber, footrest angle
- **Indication**: **High-performance users** (e.g., active paraplegics, athletes)
- **Camber angle**: Typically **3°–8°**; increased camber improves lateral stability and protects shoulders from overuse injury.

### **Class 6 — Heavy-Duty (Bariatric) Wheelchair (K0006)**
- **Load capacity**: **113–180 kg** user weight
- **Frame**: Reinforced steel or aluminum, double cross-brace
- **Seat width**: **20–26 inches**
- **Indication**: Bariatric / obese users
- **Trade-off**: Heavier chair, larger footprint

### **Class 7 — Extra Heavy-Duty Wheelchair (K0007)**
- **Load capacity**: **> 180 kg** (up to **270 kg** in some models)
- **Frame**: Reinforced steel, reinforced wheels
- **Indication**: Severe bariatric cases

## 2.3 Specialized Sub-Classes (Beyond K-Code)

| Sub-Class | Defining Feature | Clinical Indication |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport / Companion Chair** | Small rear wheels (12 in), cannot be self-propelled | Patients who cannot self-propel; airport/hospital transit |
| **Reclining Wheelchair** | Backrest reclines to > 90° | Users with kyphosis, hip extension limitations, pressure relief |
| **Tilt-in-Space Wheelchair** | Entire seat tilts 0°–45° while maintaining hip/knee angle | Users with poor trunk control, pressure ulcer prevention |
| **Pediatric Wheelchair** | Smaller frame, growth adjustability | Children; growth kits available |
| **Sports / Racing Wheelchair** | Three-wheel design, low-slung seat, cambered rear wheels | Wheelchair basketball, racing, rugby |
| **One-Arm-Drive Wheelchair** | Two rear wheels linked by a single push-handle system on one side | Hemiplegic users with no leg function |
| **Standing Wheelchair** | Hydraulic/manual mechanism elevates user to standing | Pressure relief, psychological, social inclusion |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Value / Unit | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| $F_r$ | Rolling resistance force | N (Newton) | Propulsion effort |
| $\mu_r$ | Rolling resistance coefficient | 0.008 – 0.015 (pneumatic) | Surface analysis |
| $W$ | Static load on wheel | N | Force analysis |
| $M_{chair}$ | Mass of wheelchair | kg (typically 7 – 22) | Classification |
| $M_{user}$ | Body mass of user | kg (typically 40 – 180+) | Prescription, K-code |
| $M_{total}$ | $M_{chair} + M_{user}$ | kg | Load on rear wheels |
| $\theta_{camber}$ | Rear-wheel camber angle | 0° – 8° | Stability & ergonomics |
| $H_{seat}$ | Seat-to-floor height | 43 – 53 cm | Foot-propulsion eligibility |
| $W_{seat}$ | Seat width | 16 – 30 in | Comfort, fit |
| $E$ | Metabolic energy of propulsion | kcal / min or J | Performance comparison |
| $\eta$ | Propulsion efficiency | 5 % – 15 % | Chair design |
| $C$ | Load capacity (rated) | kg | Safety margin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip**: The relationship $F_r = \mu_r \cdot W$ is a *favourite* 3-mark question. Be ready to compute $F_r$ given $M_{total}$ and a surface.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

This classification is foundational to:
- **Prescription practice** by rehabilitation physicians and occupational therapists.
- **Insurance reimbursement** (e.g., Medicare K-codes in the USA, similar schemes in India).
- **Ergonomic design** — to prevent repetitive-strain injuries (RSI) in long-term users, especially **carpal tunnel syndrome** caused by high rolling resistance and poor frame geometry.
- **Biomechanics research** — propulsion efficiency $\eta$ studies use this classification as a control variable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rolling Resistance on a Manual Wheelchair

**Problem Context**: A **Class 4 high-strength lightweight wheelchair** of mass $M_{chair} = 12 \text{ kg}$ carries a user of mass $M_{user} = 70 \text{ kg}$. The rear pneumatic tires operate on a smooth indoor surface with $\mu_r = 0.010$. Compute the **rolling resistance force** $F_r$ that the user must overcome to maintain motion.

### Step 1 — Identify the total static load
The total mass borne by the chair's wheels (assuming level ground and symmetric loading) is:

$$
M_{total} = M_{chair} + M_{user}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values

$$
M_{total} = 12 + 70 = 82 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert mass to weight (force)
The **weight** $W$ (Newton) is:

$$
W = M_{total} \cdot g
$$

where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ is the acceleration due to gravity.

### Step 4 — Substitute

$$
W = 82 \cdot 9.81 = 804.42 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the rolling-resistance relation

$$
F_r = \mu_r \cdot W
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute values

$$
F_r = 0.010 \cdot 804.42 = 8.04 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 7 — Engineering interpretation

> **Result**: The user must continuously produce a horizontal push force exceeding **≈ 8.04 N** to maintain motion. Note that this is *only the rolling component*; **gradient**, **acceleration**, and **internal friction** of bearings add to this baseline. This is why an **ultra-lightweight K0005 chair** is prescribed for active long-term users — every kilogram saved directly reduces $F_r$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Cost Comparison Between Two Classes

**Problem Context**: Compare the **propulsive energy cost per kilometre** for a user in (a) a **Class 1 standard wheelchair** ($M_1 = 20 \text{ kg}$) vs. (b) a **Class 5 ultra-lightweight rigid chair** ($M_2 = 8 \text{ kg}$). Same user mass $M_{user} = 65 \text{ kg}$, $\mu_r = 0.012$, distance $d = 1 \text{ km} = 1000 \text{ m}$.

### Step 1 — General energy formula
The mechanical work done against rolling resistance is:

$$
E = F_r \cdot d
$$

### Step 2 — Case (a): Standard chair
- $M_{1,total} = 20 + 65 = 85 \text{ kg}$
- $W_1 = 85 \cdot 9.81 = 833.85 \text{ N}$
- $F_{r,1} = 0.012 \cdot 833.85 = 10.006 \text{ N}$
- $E_1 = 10.006 \cdot 1000 = 10\,006 \text{ J} = 10.0 \text{ kJ}$

### Step 3 — Case (b): Ultra-lightweight chair
- $M_{2,total} = 8 + 65 = 73 \text{ kg}$
- $W_2 = 73 \cdot 9.81 = 716.13 \text{ N}$
- $F_{r,2} = 0.012 \cdot 716.13 = 8.594 \text{ N}$
- $E_2 = 8.594 \cdot 1000 = 8\,594 \text{ J} = 8.59 \text{ kJ}$

### Step 4 — Percentage reduction

$$
\Delta E \,\% = \frac{E_1 - E_2}{E_1} \cdot 100 = \frac{10.0 - 8.59}{10.0} \cdot 100
$$

$$
\Delta E \,\% = \frac{1.41}{10.0} \cdot 100 = 14.1 \,\%
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

> **Result**: Switching from a standard K0001 to an ultra-lightweight K0005 reduces rolling-resistance mechanical work by approximately **14 %** per kilometre. In metabolic terms (using a typical human muscular efficiency of 20–25 %), this translates into a **substantial reduction in perceived exertion and shoulder-joint loading**, justifying the prescription of K0005 chairs for long-term active users.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Class Selection Logic

The following matrix maps **user profile → recommended class** and is a *high-yield* answer for 14-mark descriptive questions.

| User Profile | Strength / Endurance | Environment | Recommended Class | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Post-stroke, hemiplegic, foot-propeller | Low upper-limb, intact leg | Indoor / occasional outdoor | **K0002 (Hemi)** | Low seat enables foot propulsion |
| Elderly, nursing-home resident | Low | Indoor only | **K0001 (Standard)** | Low cost, minimal use |
| Active paraplegic, daily commuter | High | Mixed urban | **K0005 (Ultra-lightweight rigid)** | Performance, low mass, adjustability |
| Long-term user, full-time, no insurance limit | Moderate-High | Mixed | **K0004 (High-strength lightweight)** | Durability |
| Obese user, 150 kg | Any | Indoor/outdoor | **K0006 (Heavy-duty)** | Load capacity |
| Wheelchair basketball player | Very high | Sports court | **Custom sports chair** | Agility, camber |
| Pediatric user, growing child | Variable | School/play | **Pediatric with growth kit** | Adjustable dimensions |
| Quadriplegic, poor trunk control | Very low | Indoor | **Tilt-in-space** | Postural support, pressure relief |
| Hospital / airport transit, no self-propulsion | None | Transit only | **Transport chair** | Small wheels, pushed by attendant |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Classification Tree of Manual Wheelchairs

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Manual Wheelchairs] --> B[By Propulsion Type]
    A --> C[By K-Code Class]
    A --> D[By Specialized Function]

    B --> B1[Self-Propelled - Large Rear Wheels]
    B --> B2[Attendant-Propelled - Small Rear Wheels]

    C --> C1[K0001 Standard]
    C --> C2[K0002 Standard Hemi]
    C --> C3[K0003 Lightweight]
    C --> C4[K0004 High-Strength Lightweight]
    C --> C5[K0005 Ultra-Lightweight]
    C --> C6[K0006 Heavy-Duty Bariatric]
    C --> C7[K0007 Extra Heavy-Duty]

    D --> D1[Tilt-in-Space]
    D --> D2[Reclining]
    D --> D3[Pediatric]
    D --> D4[Sports / Racing]
    D --> D5[Standing Wheelchair]
    D --> D6[One-Arm-Drive]
    D --> D7[Transport Chair]

    C1 --> C1a[Mass 17-22 kg]
    C1 --> C1b[Short-term institutional use]
    C5 --> C5a[Mass 6-9 kg]
    C5 --> C5b[Active full-time users]
    C6 --> C6a[Load capacity 113-180 kg]
    C6 --> C6b[Reinforced frame]
```

> **Diagram Note**: This is a hierarchical taxonomy. For exams, you may reproduce a simplified version (root → 3 main branches → 7 K-codes) using either a tree diagram or a tabular format. Both are accepted.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Key Components Common to All Classes

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FRAME[Frame Assembly]
        F1[Cross Brace / X-Frame]
        F2[Side Frames]
        F3[Folding Hinges]
    end

    subgraph SEATING[Seating Subsystem]
        S1[Seat Upholstery]
        S2[Backrest]
        S3[Cushion]
        S4[Headrest - optional]
    end

    subgraph PROPULSION[Propulsion Subsystem]
        P1[Rear Wheels 24 inch]
        P2[Handrims]
        P3[Front Casters 6-8 inch]
        P4[Axle Plate]
    end

    subgraph FOOTREST[Support Subsystem]
        R1[Footrests]
        R2[Armrests]
        R3[Wheel Locks / Brakes]
        R4[Anti-Tipper]
    end

    FRAME --> SEATING
    SEATING --> PROPULSION
    PROPULSION --> FOOTREST
```

> **Engineering Insight**: The **rear-wheel axle position** is the *single most adjustable parameter* that distinguishes K0003 / K0004 / K0005 classes. Moving the axle **forward** (toward the user's COG) reduces the **rolling-resistance moment arm** and improves propulsion efficiency — at the cost of reduced **tipping stability** (mitigated by anti-tippers).

## 4.3 Schematic — Rear-Wheel Camber Geometry

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TOP[Top View of Rear Wheels]
        W1[Rear Wheel Left]
        W2[Rear Wheel Right]
        A1[Vertical Axis - 0 deg camber]
        A2[Cambered Axis - 6 deg]
    end
    A1 -.reference.-> W1
    A2 -.actual.-> W2
```

> **Camber benefit**: At **6° camber**, the handrims sit closer to the user's hands at the top of the stroke (improving shoulder mechanics) and farther at the bottom (improving lateral stability). K0005 chairs typically offer **0°–8°** camber adjustability.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
**Define the term "K0005 ultra-lightweight manual wheelchair" and list two distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A K0005 ultra-lightweight manual wheelchair is a high-performance, manually propelled mobility device defined by a frame mass of **less than 10 kg** (typically 6–9 kg), constructed from materials such as aircraft-grade aluminum, titanium, or carbon fiber.

**Two distinguishing features (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Extensive adjustability** — including adjustable center-of-gravity (COG) axle plate, seat-to-floor height, back angle, and rear-wheel camber (typically 0°–8°).
2. **Rigid or folding frame** with reinforced axles and high-end components (e.g., quick-release wheels, ergonomic handrims), designed for **full-time active users** such as those with spinal cord injuries leading to paraplegia.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
**Differentiate between a "transport wheelchair" and a "self-propelled manual wheelchair" in two points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Transport Wheelchair | Self-Propelled Wheelchair |
|---|---|---|
| **Rear wheel size** | Small (≈ 12 inches) | Large (≈ 24 inches) |
| **Propulsion** | Attendant-pushed only | User self-propels via handrims |
| **Typical user** | Patients who cannot self-propel | Independent active users |
| **Mobility independence** | Low (requires caregiver) | High |

(Any **two valid distinguishing points** earn 1.5 marks each.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** Classify manual wheelchairs into the **seven K-code classes (K0001–K0007)** with reference to **frame mass, load capacity, and clinical indication**. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A **standard manual wheelchair (K0001)** of mass 18 kg carries a user of mass 75 kg on a flat surface. The rolling resistance coefficient is **0.012**. Compute the rolling resistance force. If the same user is transferred to an **ultra-lightweight chair (K0005)** of mass 8 kg, what is the **percentage reduction** in rolling resistance force? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks**

| K-Code | Class Name | Mass (kg) | Load Capacity (kg) | Clinical Indication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| K0001 | Standard | > 15 | ≤ 113 | Short-term / institutional use, low-activity users |
| K0002 | Standard Hemi | ≈ 15 | ≤ 113 | Hemiplegics who foot-propel (low seat height) |
| K0003 | Lightweight | 12–15 | ≤ 113 | Active users with moderate strength |
| K0004 | High-Strength Lightweight | 10–13 | ≤ 113 | Long-term full-time users; high durability |
| K0005 | Ultra-Lightweight | < 10 | ≤ 113 | High-performance active users, paraplegics, athletes |
| K0006 | Heavy-Duty | ≈ 18–25 | 113–180 | Bariatric / obese users |
| K0007 | Extra Heavy-Duty | > 25 | > 180 | Severe bariatric cases |

(Each row well explained = 1 mark × 7 = 7 marks)

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — K0001 rolling resistance:**

$$
M_{1,total} = 18 + 75 = 93 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
W_1 = 93 \times 9.81 = 912.33 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{r,1} = 0.012 \times 912.33 = 10.95 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — K0005 rolling resistance:**

$$
M_{2,total} = 8 + 75 = 83 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
W_2 = 83 \times 9.81 = 814.23 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{r,2} = 0.012 \times 814.23 = 9.77 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage reduction:**

$$
\Delta\% = \frac{F_{r,1} - F_{r,2}}{F_{r,1}} \times 100 = \frac{10.95 - 9.77}{10.95} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta\% = \frac{1.18}{10.95} \times 100 = 10.78 \,\%
$$

> **Final Answer (1 mark)**: The rolling resistance is reduced by **≈ 10.8 %** when switching from K0001 to K0005.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **(a)** Explain with examples the role of **specialized manual wheelchairs** in rehabilitation, covering **tilt-in-space, reclining, sports, and one-arm-drive** chairs. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A bariatric user of mass **150 kg** uses a K0006 heavy-duty wheelchair of mass **22 kg** on a ramp inclined at **5°** to the horizontal. The rolling resistance coefficient is **0.015**. Compute the **total force the user must produce** to move up the ramp at constant velocity. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Tilt-in-space chair**: The seat-to-back angle remains fixed (typically 90°–100°) while the entire seat assembly tilts backward by 0°–45°. This redistributes pressure from the ischial tuberosities to the posterior trunk, **preventing pressure ulcers** in users with poor postural control (e.g., advanced multiple sclerosis, high-level spinal cord injury). *(2 marks)*

2. **Reclining chair**: The backrest reclines to angles greater than 90° (often up to 180°). It is indicated for users with **fixed hip contractures, kyphosis, or who need supine positioning** for catheterization and hygiene. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Sports / racing chair**: Three-wheeled (two large cambered rear wheels + one front caster), rigid frame, very low seat height, with camber up to **16°–20°** for lateral stability during quick turns. Used in wheelchair basketball, rugby, racing. *(2 marks)*

4. **One-arm-drive chair**: Two handrims on one side, mechanically linked (often by a chain or double-rim) so that pushing one rim drives the chair forward and the other rim drives it backward. Used by **hemiplegic users with no leg propulsion** ability. *(1.5 marks)*

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Total mass on incline**

$$
M_{total} = 22 + 150 = 172 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Weight component along the ramp (gravitational resistance)**

$$
F_{g} = M_{total} \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta
$$

$$
F_{g} = 172 \times 9.81 \times \sin(5°)
$$

$$
\sin(5°) \approx 0.0872
$$

$$
F_{g} = 172 \times 9.81 \times 0.0872 = 147.16 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Normal force on the ramp**

$$
N = M_{total} \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta
$$

$$
\cos(5°) \approx 0.9962
$$

$$
N = 172 \times 9.81 \times 0.9962 = 1680.86 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Rolling resistance on the ramp**

$$
F_r = \mu_r \cdot N = 0.015 \times 1680.86 = 25.21 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Total force for constant-velocity motion**

For motion at **constant velocity**, net force = 0, so the propulsive force must balance both the gravity component and the rolling resistance:

$$
F_{total} = F_g + F_r = 147.16 + 25.21 = 172.37 \text{ N}
$$

> **Final Answer (1 mark)**: The user must produce approximately **172.4 N** of push force to climb the 5° ramp at constant velocity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common Mistake (–2 marks)**: Forgetting the gravity component $F_g$ on the ramp and computing *only* rolling resistance. Always draw a free-body diagram on an incline.
> 2. **Common Mistake (–1 mark)**: Using $W = M_{total} \cdot g$ (full weight) for rolling resistance on the ramp — this is wrong; the *normal component* $N = M \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ is correct.
> 3. **Common Mistake (–1 mark)**: Rounding $\sin(5°)$ as 0.1 — use 0.0872 or a calculator-derived value.
> 4. **Unit Error**: Reporting force in kg·m/s² instead of N — **always use Newtons** in KTU answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read before exam)**

- ✅ The **seven K-codes K0001–K0007** are the **international standard** for classifying manual wheelchairs by frame mass and load capacity.
- ✅ **K0005 (ultra-lightweight)** is the **highest-performance class** and is most commonly prescribed for **active long-term users with paraplegia**.
- ✅ **K0002 (hemi)** has a **lower seat-to-floor height** to enable **foot propulsion in hemiplegics**.
- ✅ **K0006 and K0007** are **bariatric** classes with **load capacities 113–180 kg** and **> 180 kg** respectively.
- ✅ **Rolling resistance force**: $F_r = \mu_r \cdot W = \mu_r \cdot M \cdot g$ (flat ground) or $F_r = \mu_r \cdot M \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ (on incline).
- ✅ **On an incline**, total propulsive force = $M g \sin\theta + \mu_r M g \cos\theta$ (constant velocity).
- ✅ **Transport chairs** have **12-inch rear wheels** and **cannot be self-propelled**; they are pushed by an attendant.
- ✅ **Tilt-in-space** chairs maintain **hip angle constant**; **reclining** chairs alter the **hip angle** — a key clinical distinction.
- ✅ **Camber angle** in K0005 chairs is typically **0°–8°**, improving lateral stability and shoulder biomechanics.
- ✅ **Energy savings** of switching from K0001 to K0005 are typically **10–15 %** per km — clinically significant for long-term users.
- ✅ **Sports wheelchairs** use a **three-wheel configuration** with high camber (up to 20°).
- ✅ **One-arm-drive chairs** are prescribed for **bilateral hemiplegia / hemiplegia without leg function**.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Day Tip**
> When asked *"Which class would you prescribe for a 25-year-old active paraplegic?"*, the answer is **K0005 ultra-lightweight** — and you can earn **bonus marks** by adding: *"with adjustable COG axle plate, 6° camber, and rigid titanium frame."* Specificity in KTU answers **always** scores higher.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frame Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frame Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Frame Design in Rehabilitation Engineering** is the systematic engineering process of structurally configuring, dimensioning, and material-selecting the load-bearing skeleton (chassis) of an assistive mobility or therapeutic device — such as a **wheelchair, walker, standing frame, orthosis, or transfer aid** — such that it safely transmits all user-induced **biomechanical loads** (static body weight, dynamic impact, fatigue cycles) to the ground or support surface while satisfying ergonomic, weight, durability, and regulatory (ISO 7176, IS 14664) constraints.

Mathematically, it is the synthesis of:
$$\boxed{F_{design} = \arg\min_{F}\bigl\{M(F),\,C(F),\,V(F)\bigr\} \;\;\text{subject to}\;\; \sigma_{max}(F) \le \frac{\sigma_{y}}{n},\;\; \delta_{max}(F) \le \delta_{allow}}$$

where $M$ = mass, $C$ = cost, $V$ = user-volume clearance, $\sigma_{y}$ = yield strength, $n$ = **factor of safety (typically 2 to 4)**, and $\delta_{allow}$ = permissible deflection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Frame design in rehabilitation engineering bridges **mechanical design methodology** with **human biomechanics** — it is *not* a generic machine design exercise. Every load, dimension, and material choice is dictated by the **end-user's anthropometry, pathology, and activity profile**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are building a **customized bicycle frame for a differently-abled cyclist**. The frame must:
1. Carry the rider's **entire body weight** (sometimes with sudden jerks on uneven terrain).
2. Be **light enough** so the rider (or caregiver) can lift, fold, or push it.
3. Be **strong enough** to survive years of daily loading without breaking.
4. **Fit the rider's body dimensions** like a tailored suit.

Frame design in rehabilitation engineering is exactly this — except the "rider" is a person with a disability, the "terrain" might be a hospital ramp or a home threshold, and the consequences of failure (a frame crack) can mean a **fall, re-injury, or loss of independence**. The engineer must therefore balance **strength, weight, ergonomics, cost, and safety factor** simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world anchor:** The iconic **folding manual wheelchair** uses an "X-frame" or "cross-brace" geometry. This is no accident — the X-brace converts vertical user loads into **pure tension and compression** in two straight members, eliminating bending stresses. This is frame design intuition at its finest.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Metric / Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Standard user body mass (design load) | **75 kg (ISO 7176-8)** | Up to 150 kg for bariatric devices |
| Static safety factor, $n_s$ | **2.0 – 3.0** | For static wheelchair frame |
| Fatigue safety factor, $n_f$ | **1.5 – 2.0** | For dynamic/cyclic loading |
| Permissible deflection, $\delta_{allow}$ | **L / 100 to L / 150** | L = span length |
| Young's modulus — Aluminum 6061-T6 | **E = 69 GPa** | Most common wheelchair tube |
| Young's modulus — Steel AISI 1020 | **E = 200 GPa** | Heavy-duty frames |
| Yield strength — Al 6061-T6 | **σ_y = 240 MPa** | Lightweight rehab frames |
| Yield strength — Ti Grade 9 | **σ_y = 380 MPa** | Premium orthotic frames |
| Density — Aluminum 6061 | **ρ = 2700 kg/m³** | 1/3 the weight of steel |
| Density — Carbon-fiber composite | **ρ = 1600 kg/m³** | Sports/active wheelchairs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Pillar Design Philosophy

Rehabilitation frame design rests on **five pillars** that the KTU examiner expects you to enumerate in any 7-or-14-mark question:

1. **Biomechanical Load Estimation** — Quantify *what* loads the frame must carry.
2. **Material Selection** — Choose the right *substance* for the job.
3. **Structural Configuration / Geometry** — Decide the *shape* (X-frame, T-frame, modular, folding).
4. **Stress & Deflection Analysis** — Verify *how* the frame behaves under load.
5. **Ergonomic & Regulatory Compliance** — Ensure the frame *fits the human* and meets standards.

## 2.2 Pillar 1 — Biomechanical Load Estimation

The frame sees **three principal load classes**:

- **Static load** = user's body weight transmitted through the seat/back. $F_{static} = m \cdot g$ where $m$ = body mass.
- **Dynamic / impact load** = sudden loading on bumps, curbs, or stairs. Captured by a **dynamic amplification factor (DAF)**:
$$F_{dynamic} = \text{DAF} \cdot m \cdot g \quad ; \quad \text{DAF} = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.5$$
- **Cyclic / fatigue load** = millions of load cycles over a 5-year design life. Treated via **Goodman / Soderberg / Gerber fatigue criteria**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key biomechanical insight:** During a **wheelchair push-stroke**, the user applies a peak propulsive force of approximately **500 N** (well above their body weight divided over the rear wheels). This is why *rear-frame joints* are the most fatigue-critical region of a manual wheelchair.

## 2.3 Pillar 2 — Material Selection Rationale

Frame materials are evaluated against a **specific strength index**:

$$\Psi = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\rho \cdot g}$$

Higher $\Psi$ ⇒ lighter frame for the same strength. A **merit-index comparison** for typical rehabilitation-frame materials:

| Material | $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $\Psi = \sigma_y / (\rho g)$ | Relative merit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild steel AISI 1020 | 350 | 7850 | 4.55 | 1.0× (baseline) |
| Aluminum 6061-T6 | 240 | 2700 | 9.06 | 2.0× |
| Titanium Grade 9 | 380 | 4430 | 8.74 | 1.92× |
| Carbon-fiber composite | 600 | 1600 | 38.2 | 8.4× |

This justifies why **aluminum and carbon-fiber dominate modern rehab frames**, and steel is reserved for **bariatric / heavy-duty** cases.

## 2.4 Pillar 3 — Frame Geometry (Configuration Theory)

The geometric choice dramatically alters the **internal force distribution**:

| Geometry | Internal Force State | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Truss (X-cross brace)** | Pure axial (T/C) | Folding manual wheelchairs |
| **Cantilever rigid** | Bending + torsion | Rigid sports / active wheelchairs |
| **Box / tubular ladder** | Bending + shear | Walking frames, rollators |
| **T-frame (T-chassis)** | Mixed bending + axial | Powered wheelchair base |
| **Modular (orthosis-style)** | Bending only | Standing frames, postural supports |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The **X-cross brace** of a folding wheelchair acts as a **four-bar mechanism** in the *folded* state and as a **pin-jointed truss** in the *unfolded* state. In the unfolded state, vertical user load $W$ is split into two **axial components** along the two X-bars, each carrying $T = W / (2 \sin\theta)$, where $\theta$ = angle of the cross-bar with horizontal.

## 2.5 Pillar 4 — Stress & Deflection Analysis

For a frame member treated as a **simply-supported or cantilever beam**, the governing equations are:

$$\sigma_{bending} = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{M}{Z} \quad ; \quad Z = \frac{I}{c}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{W L^3}{48 EI} \;\;(\text{simply supported, central load}) \quad \text{or} \quad \delta_{tip} = \frac{W L^3}{3 EI} \;\;(\text{cantilever})$$

$$\sigma_{axial} = \frac{F}{A} \quad ; \quad \sigma_{buckling, Euler} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L)^2}$$

The **simultaneous yielding criterion** under combined bending + axial load:

$$\sigma_{combined} = \frac{F}{A} + \frac{M \cdot c}{I} \le \frac{\sigma_{y}}{n}$$

## 2.6 Pillar 5 — Ergonomic & Regulatory Compliance

A structurally optimal frame is **useless** if it doesn't fit the user. Compliance checks include:

- **Seat-width** = hip-width + 2 × 25 mm clearance (ISO 7176-7)
- **Seat-depth** = buttock–popliteal length − 1 to 2 cm
- **Backrest height** = axilla-to-seat for low-back, shoulder-to-seat for high-back
- **Wheelchair mass (unloaded)** ≤ **15 kg** for self-propelled (ISO 7176)
- **Tipping angle** ≥ 10° (rearward static stability, ISO 7176-1)

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{dynamic} = \text{DAF} \cdot m g$ | Impact load on frame | N |
| 2 | $\Psi = \sigma_y / (\rho g)$ | Specific strength (material index) | m |
| 3 | $T = W / (2\sin\theta)$ | Axial force in X-brace member | N |
| 4 | $\sigma_b = Mc/I = M/Z$ | Bending stress in frame tube | Pa |
| 5 | $Z = \frac{\pi}{32}(D^4 - d^4) / D$ | Section modulus, hollow tube | m³ |
| 6 | $I = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)$ | Area moment, hollow tube | m⁴ |
| 7 | $\delta_{SS,center} = WL^3 / (48 EI)$ | Deflection, simply-supported, central load | m |
| 8 | $\delta_{cantilever,tip} = WL^3 / (3 EI)$ | Deflection, cantilever tip load | m |
| 9 | $\sigma_{Euler} = \pi^2 E I / (K L)^2$ | Critical buckling stress | Pa |
| 10 | $\sigma_{comb} = F/A + Mc/I$ | Combined axial + bending | Pa |
| 11 | $n = \sigma_y / \sigma_{working}$ | Factor of safety (dimensionless) | — |
| 12 | $\sigma_a/\sigma_{y'} + \sigma_m/\sigma_y = 1/n$ | Soderberg fatigue criterion | Pa |
| 13 | $L_{eff} = K \cdot L$ | Effective length for buckling | m |
| 14 | $\text{Seat width} = b_{hip} + 50$ mm | Ergonomic seat width | m |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1 — Axial Force in an X-Brace Wheelchair Frame

**Statement:** A folding wheelchair uses two X-brace members. A vertical load $W$ acts on the cross-bar. Show that the axial force in each member is $T = W / (2\sin\theta)$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Free-body setup.**
At the cross-bar joint, vertical equilibrium gives:
$$\sum F_y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 2 T \sin\theta - W = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $T$.**
Rearranging:
$$2 T \sin\theta = W$$

$$T = \frac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Limiting cases.**
- When $\theta = 90°$ (vertical bars) → $\sin\theta = 1$ → $T = W/2$.
- When $\theta = 30°$ (shallow angle) → $\sin\theta = 0.5$ → $T = W$.

> This shows that a **shallow X-brace** magnifies member force, demanding stronger tubes.

**Step 4 — Stress check.**
Cross-section of each tube is hollow circular with outer diameter $D$, inner $d$.
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$$
$$\sigma_{axial} = \frac{T}{A} = \frac{W}{2 A \sin\theta} \le \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivative 2 — Minimum Weight of a Cantilever Frame Member

**Statement:** Find the minimum-mass cantilever beam of length $L$, carrying tip load $W$, with a circular hollow cross-section of outer diameter $D$ and inner $d = 0.8 D$, subject to a deflection constraint $\delta \le \delta_{allow}$ and using a material of modulus $E$ and density $\rho$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Deflection of a cantilever with tip load.**
$$\delta_{tip} = \frac{W L^3}{3 E I}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia for hollow circular section.**
$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4) = \frac{\pi D^4}{64}(1 - 0.8^4) = \frac{\pi D^4}{64}(1 - 0.4096) = \frac{0.5904 \pi D^4}{64}$$

**Step 3 — Impose deflection constraint (worst case).**
$$\frac{W L^3}{3 E I} = \delta_{allow}$$

Solve for $D$:
$$I = \frac{W L^3}{3 E \delta_{allow}}$$

$$D^4 = \frac{64\, W L^3}{3 E \delta_{allow} \cdot 0.5904 \,\pi}$$

$$D = \left[\frac{64\, W L^3}{1.7712\,\pi\, E\, \delta_{allow}}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of the member.**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2(1 - 0.64) = 0.36 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = 0.09 \pi D^2$$

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L = 0.09 \pi \rho L D^2$$

**Step 5 — Substitute D².**
$$D^2 = \left[\frac{64\, W L^3}{1.7712\,\pi\, E\, \delta_{allow}}\right]^{1/2} = \frac{8}{\sqrt{1.7712 \pi}} \sqrt{\frac{W L^3}{E \delta_{allow}}}$$

$$D^2 = \frac{8}{2.359}\sqrt{\frac{W L^3}{E \delta_{allow}}} \approx 3.391 \sqrt{\frac{W L^3}{E \delta_{allow}}}$$

$$m_{min} = 0.09 \pi \rho L \cdot 3.391 \sqrt{\frac{W L^3}{E \delta_{allow}}}$$

$$\boxed{m_{min} \approx 0.958 \,\rho L \sqrt{\frac{W L^3}{E \delta_{allow}}}}$$

This is the **classic stiffness-driven minimum-mass design** result and the foundation of all lightweight rehabilitation-frame optimization.

---

## 3.3 Derivative 3 — Combined-Load Safety Verification (Worked Example)

**Problem:** A side-frame of a wheelchair carries: (i) a vertical user weight $W = 750$ N through a 300 mm offset, and (ii) an axial push force $F = 200$ N. Frame tube: 25 mm OD × 1.6 mm wall Al 6061-T6. $\sigma_y = 240$ MPa, $n_{req} = 2.5$. Verify adequacy.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Section properties.**
$$D = 0.025 \text{ m}, \quad t = 0.0016 \text{ m}, \quad d = D - 2t = 0.0218 \text{ m}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025^2 - 0.0218^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(6.25\times 10^{-4} - 4.7524\times 10^{-4})$$

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.4976 \times 10^{-4}) = 1.176 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}(3.906\times 10^{-7} - 2.259\times 10^{-7}) = 8.084 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^4$$

$$Z = I / c = I / (D/2) = 8.084 \times 10^{-9} / 0.0125 = 6.467 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Bending moment.**
$$M = W \cdot e = 750 \times 0.300 = 225 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Bending stress.**
$$\sigma_b = M / Z = 225 / (6.467 \times 10^{-7}) = 3.479 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 347.9 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Axial stress.**
$$\sigma_a = F / A = 200 / (1.176 \times 10^{-4}) = 1.701 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 1.70 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Combined stress.**
$$\sigma_{comb} = \sigma_b + \sigma_a = 347.9 + 1.70 = 349.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with allowable.**
$$\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_y / n = 240 / 2.5 = 96 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7 — Verdict.**
$$\sigma_{comb} = 349.6 \text{ MPa} \;>\; \sigma_{allow} = 96 \text{ MPa} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{NOT ADEQUATE}$$

**Step 8 — Redesign hint.**
Either (a) increase tube OD to 32 mm, (b) use **Ti Grade 9** ($\sigma_y = 380$ MPa), or (c) reduce the load offset by repositioning the seat support.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode × Design Mitigation

| Failure Mode | Governing Equation | Typical Cause | Design Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static yielding** | $\sigma \le \sigma_y / n$ | Overload, low material strength | Increase wall thickness, higher-grade alloy |
| **Excess deflection** | $\delta \le \delta_{allow}$ | Long span, low $E$ | Larger $I$ (bigger OD), stiffer material |
| **Euler buckling** | $P \le \pi^2 EI / (KL)^2$ | Slender compression member | Add cross-bracing, use shorter members |
| **Fatigue cracking** | Soderberg / Goodman | Cyclic push-stroke loading | Radiused joints, shot-peening, avoid sharp corners |
| **Joint loosening** | Bolt preload decay | Vibration, repeated folding | Use locking nuts, Nord-Lock washers, rivets |
| **Corrosion** | Surface oxidation | Sweat, humidity, outdoor use | Anodizing (Al), powder-coating, SS fasteners |
| **Ergonomic mismatch** | Fit indices | Generic frame size | Modular / adjustable design |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Rehabilitation Frame Design Process

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **complete design-flow architecture** — from user assessment to certification — that underpins all frame-design deliverables in KTU 2024 Scheme Module-3 questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Profile Intake] --> B[Anthropometric Data Capture]
    A --> C[Pathology & Activity Mapping]
    A --> D[Environment Survey]
    B --> E[Load Estimation Module]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Material Selection Index]
    F --> G[Frame Geometry Configuration]
    G --> H[Stress & Deflection Analysis]
    H --> I[Factor of Safety Check]
    I --> J{Pass or Fail}
    J -->|Fail| K[Redesign Loop]
    K --> G
    J -->|Pass| L[Ergonomic Fit Verification]
    L --> M[ISO 7176 Compliance Audit]
    M --> N[Prototype Fabrication]
    N --> O[Static Load Testing]
    N --> P[Fatigue Cycle Testing]
    O --> Q{Test Outcome}
    P --> Q
    Q -->|Fail| K
    Q -->|Pass| R[Certification and Release]
```

## 4.2 Functional Decomposition — Forces on a Manual Wheelchair Frame

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[User Body Weight W] --> S1[Seat Tube]
    W2[Push Stroke Force P] --> S2[Rear Frame Joint]
    S1 --> S3[X Brace Left Member]
    S1 --> S4[X Brace Right Member]
    S2 --> S5[Rear Axle Bracket]
    S3 --> S6[Front Frame Tube]
    S4 --> S6
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Footrest Hanger]
    S6 --> S8[Ground Reaction Forces]
    S7 --> S8
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above functional diagram shows how a **single user load $W$** is **distributed, redistributed, and resolved** through the frame hierarchy until it reaches the **ground reaction forces** at the wheels. Each transition is a *potential design vulnerability* — for KTU exam purposes, identify which member is most loaded and why.

## 4.3 Geometry-Configuration Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Frame Requirement] --> Q1{Is the device foldable?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| A1[X Cross Brace Geometry]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is the user active or sports-grade?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2[Single Tube Cantilever Rigid]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Powered or manual?}
    Q3 -->|Manual| A3[Box Tubular Ladder Frame]
    Q3 -->|Powered| A4[T Frame Chassis]
    A1 --> End[Proceed to Stress Analysis]
    A2 --> End
    A3 --> End
    A4 --> End
```

## 4.4 Force-Flow Schematic of an X-Brace Wheelchair (Unfolded State)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UnfoldedWheelchair
        U[User Seated Weight W] -->|Vertical| J[Central Cross Joint]
        J -->|Tension| X1[Left X Bar]
        J -->|Tension| X2[Right X Bar]
        X1 --> R1[Rear Wheel Axle]
        X2 --> R2[Front Caster]
        R1 --> G[Ground Reaction]
        R2 --> G
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define *frame design* in rehabilitation engineering. List the four major load types a wheelchair frame must resist.

**Model Answer:**
Frame design in rehabilitation engineering is the structured process of selecting geometry, material, and dimensions of the load-bearing skeleton of an assistive device so that it safely transfers user-induced biomechanical loads to the support surface while satisfying ergonomic and regulatory constraints.

The four major load types are:
1. **Static load** — sustained body weight.
2. **Dynamic / impact load** — sudden jolts (DAF = 1.5–2.5).
3. **Cyclic / fatigue load** — repeated push strokes.
4. **Tipping / overturning moment** — instability-induced reaction.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State the **specific strength index** and explain why it is the primary material-selection criterion for rehabilitation frames.

**Model Answer:**
$$\Psi = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho g}$$
It represents **strength per unit self-weight**. A higher $\Psi$ means a lighter frame for the same load-bearing capacity, which directly improves **portability, self-propulsion efficiency, and caregiver handling** — the three cardinal priorities in rehabilitation engineering. For example, carbon-fiber composite ($\Psi \approx 38$) yields a frame ~8× lighter than mild steel ($\Psi \approx 4.5$) for the same strength.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> *As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations: each Part-B question carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice is mandatory.*

---

### **Question A** — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A folding manual wheelchair uses two X-brace members. Each tube is **aluminum 6061-T6**, outer diameter 22 mm, wall thickness 1.5 mm. The cross-bar angle with horizontal is $\theta = 35°$. User mass including seat = 80 kg. Take DAF = 2.0 for an emergency curb drop. Calculate the axial stress in each X-bar and verify whether it is within allowable limits (use $n = 2.0$, $\sigma_y = 240$ MPa).

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to the frame above, discuss the **fatigue-design considerations** that a designer must adopt to extend service life to **5 years of daily use (≈ 5 lakh push cycles)**. Mention the relevant ISO standard and two design modifications that reduce fatigue-failure risk at the X-brace pivot.

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Vertical dynamic load.**
$$W = \text{DAF} \cdot m \cdot g = 2.0 \times 80 \times 9.81 = 1569.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Axial force in each X-bar.**
$$T = \frac{W}{2 \sin\theta} = \frac{1569.6}{2 \times \sin 35°} = \frac{1569.6}{2 \times 0.5736} = 1368.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-sectional area.**
$$D = 0.022 \text{ m}, \quad t = 0.0015 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.022 - 0.003 = 0.019 \text{ m}$$
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.022^2 - 0.019^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(4.84 \times 10^{-4} - 3.61 \times 10^{-4}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.23 \times 10^{-4})$$
$$A = 9.66 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Axial stress.**
$$\sigma_{axial} = \frac{T}{A} = \frac{1368.2}{9.66 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.416 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 14.16 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Allowable stress.**
$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n} = \frac{240}{2.0} = 120 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Verdict.**
$$\sigma_{axial} = 14.16 \text{ MPa} \ll 120 \text{ MPa} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{ADEQUATE (with very high margin)}$$

The frame is over-designed for the *static* curb drop — confirming that **fatigue, not static strength, is the governing design criterion** for rehabilitation frames.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Five-year fatigue design life = 5,00,000 push cycles.**

Key considerations and design modifications:

1. **Use a fatigue strength (endurance limit) value** for Al 6061-T6: $\sigma_e \approx 0.4 \sigma_y = 96$ MPa. Apply the **Soderberg criterion** to ensure the alternating stress stays below this limit.

2. **Pivot/joint redesign at the X-brace center:**
   - Replace solid bar with **radiused, filleted weldment** to eliminate sharp stress concentrations.
   - Use a **reinforcing gusset plate** at the central pivot to spread the load over a larger weld footprint.

3. **Surface treatment:**
   - **Shot-peening** the X-bars to introduce compressive residual stresses that retard crack initiation.
   - **Hard anodizing** to improve wear resistance at the pivot bush.

4. **Standard compliance:**
   - **ISO 7176-8** (static, impact, and fatigue strength requirements for wheelchairs) and **ISO 7176-25** (battery-powered wheelchairs) are the relevant standards.
   - The frame must survive the **ISO 7176-8 double-drum and curb-drop fatigue test sequence** without visible cracking.

5. **Service-life extension via maintenance:**
   - Specify **replacement bushings** at the X-brace pivot (every 2 years) to prevent wear-induced play that accelerates fatigue.

---

### **Question B** — *Alternative Choice for Internal Option*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **five-pillar design philosophy** of rehabilitation frame design. Illustrate with the example of a **standing frame** for a child with cerebral palsy.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **minimum-mass expression for a cantilever frame member** of length $L$ carrying tip load $W$, subject to a tip-deflection constraint $\delta_{tip} \le \delta_{allow}$, using a hollow circular cross-section with $d = 0.8 D$, modulus $E$, and density $\rho$. (Refer to derivation 3.2 above for complete step-by-step solution.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Frame-Design questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include DAF** — KTU answers that use only $mg$ for "design load" on a wheelchair frame are typically docked 1–2 marks. Always state: *"A dynamic amplification factor of 1.5–2.5 is applied to account for impact loading."*
> 2. **Mixing up axial force direction** — In an X-brace, the two members are in **tension** when loaded vertically from the top (not compression). Drawing them as compression members is a frequent error.
> 3. **Ignoring ergonomics** — A purely structural answer that omits *seat width, depth, tip-angle, ISO 7176 compliance* scores below 70% of the marks available. Always close your answer with an **ergonomic / regulatory compliance** statement.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** — Use SI units (N, m, Pa) throughout. Mixing mm with m inside the same calculation is a guaranteed mark-loser.
> 5. **Skipping the factor-of-safety comparison** — Computing $\sigma_{working}$ without comparing it to $\sigma_y/n$ leaves the question half-answered.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Frame design = geometry + material + sizing + ergonomics + safety**, all governed by biomechanical load data.
- ✅ Always apply **DAF = 1.5–2.5** to static body weight for dynamic events (curb drops, stair impacts).
- ✅ **Specific strength index** $\Psi = \sigma_y / (\rho g)$ is the primary material-selection metric — Al 6061-T6 is the workhorse alloy; CFRP for high-end.
- ✅ **X-brace geometry** converts vertical loads into **pure axial tension** in two members, minimizing bending and maximizing efficiency.
- ✅ Use **hollow circular tubes** (high $I/A$ ratio) for lightweight, stiff frames.
- ✅ **Factor of safety**: $n_s = 2$–3 (static), $n_f = 1.5$–2 (fatigue).
- ✅ Allowable deflection: $\delta_{allow} = L/100$ to $L/150$.
- ✅ **Euler buckling** must be checked for any slender compression member — the effective length is $K L$, where $K$ depends on end conditions ($K=1$ pinned-pinned, $K=2$ cantilever, $K=0.5$ fixed-fixed).
- ✅ **Fatigue design** uses the **Soderberg / Goodman / Gerber** criteria; Al 6061-T6 endurance limit $\approx 0.4 \sigma_y$.
- ✅ **Ergonomic dimensions** are not optional — ISO 7176-7 defines seat width = hip width + 50 mm; seat depth = buttock-popliteal − 10–20 mm.
- ✅ **Regulatory standards** to memorize: **ISO 7176** (wheelchairs), **IS 14664** (Indian standard), **ISO 16840** (seating).
- ✅ **Joint design** — prefer welded-and-filleted or riveted joints over threaded fasteners for fatigue-critical regions.
- ✅ **Finish and surface treatment** — anodizing for Al, powder-coating for steel, biocompatible coatings for orthotic-skin contact zones.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Materials in Rehabilitation Engineering

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomaterials** are natural or synthetic substances that are engineered to interact with biological systems for medical purposes — either to evaluate, treat, augment, or replace any tissue, organ, or function of the body. In the context of **Rehabilitation Engineering**, materials form the foundational building blocks of every **prosthetic limb, orthotic brace, wheelchair frame, assistive device, and implantable sensor**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEBRT752, the study of materials in rehabilitation engineering encompasses the classification, properties, selection criteria, biocompatibility, and failure mechanisms of substances used in the design and fabrication of assistive technology for persons with disabilities.

## 1.2 Hierarchical Classification of Materials

Materials used in rehabilitation devices can be broadly classified into **four primary families**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Pillars of Biomaterials**
> 1. **Metals** — High strength, durability, load-bearing capability (e.g., Titanium, Stainless Steel 316L, Co-Cr alloys)
> 2. **Polymers** — Lightweight, flexible, corrosion-resistant (e.g., Polyethylene, PMMA, Silicone, Polyurethane)
> 3. **Ceramics** — High wear resistance, biocompatibility (e.g., Alumina, Zirconia, Hydroxyapatite)
> 4. **Composites** — Tailored properties combining two or more phases (e.g., Carbon-fiber reinforced polymers, Glass-fiber composites)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **The Building Block Analogy** 🧱
> Imagine you are building a custom house for a person with specific needs. Just as a civil engineer selects **steel beams for structural support, glass for transparency, plastic for insulation, and wood for aesthetics**, a rehabilitation engineer must select appropriate **materials based on the functional, mechanical, and biological demands** of each assistive device. The "occupant" here is the human body, which is itself a complex, sensitive, and reactive environment. The wrong material choice can lead to **rejection, infection, fatigue failure, or discomfort**.

## 1.4 Why Material Selection is Critical in Rehabilitation

- **Patient Safety:** Materials must be **non-toxic, non-carcinogenic, and non-allergenic**.
- **Functional Performance:** Mechanical properties (strength, stiffness, fatigue life) must match the **anatomical loading conditions**.
- **Longevity:** Devices may be used **continuously for years**; material degradation must be minimal.
- **Comfort:** Density, thermal conductivity, and flexibility influence **patient compliance**.
- **Manufacturability:** Materials must be **processable** into complex anatomical shapes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics** (Bolded Standard Constants/Metrics)
> - **Young's Modulus (E):** Measured in **GPa**; indicates stiffness.
> - **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS):** Measured in **MPa**; maximum stress before failure.
> - **Biocompatibility Index:** Qualitative ISO 10993 classification (non-cytotoxic, non-sensitizing, non-irritant).
> - **Density (ρ):** Measured in **g/cm³**; critical for lightweight prosthetic limbs.
> - **Fatigue Limit:** Measured in **MPa** for 10⁷ cycles; essential for long-term implants.

## 1.5 Application Context in Rehabilitation Devices

| Device Category | Primary Material Used | Functional Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Lower-limb prosthetics (socket) | Thermoplastic polymers, Carbon-fiber composites | Lightweight, customizable, high strength-to-weight ratio |
| Upper-limb prosthetics (terminal device) | Aluminum alloys, Polymers | Modularity, aesthetics, dexterity |
| Orthotic braces (AFO, KAFO) | Polypropylene, Carbon composites | Flexibility, fatigue resistance, low mass |
| Wheelchair frames | Aluminum 7000-series, Titanium | High strength-to-weight, corrosion resistance |
| Implant electrodes (FES) | Platinum-Iridium, Silicone encapsulation | Biocompatibility, electrical conductivity |
| Bone plates and screws | Ti-6Al-4V, Stainless Steel 316L | High strength, osseointegration, MRI compatibility |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Material Property Requirements

The selection of a material for any rehabilitation device follows a **multi-objective optimization** governed by four interrelated criteria:

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Suitability
- The material must withstand **static loads** (body weight, gravitational forces) and **dynamic loads** (gait cycles, impact forces) without permanent deformation or fracture.
- Key parameters: **Young's Modulus (E), Yield Strength (σ_y), Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_uts), Fatigue Strength (σ_f), Fracture Toughness (K_IC).**

### 2.1.2 Biological Compatibility
- The material must not elicit a **toxic, allergic, or inflammatory response**.
- Governed by **ISO 10993** standards.
- Surface properties (roughness, energy, charge) influence **protein adsorption and cell adhesion**.

### 2.1.3 Chemical Stability
- Resistance to **corrosion** (for metals in physiological saline environments).
- Resistance to **hydrolysis, oxidation, and enzymatic degradation** (for polymers).
- **Passivation** is critical: formation of a stable oxide layer (e.g., Cr₂O₃ on stainless steel, TiO₂ on titanium).

### 2.1.4 Manufacturability and Processability
- Compatibility with **casting, machining, molding, 3D-printing, and sterilization** methods.
- Sterilization methods (autoclave at **121°C, 15 psi for 20 minutes**; gamma radiation; ethylene oxide) must not degrade the material.

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Material Families

### 2.2.1 Metallic Biomaterials

> [!NOTE]
> **Metals** are used where **high strength, toughness, and fatigue resistance** are required. They are typically employed in **load-bearing implants, bone fixation devices, and structural components of prosthetic limbs**.

**a) Stainless Steel 316L (ASTM F138)**
- Composition: **Fe – 18% Cr – 14% Ni – 2.5% Mo – <0.03% C**
- The "L" denotes **low carbon** to prevent carbide precipitation and intergranular corrosion.
- Properties: σ_uts ≈ **485–620 MPa**, E ≈ **193 GPa**, ρ ≈ **7.9 g/cm³**
- Application: Temporary fracture fixation plates, screws, external fixators.

**b) Titanium and Ti-6Al-4V (ASTM F136)**
- Commercially Pure (CP) Titanium: Four grades (Grade 1–4) based on oxygen content.
- Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5): The workhorse alloy for permanent implants.
- Properties: σ_uts ≈ **895–1050 MPa**, E ≈ **110 GPa**, ρ ≈ **4.5 g/cm³**
