- **Why it's preferred:** Excellent biocompatibility, superior corrosion resistance (passive TiO₂ layer), and **lowest density-to-strength ratio** among structural metals.
- Application: Bone plates, spinal cages, dental implants, prosthetic pylon connectors.

**c) Cobalt-Chromium Alloys (ASTM F75, F90)**
- Co-28Cr-6Mo (Cast); Co-20Cr-15W-10Ni (Wrought, L605).
- Properties: σ_uts ≈ **655–1500 MPa**, E ≈ **210–240 GPa**, ρ ≈ **8.3–9.2 g/cm³**
- Application: Highly loaded joint replacements (hip, knee), spinal rods.

### 2.2.2 Polymeric Biomaterials

> [!NOTE]
> **Polymers** dominate the rehabilitation device landscape due to their **lightweight nature, ease of fabrication, and tunable mechanical properties**. They are classified based on their response to thermal treatment: **thermoplastics, thermosets, and elastomers**.

**a) Ultra-High Molecular Weight Polyethylene (UHMWPE)**
- Used as the **articulating bearing surface** in total joint replacements.
- Excellent wear resistance; molecular weight typically **3–6 million g/mol**.
- E ≈ **0.5–1 GPa** (highly compliant compared to metals).

**b) Polymethyl Methacrylate (PMMA)**
- Bone cement; used to anchor prosthetic components to bone.
- Polymerizes *in situ* from powder + liquid monomer.
- Application: Grouting agent in cemented arthroplasty.

**c) Silicone Elastomers (PDMS)**
- Biocompatible, flexible, gas-permeable.
- Application: Catheters, finger joints in hand prosthetics, cosmetic skin overlays, padding in orthotic interfaces.

**d) Polypropylene (PP) and Polyethylene (PE)**
- Lightweight thermoplastics used for **orthotic shells** (AFOs, spinal braces).
- Can be thermoformed directly over a plaster model of the patient's limb at approximately **160–180°C**.

**e) Polyetheretherketone (PEEK)**
- A high-performance thermoplastic with **E ≈ 3–4 GPa** (closer to bone than titanium, reducing **stress shielding**).
- Application: Spinal interbody cages, cranial implants, prosthetic socket components.

### 2.2.3 Ceramic Biomaterials

**a) Alumina (Al₂O₃)**
- High hardness (Vickers ~**1800–2000 HV**), excellent wear resistance.
- E ≈ **380 GPa**, σ_uts (compressive) ≈ **2000–4000 MPa**.
- Application: Femoral head in hip prostheses.

**b) Zirconia (ZrO₂)**
- Transformation toughening mechanism enhances fracture toughness.
- Application: Dental crowns, femoral heads (now less favored due to concerns about phase transformation over time).

**c) Hydroxyapatite (HA) — Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆(OH)₂**
- Chemically similar to the mineral phase of bone.
- **Bioactive:** Promotes direct bone bonding (osseointegration).
- Applied as coatings on metallic implants via plasma spraying.

### 2.2.4 Composite Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composites** represent the **state-of-the-art** in modern prosthetics. A composite combines two or more distinct phases — a **matrix** (binder) and a **reinforcement** (fiber or particulate) — to achieve properties unattainable by either component alone.

**a) Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP)**
- High-modulus carbon fibers embedded in an epoxy or PEEK matrix.
- **Specific stiffness (E/ρ)** is **5–10× that of aluminum**.
- Application: Prosthetic pylon tubes, running blades (e.g., Össur Cheetah), transfemoral socket laminations.
- Typical layup: **0°/90°/±45°** quasi-isotropic stacking for multidirectional loading.

**b) Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymer (GFRP)**
- Cheaper alternative to CFRP; lower stiffness but adequate for many orthotic applications.
- Application: KAFO uprights, scoliosis braces (Boston-type).

**c) Sandwich Composites**
- A **low-density core** (honeycomb, foam) bonded between two **high-modulus face sheets**.
- Provides **extremely high bending stiffness-to-weight ratio**.
- Application: Prosthetic socket shells, seating systems for wheelchairs.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these equations — they form the backbone of every material-related numerical problem in KTU examinations.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stress | $\sigma = F/A$ | **MPa (N/mm²)** | Tensile/compressive loading analysis |
| 2 | Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Dimensionless | Deformation calculation |
| 3 | Young's Modulus (Hooke's Law) | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | **GPa** | Stiffness determination |
| 4 | Specific Strength | $\sigma_{uts} / \rho$ | **kN·m/kg** | Lightweight design optimization |
| 5 | Specific Stiffness | $E / \rho$ | **MN·m/kg** | Structural efficiency comparison |
| 6 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral} / \varepsilon_{axial}$ | Dimensionless | Lateral strain computation |
| 7 | Shear Modulus (Isotropic) | $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ | **GPa** | Torsional analysis of prosthetic pylons |
| 8 | Bulk Modulus | $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$ | **GPa** | Hydrostatic loading (e.g., underwater pressure) |
| 9 | Rule of Mixtures (CFRP, longitudinal) | $E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ | **GPa** | Composite stiffness prediction |
| 10 | Rule of Mixtures (Strength, longitudinal) | $\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m$ | **MPa** | Composite strength prediction |
| 11 | Volume Fraction | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Dimensionless | Composite composition check |
| 12 | Fatigue Strength (Basquin's Law) | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N_f)^b$ | **MPa** | Cyclic life prediction |
| 13 | Stress Range | $\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ | **MPa** | Fatigue analysis |
| 14 | Stress Amplitude | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$ | **MPa** | Alternating stress component |
| 15 | Mean Stress | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$ | **MPa** | Static stress component |
| 16 | Stress Ratio | $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$ | Dimensionless | Loading characterization |
| 17 | Modulus of Resilience | $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ | **MJ/m³** | Energy absorbed elastically |
| 18 | Modulus of Toughness | $U_t = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | **MJ/m³** | Total energy absorbed to fracture |
| 19 | Hardness (Vickers) Conversion (approx.) | $HV \approx 3 \times \sigma_{uts}$ | — | Quick UTS estimation |
| 20 | Corrosion Rate (Penetration) | $CR = (K \times W) / (A \times T \times D)$ | **mm/yr** | Implant longevity assessment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** In **prosthetic socket design**, engineers routinely use the **rule of mixtures** to predict the effective stiffness of a **carbon-fiber laminated socket**. The **Basquin's Law** is the standard tool for **fatigue life prediction** of prosthetic components, which may experience **1–2 million gait cycles per year** of use.

## 2.4 The Stress Shielding Problem (Conceptual Deep Dive)

> [!WARNING]
> **Stress Shielding** is a critical phenomenon in implant design. When a stiff metallic implant (e.g., titanium, E ≈ 110 GPa) is placed alongside bone (E ≈ 10–30 GPa), it **bears a disproportionate share of the physiological load**, causing the surrounding bone to **resorb** (Wolff's Law). This leads to **osteopenia, implant loosening, and revision surgery**. Modern materials like **PEEK (E ≈ 3–4 GPa)** and **isoelastic composite stems** are being developed to **match the host bone's stiffness** and minimize this effect.

## 2.5 Smart Materials in Rehabilitation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart materials** are those that respond predictably to external stimuli by changing their properties (shape, stiffness, conductivity). They represent the **frontier of rehabilitation engineering**.

- **Shape Memory Alloys (SMA):** **Nitinol** (Nickel-Titanium) — recovers its original shape upon heating above its transformation temperature (~**35°C for body-activated applications**). Used in **orthodontic wires, self-expanding stents, and dynamic orthoses**.
- **Piezoelectric Materials:** **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate), PVDF** — generate voltage under mechanical stress (used in **energy-harvesting prosthetics and gait sensors**).
- **Magnetorheological (MR) Fluids:** Viscosity changes under magnetic field — used in **adaptive dampers for prosthetic knees**.
- **Electroactive Polymers (EAP):** Deform under applied voltage — experimental use in **artificial muscle actuators**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Composite Stiffness Using the Rule of Mixtures

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A transtibial prosthetic socket is fabricated as a carbon-fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP) composite. The composite contains **40% by volume of high-modulus carbon fibers** in an epoxy matrix. The fiber modulus is **E_f = 230 GPa** and the matrix modulus is **E_m = 3.5 GPa**. Calculate the **effective longitudinal Young's Modulus** of the composite and the **specific stiffness**, given the composite density is **ρ_c = 1.6 g/cm³**. Compare with commercially pure titanium (E = 110 GPa, ρ = 4.5 g/cm³).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the volume fractions.**

We are given that the volume fraction of fibers is **V_f = 0.40**. Since the composite consists of only fibers and matrix, the volume fraction of the matrix is:

$$V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60$$

**Step 2: Apply the Rule of Mixtures for longitudinal stiffness (iso-strain condition).**

Under longitudinal loading, both fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** (iso-strain Voigt model). The effective composite modulus is:

$$E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$$

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$E_c = (0.40 \times 230) + (0.60 \times 3.5)$$

$$E_c = 92.0 + 2.1$$

$$E_c = 94.1 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the specific stiffness of the composite.**

The specific stiffness is defined as the **modulus-to-density ratio**, a critical metric for prosthetic applications where weight minimization is paramount:

$$\left(\frac{E}{\rho}\right)_c = \frac{94.1 \text{ GPa}}{1.6 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

$$\left(\frac{E}{\rho}\right)_c = 58.8 \text{ GPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the specific stiffness of commercially pure titanium for comparison.**

$$\left(\frac{E}{\rho}\right)_{Ti} = \frac{110 \text{ GPa}}{4.5 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

$$\left(\frac{E}{\rho}\right)_{Ti} = 24.4 \text{ GPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Step 6: Compute the performance ratio.**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{(E/\rho)_c}{(E/\rho)_{Ti}} = \frac{58.8}{24.4} \approx 2.41$$

### Analytical Interpretation

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Although the composite's absolute stiffness (**94.1 GPa**) is **lower** than titanium's (**110 GPa**), its **specific stiffness is 2.41× higher**. For a prosthetic pylon, where the mass is supported at the distal end, this translates to **dramatically lower inertial loading and reduced energy expenditure** during gait — a decisive advantage for amputees.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fatigue Life Prediction Using Basquin's Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A prosthetic hip femoral stem made of Ti-6Al-4V is subjected to a **fully reversed cyclic load** (R = −1) during walking. The fatigue strength coefficient is **σ_f' = 1050 MPa** and the fatigue strength exponent is **b = −0.09**. Calculate the **number of cycles to failure** if the stress amplitude is **σ_a = 450 MPa**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Recall Basquin's Law.**

For high-cycle fatigue, the relationship between stress amplitude and number of cycles to failure is:

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b$$

**Step 2: Substitute the known values.**

$$450 = 1050 \cdot (2N_f)^{-0.09}$$

**Step 3: Isolate the term containing N_f.**

$$(2N_f)^{-0.09} = \frac{450}{1050} = 0.4286$$

**Step 4: Take the logarithm of both sides.**

$$-0.09 \cdot \ln(2N_f) = \ln(0.4286)$$

$$\ln(2N_f) = \frac{\ln(0.4286)}{-0.09} = \frac{-0.8473}{-0.09} = 9.414$$

**Step 5: Solve for 2N_f.**

$$2N_f = e^{9.414} = 12{,}335$$

**Step 6: Solve for N_f.**

$$N_f = \frac{12{,}335}{2} = 6{,}168 \text{ cycles}$$

### Analytical Interpretation

> [!WARNING]
> **Clinical Significance:** An active amputee takes approximately **1.5–2.0 million gait cycles per year**. A fatigue life of only **6,168 cycles** at 450 MPa indicates that this stress level is **unacceptably high** for the implant. The design engineer must either (a) **reduce the stress amplitude** through geometric optimization (larger cross-section, fillet radii), or (b) **select a material with a higher fatigue strength coefficient**, or (c) apply a **surface treatment** (shot peening, nitriding) to introduce compressive residual stresses.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shear Modulus and Torsional Rigidity of a Prosthetic Pylon

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A tubular CFRP pylon for a transtibial prosthesis has an **outer diameter of 30 mm** and an **inner diameter of 28 mm**. The longitudinal Young's modulus of the composite is **E = 95 GPa** and Poisson's ratio is **ν = 0.30**. Calculate: (a) the **shear modulus G**, and (b) the **torsional rigidity (GJ)** of the pylon.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the shear modulus from the isotropic relationship.**

For an isotropic material (assumption for the composite in this simplified analysis):

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

$$G = \frac{95}{2(1+0.30)} = \frac{95}{2.60}$$

$$G = 36.54 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the polar moment of inertia (J) for a hollow circular tube.**

The formula for the polar moment of inertia of a hollow cylinder is:

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32} \left(D_o^4 - D_i^4\right)$$

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32} \left(30^4 - 28^4\right) \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the fourth powers.**

$$30^4 = 810{,}000 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$28^4 = 614{,}656 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 4: Compute the difference.**

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32} (810{,}000 - 614{,}656)$$

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32} \times 195{,}344$$

$$J = \frac{613{,}673}{32}$$

$$J = 19{,}177 \text{ mm}^4$$

Converting to standard SI units:

$$J = 19{,}177 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^4 = 1.918 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 5: Calculate the torsional rigidity (GJ).**

$$GJ = (36.54 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}) \times (1.918 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4)$$

$$GJ = 700.8 \text{ N·m}^2$$

### Analytical Interpretation

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The torsional rigidity **GJ** governs the pylon's resistance to **rotational twisting** during uneven terrain traversal and pivoting movements. A higher GJ value indicates the pylon will transfer ground-reaction torques to the socket with **minimal angular deflection**, improving the amputee's balance and proprioception.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "biomaterial" and list any four essential properties required for a material to be used in an implantable rehabilitation device.**

**Model Answer:**

A **biomaterial** is a natural or synthetic substance engineered to interface with biological systems for therapeutic or diagnostic purposes — evaluating, treating, augmenting, or replacing a bodily tissue, organ, or function.

**Four Essential Properties:**

1. **Biocompatibility** — Must not provoke toxic, allergic, inflammatory, or carcinogenic response (ISO 10993 compliant).
2. **Adequate Mechanical Strength** — Must withstand physiological static and dynamic (fatigue) loads over the device lifetime.
3. **Corrosion / Degradation Resistance** — Must resist chemical attack by body fluids (pH ~7.4, 0.9% NaCl) to prevent release of toxic ions.
4. **Manufacturability and Sterilizability** — Must be formable into complex anatomical shapes and survive standard sterilization (autoclave, gamma, EtO) without property loss.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the phenomenon of "stress shielding" in bone-implant systems. How does the choice of implant material influence it?**

**Model Answer:**

**Stress Shielding** is the *reduction in physiological stress* experienced by bone when a much stiffer metallic implant (e.g., titanium E ≈ 110 GPa) carries a disproportionate share of the mechanical load, leaving the adjacent bone in a state of *under-loading*. According to **Wolff's Law**, bone remodels in response to mechanical demand; under-loaded bone **resorbs**, leading to **osteopenia, implant loosening, and potential revision surgery**.

**Influence of Material Choice:**

- **Stiff metals (Ti, Co-Cr, SS):** High stress shielding risk.
- **Polymer composites (CFRP, PEEK):** Lower modulus, more closely matched to cortical bone (E ≈ 10–30 GPa) — **reduces stress shielding**.
- **Isoelastic implants:** Engineered to have a stiffness profile matching host bone, minimizing the effect.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically divided into two sub-parts: **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Both Question A and Question B cover the same topic area, allowing students to choose one.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q.A. (a) Classify the biomaterials used in rehabilitation engineering with suitable examples. Discuss in detail the properties and applications of metallic biomaterials.**  **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Biomaterials (with examples):**

| Class | Examples |
|---|---|
| Metals | Ti-6Al-4V, SS 316L, Co-Cr-Mo |
| Polymers | UHMWPE, PMMA, Silicone, PEEK |
| Ceramics | Alumina, Zirconia, Hydroxyapatite |
| Composites | CFRP, GFRP, Sandwich structures |
| Natural biomaterials | Collagen, Chitosan, Silk fibroin |

**Metallic Biomaterials — Detailed Discussion:**

**1. Stainless Steel 316L:**
- Composition: Fe–18Cr–14Ni–2.5Mo–<0.03C.
- Properties: σ_uts ≈ 485–620 MPa, E ≈ 193 GPa, ρ ≈ 7.9 g/cm³.
- The **"L"** (low carbon) prevents **chromium carbide precipitation** at grain boundaries, which would otherwise cause **intergranular corrosion**.
- **Applications:** Temporary bone plates, screws, external fixators, orthopedic pins. (Not preferred for permanent implants due to **nickel ion release**.)

**2. Titanium and Ti-6Al-4V:**
- CP Ti has four grades (Grade 1: highest purity, lowest strength; Grade 4: highest strength).
- Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5) is the **workhorse alloy**: σ_uts ≈ 895–1050 MPa, E ≈ 110 GPa, ρ ≈ 4.5 g/cm³.
- A stable, adherent **TiO₂ passive layer** (~2–7 nm thick) provides **exceptional corrosion resistance** and **osseointegration** capability.
- **Applications:** Permanent implants — dental, spinal cages, femoral stems, bone plates, prosthetic connectors. Fully **MRI-compatible** (non-ferromagnetic).

**3. Cobalt-Chromium Alloys:**
- Cast Co-Cr-Mo (ASTM F75) and wrought Co-Cr-W-Ni (ASTM F90, L605).
- Properties: σ_uts ≈ 655–1500 MPa, E ≈ 210–240 GPa, ρ ≈ 8.3–9.2 g/cm³.
- Very high **wear resistance** and **corrosion fatigue strength**.
- **Applications:** Articulating surfaces in total hip/knee replacements, spinal rods, dental frameworks.

**Comparative Summary:** Titanium is preferred for permanent implants due to its **biocompatibility, low density, and corrosion resistance**, while SS 316L remains popular for **temporary fixation devices** due to lower cost and adequate mechanical performance.

---

**Q.A. (b) A CFRP composite used in a prosthetic pylon has a fiber volume fraction of 0.35. The carbon fiber has E_f = 220 GPa and the epoxy matrix has E_m = 3.0 GPa. Calculate (i) the effective longitudinal Young's modulus, (ii) the specific stiffness of the composite (ρ_c = 1.55 g/cm³), and (iii) the percentage improvement in specific stiffness over aluminum alloy (E = 70 GPa, ρ = 2.7 g/cm³).**  **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Effective Longitudinal Young's Modulus:**

Using the **Rule of Mixtures** (iso-strain Voigt model):

$$E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m = (0.35 \times 220) + (0.65 \times 3.0)$$

$$E_c = 77.0 + 1.95 = 78.95 \text{ GPa} \approx 79.0 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Specific Stiffness of the Composite:**

$$\left(\frac{E}{\rho}\right)_c = \frac{79.0 \text{ GPa}}{1.55 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 50.97 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**(iii) Specific Stiffness of Aluminum and Percentage Improvement:**

$$\left(\frac{E}{\rho}\right)_{Al} = \frac{70}{2.7} = 25.93 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

$$\text{Improvement \%} = \frac{50.97 - 25.93}{25.93} \times 100 = \frac{25.04}{25.93} \times 100 \approx 96.6\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The CFRP composite offers **~97% higher specific stiffness** than aluminum, justifying its use in energy-storing prosthetic feet and pylons where mass reduction directly correlates with reduced gait energy expenditure.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q.B. (a) Describe the classification, properties, and applications of polymeric biomaterials used in orthotics and prosthetics. Explain with examples why polymers are the most widely used material class in rehabilitation devices.**  **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Polymeric Biomaterials:**

| Sub-class | Examples | Key Trait |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermoplastics** | PP, PE, PEEK, PMMA | Reversible softening on heating; recyclable; thermoformable |
| **Thermosets** | Epoxy, Phenolic | Irreversibly hardened; high crosslink density; high strength |
| **Elastomers** | Silicone (PDMS), Polyurethane | High elastic strain (>100%); flexible; resilient |
| **Hydrogels** | PVA, PHEMA, Hyaluronic acid | High water content; soft tissue mimicry |

**Properties and Applications:**

1. **UHMWPE** — Molecular weight 3–6 million g/mol; used as the **articulating bearing** in total joint replacements; near-zero wear rate.

2. **PMMA (Bone Cement)** — Polymerizes *in situ*; used to **anchor prosthetic stems** into the medullary canal; compressive strength ~70–100 MPa.

3. **Polypropylene (PP)** — Used for **thermoformed orthotic shells** (AFOs, spinal orthoses); can be drape-molded at 160–180°C over a plaster positive.

4. **Silicone (PDMS)** — Biocompatible, gas-permeable; used in **cosmetic prosthetic skins, finger joints, padding interfaces, and socket liners** for pressure distribution.

5. **PEEK** — E ≈ 3–4 GPa (close to bone); used in **spinal cages, cranial implants, and prosthetic connectors**; radiolucent (does not interfere with X-ray imaging).

6. **Carbon-fiber reinforced epoxy** — Used in **structural laminations** of prosthetic sockets; provides high strength-to-weight ratio.

**Why Polymers Dominate Rehabilitation Devices:**

- **Lightweight** (ρ ≈ 0.9–1.5 g/cm³) — critical for patient comfort.
- **Easily fabricated** into custom anatomical shapes via molding, 3D printing, and lamination.
- **Tunable stiffness** — by adjusting molecular weight, crystallinity, and reinforcement.
- **Cost-effective** compared to titanium and Co-Cr alloys.
- **Corrosion-proof** in physiological environments (unlike metals).
- **Aesthetically versatile** — can be colored, textured, and surface-treated to match skin tones.

---

**Q.B. (b) With a neat sketch, explain the structure of a typical fiber-reinforced composite used in a transtibial prosthetic socket. Discuss the role of fiber orientation, matrix, and stacking sequence on its mechanical performance.**  **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Structure of a Transtibial Prosthetic Socket (CFRP Laminate):**

A modern transtibial socket is typically fabricated as a **multi-ply composite laminate** built around a plaster or 3D-printed positive model of the residual limb. The layup sequence (from inner to outer surface) is:

1. **Inner Liner:** A flexible polymer (e.g., silicone or PE foam) for **skin interface comfort and pressure distribution**.
2. **Nylon Stockinette:** Provides a smooth base for resin application.
3. **Composite Layup:** Typically **3–6 plies** of woven or unidirectional carbon fabric, each pre-impregnated with **epoxy or vinylester resin**.
4. **Outer Curing Layer:** A PVA bag is sealed over the layup, and **vacuum is applied** to consolidate the laminate; cured at room temperature or in a **60–80°C oven for 2–4 hours**.

**Schematic Block Diagram of Composite Layup:**

```
+-------------------------------------+
|  Outer PVA Bag + Vacuum             |
+-------------------------------------+
|  Ply 6: 0 deg carbon (hoop)         |
+-------------------------------------+
|  Ply 5: 45 deg carbon (shear)       |
+-------------------------------------+
|  Ply 4: 90 deg carbon (circumf.)    |
+-------------------------------------+
|  Ply 3: -45 deg carbon (shear)      |
+-------------------------------------+
|  Ply 2: 0 deg carbon (axial)        |
+-------------------------------------+
|  Ply 1: 0 deg carbon (axial)        |
+-------------------------------------+
|  Nylon Stockinette                   |
+-------------------------------------+
|  Inner Socket Liner (Silicone)       |
+-------------------------------------+
|  Residual Limb Interface            |
+-------------------------------------+
```

**Role of Design Parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect on Performance |
|---|---|
| **Fiber Orientation (0°)** | Maximizes **axial (longitudinal) stiffness** — resists bending along the limb axis. |
| **Fiber Orientation (±45°)** | Maximizes **shear and torsional stiffness** — critical for mediolateral and rotational loads during gait. |
| **Fiber Orientation (90°)** | Provides **hoop (circumferential) strength** — resists radial expansion and burst loads. |
| **Matrix (Epoxy Resin)** | Transfers load **between fibers**, maintains fiber position, and provides **environmental sealing**; low matrix modulus keeps the composite lightweight. |
| **Stacking Sequence** | A **quasi-isotropic layup** [0/45/90/−45]_s provides **balanced properties** in all directions; unsymmetric stacking induces **bending-stretch coupling** (undesirable for a socket). |
| **Number of Plies** | More plies = higher thickness = higher stiffness and strength, but **increased weight**; optimization required. |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A well-designed transtibial socket must be **stiff in the anterior-posterior plane** (to transfer body weight efficiently) yet allow **slight flexure distally** for shock absorption. This is achieved by **grading the laminate** — adding more plies in high-stress proximal regions and fewer in distal regions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Material Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing E and σ_uts:** Students often interchange Young's Modulus (stiffness) with Ultimate Tensile Strength (failure stress). Remember: *E* is from the **elastic region** of the stress-strain curve; *σ_uts* is the **maximum stress** the material can sustain.
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** Always include **MPa or GPa** with stress/modulus values and **g/cm³** with density. KTU examiners deduct marks for unit omissions.
> 3. **Incorrect Rule of Mixtures Application:** The **longitudinal (Voigt) model** assumes iso-strain and is valid for fibers aligned with the load. For **transverse loading**, the **Reuss (iso-stress) model** must be used: $E_c = E_f E_m / (V_f E_m + V_m E_f)$.
> 4. **Ignoring Biocompatibility in Numerical Problems:** Even if a question is computational, mention the **biocompatibility grade** in your final conclusion. KTU examiners reward **holistic engineering judgment**.
> 5. **No "Because" in Definitions:** A 3-mark definition question is incomplete without **explaining the mechanism or significance** — not just stating the term.
> 6. **Confusing "L" in 316L:** Students often write "316L stainless steel has *low density*" — it actually means **"low carbon"** (≤0.03%), which prevents intergranular corrosion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Materials**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- **Biomaterial:** Substance engineered to interface with biological systems for medical purposes.
- **Biocompatibility:** Ability of a material to elicit an appropriate host response (per ISO 10993).
- **Stress Shielding:** Bone resorption due to load transfer to a stiffer implant.
- **Osseointegration:** Direct structural connection between living bone and implant surface.
- **Fatigue:** Progressive, localized structural damage under cyclic loading.

### ✅ Key Materials and Their "Signature Properties"
- **Ti-6Al-4V:** E ≈ 110 GPa, ρ ≈ 4.5 g/cm³, MRI-safe, workhorse implant alloy.
- **SS 316L:** E ≈ 193 GPa, ρ ≈ 7.9 g/cm³, "L" = low carbon, temporary fixation.
- **Co-Cr-Mo:** E ≈ 210 GPa, ρ ≈ 8.3 g/cm³, high wear resistance, joint replacements.
- **UHMWPE:** Articulating surface in joint prostheses.
- **PMMA:** Bone cement; polymerizes in situ.
- **PEEK:** E ≈ 3–4 GPa, bone-matched, radiolucent, minimal stress shielding.
- **CFRP:** E/ρ ≈ 5–10× aluminum; prosthetic pylons, running blades.
- **Nitinol:** Shape-memory alloy, orthodontic wires, stents.
- **Hydroxyapatite:** Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆(OH)₂, bioactive ceramic, bone-bonding coatings.

### ✅ Formulae You MUST Memorize
- $\sigma = F/A$ — Stress
- $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$ — Young's Modulus
- $E/\rho$ — Specific Stiffness
- $E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ — Rule of Mixtures (longitudinal)
- $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ — Shear Modulus
- $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N_f)^b$ — Basquin's Fatigue Law
- $J = \pi(D_o^4 - D_i^4)/32$ — Polar Moment of Inertia (hollow tube)

### ✅ Application-to-Material Mapping
- **Prosthetic socket:** CFRP laminate (high specific stiffness).
- **Orthotic AFO/KAFO:** Polypropylene, GFRP (formability, light weight).
- **Bone plate:** Ti-6Al-4V (biocompatibility, strength).
- **Socket liner:** Silicone (soft, skin-friendly).
- **Spinal cage:** PEEK (bone-matched modulus, radiolucency).
- **Hip femoral head:** Alumina or Co-Cr (wear resistance).
- **Wire in orthodontic/orthotic SMA device:** Nitinol (shape memory).

### ✅ High-Frequency Exam Buzzwords
- *Biocompatibility, ISO 10993, Osseointegration, Stress Shielding, Wolff's Law, Passivation, Basquin's Law, Rule of Mixtures, Specific Stiffness, Quasi-isotropic Layup, Transformation Toughening, Shape Memory Effect, Galvanic Corrosion, Creep, Hydrolysis.*

### ✅ Common Mistakes to Avoid
- Mixing up **MPa** (strength) with **GPa** (modulus) — 1000× difference!
- Writing **316L** as "low-carbon" is correct; writing "low-alloy" is wrong.
- For composite problems, **always check V_f + V_m = 1** before calculation.
- In fatigue problems, **R = −1** means fully reversed loading; **R = 0** means pulsating (zero-to-tension).
- Do not confuse **Vickers Hardness (HV)** with **Young's Modulus (E)** — they have different units and meanings.

---

> **End of Notes — Module 3: Materials | PEBRT752 — Rehabilitation Engineering**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | B.Tech (NEP 2020)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheelchair and Rider
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wheelchair and Rider

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🦽 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Wheelchair–Rider System — A Formal Definition

In the context of **Rehabilitation Engineering**, a **wheelchair** is a manually or externally powered mobility device prescribed for individuals with **locomotor disabilities** (paraplegia, quadriplegia, spina bifida, post-polio, lower-limb amputation, cerebral palsy, geriatric frailty, etc.) who cannot achieve efficient, independent, or safe ambulation.

The term **"Wheelchair and Rider"** (sometimes called the **Rider–Wheelchair System** or **Human–Machine Interface** in mobility engineering) refers to the **integrated biomechanical–mechanical unit** formed when a human user operates the wheelchair. The rider is **not a passive load** — they are the *prime mover* (in manual chairs) or the *high-level controller and dynamic payload* (in powered chairs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *The Wheelchair–Rider system is a coupled dynamic system in which the anthropometric, biomechanical, and physiological characteristics of the user interact with the mechanical, ergonomic, and tribological properties of the chair to determine overall mobility performance, energy efficiency, and risk of secondary injury.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍽️

Think of a **bicycle and cyclist** at the Tour de France. The bike alone is just a frame with wheels. The cyclist alone cannot move at 60 km/h. The performance record, comfort, and safety come from the **fit between human and machine** — the saddle height, handlebar reach, pedal ergonomics, weight distribution.

A wheelchair is *exactly* the same, but the stakes are higher because:

- The user may have **impaired sensation** (cannot feel pressure sores forming).
- The user may have **asymmetric strength** (hemiplegia) → chair must compensate.
- The chair may be used **16+ hours a day** (chronic users) → ergonomic failure = medical complications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** The wheelchair is the rider's **legs**. Just as a prosthetic leg must be socket-fit to the residual limb, the wheelchair must be "socket-fit" to the rider's pelvis, spine, hands, and feet. A poor fit causes pressure ulcers, shoulder injuries, scoliosis, and chronic pain.

---

## 1.3 Wheelchair Categories (KTU 2024 Classification)

| Category | Drive Type | Typical User | Energy Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual – Self-Propelled** | Rear-wheel drive (large rear wheels) | Bilateral upper-limb function | Human (rider) |
| **Manual – Attendant-Propelled** | Small rear wheels (transit chair) | Children, frail elderly, severely disabled | Caregiver |
| **Powered Wheelchair (PWC)** | Joystick / head / sip-and-puff | Quadriplegia, advanced MS, ALS | Battery + DC motors |
| **Sports / Active Wheelchair** | Cambered rear wheels, rigid frame | Athletes, active users | Human |
| **Powered Standing / Tilt-in-Space** | Powered actuators | Pressure-sore prevention, postural care | Battery |
| **Pediatric Wheelchair** | Modular, growth-adjustable | Children (CP, spina bifida) | Human or powered |

---

## 1.4 The Rider — Anthropometric and Clinical Profile

The rider is characterized by:

1. **Anthropometry** — body segment lengths (thigh, lower leg, sitting height, arm reach, hip width).
2. **Postural status** — fixed deformities (windswept hips, scoliosis, contractures).
3. **Neuromuscular function** — strength, spasticity, coordination, sensation.
4. **Cognitive ability** — judgment, spatial awareness, ability to learn joystick control.
5. **Lifestyle/Environment** — indoor vs. outdoor, rural Kerala terrain vs. city Kochi roads, work demands.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard:** Wheelchair prescription follows **ISO 7176 (Wheelchairs — Series of International Standards)** for dimensions, performance, and safety. Anthropometric reference data comes from **ISO 7250** and national surveys (e.g., **IAPQR — Indian Anthropometric Dimensions**).

---

## 1.5 The Four Pillars of a Successful Wheelchair–Rider System

| Pillar | Engineering Question |
|---|---|
| **Fit** | Are the seat depth, width, back height, footrest length matched to the rider? |
| **Function** | Can the rider propel, brake, transfer, and reach objects? |
| **Comfort & Health** | Are pressure, posture, and temperature managed to prevent ulcers? |
| **Safety & Stability** | Will the chair tip over, or will it crash the rider? |

These pillars map directly to **KTU Course Outcomes CO3 and CO4** (design and evaluate rehabilitation devices).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Coupled Rider–Wheelchair Dynamic Model

The complete system can be represented as a **2-DOF or 3-DOF multibody model** in the sagittal plane:

$$
M_{total}\,\ddot{x} = F_{prop} - F_{roll} - F_{grade} - F_{aerodynamic}
$$

where:

- $M_{total} = M_{rider} + M_{chair} + M_{payload}$ → total effective mass.
- $F_{prop}$ → propulsive force generated by the rider (or motors).
- $F_{roll}$ → rolling resistance at the rear wheels.
- $F_{grade$ → gravitational component on a slope of angle $\theta$.
- $F_{aerodynamic$ → wind drag (negligible at typical wheelchair speeds <2 m/s, but required for ISO 7176-6).

---

## 2.2 The Rider as Prime Mover — Propulsion Biomechanics

For a **manual rear-wheel-drive wheelchair**, the rider applies force to the **pushrim** (handrim) of the rear wheel.

### Effective Mechanical Advantage (EMA)

$$
\text{EMA} = \frac{r_{wheel}}{r_{pushrim}}
$$

where $r_{wheel}$ is the rear-wheel radius and $r_{pushrim}$ is the pushrim radius.

> **Standard:** A typical **24-inch (610 mm)** wheel with an **8-inch (203 mm)** pushrim gives EMA ≈ 3.0. A higher EMA (small pushrim) → easier to start from rest, but slower top speed. A lower EMA (large pushrim or geared handrim) → faster, but requires more torque at start.

### Energy Cost of Propulsion

$$
E_{per\,stroke} = \int F_{pushrim} \cdot r_{pushrim} \cdot d\theta
$$

The **physiological cost** is often reported as **Oxygen Cost (mL O₂/kg/m)** or **Physiological Cost Index (PCI)**:

$$
\text{PCI} = \frac{\text{Heart Rate during walking/resting} - \text{Resting Heart Rate}}{\text{Speed (m/min)}}
$$

A poorly configured wheelchair dramatically increases PCI → rider fatigue → reduced community participation.

---

## 2.3 Static Stability of the Rider–Chair System

This is **the most-tested concept in KTU papers** for Module 3.

A wheelchair can tip **forward** (about the front casters) or **rearward** (about the rear axle). The condition for **static stability** is that the **anti-tip moment** must exceed the **tip moment**.

### Forward Tip (about front caster axis)

The rider's center of gravity (CoG) must lie **behind** the front-caster contact point:

$$
x_{CoG} < x_{caster} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Stable}
$$

The **forward safety margin** is:

$$
\text{FSM}_{forward} = \frac{x_{caster} - x_{CoG}}{L_{wheelbase}} \times 100\%
$$

### Rearward Tip (about rear axle)

The combined CoG of rider + chair must lie **in front of** the rear-wheel contact point:

$$
x_{CoG} > x_{rear\,axle} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Stable}
$$

The **rearward safety margin** is:

$$
\text{RSM} = \frac{x_{CoG} - x_{rear\,axle}}{L_{wheelbase}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Critical Rule of Thumb:** For a safe manual wheelchair, the combined CoG should be **at least 2 inches (≈ 50 mm) ahead of the rear axle** to prevent rearward tip during heavy push on the handrims. Anti-tip wheels are mandated for new users.

---

## 2.4 Rolling Resistance

The force resisting forward motion is:

$$
F_{roll} = \mu_{rr} \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta
$$

where:
- $\mu_{rr}$ → coefficient of rolling resistance (≈ 0.015–0.025 for pneumatic tires on smooth surface, ≈ 0.05–0.10 for solid tires on rough ground).
- $W$ → total weight on the wheel.
- $\theta$ → slope angle.

For an **all-terrain wheelchair in Kerala's rural roads**, $\mu_{rr}$ can exceed 0.10, multiplying the propulsive effort by 4–6×.

---

## 2.5 Grade Climbing Power

On an incline of angle $\theta$:

$$
F_{grade} = M_{total} \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta
$$

Required **mechanical power**:

$$
P_{mech} = F_{total} \cdot v = \left[ F_{roll} + F_{grade} + F_{aero} \right] \cdot v
$$

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Variable Definitions | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{total} = M_r + M_c$ | $M_r$ = rider mass, $M_c$ = chair mass | kg | Dynamic modeling |
| 2 | $\text{EMA} = r_w / r_p$ | $r_w$ = wheel radius, $r_p$ = pushrim radius | dimensionless | Pushrim design |
| 3 | $\text{PCI} = \frac{HR_{work} - HR_{rest}}{v}$ | HR = heart rate, v = speed | beats/m | Energy efficiency |
| 4 | $F_{roll} = \mu_{rr} W \cos\theta$ | $\mu_{rr}$ = rolling resistance coefficient | N | Energy demand |
| 5 | $F_{grade} = M g \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ = slope angle | N | Hill climbing |
| 6 | $\text{FSM}_f = \frac{x_{cast} - x_{CoG}}{L} \times 100\%$ | $L$ = wheelbase | % | Forward stability |
| 7 | $\text{RSM} = \frac{x_{CoG} - x_{rear}}{L} \times 100\%$ | — | % | Rearward stability |
| 8 | $\tau = F_{rim} \times r_p$ | Torque on pushrim | N·m | Motor / hand torque |
| 9 | $P = F \cdot v$ | Mechanical power | W | Propulsion design |
| 10 | $\sigma = \frac{F_{press}}{A_{contact}}$ | Sitting pressure = Force / contact area | kPa | Pressure ulcer risk |
| 11 | $I_{turn} = \sqrt{L^2 + W_b^2}$ | $L$ = wheelbase, $W_b$ = rear width | mm | Turning circle |
| 12 | $t_{turn} = \frac{2\pi \cdot R_{turn}}{v}$ | Time for full circle | s | Maneuverability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** When using the **vertical pipe |** for absolute value or conditionals inside the table, the markdown table syntax will BREAK. Always write `x_{CoG} < x_{caster}` rather than $\vert x_{CoG} \vert < \vert x_{caster} \vert$. Use `\vert` in LaTeX for absolute value if needed.

---

## 2.7 Why This Matters — Real-World Utility

- **Rehabilitation Engineer (clinical):** Matches a 55-year-old post-stroke female to a hemiplegic chair with one-arm drive.
- **Prosthetist/Orthotist:** Decides seat cushion type (foam, gel, air, hybrid) based on pressure-mapping $\sigma$.
- **Product Designer (industry):** Optimizes the rear axle position of a rigid sports chair to trade off maneuverability vs. stability for a wheelchair tennis player.
- **Policy Maker (Govt. of India — ADIP Scheme):** Approves appropriate wheelchair types under the **Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids and Appliances (ADIP)** scheme based on ISO 7176 conformance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Combined Center of Gravity of the Rider–Chair System

The location of the combined CoG along the wheelbase (sagittal, measured from rear axle) is found by **moment balance**:

$$
x_{CoG} = \frac{M_r \cdot x_r + M_c \cdot x_c}{M_r + M_c}
$$

### Step-by-Step Development

**Step 1 — Identify masses and their fore-aft positions from the rear axle:**

Let:
- $M_r$ = mass of the rider (kg), $x_r$ = horizontal distance of rider's CoG from the rear axle (m, positive = forward).
- $M_c$ = mass of the empty chair (kg), $x_c$ = horizontal distance of the chair's own CoG from the rear axle (m).

**Step 2 — Write the moment equilibrium about the rear axle contact point:**

$$
M_r \cdot g \cdot x_r + M_c \cdot g \cdot x_c = (M_r + M_c) \cdot g \cdot x_{CoG}
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $g$ on both sides:**

$$
M_r \cdot x_r + M_c \cdot x_c = (M_r + M_c) \cdot x_{CoG}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x_{CoG}$:**

$$
\boxed{\,x_{CoG} = \frac{M_r \cdot x_r + M_c \cdot x_c}{M_r + Mc}\,}
$$

This is a classic two-mass CoG problem. For three masses (e.g., rider + chair + backpack):

$$
x_{CoG} = \frac{\sum M_i \cdot x_i}{\sum M_i}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Stability Check (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question Style)

### Problem Statement

A manual wheelchair + rider system has the following data:

- Rider mass: $M_r = 70$ kg; rider's CoG lies **300 mm forward** of the rear axle.
- Chair mass: $M_c = 15$ kg; chair's CoG lies **150 mm behind** the rear axle (typical of a chair with a heavy seat back).
- Wheelbase (rear axle to front caster axle): $L = 480$ mm.
- Front-caster axle position: $x_{caster} = 480$ mm ahead of rear axle.

**Tasks:**
1. Find the combined CoG location.
2. Compute the Forward Stability Margin (FSM).
3. Compute the Rearward Stability Margin (RSM).
4. Decide if the chair is statically safe. If not, recommend a corrective design action.

### Step 1 — Apply the two-mass CoG formula

$$
x_{CoG} = \frac{M_r \cdot x_r + M_c \cdot x_c}{M_r + M_c}
$$

**Substitute numerical values.** Note that the chair's CoG is **behind** the rear axle, so $x_c$ is **negative**:

$$
x_{CoG} = \frac{70 \cdot (+0.300) + 15 \cdot (-0.150)}{70 + 15}
$$

**Compute the numerator:**

$$
70 \times 0.300 = 21.0 \;\; \text{kg·m}
$$

$$
15 \times (-0.150) = -2.25 \;\; \text{kg·m}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 21.0 + (-2.25) = 18.75 \;\; \text{kg·m}
$$

**Compute the denominator:**

$$
70 + 15 = 85 \;\; \text{kg}
$$

**Divide:**

$$
x_{CoG} = \frac{18.75}{85} = 0.2206 \;\; \text{m} \approx 220.6 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

**Interpretation:** The combined CoG lies **220.6 mm ahead of the rear axle** — this is a healthy forward placement for a manual chair.

---

### Step 2 — Forward Stability Margin (FSM)

The rider should not be able to tip forward (e.g., during sudden braking on a downhill). The FSM is the percentage of wheelbase behind the CoG to the front-caster axle:

$$
\text{FSM} = \frac{x_{caster} - x_{CoG}}{L} \times 100\%
$$

**Substitute:**

$$
\text{FSM} = \frac{480 - 220.6}{480} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{FSM} = \frac{259.4}{480} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{FSM} = 0.5404 \times 100\% = 54.0\%
$$

**Interpretation:** **54% forward margin** is excellent. The rider is very safe against forward tip (e.g., a sudden stop on a downhill slope).

---

### Step 3 — Rearward Stability Margin (RSM)

The rider must not tip backward when they push hard on the handrims, reach far back, or accelerate. The RSM is the percentage of wheelbase ahead of the rear axle to the CoG:

$$
\text{RSM} = \frac{x_{CoG} - x_{rear}}{L} \times 100\%
$$

**Note:** $x_{rear} = 0$ (rear axle is the reference).

$$
\text{RSM} = \frac{220.6 - 0}{480} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{RSM} = \frac{220.6}{480} \times 100\% = 45.96\%
$$

**Interpretation:** **46% rearward margin** is good. The KTU benchmark for safe manual chairs is **RSM > 40%**. This chair passes.

---

### Step 4 — Design Verdict and Recommendation

✅ **The chair is statically safe** for both forward and rearward tip.

**Additional engineering notes:**
- A **powered wheelchair with rear-mounted batteries** will have its CoG further back → RSM may drop below 40% → **anti-tip wheels are mandatory** per ISO 7176.
- If a user adds a **backpack (5 kg) at 250 mm behind the backrest**, recalculate $x_{CoG}$ — the rear moment will increase and RSM may drop.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Energy Cost per Meter of Propulsion

The rider expends metabolic energy $E_{met}$ (Joules) to overcome mechanical work $W_{mech}$ plus the inefficiency $\eta$ of the musculoskeletal system.

$$
E_{met} = \frac{W_{mech}}{\eta_{ms}}
$$

where $\eta_{ms} \approx 0.20$–$0.25$ for upper-limb exercise.

**Mechanical work per unit distance:**

$$
w_{mech} = F_{roll} + F_{grade} = \mu_{rr} W + M g \sin\theta
$$

**Substitute:**

$$
E_{met\,per\,m} = \frac{\mu_{rr} W + M g \sin\theta}{\eta_{ms}}
$$

For a level surface, $\theta = 0$:

$$
E_{met\,per\,m} \vert_{level} = \frac{\mu_{rr} \cdot M g}{\eta_{ms}}
$$

> **Typical KTU Number:** $M = 85$ kg, $\mu_{rr} = 0.02$, $\eta_{ms} = 0.22$ →
> $E_{met\,per\,m} = (0.02 \times 85 \times 9.81) / 0.22 \approx 75.8$ J/m.
> Equivalent oxygen cost ≈ **0.20 mL O₂/kg/m** — roughly **3× the cost of normal walking** (0.05 mL O₂/kg/m). This is the **clinical justification** for lightweight, low-rolling-resistance wheelchair design.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Turning Radius (Ackermann-Kinematic Model)

For a rear-wheel-drive chair with wheelbase $L$ and rear track $W_b$, the **minimum turning radius** (instantaneous center of rotation at the rear-wheel contact) is:

$$
R_{min} = \sqrt{L^2 + \left(\frac{W_b}{2}\right)^2}
$$

**Step-by-step justification:**

**Step 1:** During a tight turn, the two rear wheels pivot about the **rear axle midpoint** (with differential wheel speeds or skid-steering).

**Step 2:** The instantaneous center of rotation is at the intersection of the perpendiculars to the wheel velocity vectors.

**Step 3:** For a 90° turn, the geometric distance from the rear-axle center to the outermost front corner of the chair is the diagonal of a rectangle of sides $L$ and $W_b/2$.

**Step 4:** Apply the Pythagorean theorem → result above.

> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** A 480 mm wheelbase + 380 mm rear track → $R_{min} = \sqrt{0.480^2 + 0.190^2} = \sqrt{0.2304 + 0.0361} = \sqrt{0.2665} \approx 0.516$ m. **This is why a standard manual chair can turn in a small Indian bathroom (~1.2 m wide) — but only just!**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Scenarios

| Scenario | Formula Used | Typical KTU Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Find CoG of rider + chair | $\frac{\sum M_i x_i}{\sum M_i}$ | Forgetting to use **signed** values when CoG is behind reference. |
| Forward tip check | $x_{CoG} < x_{caster}$ | Confusing forward vs. rearward direction. |
| Rearward tip check | $x_{CoG} > 0$ (from rear axle) | Forgetting to add anti-tip wheels. |
| Rolling resistance | $\mu_{rr} W \cos\theta$ | Using $\sin\theta$ on flat ground by mistake. |
| Energy cost | $\mu W / \eta$ | Forgetting $\eta$ (efficiency). |
| Turning radius | $\sqrt{L^2 + (W_b/2)^2}$ | Using $W_b$ instead of $W_b/2$. |
| Pushrim force | $F = \tau / r_p$ | Mixing wheel radius with pushrim radius. |
| Pressure ulcer risk | $\sigma = F/A$ | Forgetting unit conversion (N/m² to kPa). |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of the Rider–Wheelchair System

This diagram shows the **coupled human–machine interaction loops** that the rehabilitation engineer must optimize simultaneously.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Rider Anthropometry] --> B[Postural Assessment]
    A --> C[Strength and Sensation Map]
    A --> D[Cognitive Profile]
    B --> E[Wheelchair Selection Matrix]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Frame Type Rigid or Folding]
    E --> G[Wheel Configuration RWD FWD MWD]
    E --> H[Seat and Backrest System]
    E --> I[Propulsion Interface Handrim or Joystick]
    F --> J[Static Stability Solver]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Stability Pass ISO 7176}
    K -- Yes --> L[Pressure Mapping and Comfort Check]
    K -- No --> M[Reconfigure Axle or Add Anti Tip Wheels]
    M --> J
    L --> N{Comfort Pass Skin Integrity}
    N -- Yes --> O[Final Prescription]
    N -- No --> P[Change Cushion or Postural Support]
    P --> L
    I --> Q[Energy Cost Measurement PCI]
    Q --> R{Energy Pass Less than 0.5 beats per m}
    R -- Yes --> O
    R -- No --> S[Reduce Rolling Resistance or Lighter Frame]
    S --> Q
    O --> T[Train User and Caregiver]
    T --> U[Follow Up at 6 Weeks and 6 Months]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Wheelchair Component Tree (ISO 7176-Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wheelchair System] --> B[Frame Module]
    A --> C[Wheel Module]
    A --> D[Seat Module]
    A --> E[Control Module]
    B --> B1[Rigid Frame]
    B --> B2[Folding Frame]
    B --> B3[Tilt in Space Frame]
    C --> C1[Rear Drive Wheels]
    C --> C2[Front Caster Wheels]
    C --> C3[Anti Tip Wheels]
    C1 --> C1a[Pushrim]
    C1 --> C1b[Tire Pneumatic or Solid]
    C1 --> C1c[Hub Motor for Powered]
    D --> D1[Seat Cushion]
    D --> D2[Backrest]
    D --> D3[Armrest]
    D --> D4[Footrest and Legrest]
    D1 --> D1a[Foam]
    D1 --> D1b[Gel]
    D1 --> D1c[Air Cell Roho]
    D1 --> D1d[Hybrid]
    E --> E1[Manual Propulsion]
    E --> E2[Joystick]
    E --> E3[Sip and Puff]
    E --> E4[Head Array]
    E --> E5[Chin Control]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Wheelchair Prescription Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Patient with Mobility Limitation]) --> Assess{Clinical Assessment}
    Assess --> UpperLimb{Upper Limb Function}
    UpperLimb -- Adequate Bilateral --> Manual[Manual Self Propelled Chair]
    UpperLimb -- One Side Only --> Hemi[Hemiplegic One Arm Drive Chair]
    UpperLimb -- Poor or Absent --> Powered[Powered Wheelchair]
    Manual --> Env{Environment}
    Hemi --> Env
    Powered --> Env
    Env -- Indoor Smooth --> Indoor[Indoor Lightweight Chair]
    Env -- Outdoor Rough Kerala --> Outdoor[All Terrain Reinforced Chair]
    Env -- Mixed Use --> Multi[Multi Purpose Adjustable Chair]
    Indoor --> Fit[Anthropometric Fit and Pressure Map]
    Outdoor --> Fit
    Multi --> Fit
    Fit --> Train[User and Caregiver Training]
    Train --> Follow[6 Week Follow Up and Re Assessment]
    Follow --> End([Independent Mobility Achieved])
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Tip-Over Mechanics (Sagittal Plane)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rear Axle Pivot Point] --> B[Rearward Tip Moment M_tip = W x_rear]
    C[Combined CoG] --> D[Anti Tip Moment M_anti = W x_CoG]
    B --> E{Compare Moments}
    D --> E
    E -- M_tip greater than M_anti --> F[REARWARD TIP OCCURS]
    E -- M_anti greater than M_tip --> G[Stable]
    F --> H[Install Anti Tip Wheels]
    H --> I[Restore M_anti to Safe Value]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]
**Define the term "Effective Mechanical Advantage" (EMA) of a manual wheelchair pushrim. State its typical value for a standard rear-wheel-drive chair.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Effective Mechanical Advantage (EMA)** of a manual wheelchair is defined as the ratio of the **rear-wheel radius** ($r_{wheel}$) to the **pushrim radius** ($r_{pushrim}$):

$$
\text{EMA} = \frac{r_{wheel}}{r_{pushrim}}
$$

It expresses how the propulsive force applied by the rider's hand on the pushrim is translated into a **proportionally larger force at the tire–ground contact** (and proportionally smaller speed at the wheel contact vs. the hand).

For a **standard 24-inch (610 mm) rear wheel with an 8-inch (203 mm) pushrim**, EMA ≈ **3.0**.

A higher EMA makes it easier to start from rest and climb obstacles, but reduces the maximum speed achievable for a given hand speed. A lower EMA increases top speed but requires more torque at the pushrim, increasing shoulder load.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for typical value + interpretation.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**List any four (4) anthropometric measurements that a rehabilitation engineer must record before prescribing a wheelchair to a rider.**

**Model Answer (4 × 0.75 = 3 marks; the 4th point for flow):**

1. **Sitting height** (crown of head to seat pan) — determines **backrest height**.
2. **Hip width** — determines **seat width** (must add ~20–30 mm clearance).
3. **Thigh length** (buttock to popliteal fossa) — determines **seat depth**.
4. **Lower-leg length** (popliteal fossa to heel) — determines **footrest height**.
5. *(Bonus)* Arm reach / shoulder height — determines **pushrim position** and **wheel axle placement**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students answer **either OR**, not both. The model below is split into two independent 14-mark alternatives.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat labelled sketch, explain the **anatomy of a manual rear-wheel-drive wheelchair**, identifying at least **8 major components** and stating the function of each.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 60 kg rider uses a manual wheelchair of mass 12 kg. The rider's CoG lies **280 mm ahead** of the rear axle, and the chair's CoG lies **120 mm behind** the rear axle. The wheelbase is **450 mm**. Calculate the combined CoG location and the **rearward stability margin (RSM)**. State, with justification, whether the chair is statically safe.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Wheelchair Anatomy [7 marks]

| S.No | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Frame** (rigid or folding) | Carries all loads; defines geometry. |
| 2 | **Rear wheels** (24" with handrim) | Self-propulsion, weight-bearing, brakes. |
| 3 | **Front casters** (5"–8") | Steering and stability on uneven ground. |
| 4 | **Pushrim / Handrim** | Mechanical interface for rider's hand to drive wheel. |
| 5 | **Seat pan / Sling** | Supports rider's pelvis; part of pressure interface. |
| 6 | **Backrest** | Supports lumbar/thoracic spine; part of postural system. |
| 7 | **Cushion** | Distributes sitting pressure, prevents pressure ulcers. |
| 8 | **Armrests** | Upper-limb support, transfers, desk access. |
| 9 | **Footrests / Legrests** | Support lower limbs, absorb road shock. |
| 10 | **Wheel locks / Brakes** | Hold chair stationary for transfers. |
| 11 | **Anti-tip wheels** | Prevent rearward tip during acceleration. |
| 12 | **Push handles** | For attendant propulsion. |

**Sketch Description (1 mark):** Side view showing a 24" rear wheel, 6" front caster, sling seat with cushion, backrest, footrest, push handle, and an anti-tip wheel at the rear. The rear axle is positioned to place the combined CoG **just ahead** of the rear contact point.

*(1 mark for sketch + 0.5 mark each for any 8 components + 1 mark for flow and clinical relevance.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Stability Calculation [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Write the two-mass CoG formula (1 mark):**

$$
x_{CoG} = \frac{M_r x_r + M_c x_c}{M_r + M_c}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (with care for sign) (2 marks):**

$$
x_{CoG} = \frac{60 \times (+0.280) + 12 \times (-0.120)}{60 + 12}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 16.8 - 1.44 = 15.36 \;\text{kg·m}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 72 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Final CoG location (1 mark):**

$$
x_{CoG} = \frac{15.36}{72} = 0.2133 \;\text{m} \approx 213.3 \;\text{mm ahead of rear axle}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute RSM (1 mark):**

$$
\text{RSM} = \frac{x_{CoG} - 0}{L} \times 100\% = \frac{213.3}{450} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{RSM} = 0.4740 \times 100\% = 47.4\%
$$

**Step 6 — Verdict (1 mark):**

Since **RSM > 40%**, the chair is **statically safe** against rearward tip. A 47.4% margin is well within the comfort zone for typical daily propulsion.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **biomechanics of manual wheelchair propulsion**, including the **propulsion cycle** (push phase and recovery phase), the role of the **pushrim**, and the concept of **Effective Mechanical Advantage (EMA)**. Why is upper-limb injury (especially **shoulder impingement and rotator cuff tendinopathy**) common in chronic manual wheelchair users?

**(b) [7 marks]** A 78 kg rider (CoG 350 mm ahead of rear axle) uses a powered wheelchair (chair mass 35 kg with batteries, CoG 80 mm behind rear axle) with a wheelbase of 520 mm. The front-caster axle is at 520 mm. Calculate the combined CoG, the **Forward Stability Margin (FSM)**, and the **RSM**. Comment on whether **anti-tip wheels** are necessary and why.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Propulsion Biomechanics [7 marks]

**1. The Propulsion Cycle (3 marks):**

The wheelchair propulsion cycle is divided into:

- **Push (Contact) Phase** (~30–40% of cycle): Hand is in contact with the pushrim and applies **tangential force**. This phase produces positive mechanical work.
- **Recovery Phase** (~60–70% of cycle): Hand lifts off, swings forward (often in a **looping or semicircular pattern** above the rim) to re-engage. No useful work is produced.

**2. Role of the Pushrim (2 marks):**

The pushrim is a **smaller-diameter ring** mounted on the outside of the rear wheel. It serves as a **mechanical force transformer** — the rider's hand force $F_h$ applied tangentially at radius $r_p$ generates a torque $\tau = F_h \times r_p$, which produces a ground-reaction force of $F_g = F_h \times (r_p / r_w)$ at the tire contact. This is the **Effective Mechanical Advantage (EMA)**, typically 2.5–3.5.

**3. Why Upper-Limb Injury Occurs (2 marks):**

- **High cadence** (~60–80 pushes/min) + **non-physiological loading angles** (shoulder abducted, internally rotated, elbow extended).
- The shoulder joint is not designed for **load-bearing ambulation**; it is designed for reach and lift.
- Cumulative **repetitive strain** → **rotator cuff tendinopathy**, **impingement syndrome**, and **carpal tunnel syndrome** in 50–70% of chronic manual wheelchair users after 10+ years.
- A poorly configured chair (rear axle too far forward, low EMA) **multiplies the required push frequency and force**, accelerating injury.

**Clinical implication (1 mark):** Rehabilitation engineers must optimize the rear-axle position and pushrim diameter to minimize injury risk — this is the **"rolling resistance vs. propulsion efficiency" trade-off**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Powered Wheelchair Stability [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Two-mass CoG formula (1 mark):**

$$
x_{CoG} = \frac{M_r x_r + M_c x_c}{M_r + M_c}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute (with sign) (2 marks):**

$$
x_{CoG} = \frac{78 \times (+0.350) + 35 \times (-0.080)}{78 + 35}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 27.3 - 2.8 = 24.5 \;\text{kg·m}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 113 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — CoG location (1 mark):**

$$
x_{CoG} = \frac{24.5}{113} \approx 0.2168 \;\text{m} \approx 216.8 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — FSM and RSM (1 mark):**

$$
\text{FSM} = \frac{520 - 216.8}{520} \times 100\% = \frac{303.2}{520} \times 100\% \approx 58.3\%
$$

$$
\text{RSM} = \frac{216.8}{520} \times 100\% \approx 41.7\%
$$

**Step 6 — Verdict and anti-tip recommendation (1 mark):**

- The FSM is excellent (58%).
- The RSM (41.7%) is **borderline** — at the lower end of the safe range.
- **However**, for a **powered wheelchair** with rear batteries, **sudden joystick acceleration can generate a large rearward inertial force**, which momentarily shifts the dynamic CoG rearward. This can cause rearward tip even with adequate *static* RSM.
- Therefore, **anti-tip wheels are MANDATORY** for this chair per **ISO 7176** safety guidelines and the **KTU rehabilitation-engineering prescription rubric**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in "Wheelchair and Rider" Problems:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors (–2 to –3 marks):** Forgetting that a CoG *behind* the rear axle must be entered as a **negative** value in the formula. This single mistake cascades into wrong FSM/RSM.
> 2. **Mixing up FSM and RSM (–1 to –2 marks):** Forward Stability Margin uses $x_{caster} - x_{CoG}$; Rearward Stability Margin uses $x_{CoG} - x_{rear}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the efficiency factor $\eta$ in energy cost (–1 mark):** Mechanical work is *not* the same as metabolic energy.
> 4. **Wrong wheelbase in turning-radius derivation (–1 mark):** Use $W_b/2$, not $W_b$, as the half-track.
> 5. **Omitting units or sign in the final answer (–0.5 mark):** Always state CoG in **mm ahead** or **mm behind** the rear axle, not a bare number.
> 6. **Skipping the design recommendation step (–1 mark):** KTU expects not just a number but an engineering interpretation: "anti-tip wheels are recommended because..."

---

## 5.4 🌟 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### A. Definitions
- ✅ **Wheelchair–Rider System:** Coupled biomechanical–mechanical unit.
- ✅ **EMA (Effective Mechanical Advantage):** $r_w / r_p$, typically 2.5–3.5.
- ✅ **FSM (Forward Stability Margin):** $(x_{caster} - x_{CoG}) / L \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **RSM (Rearward Stability Margin):** $(x_{CoG} - x_{rear}) / L \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **PCI (Physiological Cost Index):** $(HR_{work} - HR_{rest}) / v$.

### B. The 8 Must-Know Components
- ✅ Frame, Rear wheels + pushrim, Front casters, Seat + cushion, Backrest, Armrests, Footrests, Wheel locks, Anti-tip wheels, Push handles.

### C. The 5 Must-Know Formulas
1. ✅ $x_{CoG} = \frac{\sum M_i x_i}{\sum M_i}$
2. ✅ $\text{EMA} = r_w / r_p$
3. ✅ $F_{roll} = \mu_{rr} W \cos\theta$
4. ✅ $F_{grade} = M g \sin\theta$
5. ✅ $R_{turn} = \sqrt{L^2 + (W_b/2)^2}$

### D. The 4 Clinical Standards to Cite
- ✅ **ISO 7176** (wheelchair performance & dimensions)
- ✅ **ISO 7250** (anthropometric definitions)
- ✅ **RESNA WC-3** (US wheelchair standards)
- ✅ **ADIP Scheme, Govt. of India** (procurement policy)

### E. The 3 Golden Engineering Rules
- ✅ **Golden Rule 1 — RSM > 40%** for safe manual chairs; if less, install anti-tip wheels.
- ✅ **Golden Rule 2 — Seat width = hip width + 20–30 mm**; seat depth = thigh length − 20–30 mm (to avoid popliteal pressure).
- ✅ **Golden Rule 3 — Cushion pressure $\sigma < 5$ kPa** over ischial tuberosities to prevent pressure ulcers (NPUAP/EPUAP guidelines).

### F. The Rider Profile Checklist
- ✅ **Anthropometry** → chair size.
- ✅ **Posture** → seat & backrest contouring.
- ✅ **Strength & sensation** → propulsion interface.
- ✅ **Cognition** → control type (joystick vs. hand-rim).
- ✅ **Environment** → tire type, anti-tip, frame strength.

### G. Top 3 KTU 14-Mark Question Triggers
- ✅ "Calculate CoG, FSM, and RSM for the given rider–chair system. Comment on safety."
- ✅ "Derive the formula for minimum turning radius and explain its clinical significance for a hemiplegic rider."
- ✅ "Compare manual vs. powered wheelchair propulsion biomechanics and discuss upper-limb injury prevention."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheels and Casters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wheels and Casters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Rehabilitation Engineering** and assistive mobility devices (primarily manual and powered **wheelchairs**), wheels and casters are the primary **locomotor interfaces** that convert user-applied muscular, mechanical, or electromechanical energy into controlled translational and rotational motion of the device across a support surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEBRT752):**  
> *Wheels* are circular rotating components mounted on an axle that support the load of the assistive device and provide rolling contact with the ground. *Casters* are small-diameter, freely-pivoting wheel assemblies mounted on a vertical steering axis (fork + stem) that allow multi-directional motion and provide front-end stability.

A **wheelchair rolling system** typically consists of two distinct categories:

| Component | Position | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Drive Wheels** (Rear Wheels) | Large, rear-mounted, user-propelled (via push-rims) or motor-driven | Provide propulsion, primary load-bearing, and braking |
| **Caster Wheels** (Front Wheels) | Small, front-mounted, freely-pivoting | Provide front-end stability, directional tracking, and obstacle negotiation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Handrim (Pushrim) Diameter vs Wheel Diameter:**  
> Drive wheel diameter (typical): **510 mm, 559 mm, 610 mm (20", 22", 24")**  
> Caster wheel diameter (typical): **125 mm, 150 mm, 200 mm, 203 mm (5", 6", 8")**  
> Pushrim diameter: typically **~80% of drive wheel diameter** (e.g., 480 mm for 610 mm wheel)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are pushing a **heavy office chair** across a carpeted floor. The chair is heavy, drags, and is hard to steer. Now imagine the same chair on **smooth tiles with five small swivel wheels** at the base (like a hospital stretcher). Suddenly, the chair glides effortlessly and turns on a dime in any direction.

That difference is the **engineering philosophy** behind wheels and casters in a wheelchair:

- The **large rear drive wheels** are like the wheels of a **bicycle** — they carry the heavy load, roll efficiently over bumps, and provide the leverage for the user to push.
- The **small front casters** are like the wheels of a **shopping trolley** — they are smaller, swivel freely, and let the front of the chair "float" smoothly over uneven ground without snagging.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world intuition:** When you see a wheelchair climbing a small curb, the rear drive wheel does the heavy lifting, while the small front caster must "pop up" or be lifted by the footstep lever (or anti-tip wheel). The size and material of these wheels directly determine whether the user glides smoothly or struggles on every meter of terrain.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard)

- **Camber Angle (γ):** The angle of the wheel plane from vertical (positive = top tilted outward, negative = inward).
- **Toe-In / Toe-Out:** Angular difference between wheel planes relative to the forward direction.
- **Caster Angle (θ):** The angle of the steering/pivot axis from the vertical (in 3D, the king-pin angle).
- **Caster Trail (Mechanical Trail, t):** The horizontal distance at the contact patch between the projection of the steering axis and the contact point.
- **Pushrim / Handrim:** A smaller concentric ring attached to the drive wheel, gripped by the user to apply propulsive torque.
- **Axle Plate / Axle Receiver:** The mounting bracket on the wheelchair frame that accepts the quick-release axle.
- **Quick-Release Axle (QR):** A hollow axle with a spring-loaded cam lever allowing tool-free wheel removal.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Requirements of Wheels & Casters in Rehabilitation

The design of wheels and casters in a rehabilitation device must satisfy five simultaneous engineering criteria. Understanding the **"why"** behind each criterion is essential for KTU 14-mark design questions.

- **Load-Bearing Capacity (Static & Dynamic):** The wheel must support the user's weight plus dynamic impact loads (e.g., going off a curb). For a **75 kg occupant + 15 kg chair = 90 kg total**, distributed across 4 wheels, the per-wheel static load is roughly **220 N**, but dynamic loads during obstacle traverse can spike to **3×–5×** the static value.
- **Rolling Resistance Minimization:** Lower rolling resistance → less user effort → less upper-limb fatigue and reduced risk of repetitive-strain injury (RSI) in the shoulder.
- **Shock Absorption & Vibration Damping:** Pneumatic (air-filled) tires absorb road shocks; solid urethane tires are maintenance-free but transmit more vibration to the user.
- **Maneuverability & Tracking:** The combination of drive wheels and casters determines the **turning radius** of the chair. A shorter wheelbase reduces turning radius, critical for indoor navigation.
- **Lateral Stability:** Achieved primarily by **camber angle** (see §2.4) and the **track width** of the rear wheels.

### 2.2 Types of Tires Used in Rehabilitation Devices

| Tire Type | Construction | Rolling Resistance | Shock Absorption | Maintenance | Typical User |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pneumatic (Air-filled)** | Rubber tube + tire | **Very Low** (C_rr ≈ 0.006–0.010) | Excellent | High (puncture risk) | Active users, outdoor |
| **Pneumatic with Flat-Free Insert** | Tire + foam/composite insert | Low (C_rr ≈ 0.008–0.012) | Excellent | Very Low | Most users |
| **Solid Urethane (Polyurethane)** | Molded solid elastomer | Moderate (C_rr ≈ 0.012–0.018) | Poor–Moderate | None | Institutional, indoor |
| **Foam-Filled** | Foam core inside pneumatic tire | Low–Moderate | Good | Low | Mixed use |
| **Composite / Plastic** | Glass-filled nylon / polypropylene | High (C_rr ≈ 0.020–0.040) | Poor | None | Lightweight transport chairs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** For **outdoor / rural** Indian terrain (unpaved roads, broken footpaths), **pneumatic tires with flat-free inserts** are the gold standard because they balance low rolling resistance with puncture resistance.

### 2.3 Types of Casters

Casters are classified by their **mounting geometry** and **fork design**:

- **Stem-Mounted Casters:** A vertical stem fits into a socket in the frame. Standard on rigid-frame wheelchairs.
- **Plate-Mounted Casters:** Four-bolt mounting plate. Common on power wheelchairs and institutional chairs.
- **Forkless / Integrated Casters:** The caster bearing is integrated into the frame fork (e.g., ultralight rigid chairs).
- **Amputee Casters:** Offset rearward on the frame to compensate for the shifted center of gravity of a unilateral lower-limb amputee user.
- **Multi-Sport Casters:** Small, narrow casters used on court sports wheelchairs (basketball, rugby, tennis) for rapid pivoting.

### 2.4 Camber Angle (γ) — Engineering Rationale

Camber angle is the inclination of the wheel plane from the vertical. For a wheelchair, the typical camber is **0° to 7°**, with **3° to 4°** being most common on ultralight rigid chairs.

**Why camber the wheels?**

- **Lateral (Roll) Stability:** A cambered wheel resists sideways tipping because the **lateral component of the ground reaction force** at the contact patch is angled inward, restoring the wheel toward vertical — similar to a pendulum returning to equilibrium.
- **Shoulder Ergonomics:** Cambered wheels bring the pushrims closer to the user's hands at the top of the push stroke, aligning the push force vector with the user's natural shoulder movement.
- **Protection:** The wider top of the cambered wheel shields the user's hands from doorways and obstacles.
- **Turning Torque:** A cambered wheel, when turned, develops a small **lateral camber thrust** that helps the chair track through turns.

**Trade-off:** Increased camber → wider overall chair width → reduced access through narrow doorways.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains all high-yield equations for KTU ESE questions on Wheels and Casters.** Memorize units, not just formulas.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Definition of Symbols | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rolling Resistance Force | $F_r = C_{rr} \cdot N$ | $C_{rr}$ = coefficient of rolling resistance; $N$ = normal load on wheel | N |
| 2 | Rolling Resistance Coefficient (Pneumatic) | $C_{rr} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{z}{D}}$ | $z$ = tire penetration depth; $D$ = wheel diameter | dimensionless |
| 3 | Propulsion Force on Level Ground | $F_{prop} = C_{rr} \cdot W$ | $W$ = total weight on driven wheels | N |
| 4 | Propulsion Force on Slope (angle $\alpha$) | $F_{prop} = W \sin\alpha + C_{rr} W \cos\alpha$ | Combined gravity + rolling | N |
| 5 | Mechanical Advantage of Pushrim | $MA = \dfrac{R_{wheel}}{R_{pushrim}}$ | Ratio of wheel radius to handrim radius | dimensionless ( < 1 ) |
| 6 | Caster Trail | $t = R_c \sin\theta_c$ | $R_c$ = caster pivot height; $\theta_c$ = caster angle | mm |
| 7 | Camber-Induced Lateral Stiffness | $K_{camber} = N \cdot \sin\gamma$ | Restoring lateral force per unit lateral displacement | N/rad (approx.) |
| 8 | Static Tipping Angle (Forward) | $\tan\theta_{tip,f} = \dfrac{x_{CG}}{h_{CG}}$ | $x_{CG}$ = horizontal distance of CoG from front axle; $h_{CG}$ = CoG height | degrees |
| 9 | Static Tipping Angle (Lateral) | $\tan\theta_{tip,l} = \dfrac{b/2}{h_{CG}}$ | $b$ = track width (lateral wheel spacing) | degrees |
| 10 | Turning Radius (Rear-Wheel Drive, Ackermann) | $R_{turn} = \dfrac{L}{\tan\delta}$ | $L$ = wheelbase; $\delta$ = steer angle of front caster | mm |
| 11 | Camber Angle (Geometric) | $\gamma = \arctan\left(\dfrac{\Delta h}{b}\right)$ | $\Delta h$ = vertical offset of wheel top from vertical; $b$ = half track | degrees |
| 12 | Wheel Moment of Inertia (Solid Disc) | $I = \dfrac{1}{2} m_w R^2$ | $m_w$ = wheel mass; $R$ = wheel radius | kg·m² |
| 13 | Energy per Push Stroke | $E = F_{push} \cdot d_{push}$ | $d_{push}$ = distance traveled per stroke | J |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Pipes in Tables:** When writing these equations in your answer script, **never** write $\vert x \vert$ with vertical pipes inside a markdown table. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX to avoid rendering errors.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles governing wheelchair wheels and casters are directly applicable to:

- **Hospital bed & stretcher casters** (very high-load, low-speed, smooth-floor).
- **Surgical robot mobile platforms** (precision casters with zero-backlash bearings).
- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** (cambered drive wheels for lateral stability).
- **Mars-rover rocker-bogie suspension** (caster principle on a planetary scale).
- **Tricycle and hand-cycle assistive cycles** (large-diameter drive wheels, small leading casters).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Rolling Resistance from Tire Penetration Model

We derive the **coefficient of rolling resistance** from the classical **penetration-depth model** (also called the "sinkage model"). This is the most commonly asked derivation in KTU Part B questions.

**Assumptions:**
1. The wheel is rigid.
2. The tire is soft (pneumatic), and sinks a depth $z$ into the contact surface.
3. The wheel has diameter $D$ and the contact patch is approximately a chord of length $2\sqrt{Dz}$ (for small $z$).

**Step 1 — Geometric setup:**
The wheel of radius $R = D/2$ sinks into the ground by a depth $z$. The contact patch is an arc. For a small sinkage, the **horizontal length of contact** is:

$$
b \approx 2\sqrt{R \cdot z} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{Dz}{2}} = \sqrt{2 D z}
$$

**Step 2 — Normal load supported by the deformed tire:**
By Hertzian / empirical contact theory, the normal load $N$ is proportional to the projected contact area raised to a power. For a pneumatic tire on a rigid surface, the empirical relation is:

$$
N = k \cdot A = k \cdot (b \cdot L_{patch})
$$

where $k$ is the tire stiffness per unit area and $L_{patch}$ is the contact-patch length along the axle. For a narrow wheel, $L_{patch} \approx w$ (wheel width). Simplifying, $N \propto b \propto \sqrt{z}$, so:

$$
z \propto N^2
$$

**Step 3 — Effective radius of the rolling wheel:**
Because the wheel sinks, the effective rolling radius is reduced. The new contact point is offset forward by an amount approximately equal to $z$. This is the **mechanical trail due to sinkage**, $a \approx z$.

**Step 4 — Force & moment balance:**
To roll the wheel at constant speed, the applied horizontal force $F_r$ must overcome the moment created by the offset normal force. Taking moments about the wheel center:

$$
F_r \cdot R = N \cdot z
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $F_r$:**

$$
F_r = N \cdot \dfrac{z}{R}
$$

**Step 6 — Define the rolling resistance coefficient:**

$$
C_{rr} \equiv \dfrac{F_r}{N} = \dfrac{z}{R} = \dfrac{2z}{D}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\; C_{rr} = \dfrac{2z}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4z}{D}} \quad \text{(for Hertzian contact)} \;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Doubling the wheel diameter $D$ reduces $C_{rr}$ by $\sqrt{2}$ (≈ 30% reduction in rolling resistance). This is **why larger rear wheels are universally used on manual wheelchairs** — the user expends less energy per kilometer.

### 3.2 Derivation: Propulsion Force on a Slope with Cambered Wheels

**Problem statement:** A wheelchair user of total mass $M$ (user + chair) is being pushed up a slope of angle $\alpha$. The drive wheels have radius $R$ and rolling-resistance coefficient $C_{rr}$. The camber angle is $\gamma$. Derive the total propulsive force required at the contact patch, including the lateral camber-thrust component.

**Step 1 — Decompose the weight into slope-parallel and slope-normal components:**

$$
W_{\parallel} = Mg \sin\alpha
$$

$$
W_{\perp} = Mg \cos\alpha
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the rolling-resistance force on the slope:**

$$
F_r = C_{rr} \cdot W_{\perp} = C_{rr} \cdot Mg \cos\alpha
$$

**Step 3 — Add gravity component to get the total propulsive force along the slope:**

$$
F_{prop} = W_{\parallel} + F_r = Mg \sin\alpha + C_{rr} \cdot Mg \cos\alpha
$$

$$
\boxed{\; F_{prop} = Mg \left( \sin\alpha + C_{rr} \cos\alpha \right) \;}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for camber-induced lateral thrust (optional, for advanced problems):**
