A cambered wheel rolling without slip generates a **lateral camber force** that is non-zero only when there is slip. For a freely rolling cambered wheel on a level surface, the lateral force is zero, but during turning it produces a **camber thrust**:

$$
F_{camber} = K_\gamma \cdot \gamma
$$

where $K_\gamma$ is the **camber thrust coefficient** (≈ 100–500 N/rad for a typical wheelchair tire).

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check:**
For $M = 90$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $C_{rr} = 0.010$, $\alpha = 5°$:

$$
F_{prop} = 90 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \left( \sin 5° + 0.010 \cos 5° \right)
$$

$$
= 882.9 \cdot \left( 0.0872 + 0.00996 \right) = 882.9 \cdot 0.0972
$$

$$
\boxed{\; F_{prop} \approx 85.8 \text{ N} \;}
$$

This is a comfortable single-hand push force, consistent with ISO 7176 energy-expenditure norms for manual wheelchairs.

### 3.3 Derivation: Static Lateral Tipping Angle

A wheelchair will tip laterally (sideways) when the line of gravity from the combined user-chair center of gravity (CG) passes outside the contact patch of the outer wheel. We derive the critical tipping angle.

**Step 1 — Define geometry:**
- $h_{CG}$ = height of combined user + chair CG above ground.
- $b/2$ = half of the lateral track width (distance from chair centerline to outer wheel contact).

**Step 2 — On a laterally tilted surface at angle $\theta$,** the CG shifts in the body frame. Tipping occurs when:

$$
\tan\theta_{tip} = \dfrac{b/2}{h_{CG}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\theta_{tip}$:**

$$
\boxed{\; \theta_{tip} = \arctan\left( \dfrac{b/2}{h_{CG}} \right) \;}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:**
For a typical adult male user: $h_{CG} \approx 0.95$ m (sitting + chair), $b/2 = 0.30$ m (track width = 0.60 m).

$$
\theta_{tip} = \arctan\left( \dfrac{0.30}{0.95} \right) = \arctan(0.3158) = 17.5°
$$

This is consistent with KTU/ANSI-RESNA standards requiring wheelchairs to remain stable up to at least **15°** lateral tilt without anti-tip wheels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design implication:** To increase $\theta_{tip}$ (i.e., make the chair more laterally stable), either **widen the track** ($b \uparrow$) or **lower the CG** ($h_{CG} \downarrow$). Sports wheelchairs deliberately lower the seat and widen the rear track for exactly this reason.

### 3.4 Derivation: Mechanical Advantage of the Pushrim

The pushrim is concentric with the drive wheel. The user applies force $F_h$ tangentially on the pushrim of radius $R_h$. This generates a torque $\tau = F_h R_h$ on the wheel.

**Step 1 — Torque balance (steady rolling, neglecting inertia):**

$$
F_h \cdot R_h = F_{ground} \cdot R
$$

where $F_{ground}$ is the tangential force at the contact patch (this is the propulsive force $F_{prop}$).

**Step 2 — Solve for the pushrim mechanical advantage:**

$$
MA = \dfrac{F_{ground}}{F_h} = \dfrac{R_h}{R}
$$

**Step 3 — Typical value:**
For $R = 305$ mm (24-inch wheel) and $R_h = 245$ mm (pushrim):

$$
MA = \dfrac{245}{305} = 0.80
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** $MA < 1$ means the user pushes with **more force** than is delivered to the ground, but travels the pushrim through a **smaller distance** than the chair. The trade-off is **force amplification** in exchange for **stroke length**. This is the inverse of a bicycle's gear system — manual wheelchairs are intentionally "geared down" for higher torque at the cost of stroke range.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Wheelchair Wheel & Caster System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **subsystems and interactions** of a wheelchair's wheel-caster assembly, suitable for KTU 14-mark design questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Input: Push Force on Handrim] --> B[Handrim Pushrim Subsystem]
    B --> C[Drive Wheel Rim and Spokes]
    C --> D[Tire Contact Patch with Ground]
    E[User Body Mass and Frame] --> F[Center of Gravity CG]
    F --> G[Load Distribution 4 Wheels]
    G --> D
    D --> H[Rolling Resistance F_r]
    D --> I[Lateral Camber Thrust F_camber]
    H --> J[Net Propulsive Force F_net]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Translational Motion of Chair]
    K --> L[Front Caster Steering Pivot]
    L --> M[Caster Trail and Self-Centering]
    M --> K
    D --> N[Braking Force F_brake applied at Pushrim or Tire]
    N --> K
    F --> O[Tipping Stability Check]
    O --> P{Within Safe Tipping Angle}
    P -- Yes --> K
    P -- No --> Q[Anti-Tip Wheel Engaged]
    Q --> K
```

### 4.2 Comparison Block — Drive Wheel vs Caster Wheel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DW[Drive Wheel Subsystem]
        DW1[Wheel Rim] --> DW2[Spokes or Composite Disc]
        DW2 --> DW3[Tire Pneumatic or Solid]
        DW3 --> DW4[Pushrim Handrim]
        DW3 --> DW5[Quick Release Axle]
        DW5 --> DW6[Axle Plate on Frame]
    end
    subgraph CW[Caster Wheel Subsystem]
        CW1[Small Wheel Tire] --> CW2[Caster Hub and Bearings]
        CW2 --> CW3[Caster Fork Yoke]
        CW3 --> CW4[Steering Stem Vertical Axis]
        CW4 --> CW5[Caster Mount Socket on Frame]
        CW1 --> CW6[Caster Trail t]
    end
    DW6 --> FR[Wheelchair Frame]
    CW5 --> FR
```

### 4.3 Caster Geometry Schematic (Camber + Caster Angle)

The following ASCII schematic shows the **camber angle γ** at the rear drive wheel and the **caster angle θ** at the front caster pivot, both relative to the true vertical. This is a typical sketch expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

```
                              FRONT VIEW (looking from front of chair)
                              
                                          
                    LEFT REAR WHEEL         RIGHT REAR WHEEL
                          |                       |
                          |\__γ___                 |___γ__/|
                          |      \                /      |
                          |       \              /       |
                          |        \____________/        |
                          |             |               |
                          |        CONTACT PATCH         |
                          |                              |
                              \                          /
                               \                        /
                                \  CAMBER ANGLE  γ     /
                                 \  (top tilted out) /
                                  
                                          
                                          
                    LEFT FRONT CASTER        RIGHT FRONT CASTER
                          |                       |
                          |  __θ___         ___θ__  |
                          | /      \       /      \ |
                          |/        \_____/        \|
                          |         |     |         |
                          |         | STEERING AXIS |
                          |         | (tilted from  |
                          |         |   vertical)   |
                          |         |               |
                          |     CASTER TRAIL t     |
                          |     (horizontal offset)|
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Wheel Selection for a Manual Wheelchair

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Wheel Selection] --> S1{Primary Use Environment}
    S1 -- Indoor Smooth --> S2[Solid Urethane Tires]
    S1 -- Mixed Indoor Outdoor --> S3[Pneumatic with Flat-Free Insert]
    S1 -- Outdoor Off-Road --> S4[Large Diameter Pneumatic Tires]
    S2 --> S5{User Weight}
    S3 --> S5
    S4 --> S5
    S5 -- < 75 kg --> S6[Standard Spoke Wheel]
    S5 -- 75 to 110 kg --> S7[Reinforced Spoke or Composite]
    S5 -- > 110 kg --> S8[Heavy-Duty Mag Wheel]
    S6 --> S9{Camber Required}
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S9
    S9 -- Yes --> S10[Adjustable Axle Plate with Camber]
    S9 -- No --> S11[Fixed Axle Plate]
    S10 --> S12[Final Drive Wheel Selection]
    S11 --> S12
    S12 --> S13{Front Caster Diameter}
    S13 -- Tight Indoor --> S14[Small Caster 125 to 150 mm]
    S13 -- Outdoor Rough --> S15[Large Caster 200 to 203 mm]
    S14 --> S16[Final Caster Selection]
    S15 --> S16
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBRT752]**
> **Define the terms "camber angle" and "caster trail" as applied to a wheelchair. State the typical range of camber angle used in manual wheelchairs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Camber angle (γ):** The angle made by the plane of the wheel with respect to the true vertical, measured at the wheel hub. A positive camber means the top of the wheel is tilted **outward** (away from the chair centerline). (1½ marks)
- **Caster trail (t):** The horizontal distance at ground level between the projection of the steering axis and the actual tire contact patch. It is calculated as $t = R_c \sin\theta_c$, where $R_c$ is the height of the caster pivot above the ground and $\theta_c$ is the caster angle. (1 mark)
- **Typical camber range:** **0° to 7°**, with **3° to 4°** being most common on ultralight rigid-frame manual wheelchairs. (½ mark)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBRT752]**
> **List any three types of tires used on wheelchair drive wheels and compare their rolling resistance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Tire Type | Typical C_rr | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| **Pneumatic (Air-filled)** | 0.006 – 0.010 | Lowest rolling resistance; best for outdoor; puncture risk |
| **Solid Urethane** | 0.012 – 0.018 | Moderate; maintenance-free; transmits vibration |
| **Foam-Filled** | 0.010 – 0.014 | Good compromise; no puncture; slightly higher resistance than pneumatic |

(3 marks — 1 mark each for type + characteristic)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Note:** In KTU ESE, Part B Module questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice between two questions on the same module. The two alternatives below are constructed to be of **equal difficulty** but cover **different analytical sub-topics**, exactly as per the KTU 2024 pattern.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEBRT752, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **camber angle** and **caster trail** in a manual wheelchair. Discuss how each contributes to the **stability** and **maneuverability** of the chair. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manual wheelchair has the following specifications:
- Total mass (user + chair): $M = 85$ kg
- Drive wheel diameter: $D = 610$ mm
- Pushrim (handrim) diameter: $d = 480$ mm
- Tire penetration depth: $z = 1.5$ mm
- Slope angle: $\alpha = 8°$

Calculate:  
(i) The coefficient of rolling resistance $C_{rr}$.  
(ii) The mechanical advantage of the pushrim.  
(iii) The total propulsive force required at the contact patch to move up the slope.  
Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Diagram (3 marks):** Neat front-view sketch showing the camber angle γ at the rear wheels (top tilted outward) and the caster angle θ at the front caster pivot (steering axis tilted from vertical). The caster trail $t$ is the horizontal offset at the contact patch.
- **Camber angle (2 marks):**  
  - Provides **lateral stability** via the camber-thrust effect — when the chair tilts laterally, the cambered wheel develops a restoring lateral force, similar to a pendulum.  
  - Brings pushrims closer to the user's hands at the top of the push stroke, improving shoulder ergonomics.  
  - Protects hands from doorframes and obstacles.
- **Caster trail (2 marks):**  
  - Makes the caster wheel **self-centering** (like a shopping trolley) — if disturbed, the trail geometry produces a corrective moment returning the wheel to the forward direction.  
  - Provides **directional tracking stability** during straight-line travel.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Coefficient of rolling resistance:**

$$
C_{rr} = \dfrac{2z}{D} = \dfrac{2 \times 1.5 \text{ mm}}{610 \text{ mm}} = \dfrac{3.0}{610} = 0.00492
$$

**$C_{rr} \approx 0.0049$ (dimensionless)** — (2 marks)

**(ii) Mechanical advantage of the pushrim:**

$$
MA = \dfrac{R_{pushrim}}{R_{wheel}} = \dfrac{240 \text{ mm}}{305 \text{ mm}} = 0.787
$$

**$MA \approx 0.787$** — (1 mark)

**(iii) Propulsive force on the slope:**

Weight of the system: $W = Mg = 85 \times 9.81 = 833.85$ N

$$
F_{prop} = W \sin\alpha + C_{rr} \cdot W \cos\alpha
$$

$$
= 833.85 \times \sin 8° + 0.00492 \times 833.85 \times \cos 8°
$$

$$
= 833.85 \times 0.1392 + 0.00492 \times 833.85 \times 0.9903
$$

$$
= 116.07 + 4.06 = 120.13 \text{ N}
$$

**$F_{prop} \approx 120.1$ N** — (4 marks)

> This is a realistic single-hand push force, well within the comfortable exertion range (typically up to ~150 N per push) for a manual wheelchair user.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of casters** used in rehabilitation engineering, with examples. How does **caster wheel diameter** affect the rolling resistance and obstacle-climbing ability of a wheelchair? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wheelchair user is sitting in a rigid-frame manual wheelchair. The combined center of gravity of the user and chair is located at a height of $h_{CG} = 0.92$ m above the ground, and the lateral track width of the rear drive wheels is $b = 0.58$ m. Calculate:  
(i) The **static lateral tipping angle** of the chair.  
(ii) Comment on whether the chair meets the **ANSI-RESNA / ISO 7176 stability requirement** of remaining stable up to a lateral tilt of **15°** without anti-tip wheels.  
(iii) If anti-tip wheels are engaged, what additional **lateral restoring moment** do they provide at the tipping point, given they are mounted 0.20 m behind the rear axle at a height of 0.05 m above ground? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Classification of casters (4 marks):**
  - **Stem-mounted casters:** Vertical stem fits into a frame socket; standard on rigid-frame manual wheelchairs.
  - **Plate-mounted casters:** Four-bolt plate mounting; common on power wheelchairs.
  - **Amputee casters:** Offset rearward on the frame to compensate for the shifted CG of a unilateral lower-limb amputee.
  - **Sports / multi-directional casters:** Small, narrow casters used on court sports chairs (basketball, rugby, tennis) for rapid pivoting.
  - **Forkless / integrated casters:** Bearings integrated into the frame fork; used on ultralight chairs to reduce unsprung mass.
- **Effect of caster diameter (3 marks):**
  - **Larger caster** → lower rolling resistance ($C_{rr} \propto 1/D$) and better obstacle climbing (can roll over bumps up to ~⅓ of caster diameter).
  - **Smaller caster** → lower frontal area, lighter weight, reduced "flutter" at high speed, but worse obstacle clearance and higher rolling resistance.
  - **Trade-off:** Indoor maneuverability favors small casters (125–150 mm); outdoor terrain favors large casters (200–203 mm).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Static lateral tipping angle:**

$$
\theta_{tip} = \arctan\left( \dfrac{b/2}{h_{CG}} \right) = \arctan\left( \dfrac{0.29}{0.92} \right) = \arctan(0.3152)
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \theta_{tip} \approx 17.5° \;}
$$

— (2 marks)

**(ii) Compliance check:**

The computed $\theta_{tip} = 17.5°$ is **greater than** the 15° ANSI-RESNA / ISO 7176 minimum requirement. Therefore, **the chair passes the stability test** and can be used without anti-tip wheels for normal indoor and moderate outdoor use. — (2 marks)

**(iii) Additional restoring moment from anti-tip wheels (when engaged):**

If the chair is on the verge of lateral tipping, the anti-tip wheel (mounted 0.20 m behind the rear axle) is just touching the ground. The lateral restoring moment arm about the tipping edge (outer rear wheel contact) is the perpendicular distance from the anti-tip contact to the tipping axis.

For a small tilt, the **vertical** arm component is approximately $h_{anti\text{-}tip} = 0.05$ m (height above ground) and the **horizontal** arm component is $x_{anti\text{-}tip} = 0.20$ m (behind the axle).

The total moment arm (approximation):

$$
L_{anti} = \sqrt{h^2 + x^2} = \sqrt{0.05^2 + 0.20^2} = \sqrt{0.0025 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.0425} = 0.206 \text{ m}
$$

If we assume a normal force $N_{anti}$ of 30 N is borne by the anti-tip wheel at the tipping point, the restoring moment is:

$$
M_{anti} = N_{anti} \times L_{anti} = 30 \times 0.206 = 6.18 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; M_{anti} \approx 6.2 \text{ N·m} \;}
$$

— (3 marks)

> This restoring moment delays the onset of full lateral tip-over and provides a safety margin for the user.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Wheel diameters are given in **mm**, but formulas expect **m**. A 610 mm wheel = 0.610 m. Many students compute $C_{rr}$ using 610 directly and get 0.00492 — but then forget that the rolling-resistance equation $F_r = C_{rr} N$ works only with consistent units in $N$.
> 2. **Confusing camber with caster:** Camber is the **wheel-plane** tilt (top-out, top-in). Caster is the **steering-axis** tilt. They are completely different — mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Sign of camber:** A wheel whose **top tilts outward** is said to have **positive camber**. Many students describe it as "negative" because they imagine the bottom going outward.
> 4. **Mechanical advantage direction:** Manual wheelchairs have $MA < 1$ because the pushrim is **smaller** than the wheel. This means **force amplification at the cost of stroke length** — not the other way around.
> 5. **Skipping the free-body diagram:** For 7-mark design questions, always draw a labeled diagram. Examiners allocate **2–3 marks** for the diagram itself, even if the calculation is perfect.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **wheelchair rolling system** consists of two **large drive wheels** (rear) and two **small caster wheels** (front).
- [x] The **drive wheel** provides propulsion, load-bearing, and braking; it is operated via a **pushrim/handrim** concentric with the wheel.
- [x] The **caster wheel** is mounted on a freely-pivoting vertical stem; it provides **front stability** and **multi-directional tracking**.
- [x] **Pneumatic tires** have the **lowest rolling resistance** ($C_{rr} \approx 0.006$–$0.010$) but are puncture-prone; **solid urethane** tires are maintenance-free but transmit more vibration.
- [x] **Camber angle γ** is the tilt of the wheel plane from vertical; typical range is **0°–7°**, with **3°–4°** standard on ultralight rigid chairs. Benefits: lateral stability, pushrim ergonomics, hand protection.
- [x] **Caster trail t** = $R_c \sin\theta_c$ makes the caster **self-centering**, giving the chair directional stability like a shopping trolley.
- [x] The **rolling resistance force** is $F_r = C_{rr} \cdot N$, and the **penetration model** gives $C_{rr} = 2z/D$. Doubling the wheel diameter reduces $C_{rr}$ by ~30%.
- [x] **Propulsive force on a slope:** $F_{prop} = W(\sin\alpha + C_{rr}\cos\alpha)$.
- [x] **Mechanical advantage of the pushrim** is $MA = R_{pushrim} / R_{wheel} < 1$ (force amplification at cost of stroke length).
- [x] **Static lateral tipping angle** is $\theta_{tip} = \arctan\bigl((b/2) / h_{CG}\bigr)$; must be ≥ 15° per ISO 7176.
- [x] **Quick-release axles** allow tool-free wheel removal — important for transport and storage.
- [x] **Anti-tip wheels** add a restoring moment to prevent rear or lateral tip-over.
- [x] **ANSI-RESNA / ISO 7176** standards govern wheelchair stability, energy expenditure, and dimensional limits.
- [x] For Indian terrain (mixed paved / unpaved), the recommended tire is **pneumatic with a flat-free insert**.
- [x] **Sports wheelchairs** use **low CG + wide rear track + high camber** for maximum lateral stability during rapid maneuvers.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 3 — Wheels and Casters Notes.**  
> Cross-reference with **Module 3 related topics**: Wheelchair Frame Design, Propulsion Biomechanics, and Power Wheelchair Drive Systems for complete KTU ESE coverage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Components in Rehabilitation Engineering** refer to the fundamental functional building blocks—mechanical, electronic, sensory, actuation, and material elements—engineered and integrated into assistive devices such as prosthetics, orthoses, wheelchairs, hearing aids, and therapeutic equipment to restore, substitute, or augment physical, sensory, or cognitive function in individuals with disabilities.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBRT752)**, Module 3 systematically addresses these components as the *enabling sub-systems* that bridge the gap between a user's residual biological capability and the device's intended functional output.

### 1.2 Classification of Rehabilitation Components

The vast array of components can be organized into **seven functional families**:

| Family | Primary Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensors / Transducers** | Acquire bioelectric and physical signals | EMG electrodes, force-sensing resistors |
| **Actuators** | Convert energy into motion | DC motors, pneumatic cylinders |
| **Power Sources** | Supply electrical or pneumatic energy | Li-ion batteries, compressed air tanks |
| **Control Electronics** | Process signals and command actuators | Microcontrollers, DSP chips |
| **Structural Materials** | Provide mechanical frame, comfort, biocompatibility | Carbon fiber, titanium, silicone |
| **Interfaces / Couplers** | Connect device to the human body | Sockets, harnesses, electrode pads |
| **Feedback / Display Units** | Inform user or clinician about device status | Buzzers, LEDs, tactile displays |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module emphasizes the *inter-disciplinary nature* of components. A single prosthetic hand, for example, is a system where **sensors** (myoelectric), **actuators** (micro-motors), **controllers** (microcontroller), **materials** (carbon fiber socket), and **interfaces** (silicone liner) must work in concert.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — "The Body as a Car, the Device as a Mechanic":**
> Imagine a person with a spinal cord injury as a high-performance car whose accelerator pedal (muscle) and steering wheel (nerve signal) are damaged. A rehabilitation device acts like an *aftermarket control kit*:
> - **Sensors** are the *new pedal* (they capture the driver's remaining intent — a twitch, a breath, a glance).
> - **Actuators** are the *engine and brakes* (they produce the actual movement).
> - **Controllers** are the *ECU* (the on-board computer that translates pedal press to wheel rotation).
> - **Power sources** are the *fuel tank* (without it, nothing runs).
> - **Structural materials** are the *chassis* (holding everything together, safely, comfortably).
>
> A rehabilitation engineer, therefore, is essentially an *automotive systems integrator* — but the "car" is a human body, and the "stakes" are mobility, dignity, and independence.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **EMG signal amplitude:** **20 µV to 5 mV** (surface), **up to 1 mV** (intramuscular)
> - **Frequency range of surface EMG:** **20 Hz to 500 Hz** (dominant power: **50–150 Hz**)
> - **Human skin impedance (dry):** **~100 kΩ** at 1 Hz, drops to **~1 kΩ** with gel
> - **Safe battery voltage for body-worn devices:** typically **≤ 24 V DC**
> - **Standard wheelchair motor rating:** **24 V, 150–450 W**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sensor Subsystem — The "Nervous System" of the Device

A sensor in rehabilitation engineering is a *transducer* that converts a biological or mechanical stimulus into a measurable electrical signal.

**Major Sensor Types:**

- **Electromyography (EMG) Sensors** — detect muscle electrical activity. Used in myoelectric prosthetic arms.
  - Surface EMG electrodes use Ag/AgCl (silver/silver chloride) for stable biopotential pickup.
  - Equation of surface EMG amplitude: rectified, then integrated, or the **Root Mean Square (RMS)** is computed.

- **Electroencephalography (EEG) Sensors** — detect cortical activity for brain-computer interfaces (BCI).

- **Force-Sensing Resistors (FSR)** — variable-resistance polymer that changes resistance with applied force; used in foot-pressure mapping, orthotic load monitoring.

- **Goniometers & Inertial Measurement Units (IMU)** — measure joint angles (MEMS gyroscope + accelerometer). Used in smart orthoses, gait analysis.

- **Tilt sensors & accelerometers** — detect posture changes in wheelchairs, fall-detection pendants.

- **Temperature & humidity sensors** — monitor socket comfort in prosthetics (sweat detection).

### 2.2 Actuator Subsystem — The "Muscles" of the Device

Actuators deliver the mechanical work that moves the limb, joint, or assistive device.

**Categories:**

- **Electric Motors**
  - **DC motors:** Simple, cheap, used in powered wheelchairs.
  - **Brushless DC (BLDC) motors:** High efficiency, long life, used in robotic prosthetics.
  - **Servo motors:** Closed-loop position control, used in exoskeletons.
  - **Stepper motors:** Precise open-loop positioning, used in therapeutic robots.

- **Pneumatic Actuators**
  - McKibben air muscles — contract when inflated. Compliance mimics human muscle.
  - Used in soft exoskeletons and rehabilitation robots.

- **Hydraulic Actuators**
  - High force density; used in heavy exoskeletons (e.g., BLEEX).

- **Shape Memory Alloys (SMA)**
  - Nitinol wires contract when heated. Silent, light, but slow.
  - Used in lightweight orthotic fingers.

- **Piezoelectric Actuators**
  - Sub-micron precision, used in micro-displacement applications and some tactile displays.

- **Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES)**
  - Uses **electrical current** to *directly* contract the user's own paralyzed muscle — the patient is the actuator!

### 2.3 Control Electronics — The "Brain" of the Device

The control subsystem executes a *sense → decide → act* loop.

- **Microcontrollers (MCU):** ATmega328, STM32, ESP32 — low-power, embedded.
- **Digital Signal Processors (DSP):** for real-time EMG/EEG filtering and feature extraction.
- **FPGAs:** parallel high-speed processing for advanced prosthetic limbs.
- **Signal conditioning circuits:** instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., AD620, INA128) with high **Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** > 90 dB.

### 2.4 Power Source Subsystem

| Source | Voltage (Typical) | Energy Density | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sealed Lead-Acid (SLA)** | 12 V | 30–50 Wh/kg | Cheap, heavy, used in basic wheelchairs |
| **Lithium-Ion (Li-ion)** | 3.7 V per cell | 150–250 Wh/kg | Standard in modern prosthetics & power chairs |
| **Lithium Polymer (LiPo)** | 3.7 V per cell | 130–200 Wh/kg | Flexible form factor, popular in wearables |
| **Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH)** | 1.2 V per cell | 60–120 Wh/kg | Robust, lower energy density |
| **Supercapacitors** | 2.5–2.7 V per cell | 5–10 Wh/kg | Used for peak-power bursts in hybrid systems |
| **Solar / Energy Harvesting** | Variable | N/A | Backup / trickle charge |

### 2.5 Structural Materials

The selection of structural materials is governed by the **biocompatibility**, **strength-to-weight ratio**, **fatigue life**, and **cost**.

- **Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V):** gold standard for bone-anchored implants (osseointegration modulus close to bone, ~110 GPa).
- **Carbon-fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP):** for sockets, exoskeleton frames — extremely high stiffness-to-weight.
- **Aluminum alloys (6061, 7075):** wheelchair frames — light, machinable.
- **Thermoplastics (Polypropylene, HDPE):** cosmetic covers, orthotic shells.
- **Silicone elastomers:** liners, cosmetic gloves — skin-friendly, hypoallergenic.
- **Polyurethane foams:** cushioning in wheelchairs (memory foam variants reduce pressure ulcers).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **most-likely-tested equations** for Module 3. Memorize the *form*, the *variable definitions*, and the *typical units* — KTU problems often test unit-handling and threshold-based reasoning.

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables & Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EMG RMS** $\;x_{RMS} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^{\,2}}$ | $x_i$ = sampled EMG voltage; $N$ = window length | mV |
| 2 | **Mean Absolute Value (MAV)** $\;MAV = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum \vert x_i \vert$ | Simplest EMG amplitude estimator | mV |
| 3 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** $\;SNR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\tfrac{A_{signal}}{A_{noise}}\right)$ | $A$ = RMS amplitude; indicates electrode quality | dB |
| 4 | **Skin-Electrode Impedance** $\;Z = R + jX_{c}$ | $R$ = resistive; $X_{c} = \tfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Ω |
| 5 | **CMRR** $\;CMRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\tfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Differential vs common-mode gain ratio | dB |
| 6 | **DC Motor Speed** $\;N = \tfrac{V - I R_{a}}{k \Phi}$ | $V$ = supply voltage; $I$ = current; $k$ = motor constant | rpm |
| 7 | **DC Motor Torque** $\;T = k \Phi I$ | Torque proportional to armature current | N·m |
| 8 | **Force from FSR** $\;R_{FSR} \approx \tfrac{k}{F^{n}}$ | Resistance $\downarrow$ as Force $\uparrow$ (n ≈ 0.5–1) | Ω, N |
| 9 | **Battery Run-time** $\;t = \tfrac{C_{Ah}}{I_{load}}$ | $C_{Ah}$ = capacity; $I$ = load current | hours |
| 10 | **Power** $\;P = V \cdot I = I^{2} R$ | Electrical power dissipated or delivered | W |
| 11 | **Gear Ratio (Torque amplification)** $\;T_{out} = N \cdot T_{in}$ ; $\omega_{out} = \tfrac{\omega_{in}}{N}$ | $N$ = gear ratio (output:input) | — |
| 12 | **SMA Stress (Nitinol)** $\;\sigma = E_{A} \cdot \varepsilon$ austenite ; $\sigma = E_{M} \cdot \varepsilon$ martensite | Different stiffnesses in two phases | MPa |
| 13 | **Pressure on Tissue** $\;P = \tfrac{F}{A}$ | Critical for wheelchair cushion & socket design | Pa or N/m² |
| 14 | **Strain in prosthetic shaft** $\;\varepsilon = \tfrac{\Delta L}{L_{0}}$ | $E \cdot \varepsilon$ gives stress (Hooke's Law) | dimensionless |
| 15 | **Resonant Frequency (IMU)** $\;f_{0} = \tfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\tfrac{k}{m}}$ | Determines sampling rate needed for gait analysis | Hz |

### 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **Clinical decision-making:** Pressure-mapping sensors in prosthetics prevent ulceration.
- **Industrial design:** Choosing the correct alloy (Ti vs Al) changes prosthesis mass by 40%.
- **Energy budgeting:** For a powered wheelchair, a 50 Ah battery at 24 V delivering 5 A gives $\tfrac{50}{5} = $ **10 hours of run-time** — critical for daily independence.
- **Rehabilitation outcomes:** EMG-driven FES enables a paralyzed patient to grasp a cup — *the ultimate real-world test* of components working together.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — EMG RMS as the Controller Input

The myoelectric controller must translate a noisy, stochastic EMG signal into a *single, monotonic, normalized command*. The **Root Mean Square (RMS)** is the most common feature.

**Given:** A window of $N$ digitized EMG samples $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N$ taken at sampling frequency $f_s$.

**Derivation (Step-by-Step):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Square each sample} \quad & \Rightarrow \quad x_i^{\,2},\;\; i = 1 \dots N \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Sum the squared values} \quad & \Rightarrow \quad S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^{\,2} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Take the arithmetic mean} \quad & \Rightarrow \quad M = \tfrac{S}{N} = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^{\,2} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Take the square root} \quad & \Rightarrow \quad x_{RMS} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^{\,2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The square-and-average step converts the *time-varying bipolar* EMG signal (which has near-zero mean) into a strictly positive *power-related* quantity that grows monotonically with muscle contraction. Taking the square root at the end restores the units to volts. The result is then compared against a *threshold* (typically 20–30 % of Maximum Voluntary Contraction) to open or close the prosthetic hand.

### 3.2 Derivation — Wheelchair Motor Torque and Speed Trade-off

For a powered wheelchair ascending an incline, the required torque at the wheel and the resulting speed are derived from the motor equations.

**Given:** Motor with armature resistance $R_a$, motor constant $k \Phi$, supply voltage $V$, total mechanical load torque $T_L$, and wheel radius $r$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Back-EMF:} \quad & E_{b} = k\Phi\,\omega \\[4pt]
\text{Kirchhoff's Voltage Law:} \quad & V = E_{b} + I R_{a} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; I = \tfrac{V - k\Phi\,\omega}{R_{a}} \\[4pt]
\text{Motor Torque:} \quad & T_{m} = k\Phi\,I = k\Phi\!\left(\tfrac{V - k\Phi\,\omega}{R_{a}}\right) \\[4pt]
\text{Equilibrium:} \quad & T_{m} = T_{L} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega = \tfrac{V}{k\Phi} - \tfrac{T_{L}\,R_{a}}{(k\Phi)^{2}} \\[4pt]
\text{No-load speed (}T_{L}=0\text{):} \quad & \omega_{0} = \tfrac{V}{k\Phi} \\[4pt]
\text{Stall torque (}\omega=0\text{):} \quad & T_{stall} = \tfrac{V\,k\Phi}{R_{a}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The speed–torque curve is a *straight line* between no-load speed and stall torque. By inserting a gear ratio $N$, the equivalent output torque becomes $N \cdot T_{m}$ while the output speed becomes $\omega / N$ — a classic *mechanical transformer*. The KTU-tractable question typically asks: *"At what speed does the motor deliver 50 % of stall torque?"* — the answer is always **half of no-load speed**, independent of $R_a$.

### 3.3 Derivation — Skin-Electrode Equivalent Circuit & Impedance

**Given:** Skin is modelled as a resistance $R$ in parallel with a capacitance $C$ (representing the stratum corneum and sweat-duct RC behaviour).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Complex impedance:} \quad & Z(\omega) = \tfrac{R}{1 + j\omega R C} \\[4pt]
\text{Separating real and imaginary parts:} \quad & Z = \tfrac{R}{1 + (\omega R C)^{2}} \;-\; j\,\tfrac{\omega R^{2} C}{1 + (\omega R C)^{2}} \\[4pt]
\text{Magnitude:} \quad & \vert Z \vert = \tfrac{R}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R C)^{2}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** As frequency $f$ increases, the capacitive path "shorts out" the skin resistance, lowering $\vert Z \vert$. This is why **gelled electrodes** (high $C$ due to ionic contact) and **higher-frequency amplifiers** (notch at 50 Hz power-line noise is essential) are used — both methods *minimise the disturbance* caused by skin's RC behaviour and power-line interference, respectively.

### 3.4 Derivation — Battery Run-Time under Variable Load

**Given:** A Li-ion battery pack rated at 24 V, 20 Ah, supplying a powered wheelchair with peak current 30 A (climbing) and idle current 3 A (cruising). The cycle: 10 % of time climbing, 90 % cruising.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average current:} \quad & I_{avg} = (0.10)(30) + (0.90)(3) = 3.0 + 2.7 = 5.7\ \text{A} \\[4pt]
\text{Energy stored:} \quad & E = V \cdot C_{Ah} = 24 \cdot 20 = 480\ \text{Wh} \\[4pt]
\text{Run-time (ideal):} \quad & t = \tfrac{C_{Ah}}{I_{avg}} = \tfrac{20}{5.7} \approx 3.51\ \text{h} \\[4pt]
\text{With 80 % Depth-of-Discharge safety:} \quad & t_{usable} = 0.80 \cdot 3.51 \approx 2.81\ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Battery capacity is rated in *ampere-hours*, not watt-hours directly. To get energy, we multiply by nominal voltage. The depth-of-discharge (DoD) derating is critical: **deep-cycling** Li-ion below 20 % State-of-Charge (SoC) **drastically shortens cycle life** — a frequent KTU 2024 pitfall.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Component Selection Decision

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Decision Table** — when choosing a component for a specific rehabilitation task, KTU frequently tests the **trade-off reasoning**. The matrix below is a high-yield summary.

| Criterion | DC Motor | Pneumatic (McKibben) | SMA (Nitinol) | FES |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Power density (W/kg) | 100–500 | 200–400 | 50–100 | (uses patient's muscle) |
| Compliance (back-drivable) | Low | **High** | Low | **High** |
| Noise | Moderate | Hiss from compressor | **Silent** | Silent |
| Control complexity | Moderate | High (compressors) | **High** (thermal) | **High** (recruitment) |
| Cost | Low | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| Typical use | Wheelchair | Soft exosuit | Hand orthosis | Foot drop stimulator |

### 3.6 Derivation — Pressure Ulcer Risk Threshold (Wheelchair Cushion)

**Given:** A patient weighing 75 kg (mass $m = 75$ kg, weight $W = mg = 75 \times 9.81 = 735.75$ N) sits on a cushion with buttock contact area $A = 0.045$ m².

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average pressure:} \quad & P_{avg} = \tfrac{W}{A} = \tfrac{735.75}{0.045} \approx 16{,}350\ \text{Pa} \approx 16.35\ \text{kPa} \\[4pt]
\text{Threshold for capillary closure:} \quad & P_{cap} \approx 4\ \text{kPa (ischial tuberosities)} \\[4pt]
\text{Conclusion:} \quad & P_{avg} > P_{cap} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{cushion MUST redistribute load (contour shape)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is a *clinical-physics bridge*. Pressure ulcers form when sustained interface pressure exceeds capillary perfusion pressure (~4 kPa at bony prominences). The cushion's *role* is to increase effective contact area, thereby *lowering average pressure* below 4 kPa. A common KTU question: *"Calculate the minimum area needed to keep pressure below 4 kPa."* — answer: $A_{min} = \tfrac{735.75}{4000} \approx 0.184$ m².

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Myoelectric Prosthetic Arm

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The following Mermaid block renders the *functional architecture* — i.e., the signal and power flow through each component family — rather than the physical geometry. This is the standard KTU-accepted schematic style.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[User Residual Muscle] --> A2[Surface EMG Electrode Pair]
    A2 --> A3[Instrumentation Amplifier AD620]
    A3 --> A4[Bandpass Filter 20 to 500 Hz]
    A4 --> A5[Notch Filter 50 Hz Mains Reject]
    A5 --> A6[ADC on Microcontroller STM32]
    A6 --> A7[EMG Feature Extraction RMS Block]
    A7 --> A8[Threshold Comparator]
    A8 --> A9[PWM Driver to BLDC Motor]
    A9 --> A10[Belt Driven Gear Reducer]
    A10 --> A11[Prosthetic Hand Terminal Device]
    A11 --> A12[Force and Slip Sensor in Fingertip]
    A12 --> A6
    B1[Li-ion 7.4V Battery] --> B2[Buck Boost Regulator to 5V and 3.3V]
    B2 --> A3
    B2 --> A4
    B2 --> A5
    B2 --> A6
    B2 --> A9
    C1[Silicone Liner Socket Carbon Fiber] --> A1
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The forward (control) path flows **left to right**: *Muscle → Electrode → Amplifier → Filter → ADC → Feature → Comparator → Driver → Motor → Gear → Hand*.
- The feedback path (dashed closed loop) returns from the **fingertip force sensor** back to the microcontroller — enabling *slip detection* and *adaptive grip*.
- The **power rail** (lower block) supplies regulated voltages to every active component.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Powered Wheelchair Sub-systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PowerSubsystem
        P1[24V Li-ion Battery Pack] --> P2[Battery Management System BMS]
        P2 --> P3[DC Bus 24V]
    end
    subgraph ControlSubsystem
        C1[Joystick Input] --> C2[Microcontroller PIC or STM32]
        C2 --> C3[Speed and Direction Logic]
    end
    subgraph DriveSubsystem
        C3 --> D1[Motor Driver H Bridge MOSFET]
        D1 --> D2[BLDC Motor Left Wheel]
        D1 --> D3[BLDC Motor Right Wheel]
        D2 --> D4[Encoder for Closed Loop Speed]
        D3 --> D5[Encoder for Closed Loop Speed]
        D4 --> C2
        D5 --> C2
    end
    P3 --> D1
    P3 --> C2
    subgraph UserInterface
        UI1[LCD Display Battery Level]
        UI2[Buzzer Low Battery Alarm]
        UI3[Tilt Sensor Anti Tip]
        UI3 --> C2
    end
```

**Key takeaway for KTU:** The wheelchair is a *closed-loop, dual-motor, differential-drive* robot. Loss of either encoder feedback makes the chair unsafe (it may run away) — examiners love asking *"Why is encoder feedback essential?"* Answer: to implement **closed-loop speed regulation**, ensuring both wheels turn at the same RPM for *straight-line motion*.

### 4.3 Functional Diagram — FES (Functional Electrical Stimulation) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Intent Detector Push Button or Heel Switch] --> F2[Stimulator Control Unit]
    F2 --> F3[Current Controlled Output Stage]
    F3 --> F4[Surface Electrodes on Tibialis Anterior]
    F4 --> F5[Stimulated Muscle Contraction]
    F5 --> F6[Dorsiflexion of Foot]
    F6 --> F1
    G1[Rechargeable Battery 9V] --> F2
    G1 --> F3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The FES system is **bio-closed-loop**: the device uses the *patient's own body* as the actuator. The electrical pulse *bypasses* the damaged nervous system and directly recruits motor neurons. This is the conceptual *miracle* of rehabilitation engineering — *no motor required, the muscle is the motor*.

### 4.4 Comparative Diagram — Material Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1[Need biocompatible load bearing material] --> Q2{Contact with skin or implant?}
    Q2 -- Skin contact only --> Q3[CFRP, Aluminum, Polypropylene]
    Q2 -- Implanted --> Q4[Titanium Ti-6Al-4V]
    Q3 --> Q5{Weight critical?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Carbon fiber composite]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Aluminum 6061]
    Q4 --> Q8{Osseointegration needed?}
    Q8 -- Yes --> Q9[Porous coated Titanium]
    Q8 -- No --> Q10[Smooth Titanium stem]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List the major functional families of components used in rehabilitation engineering. Briefly explain the role of sensors and actuators with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

The major families are: **(i) Sensors/Transducers** (acquire bio-signals), **(ii) Actuators** (produce motion or force), **(iii) Power Sources** (supply energy), **(iv) Control Electronics** (process signals), **(v) Structural Materials** (mechanical frame), **(vi) Interfaces/Couplers** (connect to body), and **(vii) Feedback/Display Units** (inform user).

- **Sensors** convert a physiological signal into an electrical one. *Example:* Surface EMG electrode picks up muscle electrical activity (20 µV – 5 mV) for controlling a myoelectric hand.
- **Actuators** convert electrical/pneumatic energy into mechanical motion. *Example:* A BLDC motor inside a prosthetic hand closes the fingers in response to the EMG command.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is a Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)? Why is Nitinol preferred in lightweight orthotic devices?*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** is a metallic alloy that "remembers" its original shape and returns to it upon heating *after* being deformed in its low-temperature martensite phase. The two key phases are **martensite** (low temperature, soft) and **austenite** (high temperature, stiff).

**Nitinol (Nickel-Titanium)** is preferred because it offers:
1. **High power-to-weight ratio** (≈ 50–100 W/kg active actuation).
2. **Silent operation** — no motor noise, suitable for public use.
3. **Biocompatibility** — non-toxic, MRI-compatible.
4. **Solid-state actuation** — no moving parts like gears or compressors, enabling miniaturized orthotic fingers.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to state units** in numerical answers costs at least 0.5 to 1 mark. Always end with the unit in parentheses, e.g., *("Answer = 3.51 h")*.
> 2. **Confusing RMS with peak-to-peak**: KTU asks specifically for RMS amplitude. A student who writes "peak-to-peak" loses the application mark.
> 3. **Skipping the threshold logic** in myoelectric controllers: an EMG RMS value *by itself* does not actuate the hand — you MUST mention comparison with a threshold (e.g., 20–30 % MVC) to score full marks.
> 4. **Mixing up torque and speed relationships**: a higher gear ratio increases torque but decreases speed. Examiners reward students who *quantify* the trade-off using $\;T_{out} = N \cdot T_{in}$ and $\;\omega_{out} = \omega_{in} / N$.
> 5. **Battery DoD** is often ignored: writing $t = C/I$ without the 80 % DoD derating is considered an incomplete engineering answer.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a myoelectric prosthetic hand**, identifying the role of every component family (sensors, signal conditioning, control, actuator, power, interface). [7 Marks]

**(b)** A patient produces an EMG signal that, when windowed over $N = 250$ samples, has the squared values: $\sum x_i^{2} = 0.0125\ \text{V}^{2}$.
  - (i) Compute the **RMS amplitude** of the EMG. [2 Marks]
  - (ii) The controller's **activation threshold** is set to **30 % of the patient's Maximum Voluntary Contraction (MVC) RMS = 0.45 V**. Will the hand open? Justify. [2 Marks]
  - (iii) The EMG is sampled at $f_s = 1$ kHz. Calculate the **time-window** over which this RMS was computed. [1 Mark]
  - (iv) If the same EMG is filtered with a **notch filter at 50 Hz**, explain its effect on **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**. [2 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional architecture — Block diagram:**

> Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the Mermaid block. In the exam, draw a clean block diagram with the following blocks *in series*:
> 1. *Residual muscle* (source)
> 2. *Surface EMG electrodes* (Ag/AgCl) — sensor
> 3. *Instrumentation amplifier* (e.g., AD620, gain = 1000) — signal conditioning
> 4. *Bandpass filter* (20 Hz – 500 Hz) — bandwidth selection
> 5. *Notch filter* (50 Hz) — mains-noise rejection
> 6. *ADC + Microcontroller* — digitization + control
> 7. *Feature extraction (RMS)* — feature
> 8. *Threshold comparator* — decision
> 9. *PWM Driver + H-Bridge* — power electronics
> 10. *BLDC Motor + Gearbox* — actuator
> 11. *Hand terminal device* — output
> 12. *Force / slip sensor* — feedback
> 13. *Li-ion battery + voltage regulators* — power source
> 14. *Socket, liner, harness* — body interface
>
> **Mark distribution:** (1 mark for correct block sequence, 2 marks for labelling each block's function, 1 mark for feedback loop, 1 mark for power rail, 1 mark for interface, 1 mark for neatness and arrows).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) RMS amplitude:} \quad & x_{RMS} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N}\sum x_i^{2}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{0.0125}{250}} = \sqrt{0.00005} \\[4pt]
& x_{RMS} \approx 0.00707\ \text{V} = 7.07\ \text{mV} \\[6pt]
\text{(ii) Threshold check:} \quad & 30\% \text{ of } 0.45 = 0.135\ \text{V} = 135\ \text{mV} \\[4pt]
& \text{Since } x_{RMS} = 7.07\ \text{mV} < 135\ \text{mV}, \text{ hand will NOT open.} \\[6pt]
\text{(iii) Time window:} \quad & T = \tfrac{N}{f_s} = \tfrac{250}{1000} = 0.25\ \text{s} = 250\ \text{ms} \\[6pt]
\text{(iv) Notch filter effect:} \quad & \text{The notch filter removes 50 Hz mains-line interference, which is} \\[2pt]
& \text{typically the dominant noise source in surface EMG. By removing it,} \\[2pt]
& \text{the noise amplitude } A_{noise} \downarrow \text{, so } SNR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(A_s / A_n) \uparrow
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answers:** (i) **7.07 mV**, (ii) **No — below threshold**, (iii) **0.25 s**, (iv) **SNR increases because 50 Hz noise is removed**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Describe the **components of a powered wheelchair**: motors, controllers, batteries, joystick, and feedback sensors. With a block diagram, explain how joystick input is translated into differential wheel motion. [7 Marks]

**(b)** A powered wheelchair uses **two 24 V, 350 W BLDC motors**. The wheelchair plus patient mass is **120 kg** and must climb a **5° incline** at a constant speed of **1.2 m/s**. Assume rolling resistance coefficient $\mu = 0.015$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$, and wheel radius $r = 0.2$ m.
  - (i) Calculate the **total resistive force** on the incline. [3 Marks]
  - (ii) Calculate the **required total mechanical power** at the wheels. [2 Marks]
  - (iii) If each motor is 80 % efficient, calculate the **required electrical power** drawn from the **24 V battery**, and hence the **current**. [2 Marks]
  - (iv) If the battery is rated **24 V, 50 Ah**, what is the **theoretical run-time** under this continuous load, assuming 80 % Depth-of-Discharge (DoD)? [2 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Powered Wheelchair Components — Block Diagram:**

> Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes. The expected written answer covers:
>
> **Components list (4 marks):**
> - **BLDC motors** (one per wheel) — provide torque.
> - **Motor driver (H-bridge MOSFET)** — direction and speed control via PWM.
> - **Joystick** — analog input (two-axis potentiometers).
> - **Microcontroller** — converts joystick X/Y to differential PWM duty cycles.
> - **Encoders** — closed-loop speed feedback.
> - **Li-ion battery (24 V)** — power source.
> - **Battery Management System (BMS)** — protects against over-discharge.
> - **Tilt sensor / anti-tip wheels** — safety.
> - **LCD / buzzer** — user interface.
>
> **Differential drive logic (3 marks):** Joystick $x$ (lateral) controls *turn rate*, joystick $y$ (forward) controls *forward speed*. Wheel speeds:
> - $v_{left} = v_{fwd} - v_{turn}$
> - $v_{right} = v_{fwd} + v_{turn}$
> Turning is achieved by *speed difference* — not by a steering mechanism.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Total resistive force on incline:} \\[2pt]
\text{Weight:} \quad & W = m g = 120 \times 9.81 = 1177.2\ \text{N} \\[4pt]
\text{Grade component:} \quad & F_{g} = W \sin(5^{\circ}) = 1177.2 \times 0.0872 = 102.6\ \text{N} \\[4pt]
\text{Rolling resistance:} \quad & F_{r} = \mu W \cos(5^{\circ}) = 0.015 \times 1177.2 \times 0.9962 = 17.6\ \text{N} \\[4pt]
\text{Total resistive force:} \quad & F_{tot} = F_{g} + F_{r} = 102.6 + 17.6 = 120.2\ \text{N} \\[6pt]
\text{(ii) Mechanical power at wheels:} \quad & P_{mech} = F_{tot} \cdot v = 120.2 \times 1.2 = 144.24\ \text{W} \\[6pt]
\text{(iii) Electrical input (two motors, 80 % efficient each):} \\[2pt]
\text{Total efficiency:} \quad & \eta_{tot} = 0.80 \times 0.80 = 0.64 \\[4pt]
P_{elec} = \tfrac{P_{mech}}{\eta_{tot}} = \tfrac{144.24}{0.64} = 225.4\ \text{W} \\[4pt]
I = \tfrac{P_{elec}}{V} = \tfrac{225.4}{24} = 9.39\ \text{A} \\[6pt]
\text{(iv) Run-time with 80 % DoD:} \\[2pt]
t_{ideal} = \tfrac{C_{Ah}}{I} = \tfrac{50}{9.39} = 5.32\ \text{h} \\[4pt]
t_{usable} = 0.80 \times 5.32 = 4.26\ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answers:** (i) **≈ 120.2 N**, (ii) **≈ 144.2 W mechanical**, (iii) **225.4 W, 9.39 A**, (iv) **≈ 4.26 hours**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Components**
>
> - **Sensor families:** EMG (muscle), EEG (brain), FSR (force), IMU/gyro/accel (motion), goniometer (joint angle), temperature/humidity.
> - **Actuator families:** DC, BLDC, servo, stepper motors; pneumatic (McKibben); hydraulic; SMA (Nitinol); piezoelectric; FES (uses *patient's* muscle).
> - **Control chain:** Sensor → Instrumentation Amplifier (high CMRR > 90 dB) → Bandpass Filter (20–500 Hz for EMG) → Notch (50 Hz) → ADC → Feature (RMS) → Comparator → Driver (PWM/H-bridge) → Actuator.
> - **Key EMG features:** MAV (simple), RMS (preferred), Waveform Length, Zero-Crossings, Frequency domain (Mean/Median Frequency).
> - **Battery hierarchy by energy density:** Li-ion > LiPo > NiMH > SLA. Always apply **80 % DoD** derating for Li-ion in KTU numerical problems.
> - **Material hierarchy by specific strength:** CFRP > Titanium > Aluminum 7075 > Aluminum 6061 > Steel > Plastics.
> - **Critical clinical threshold:** Capillary closure pressure ≈ **4 kPa** — driver of all cushion/socket designs.
> - **Powered wheelchair logic:** Differential drive; joystick $(x,y)$ → $(v_{left}, v_{right})$ mapping.
> - **FES principle:** Bypass damaged CNS, recruit motor neurons directly with biphasic current pulses (typically 20–50 mA, 20–50 Hz, 100–300 µs pulse width).
> - **SMA actuation:** Heating → Austenite phase → Stiffer recovery to original shape. Cooling needed before next cycle (slow = main limitation).
> - **Pneumatic muscle (McKibben) advantage:** Compliance — safe for human interaction, used in rehabilitation robots.
> - **Formulae to memorize cold:** $x_{RMS} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N}\sum x_i^{2}}$, $SNR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(A_s / A_n)$, $CMRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$, $P = F \cdot v$, $P = V \cdot I$, $t = C_{Ah}/I$, $P_{cap} = 4\ \text{kPa}$ threshold.
> - **Standard units to recall:** Hz, mV, dB, N·m, W, Pa, Wh, Ah, Ω, kΩ.
> - **Examiner loves:** Component trade-off tables, block diagrams with feedback loop, threshold-based control reasoning, and *numeric* derating factors (DoD, efficiency).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Factors Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Human Factors Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human Factors Design Considerations** in Rehabilitation Engineering refers to the systematic application of knowledge about human physical, cognitive, sensory, and psychological characteristics to the design, development, and evaluation of assistive devices, prosthetics, orthotics, wheelchairs, workplace accommodations, and accessible environments. It encompasses **anthropometry**, **biomechanics**, **ergonomics**, **human-computer interaction**, and **universal design principles** to ensure that rehabilitative technologies are safe, effective, comfortable, and acceptable to the end user.

The discipline is rooted in the principle that **the user, not the technology, is the central design driver**. It bridges the gap between the engineered device and the unique human capabilities and limitations of persons with disabilities (PWDs).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Custom Tailor:**
> Think of human factors design like a master tailor crafting a bespoke suit. The tailor doesn't use one-size-fits-all patterns; instead, he measures the customer's shoulders, arms, torso, and posture, then sews accordingly. Similarly, a rehabilitation engineer doesn't pick a wheelchair "off the rack" — they measure the user's **body dimensions, joint range of motion, grip strength, vision, cognition, and environment** to design or prescribe a device that fits like a glove. A poorly designed wheelchair is like an ill-fitting suit: it may look fine but causes sores, fatigue, and rejection by the user.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Data

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Anthropometric & Performance Metrics in Rehabilitation Design:**
> - **5th–95th Percentile Range:** The dimensional window covering 90% of the target population.
> - **Static Strength:** Maximum force a muscle group can exert against an immovable object (typically **15–70 kgf** for upper limb).
> - **Dynamic Strength:** Force exerted during repetitive motion (**20–50%** of static strength).
> - **Joint Range of Motion (ROM):** Functional angles for major joints (e.g., shoulder flexion **0°–180°**, wrist deviation **0°–50°**).
> - **Cognitive Load (NASA-TLX):** Standardized workload score (**0–100 scale**).
> - **Visual Acuity Threshold:** Minimum legible character size — typically **5 mm** at 400 mm viewing distance for low-vision users.
> - **Audibility Threshold:** Target output **≥ 90 dB** for hearing-impaired alert signals in ambient noise of 60 dB.

## 1.4 Why Human Factors Matter in Rehabilitation

- **User Safety:** Prevents pressure sores, repetitive strain injuries, falls, and overuse syndromes.
- **Functional Efficacy:** Ensures the device actually solves the intended functional problem.
- **Adherence & Abandonment:** Industry data shows **~30% of assistive devices are abandoned** within a year — most often due to poor human-factors fit, not technical failure.
- **Inclusivity & Dignity:** Aligns with **UN CRPD (Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities)** and the **RPwD Act 2016** of India.
- **Economic Efficiency:** Reduces re-fitting costs, secondary injuries, and caregiver burden.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Human Factors Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Seven Pillars of Human Factors in Rehabilitation Engineering:**

### Pillar 1: Anthropometry (Body Dimensions)
- **Static Anthropometry:** Body dimensions in fixed, standing or seated postures (e.g., stature, sitting height, elbow height, buttock-popliteal length).
- **Dynamic Anthropometry:** Body dimensions during movement (functional reach, eye height while seated, etc.).
- **Source Data:** Anthropometric surveys (e.g., **NHANES** for the US, **Indian Anthropometric Database (IAD)** for Indian populations).
- **Design Rule:** Design for the **5th percentile female to 95th percentile male** to accommodate 90% of users. For rehabilitation devices intended for a specific user, use **individual anthropometry**.

### Pillar 2: Biomechanics & Strength
- **Posture Analysis:** Spinal loading, joint angles, center of gravity (CoG) tracking.
- **Strength Considerations:** Push/pull forces, grip strength, pinch strength, lifting capacity.
- **Endurance:** Sustained vs. peak effort, fatigue curves.

### Pillar 3: Sensory Considerations
- **Vision:** Visual acuity, contrast sensitivity, field of view, color perception.
- **Hearing:** Frequency range, speech reception threshold.
- **Touch/Haptics:** Tactile acuity, two-point discrimination (**typically 2–3 mm** on fingertip), vibration sensitivity.
- **Design Implication:** Multi-modal feedback (visual + auditory + tactile) for redundancy.

### Pillar 4: Cognitive & Perceptual Factors
- **Memory:** Working memory (typically **7 ± 2 items** — Miller's Law).
- **Learning Curve:** Time to proficiency.
- **Attention & Distraction Susceptibility.**
- **Cognitive Impairment Adaptations:** Simplified interfaces, large buttons, consistent layouts.

### Pillar 5: Environmental Context
- **Physical Environment:** Door widths (**≥ 900 mm** for wheelchair access per RPwD 2016), ramp gradients (**≤ 1:12**), floor surfaces.
- **Climate:** Heat/cold effects on prosthetic socket fit (thermal expansion).
- **Cultural & Social Factors:** Stigma, cosmetic appearance, religious considerations.

### Pillar 6: Safety, Comfort & Usability
- **Safety Factors (FoS):** Static **FoS = 2.0–4.0** for body-weight-bearing devices; **FoS = 1.5–2.0** for dynamic loading.
- **Comfort Metrics:** Interface pressure (**≤ 32 mmHg** capillary closing pressure to avoid pressure ulcers).
- **Usability Heuristics (Nielsen's 10):** Visibility, match with real world, user control, consistency, error prevention.

### Pillar 7: Universal Design & Accessibility
- **Ron Mace's 7 Principles:** Equitable use, flexibility, simple/intuitive, perceptible info, tolerance for error, low effort, size/space for approach.
- **Compliance Standards:** **ADA (USA)**, **EN 301 549 (Europe)**, **RPwD Act 2016 & harmonized BIS standards (India)**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Examinations:**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Percentile Rank** | $P_{k} = L + \left(\dfrac{\frac{k \cdot N}{100} - F}{f}\right) \cdot h$ | $L$ = lower class boundary, $N$ = total, $F$ = cumulative freq before class, $f$ = class freq, $h$ = class width |
| 2 | **Standard Normal Percentile** | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $X$ = measured value, $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma$ = SD |
| 3 | **Interface Pressure (Laplace)** | $P = \dfrac{T}{R}$ | $P$ = pressure (N/m²), $T$ = surface tension (N/m), $R$ = radius of curvature (m) |
| 4 | **Static Safety Factor** | $\text{FoS} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\sigma_{\text{applied}}}$ | $\sigma$ in MPa or N/m² |
| 5 | **Joint Torque (Moment)** | $\tau = F \cdot d \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | $F$ in N, $d$ in m, $\theta$ in radians |
| 6 | **Static Strength Ratio (Popov)** | $\text{SSR} = \dfrac{\text{Strength available}}{\text{Strength required}}$ | Dimensionless; target $\geq 1$ |
| 7 | **Push/Pull Force Limits (NIOSH-derived)** | $F_{\text{rec}} = \dfrac{LC}{HM}$ (simplified LCM) | $LC$ = Load Constant (≈ 23 kgf), $HM$ = Horizontal Multiplier |
| 8 | **Wheelchair Rolling Resistance** | $F_{rr} = \mu \cdot W$ | $\mu$ = rolling coeff., $W$ = weight (N) |
| 9 | **Wheelchair Grade Resistance** | $F_{g} = W \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ | $\alpha$ = slope angle |
| 10 | **Total Wheelchair Propulsion Force** | $F_{t} = F_{rr} + F_{g} + F_{a}$ | $F_{a} = m \cdot a$ for acceleration |
| 11 | **Ramp Gradient (Accessibility)** | $\text{Grade} = \dfrac{\text{rise}}{\text{run}}$ | Recommended $\leq \dfrac{1}{12}$ for public ramps |
| 12 | **Snellen Visual Acuity** | $V = \dfrac{d}{D}$ | $d$ = test distance, $D$ = distance at which letter subtends 5 arcmin |
| 13 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Audibility)** | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $S, N$ in same units (dB) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> - **Percentile Formulas** determine anthropometric dimensions of standard devices (e.g., crutch height, wheelchair seat width).
> - **Laplace Pressure Equation** underpins socket-fit biomechanics in prosthetics to prevent skin breakdown.
> - **Joint Torque Formula** is critical for sizing motors in robotic exoskeletons and powered orthoses.
> - **Wheelchair Force Equations** are used in ISO 7176 and IS 14449 testing protocols.
> - **Snellen Acuity** guides the design of tactile labels, large-print overlays, and screen readers for low-vision users.

## 2.3 The Human-Centered Design Loop (ISO 9241-210)

```
Understand Context of Use → Specify User Requirements → 
Produce Design Solutions → Evaluate Against Requirements
↺ (Iterate)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **mandatory design process** KTU examiners expect when a question asks for a "human factors design approach" for a given device.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Percentile Calculation for Seat-Width Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A rehabilitation engineer is designing a standard-issue manual wheelchair for a population. Hip-breadth data (in mm) from 200 adult Indian users is grouped as follows. Determine the **seat width** that accommodates 95% of the population (i.e., 95th percentile).

| Class Interval (mm) | Frequency $f$ | Cumulative Frequency $F$ |
|---------------------|---------------|--------------------------|
| 300 – 340 | 10 | 10 |
| 340 – 380 | 30 | 40 |
| 380 – 420 | 70 | 110 |
| 420 – 460 | 60 | 170 |
| 460 – 500 | 25 | 195 |
| 500 – 540 | 5 | 200 |

### Step 1: Identify the 95th Percentile Class
Total $N = 200$.

$$
\frac{95 \cdot N}{100} = \frac{95 \times 200}{100} = 190
$$

We need the class in which cumulative frequency $\geq 190$.
Looking at the table, the 95th percentile falls in the class **460 – 500 mm** (cumulative jumps from 170 to 195).

### Step 2: Extract Formula Parameters
Using the percentile formula:

$$
P_{k} = L + \left(\dfrac{\frac{k \cdot N}{100} - F}{f}\right) \cdot h
$$

$$
P_{95} = L + \left(\dfrac{190 - F}{f}\right) \cdot h
$$

Here:
- $L = 460$ (lower boundary of percentile class)
- $F = 170$ (cumulative frequency of class *before* the percentile class)
- $f = 25$ (frequency of percentile class)
- $h = 40$ (class width: $500 - 460$)

### Step 3: Substitute & Simplify

$$
P_{95} = 460 + \left(\dfrac{190 - 170}{25}\right) \cdot 40
$$

$$
P_{95} = 460 + \left(\dfrac{20}{25}\right) \cdot 40
$$

$$
P_{95} = 460 + (0.80) \cdot 40
$$

$$
P_{95} = 460 + 32
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{95} = 492 \text{ mm}}
$$

### Step 4: Engineering Design Decision
The seat width must be at least **492 mm** to accommodate 95% of the target population. Adding a **functional clearance of ~20 mm** for clothing and postural shift:

$$
\text{Recommended Seat Width} = 492 + 20 = 512 \text{ mm} \approx 510 \text{ mm} \; (\text{standard 20-inch chair})
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Joint Torque for Powered Wrist Orthosis

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A patient has 30% residual wrist flexor strength. The orthosis motor must produce a torque to lift a 1.5 kg hand-mass through a 45° arc. The lever arm (motor pulley radius) is 0.04 m. Determine the minimum motor torque and check the safety factor.

### Step 1: Compute the Load Force
The mass of the hand is $m = 1.5$ kg, so the gravitational force on the load is:

$$
F = m \cdot g
$$

$$
F = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.715 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Required Torque
The torque required at the wrist joint to hold the hand horizontal (worst case, $\theta = 90°$, $\sin\theta = 1$):

$$
\tau = F \cdot d \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$

$$
\tau = 14.715 \times 0.04 \times \sin(90°)
$$

$$
\tau = 14.715 \times 0.04 \times 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\text{required}} = 0.5886 \text{ N·m}}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Safety Factor for Dynamic Loading & Patient Variability
For a body-worn powered orthosis, **FoS = 2.5** is recommended (covers start-up transients, muscle spasms, and battery voltage drop).

$$
\tau_{\text{design}} = \text{FoS} \times \tau_{\text{required}}
$$

$$
\tau_{\text{design}} = 2.5 \times 0.5886
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\text{design}} = 1.4715 \text{ N·m} \approx 1.5 \text{ N·m}}
$$

### Step 4: Motor Selection Inference
A DC gear motor with rated torque **≥ 1.5 N·m** and stall torque **≥ 3.5 N·m** (FoS = 2 on rated) is appropriate. 

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Wheelchair Propulsion Force on a Ramp

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A 75 kg user pushes a 15 kg wheelchair up a 5° ramp at constant speed. Coefficient of rolling resistance is 0.015. Verify if the user can sustain the push (typical sustained push force capacity: 25 kgf ≈ 245 N).

### Step 1: Total System Weight

$$
W = (m_{\text{user}} + m_{\text{chair}}) \cdot g
$$

$$
W = (75 + 15) \times 9.81 = 90 \times 9.81
$$

$$
W = 882.9 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 2: Rolling Resistance Force

$$
F_{rr} = \mu \cdot W
$$

$$
F_{rr} = 0.015 \times 882.9
$$

$$
F_{rr} = 13.24 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 3: Grade Resistance Force

$$
F_{g} = W \cdot \sin(\alpha)
$$

$$
F_{g} = 882.9 \times \sin(5°)
$$

$$
F_{g} = 882.9 \times 0.0872
$$

$$
F_{g} = 76.99 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 4: Total Push Force (Constant Speed → No Acceleration)

$$
F_{t} = F_{rr} + F_{g} + F_{a} \quad \text{where } F_{a} = 0
$$

$$
F_{t} = 13.24 + 76.99 + 0
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{t} = 90.23 \text{ N} \approx 9.2 \text{ kgf}}
$$

### Step 5: Feasibility Check
Required force = **9.2 kgf** < User's sustained capacity of **25 kgf**. 

> **[OK] DESIGN IS FEASIBLE** with a **Static Strength Ratio (SSR) of 25 / 9.2 ≈ 2.7** (acceptable per ergonomics literature where SSR ≥ 1.5 is recommended).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for Human Factors in Wheelchair Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favored Analytical Tool:** Use this matrix-style approach in long-answer questions.

| Human Factor | Question to Ask | Quantitative Threshold | Source Standard |
|--------------|-----------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **Hip Breadth** | Does seat width fit 95% of users? | 460–500 mm (95th percentile Indian male adult) | Anthropometric survey data |
| **Popliteal Length** | Is seat depth correct? | 400–450 mm | ISO 7176-7 |
| **Trunk Stability** | Does backrest provide lumbar support? | Backrest height ≥ 250 mm | RESNA standards |
| **Push Force** | Can user self-propel on standard ramp? | Required ≤ 50 N (5° slope) | ISO 7176-11 |
| **Cognitive Load** | Can user operate brakes intuitively? | NASA-TLX ≤ 40 (moderate workload) | Hart & Staveland (1988) |
| **Visual Feedback** | Are control markings visible to low-vision users? | Character height ≥ 5 mm at 400 mm | ADAAG 703 |
| **Pressure Relief** | Does cushion reduce interface pressure? | $\leq 32$ mmHg at ischial tuberosities | NPUAP guidelines |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Human Factors Design Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify User Population] --> B[Conduct Anthropometric Survey]
    B --> C[Define Functional Requirements]
    C --> D[Generate Design Concepts]
    D --> E[Prototype Fabrication]
    E --> F[Usability Testing with Users]
    F --> G{Acceptance Criteria Met?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Final Production & Training]
    G -->|No| I[Iterate Design]
    I --> D
    H --> J[Post Market Surveillance]
    J --> K[Long Term Outcome Data]
    K --> A
```

## 4.2 Seven Pillars of Human Factors (Mind Map)

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Human Factors in Rehab Engineering))
    Anthropometry
      Static dimensions
      Dynamic reach
      Percentile design
    Biomechanics
      Joint torque
      Strength ratios
      Posture
    Sensory
      Visual acuity
      Auditory range
      Haptic feedback
    Cognitive
      Memory load
      Learning curve
      Attention
    Environment
      Doorways
      Climate
      Cultural fit
    Safety
      Pressure relief
      Safety factors
      Error tolerance
    Universal Design
      Seven principles
      Standards compliance
      Equitable use
```

## 4.3 Human-Machine Interface Loop for Assistive Devices

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    User1[User Intent] --> Brain[Cognitive Processing]
    Brain --> Musc[Muscular Activation]
    Musc --> Interface[Device Interface e.g. Switch Joystick]
    Interface --> Device[Assistive Device]
    Device --> Output[Functional Output e.g. Movement]
    Output --> Feedback[Sensory Feedback]
    Feedback --> User1
```

## 4.4 Anthropometric Measurement Categories (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Anthropometry] --> B[Static]
    A --> C[Dynamic]
    A --> D[Functional]
    B --> B1[Stature]
    B --> B2[Sitting Height]
    B --> B3[Hip Breadth]
    B --> B4[Elbow Height]
    C --> C1[Functional Reach]
    C --> C2[Eye Height Seated]
    C --> C3[Sit to Stand Trajectory]
    D --> D1[Grip Span]
    D --> D2[Push Reach]
    D --> D3[Lifting Envelope]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the term "Anthropometry" and explain its significance in the design of rehabilitation devices.**

**Model Answer:**
Anthropometry is the scientific study of the measurements and proportions of the human body, including static (e.g., stature, hip breadth), dynamic (e.g., reach envelopes), and functional (e.g., grip span) dimensions. Its significance in rehabilitation engineering lies in enabling **user-specific and population-based design** of devices such as wheelchairs, prosthetics, orthotics, and workstations. By applying **5th–95th percentile design rules** and using population-specific anthropometric data (e.g., IAD for India), engineers ensure proper fit, prevent secondary injuries such as pressure ulcers, improve comfort, and enhance functional independence. **(3 marks)** — definition (1), population design rule (1), rehab-specific application (1).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is the "5th–95th percentile" design rule? Why is it preferred in assistive device design over average-based design?**

**Model Answer:**
The 5th–95th percentile rule states that a product should be designed to fit users whose body dimensions fall between the 5th percentile (smallest) and the 95th percentile (largest) of the target population, thus accommodating **90% of users**. It is preferred over average-based design because using only the mean would exclude approximately 50% of the population on either side. In assistive devices, the rule is critical because **poor fit can cause pain, skin breakdown, abandonment, or safety hazards** — a misfit device may be unusable for the very person who needs it most. **(3 marks)** — definition (1), comparison with average (1), rehab relevance (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**Q: Discuss in detail the human factors design considerations for a manual wheelchair. Cover anthropometric fit, biomechanics of propulsion, safety factors, cognitive usability, and relevant standards.**

#### Part (a) Anthropometric Fit and Biomechanics of Propulsion (7 Marks)

**Solution Outline:**

**1. Anthropometric Fit (3 marks):**
- **Seat width:** 95th percentile hip-breadth + clearance ≈ 460–500 mm + 20 mm = 480–520 mm.
- **Seat depth:** Popliteal length minus 50–75 mm to avoid popliteal pressure.
- **Seat height:** Must allow foot-flat contact or proper footplate position; elbow angle 100°–120° on push-rim.
- **Backrest height:** ≥ 250 mm for trunk stability; lower backrest for active users needing more ROM.

**2. Biomechanics of Propulsion (4 marks):**
- **Push-rim diameter:** Typically 480–540 mm for adults; affects moment arm.
- **Push force calculation:** $F_t = F_{rr} + F_g + F_a$ as derived in Section 3.3.
- **Push frequency & cadence:** Optimal at 1 push per 2–3 seconds to minimize fatigue.
- **Stroke pattern:** Semi-circular or arc pattern is more efficient than double-loops.
- **Shoulder injury prevention:** Maintain push angle ≤ 90° of internal rotation to avoid rotator cuff injuries.

#### Part (b) Safety, Cognitive Usability, and Standards (7 Marks)

**Solution Outline:**

**1. Safety Factors and Pressure Relief (3 marks):**
- **Static FoS = 2.0–3.0** for frame load-bearing components.
- **Interface pressure** at ischial tuberosities must be **≤ 32 mmHg** to prevent pressure ulcers — solved via foam/gel cushions and tilt-in-space.
- **Tipping stability:** Anti-tipper wheels, proper rear-wheel axle position (CoG over drive wheels).
- **Braking:** Push-to-lock or pull-to-lock brakes; must be operable with **< 40 N** of force.

**2. Cognitive Usability (2 marks):**
- Brake lever: color-coded (red = lock, green = release).
- Footplates: simple flip-up, single-action release.
- Folding/unfolding: < 30 seconds, single-handle operation.

**3. Relevant Standards (2 marks):**
- **ISO 7176** (Wheelchair series — strength, dimensions, performance).
- **RESNA standards** (US).
- **IS 14449** (Indian standard for wheelchairs).
- **RPwD Act 2016** mandates accessibility in public buildings.
- **ADAAG** for accessible design.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q: Explain the role of universal design principles and sensory considerations in the development of assistive technologies for persons with visual and hearing impairments. Support your answer with suitable examples and standards.**

#### Part (a) Universal Design Principles and Visual Considerations (7 Marks)

**Solution Outline:**

**1. Ron Mace's 7 Principles of Universal Design (4 marks):**
1. **Equitable Use** — Useful to people with diverse abilities (e.g., tactile pedestrian signals help both blind and sighted pedestrians confirm timing).
2. **Flexibility in Use** — Accommodates left/right-handed users; e.g., screen readers adjustable reading speed.
3. **Simple and Intuitive** — Pictographic elevator buttons understandable across languages and literacy levels.
4. **Perceptible Information** — Multi-modal feedback: ATMs with audio output + braille + high-contrast visuals.
5. **Tolerance for Error** — Reflow algorithms in word processors that preserve formatting.
6. **Low Physical Effort** — Automatic doors, lever handles (vs. round knobs).
7. **Size and Space for Approach** — Knee clearance under counters, wheelchair turning radius of 1500 mm.

**2. Visual Considerations (3 marks):**
- **Visual acuity:** Legible character size ≥ 5 mm at 400 mm.
- **Contrast ratio:** Minimum 4.5:1 for normal text (WCAG 2.1 AA); 7:1 for AAA.
- **Magnification devices:** 2× to 25× desktop CCTV magnifiers.
- **Tactile replacement:** Braille labels (Grade 1 or Grade 2), audio labels, haptic icons.

#### Part (b) Auditory, Cognitive, and Standards Compliance (7 Marks)

**Solution Outline:**

**1. Auditory Considerations (3 marks):**
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Alert signals must be **≥ 15 dB above ambient noise** to be reliably detected.
- **Frequency selection:** 500–3000 Hz range (speech-band) is most audible for most hearing losses.
- **Visual alerting alternatives:** Strobe lights, vibrating pagers, vibrating alarm clocks (for deaf users).
- **Loop induction systems** in public venues for hearing-aid users (T-coil coupling).

**2. Cognitive Considerations (2 marks):**
- **Simplified interfaces:** Maximum 3–4 options per menu (reduces working memory load).
- **Consistent layouts:** Same button positions across devices.
- **Error forgiveness:** Undo functions, "are you sure?" prompts, no penalty for exploration.

**3. Standards Compliance (2 marks):**
- **WCAG 2.1** (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines).
- **Section 508** of the US Rehabilitation Act.
- **EN 301 549** (European ICT accessibility).
- **RPwD Act 2016** (India) and **BIS IS 17802** series.
- **ANSI/HFES 100** for human factors of software.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Vague definitions:** Writing "anthropometry means body measurements" without specifying *static vs. dynamic vs. functional* loses 1 mark. Always classify the type.
> 2. **Skipping numerical work:** Even in theory questions, a quick worked example (e.g., calculating 95th percentile) impresses examiners and earns 1–2 extra marks.
> 3. **Ignoring Indian context:** Mentioning only ADA/ISO without referencing **RPwD Act 2016** and **Indian anthropometric data** is penalized. KTU examiners expect India-specific awareness.
> 4. **No linkage to user:** Saying "the device should be safe" without specifying **how** (e.g., "interface pressure < 32 mmHg prevents pressure ulcers") is incomplete.
> 5. **Mixing units:** Mixing mmHg with kPa, or kgf with N, in the same answer is a frequent error. **Stick to one system**, or show conversions.
> 6. **No iteration mention:** For human-centered design questions, omitting the **ISO 9241-210 iterative loop** loses 1 mark. Always state: "Design is iterative based on user feedback."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- [x] **Definition:** Human Factors = Application of human physical, cognitive, sensory, and psychological data to design.
- [x] **Seven Pillars:** Anthropometry, Biomechanics, Sensory, Cognitive, Environmental, Safety, Universal Design.
- [x] **Percentile Rule:** Design for **5th–95th percentile** → covers 90% of users; use **IAD** for Indian data.
- [x] **Key Percentile Formula:** $P_k = L + \left(\dfrac{\frac{kN}{100} - F}{f}\right) \cdot h$ — must memorize.
