- Select a motor with lower $K_t$ (higher torque per amp) — e.g., $K_t = 0.15$ N·m/A reduces $I_a$ to ~10.3 A.
- Use a battery with higher voltage (e.g., 48 V) to operate at lower current for the same power.
- Add a **gear reduction** to lower the required motor torque.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to reflect load to motor shaft:** Many students compute $T_L$ but then forget to divide by $N_g \cdot \eta_g$ when computing $T_m$. This leads to a 4–5 mark loss on Part B.
> 2. **Mixing up $\omega_{no\_load}$ and stall torque in the same equation:** The T–ω line is valid only when **operating below saturation**; never extrapolate beyond $T_{stall}$ or $\omega_{no\_load}$.
> 3. **Unit errors:** Students frequently forget to convert RPM → rad/s (multiply by $2\pi/60$).
> 4. **Ignoring $\eta_g$ in back-of-envelope calculations:** A $10\%$ efficiency loss can require a $20\%$ larger motor. KTU examiners explicitly test this.
> 5. **Not checking the thermal limit:** Always verify $I_a \leq I_{max}$ at the end of every motor-sizing problem.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Motor Selection (Module 3)**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Motor selection = matching motor T–ω curve to load T–ω curve under weight, volume, battery, and safety constraints.
>
> ✅ **Five Stages:** Load Profile → Speed Profile → Power Budget → Drive Topology → Motor Family.
>
> ✅ **Core Equations (must memorize):**
>   - $V = I_a R_a + K_e \omega$
>   - $T = K_t I_a$
>   - $P = T \omega$
>   - $T_{stall} = K_t V / R_a$
>   - $\omega_{no\_load} = V / K_e$
>   - $T_m = T_L \eta_g / N_g$
>   - $J_{eq} = J_{motor} + J_{load} / N_g^{\,2}$
>   - $P_{max}$ at $\omega = \omega_{no\_load}/2$ and $T = T_{stall}/2$.
>
> ✅ **Motor Family Quick-Select:**
>   - **BLDC** → exoskeleton, wheelchair, high-end prosthetics (best efficiency).
>   - **PMSM Servo** → robotic rehab arms, gait trainers.
>   - **Stepper** → low-speed precision (prosthetic fingers).
>   - **Pneumatic Muscle (PMA)** → soft exosuits, variable-stiffness joints.
>   - **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)** → safe HRI in physical human–robot interaction.
>
> ✅ **Key Design Trade-offs:**
>   - Weight vs. torque density.
>   - Backlash vs. gear ratio.
>   - Battery life vs. peak power.
>   - Cost vs. efficiency.
>
> ✅ **Rehabilitation-Specific Considerations:**
>   - Quiet operation (< 40 dB preferred for patient comfort).
>   - Low electromagnetic emission (pacemaker-safe).
>   - Biocompatible, sterilizable outer housing.
>   - Fail-safe braking (in case of power loss).
>   - ISO 13482 / IEC 60601 compliance for personal-care robots.
>
> ✅ **Common Units to Convert:**
>   - 1 RPM = $2\pi / 60$ rad/s ≈ 0.1047 rad/s
>   - 1 N·m = 1000 N·mm
>   - 1 Wh = 3600 J

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servo Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Servo Amplifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Servo Amplifier (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **servo amplifier** (also called a **servo drive** or **servo controller amplifier**) is a specialized electronic power amplifier that receives low-level command signals (typically ±10 V, 0–10 V, or pulse-width modulated logic) from a motion controller or microcontroller and converts them into high-current, high-voltage outputs capable of precisely driving electromechanical actuators such as **DC servo motors, brushless DC (BLDC) motors, AC servomotors, and proportional solenoids** used in rehabilitation devices.

In the specific context of **Rehabilitation Engineering (PEBRT752)**, servo amplifiers form the critical *actuation interface* between the bio-signal processing stage (EMG, EEG, joystick, sip-and-puff, or brain-computer interface) and the **powered orthosis, prosthetic limb, electric wheelchair, or robotic exoskeleton**. They are the muscles of the assistive device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Servo"?**
> The term "servo" originates from the Latin *servus* meaning "to serve." A servo system is a **closed-loop feedback control system** where the amplifier is the *muscle* and the feedback loop is the *nervous system* of the device. The amplifier alone, however, is just one stage of the larger servo system.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Car Steering System

Imagine you are driving a car:

| Real-World Element | Servo Amplifier Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Driver's hand on the steering wheel | Low-level command signal (±10 V) | **Input reference** |
| Hydraulic power steering pump | **Servo Amplifier** | **Boosts weak input to strong output** |
| Wheels turning (heavy load) | DC / BLDC Servo Motor | **Actuator (does the work)** |
| Driver's eyes seeing the road | Encoder / Resolver feedback | **Sensory feedback (closes the loop)** |
| Brain comparing position | PID Controller | **Error computation** |

> [!TIP]
> Just as a power steering system multiplies a *gentle* hand motion into a *forceful* wheel turn, a servo amplifier multiplies a *tiny* controller signal (milliwatts) into the *high-power* current (amperes) needed to move a prosthetic knee joint or a wheelchair motor against a human body load.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Emphasis)

- **Rated Output Power: 25 W – 5 kW** (typical range for rehabilitation devices)
- **Signal Input Range: ±10 V** (industry standard, or **0–10 V / 4–20 mA**)
- **Switching Frequency: 20 kHz – 50 kHz** (above audible range to avoid motor whine)
- **Current Loop Bandwidth: 1 kHz – 5 kHz** (speed of response)
- **Efficiency: 60 % (Linear) vs 90 – 98 % (PWM/Class-D)**
- **Operating Temperature: -10 °C to +50 °C** (medical grade)
- **Compliance: IEC 60601-1** (medical electrical equipment safety)

---

## 1.4 Block-Level Position in a Rehabilitation System

```
[Bio-signal / Switch] → [Signal Conditioner] → [Controller (PID / Fuzzy / Neural)]
         → [SERVO AMPLIFIER] → [Actuator (Motor / Solenoid)] → [Mechanical Linkage]
                                       ↑
                                  [Feedback Sensor]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The servo amplifier sits at the **power conditioning stage** — it does not make "decisions" (that is the controller's job), it simply executes the controller's commands with high fidelity and adequate power.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Role in Closed-Loop Control

A servo amplifier in rehabilitation engineering performs **four sequential functions**:

1. **Signal Reception & Scaling** – Accepts a low-power reference (e.g., from a microcontroller DAC output of 0–3.3 V) and scales it to the actuator's operating range.
2. **Power Amplification** – Uses active semiconductor devices (BJTs, MOSFETs, IGBTs) to deliver high current at the required voltage.
3. **Modulation** – For digital/switching amplifiers, encodes the analog command into a **PWM duty cycle** before amplification.
4. **Protection & Feedback Conditioning** – Monitors current, voltage, temperature, and commutates feedback signals.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Servo Amplifiers

### A. By Amplifier Class (Conduction Mode)

| Class | Conduction Angle | Efficiency | Application in Rehabilitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | 360° (full cycle) | ~25–30 % | Small signal pre-amplifier stage in EMG-driven prosthetics |
| **Class B** | 180° | ~50–70 % | Push-pull audio stages in hearing aids |
| **Class AB** | 180°–200° | ~50–60 % | Hybrid servo drives, small motor drivers |
| **Class C** | < 180° | ~70–80 % | RF/rarely used in rehab |
| **Class D (Switching)** | ON/OFF | **90–98 %** | **Most common** for powered wheelchair, prosthetic, exoskeleton drives |

> [!NOTE]
> **Class D amplifiers are dominant in modern rehabilitation robotics** because they generate minimal heat, have long battery life, and can deliver kW-level bursts for stair-climbing exoskeletons.

### B. By Modulation Technique

1. **Linear (Analog) Servo Amplifiers** – Use the active device in its linear region. Smooth output, but dissipate excess power as heat. Used in **proportional hydraulic valve drivers** for hospital beds and therapy chairs.
2. **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) Servo Amplifiers** – The workhorse of modern rehab. The output is a high-frequency square wave whose **duty cycle** is proportional to the command signal.
3. **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) / Sigma-Delta Amplifiers** – Used in **digital servo drives** with CAN bus or EtherCAT communication.
4. **Resonant (Quasi-Resonant) Amplifiers** – Soft-switching techniques that reduce EMI for sensitive EEG-driven devices.

### C. By Topology

- **Single-Ended (Half-Bridge) Amplifier** – Unidirectional drive.
- **H-Bridge (Full-Bridge) Amplifier** – Bidirectional; essential for **prosthetic knee flexion/extension** and **wheelchair forward/reverse**.
- **Three-Phase Bridge Inverter** – For BLDC and PMSM motors in **robotic gait trainers**.

---

## 2.3 The H-Bridge – The Heart of Bidirectional Servo Drives

The H-bridge is **mandatory knowledge for KTU** because most rehabilitation actuators require bidirectional motion (e.g., elbow flex and extend, knee flex and extend, gripper open and close).

**Configuration**: Four switching elements (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4 — typically MOSFETs or IGBTs) arranged in an "H" shape with the motor in the middle.

- **Q1 & Q4 ON** → Current flows left-to-right → Motor turns **forward**
- **Q2 & Q3 ON** → Current flows right-to-left → Motor turns **reverse**
- **Q1 & Q2 ON simultaneously** → **Shoot-through (DESTRUCTIVE short circuit)** — protection circuitry must prevent this!

---

## 2.4 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) – Detailed Analysis

In a PWM servo amplifier:
- The **command signal** $v_c(t)$ is compared with a **high-frequency triangular carrier** $v_{tri}(t)$.
- The comparator output is **HIGH** when $v_c > v_{tri}$ and **LOW** otherwise.
- The resulting square wave has a **duty cycle** proportional to the command.

**Key Equations:**

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_s}$$

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$$

$$V_{avg} = \left(\frac{v_c}{V_{tri,peak}}\right) \cdot V_{supply}$$

For a **bipolar PWM** (used in H-bridge servo drives):

$$V_{motor} = (2D - 1) \cdot V_{supply}$$

This gives a **bidirectional** average voltage from **+Vsupply to –Vsupply** — perfect for servo control.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Power dissipation | W | Heat sink sizing |
| 2 | $A_v = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ | Voltage Gain | dimensionless | Pre-amp stage |
| 3 | $A_i = \dfrac{I_{out}}{I_{in}}$ | Current Gain | dimensionless | Power stage |
| 4 | $A_p = A_v \cdot A_i$ | Power Gain | dimensionless | Overall amplifier |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency | % | Battery life in wheelchair |
| 6 | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_s}$ | PWM Duty Cycle | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Motor speed command |
| 7 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$ | Average DC output | V | DC motor drive |
| 8 | $V_m = (2D-1)V_s$ | Bipolar PWM output | V | Bidirectional servo |
| 9 | $T_m = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Motor torque | N·m | Prosthetic joint torque |
| 10 | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | Back EMF | V | Speed feedback |
| 11 | $V_s = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature equation | V | DC motor model |
| 12 | $I_{RMS} = I_{peak}\sqrt{D}$ | RMS current | A | Heatsink calc |
| 13 | $P_{switch} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_s I_{peak} (t_r + t_f) f_s$ | Switching loss | W | MOSFET losses |
| 14 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}$ | Natural frequency | rad/s | Servo bandwidth |
| 15 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{KJ}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless | Servo stability |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Hallucination Check:** All formulas above are derivable from first principles of electrical machines and power electronics. Memorize formulas 6, 7, 8, 11, and 13 — these are **highest weightage** in KTU examinations.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Servo Amplifier Role |
|---|---|
| **Powered Wheelchair** | Drives two 24 V / 5 A BLDC hub motors via dual H-bridge |
| **Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand** | Miniature Class-D amp drives 5–6 micro-gripper motors |
| **Robotic Exoskeleton (ReWalk, Ekso)** | 50 A peak PWM amplifier for knee/hip actuators |
| **Functional Electrical Stimulator (FES) Bike** | Current-controlled amplifier delivers stimulus pulses to quadriceps |
| **C-Pap / Ventilator** | BLDC blower motor amplifier with closed-loop pressure control |
| **Hospital Bed Actuator** | Linear amplifier with proportional valve for smooth motion |
| **BCI-Driven Wheelchair** | Low-noise PWM amplifier (EMI-shielded) for EEG signal environment |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Output Voltage of a Unipolar PWM Servo Amplifier

**Given:**
- DC bus supply voltage $V_s$ = **24 V**
- Switching period $T_s$ = **50 μs** (i.e., $f_s = 20$ kHz)
- ON time $T_{on}$ = **15 μs**
- Command signal is constant DC level

**Find:** Average voltage delivered to the DC servo motor armature.

### Step 1: State the governing relation
The output of a unipolar PWM is a rectangular pulse train of amplitude $V_s$ with duration $T_{on}$ in every period $T_s$.

### Step 2: Express the average mathematically
The average (DC) value of any periodic waveform is the integral over one period divided by the period.

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_s}\int_{0}^{T_s} v(t)\, dt$$

### Step 3: Split the integral
Since the waveform is $V_s$ for $T_{on}$ and $0$ for $(T_s - T_{on})$:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_s}\left[\int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_s\, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T_s} 0\, dt\right]$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the integrals
The first integral gives $V_s \cdot T_{on}$; the second gives 0.

$$V_{avg} = \frac{V_s \cdot T_{on}}{T_s}$$

### Step 5: Substitute the duty cycle definition
Using the definition $D = T_{on} / T_s$:

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$$

### Step 6: Plug numerical values
$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_s} = \frac{15\,\mu s}{50\,\mu s} = 0.30$$

$$V_{avg} = 0.30 \times 24\,V = 7.2\,V$$

### **Result:** The motor armature "sees" an average of **7.2 V**, which is **30 %** of full supply. The motor will rotate at **30 %** of its no-load speed (idealized, ignoring load and back-EMF).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bipolar PWM Output for H-Bridge Servo Drive

**Given:** A bipolar H-bridge switching scheme where:
- $Q_1$ and $Q_4$ ON for time $T_{on}$
- $Q_2$ and $Q_3$ ON for time $T_s - T_{on}$
- Supply voltage $V_s = 48\,V$ (typical for a powered wheelchair)

**Find:** Motor terminal voltage as a function of duty cycle.

### Step 1: Define the two switching states
- **State A (forward)**: $V_{motor} = +V_s$ for duration $T_{on}$
- **State B (reverse)**: $V_{motor} = -V_s$ for duration $(T_s - T_{on})$

### Step 2: Apply the averaging integral
$$V_{motor,avg} = \frac{1}{T_s}\left[\int_{0}^{T_{on}} (+V_s)\,dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T_s}(-V_s)\,dt\right]$$

### Step 3: Evaluate
$$V_{motor,avg} = \frac{V_s \cdot T_{on} - V_s(T_s - T_{on})}{T_s}$$

$$V_{motor,avg} = \frac{V_s(2T_{on} - T_s)}{T_s}$$

### Step 4: Express in terms of duty cycle
Using $D = T_{on} / T_s$, so $T_{on} = D \cdot T_s$:

$$V_{motor,avg} = \frac{V_s(2D\cdot T_s - T_s)}{T_s} = V_s(2D - 1)$$

### Step 5: Verify boundary conditions
- If $D = 1$ (always forward): $V_{motor,avg} = V_s = +48\,V$ ✓
- If $D = 0.5$ (50 % each): $V_{motor,avg} = 0\,V$ (motor stopped) ✓
- If $D = 0$ (always reverse): $V_{motor,avg} = -V_s = -48\,V$ ✓

### **Result:** 
$$V_{motor,avg} = (2D - 1) \cdot V_s$$

This is the **canonical equation of bipolar PWM** that KTU expects in derivations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Switching Power Loss in a Class-D Servo Amplifier

**Given:** A MOSFET H-bridge driving a 48 V / 10 A motor at $f_s = 20$ kHz, with rise time $t_r = 50$ ns and fall time $t_f = 60$ ns.

**Find:** Total switching power loss per MOSFET.

### Step 1: Energy loss during one turn-on transition
The voltage across the MOSFET falls from $V_s$ to 0 while current rises from 0 to $I_{peak}$. The instantaneous power is the product, integrated over the rise time:

$$E_{on} = \int_{0}^{t_r} V_s\left(1 - \frac{t}{t_r}\right) \cdot I_{peak}\left(\frac{t}{t_r}\right)dt$$

### Step 2: Simplify
Let $u = t / t_r$:

$$E_{on} = V_s \cdot I_{peak} \cdot t_r \int_{0}^{1}(1-u)u\,du = V_s \cdot I_{peak} \cdot t_r \cdot \frac{1}{6}$$

### Step 3: Similarly for turn-off
By symmetry, with $t_f$ replacing $t_r$:

$$E_{off} = \frac{1}{6} V_s \cdot I_{peak} \cdot t_f$$

### Step 4: Total energy per switching cycle
$$E_{total} = \frac{V_s I_{peak}}{6}(t_r + t_f)$$

### Step 5: Multiply by switching frequency to get power
$$P_{sw} = E_{total} \cdot f_s = \frac{1}{6} V_s I_{peak} (t_r + t_f) f_s$$

### Step 6: Plug in numbers
$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{6} \times 48 \times 10 \times (50 + 60) \times 10^{-9} \times 20 \times 10^{3}$$

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{6} \times 48 \times 10 \times 110 \times 10^{-9} \times 2 \times 10^{4}$$

$$P_{sw} = \frac{48 \times 10 \times 110 \times 2}{6} \times 10^{-9+4}$$

$$P_{sw} = \frac{105600}{6} \times 10^{-5} = 17600 \times 10^{-5} = 0.176\,W$$

### **Result:** Each MOSFET dissipates **0.176 W** due to switching. With four MOSFETs in the H-bridge, total switching loss = **0.704 W**, which is why a small heat sink is sufficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Switching losses scale **linearly with frequency**. This is why $f_s > 50$ kHz is rarely used — conduction losses also rise. The optimum is typically **20–30 kHz** (above audible, below excessive loss).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Current Loop Bandwidth Requirement for Prosthetic Hand

**Given:** A myoelectric prosthetic hand must close its gripper in **200 ms** to grasp a falling object. The system has a moment of inertia $J = 0.001\,kg \cdot m^2$ and motor + load stiffness $K = 100\,N/m$ (linearized).

**Find:** Required closed-loop bandwidth.

### Step 1: Natural frequency of the mechanical system
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{J}} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{0.001}} = \sqrt{10^5} = 316.2\,rad/s$$

### Step 2: Convert to Hz
$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{316.2}{6.283} = 50.3\,Hz$$

### Step 3: Bandwidth rule of thumb
For a critically damped second-order system, the closed-loop bandwidth must be **at least 5× the natural frequency** to achieve a 200 ms step response with < 5 % overshoot.

$$f_{BW,req} \geq 5 \times f_n = 5 \times 50.3 = 251.5\,Hz$$

### Step 4: Implication for servo amplifier
The amplifier's current loop must have a bandwidth of **≥ 250 Hz** (well within the typical 1–5 kHz capability).

### **Result:** A standard Class-D amplifier easily meets this; no special high-bandwidth design is required for this application.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting a Servo Amplifier for a Rehabilitation Device

| Design Parameter | Linear Amplifier | PWM (Class D) Amplifier | Recommended Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Battery life priority | ✗ Poor (low η) | ✓ Excellent (η > 90 %) | **PWM** |
| Smooth, ripple-free motion | ✓ Excellent | △ Acceptable (filter needed) | **Linear** |
| High peak torque needed | ✗ Limited by heat | ✓ Handles 10× rated briefly | **PWM** |
| EMI-sensitive environment (BCI) | ✓ Low EMI | ✗ High EMI (need shielding) | **Linear** |
| Compactness / weight | ✗ Heavy heatsink | ✓ Lightweight | **PWM** |
| Cost | △ Moderate | ✓ Low (mass-produced ICs) | **PWM** |
| Audible noise | ✓ Silent | ✗ 20 kHz whine possible | Both acceptable |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For powered prosthetics and wheelchairs, **always choose PWM (Class D) with a small LC output filter** to balance efficiency, weight, and EMI.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Servo Amplifier System with Closed-Loop Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CMD[Command Input\nplus minus 10 V or PWM] --> SIGCON[Signal\nConditioner]
    SIGCON --> ERRAMP[Error Amplifier\nPID Controller]
    FB[Feedback Sensor\nEncoder or Resolver] --> FBCOND[Feedback\nConditioner]
    FBCOND --> ERRAMP
    ERRAMP --> PWMGEN[PWM Modulator\nTriangle Wave Comparator]
    PWMSUP[DC Bus Supply\n24 V to 96 V] --> GATE[Gate Driver\nIsolation Stage]
    PWMGEN --> GATE
    GATE --> HBRIDGE[H-Bridge Power Stage\n4 MOSFETs or IGBTs]
    HBRIDGE --> MOTOR[DC or BLDC\nServo Motor]
    MOTROLOAD((Mechanical\nLoad Joint))
    MOTOR --- MOTROLOAD
    MOTOR --> FB
    HBRIDGE --> CURRSENSE[Current Sensor\nShunt or Hall Effect]
    CURRSENSE --> PROT[Protection\nLogic]
    PROT --> GATE
    HBRIDGE --> TEMPSENSE[Thermal Sensor\nNTC or PTC]
    TEMPSENSE --> PROT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Follow the control flow clockwise — *Command → Conditioner → PID → PWM → Gate Drive → H-Bridge → Motor → Load*. The **feedback path** (right side) closes the loop. The **protection path** (bottom) is a safety override that can disable the gate driver independently of the control path.

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Classic H-Bridge with Shoot-Through Protection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HBRIDGE_TOP[Top Rail plus V supply]
        Q1[Q1 High Side\nP Channel MOSFET]
        Q2[Q2 High Side\nP Channel MOSFET]
    end
    subgraph MOTOR_ARM[Motor Terminals]
        MA[Terminal A]
        MB[Terminal B]
    end
    subgraph HBRIDGE_BOT[Bottom Rail Ground]
        Q3[Q3 Low Side\nN Channel MOSFET]
        Q4[Q4 Low Side\nN Channel MOSFET]
    end
    Q1 --- MA
    Q2 --- MB
    MA --- Q3
    MB --- Q4
    Q1 -.Gate Drive PWM Phase A.-> CTRL[Microcontroller\nor DSP]
    Q2 -.Gate Drive PWM Phase B.-> CTRL
    Q3 -.Gate Drive PWM Phase A Bar.-> CTRL
    Q4 -.Gate Drive PWM Phase B Bar.-> CTRL
    CTRL --- DEADTIME[Dead Time\nGenerator 2 us]
    DEADTIME --- CTRL
    CTRL --- SHUTDOWN[Shutdown Input\nfrom Protection]
    SHUTDOWN --- CTRL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dead-time generator** inserts a 1–5 μs blanking interval between turning OFF one switch and turning ON the complementary switch in the same leg. This **prevents shoot-through** — a catastrophic short circuit across the DC bus that destroys MOSFETs in microseconds.

---

## 4.3 Functional State Diagram — PWM Servo Amplifier Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STANDBY
    STANDBY --> ENABLE : Control Input HIGH\nand No Fault
    ENABLE --> RUNNING : PWM Command Present
    RUNNING --> REGEN : Back EMF exceeds V supply\nRegenerative Braking
    REGEN --> RUNNING : Normal Operation Resumes
    RUNNING --> OVERCURRENT : Current exceeds limit
    OVERCURRENT --> SHUTDOWN : Latch Fault
    STANDBY --> OVERTEMP : Thermal Sensor trips
    OVERTEMP --> SHUTDOWN : Latch Fault
    RUNNING --> UNDERVOLT : V supply below threshold
    UNDERVOLT --> SHUTDOWN : Latch Fault
    SHUTDOWN --> STANDBY : Manual Reset\nafter 5 second cool down
    RUNNING --> [*] : Power Removed
```

> [!TIP]
> This state diagram mirrors the **FSM (Finite State Machine)** typically implemented in the firmware of modern digital servo amplifier ICs (e.g., TI DRV8301, ST L6234). Examiners love asking about fault transitions — note that **OVERCURRENT, OVERTEMP, UNDERVOLTAGE** are all *latched faults* requiring manual reset for **patient safety**.

---

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Multi-Axis Servo Amplifier for a Lower-Limb Exoskeleton

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BC[Main Battery\n48 V Lithium Ion] --> DCBUS[DC Bus\nwith Bulk Capacitor]
    DCBUS --> AXIS1[Axis 1 Amplifier\nHip Flexion PWM]
    DCBUS --> AXIS2[Axis 2 Amplifier\nHip Extension PWM]
    DCBUS --> AXIS3[Axis 3 Amplifier\nKnee Flexion PWM]
    DCBUS --> AXIS4[Axis 4 Amplifier\nKnee Extension PWM]
    AXIS1 --> M1[Hip Motor]
    AXIS2 --> M2[Hip Motor]
    AXIS3 --> M3[Knee Motor]
    AXIS4 --> M4[Knee Motor]
    CAN[CAN Bus or EtherCAT\nCommunication] --> MASTER[Master Motion\nController DSP or ARM]
    MASTER --> AXIS1
    MASTER --> AXIS2
    MASTER --> AXIS3
    MASTER --> AXIS4
    MASTER --> IMU[IMU Inertial\nMeasurement Unit]
    IMU --> MASTER
    EMG[EMG Electrode\nArray] --> MASTER
    MASTER --> FSR[Foot Pressure\nForce Sensitive Resistor]
    FSR --> MASTER
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice the **centralized power bus** (DC BUS) feeding **distributed amplifier modules** (one per joint axis). This topology is standard in the **Ekso GT, ReWalk, and HAL (Cyberdyne)** lower-limb exoskeletons. It simplifies cabling, allows hot-swapping of individual axis amplifiers, and enables per-joint safety shutdown.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Reference:**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short answer (define / state / list)
> - **Part B:** 14-mark descriptive with internal choice (a) + (b) = 7 + 7
> - All questions below are aligned to **CO3 / CO4** of the Rehabilitation Engineering syllabus.

---

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a servo amplifier. List its four main functional blocks.**

**Model Answer:**

A **servo amplifier** is an electronic power amplifier that converts a low-level command signal into a high-power output suitable for driving a servo motor, while incorporating closed-loop feedback to achieve precise position, velocity, or torque control.

**Four main functional blocks:**
1. **Signal Conditioner** – filters and scales the input command
2. **Controller (PID / PI)** – computes the error between command and feedback
3. **Power Output Stage** (H-Bridge / Inverter) – delivers current to the motor
4. **Feedback & Protection Block** – monitors current, voltage, temperature

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Compare linear and PWM (Class D) servo amplifiers on the basis of efficiency and EMI.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Linear Amplifier | PWM (Class-D) Amplifier |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency | 25 – 60 % | 90 – 98 % |
| EMI Generation | Low | High (requires LC filter and shielding) |
| Heat Dissipation | High (requires large heatsink) | Low (small heatsink) |
| Output Smoothness | Smooth, ripple-free | Requires filtering |

**Conclusion:** PWM is preferred in **battery-powered rehabilitation devices** for its high efficiency, while linear is preferred in **BCI / EEG-driven systems** where low EMI is critical.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]`

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** [7 marks] With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a closed-loop servo amplifier system used in a myoelectric prosthetic arm. Identify the role of each block.

**(b)** [7 marks] Derive the expression for the average output voltage of a bipolar PWM scheme used in an H-bridge servo amplifier. A bipolar PWM servo drive has a supply of 60 V and a switching frequency of 25 kHz. If the command signal produces an ON time of 18 μs in a period, calculate the average motor voltage.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### (a) Block Diagram & Working [7 marks]

**Diagram (describe in text — draw in exam):**

```
EMG Electrodes → Amplifier & Filter → Rectifier & Smoother
   → Threshold / Classifier → PID Controller → Servo Amplifier
   → DC Motor → Prosthetic Hand → Position Sensor (Encoder) → Feedback to PID
```

**Role of each block:**

1. **EMG Electrodes (Surface)** – Pick up muscle action potentials (50 μV – 5 mV).
2. **Amplifier & Bandpass Filter (20–500 Hz)** – Boosts and isolates the EMG signal from 50 Hz mains noise.
3. **Rectifier & Smoother (Envelope Detector)** – Converts AC EMG to a DC-like envelope proportional to muscle contraction.
4. **Threshold / Classifier** – Compares envelope to threshold; outputs ON/OFF or proportional command.
5. **PID Controller** – Computes error = (Command − Position feedback); outputs control voltage.
6. **Servo Amplifier (H-Bridge PWM)** – Power stage driving the hand's DC motor.
7. **DC Servo Motor** – Closes/opens the hand.
8. **Position Sensor (Encoder or Potentiometer)** – Reports actual grip position back to the PID.

**Working:** When the user flexes the residual limb muscle, EMG amplitude rises, the command exceeds threshold, PID drives the amplifier, motor moves, and feedback confirms position. The patient feels **proportional control** of grip force.

---

#### (b) Derivation & Numerical [7 marks]

**Derivation:** (See SECTION 3, Derivation 2 for full derivation. Recap the final equation here.)

The average motor voltage in a bipolar PWM H-bridge is:

$$V_{motor,avg} = (2D - 1) \cdot V_s$$

**Numerical calculation:**

**Given:** $V_s = 60\,V$, $f_s = 25\,kHz$ → $T_s = 1/f_s = 40\,\mu s$, $T_{on} = 18\,\mu s$.

**Step 1:** Compute duty cycle
$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_s} = \frac{18}{40} = 0.45$$

**Step 2:** Apply the bipolar PWM formula
$$V_{motor,avg} = (2 \times 0.45 - 1) \times 60 = (0.90 - 1) \times 60 = -0.10 \times 60 = -6\,V$$

**Result:** $V_{motor,avg} = -6\,V$ (i.e., **6 V in the reverse direction**). The motor will rotate in the *reverse* direction (e.g., prosthetic hand *opens* if forward was *close*).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do not confuse unipolar and bipolar formulas.** Unipolar: $V_{avg} = D V_s$ (range 0 to $V_s$). Bipolar: $V_m = (2D-1)V_s$ (range $-V_s$ to $+V_s$).
> - **Sign convention matters.** If the question does not specify, draw a current arrow and stick to it. Marking is deducted for ambiguous sign.
> - **Common error:** Students write $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$ for H-bridge and get 27 V instead of -6 V. **Both are wrong — the correct formula is bipolar.**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]`

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the working of an H-bridge servo amplifier. How is shoot-through prevented?

**(b)** [7 marks] A Class-D servo amplifier drives a 24 V / 5 A DC motor. The switching frequency is 20 kHz, rise time is 80 ns, and fall time is 90 ns. Calculate the switching power loss per MOSFET and the total H-bridge loss. Comment on the design implication.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### (a) H-Bridge Working & Shoot-Through Prevention [7 marks]

**Working of H-Bridge:**

An H-bridge consists of **four switching elements** (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4) arranged in an "H" configuration with the motor in the middle of the H.

| Mode | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Motor Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forward | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | Current flows left → right, motor spins **CW** |
| Reverse | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | Current flows right → left, motor spins **CCW** |
| Brake (slow) | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | Both motor terminals at $V_s$ — regenerative braking |
| Coast (fast) | OFF | OFF | OFF | OFF | Motor coasts freely |

**Shoot-Through Prevention:**

**Shoot-through** is the simultaneous ON-state of Q1 and Q3 (or Q2 and Q4) — this creates a direct short circuit from $V_s$ to ground through the MOSFETs, causing **catastrophic destruction** in microseconds.

**Prevention methods:**
1. **Hardware Dead-Time Insertion** – A dedicated dead-time generator IC inserts a 1–5 μs delay between turn-off of one switch and turn-on of its complement in the same leg.
2. **Bootstrap Gate Drive with RC Delay** – RC network slows down the rising edge of the gate signal, ensuring one switch is fully off before the other turns on.
3. **Logic Interlock** – A combinational logic circuit (e.g., AND-NAND gate) makes it *physically impossible* to enable both switches simultaneously.
4. **Software Dead Band** – Firmware ensures complementary PWM outputs never overlap in the high state.

---

#### (b) Switching Power Loss Numerical [7 marks]

**Given:** $V_s = 24\,V$, $I_{peak} = 5\,A$, $f_s = 20\,kHz$, $t_r = 80\,ns$, $t_f = 90\,ns$.

**Formula:**
$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{6} V_s I_{peak} (t_r + t_f) f_s$$

**Step 1: Substitute**
$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{6} \times 24 \times 5 \times (80 + 90) \times 10^{-9} \times 20 \times 10^{3}$$

**Step 2: Group terms**
$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{6} \times 24 \times 5 \times 170 \times 10^{-9} \times 2 \times 10^{4}$$

**Step 3: Simplify step-by-step**
$$24 \times 5 = 120$$
$$120 \times 170 = 20400$$
$$20400 \times 10^{-9} \times 2 \times 10^{4} = 20400 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} = 40800 \times 10^{-5} = 0.408\,W \cdot \mu s$$
$$\frac{0.408}{6} = 0.068\,W$$

**Step 4: Total H-bridge loss (4 MOSFETs)**
$$P_{total} = 4 \times 0.068 = 0.272\,W$$

**Step 5: Design implication comment**
A loss of only **0.27 W** means **no heatsink is required** — the MOSFETs can be cooled by PCB copper alone. This is ideal for a **compact, lightweight prosthetic hand** where size and weight are critical. The amplifier is highly efficient (> 95 %).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Unit conversion mistakes** are the #1 reason students lose marks in numericals. Always convert kHz → Hz and ns → s *before* substituting.
> - Students often forget to **multiply by 4** for the full H-bridge.
> - **Comment on design implication** is mandatory for full marks (1–2 marks reserved for engineering judgment in the 7-mark question).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Servo Amplifier Definition:** Power amplifier that drives a servo motor using a closed-loop feedback signal.
- [x] **Four Functional Blocks:** Signal Conditioner → PID Controller → Power Stage (H-Bridge) → Feedback & Protection.
- [x] **Class D (PWM) is dominant** in modern rehab due to **> 90 % efficiency** and small heat sink.
- [x] **H-Bridge provides bidirectional motion** — essential for prosthetic joints and wheelchair drive.
- [x] **Shoot-through** destroys MOSFETs — prevent with **dead-time** (1–5 μs).
- [x] **Unipolar PWM average:** $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$ → range 0 to $+V_s$.
- [x] **Bipolar PWM average:** $V_m = (2D-1) \cdot V_s$ → range $-V_s$ to $+V_s$ (**memorize this!**).
- [x] **Switching loss formula:** $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{6} V_s I_{peak} (t_r + t_f) f_s$ (per MOSFET).
- [x] **Typical switching frequency:** 20–50 kHz (above audible, below excessive loss).
- [x] **Standard command signal:** ±10 V analog (or 0–10 V / 4–20 mA / PWM digital).
- [x] **Motor torque:** $T = K_t I_a$; **Back EMF:** $E_b = K_e \omega$.
- [x] **Armature equation:** $V_s = E_b + I_a R_a$.
- [x] **Safety standards:** IEC 60601-1 (medical electrical equipment).
- [x] **Applications to recall:** Wheelchair, myoelectric hand, exoskeleton, FES, hospital bed, ventilator blower.
- [x] **PWM Regenerative braking:** When $E_b > V_s$, motor acts as generator — current flows back to battery (or dissipates in brake resistor).

> **End of Module 3 — Servo Amplifiers Notes**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, PEBRT752 Rehabilitation Engineering, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*
