topics.title: Microprocessor Control, Shared Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Microprocessor Control, Shared Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Microprocessor Control in Rehabilitation Engineering

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Microprocessor-Controlled Rehabilitation System** is an embedded electronic architecture in which a central processing unit (CPU/MPU) acquires real-time physiological, mechanical, and environmental sensor data, executes a control algorithm, and dispatches command signals to actuators (motors, valves, FES channels) in order to assist, augment, or restore human motor function.

The microprocessor is the **"digital brain"** of modern assistive devices such as powered wheelchairs, smart prosthetics, robotic exoskeletons, FES (Functional Electrical Stimulation) systems, and environmental controllers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The student must distinguish between **open-loop micro-controller systems** (e.g., simple TENS units) and **closed-loop sensor-driven systems** (e.g., a C-Leg knee with stance-phase detection). Both fall under the microprocessor umbrella, but the latter is the board-favourite for 14-mark questions.

### 🔧 Core Building Blocks of a Microprocessor-Controlled Rehab Device

| Block | Function | Rehab Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensors** | Capture user intent & environment | EMG electrodes, force sensors, encoders, IMU |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Amplify / filter / digitize (ADC) | Instrumentation amp, anti-alias filter |
| **CPU / MPU** | Decision-making & control law execution | ARM Cortex-M, dsPIC, ATmega |
| **Actuator Drivers** | Power electronics to drive motors / FES | H-bridge MOSFET, optocoupler-isolated FES |
| **Feedback Path** | Closes the loop for stability | Tachogenerator, position encoder |
| **HMI** | User interface | Joystick, push buttons, sip-and-puff |

---

## 1.2 Shared Control — The Heart of Human–Machine Cooperation

**Definition (Formal KTU 2024 phrasing):**
> **Shared Control** (also termed **Shared Autonomy**) is a hybrid control paradigm in which the human user and an intelligent automated controller *simultaneously* contribute to the control input of a rehabilitative device, with the contribution of each governed by an arbitration algorithm that adapts in real time to user intent, task context, and system state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Traded Control** → Either the human OR the robot is in charge at any instant (handover).
> - **Shared Control** → BOTH contribute at the SAME instant through a weighted blending law.

### 🏠 Real-World Intuition — The "Power-Steering Analogy"

Imagine driving an old car without power steering (full manual control) vs. a modern car with power steering (shared control):

| Component | Car Analogy | Rehab System Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Driver (you)** | User's residual muscle / EMG signal | User's voluntary intent |
| **Power-steering pump | Autonomous assistance controller | Microprocessor + actuator |
| **Steering wheel torque** | Sum of user + assist torque | Blended control output |
| **Road condition sensor** | Steering resistance feedback | Force / slip / obstacle sensor |

You still steer, but the **microprocessor assists** the moment torque demand exceeds safe thresholds. That blending is shared control.

### 📊 The Three Levels of Shared Autonomy (per KTU 2024)

1. **Low-Level (Sensor-Level) Sharing** — Sensors are shared; control law is one-sided.
2. **Mid-Level (Trajectory / Command Sharing)** — Both human and robot generate *position/velocity references*; arbitration blends them.
   $$\tau_{cmd} = \alpha\,\tau_{human} + (1-\alpha)\,\tau_{robot}$$
3. **High-Level (Goal / Intent Sharing)** — The robot infers user **goal**; the human supplies high-level intent (e.g., "go to kitchen"); the robot supplies low-level obstacle avoidance and path planning.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark answers, always **state the level** you are describing. Examiners award marks for correct taxonomy usage.

### 📐 Standard Metrics in Shared-Control Rehab Systems

- **Blending weight (α)** — typically **0 ≤ α ≤ 1**, dimensionless.
- **Control bandwidth** — typical rehab devices: **10 Hz to 1 kHz**.
- **Sensor sampling rate** — EMG: **1–2 kHz**; IMU: **100–500 Hz**; encoders: **1–10 kHz**.
- **End-effector force range** — upper-limb exoskeletons: **0 to 50 N** typical.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Microprocessor-Controlled Rehab System

The system executes a **Sense → Decide → Act → Sense** loop. The following bullet list breaks down each phase with the *why* and *how*:

1. **Sensing (Acquisition)**
   - *Why:* The microprocessor is blind without data; rehab devices operate on **disabilities**, so user input is weak and noisy.
   - *How:* Multiple sensors (EMG, force, position, IMU) are time-multiplexed through an **ADC** at a rate satisfying the **Nyquist criterion**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

2. **Conditioning & Feature Extraction**
   - *Why:* Raw EMG contains 50/60 Hz mains interference; force signals drift; encoders quantize.
   - *How:* Use **band-pass filters (20–500 Hz)**, **notch filters (50 Hz)**, **RMS rectification** for EMG, and **moving-average filters** for force.

3. **Decision (Control Algorithm)**
   - *Why:* Map noisy intent into safe, smooth actuator command.
   - *How:* Run **PID**, **state machine**, **fuzzy logic**, or **adaptive impedance control** in firmware (often at **1 kHz** loop rate).

4. **Actuation**
   - *Why:* Translate low-level digital commands into muscle/limb movement.
   - *How:* **PWM** signals to H-bridge drivers (motors) or **current-controlled FES** channels (functional electrical stimulation).

5. **Feedback (Closing the Loop)**
   - *Why:* Without feedback, drift and instability will injure the user.
   - *How:* Encoder/IMU/force sensor re-feeds the ADC; the cycle repeats within one sampling period **T**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **sampling period T** must be at least **10× smaller** than the fastest mechanical time constant of the assisted limb — KTU examiners frequently test this design rule.

---

## 2.2 Shared Control — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 The Arbitration (Blending) Function

The classical shared-control blending law is:

$$u(t) = \alpha(t)\,u_h(t) + \bigl(1-\alpha(t)\bigr)\,u_a(t)$$

where:

- $u(t)$ = final command sent to the actuator
- $u_h(t)$ = command derived from the **human** (EMG, joystick, sip-and-puff)
- $u_a(t)$ = command derived from the **autonomous** controller (obstacle avoidance, gravity compensation, balance)
- $\alpha(t)$ = **arbitration weight**, a function of context (often **0.5 ≤ α ≤ 1.0** in rehab — user retains primacy)

### 2.2.2 Adaptive α (Confidence-Based Arbitration)

A more advanced form used in modern smart wheelchairs and exoskeletons:

$$\alpha(t) = \frac{\phi_h(t)}{\phi_h(t) + \phi_a(t)}$$

where $\phi_h$ is human **intent confidence** (e.g., high when EMG SNR is good) and $\phi_a$ is autonomous **obstacle risk** (high when an obstacle is near).

### 2.2.3 Shared Impedance Control (Robotic Therapy)

For upper-limb therapy robots (e.g., InMotion ARM, Armeo Power):

$$F_{shared} = K_p\bigl(x_d - x_{blend}\bigr) + K_d\bigl(\dot{x}_d - \dot{x}_{blend}\bigr)$$

where the blended reference is $x_{blend} = \alpha\,x_h + (1-\alpha)\,x_a$.

---

## 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables & Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u = \alpha u_h + (1-\alpha) u_a$ | $\alpha$ dimensionless, $u$ in N·m or V | Linear blending (most cited) |
| 2 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | $f_s$ in Hz | ADC sampling design |
| 3 | $\tau_{motor} = J\ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} + K\theta$ | J: kg·m², B: N·m·s/rad, K: N·m/rad | Motor dynamics for prosthesis |
| 4 | $F_{FES} = I \cdot t_{stim} \cdot f_{stim}$ | I: mA, $t_{stim}$: µs, $f_{stim}$: Hz | FES output intensity |
| 5 | $G_{PID}(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | Gains dimensionless | Standard rehab control law |
| 6 | $Z(s) = \dfrac{F(s)}{V(s)} = M s + B + \dfrac{K}{s}$ | Impedance control | Robotic therapy arm |
| 7 | $\alpha(t) = \dfrac{\phi_h}{\phi_h + \phi_a}$ | Confidence in [0,1] | Adaptive arbitration |
| 8 | $e(t) = x_d(t) - x(t)$ | Position error in m | Tracking loop |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{User effort reduction}}{\text{Baseline effort}}$ | Dimensionless | Exoskeleton efficiency metric |
| 10 | $\theta_{enc} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N_{counts}}{N_{pulses/rev}}$ | Counts per revolution | Encoder resolution check |

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this in KTU exams:** Always quote the formula, then define EACH variable with its unit, then state one assumption. This single habit typically earns full marks on derivations.

### 🌎 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Powered wheelchairs** → shared control prevents collisions while respecting user steering intent.
- **Smart prosthetics** → microprocessors detect gait phase; shared control adjusts ankle stiffness.
- **Exoskeletons (ReWalk, Ekso)** → the user supplies balance intent; the robot supplies joint torque.
- **FES-cycling (e.g., Parastep)** → closed-loop microprocessor tracks cadence; controller delivers biphasic pulses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Microprocessor-Controlled DC Motor for a Prosthetic Hand

A standard derivation KTU examiners love — connects **microprocessor control** with **rehab actuator dynamics**.

### Step 1 — Electrical Equation (Armature Circuit)

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law across the armature:

$$V_a(t) = R_a i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + e_b(t)$$

- $V_a$ = applied armature voltage (V)
- $R_a$ = armature resistance (Ω)
- $L_a$ = armature inductance (H)
- $i_a$ = armature current (A)
- $e_b$ = back-EMF (V)

**Conversion logic:** $e_b$ opposes the applied voltage and is proportional to angular velocity.

### Step 2 — Back-EMF Relationship

$$e_b(t) = K_b \,\dot{\theta}(t)$$

- $K_b$ = back-EMF constant (V·s/rad)
- $\dot{\theta}$ = angular velocity (rad/s)

### Step 3 — Mechanical Equation (Newton's 2nd Law for Rotation)

$$J\ddot{\theta}(t) + B\dot{\theta}(t) = \tau_m(t) - \tau_L(t)$$

- $J$ = rotor + load inertia (kg·m²)
- $B$ = viscous friction coefficient (N·m·s/rad)
- $\tau_m = K_t\,i_a$ = motor torque
- $\tau_L$ = load torque (prosthetic hand gripping force equivalent)

### Step 4 — Combine Electrical and Mechanical Domains

Take the Laplace transform of both equations (zero initial conditions):

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + K_b s \Theta(s)$$

$$(J s^2 + B s)\Theta(s) = K_t I_a(s) - \tau_L(s)$$

### Step 5 — Eliminate $I_a(s)$ to Get the Open-Loop Transfer Function

From the mechanical equation:

$$I_a(s) = \frac{(J s^2 + B s)\Theta(s) + \tau_L(s)}{K_t}$$

Substituting into the electrical equation:

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s)\left[\frac{(J s^2 + B s)\Theta(s) + \tau_L(s)}{K_t}\right] + K_b s \Theta(s)$$

**Conversion logic:** Multiply out to express $\Theta(s)$ in terms of $V_a(s)$ and $\tau_L(s)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_t V_a(s) &= (R_a + L_a s)(J s^2 + B s)\Theta(s) + (R_a + L_a s)\tau_L(s) + K_t K_b s\,\Theta(s) \\[4pt]
\Theta(s) &= \frac{K_t\,V_a(s) - (R_a + L_a s)\tau_L(s)}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s^2 + B s) + K_t K_b s}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Standard Form (with $\tau_L = 0$ for unloaded case)

$$G(s) = \frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s\bigl[(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b\bigr]}$$

This third-order transfer function is the **plant** that the microprocessor-based PID controller will close the loop on.

### Step 7 — Adding a Microprocessor PID Controller

$$U(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) E(s)$$

where the tracking error is $E(s) = \Theta_{ref}(s) - \Theta(s)$.

Closed-loop transfer function:

$$T(s) = \frac{G_c(s) G(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G(s)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU trick:** Examiners often ask for the **stability condition** from $1 + G_c(s)G(s) = 0$. State the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** and find the $K_p$ range that keeps all roots in the left-half plane. This is a guaranteed 3–4 marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Linear Shared-Control Blending Law Applied to a Smart Wheelchair

### Problem Setup

A smart wheelchair must navigate toward a goal $(x_g, y_g)$ while the user supplies a joystick command $(x_u, y_u)$. The autonomous controller has computed a safe path waypoint $(x_a, y_a)$.

### Step 1 — Define the Blended Reference Point

$$x_{cmd} = \alpha x_u + (1 - \alpha) x_a, \qquad y_{cmd} = \alpha y_u + (1 - \alpha) y_a$$

### Step 2 — Choose an Adaptive α Based on Obstacle Proximity

Define obstacle confidence $\phi_a = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{k(d - d_0)}}$ (a sigmoid: high near an obstacle, low when far). Let human confidence $\phi_h = 1 - \sigma_{EMG}$ (high when EMG signal is clean).

$$\alpha = \frac{\phi_h}{\phi_h + \phi_a} \in [0, 1]$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Resulting Velocity Command

$$v_{cmd} = K_v \cdot \sqrt{(x_{cmd} - x_{current})^2 + (y_{cmd} - y_{current})^2}$$

### Step 4 — Demonstrate Edge Cases

| Case | $\phi_h$ | $\phi_a$ | $\alpha$ | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Open hallway, calm user | 0.9 | 0.1 | **0.90** | User dominant |
| Obstacle detected, weak user | 0.2 | 0.9 | **0.18** | Robot dominant |
| Equal confidence | 0.5 | 0.5 | **0.50** | Equal blend |
| Emergency (obstacle very close) | 0.0 | 1.0 | **0.00** | Robot takes over |

> [!NOTE]
> This sigmoid-based arbitration is a standard KTU 14-mark derivation — easy to draw, easy to mark.

---

## 3.3 📊 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Control Paradigms

| Property | Open-Loop μP | Closed-Loop μP | Full Autonomous | Shared Control | Traded Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **User in loop?** | Indirect | Indirect | No | **Yes, simultaneously** | Yes, but alternating |
| **Sensor feedback** | None | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Suitable for severe disability** | Limited | Moderate | Yes | **Yes (best)** | Partial |
| **Risk of override injury** | Low | Low | High (no veto) | **Low (user has α)** | Medium |
| **Computational load** | Very low | Moderate | High | High | Moderate |
| **KTU example device** | TENS unit | C-Leg | Auto-balancer walker | **Smart wheelchair, Armeo** | BrainGate switch |
| **Mark weight in 14-mark Q** | 2 marks | 5 marks | 6 marks | **14 marks (full Q)** | 7 marks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Microprocessor-Controlled Rehabilitation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Physiological Signal EMG EEG EOG] --> B[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]
    B --> C[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    C --> D[Microprocessor CPU Control Algorithm]
    D --> E[DAC or PWM Generator]
    E --> F[Actuator Driver H-Bridge or FES Frontend]
    F --> G[Rehabilitation Actuator Motor FES or Pneumatic Muscle]
    G --> H[Human Limb or Prosthesis]
    H --> I[Feedback Sensors Encoder Force IMU]
    I --> C
    D --> J[HMI Display Buzzer Joystick]
    J --> D
    style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style D fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style G fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    style H fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The signal flow is **clockwise**. The user's body *IS* part of the feedback loop — a defining feature of rehab control, unlike industrial robots.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Three-Level Shared Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SL1[LEVEL 1 SENSOR SHARING]
        S1[EMG Sensor] --> S2[Force Sensor] --> S3[IMU and Vision]
    end
    subgraph SL2[LEVEL 2 COMMAND AND TRAJECTORY SHARING]
        H1[User Intent Decoder] --> AB[Arbitration Blending Module alpha]
        R1[Autonomous Planner Obstacle Avoidance] --> AB
    end
    subgraph SL3[LEVEL 3 GOAL AND INTENT SHARING]
        G1[User Goal Kitchen] --> G2[Robot Sub Goal Path Plan]
        G2 --> R1
    end
    AB --> ACT[Blended Actuator Command]
    ACT --> EX[Exoskeleton or Wheelchair Motor]
    EX --> FB[Feedback to Sensors]
    FB --> S1
    style AB fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
    style ACT fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style EX fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The arbitration module (highlighted) is the *only* path to the actuator. This visually emphasizes that the user NEVER loses veto power (α ≥ 0).

---

## 4.3 State Machine — Traded vs Shared Control Handover

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UserOnly
    UserOnly --> Shared : User intent weak OR obstacle near
    Shared --> UserOnly : Confidence restored
    Shared --> RobotOnly : Emergency stop
    RobotOnly --> Shared : Emergency cleared
    RobotOnly --> UserOnly : User explicit override button
    UserOnly --> RobotOnly : User releases control
    note right of Shared : ALPHA is 0 to 1 continuously updated
    note right of RobotOnly : ALPHA is forced to 0
    note left of UserOnly : ALPHA is forced to 1
```

> **Why this matters in KTU:** Examiners frequently test the difference between *traded* (state transitions) and *shared* (continuous blending). This state machine makes the distinction visual and unforgettable.

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Smart Wheelchair Shared-Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    USR[User Inputs Joystick SipPuff HeadArray] --> INT[Intent Decoder]
    CAM[Camera and LIDAR] --> OBS[Obstacle Detector]
    IMU[Wheelchair IMU] --> ST[State Estimator]
    INT --> ARB[Adaptive Arbitrator alpha]
    OBS --> ARB
    ST --> ARB
    ARB --> CMD[Blended Velocity Command]
    CMD --> DRV[Motor Driver PWM]
    DRV --> WH[Drive Wheels]
    WH --> ST
    OBS -.emergency.-> STO[Emergency Stop]
    USR -.panic button.-> STO
    style ARB fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412
    style STO fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 2 Mark Conceptual Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
>
> **Q1.** Define **shared control** as applied to a rehabilitation robotic system. State ONE device where it is commonly implemented.
>
> **Model Answer (2 marks):**
> Shared control is a hybrid control strategy in which the human user and an autonomous microprocessor-based controller contribute *simultaneously* to the final actuator command, with their relative influence governed by a real-time arbitration weight $\alpha$. (1½ marks)
> It is commonly implemented in **smart powered wheelchairs** (1 mark).
>
> ---
>
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
>
> **Q2.** List **any four** building blocks of a microprocessor-based rehabilitation control system.
>
> **Model Answer (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Sensors** (EMG, force, encoder)
> 2. **Signal conditioner & ADC**
> 3. **Microprocessor / CPU**
> 4. **Actuator driver & actuator** (motor / FES)
> *(Optional: feedback path, HMI)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **⚠️ KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Module questions carry **14 marks** with a compulsory internal choice (a) OR (b). Each sub-part is typically **7 marks**.

### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

> **A(a).** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **microprocessor-controlled prosthetic hand**. Describe the role of each block in restoring grasp function. **(7 marks)**
>
> **A(b).** Derive the **transfer function** of a DC motor driven by a microprocessor-based PID controller for a prosthetic wrist joint. State the **Nyquist sampling requirement** for the position encoder. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer A(a) — 7 marks

| Block | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **EMG electrode pair** | Detects residual muscle activity; provides user intent. | 1 |
| 2. **Instrumentation amplifier** | Amplifies mV-level EMG to ~V-level; rejects common-mode noise. | 1 |
| 3. **Band-pass + notch filter (20–500 Hz; 50 Hz notch)** | Removes motion artefact and mains hum. | 1 |
| 4. **RMS rectifier + smoothing** | Extracts EMG envelope proportional to contraction force. | 1 |
| 5. **ADC + Microprocessor (dsPIC/ARM)** | Samples EMG at ≥ 1 kHz; runs pattern-recognition (e.g., LDA) to classify grasp type. | 1 |
| 6. **Motor driver (H-bridge MOSFET)** | Receives PWM from μP; drives 6–12 V DC motor or BLDC. | 1 |
| 7. **Encoder + force sensor (feedback)** | Provides closed-loop position & grip force; protects against slip. | 1 |

**Closing statement (½ mark):** The closed-loop architecture adapts to object stiffness and prevents slip — a key rehab-engineering advantage.

#### ✅ Model Answer A(b) — 7 marks

**(i) Plant derivation — 4 marks**

From Section 3.1 we established:

$$G(s) = \frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s\bigl[(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b\bigr]}$$

Show the substitution steps:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_a(s) &= (R_a + L_a s)I_a(s) + K_b s\,\Theta(s) \\
(J s^2 + B s)\Theta(s) &= K_t I_a(s) \\
\Rightarrow \Theta(s) &= \frac{K_t V_a(s) - (R_a + L_a s)\tau_L(s)}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s^2 + B s) + K_t K_b s} \\
G(s) &= \frac{K_t}{s\bigl[(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b\bigr]} \quad (\tau_L = 0)
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) PID closure — 2 marks**

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

Closed-loop:

$$T(s) = \frac{G_c(s)G(s)}{1 + G_c(s)G(s)}$$

**(iii) Nyquist — 1 mark**

$$f_{s,encoder} \geq 2 f_{max,signal}$$

For typical wrist motion $f_{max} \approx 5$ Hz, so $f_s \geq 10$ Hz. In practice **≥ 100 Hz** is used to leave a **10× safety margin** and capture transients.

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

> **B(a).** Explain the **three levels of shared autonomy** in rehabilitation robotics. Give **one clinical example** for each level. **(7 marks)**
>
> **B(b).** A smart wheelchair uses a **linear blending shared-control law** with α = 0.7. The user commands a velocity of 1.2 m/s; the autonomous controller commands 0.4 m/s. Compute the **blended command** and **interpret the result** in terms of user primacy and safety. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer B(a) — 7 marks

1. **Sensor-Level Sharing (2 marks)** — Sensors are shared between user and device; the control is uni-directional. *Example:* Shared EMG electrodes in a hybrid exoskeleton–FES system.

2. **Command/Trajectory-Level Sharing (2 marks)** — Both generate references; arbitration blends them. *Example:* Smart wheelchair (Kengoro) where the user steers and the robot avoids obstacles.

3. **Goal/Intent-Level Sharing (3 marks)** — Robot infers high-level goal from user input (gaze, EEG) and executes sub-tasks autonomously. *Example:* **BrainGate / BCI wheelchair** where the user thinks "kitchen" and the robot plans the path, opens doors, avoids humans.

#### ✅ Model Answer B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the blending law.

$$u = \alpha\,u_h + (1-\alpha)\,u_a$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute.

$$u = (0.7)(1.2) + (1 - 0.7)(0.4)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Evaluate.

$$u = 0.84 + 0.12 = 0.96 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Interpret.
- Since α = 0.7 > 0.5, **user intent is dominant** (60% stronger weight in the blend).
- The autonomous controller has **reduced** the user's demand from 1.2 → 0.96 m/s — likely because of an obstacle or speed-limit context.
- This preserves **user primacy** (the wheelchair respects intent) while **enforcing safety** (it does not blindly obey). This is the essence of shared control.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "shared" with "traded" control** — examiners deduct 2–3 marks if you use the words interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting to define α's range** — always state $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ when writing the blending law.
> 3. **Skipping the unit on a derived quantity** — e.g., writing "0.96" without "m/s" loses ½ mark.
> 4. **Drawing the block diagram without arrows or labels** — a 7-mark diagram question demands a labelled, directional, functional block diagram.
> 5. **Forgetting the Nyquist criterion** in ADC/encoder problems — almost always asked.
> 6. **Writing "μP" or "microprocessor" without naming a specific example** (e.g., dsPIC33, ARM Cortex-M4) — KTU expects engineering specificity.
> 7. **Treating FES as a "muscle"** — it is an *electrode interface*; the muscle is the actuator. Examiners flag this confusion.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Microprocessor control** = sensor → ADC → CPU → DAC/PWM → actuator → feedback loop.
- ✅ The **human body is part of the feedback loop** in rehab systems (a unique feature).
- ✅ **Shared control** = simultaneous contribution; **traded control** = alternating handover.
- ✅ Blending law: $u = \alpha u_h + (1-\alpha) u_a$, with $0 \le \alpha \le 1$.
- ✅ **Adaptive α** uses sigmoid of obstacle distance and EMG confidence.
- ✅ **Nyquist sampling:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$, with a **10× practical safety margin**.
- ✅ **Three levels of shared autonomy:** sensor → command/trajectory → goal/intent.
- ✅ Standard control law: $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ (PID).
- ✅ Always quote **units** on every variable; always state **assumptions**.
- ✅ Real-world devices: **C-Leg, ReWalk, Armeo, Kengoro, smart wheelchairs, Parastep FES cycle**.
- ✅ **Sigmoid arbitration** is the most common adaptive α in KTU problems.
- ✅ For 14-mark answers: provide **diagram + derivation + numerical example + clinical interpretation**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault-Tolerant Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fault-Tolerant Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fault-Tolerant Control (FTC)** is a branch of advanced control engineering concerned with the design of control systems that retain acceptable stability and performance levels even when **faults** (abnormal operating conditions) occur in the plant, sensors, actuators, or controllers themselves.

In the context of **Rehabilitation Engineering**, FTC refers to control architectures used in **assistive devices, robotic prostheses, orthoses, functional electrical stimulators (FES), powered wheelchairs, and exoskeletons** that must continue to function safely in the presence of component failures — because failure can directly harm a vulnerable human user.

A **fault** is formally distinguished from a **failure**:
- A **fault** is an *unpermitted deviation* of at least one characteristic property (parameter or variable) of the system from its nominal condition.
- A **failure** is a *permanent interruption* of a system's ability to perform a required function under specified operating conditions.

The mathematical definition of a fault $f(t)$ acting on a state-space system is:

$$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t) + E_f f(t)$$
$$y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t) + F_f f(t)$$

where $E_f$ and $F_f$ are the **fault distribution matrices** mapping the fault into the state and output channels respectively.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Modern Airplane**
> Think of Fault-Tolerant Control like a **commercial aircraft's redundant flight computer system**. A modern jet (e.g., Boeing 787) has **triple or quadruple-redundant flight computers** that vote on every control decision. If one computer "thinks" differently (a fault), the others outvote it. The plane keeps flying safely. In rehabilitation robotics, a powered prosthetic leg must keep a person standing even if one torque sensor on the knee fails — the controller must "reconfigure" on the fly and limp along gracefully until service.

> Another helpful analogy: a **car's airbag system** is *passive* (it reacts the same way whether you crash at 30 km/h or 80 km/h), while **electronic stability control (ESC)** is *active* — it senses a slide and *reconfigures* braking per wheel. Both are FTC, but at different sophistication levels.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics in FTC

The standard quantitative metrics used to benchmark an FTC system in KTU-level problems are:

- **Fault Detectability Time (FDT)** — time elapsed between fault occurrence and detection. Expressed in **seconds (s)**. Typical target: **< 100 ms** for safety-critical rehab devices.
- **False Alarm Rate (FAR)** — number of false fault detections per unit time. Unit: **events/hour**.
- **Missed Detection Rate (MDR)** — probability of failing to detect an actual fault. Dimensionless. Target: **< 0.01** for medical devices per **IEC 60601-1**.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** — average operational time before a fault. Unit: **hours (h)**.
- **Recovery Time** — duration for the FTC to restore acceptable performance post-fault. Unit: **seconds (s)**.
- **Graceful Degradation Factor (GDF)** — ratio of post-fault performance to nominal performance. Range: **[0, 1]**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must be able to differentiate between **Passive FTC (PFTC)** and **Active FTC (AFTC)**, explain **Fault Detection and Isolation (FDI)** schemes, and apply the concept of **reconfiguration** to rehabilitation devices.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Faults in Rehabilitation Devices

Faults are classified along multiple axes. In KTU 2024 questions, students are often asked to "classify the faults" — so a clear taxonomy is essential.

**A. By Location:**
- **Sensor faults** — e.g., potentiometer in a prosthetic knee gives a noisy/stuck reading. Mathematically modelled as an additive bias $f_s(t)$ on the output: $y(t) = Cx(t) + f_s(t)$.
- **Actuator faults** — e.g., partial loss of motor torque in a powered exoskeleton. Modelled as a loss of effectiveness factor $\gamma \in (0, 1]$ on the input: $u_{eff}(t) = \gamma \, u(t)$.
- **Process (component) faults** — e.g., mechanical wear in a wheelchair's gear train changing the plant dynamics from $A$ to $A + \Delta A$.

**B. By Time Behaviour:**
- **Abrupt (step) fault** — sudden appearance, $f(t) = f_0 \cdot \mathbb{1}(t \geq t_f)$.
- **Incipient (drift) fault** — slow growth, $f(t) = \alpha (t - t_f) \mathbb{1}(t \geq t_f)$, $\alpha$ small. **Hardest to detect.**
- **Intermittent fault** — appears and disappears cyclically (e.g., loose connector).

**C. By Magnitude:**
- **Hard fault** — total loss of function (motor burnout).
- **Soft fault** — partial degradation (sensor drift by 5%).

### 2.2 The Two Pillars: PFTC vs AFTC

**2.2.1 Passive Fault-Tolerant Control (PFTC)**

PFTC uses a **fixed (non-reconfiguring) controller** designed *a priori* to be robust against a *known* set of faults. It does not perform fault detection. The controller is essentially a **robust $H_\infty$** or **sliding-mode** controller.

- **Advantages:** Fast response (no detection latency), simple architecture, no false-alarm problem.
- **Disadvantages:** Conservative performance under nominal (no-fault) operation, limited to known fault classes.

**2.2.2 Active Fault-Tolerant Control (AFTC)**

AFTC is a **two-stage closed loop**:
- **Stage 1 — FDI Module:** Continuously monitors the system using observers, parity checks, or AI-based classifiers; generates a **residual** $r(t)$ and isolates which fault has occurred.
- **Stage 2 — Reconfiguration Module:** Switches or adapts the controller parameters in real time.

- **Advantages:** Near-nominal performance under healthy operation; can handle a wider fault class.
- **Disadvantages:** Detection delay, false alarms, computational cost.

### 2.3 Fault Detection and Isolation (FDI) — The Core Engine

The most common **model-based FDI** approach uses an **observer (Luenberger or Kalman)** running in parallel with the plant. The observer's output $\hat{y}(t)$ is compared with the actual output $y(t)$ to form a **residual**:

$$r(t) = y(t) - \hat{y}(t)$$

Under healthy operation, $r(t) \approx 0$ (only measurement noise). When a fault occurs, $r(t)$ deviates from zero. A **decision logic** unit (threshold test) then declares:

$$\| r(t) \| > T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FAULT DECLARED}$$

where $T$ is the **detection threshold**, often chosen statistically from noise covariance.

### 2.4 Reconfiguration Strategies

Once a fault is isolated, the controller is reconfigured. Standard KTU-recognised strategies include:

- **Controller Switching** — a bank of pre-designed controllers $\{K_1, K_2, \ldots, K_m\}$ is available; the matching $K_i$ is selected based on FDI decision.
- **Control Law Re-design** — online recomputation of $K$ (e.g., via Linear Matrix Inequality (LMI) on-line solvers).
- **Gain Scheduling** — controller gains are interpolated from a look-up table indexed by estimated fault magnitude.
- **Virtual Sensor / Actuator** — for sensor loss, a software sensor is created from remaining signals; for actuator loss, the control input is redistributed to healthy actuators.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---------|--------------------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | Faulty state model | $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu + E_f f$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $u \in \mathbb{R}^m$ | $E_f$ = fault input matrix |
| 2 | Faulty output model | $y = Cx + Du + F_f f$ | $y \in \mathbb{R}^p$ | $F_f$ = fault output matrix |
| 3 | Actuator effectiveness loss | $u_{eff} = \gamma u$, $\gamma \in (0, 1]$ | dimensionless $\gamma$ | $\gamma = 1$ → healthy |
| 4 | Sensor bias fault | $y_s = y + f_s$ | $f_s \in \mathbb{R}^p$ | additive bias |
| 5 | Residual signal | $r(t) = y(t) - \hat{y}(t)$ | $r \in \mathbb{R}^p$ | zero-mean under healthy |
| 6 | Threshold rule | $\vert r(t) \vert > T$ | $T > 0$ (scalar or vector) | common logic test |
| 7 | Luenberger observer | $\dot{\hat{x}} = A\hat{x} + Bu + L(y - C\hat{x})$ | $L$ = observer gain | drives $r \to 0$ in healthy case |
| 8 | Observer error dynamics | $\dot{e} = (A - LC)e + E_f f$ | $e = x - \hat{x}$ | $e$ responds to $f$ |
| 9 | Steady-state residual gain | $\hat{r}_{ss} = -C(A - LC)^{-1} E_f f$ | matrix expression | used in isolation logic |
| 10 | Kalman filter gain | $L = PC^T R^{-1}$ | $P$ = error covariance, $R$ = noise cov | optimal under Gaussian noise |
| 11 | $H_\infty$ PFTC objective | $\min_K \sup_{\omega} \vert T_{zr}(j\omega) \vert$ | $T_{zr}$ = closed-loop transfer fn | robust to model uncertainty |
| 12 | Graceful Degradation Factor | $\text{GDF} = \dfrac{J_{post}}{J_{nominal}}$ | $J$ = performance index | $\text{GDF} \in [0, 1]$ |
| 13 | MTTF (exponential dist.) | $\text{MTTF} = 1 / \lambda$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate (h$^{-1}$) | for non-repairable systems |
| 14 | False alarm rate | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{N_{fa}}{\Delta t}$ | events/hour | $N_{fa}$ = false alarms in $\Delta t$ |
| 15 | Reconfigured control law | $u_{new} = K_{new} x + r_{reconfig}$ | $K_{new}$, $r_{reconfig}$ | depends on fault class |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Every medical device approved for clinical use must demonstrate fault tolerance per **ISO 13485** and **IEC 60601-1**. The FDA's 2023 guidance on "Predetermined Change Control Plans" for AI-enabled medical devices explicitly requires documented FTC strategies. KTU examiners love to link these industry standards to your answer for "extra mile" credit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Observer-Based Residual Generation

Consider a linear time-invariant (LTI) plant with a possible actuator fault:

$$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + B u(t) + E_f f_a(t)$$
$$y(t) = Cx(t)$$

where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$, $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$, and $E_f \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times q}$ maps the actuator fault vector $f_a(t) \in \mathbb{R}^q$ into the state.

**Step 1 — Design the Luenberger Observer.**

The standard observer has the form:

$$\dot{\hat{x}}(t) = A\hat{x}(t) + B u(t) + L \bigl( y(t) - C\hat{x}(t) \bigr)$$

Here $L \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ is the **observer gain matrix**, chosen such that $(A - LC)$ is Hurwitz (all eigenvalues strictly in the left half-plane), guaranteeing $\hat{x}(t) \to x(t)$ as $t \to \infty$ in the absence of a fault.

**Step 2 — Derive the State Estimation Error Dynamics.**

Define the estimation error $e(t) = x(t) - \hat{x}(t)$. Subtract the observer equation from the plant equation:

$$\dot{x} - \dot{\hat{x}} = A(x - \hat{x}) + E_f f_a - L(y - C\hat{x})$$

Recognising that $y - C\hat{x} = C(x - \hat{x}) = Ce$, we obtain:

$$\dot{e}(t) = (A - LC)\, e(t) + E_f\, f_a(t)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Error in the Laplace Domain.**

Taking the Laplace transform (assuming zero initial conditions for clarity):

$$sE(s) = (A - LC) E(s) + E_f F_a(s)$$

$$E(s) = \bigl[ sI - (A - LC) \bigr]^{-1} E_f F_a(s)$$

**Step 4 — Express the Residual in the Laplace Domain.**

The residual is defined as $r(t) = y(t) - \hat{y}(t)$ with $\hat{y}(t) = C\hat{x}(t)$. In the Laplace domain:

$$R(s) = C E(s) = C \bigl[ sI - (A - LC) \bigr]^{-1} E_f F_a(s)$$

Define the **residual transfer matrix** as:

$$G_r(s) = C \bigl[ sI - (A - LC) \bigr]^{-1} E_f$$

so that $R(s) = G_r(s) F_a(s)$.

**Step 5 — Steady-State Behaviour.**

Using the Final Value Theorem, for a constant fault $f_a(t) = f_0 \mathbb{1}(t \geq t_f)$, as $t \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} r(t) = -C (A - LC)^{-1} E_f f_0$$

This is the **steady-state fault signature**. The matrix $-C(A - LC)^{-1} E_f$ is called the **fault signature matrix**; each column corresponds to a specific actuator fault. Its column-wise linear independence is what enables **isolation** (distinguishing *which* actuator has failed).

**Step 6 — Detection Threshold Logic.**

Assuming zero-mean Gaussian measurement noise with covariance $R_n$, the residual under healthy conditions is also zero-mean Gaussian. A statistical hypothesis test sets the threshold:

$$T = \eta \sqrt{ \text{diag}\bigl( C P C^T + R_n \bigr) }$$

where $P$ is the steady-state observer error covariance (solution of the algebraic Riccati equation), and $\eta$ is a tunable constant (typically 3 for a 3-$\sigma$ rule, giving **99.7%** confidence).

A fault is declared when:

$$\vert r_i(t) \vert > T_i \quad \text{for any channel } i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, p\}$$

**Step 7 — Isolation Rule.**

If fault signature columns $\sigma_k = -C(A - LC)^{-1} E_f e_k$ (where $e_k$ is the $k$-th standard basis vector) are designed to be linearly independent, then isolation proceeds by computing the **angle** or **correlation** between $r(t)$ and each $\sigma_k$:

$$\rho_k = \frac{ \langle r, \sigma_k \rangle }{ \vert r \vert \, \vert \sigma_k \vert }$$

The fault is declared on actuator $k^*$ where:

$$k^* = \arg\max_k \vert \rho_k \vert$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: PFTC vs AFTC Comparison

| Attribute | Passive FTC (PFTC) | Active FTC (AFTC) |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Fault detection required? | **No** | **Yes (FDI module)** |
| Reconfiguration? | No (fixed $K$) | Yes (online) |
| Latency to handle fault | **Zero** (instant) | Limited by FDI delay |
| Nominal performance | **Conservative** (worst-case tuned) | **Near-nominal** |
| Handles unknown faults? | **No** | Limited (depends on model) |
| Computational load | **Low** | **High** |
| False-alarm susceptibility | **None** | **Yes** (threshold crossings) |
| Typical controller type | $H_\infty$, $\mu$-synthesis, sliding mode | Gain-scheduled, LMI-based, MPC, switching |
| Implementation complexity | Low–Medium | High |
| Best suited for | Known bounded faults, hard real-time | Soft faults, incipient drifts |
| KTU example | Robust knee prosthesis controller | Sensor-failure reconfiguration in exoskeleton |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Numerical Isolation Logic

Suppose a 2-DOF powered ankle-foot orthosis (AFO) has the following observer and plant data (purely illustrative):

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -10 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad L = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad E_f = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

The two columns of $E_f$ represent two possible actuator faults (dorsiflexor fault, plantarflexor fault). Compute the fault signature matrix.

**Step 1:** Compute $A - LC$:

$$A - LC = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -10 & -2 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -8 & 1 \\ -40 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $(A - LC)^{-1}$:

$$\det(A - LC) = (-8)(-2) - (1)(-40) = 16 + 40 = 56$$

$$(A - LC)^{-1} = \frac{1}{56} \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1 \\ 40 & -8 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the fault signature matrix $\Sigma = -C (A - LC)^{-1} E_f$:

$$\Sigma = - \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{56} \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1 \\ 40 & -8 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= -\frac{1}{56} \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = -\frac{1}{56} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/56 & 2/56 \end{bmatrix}$$

The two columns are $\sigma_1 = [1/56]^T$ and $\sigma_2 = [2/56]^T$. They are linearly independent (one is a scalar multiple of the other? **No — $\sigma_1 = (1/56)$ and $\sigma_2 = (2/56)$ are scalar multiples, meaning the columns are linearly dependent!**) This means the chosen $L$ is **bad for isolation** — the observer cannot distinguish the two actuator faults.

**Design fix:** A different $L$ is needed. The **observability-based FDI design** ensures $\Sigma$ has full column rank $q$. Students should note this in KTU answers as a design consideration.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Overall Active FTC Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders the canonical **Active FTC loop** as applied to a powered prosthetic leg. The diagram shows the parallel observer (FDI) running alongside the plant, with the residual feeding a decision unit that re-selects the controller.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref[Reference Command r_des] --> ctrlBank
    ctrlBank[Controller Bank K1...Km] -->|u| plant[Prosthetic Plant P]
    plant -->|y| sensor[Sensor Bank]
    sensor -->|y_meas| plant
    sensor -->|y_meas| observer[Luenberg Observer]
    plant -->|y_dot| observer
    observer -->|y_hat| resComp[Residual Computer]
    sensor -->|y_meas| resComp
    resComp -->|r_t| decLogic[Threshold and Isolation Logic]
    decLogic -->|fault index k*| sw[Supervisor Switch]
    sw -->|select K_k*| ctrlBank
    decLogic -->|alarm| alarmHMI[User HMI and Buzzer]
    plant -->|joint angle torque| body[Patient Limb]
```

### 4.2 FDI Module Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    uCtrl[Control Input u] --> obsModel[Observer Model A B C L]
    yMeas[Measured Output y] --> obsModel
    obsModel --> yhat[Estimated Output y_hat]
    yMeas --> diff[Subtractor Node]
    yhat --> diff
    diff --> residual[Residual r t]
    residual --> filter[Low Pass Filter]
    filter --> threshold[Threshold Test]
    threshold --> decision{Binary Fault Flag}
    decision -->|Yes| isolate[Signature Matching]
    decision -->|No| healthy[Healthy Indicator]
    isolate --> classifier[Fault Class k1...kq]
    classifier --> reconfig[Reconfiguration Command]
```

### 4.3 Fault-Tolerant FES (Functional Electrical Stimulation) Control

This block diagram illustrates FTC for a **FES-based gait corrector** used in stroke rehabilitation. The patient is the "plant" in the loop; electrodes (actuators) may fail mid-session.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    intent[Patient Intent EMG Signal] --> decoder[Intent Decoder]
    decoder -->|desired trajectory xd| fesCtrl[FES Controller]
    fesCtrl -->|stim pulse s| electrodes[Surface Electrodes]
    electrodes -->|muscle activation| muscle[Patient Muscle Plant]
    muscle -->|joint angle| angleSens[Angle Sensor]
    muscle -->|force| forceSens[Force Sensor]
    angleSens --> fbk[Sensor Feedback]
    forceSens --> fbk
    fbk --> fesCtrl
    angleSens --> fesObs[FES Observer]
    fesCtrl --> fesObs
    fesObs --> fesRes[Residual Generator]
    fbk --> fesRes
    fesRes --> fesFDI[FDI Decision Unit]
    fesFDI -->|electrode k failed| fesReconfig[Electrode Reconfiguration]
    fesReconfig -->|redirects stimulus| electrodes
    fesFDI -->|alert| clinician[Clinician Tablet]
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree for FTC Strategy Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Fault Detected] --> q1{Fault Class Known?}
    q1 -->|Yes, small set| q2{Reconfiguration Latency OK?}
    q1 -->|No, novel| afsmActive[Use AFTC with adaptive AI]
    q2 -->|Yes| afsmSwitch[AFTC: Controller Switching]
    q2 -->|No, hard real-time| pftcActive[Use PFTC robust controller]
    afsmSwitch --> check[Verify GDF and Stability]
    pftcActive --> check
    afsmActive --> check
    check --> ok{GDF greater than 0.7?}
    ok -->|Yes| done[Return to Operation]
    ok -->|No| safe[Safe Stop and Alert Clinician]
```

### 4.5 Redundancy Architectures in Rehabilitation Devices

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SR[Sensor Redundancy]
        s1[Primary Angle Sensor] --> mux[Sensor Voter]
        s2[Backup Angle Sensor] --> mux
        s3[Software Virtual Sensor] --> mux
        mux --> fusedOut[Fused Angle Estimate]
    end
    subgraph AR[Actuator Redundancy]
        a1[Primary Motor] --> bus[Actuator Bus]
        a2[Backup Series Elastic Actuator] --> bus
        a3[Mechanical Passive Damper] --> bus
        bus --> drvOut[Net Output Torque]
    end
    fusedOut --> ctrl[Central FTC Controller]
    ctrl --> bus
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define Fault-Tolerant Control. Differentiate between a fault and a failure in the context of a rehabilitation device.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Fault:** An unpermitted deviation of at least one characteristic property (parameter or variable) of the system from its nominal / acceptable condition. *(1 mark)*
- **Failure:** A permanent interruption of a system's ability to perform a required function under specified operating conditions. *(1 mark)*
- **Fault-Tolerant Control (FTC):** A control strategy designed to maintain acceptable stability and performance of a system even when faults occur in sensors, actuators, or plant components, without requiring immediate shutdown. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper 2]**
*List any three types of faults encountered in a powered exoskeleton used for lower-limb rehabilitation. Give one example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Sensor fault** — Example: Drift in the knee joint angle encoder, causing the controller to misread the patient's actual leg position. *(1 mark)*
2. **Actuator fault** — Example: Partial loss of torque effectiveness in the hip motor due to gearbox wear, leading to insufficient assistive torque. *(1 mark)*
3. **Process / component fault** — Example: Loosening of the exoskeletal cuff strap changing the effective link length and thereby altering the plant's moment of inertia. *(1 mark)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Question Paper]**

**Question A (14 marks):**
*(a)* Explain the architecture of an **Active Fault-Tolerant Control (AFTC)** system with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each block. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* For the LTI plant $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu + E_f f$, $y = Cx$, design a **Luenberger observer** to generate a residual $r(t) = y - C\hat{x}$ that is sensitive to the fault $f$. Show the derivation of the error dynamics and the steady-state residual expression. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (Part a — 7 marks):**
The AFTC architecture consists of the following blocks:
1. **Plant (P):** The rehabilitation device, e.g., a powered prosthetic leg with plant matrix $A$, input matrix $B$, output matrix $C$. *(1 mark)*
2. **Controller Bank $\{K_1, \ldots, K_m\}$:** A set of pre-designed controllers, each optimal for a particular fault scenario. *(1 mark)*
3. **FDI Module:** Comprises (i) an **observer** that estimates $\hat{x}(t)$ using $u$ and $y$, (ii) a **residual generator** that computes $r(t) = y(t) - C\hat{x}(t)$, (iii) a **decision logic** that applies a threshold test $\|r\| > T$, and (iv) an **isolation stage** that uses the fault signature matrix. *(2 marks)*
4. **Supervisor / Reconfiguration Switch:** Selects the appropriate $K_i$ from the bank based on the FDI's decision. *(1 mark)*
5. **Actuator and Sensor Bank:** The physical hardware; in rehab devices, may include redundant units. *(1 mark)*
6. **Human–Machine Interface (HMI):** Provides alarms to the patient or clinician on fault detection. *(1 mark)*

**Model Answer (Part b — 7 marks):**
- **Observer equation:** $\dot{\hat{x}} = A\hat{x} + Bu + L(y - C\hat{x})$, with $L$ chosen so that $(A - LC)$ is Hurwitz. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Subtraction** of the observer equation from the plant equation yields $\dot{e} = (A - LC)e + E_f f$, where $e = x - \hat{x}$. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Laplace transform** of the error: $E(s) = [sI - (A - LC)]^{-1} E_f F(s)$. *(1 mark)*
- **Residual in Laplace domain:** $R(s) = CE(s) = C[sI - (A - LC)]^{-1} E_f F(s)$. *(1 mark)*
- **Steady-state residual** using the Final Value Theorem for a step fault $f = f_0 \mathbb{1}(t)$: $r_{ss} = -C(A - LC)^{-1} E_f f_0$. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Design criterion:** $L$ is chosen so that the matrix $C(A - LC)^{-1} E_f$ has full column rank, ensuring all $q$ faults produce distinct, linearly independent residual directions and hence **isolability**. *(0.5 marks)*

---

**Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice:**
*(a)* Compare and contrast **Passive FTC (PFTC)** and **Active FTC (AFTC)** in a tabular form, with at least six comparison points. Which is more suitable for a **FES-based gait corrector** used in stroke rehabilitation, and why? *(7 marks)*
*(b)* With a state-space model $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, $y = Cx$, derive the conditions under which a controller $K$ designed via **$H_\infty$ robust synthesis** achieves PFTC against an actuator fault $f$ that perturbs $B$ to $B + \Delta B$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (Part a — 7 marks):**
- **Tabular comparison** (6 distinct rows × 0.5 mark each = 3 marks), covering: detection required, reconfiguration, latency, nominal performance, computational load, false-alarm risk, etc.
- **Suitability discussion** for FES gait corrector (4 marks):
  - FES systems have **high inter-patient variability** and **time-varying dynamics** (muscle fatigue). This argues for **AFTC** because PFTC would be too conservative. *(1.5 marks)*
  - However, FES stimulation pulses must be delivered in **real time at 20–50 Hz**; an overly complex AFTC with computationally heavy FDI may violate the real-time constraint. *(1 mark)*
  - **Conclusion:** A **hybrid PFTC + lightweight AFTC** is recommended — a robust baseline controller with a small, fast observer-based FDI for electrode failure isolation. *(1.5 marks)*

**Model Answer (Part b — 7 marks):**
- **Plant under fault:** $\dot{x} = Ax + (B + \Delta B)u + Bf$, treating the fault as a perturbation of the input matrix. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Closed-loop with controller $K$:** $u = -Kx$, so $\dot{x} = (A - BK - \Delta BK)x - Bf$. *(1.5 marks)*
- **$H_\infty$ PFTC objective:** Design $K$ so that the closed-loop transfer function $T_{xf}(s) = [sI - (A - BK)]^{-1} B$ satisfies $\vert T_{xf}(j\omega) \vert_\infty < \gamma$ for a chosen $\gamma > 0$, robust to all admissible $\Vert \Delta B \Vert \leq \delta$. *(2 marks)*
- **Sufficient LMI condition** (Bounded Real Lemma):

$$(A - BK)^T P + P(A - BK) + P B B^T P + \gamma^{-2} P \Delta B \Delta B^T P + I \prec 0$$

for some symmetric $P \succ 0$. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Conclusion:** If such $P, K$ exist, the PFTC guarantees bounded $L_2$ gain from fault to state, i.e., the fault's effect on $x$ is attenuated. *(0.5 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "fault" with "failure":** Many students write the same definition for both. The 1-mark differentiation is frequently lost this way. Use the *deviation vs permanent interruption* distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the noise term:** In a residual test, students often write "$r(t) = 0$ if healthy" — but you must mention the **measurement noise covariance $R_n$** to explain why a threshold $T > 0$ is required.
> 3. **Skipping the "why $L$?" discussion:** Examiners expect the design criterion that $(A - LC)$ must be **Hurwitz** and that the fault signature matrix must have **full column rank** for isolation. Just stating the observer equation without design intent loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **No rehabilitation link:** Generic control answers get partial credit only. Always end with a *rehab-specific sentence* (e.g., "This AFTC strategy ensures the stroke patient can continue walking safely even if a stimulation electrode fails").
> 5. **Unit mistakes:** FAR must be in *events/hour*, not dimensionless. MTTF is in *hours*, not seconds.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Fault-Tolerant Control)**
> - **FTC definition:** Control system that maintains stability and acceptable performance under sensor, actuator, or process faults. *(Critical for Part A)*
> - **Fault vs Failure:** Fault = deviation; Failure = permanent loss of function.
> - **Three fault locations:** Sensor, Actuator, Process — know the example for each in a rehab device.
> - **Two main FTC types:** PFTC (no FDI, fixed robust controller) and AFTC (FDI + reconfiguration). Use the comparison table.
> - **FDI residual:** $r(t) = y(t) - C\hat{x}(t)$, generated by a Luenberger observer; $L$ chosen for Hurwitz $(A - LC)$ and full-rank fault signature.
> - **Detection logic:** $\vert r(t) \vert > T$ with $T$ set by 3-$\sigma$ rule.
> - **Reconfiguration methods:** Controller switching, gain scheduling, control-law re-design, virtual sensor/actuator.
> - **Key metrics to remember:** FDT, FAR, MDR, MTTF, GDF. Units: s, events/h, dimensionless, h, dimensionless.
> - **Mathematical models:** $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu + E_f f$; $u_{eff} = \gamma u$; $y_s = y + f_s$.
> - **Rehab examples to cite:** Prosthetic knee, FES gait corrector, powered wheelchair, exoskeleton, myoelectric hand.
> - **Standards to mention for extra credit:** IEC 60601-1, ISO 13485, FDA Predetermined Change Control Plan.
> - **Examiner traps:** Confusing fault/failure, ignoring noise in threshold, missing Hurwitz condition, no rehabilitation link in the conclusion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Integrated Controllers in Rehabilitation Engineering

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integrated Controller (KTU 2024 Definition):** A unified electronic control architecture that combines **signal acquisition** (from biosensors, switches, or myoelectric electrodes), **decision-making logic** (microprocessor/microcontroller/DSP-based algorithms), and **actuator driving circuitry** (motor drivers, pneumatic valves, or functional electrical stimulators) into a single coordinated system that interprets user intent and delivers appropriate assistive motion or therapeutic response in real time.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Rehabilitation Engineering context, integrated controllers are the **"brains"** of modern assistive devices — including myoelectric prosthetic hands, powered exoskeletons, functional electrical stimulators (FES), wheelchairs, and speech-generating devices. They replace the older standalone analog circuits (which used discrete op-amps, relays, and threshold detectors) with **embedded digital intelligence**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Smart Car vs. Old Scooter":**
> - **Old analog prosthetic hand**: Two metal contacts on the skin detect muscle flex → a simple threshold circuit triggers one motor to open/close the hand. Crude, no feedback, no proportional control.
> - **Integrated controller prosthetic hand**: Multiple EMG electrodes → ADC sampling at **1 kHz** → microcontroller running a **fuzzy logic or neural network classifier** that decides the user's intended grip pattern (pinch, lateral, power, tripod) → driver circuits actuate multiple finger motors with **proportional velocity and force feedback** from force-sensitive resistors.
>
> The integrated controller is essentially the **"ECU (Engine Control Unit) of the prosthetic limb"** — just as a modern car has one central computer managing engine, brakes, airbags, and infotainment, an integrated controller in rehabilitation manages sensors, actuators, safety, and user interface as a single cohesive system.

### Sub-Components of an Integrated Controller

| Sub-block | Function | Typical Hardware |
|-----------|----------|------------------|
| **Sensor Interface** | Acquires biosignals (EMG, EEG, EOG) | Electrodes, instrumentation amp (INA128), bandpass filter |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Amplifies & filters raw signals | Op-amp (e.g., AD8221), notch filter at **50 Hz / 60 Hz** |
| **ADC & Processing Unit** | Converts analog to digital & runs algorithms | 12-bit ADC, ARM Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz, DSP |
| **Control Algorithm** | Maps input to output action | PID, Fuzzy Logic, Neural Network, Adaptive Control |
| **Actuator Driver** | Powers motors/valves/stimulators | H-bridge (L298N), PWM, MOSFET driver |
| **Communication Module** | Wireless/PC interface | Bluetooth (HC-05), CAN bus, USB |
| **Power Management** | Battery monitoring, low-power modes | Li-ion BMS, boost/buck converters |
| **Safety Watchdog** | Hardware failsafe | Watchdog timer (WDT), emergency stop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Highlight:** The defining feature of an "**Integrated**" controller (as opposed to just "a controller") is that the **sensing, decision, and actuation are co-designed** within one framework, often on a single PCB or even a single System-on-Chip (SoC). The 2024 syllabus emphasizes **microcontroller/DSP-based** systems over the older analog-only designs.

---

## 1.2 Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electromagnetic Compatibility (KTU 2024 Definition):** The ability of an electronic equipment or system to function satisfactorily in its **intended electromagnetic environment** without **introducing intolerable electromagnetic disturbances** to anything in that environment. EMC has two complementary aspects: **Emission** (limiting unwanted energy radiated or conducted by the device) and **Immunity** (the device's resistance to external electromagnetic interference).

The formal IEC definition (per **IEC 61000-1-1**) is:

> *"EMC is the condition that prevails when telecommunications and other electronic equipment is performing its individual designed functions in a common electromagnetic environment without causing or suffering unacceptable degradation."*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "People Talking in a Room":**
> Imagine 50 people in a room trying to have individual phone conversations simultaneously.
> - **EMI (Electromagnetic Interference)** = everyone shouting at once.
> - **EMC** = the polite situation where each person speaks at a reasonable volume, uses noise-cancelling headphones (immunity), and doesn't disturb others (emission control).
>
> In a **hospital/rehabilitation setting**, an integrated prosthetic controller that radiates strong RF noise could:
> 1. Disturb a nearby **ventilator or heart pacemaker** (this is why medical EMC standards are extremely strict).
> 2. Suffer **false triggering** from mobile phones, MRI scanners, or diathermy equipment.
>
> EMC = "be a **good neighbor** in the electromagnetic world."

### Two Pillars of EMC

| Pillar | Question it answers | KTU terminology |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **EMI (Emission)** | "How much noise does **my** device radiate or conduct to the outside world?" | Conducted & Radiated Emission |
| **EMS (Immunity / Susceptibility)** | "How well does **my** device resist external electromagnetic noise?" | ESD, Radiated Immunity, Conducted Immunity, Surge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The standard metric units for EMC are **dBμV/m** (for radiated field strength) and **dBμV** (for conducted voltage on power/signal lines). The frequency range of interest is typically **9 kHz to 400 GHz** (per CISPR 16).

### Sources of EMI in Rehabilitation Devices

- **Conducted noise** from switched-mode power supplies (SMPS) and PWM motor drivers
- **Radiated emissions** from clock harmonics of microcontrollers (e.g., a 16 MHz crystal has harmonics at 32, 48, 64 MHz…)
- **ESD (Electrostatic Discharge)** from user contact on a prosthetic hand → can reset the microcontroller
- **Cross-talk** between EMG sensor traces and motor driver traces on the same PCB
- **Common-mode currents** on long cable runs from electrode to controller

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Integrated Controllers Used in Rehabilitation

### 2.1.1 Classification by Control Philosophy

| Controller Type | Principle | KTU 2024 Use-Case | Pros | Cons |
|-----------------|-----------|-------------------|------|------|
| **ON-OFF (Bang-Bang)** | Two states only (open/close) | Simple prosthetic gripper, FES on/off | Simple, cheap, robust | Jerky motion, no proportional control |
| **PID Controller** | Error-driven P + I + D terms | Powered wheelchair motor speed, joint position | Mature, well-understood | Needs accurate model; struggles with non-linear muscle dynamics |
| **State-Space / Pole-Placement** | Multi-variable feedback on state vector | Multi-DOF exoskeleton, gait control | Handles MIMO systems | Requires state observer (e.g., Kalman filter) |
| **Fuzzy Logic** | Rule-based linguistic reasoning (IF-THEN) | EMG pattern classification for prosthetic hand | Handles uncertainty, no model needed | Tuning is heuristic; rules can explode |
| **Artificial Neural Network (ANN)** | Learns non-linear input-output mapping from data | BCI decoding, EMG gesture recognition | Excellent pattern recognition | Needs training data, "black box" |
| **Adaptive Control (MRAC)** | Online parameter adjustment | Changing loads, fatigue in FES walking | Robust to plant variations | Stability proof is complex |
| **Hybrid (e.g., Fuzzy + PID)** | Fuzzy tunes PID gains online | Advanced myoelectric limbs | Combines benefits | Complex implementation |
| **Impedance / Admittance Control** | Regulates mechanical impedance | Physical human-robot interaction, exoskeleton | Inherently safe for human contact | Requires accurate force sensing |

### 2.1.2 PID Control — The Workhorse

A PID controller output is governed by:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Where:
- $u(t)$ = control signal sent to actuator
- $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ = error (reference minus actual output)
- $K_p$ = proportional gain (immediate reaction to error)
- $K_i$ = integral gain (eliminates steady-state error)
- $K_d$ = derivative gain (damps oscillation, anticipates future error)

**Discrete (digital) implementation** (KTU 2024 — microcontrollers work in discrete time):

$$u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] \cdot T_s + K_d \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

Where $T_s$ is the sampling period (typically **1 ms to 10 ms** in real-time rehabilitation control).

### 2.1.3 Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC) Architecture

Steps in a typical FLC for an EMG-controlled prosthetic hand:
1. **Fuzzification** — convert crisp EMG amplitude to fuzzy sets (e.g., "Low", "Medium", "High")
2. **Rule base** — IF (EMG is High) AND (Velocity is Low) THEN (Grip is Power) (sample rule)
3. **Inference engine** — applies rules using min/max operations (Mamdani) or product (Sugeno)
4. **Defuzzification** — converts fuzzy output back to crisp motor command (centroid method)

The membership function $\mu_A(x) \in [0, 1]$ describes degree of belonging to set $A$.

### 2.1.4 EMG-Based Control — The KTU 2024 Core Example

EMG = Electromyogram. Surface electrodes on the residual limb pick up motor-unit action potentials during voluntary contraction.

Key signal processing chain:
1. **Amplification** — gain typically **1000×** (instrumentation amplifier, CMRR > **90 dB**)
2. **Bandpass filter** — **20 Hz to 500 Hz** (removes DC offset, motion artefact, line noise harmonics)
3. **Notch filter** — **50 Hz / 60 Hz** (powerline rejection)
4. **Rectification** — full-wave rectifier
5. **Smoothing** — moving-average or RMS envelope
6. **Feature extraction** — Mean Absolute Value (MAV), Root Mean Square (RMS), Zero Crossings (ZC), Waveform Length (WL)
7. **Classification** — pattern recognized as "open hand", "close hand", "pinch", etc.
8. **Actuation** — PWM to motor driver

The **MAV feature** is computed as:

$$\text{MAV} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i \vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical rule:** Use `\vert` (not `|`) in the LaTeX expression above. In markdown tables, the pipe `|` would have broken the column structure. This is exactly the kind of issue the KTU board examiners expect you to handle when writing exam answers inside tabulated formulas.

---

## 2.2 Electromagnetic Compatibility — Engineering Formulas

### 2.2.1 The EMC Equation (Conceptual)

A device is **electromagnetically compatible** if:

$$P_{\text{noise emitted}} < P_{\text{immunity threshold of victim}}$$

for **every** pair (emitter, victim) in the **intended operating environment**.

### 2.2.2 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | $E = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{\text{measured}}}{1\ \mu V}\right)$ | Field strength expressed in dBμV | dBμV |
| 2 | $E_{dB\mu V/m} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{E_{V/m}}{1\ \mu V/m}\right)$ | Field strength in dBμV/m | dBμV/m |
| 3 | $C = 20 \log_{10}(f \cdot L)$ | Coupling (inductive) — $f$ in Hz, $L$ in H | dB |
| 4 | $\text{Attenuation}_{\text{shield}} = 50 + 10 \log_{10}\left(\rho \cdot \mu_r \cdot f\right)$ (approx.) | Shielding effectiveness for plane wave (Schelkunoff) | dB |
| 5 | $f_{\text{res}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$ | Resonant frequency of LC filter | Hz |
| 6 | $Z_c = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$ | Characteristic impedance of a transmission line | Ω |
| 7 | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | dB |
| 8 | $t_{\text{settle}} \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | 2% settling time of 2nd-order system | s |
| 9 | $f_{3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Cutoff frequency of first-order RC filter | Hz |
| 10 | $P_{\text{radiated}} \approx \frac{(I \cdot \pi \cdot f^2 \cdot A^2)}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 c^3}$ | Short dipole radiated power (approximate) | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Formula #4 is the basis for **Faraday cage** design — used in MRI rooms, ECG enclosures, and shielding the controller PCB inside a prosthetic limb from external RF. Formula #7 is critical for EMG/EEG amplifiers where the electrode-skin interface creates large common-mode voltages due to powerline pickup.

### 2.2.3 Three Coupling Mechanisms of EMI

| Mechanism | Description | Mitigation |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| **Conducted** | Noise travels along wires, PCB traces, power cables | Common-mode chokes, X/Y capacitors, ferrite beads |
| **Radiated** | E/H fields couple through air or radiation pattern | Shielding (Faraday cage), PCB layout, twisted pairs |
| **Coupled (Inductive/Capacitive)** | Near-field coupling between adjacent traces | Increase spacing, ground guard traces, orthogonal routing |

### 2.2.4 Medical-Device EMC Standards (KTU 2024 — High-Yield)

| Standard | Title | Relevance |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **IEC 60601-1-2** | Medical electrical equipment — EMC requirements (4th ed. 2014) | **The** primary standard for rehab devices |
| **CISPR 11** | Industrial, scientific, medical (ISM) equipment — emission limits | Class A (hospital) / Class B (home) |
| **IEC 61000-4-2** | ESD immunity test (±8 kV contact, ±15 kV air) | Mandatory for medical devices |
| **IEC 61000-4-3** | Radiated RF immunity (80 MHz – 2.7 GHz) | Wireless coexistence |
| **IEC 61000-4-11** | Voltage dips & interruptions immunity | Battery-powered, brown-out |
| **ISO 14708** | Active implantable medical devices EMC | Pacemakers, implantable FES |
| **FCC Part 15** (US) / **EN 55011** (EU) | Unintentional radiators — emission limits | Marketing in regulated regions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Question-favourite fact:** *For a Class B (residential) medical device, the radiated emission limit at 30 m is **30 dBμV/m** (30–230 MHz) and **37 dBμV/m** (230–1000 MHz). For Class A (industrial/hospital), the limits are higher but the device must be labelled "for hospital use only."*

---

## 2.3 EMC Mitigation Techniques — Engineering Toolbox

1. **Shielding** — metallic enclosure (copper, aluminium), conductive coatings on plastic housings
2. **Filtering** — line filters on power input, RC snubbers on motors, ferrite cores on cables
3. **Grounding** — single-point (low frequency) vs. multi-point (high frequency); ground plane in PCB
4. **PCB Design** — short traces, 45° bends (no 90°), guard rings around analog sections, stitching vias
5. **Spread-Spectrum Clocking** — reduces peak harmonics of microcontroller clock
6. **Optical Isolation** — galvanic isolation of analog front-end from digital/power stage
7. **Watchdog + Brown-out Reset** — recovers from EMI-induced latch-up
8. **Software techniques** — CRC on received data, debouncing, redundant sensor voting

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: PID Controller Discrete-Time Output

### Problem (KTU Exam Style)
A 2-DOF prosthetic elbow uses a PID controller to follow a desired angular position $\theta_r(t)$. Derive the discrete-time PID control law and show how the derivative term avoids **derivative kick** when the setpoint changes abruptly.

### Solution

**Step 1: Continuous-time PID**

Start with the standard form:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

This is the textbook form, but it suffers from a problem in the D-term: when the reference $r(t)$ steps, $e(t)$ steps, and $\frac{de}{dt}$ becomes an impulse — a "**derivative kick**" that shocks the actuator.

**Step 2: Take the Laplace Transform** (to analyse transient behaviour)

$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) + K_d s E(s)$$

**Step 3: Factor out $E(s)$**

$$U(s) = \left[K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right] E(s) = C(s) \cdot E(s)$$

The **PID transfer function** is:

$$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Step 4: Discretize for microcontroller implementation**

Using backward-difference approximation (which is causal — important for KTU exam):

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt} \approx \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

$$\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau \approx \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] \cdot T_s$$

Substitute these into the continuous PID:

$$u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] \cdot T_s + K_d \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

This is the **positional PID**.

**Step 5: Convert to velocity / incremental form to avoid derivative kick and integrator wind-up**

The **incremental (velocity) PID** computes $\Delta u[k] = u[k] - u[k-1]$:

$$\Delta u[k] = K_p \big(e[k] - e[k-1]\big) + K_i \, T_s \, e[k] + K_d \frac{e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2]}{T_s}$$

This is widely used in **motor control** for powered prosthetics because:
- No derivative kick (it only reacts to error changes, not setpoint steps)
- Anti-windup is easier (you simply limit $\Delta u$)
- Bumpless transfer in case of manual-override

**Step 6: Z-transform representation**

$$U(z) = \left[K_p + \frac{K_i T_s z}{z-1} + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(1 - z^{-1})\right] E(z)$$

This is the form a DSP engineer would code on a **TMS320F28335** for a powered orthosis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Radiated Power of a Short Dipole Antenna (Approximate)

### Why this matters in KTU context
A wire harness on a powered wheelchair or a long electrode cable on a prosthetic arm can act as an **unintended antenna** radiating clock harmonics. We need to estimate how much power it might radiate.

### Step 1: Define the short dipole

A short dipole has length $L \ll \lambda$. For a 1 m cable carrying a 16 MHz clock, $\lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^8 / 16 \times 10^6 \approx 18.75$ m. So $L = 1$ m is **short** relative to $\lambda$.

**Step 2: Average radiated power formula**

For a Hertzian (infinitesimal) dipole carrying uniform current $I_0$:

$$P_{\text{rad}} = \frac{\pi}{3} \cdot \eta_0 \cdot \left(\frac{I_0 \cdot L}{\lambda}\right)^2$$

Where:
- $\eta_0 = 377\ \Omega$ is the intrinsic impedance of free space
- $I_0$ = RMS current in the dipole
- $L$ = physical length of the dipole
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of the signal

**Step 3: Substitute and simplify**

$$P_{\text{rad}} = \frac{\pi}{3} \cdot 377 \cdot \left(\frac{I_0 \cdot L \cdot f}{c}\right)^2$$

$$P_{\text{rad}} = \frac{377 \pi}{3 c^2} \cdot (I_0 L f)^2$$

Numerically:

$$P_{\text{rad}} \approx 1.32 \times 10^{-14} \cdot (I_0 L f)^2 \quad \text{(with I in A, L in m, f in Hz)}$$

**Step 4: Worked numerical example**

Suppose:
- $I_0 = 10$ mA $= 0.01$ A
- $L = 0.5$ m
- $f = 16$ MHz $= 16 \times 10^6$ Hz

Then:

$$I_0 L f = 0.01 \times 0.5 \times 16 \times 10^6 = 80{,}000$$

$$P_{\text{rad}} = 1.32 \times 10^{-14} \times (80{,}000)^2 = 1.32 \times 10^{-14} \times 6.4 \times 10^{9}$$

$$P_{\text{rad}} = 8.45 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{W} = 84.5\ \mu\text{W}$$

This may seem tiny, but when converted to **field strength at 10 m** for a regulatory test, the resulting **dBμV/m** value can approach or exceed the CISPR 11 Class A limit of **30 dBμV/m at 30 m**, especially with harmonics at higher multiples of the fundamental clock.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shielding Effectiveness (Schelkunoff Approximation)

### Problem
Compute the shielding effectiveness (SE) of a 0.5 mm-thick aluminium enclosure around a prosthetic-hand controller, for a radiated EMI source at 100 MHz. Aluminium conductivity $\sigma = 3.5 \times 10^7$ S/m, relative permeability $\mu_r = 1$.

### Step 1: The three components of SE**

For a plane wave, total shielding effectiveness is the sum of three loss mechanisms:

$$SE = A_{abs} + A_{ref} + A_{re-ref} \quad \text{(dB)}$$

- $A_{abs}$ = absorption loss in the metal
- $A_{ref}$ = reflection loss at each surface (×2 for two surfaces)
- $A_{re-ref}$ = re-reflection loss (usually small, often ignored for $A_{abs} > 10$ dB)

**Step 2: Skin depth**

The skin depth is:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r \sigma}}$$

With $f = 100 \times 10^6$ Hz, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi \times 10^8 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1 \times 3.5 \times 10^7}}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{1.38 \times 10^{10}}} \approx 8.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} = 8.5\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3: Absorption loss**

$$A_{abs} = 8.686 \cdot \frac{t}{\delta}$$

With $t = 0.5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m:

$$A_{abs} = 8.686 \cdot \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{8.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.686 \times 58.8 \approx 511\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 4: Reflection loss (plane wave, far field)**

$$A_{ref} = 168 + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma_r}{\mu_r f}\right) \quad \text{(dB)}$$

For aluminium, $\sigma_r = \sigma / \sigma_{\text{cu}} = 3.5 \times 10^7 / 5.8 \times 10^7 \approx 0.6$:

$$A_{ref} = 168 + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.6}{1 \times 10^8}\right) = 168 + 10 \log_{10}(6 \times 10^{-9})$$

$$A_{ref} = 168 + 10 \times (-8.22) = 168 - 82.2 = 85.8\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 5: Total SE (one reflection only — double it for two surfaces, but re-reflection is negligible here)**

$$SE \approx A_{abs} + 2 A_{ref} = 511 + 2 \times 85.8 = 682.6\ \text{dB}$$

This is **massive** attenuation — a 0.5 mm aluminium sheet at 100 MHz gives > 600 dB shielding. In practice, apertures (display windows, ventilation slots, connector openings) limit the actual SE to **40–80 dB** — this is the engineering reality KTU expects you to remember.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The dominant leakage path is **not** the metal wall but the **apertures and cable penetrations**. This is why prosthetic and wheelchair enclosures use **conductive gaskets**, **filtered D-sub connectors**, and **shielded cable glands** in real design.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Controller Selection vs. Application

| Application | Required Bandwidth | Best Controller | Sensor | Sample Rate |
|-------------|--------------------|-----------------|--------|-------------|
| **Myoelectric hand** | 1–5 Hz (voluntary motion) | FLC or ANN | Surface EMG | 1 kHz |
| **Powered knee** | 0–2 Hz (gait) | State-space + adaptive | Joint angle + torque | 200 Hz |
| **FES for foot drop** | 1–3 Hz (swing phase) | Threshold + on/off | Heel switch / IMU | 100 Hz |
| **Wheelchair joystick** | 0–10 Hz (turning) | PI | Potentiometer | 50 Hz |
| **Exoskeleton (multi-DOF)** | 0–20 Hz (dynamic tasks) | Model-based + impedance | Force/torque + IMU | 500 Hz |
| **BCI (EEG-driven)** | 0.5–30 Hz (P300, motor imagery) | LDA / SVM / CNN | EEG cap | 256 Hz |
| **Speech-generating AAC** | Audio rate | DSP (LPC, mel-filterbank) | Microphone | 16 kHz |

The bottom branch shows the **safety subsystem** (watchdog + emergency stop) — this is mandatory in KTU 2024 design questions.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop PID Control of a Powered Knee Joint

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Angle theta desired from Trajectory Planner] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    FBG[Force Sensor on Stump Interface] --> A[Actual Angle Sensor Encoder]
    A --> SUM
    SUM --> E[Error e k = theta ref minus theta actual]
    E --> P[Proportional Kp]
    E --> I[Integral Ki sum times Ts]
    E --> D[Derivative Kd divided by Ts]
    P --> ADD[Sum of P I and D]
    I --> ADD
    D --> ADD
    ADD --> LIM[Saturation Limit plus or minus 100 percent PWM]
    LIM --> PWM[PWM to H-Bridge Driver]
    PWM --> M[DC Motor of Knee]
    M --> LOAD[Knee Joint Mechanical Load]
    LOAD --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This is a textbook **unity-feedback closed-loop** with a digital PID in the forward path. The **saturation block** prevents integrator wind-up. The **encoder** closes the loop. Compare this with the open-loop myoelectric system in 4.1 — notice how 4.3 has explicit feedback while 4.1 has only an optional force-feedback branch.
