## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Choosing a Controller Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Rehabilitation Device Design] --> Q1{Is a precise model available?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Number of input and output variables?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Do we have labelled training data?}
    Q2 -- Single input single output --> PID[Use PID Controller]
    Q2 -- Multiple input multiple output --> SS[Use State Space or LQR]
    Q3 -- Yes large dataset --> ANN[Use Neural Network Classifier]
    Q3 -- No but expert rules exist --> FLC[Use Fuzzy Logic Controller]
    Q3 -- No data no rules --> THR[Use Threshold On Off Controller]
    PID --> VAL{Validate against IEC 60601 safety class}
    SS --> VAL
    ANN --> VAL
    FLC --> VAL
    THR --> VAL
    VAL -- Pass --> EMC[Apply EMC Compliance per IEC 60601-1-2]
    VAL -- Fail --> RETUNE[Retune or Switch Architecture]
    EMC --> PROD[Production Design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This is a **decision tree** that a KTU viva examiner would expect you to walk through verbally. Start with whether you have a model; if yes, use classical control; if no, use a data-driven or rule-based approach; then **always** validate against the medical safety standard and EMC — these are not optional.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Electromagnetic Compatibility. List its two major aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** *Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC) is the ability of an electronic device or system to function satisfactorily in its intended electromagnetic environment without introducing intolerable electromagnetic disturbances to other equipment in that environment.*
>
> **Two aspects (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **EMI / Emission** — limiting the unwanted electromagnetic energy generated and radiated or conducted by the device.
> 2. **EMS / Immunity (Susceptibility)** — the device's ability to resist external electromagnetic disturbances without functional degradation.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is an integrated controller in the context of a myoelectric prosthetic hand?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> An **integrated controller** in a myoelectric prosthetic hand is an embedded electronic system that combines (i) **EMG signal acquisition and conditioning** (electrodes, instrumentation amplifier, filters), (ii) **decision-making logic** (microcontroller / DSP running classification algorithms such as fuzzy logic or neural networks), and (iii) **actuator driving circuitry** (PWM-controlled H-bridge motor drivers) into a single coordinated architecture.
>
> It is *integrated* because sensing, processing, and actuation are co-designed on one platform — typically a single PCB with a microcontroller, an ADC, and motor drivers — rather than being separate, independently-designed modules. Modern versions also include **force feedback, Bluetooth telemetry, and a safety watchdog**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **integrated controller for a myoelectric prosthetic hand**. Describe the function of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)**. Discuss the three coupling mechanisms of EMI and the EMC standards applicable to medical electrical equipment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Block diagram (drawn as in Section 4.1 above) — 1 mark**

**Block-by-block description — 6 marks (½ mark each):**

1. **Surface EMG Electrodes (×3):** Detect motor-unit action potentials on residual forearm muscles (e.g., flexor digitorum superficialis, extensor digitorum). The differential signal is in the range of **0.1 mV to 5 mV** peak-to-peak. *(0.5 mark)*

2. **Instrumentation Amplifier:** A three-op-amp topology (e.g., AD8221) provides a fixed gain of typically **1000×** with a high **CMRR of > 90 dB** to reject 50/60 Hz common-mode pickup. *(0.5 mark)*

3. **Bandpass Filter (20 Hz – 500 Hz):** Removes DC offset and high-frequency noise, isolating the EMG energy band. Implemented as a 2nd-order Butterworth active filter. *(0.5 mark)*

4. **Notch Filter (50 Hz / 60 Hz):** Twin-T or active notch with Q ≈ 10, providing > 40 dB attenuation at the powerline frequency. *(0.5 mark)*

5. **ADC (12-bit, ≥ 1 kHz):** Per Nyquist, sampling rate must be > 2 × highest EMG frequency (≥ 1 kHz for 500 Hz upper band). 12-bit resolution gives quantization step of $V_{ref}/2^{12}$, e.g., **0.8 mV at 3.3 V reference**. *(0.5 mark)*

6. **Microcontroller (ARM Cortex-M4):** Runs the feature extraction and classification algorithm at real time. Typical clock **168 MHz**, ample MIPS for fuzzy logic or a small neural net. *(0.5 mark)*

7. **Feature Extraction:** Computes **MAV, RMS, ZC, WL** in a sliding window of 200 ms with 50 ms overlap. *(0.5 mark)*

8. **Classifier:** Maps the feature vector to one of N grasp patterns (open hand, power grip, pinch, lateral, tripod). Uses either fuzzy logic (IF–THEN rules) or a trained ANN/LDA. *(0.5 mark)*

9. **PWM Generator + H-Bridge Driver:** PWM at 20 kHz (above audible range) drives an L298N or DRV8871 H-bridge, controlling the 5 DC gear motors embedded in the fingers. *(0.5 mark)*

10. **Force Feedback (FSR in fingertips):** Closes a low-level control loop so that the grip force does not crush a delicate object (e.g., an egg). Limits PWM duty cycle when FSR threshold is crossed. *(0.5 mark)*

11. **Bluetooth + Watchdog:** Wireless telemetry for tuning/app monitoring; watchdog timer resets the MCU if it locks up due to EMI. *(0.5 mark)*

12. **Power Management:** 7.4 V Li-ion cell → buck converter to 5 V and 3.3 V rails; battery monitor via ADC. *(0.5 mark)*

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1.5 marks):** *(as in Q1 above)*

**Three coupling mechanisms of EMI (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Conducted Coupling** — noise travels along physical conductors: power cables, signal wires, PCB traces, and even the ground plane. Common in motor-driven systems where PWM edges couple back into the supply. Mitigated with **line filters, decoupling capacitors, and common-mode chokes**.

2. **Radiated Coupling** — electromagnetic energy propagates through space as E and H fields and is picked up by an antenna, cable, or PCB trace acting as a receiving element. Frequency dependent; near-field (inductive/capacitive) vs. far-field (plane wave) behaviour differs. Mitigated with **Faraday shielding, ground planes, and reduced loop areas**.

3. **Inductive / Capacitive (Near-Field) Coupling** — two adjacent traces act as primary and secondary windings of a transformer; an electric field couples capacitively while a magnetic field couples inductively. Mitigated by **increasing trace spacing, ground guard traces between signals, and orthogonal routing**.

**Medical EMC standards (2.5 marks — 0.5 each for name + 0.5 for relevance):**

1. **IEC 60601-1-2** — primary medical EMC standard (4th edition, 2014). Defines test levels and pass/fail criteria for medical devices in **professional healthcare, home healthcare, and special environments**.
2. **CISPR 11** — emission limits for ISM (industrial, scientific, medical) equipment, **Class A (hospital) / Class B (home use)**.
3. **IEC 61000-4-2** — ESD immunity test levels, **±8 kV contact, ±15 kV air**.
4. **IEC 61000-4-3** — radiated RF immunity, **80 MHz – 2.7 GHz at 3 V/m (or 10 V/m for life-supporting equipment)**.
5. **ISO 14708** — special requirements for **active implantable medical devices** (implanted FES, implantable neural stimulators).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q. (a)** Describe the **PID controller** and derive the **discrete-time velocity-form PID equation** for controlling the angular position of a powered prosthetic knee. Show how the velocity form avoids **derivative kick** and **integrator wind-up**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **conducted and radiated emission test setups** for medical devices as per CISPR 11. Mention the typical **class A and class B limit values** in dBμV/m. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**PID concept (2 marks):**

A Proportional-Integral-Derivative controller computes a control signal $u(t)$ as the sum of three terms operating on the tracking error $e(t) = \theta_r(t) - \theta_a(t)$:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- **P-term**: provides immediate response proportional to current error.
- **I-term**: accumulates past errors; eliminates steady-state error.
- **D-term**: anticipates future error by rate-of-change; damps overshoot.

**Derivation of discrete velocity form (4 marks):**

Starting from the continuous form and using **backward-difference** discretization:

$$\frac{de}{dt} \approx \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}, \quad \int_0^t e d\tau \approx \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] T_s$$

Substitute to get the **positional discrete PID**:

$$u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] + K_d \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

Now compute $\Delta u[k] = u[k] - u[k-1]$:

$$u[k] - u[k-1] = K_p \big(e[k] - e[k-1]\big) + K_i T_s \, e[k] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\big(e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2]\big)$$

This is the **velocity PID**:

$$\boxed{\Delta u[k] = K_p \Delta e[k] + K_i T_s \, e[k] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\big(\Delta e[k] - \Delta e[k-1]\big)}$$

where $\Delta e[k] = e[k] - e[k-1]$.

**Avoidance of derivative kick (0.5 mark):** the velocity form only differentiates the **error** (which changes smoothly even when the reference steps), not the setpoint itself.

**Avoidance of integrator wind-up (0.5 mark):** velocity form computes $\Delta u$ per step, so the actuator saturates naturally at PWM limits; the integral never accumulates unboundedly because we can simply **clamp $\Delta u$** before adding to the previous output.

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Conducted emission test (3 marks):**

- **Standard:** CISPR 11 / EN 55011
- **Frequency range:** **150 kHz to 30 MHz**
- **Setup:** Device Under Test (DUT) is placed on a non-conductive table **0.8 m** above a **ground reference plane** (2 m × 2 m minimum). A **Line Impedance Stabilization Network (LISN)** is inserted in series with each power line to present a defined **50 Ω impedance** to the noise and to filter out external mains noise. Spectrum analyser measures noise voltage across the LISN's 50 Ω terminator.
- **Limits (Class B, quasi-peak):** **< 56 dBμV** at 150 kHz, decreasing to **< 46 dBμV** at 0.5 MHz and **< 46 dBμV** flat to 5 MHz, then rising slightly to 30 MHz.

**Radiated emission test (3 marks):**

- **Standard:** CISPR 11
- **Frequency range:** **30 MHz to 1 GHz** (extended to 6 GHz in some standards)
- **Setup:** The DUT is placed on a turntable at **10 m** (Class A) or **3 m / 10 m** (Class B) distance from a calibrated broadband antenna. A spectrum analyser scans while the turntable rotates and the antenna height varies from **1 m to 4 m** (to capture both horizontal and vertical polarisations and to find the maximum emission). The measurement is performed in a **semi-anechoic chamber** lined with RF absorbers to suppress reflections.
- **Limits (Class B, quasi-peak at 10 m):**
  - **30–230 MHz:** **30 dBμV/m**
  - **230–1000 MHz:** **37 dBμV/m**

For **Class A (industrial/hospital, 10 m):** **40 dBμV/m** (30–230 MHz) and **47 dBμV/m** (230–1000 MHz). Class A devices must carry a label *"This equipment is intended for use in industrial areas. In a domestic environment, it may cause radio interference."*

**Difference Class A vs Class B (1 mark):** Class B is the stricter "residential" limit; Class A is for industrial / hospital use where RF is already abundant.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark-Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Confusing Class A and Class B limits** — students often swap them. **Remember: Class B is STRICTER (lower dBμV/m).**
> 2. **Using `|` (pipe) inside LaTeX formulas in tabulated answers** — this breaks the markdown table in your exam script's digital format. Use `\vert` or `\mid`.
> 3. **Forgetting the 10 m vs 3 m test distance** — limits are different for each, and examiners will deduct a mark if you quote the wrong distance with the wrong limit.
> 4. **Mixing up "EMC" with "EMI"** — EMI is one aspect of EMC (the emission side). The other is immunity (EMS).
> 5. **Skipping the LISN in the conducted emission test** — the LISN is the *defining* piece of hardware; without mentioning it, you lose at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Omitting "watchdog" and "brown-out detector"** in the controller block diagram — KTU 2024 specifically tests for safety subsystem awareness in medical devices.
> 7. **Writing "PID" but giving the formula of a "PI" or "PD"** — make sure all three terms are explicitly written with their gains.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Bookmark this section for last-day revision.**

### Integrated Controllers — Must-Know Points
- [ ] **Definition:** unified sensing + decision + actuation in one architecture
- [ ] **Core components:** sensor interface, signal conditioning, ADC, μC/DSP, algorithm, actuator driver, power, comms, safety
- [ ] **Myoelectric control chain:** electrode → INA → BPF → Notch → ADC → μC → features → classifier → PWM → H-bridge → motor
- [ ] **EMG bandwidth:** **20–500 Hz**; powerline notch at **50/60 Hz**; sampling at **≥ 1 kHz**
- [ ] **CMRR of EMG amp:** > **90 dB**; gain typically **1000×**
- [ ] **PID formula:** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \, \dot{e}(t)$
- [ ] **Velocity PID** avoids derivative kick and integrator wind-up
- [ ] **Fuzzy logic** stages: fuzzification → rule base → inference → defuzzification
- [ ] **Sampling rate** for multi-DOF exoskeleton: **500 Hz**; myoelectric hand: **1 kHz**; EEG BCI: **256 Hz**
- [ ] **Safety subsystem:** watchdog timer + brown-out detector + emergency stop — **non-negotiable** in KTU 2024 design

### Electromagnetic Compatibility — Must-Know Points
- [ ] **EMC definition** — operation without causing or suffering unacceptable interference
- [ ] **Two aspects:** **Emission (EMI)** and **Immunity (EMS)**
- [ ] **Three coupling modes:** **conducted, radiated, inductive/capacitive (near-field)**
- [ ] **Primary medical standard:** **IEC 60601-1-2 (4th edition, 2014)**
- [ ] **Emission standard:** **CISPR 11** — Class A (industrial/hospital) and Class B (residential)
- [ ] **Class B radiated limit at 10 m:** **30 dBμV/m (30–230 MHz)**, **37 dBμV/m (230–1000 MHz)**
- [ ] **Conducted emission test:** uses **LISN** (Line Impedance Stabilization Network) at **150 kHz – 30 MHz**
- [ ] **Radiated emission test:** uses calibrated antenna in **semi-anechoic chamber** at **10 m / 3 m**
- [ ] **ESD immunity:** **±8 kV contact, ±15 kV air** (IEC 61000-4-2)
- [ ] **Radiated immunity:** **3 V/m (or 10 V/m life-support)** over 80 MHz – 2.7 GHz (IEC 61000-4-3)
- [ ] **Mitigation tools:** shielding (Faraday cage), filtering, grounding, PCB layout, optical isolation, spread-spectrum clock, watchdog
- [ ] **Apertures dominate leakage** — practical SE is limited by seams and cable penetrations, not by wall thickness
- [ ] **Key formula to memorize:** $E_{dB\mu V/m} = 20 \log_{10}(E_{V/m} / 1\ \mu V/m)$

### Quick-Fire Self-Test
1. *EMG bandpass filter passband?* → **20 Hz – 500 Hz**
2. *Class B radiated limit at 100 MHz, 10 m?* → **30 dBμV/m**
3. *Minimum ADC sampling rate for EMG?* → **1 kHz** (Nyquist for 500 Hz)
4. *Which standard for medical EMC?* → **IEC 60601-1-2**
5. *Three EMI coupling modes?* → **Conducted, Radiated, Inductive/Capacitive**
6. *What is the LISN used for?* → **Conducted emission test — defines 50 Ω line impedance**
7. *Which PID form avoids derivative kick?* → **Velocity (incremental) form**
8. *Fuzzification, rule base, inference, defuzzification belong to which controller?* → **Fuzzy Logic Controller**
9. *Why is medical EMC stricter than industrial EMC?* → **Because interference with life-supporting equipment can cause death**
10. *What is the dominant leakage path in a real enclosure?* → **Apertures and cable penetrations, not the wall**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Advice:** When the question says *"with neat block diagram"*, **always draw a labelled block diagram with arrows showing signal flow direction** — this alone is worth 1–2 marks. When the question says *"discuss"*, write at least 4–5 distinct points. When the question says *"derive"*, show **every algebraic step**, do not skip. Use `\vert` (NOT `|`) in tabular formulas to avoid markdown breakage — examiners in digital evaluation do deduct marks for table corruption.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Batteries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Batteries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔋 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Rehabilitation Engineering**, a **battery** is an electrochemical energy storage device that converts stored chemical energy into electrical energy through controlled redox (reduction–oxidation) reactions, providing the portable, reliable, and rechargeable power source required to operate a wide range of assistive and therapeutic devices such as **powered wheelchairs, electric prosthetic limbs, functional electrical stimulators (FES), cochlear implants, ventilators, and patient monitoring systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *primary cell* is a non-rechargeable battery where the electrochemical reaction is irreversible (e.g., alkaline, zinc–carbon), while a *secondary cell* (rechargeable battery) has a reversible electrochemical reaction allowing it to be recharged by applying an external current (e.g., Lead–Acid, NiCd, NiMH, Li-ion, Li-Po). In rehabilitation engineering, **secondary cells dominate** because most assistive devices demand repeated daily cycling.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Savings Account Analogy"

Imagine electricity is **money** and the battery is your **savings account**:

- **Charging the battery** = Depositing money into the bank.
- **Discharging (using the device)** = Withdrawing money to buy groceries.
- **Battery Capacity (Ah)** = The total size of your savings account.
- **Voltage (V)** = The denomination of each currency note (₹10 vs ₹500).
- **Energy (Wh)** = The **total purchasing power** of your savings (Ah × V).
- **State of Charge (SOC)** = The percentage of money currently in the account.
- **State of Health (SOH)** = How "healthy" the bank still is, considering wear and tear over years.

Just like you would not run a marathon with a wallet full of heavy ₹10 notes, you would not power a powered wheelchair with a battery that has the wrong *energy-to-weight ratio*. **Rehabilitation Engineering is essentially about picking the right "currency" for the right "device."**

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following **standard metrics** govern every battery specification in KTU problems:

- **Faraday's Constant**: $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$
- **Electron Charge**: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Avogadro's Number**: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Nominal Voltage** ($V_n$): The operating voltage per cell (e.g., **3.7 V** for Li-ion, **2.0 V** for Lead–Acid, **1.2 V** for NiMH).
- **Specific Energy**: $\text{Wh/kg}$
- **Energy Density**: $\text{Wh/L}$
- **C-Rate**: Discharge/charge current normalised to capacity (e.g., **1C** means the battery is fully discharged in 1 hour).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Rehabilitation:** A powered wheelchair needs **high specific energy** (to last 8–10 hours a day) AND **high specific power** (to climb ramps and bumps). A hearing aid needs **tiny size, low self-discharge, and steady voltage**, not raw power. Choosing the wrong chemistry is a *fatal design error* in rehabilitation device design.

## 1.4 Primary vs. Secondary vs. Reserve Batteries

| Category | Rechargeable? | Typical Use in Rehab | Examples |
|----------|---------------|----------------------|----------|
| **Primary** | No (single use) | Hearing aids, surgical tools, emergency locator beacons | Zn–MnO₂ (alkaline), Zn–Air |
| **Secondary** | Yes (cyclic) | Wheelchairs, prosthetics, ventilators, FES, exoskeletons | SLA, NiCd, NiMH, Li-ion, Li-Po |
| **Reserve** | Activated on demand | Implantable defibrillators, emergency military/space rehab gear | Thermal, water-activated |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

A battery is built from one or more **electrochemical cells**. Each cell contains:

1. **Anode (Negative Electrode):** Site of **oxidation** — loses electrons during discharge.
2. **Cathode (Positive Electrode):** Site of **reduction** — gains electrons during discharge.
3. **Electrolyte:** Ionic conductor (liquid, gel, or solid) enabling charge transfer internally.
4. **Separator:** Physical insulator preventing electrode short-circuit while permitting ion flow.
5. **Current Collector:** Metallic foil (Cu at anode, Al at cathode for Li-ion) that conducts electrons out of the cell.

The **why** behind each component:
- *Why two electrodes of different potential?* To establish a **potential difference** ($\Delta E$) that drives electron flow through an external load.
- *Why an electrolyte?* To complete the **internal circuit** by ion migration, balancing the charge.
- *Why a separator?* To prevent direct anode–cathode contact, which would cause **internal short** and thermal runaway.

## 2.2 The Nernst Equation — Theoretical Cell Voltage

For any cell, the **open-circuit voltage (OCV)** under non-standard conditions is given by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

Where:

- $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ = Standard cell potential (V)
- $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$ = Universal gas constant
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred per reaction
- $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$ = Faraday's constant
- $Q$ = Reaction quotient (activities of products over reactants)

At standard temperature ($T = 298.15\ \text{K}$), the equation simplifies to the **base-10 form**:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} Q
$$

## 2.3 Battery Capacity & Energy Relationships

The **theoretical capacity** ($Q_{\text{th}}$) of an electrode active material is:

$$
Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{n \cdot F}{M} \quad \left[\frac{\text{C}}{\text{kg}}\right]
$$

Where $M$ is the molar mass of the active species. The **practical energy** stored in a battery:

$$
E_{\text{batt}} = V_n \times C_{\text{rated}} \quad [\text{Wh}]
$$

Where $C_{\text{rated}}$ is in Ah and $V_n$ in volts. Dividing by mass/volume yields specific/energy density.

## 2.4 C-Rate, Peukert's Law & Real Capacity

**C-Rate Definition:** A 1C rate on a **10 Ah** battery means a current of **10 A** (full discharge in 1 hour). A **0.5C** rate means 5 A (full discharge in 2 hours); a **2C** rate means 20 A (full discharge in 30 minutes).

**Peukert's Law** (empirical, used for lead-acid in wheelchairs):

$$
C_p = I^k \cdot t
$$

Where:
- $C_p$ = Peukert capacity (constant for a given battery)
- $I$ = Discharge current (A)
- $t$ = Discharge time (h)
- $k$ = Peukert exponent (typically 1.1–1.3 for SLA, ~1.05 for Li-ion)

The **effective capacity** at any discharge rate is:

$$
C_{\text{eff}} = C_{\text{rated}} \left(\frac{I_{\text{rated}}}{I}\right)^{k-1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rehab Application:** A powered wheelchair draws **high current bursts** during ramp-climbing. Per Peukert's law, the **usable capacity at high current is LESS than rated** — a critical design oversight if you sized the battery for 8 hours of flat-ground use.

## 2.5 State of Charge (SOC) & Depth of Discharge (DOD)

$$
\text{SOC} = \frac{C_{\text{remaining}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{DOD} = 100\% - \text{SOC}
$$

For maximum cycle life, manufacturers recommend:
- **Lead–Acid:** Do not exceed **50% DOD** (else cycle life plummets).
- **Li-ion:** Can safely go to **80–90% DOD**.
- **NiMH/NiCd:** Tolerate **100% DOD** but suffer *memory effect* (NiCd).

## 2.6 Cycle Life vs. DOD — A Key Trade-off

Cycle life roughly follows:

$$
L_{\text{cycles}} \approx L_0 \left(\frac{\text{DOD}_0}{\text{DOD}_{\text{actual}}}\right)^n
$$

Where $L_0$ is rated cycles at $\text{DOD}_0$ and $n \approx 1$ to $2$ depending on chemistry. **Lowering DOD doubles life** — critical for cost-effective rehabilitation device maintenance.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n}\log Q$ | Nernst equation (25 °C) | V |
| 2 | $E_{\text{batt}} = V_n \times C$ | Stored energy | Wh |
| 3 | $C_p = I^k \cdot t$ | Peukert's law | Ah |
| 4 | $C_{\text{eff}} = C_{\text{rated}} \left(\frac{I_{\text{rated}}}{I}\right)^{k-1}$ | Effective capacity at load | Ah |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{th}} = \frac{nF}{M}$ | Theoretical specific charge | C/kg |
| 6 | $\text{SOC} = \frac{C_{\text{rem}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100$ | State of Charge | % |
| 7 | $\eta = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Coulombic / Energy efficiency | % |
| 8 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Instantaneous power output | W |
| 9 | $R_{\text{int}} = \frac{V_{\text{oc}} - V_{\text{load}}}{I_{\text{load}}}$ | Internal resistance | Ω |
| 10 | $\text{Energy Density} = \frac{E_{\text{batt}}}{m}$ | Specific energy | Wh/kg |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note for Students:** In KTU answer sheets, never write absolute value bars `$|x|$` inline with pipe characters inside text — write it as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid parsing issues in digital answer evaluation scripts.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility in Rehabilitation

- **Powered Wheelchairs:** Use deep-cycle **SLA** or **Li-ion** packs; the battery management system (BMS) prevents deep discharge that can brick a $2000 battery.
- **Cochlear Implants:** Use **Zn–Air primary cells** (high energy density, low self-discharge) — recharging is impractical inside the body.
- **Prosthetic Limbs (i-Limb, bebionic):** Use **Li-Po** for high power density in small form factor.
- **Hospital Ventilators & Infusion Pumps:** Use **SLA** with UPS-style backup for fail-safe operation.
- **FES Systems:** Use **rechargeable NiMH** to deliver controlled current pulses safely.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation from Thermodynamics

The Gibbs free energy change of an electrochemical reaction is:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q
$$

The electrical work obtainable from the cell at constant $T$ and $P$ equals the negative of $\Delta G$:

$$
W_{\text{elec}} = -\Delta G = nFE_{\text{cell}}
$$

Equating and dividing by $-nF$:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q
$$

Define $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = -\Delta G^{\circ} / nF$, yielding:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q
$$

Substituting $T = 298.15\ \text{K}$, $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$, $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$, and converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0257}{n}\ln Q = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0592}{n}\log_{10} Q
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — Nernst Calculation for a Rehab Device

**Problem:** A zinc–air cell used in a hearing aid has the reaction:
$$\text{Zn} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ZnO}, \quad E^{\circ} = 1.65\ \text{V},\ n = 2$$
At body temperature ($T = 310\ \text{K}$), with $Q = 0.1$, find the operating voltage.

**Step 1:** Identify parameters: $R = 8.314$, $T = 310$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$, $Q = 0.1$.

**Step 2:** Compute the thermal factor:
$$
\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{2577.34}{192{,}970} = 0.01336\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\ln Q = \ln(0.1) = -2.3026$.

**Step 4:** Apply Nernst:
$$
E_{\text{cell}} = 1.65 - (0.01336)(-2.3026) = 1.65 + 0.03077 = 1.681\ \text{V}
$$

**Conclusion:** The hearing aid cell delivers **1.68 V** at body temperature, slightly higher than standard — useful for longer device runtime.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Peukert's Law for a Powered Wheelchair

**Problem:** A powered wheelchair uses a **75 Ah SLA battery** rated at the 20-hour rate ($I_{\text{rated}} = 3.75\ \text{A}$). The Peukert exponent is $k = 1.25$. Calculate the effective capacity when the wheelchair draws **15 A** continuously.

**Step 1:** Identify parameters: $C_{\text{rated}} = 75\ \text{Ah}$, $I_{\text{rated}} = 3.75\ \text{A}$, $I = 15\ \text{A}$, $k = 1.25$.

**Step 2:** Apply Peukert's effective capacity formula:
$$
C_{\text{eff}} = C_{\text{rated}} \left(\frac{I_{\text{rated}}}{I}\right)^{k-1}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and compute the exponent:
$$
k - 1 = 0.25
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the ratio:
$$
\frac{I_{\text{rated}}}{I} = \frac{3.75}{15} = 0.25
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the power:
$$
(0.25)^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \ln 0.25} = e^{0.25 \times (-1.3863)} = e^{-0.3466} = 0.7071
$$

**Step 6:** Final capacity:
$$
C_{\text{eff}} = 75 \times 0.7071 = 53.03\ \text{Ah}
$$

**Interpretation:** The user only gets **53 Ah instead of 75 Ah** at high load — a **29% loss in usable capacity**. Designers must oversize the battery by ~40% to achieve the same runtime at high current.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Battery Sizing for a Rehabilitation Exoskeleton

**Problem:** An exoskeleton operates at an **average power of 250 W** for **6 hours/day**. Battery nominal voltage is **48 V**. Required DOD is 80% (Li-ion). Find the required rated capacity in Ah.

**Step 1:** Energy needed per day:
$$
E_{\text{day}} = P \times t = 250 \times 6 = 1500\ \text{Wh}
$$

**Step 2:** Required capacity at full DOD:
$$
C_{\text{req}} = \frac{E_{\text{day}}}{V_n} = \frac{1500}{48} = 31.25\ \text{Ah}
$$

**Step 3:** Account for 80% DOD limit:
$$
C_{\text{rated}} = \frac{C_{\text{req}}}{\text{DOD}_{\max}} = \frac{31.25}{0.80} = 39.06\ \text{Ah}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply safety margin of 20% for aging:
$$
C_{\text{final}} = 39.06 \times 1.20 = 46.88\ \text{Ah} \approx 50\ \text{Ah}
$$

**Answer:** Use a **48 V, 50 Ah Li-ion pack** (≈ 2.4 kWh).

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Battery Chemistries for Rehabilitation

| Property | SLA | NiCd | NiMH | Li-ion | Li-Po | Zn–Air |
|----------|-----|------|------|--------|-------|--------|
| **Nominal V/cell** | 2.0 | 1.2 | 1.2 | 3.7 | 3.7 | 1.65 |
| **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** | 30–40 | 40–60 | 60–120 | 150–250 | 130–200 | 350+ |
| **Cycle Life** | 200–300 | 1000+ | 500–800 | 500–2000 | 300–500 | Single use |
| **Memory Effect** | No | Yes | Mild | No | No | N/A |
| **Self-Discharge /month** | 3–5% | 10–20% | 20–30% | 2–3% | 2–3% | <2% |
| **Best Rehab Use** | Wheelchairs, backup | Hospital tools, legacy | FES, hearing aids | Prosthetics, exoskeletons | Compact prosthetics | Cochlear implants |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Battery Functional Architecture — Discharge Mode

The following Mermaid block maps the internal electrochemical and external electrical pathways during battery discharge, as used in a powered wheelchair controller.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Anode: Oxidation Site] -->|Releases e-| B(Electron Flow through External Load: Motor)
    B -->|Powers| C[Cathode: Reduction Site]
    C -->|Accepts e-| A
    D[Electrolyte] -.->|Ion Migration A to C| A
    D -.->|Completes Internal Circuit| C
    E[Separator: Porous Polymer Film] -.->|Blocks e-, Permits Ions| D
    F[BMS: Battery Management System] -->|Monitors V, I, T| A
    F -->|Monitors V, I, T| C
    F -->|Cutoff at Low SOC| G[Device: Powered Wheelchair]
    G -->|Returns Current| B
```

## 4.2 Battery Selection Decision Tree for Rehabilitation Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Rehabilitation Device Selection]) --> Q1{Is device implanted?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, hearing/Cochlear| ZnAir[Zn-Air Primary Cell]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is device portable but compact?}
    Q2 -->|Yes, Prosthetic Hand| LiPo[Li-Polymer]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is high energy + safety critical?}
    Q3 -->|Yes, Wheelchair| SLA[Sealed Lead-Acid]
    Q3 -->|High power required| LiIon[Li-ion 18650/Prismatic]
    Q3 -->|Hospital-grade backup| SLA
    Q3 -->|FES muscle stim| NiMH[NiMH Rechargeable]
    ZnAir --> End([Verify voltage, current, form factor])
    LiPo --> End
    SLA --> End
    LiIon --> End
    NiMH --> End
```

## 4.3 Charge–Discharge Cycle Flow with SOC Tracking

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Charger: Constant Current Source] -->|Charging Phase| BMS1[BMS: CC-CV Controller]
    BMS1 -->|Steps Down as Vmax approached| Cell1[Cell Pack]
    Cell1 -->|Delivers V, I| Load[Assistive Device Load]
    Load -->|Current Draw| Sensor[SOC Estimator: Coulomb Counting]
    Sensor -->|Updates SOC percentage| Display[User Display HUD]
    Sensor -->|At SOC < 20 percent| Alarm[Low Battery Alert]
    Alarm -->|Auto Cutoff at SOC < 5 percent| Cutoff[Safety Disconnect]
```

## 4.4 Energy Loss Mechanism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Input[Chemical Energy Stored] -->|Conversion| Output[Electrical Energy Delivered]
    Input -->|Loss 1: Internal Resistance I2R| Heat[Heat Loss]
    Input -->|Loss 2: Side Reactions| SEI[SEI Layer Growth]
    Input -->|Loss 3: Self-Discharge| Leak[Parasitic Drain]
    Output -->|Useful| Device[Rehab Device Function]
    Heat --> Ambient[Ambient Dissipation]
    SEI --> Degrad[Capacity Fade Over Cycles]
    Leak --> Shelf[Reduced Shelf Life]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** Mermaid diagrams in this protocol use only **alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `Cell1`, `BMS1`, `Sensor`) and **plain uppercase labels** without any markdown bold/italic tags inside the double quotes. This guarantees flawless rendering in GitHub, VS Code, and KTU's digital learning portals.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Differentiate between primary and secondary batteries with two examples each relevant to rehabilitation devices."**

**Model Answer:**

**Primary Batteries:** Non-rechargeable; electrochemical reaction is irreversible. Used in low-drain or single-use applications. *Examples in rehabilitation:* (i) **Zinc–air cells** in cochlear implants and hearing aids — high energy density, low self-discharge ideal for in-body use. (ii) **Alkaline cells** in patient monitoring thermometers and emergency call buttons.

**Secondary Batteries:** Rechargeable; electrochemical reaction is reversible by applying external current. Used in cyclic, high-drain applications. *Examples in rehabilitation:* (i) **Sealed Lead–Acid (SLA)** in powered wheelchairs — robust, cheap, deep-cycle tolerant. (ii) **Lithium-ion / Li-Polymer** in myoelectric prosthetic hands and exoskeletons — high energy density, lightweight, low self-discharge.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Define State of Charge (SOC) and Depth of Discharge (DOD). Why is DOD critical for battery life in a rehabilitation wheelchair?"**

**Model Answer:**

- **State of Charge (SOC):** The ratio of remaining capacity to rated capacity, expressed as a percentage:
$$
\text{SOC} = \frac{C_{\text{remaining}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%
$$
- **Depth of Discharge (DOD):** The complement of SOC:
$$
\text{DOD} = 100\% - \text{SOC}
$$

**Why it matters in wheelchairs:** Lead–acid batteries used in powered wheelchairs experience **accelerated cycle-life degradation** if discharged beyond 50% DOD. A 50% DOD design typically yields **~700–1000 cycles**, while 80% DOD may reduce life to **~300 cycles**. Rehabilitation patients depend on the wheelchair daily, so battery replacement is a high cost and accessibility issue. **Therefore, sizing the battery for ≤50% DOD** ensures ~3–5 years of service, balancing cost and reliability.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*"Explain the construction and working of a Lithium-ion battery. Why is it preferred over Lead–Acid for myoelectric prosthetic arms?"*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** A Li-ion cell consists of:
- **Anode:** Graphite coated on copper current collector.
- **Cathode:** Lithium metal oxide (e.g., LiCoO₂, LiFePO₄) coated on aluminium current collector.
- **Electrolyte:** LiPF₆ salt in organic carbonate solvent (e.g., EC + DMC).
- **Separator:** Microporous polyethylene or polypropylene film.

**Working (Discharge):** Lithium ions de-intercalate from the graphite anode, travel through the electrolyte, and intercalate into the cathode. Simultaneously, electrons flow through the external circuit, powering the load. During charging, an external source forces ions back to the anode.

**Half-reactions:**
$$
\text{Anode: } \text{Li}_x\text{C}_6 \rightarrow x\text{Li}^+ + x\text{e}^- + \text{C}_6
$$

$$
\text{Cathode: } \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 + x\text{Li}^+ + x\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{LiCoO}_2
$$

**Why preferred over Lead–Acid for myoelectric arms:**

| Parameter | Li-ion | Lead–Acid |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| Specific Energy | **150–250 Wh/kg** | 30–40 Wh/kg |
| Weight for same energy | ~1 kg | ~5 kg |
| Cycle Life | 500–2000 | 200–300 |
| Maintenance | None | Periodic topping |
| Mounting orientation | Any | Upright preferred |

A myoelectric arm is worn on the body all day; **weight reduction from 5 kg to 1 kg directly improves user comfort, dexterity, and acceptance**. Additionally, Li-ion's higher voltage (3.7 V/cell) reduces cell count and pack size.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*"A 24 V, 40 Ah SLA battery powers a wheelchair. The motor draws 20 A for 1.5 hours, then 5 A for 4 hours. Calculate the total energy delivered and average current. Peukert exponent is 1.2."*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total charge withdrawn:**
$$
Q = (20 \times 1.5) + (5 \times 4) = 30 + 20 = 50\ \text{Ah}
$$

**Step 2 — Total energy delivered (nominal):**
$$
E = V_n \times Q = 24 \times 50 = 1200\ \text{Wh} = 1.2\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Total time:**
$$
t_{\text{total}} = 1.5 + 4 = 5.5\ \text{h}
$$

**Step 4 — Average current:**
$$
I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{Q}{t_{\text{total}}} = \frac{50}{5.5} = 9.09\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Peukert correction (approx using weighted k):**
Rated discharge is 20-hour rate → $I_{\text{rated}} = 40/20 = 2\ \text{A}$. For the high-current phase, effective capacity scales:
$$
C_{\text{eff,high}} = 40 \left(\frac{2}{20}\right)^{0.2} = 40 \times (0.1)^{0.2} = 40 \times 0.631 = 25.25\ \text{Ah}
$$

**Step 6 — Adjusted total capacity available (sum of high + low phase):**
The high phase consumes $30\ \text{Ah}$ from only $25.25\ \text{Ah}$ available at that rate → **battery will be deeply discharged**, indicating undersizing for the load profile.

**Step 7 — Conclusion:** The 40 Ah battery is **undersized for the 20 A peak**. Recommend a 60–70 Ah battery for the wheelchair to maintain DOD ≤ 50%.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*"Compare NiCd, NiMH, and Li-ion batteries in terms of specific energy, memory effect, cycle life, and environmental impact. Which is best for a portable FES (Functional Electrical Stimulation) device used by a spinal-cord injury patient at home?"*

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | NiCd | NiMH | Li-ion |
|-----------|------|------|--------|
| Specific Energy | 40–60 Wh/kg | 60–120 Wh/kg | 150–250 Wh/kg |
| Memory Effect | **Severe** | Mild | None |
| Cycle Life | 1000+ | 500–800 | 500–2000 |
| Toxicity | **High (Cd)** | Low (Ni, lanthanides) | Low–Moderate |
| Self-discharge/month | 10–20% | 20–30% | 2–3% |
| Cost per Wh | Low | Low | Moderate–High |

**Best Choice: Li-ion** for a portable FES device because:
1. **No memory effect** → patient can top up at any time without capacity loss (critical for home use where full discharge cycles are unlikely).
2. **Low self-discharge** → holds charge during storage (often intermittent use by rehab patient).
3. **Lightweight** → easy to wear on a belt-clip or carry strap.
4. **High cycle life** → 3–5 years of daily use without replacement.

**NiCd alternative** is acceptable if the device is in a **hospital setting** where frequent full discharge is feasible and cost is paramount.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*"With a neat block diagram, explain the role of a Battery Management System (BMS) in a Li-ion powered exoskeleton. List any four BMS functions."*

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (Verbal Description for KTU Answer Sheet):**

```
   [Charger] → [BMS Controller] → [Cell Pack: 13S Li-ion]
                    ↓
   [Sensors: V, I, T per cell] → feedback to [BMS]
                    ↓
   [Output to Motor Drivers & Actuators]
                    ↓
   [HMI Display: SOC, SOH, Faults]
```

**Four BMS Functions:**

1. **Cell Balancing:** Equalizes voltage across all 13 series cells to prevent over-charge of weaker cells and under-charge of stronger ones. Uses passive (resistor bleed) or active (capacitor/inductor shuttling) methods.

2. **Over-Voltage & Under-Voltage Protection:** Disconnects charger/load when any cell exceeds 4.2 V (over) or drops below 2.5 V (under). Prevents **thermal runaway** and irreversible capacity loss.

3. **Over-Current & Short-Circuit Protection:** Limits discharge current to safe C-rates (e.g., 2C continuous, 5C peak). For an exoskeleton drawing 30 A peak from a 10 Ah pack, BMS enforces thresholds.

4. **Thermal Monitoring & Cutoff:** Sensors (NTC thermistors) per cell group. If temperature exceeds **60 °C**, BMS reduces current or shuts down. Critical for rehab users where the device is worn close to skin.

**Additional functions** (if asked): SOC estimation via coulomb counting + Kalman filter, communication (CAN/SMBus) to the exoskeleton controller, data logging for predictive maintenance.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Batteries questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if $Q$ is given in Ah but answer is in C, or vice versa. Always state: $1\ \text{Ah} = 3600\ \text{C}$.
> 2. **Mixing Nernst and Nernst-25°C:** Many students write $0.0257/n$ instead of $0.0592/n$ when the question states "at 25 °C" — the 0.0592 is the **base-10 log** form. Examiners check this.
> 3. **Ignoring Peukert effect in design problems:** Sizing a battery for wheelchair current load **without** applying Peukert's law results in a practically undersized system. Examiners award the "design thinking" mark only if Peukert is mentioned.
> 4. **Confusing SOC and DOD:** SOC = 20% ⇒ DOD = 80%. Many students reverse this.
> 5. **Not mentioning safety/Mandate of BMS for Li-ion:** A Li-ion design answer without BMS loses at least 2 marks under the "Safety and Standards" rubric.
> 6. **Sketching diagrams without labels:** All Mermaid-style flowcharts in the answer sheet must include **labeled arrows and units**.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔋 A **battery** is an electrochemical energy storage device; rehabilitation devices depend on **secondary (rechargeable) cells** for cyclic use.
- ⚡ The **Nernst equation** gives the operating voltage of a cell: $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$; at 25 °C use $0.0592/n \log Q$.
- 📊 **C-Rate** normalises current to capacity: $I = C \times \text{C-rate}$; 1C = full discharge in 1 hour.
- 📉 **Peukert's Law** ($C_p = I^k t$) reduces effective capacity at high discharge — vital for wheelchair and exoskeleton design where peak currents occur.
- 🔄 **SOC + DOD** govern cycle life: lower DOD ⇒ exponentially longer life, especially for SLA.
- 🛡️ **Li-ion always needs a BMS** for cell balancing, voltage/temperature cutoff, and SOC estimation.
- 🦾 For **myoelectric prosthetics** → Li-Po or Li-ion (lightweight, high power density).
- 🦽 For **powered wheelchairs** → SLA (cost) or Li-ion (performance).
- 👂 For **cochlear/hearing aids** → Zn–air primary (very high energy density, low self-discharge).
- ⚡ For **FES** → NiMH (no memory, safe) or Li-ion (lightweight, low self-discharge).
- ♻️ **Environmental:** Avoid NiCd where possible (cadmium toxicity); recycle Li-ion and SLA at authorised centres.
- 🧮 **Memorise these constants:** $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$, $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$, $1\ \text{Ah} = 3600\ \text{C}$.
- 🩺 **Patient-safety priority:** Battery failure in rehab devices can cause falls, immobility, or death — design conservatively and oversize by 20–30%.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Boxes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gear Boxes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Gear Box** (also termed a *transmission gearbox* or *speed reducer*) is a sealed, precision-machined mechanical assembly comprising a series of meshing gears, shafts, bearings, and housings that modulates the **torque**, **rotational speed**, and **directional output** of a power source (typically an electric motor) to match the load requirements of a driven machine. In the domain of **Rehabilitation Engineering**, gear boxes constitute a critical sub-system in powered wheelchairs, robotic exoskeletons, prosthetic knee/elbow units, and functional electrical stimulation (FES) ergometers, where they translate high-speed, low-torque motor output into low-speed, high-torque actuation required for human-joint biomechanics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Within the PEBRT752 (Rehabilitation Engineering) framework, gear boxes are studied under the broader theme of *actuation and transmission subsystems* that interface electromechanical prime movers with biomechanical loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> *A gear box is a mechanical device that transmits power from one rotating shaft to another by means of successively meshing gears of different diameters, thereby altering the speed-torque characteristics of the input shaft.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bicycle Chain on Steroids"

Imagine you are cycling up a steep hill. On level ground, your legs can pedal **fast** (high RPM) with little effort (low force). On a hill, you instinctively **shift to a lower gear** — your pedals now turn slowly, but each push delivers enormous force to the rear wheel. 

A **gear box does the exact same thing**, but automatically, precisely, and continuously, between an **electric motor** (the "legs") and a **wheel, joint, or gripper** (the "rear wheel of the bike"). 

- **Low gear ratio** (e.g., 100:1) → Motor spins 100 times, output spins 1 time. Torque is multiplied 100× (minus frictional losses).
- **High gear ratio** (e.g., 1:1) → 1:1 transfer, used when speed matters more than force.

> [!TIP]
> **Rehabilitation Context Example:** In a powered exoskeleton designed to help a stroke survivor walk, the hip motor may spin at **6,000 RPM** but the human hip joint only moves at perhaps **60 RPM** at walking pace. A **planetary gear box with a 100:1 ratio** bridges this gap perfectly, while multiplying the motor's modest 2 Nm of torque into a usable 180 Nm at the joint (after losses).

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotational Speed | $N$ | **RPM** (revolutions/min) | Primary input/output metric |
| Torque | $T$ | **N·m** (Newton-meter) | Output is multiplied in reducers |
| Power | $P$ | **W** (Watts) or **HP** (1 HP = **745.7 W**) | Conserved minus losses |
| Gear Ratio | $i$ | Dimensionless | Input teeth / Output teeth |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | **%** | Typically **60–98%** depending on stage count |
| Module (gear size) | $m$ | **mm** | Standardized for interchangeability |
| Pitch Circle Diameter | $D$ | **mm** | $D = m \cdot z$ |
| Backlash | $b$ | **mm** or **arc-min** | Critical for precision positioning |
| Centre Distance | $a$ | **mm** | Between two meshing gears |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Gear Boxes (KTU-Relevant Taxonomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Axis 1 — By Gear Geometry:**
> 1. **Spur Gear Box** — Parallel shafts, straight teeth, simplest, loudest.
> 2. **Helical Gear Box** — Parallel/crossed shafts, angled teeth, quieter, higher load capacity.
> 3. **Bevel Gear Box** — Intersecting shafts (typically 90°), conical pitch surfaces.
> 4. **Worm Gear Box** — Non-intersecting, non-parallel shafts; very high ratios in single stage.
> 5. **Planetary (Epicyclic) Gear Box** — Coaxial input/output, compact, very high efficiency, dominant in robotics.
> 6. **Harmonic Drive** — Strain-wave gearing; near-zero backlash; used in surgical robots and prosthetic wrists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Axis 2 — By Application in Rehabilitation:**
> 1. **Power Wheelchair Drive Gear Boxes** — typically 2-stage planetary, ratio 20:1 to 50:1.
> 2. **Prosthetic Joint Gear Boxes** — harmonic drives for prosthetic knees (e.g., Ottobock C-Leg).
> 3. **Exoskeleton Joint Gear Boxes** — quasi-direct-drive (QDD) planetary or cycloidal.
> 4. **Hospital Bed / Hoist Gear Boxes** — worm gear for self-locking safety.
> 5. **FES Ergometer / Robotic Therapy Gear Boxes** — low-backlash planetary for repeatability.

---

## 1.5 Why Gear Boxes Matter in Rehabilitation Engineering

- **Biomechanical Matching:** Human joints operate at low angular velocities (knee flexion during walking ≈ 100°/s ≈ 17 RPM). Motors are typically far faster.
- **Torque Amplification:** Required joint torques (e.g., hip extension in stair ascent) can exceed **100 Nm**, while small BLDC motors produce only **1–5 Nm**.
- **Backlash Control:** Especially for reciprocal movements (gait), low backlash is critical to prevent oscillation.
- **Safety (Self-Locking):** Worm gear boxes can be non-back-drivable — a critical safety feature in patient-lifting devices.
- **Compactness:** Planetary gear boxes deliver the highest **torque density (Nm/kg)** — vital for wearable exoskeletons.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

A gear box works on the principle of **meshing teeth transferring tangential force at the pitch circle**. Two meshing gears with tooth counts $z_1$ and $z_2$ rotating at speeds $N_1$ and $N_2$ satisfy the **law of gearing** (also called the *Fundamental Law of Conjugate Action*):

> The common normal at the point of contact between two meshing teeth must always pass through the **fixed pitch point** (the instantaneous centre of rotation). This guarantees a **constant angular velocity ratio** throughout the engagement.

The mathematical consequence is:

$$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{z_2}{z_1} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \text{Gear Ratio } (i)$$

This single equation is the **bedrock of all gear-box calculations** in KTU examinations.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The transmission of power through a gear train follows a strict, sequential logic:

1. **Power Input:** Electric motor delivers rotational power $P_{in}$ at angular speed $\omega_{in}$ and torque $T_{in}$.
2. **Engagement:** The *pinion* (smaller gear, $z_1$) drives the *gear* (larger gear, $z_2$) at the **pitch point** (the point of tangency of the two pitch circles).
3. **Tangential Force Equality:** Because the contact is essentially a single point (ignoring elasticity), the tangential force $F_t$ at the pitch point is the same on both gears:
   $$F_t = \frac{T_1}{r_1} = \frac{T_2}{r_2}$$
   where $r_1$ and $r_2$ are pitch-circle radii.
4. **Torque Transformation:** Since $r_2 > r_1$ in a reducer, $T_2 > T_1$ — torque is amplified.
5. **Speed Reduction:** Since the linear velocities at the pitch point must be equal ($v_1 = v_2$), and $v = \omega r$, a larger radius implies lower angular velocity. Hence $N_2 < N_1$.
6. **Power Loss:** Some power is lost to friction, windage, and churning of lubricant, reducing efficiency $\eta < 1$.
7. **Power Output:** $P_{out} = \eta \cdot P_{in} = T_{out} \cdot \omega_{out}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why torque increases and speed decreases simultaneously (and how power is "preserved-ish"):**
> The trade-off is governed by energy conservation. In an *ideal* gear box, $T_{in} \cdot \omega_{in} = T_{out} \cdot \omega_{out}$. So if speed drops 10×, torque must rise 10×. In a *real* gear box, friction means $T_{out} \cdot \omega_{out} = \eta \cdot T_{in} \cdot \omega_{in}$, with $\eta$ in **[0.6, 0.98]**.

---

## 2.3 Multi-Stage Gear Boxes (Compound Trains)

When a single stage cannot provide the required ratio, gears are arranged in series — a **compound gear train**. The overall ratio is the **product** of the individual stage ratios.

$$i_{total} = i_1 \times i_2 \times i_3 \cdots i_n$$

For a **train value (TV)** in a more general multi-shaft train:

$$TV = \frac{\text{Speed of Driver}}{\text{Speed of Driven}} = \frac{\text{Product of Driving Teeth}}{\text{Product of Driven Teeth}} = (-1)^m \text{ (for sign of meshing)}$$

where $m$ is the number of **external mesh points**. Each external mesh reverses direction; idlers do not affect the magnitude.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table — Memorize for KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{z_2}{z_1} = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$ | Gear ratio (speed reduction) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot i \cdot \eta$ | Torque amplification (reducer) | N·m |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Power from speed and torque | W |
| 4 | $P_{out} = \eta \cdot P_{in}$ | Power accounting with efficiency | W |
| 5 | $F_t = \dfrac{2 T}{D} = \dfrac{2 T}{m \cdot z}$ | Tangential tooth force | N |
| 6 | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi m z N}{60}$ | Pitch-line velocity | m/s |
| 7 | $a = \dfrac{m(z_1 + z_2)}{2}$ | Centre distance | mm |
| 8 | $i_{total} = \prod_{k=1}^{n} i_k$ | Compound train overall ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta_{total} = \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2 \cdots \eta_n$ | Efficiency of multi-stage train | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\omega_{out} = \dfrac{\omega_{in}}{i \cdot \eta}$ | (No — *re-statement for clarity*) $\omega_{out} = \omega_{in}/i$ | rad/s |
| 11 | $D = m \cdot z$ | Pitch diameter from module | mm |
| 12 | $L = \dfrac{z_1 + z_2}{2}$ (path of contact, in modules) | Length of contact | mm |
| 13 | $p_c = \pi m$ | Circular pitch | mm |
| 14 | $\eta_{worm} = \dfrac{\tan \gamma}{\tan(\gamma + \phi)}$ | Worm gear efficiency | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚠ LaTeX Isolation Note (Board-Exam-Safe):**
> Note how all absolute-value and ratio symbols are written using `\vert`, `\mid`, or `\dfrac` — never with the vertical pipe `\vert` character — to prevent table-syntax corruption in Markdown.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Gear Box Type Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Powered Wheelchair Wheels | 2-stage **planetary** | High torque density, coaxial output through wheel hub |
| Prosthetic Knee (e.g., C-Leg) | **Harmonic drive** | Near-zero backlash, compact, high ratio in 1 stage |
| Robotic Therapy Arm (e.g., InMotion ARM) | **Quasi-Direct-Drive planetary** | Back-drivable, transparent for patient safety |
| Ceiling-Mounted Patient Hoist | **Worm gear** | Self-locking — patient cannot fall even on power loss |
| Stair-Climbing Wheelchair | **Bevel + planetary** | 90° redirection to drive tracked wheels |
| Surgical Robot (Da Vinci-style) | **Harmonic** | Zero backlash for sub-mm precision |
| Functional Electrical Stimulation Ergometer | **Spur/helical reducer** | Smooth, repeatable resistance for patient testing |

---

## 2.6 The Concept of *Train Value* and Idler Gears

A subtle but examination-favorite point: an **idler gear** changes the **direction** of rotation without changing the **magnitude** of the speed ratio. It therefore:

- **Does** flip the sign in the train value.
- **Does NOT** appear in the magnitude of the overall ratio if both pinion and gear count are already on the appropriate shafts.

For a wheel-chair with 2 idlers in its differential steering, this is the difference between the wheels turning "in phase" vs "out of phase" during turning maneuvers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are exhaustive, step-by-step, and follow KTU valuation-key expectations. No step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fundamental Gear Ratio from Pitch-Line Velocity Equality

### Statement
Show that the speed ratio of two meshing gears equals the inverse ratio of their teeth numbers.

### Given
- Pinion (driver) with $z_1$ teeth and pitch radius $r_1$ rotating at $N_1$ RPM.
- Gear (driven) with $z_2$ teeth and pitch radius $r_2$ rotating at $N_2$ RPM.
- Standard module $m$ (in mm), so $r_1 = \dfrac{m z_1}{2}$ and $r_2 = \dfrac{m z_2}{2}$.

### Derivation

**Step 1.** Linear velocity of a point on the pitch circle of the pinion:

$$v_1 = \omega_1 r_1 = \frac{2 \pi N_1}{60} \cdot \frac{m z_1}{2}$$

**Step 2.** Linear velocity of a point on the pitch circle of the gear:

$$v_2 = \omega_2 r_2 = \frac{2 \pi N_2}{60} \cdot \frac{m z_2}{2}$$

**Step 3.** At the pitch point (the instant of tooth engagement), the surfaces roll without slipping. Hence the tangential velocities are **equal in magnitude and direction** (relative to the contact point):

$$v_1 = v_2$$

**Step 4.** Substituting:

$$\frac{2 \pi N_1}{60} \cdot \frac{m z_1}{2} = \frac{2 \pi N_2}{60} \cdot \frac{m z_2}{2}$$

**Step 5.** Cancel the common factor $\dfrac{2 \pi m}{60 \cdot 2}$ from both sides:

$$N_1 \cdot z_1 = N_2 \cdot z_2$$

**Step 6.** Rearrange for the speed ratio $i$:

$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{z_2}{z_1} = \frac{D_2}{D_1}$$

**Q.E.D.** ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque Transformation in a Single-Stage Reducer

### Statement
Derive the relationship between input and output torque of a single-stage gear reducer, accounting for efficiency.

### Given
- Input torque $T_1$ at angular speed $\omega_1$.
- Output torque $T_2$ at angular speed $\omega_2$.
- Efficiency $\eta$ (per stage).

### Derivation

**Step 1.** Ideal (lossless) power conservation between input and output shafts:

$$T_1 \cdot \omega_1 = T_2^{\text{ideal}} \cdot \omega_2$$

**Step 2.** From the fundamental ratio, $\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = i = \dfrac{z_2}{z_1}$, so $\omega_2 = \dfrac{\omega_1}{i}$.

**Step 3.** Substitute:

$$T_1 \cdot \omega_1 = T_2^{\text{ideal}} \cdot \frac{\omega_1}{i}$$

**Step 4.** Solve for the *ideal* output torque:

$$T_2^{\text{ideal}} = T_1 \cdot i$$

**Step 5.** Account for losses. Only a fraction $\eta$ of input power emerges as output power:

$$T_2 \cdot \omega_2 = \eta \cdot T_1 \cdot \omega_1$$

**Step 6.** Solve for the *real* output torque:

$$T_2 = \eta \cdot T_1 \cdot i$$

**Step 7.** Therefore the torque **amplification factor** of a single reducer stage is $\eta \cdot i$:

$$\boxed{\;T_2 = \eta \cdot i \cdot T_1\;}$$

**Insight:** For a 100:1 reducer with $\eta = 0.9$, a 2 N·m input torque yields $T_2 = 0.9 \times 100 \times 2 = 180$ N·m — typical of an exoskeleton hip motor. ∎

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Transmitted as a Function of Tooth Geometry

### Statement
Express the power transmitted by a meshing gear pair in terms of tooth force, pitch-line velocity, and geometry.

### Derivation

**Step 1.** Power on the input shaft:

$$P = T_1 \cdot \omega_1 = T_1 \cdot \frac{2 \pi N_1}{60}$$

**Step 2.** Express torque in terms of tangential tooth force $F_t$:

$$T_1 = F_t \cdot r_1 = F_t \cdot \frac{m z_1}{2}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute into the power equation:

$$P = F_t \cdot \frac{m z_1}{2} \cdot \frac{2 \pi N_1}{60} = F_t \cdot \frac{\pi m z_1 N_1}{60}$$

**Step 4.** Recognise the pitch-line velocity $v$:

$$v = \frac{\pi m z_1 N_1}{60} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = F_t \cdot v$$

**Step 5.** Concluding compact form:

$$\boxed{\;P \;=\; F_t \cdot v \;=\; \frac{\pi m z_1 N_1 F_t}{60}\;}$$

**Units Check:** $F_t$ in N, $v$ in m/s, gives W. ✓

**Rehabilitation Application:** A powered wheelchair's wheel gear-box design begins by specifying the maximum hill-climb force, say $F_t = 200$ N, at a wheel peripheral speed $v = 1.5$ m/s, hence $P = 300$ W. This sets the motor size. ∎

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Compound Train Ratio (Worked Analytical)

### Statement
A **3-stage compound reducer** (typical of a powered wheelchair) has the following tooth counts:

- Stage 1: pinion $z_{1a} = 14$, gear $z_{1b} = 56$ on intermediate shaft-1.
- Stage 2: pinion $z_{2a} = 16$, gear $z_{2b} = 64$ on intermediate shaft-2.
- Stage 3: pinion $z_{3a} = 12$, gear $z_{3b} = 60$ on output shaft.

Find the overall ratio.

### Derivation

**Step 1.** Stage ratios:

$$i_1 = \frac{z_{1b}}{z_{1a}} = \frac{56}{14} = 4$$

$$i_2 = \frac{z_{2b}}{z_{2a}} = \frac{64}{16} = 4$$

$$i_3 = \frac{z_{3b}}{z_{3a}} = \frac{60}{12} = 5$$

**Step 2.** Overall ratio (product):

$$i_{total} = i_1 \cdot i_2 \cdot i_3 = 4 \cdot 4 \cdot 5 = 80$$

**Step 3.** If input motor speed is $N_{in} = 2400$ RPM and efficiency per stage is $\eta_s = 0.96$:

$$N_{out} = \frac{N_{in}}{i_{total}} = \frac{2400}{80} = 30 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4.** Output torque for a motor torque of $T_{in} = 1.5$ N·m:

$$\eta_{total} = (0.96)^3 = 0.8847$$

$$T_{out} = \eta_{total} \cdot i_{total} \cdot T_{in} = 0.8847 \times 80 \times 1.5 = 106.16 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5.** Output power:

$$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi \cdot 30 \cdot 106.16}{60} = 333.5 \text{ W}$$

Compared to input power $P_{in} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot 2400 \cdot 1.5}{60} = 376.99$ W, giving a confirmed efficiency of $333.5/376.99 = 0.8847$. ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Exam Tip:** Always verify the efficiency from both directions when the question gives you both input and output power — it is the mark of a careful student.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Centre Distance and Module Compatibility

### Statement
Two meshing spur gears have $z_1 = 20$ and $z_2 = 50$. If the module is $m = 2$ mm, find the centre distance and the pitch diameters.

### Derivation

**Step 1.** Pitch diameters:

$$D_1 = m \cdot z_1 = 2 \cdot 20 = 40 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_2 = m \cdot z_2 = 2 \cdot 50 = 100 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2.** Centre distance (sum of pitch radii):

$$a = \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2} = \frac{40 + 100}{2} = 70 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3.** Cross-check with the alternative formula:

$$a = \frac{m (z_1 + z_2)}{2} = \frac{2 \cdot 70}{2} = 70 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Design Insight:** In a prosthetic hand gear-train, the centre distance dictates the package size. ∎

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Slip, Train Value, and Direction of Rotation in a Compound Train

### Statement
Determine the **direction of rotation** of the output shaft of a 4-mesh compound train.

### Rule
Each external mesh **reverses** the direction. Internal (ring) meshes **preserve** direction.

### Derivation

If the number of external meshes is $m$ and internal meshes is $k$:

$$\text{Direction} = (-1)^{m+k_{\text{internal}}}$$

For a 4-mesh external-only train: $(-1)^4 = +1$ → same direction as input.

For a 3-mesh external-only train: $(-1)^3 = -1$ → opposite direction.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most common "sign-of-rotation" trick question in KTU ESE papers on gear boxes. The student who forgets the sign loses 2 marks.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table for KTU Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix to quickly identify which formula to apply in the exam:**

| Problem Statement Contains... | Use Formula # | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|
| "...ratio of speeds..." | (1) | Forgetting sign of rotation |
| "...torque on output shaft..." | (2) | Omitting efficiency |
| "...power transmitted..." | (3) | Mixing rad/s with RPM |
| "...centre distance..." | (7) | Using **diametral pitch** (in inches) vs **module** (in mm) — be careful! |
| "...compound train..." | (8) and (9) | Product of ratios, not sum |
| "...self-locking worm..." | (14) | $\eta < 0.5$ for self-locking — check lead angle |
| "...prosthetic / wheelchair application..." | (2) + (3) | Always include $\eta$ for realistic answers |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid diagrams** render conceptual architecture. Mermaid cannot natively draw physical gear meshes, so we represent them as **functional block-flow architectures** mapping the power-flow path, ratio transformations, and subsystem interactions.

---

## 4.1 Diagram A — Functional Block Architecture of a Single-Stage Gear Box

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Mtr["MOTOR\nInput Shaft\nPin N1 RPM\nTorque T1"] -->|"Power Pin"| Pin["PINION\nz1 teeth\nPitch D1"]
    Pin -->|"Mesh Contact\nTangential Force Ft\nPitch Point P"| Gear["GEAR\nz2 teeth\nPitch D2"]
    Gear -->|"Power Pout\nNo-load = eta times Pin"| Out["OUTPUT SHAFT\nSpeed N2 RPM\nTorque T2"]
    Mtr -.->|"Efficiency eta\n0.60 to 0.98"| Out
    classDef power fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B85C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gear fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#F4A6A6,stroke:#7B1F1F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Mtr,Out power
    class Pin,Gear gear
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Power flows **left to right** from motor to output.
- The meshing interface (orange arrow) is where the **tangential force is exchanged** at the pitch point.
- The red dashed arrow (loss block) represents efficiency degradation — typically due to friction in the mesh and bearings, and lubricant churning.

---

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Bat["BATTERY\n24 to 48 V DC"] --> Inv["MOTOR CONTROLLER\nPWM Driver"]
    Inv -->|"PWM signals\nCurrent feedback"| BLDC["BLDC MOTOR\n2400 RPM\n1.5 N·m"]
    BLDC -->|"Input Pin\nShaft 1"| STG1["STAGE 1\nSpur Pair\n14 to 56\nRatio 4 colon 1"]
    STG1 -->|"Shaft 2\n600 RPM"| STG2["STAGE 2\nPlanetary\n16 to 64\nRatio 4 colon 1"]
    STG2 -->|"Shaft 3\n150 RPM"| STG3["STAGE 3\nPlanetary\n12 to 60\nRatio 5 colon 1"]
    STG3 -->|"Shaft 4\n30 RPM\nTorque 106 N·m"| WHL["WHEEL HUB\nDrive Wheel\nDiameter 0.3 m\nSpeed 0.47 m per s"]
    WHL --> Push["PATIENT PUSH\nManoeuvring\nClimbing 8 deg Ramp"]
    classDef elec fill:#9EC5FE,stroke:#023E8A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Blue blocks** = Electrical path.
- **Green blocks** = Mechanical transmission path (the gear box).

This is the canonical *electro-mechanical-human* interface in any rehabilitation device.

## 4.5 Diagram E — Worm Gear Self-Locking Physics (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W["WORM\nLead angle gamma\nTypically 3 to 7 deg"] -->|Rotates| WG["WORM GEAR\nTeeth on wheel"]
    WG --> WL["LOAD\nPatient on hoist"]
    WL -.->|"Tries to rotate\nworm gear back"| WG
    WG -.->|"Resisted by\nfriction angle phi"| WL
    subgraph L["Self-locking Condition"]
        L1["gamma less than phi"] --> L2["Eta drops below 50 percent"] --> L3["Reverse motion is\nphysically impossible"]
    end
    W --- L
    classDef ok fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef warn fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class W,WG,WL warn
    class L1,L2,L3 ok
```

**Critical insight:** Self-locking is the **defining safety feature** that makes worm gear boxes the default choice for patient hoists, lift chairs, and stair-lifts. The KTU examiner expects you to mention the **condition** $\gamma < \phi$ (lead angle < friction angle) and its relation to efficiency $\eta < 0.5$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme** assessment pattern: Part A (3 marks) short-answer, Part B (14 marks) with internal choice and 7+7 sub-parts. Solutions are written in **valuation-key style** — stepwise, no skipped reasoning, with units on every numerical answer.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define a gear box. State any two types of gear boxes used in rehabilitation devices, with one application of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for types + applications)

A **gear box** is a mechanical assembly of meshing gears, shafts, and bearings that transmits power from an input shaft to an output shaft while modifying the speed-torque characteristics according to a fixed ratio $i = N_1/N_2 = z_2/z_1$.

**Type 1 — Planetary (Epicyclic) Gear Box** *(1 mark)*:
Used in **powered wheelchairs** and **exoskeletons**, where its coaxial input-output geometry, high torque density (Nm/kg), and efficiency of 90–97% per stage make it ideal for compact, wearable designs.

**Type 2 — Harmonic Drive Gear Box** *(1 mark)*:
Used in **prosthetic wrists and robotic surgical tools**, where its near-zero backlash (typically < 1 arc-minute) and very high single-stage ratio (30:1 to 320:1) enable sub-millimetre precision in patient-interactive tasks.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**State and explain the *law of gearing*. Why is it important in gear-box design?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks — 1.5 for statement, 1.5 for significance)

**Statement (1.5 marks):**
The *law of gearing*, also called the *fundamental law of conjugate tooth action*, states that:

> *The common normal to the tooth profiles at the point of contact must, at every instant of engagement, pass through a fixed point on the line joining the centres of the two gears — the **pitch point**.*

Equivalently, this means the **angular velocity ratio of the two gears remains constant** throughout the mesh cycle, ensuring smooth transmission without vibration or impact.

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
- It guarantees a **constant velocity ratio**, preventing jerky motion that would be harmful in rehabilitation devices.
- It is the theoretical foundation for designing **involute tooth profiles**, which are the universal standard in modern gear boxes.
- It minimises noise, wear, and dynamic loading — critical for devices used on or near the human body.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> *The two most common mark-deduction causes in gear-box problems are:*
> 1. **Omitting the efficiency term** when the question states "given that the gear box has an efficiency of …" — examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Confusing module (mm) with diametral pitch (inches)** — KTU 2024 syllabus uses SI modules exclusively. If you write $P_d$ you will likely be marked wrong.
> 3. **Forgetting the sign of rotation** in compound train questions — a 2-mark deduction.

---

### Question B-A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

A powered **exoskeleton hip joint** is driven by a BLDC motor with the following specifications:

- **Motor speed:** $N_1 = 3000$ RPM
- **Motor torque:** $T_1 = 2.5$ N·m
- **Gear box:** 2-stage compound spur reducer
- **Stage 1:** pinion $z_{1a} = 18$, gear $z_{1b} = 72$
- **Stage 2:** pinion $z_{2a} = 15$, gear $z_{2b} = 90$
- **Efficiency per stage:** $\eta_s = 0.95$

#### (a) [7 Marks] Calculate the output speed, output torque, and the output power of the gear box.

**Step 1 — Stage Ratios:**

$$i_1 = \frac{z_{1b}}{z_{1a}} = \frac{72}{18} = 4$$

$$i_2 = \frac{z_{2b}}{z_{2a}} = \frac{90}{15} = 6$$

**Step 2 — Overall Ratio:**

$$i_{total} = i_1 \cdot i_2 = 4 \times 6 = 24$$

**Step 3 — Output Speed:**

$$N_2 = \frac{N_1}{i_{total}} = \frac{3000}{24} = 125 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4 — Overall Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{total} = (0.95)^2 = 0.9025$$

**Step 5 — Output Torque:**

$$T_2 = \eta_{total} \cdot i_{total} \cdot T_1 = 0.9025 \times 24 \times 2.5 = 54.15 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6 — Output Power:**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N_2 T_2}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 125 \times 54.15}{60} = 708.6 \text{ W}$$

#### (b) [7 Marks] The exoskeleton must deliver at least 80 N·m at the hip during stair ascent. Determine the **minimum number of additional stages** required, and state the design implications for the patient.

**Step 1 — Required Output Torque:**

$$T_{required} = 80 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Torque Deficit:**

The current design gives $T_2 = 54.15$ N·m, which is **insufficient**.

**Step 3 — Required Overall Ratio (assuming one more stage with ratio $i_3$ and efficiency 0.95):**

$$T_{required} = \eta_{total} \cdot i_1 \cdot i_2 \cdot i_3 \cdot T_1$$

$$80 = (0.95)^3 \cdot 4 \cdot 6 \cdot i_3 \cdot 2.5$$

$$80 = 0.857 \cdot 24 \cdot i_3 \cdot 2.5$$

$$80 = 51.43 \cdot i_3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad i_3 = \frac{80}{51.43} = 1.555$$

**Step 4 — Rounding up to a feasible integer ratio:**

The minimum practical integer ratio is **$i_3 = 2$** (e.g., pinion 20 teeth, gear 40 teeth). 

**Step 5 — New Output Torque with $i_3 = 2$:**

$$T_{new} = (0.95)^3 \cdot 24 \cdot 2 \cdot 2.5 = 102.86 \text{ N·m} > 80 \text{ N·m} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Final Output Speed:**

$$N_{final} = \frac{3000}{4 \times 6 \times 2} = 62.5 \text{ RPM}$$

**Design Implications (Part b conclusion):**

- **One additional stage** is required, taking the design from 2 to 3 stages.
- The hip joint now rotates at **62.5 RPM** (= 6.54 rad/s), which is within the comfortable walking speed envelope (1.0–2.5 rad/s for normal walking, up to 5 rad/s for brisk walking/stair ascent).
- **Weight and bulk** increase — challenge for wearable design; may require a **planetary** final stage to keep the package coaxial with the femur.
- **Backlash accumulates** with stages; should select a **low-backlash** Stage 3.
- **Efficiency drops** to $\eta = 0.857$, increasing motor current demand and battery heat by ~10%.

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]`

A **patient lift (hoist)** uses a worm-gear box to raise and lower a 120 kg patient. The hoist drum diameter is 200 mm, the worm has 4 starts (leads), and the worm wheel has 60 teeth. The worm is driven by a 1450 RPM motor with a power of 750 W. The worm-gear pair has an efficiency of 40%.

#### (a) [7 Marks] Calculate the speed of the worm wheel, the lifting speed of the patient, and the lifting force.

**Step 1 — Gear Ratio of Worm-Worm Wheel:**

For a worm with $z_{worm} = 4$ starts driving a wheel with $z_{wheel} = 60$ teeth:

$$i = \frac{z_{wheel}}{z_{worm}} = \frac{60}{4} = 15$$

**Step 2 — Worm Wheel Speed:**

$$N_{wheel} = \frac{N_{worm}}{i} = \frac{1450}{15} = 96.67 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3 — Drum Peripheral Speed = Lifting Speed:**

$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N_{wheel}}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.2 \times 96.67}{60} = 1.012 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Lifting Force from Power:**

$$P_{out} = \eta \cdot P_{in} = 0.40 \times 750 = 300 \text{ W}$$

$$F = \frac{P_{out}}{v} = \frac{300}{1.012} = 296.4 \text{ N}$$

#### (b) [7 Marks] Verify whether the worm gear is **self-locking**. Discuss the safety implications for the patient lift.

**Step 1 — Weight to be Held:**

$$W = m g = 120 \times 9.81 = 1177.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Is the Hoist Capable of Lifting the Patient?**

Lifting force $F = 296.4$ N $\ll$ $W = 1177.2$ N. **The hoist cannot lift the patient as specified!** This indicates a design error — either the motor is undersized, or the gear ratio is wrong.

**Step 3 — Required Lifting Force with Safety Factor of 1.5:**

$$F_{required} = 1.5 \times 1177.2 = 1765.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Required Output Power for 1.5× safety:**

$$P_{required} = F_{required} \times v = 1765.8 \times 1.012 = 1787 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Required Input Power:**

$$P_{in,required} = \frac{P_{required}}{\eta} = \frac{1787}{0.40} = 4467 \text{ W} \approx 4.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Self-Locking Verification:**

For a worm gear, self-locking occurs when the **efficiency is less than 50%**. Here, $\eta = 0.40 < 0.50$, so the gear is **self-locking**. ✓

Equivalently, the lead angle $\gamma$ must be less than the friction angle $\phi$. The lead angle is:

$$\tan \gamma = \frac{\text{Lead}}{\pi \cdot d_{worm}}$$

A typical worm with 4 starts on a 50 mm pitch diameter has $\gamma \approx 9.5°$, well below the friction angle of cast-iron-on-bronze ($\phi \approx 10°$–$15°$), confirming self-locking.

**Step 7 — Safety Implications:**

- **Self-locking** means that even if the motor is de-energised, the worm gear **cannot back-drive** under the patient's weight — the patient will not fall.
- This is the **defining safety feature** of worm gear boxes in patient-lift applications.
- However, the design is **undersized** — the motor must be upgraded to **at least 4.5 kW** (with a corresponding gear-box thermal rating) to lift a 120 kg patient.
- An **electromechanical brake** should be added as a redundant safety measure, in case the gear box fails.
- A **manual override** with a crank should be provided for power-failure scenarios.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. Read it 30 minutes before the exam.**

### ⚙ Fundamental Principles
- [ ] **Law of Gearing** (constant velocity ratio) is the *why* behind all gear design.
- [ ] Gear ratio $i = N_1/N_2 = z_2/z_1 = D_2/D_1$ — universal master equation.
- [ ] Pitch diameter $D = m \cdot z$ (module in mm).
- [ ] Centre distance $a = m(z_1 + z_2)/2$.
- [ ] Tangential force $F_t = 2T/D$.
- [ ] Pitch-line velocity $v = \pi D N / 60$.

### ⚙ Power & Torque Accounting
- [ ] $P = 2 \pi N T / 60$ (with $N$ in RPM, $T$ in N·m, $P$ in W).
- [ ] $T_{out} = \eta \cdot i \cdot T_{in}$ for a single reducer stage.
- [ ] $i_{total} = \prod i_k$ (product, not sum, for compound trains).
- [ ] $\eta_{total} = \prod \eta_k$ for multiple stages.
- [ ] Always include $\eta$ in numerical answers — **mark loss if forgotten**.

### ⚙ Gear-Box Types & Applications
- [ ] **Spur**: simple, noisy, parallel shafts. Cheap hospital-bed adjusters.
- [ ] **Helical**: quieter, higher load. Rehabilitation treadmills.
- [ ] **Bevel**: 90° redirection. Powered stair-climbers.
- [ ] **Worm**: self-locking, single-stage high ratio. **Patient hoists** ⭐.
- [ ] **Planetary (Epicyclic)**: compact, high torque density. **Powered wheelchairs, exoskeletons** ⭐.
- [ ] **Harmonic drive**: zero backlash, sub-mm precision. **Prosthetic wrists, surgical robots** ⭐.

### ⚙ Rehabilitation-Specific Design Rules
- [ ] **Wheelchair wheels:** 2-stage planetary, ratio 20:1–50:1, coaxial through hub.
- [ ] **Prosthetic joints:** harmonic drive, ratio 80:1–160:1, backlash < 1 arc-min.
- [ ] **Exoskeleton joints:** planetary, ratio 60:1–100:1, efficiency > 90%.
- [ ] **Patient hoists:** worm gear with $\eta < 0.5$ for self-locking.
- [ ] **Robotic therapy arms:** quasi-direct-drive (QDD) for back-drivability and patient safety.

### ⚙ Common Pitfalls (KTU-Specific)
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting the efficiency $\eta$ in torque calculations.
- [ ] ❌ Confusing module (mm) with diametral pitch (in⁻¹).
- [ ] ❌ Adding ratios instead of multiplying.
- [ ] ❌ Ignoring the sign of rotation in compound trains.
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting to convert RPM to rad/s (or vice versa) when mixing with $P = T\omega$.
