# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Robot Control Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block depicts the *complete* signal flow of a rehabilitation robot controller — from the high-level task planner all the way down to motor current.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    taskA[Task Planner] --> trajB[Trajectory Generator]
    trajB --> ctrlC[High Level Controller]
    senseA[Encoders] --> fbD[Joint State Estimator]
    senseB[Force Torque Sensor] --> fbD
    senseC[EMG Electrodes] --> fbD
    senseD[IMU Sensors] --> fbD
    fbD --> ctrlC
    ctrlC --> actA[Joint Servo Driver]
    actA --> motorA[DC or BLDC Motor]
    motorA --> robotA[Robot Link]
    robotA --> patientA[Patient Limb]
    patientA -->|reaction| senseB
    robotA --> senseA
    senseC --> intentA[Intent Decoder]
    intentA --> ctrlC
    senseD --> fbD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Outer loops close *through the patient* — a hallmark of rehabilitation robotics that does not exist in industrial robots.

---

## 4.2 Three-Layer Hierarchical Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    layer1["LAYER 1: TASK PLANNING"]:::top
    layer2["LAYER 2: TRAJECTORY AND IMPEDANCE"]:::mid
    layer3["LAYER 3: SERVO AND CURRENT LOOP"]:::bot
    layer1 -->|exercise sequence| layer2
    layer2 -->|desired q d, q dot d, stiffness profile| layer3
    layer3 -->|motor current| M1((Actuator))
    M1 -->|torque| M2((Robot + Patient))
    M2 -->|sensor feedback| layer3
    M2 -->|force and EMG| layer2
    M2 -->|task completion| layer1
    classDef top fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C00000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#DEEBF7,stroke:#2E75B6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bot fill:#E2EFDA,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Sensor–Actuator Integration Architecture for an Exoskeleton

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PERCEPTION
        ENC["Joint Encoders"]
        IMU["Limb IMUs"]
        FTS["Force Torque Sensor at Cuff"]
        EMG["Surface EMG on Skin"]
    end
    subgraph DECISION
        FUSE["Sensor Fusion Estimator"]
        INT["Intent Decoder FSM"]
        CON["Adaptive Controller"]
    end
    subgraph ACTION
        SEA["Series Elastic Actuator"]
        DRV["BLDC Motor Driver"]
        SAFE["Safety Supervisor Watchdog"]
    end
    ENC --> FUSE
    IMU --> FUSE
    FTS --> CON
    EMG --> INT
    FUSE --> CON
    INT --> CON
    CON --> SAFE
    SAFE --> DRV
    DRV --> SEA
    SEA -->|joint torque| LIMB((Patient Joint))
    LIMB -->|reaction| FTS
    LIMB -->|motion| ENC
    LIMB -->|residual EMG| EMG
    LIMB -->|motion| IMU
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** The **Safety Supervisor** block runs *parallel* to the controller and can cut motor current within **1–3 ms** if joint limits are exceeded, spasm is detected, or a fail-safe button is pressed. KTU questions on "safety in rehabilitation robots" expect this redundancy to be explicitly mentioned.

---

## 4.4 State Machine of an Assist-as-Needed Controller

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STANDBY
    STANDBY --> DETECTING : patient dons device
    DETECTING --> WAITING : low EMG threshold
    WAITING --> ASSISTING : EMG exceeds intent threshold
    ASSISTING --> COASTING : patient tracks within tunnel
    COASTING --> ASSISTING : error exceeds tunnel
    ASSISTING --> SAFE_STOP : force exceeds limit
    COASTING --> SAFE_STOP : spasm detected
    SAFE_STOP --> STANDBY : therapist reset
```

> [!NOTE]
> The states `ASSISTING`, `COASTING`, `WAITING` correspond to **impedance inside the tunnel**, **zero-assistance region**, and **EMG-triggered engagement** respectively — the three pillars of AAN control.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A in KTU 2024 scheme tests **definition-level** recall and one-line reasoning. Answers must fit in **3–4 lines**.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between impedance control and admittance control in rehabilitation robotics. State one clinical scenario where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Impedance control:** measures **position error** and outputs a **force/torque** to the patient. The robot is the *mechanical admittance* source; the patient perceives a programmed spring-damper.
- **Admittance control:** measures **force** from the patient and outputs a **velocity/position** correction. The robot is the *mechanical impedance* source.
- **Clinical preference:**
    - **Impedance** is preferred for **back-drivable, low-impedance backbones** such as cable-driven or SEA-based exoskeletons (e.g., ankle robot).
    - **Admittance** is preferred for **high-gear, stiff actuators** such as the Phantom Omni haptic device used in hand therapy.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Sensor | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Proprioceptive** | Optical encoder | Joint angle feedback in Lokomat exoskeleton |
| **Proprioceptive** | IMU (accelerometer + gyro) | Tilt of trunk in balance-rehabilitation robot |
| **Proprioceptive** | Resolver | Joint angle in ReWalk exoskeleton (outdoor use) |
| **Exteroceptive** | 6-axis F/T sensor | Wrist force measurement in InMotion ARM |
| **Exteroceptive** | Surface EMG | Trigger assist in Hand of Hope for stroke |
| **Exteroceptive** | EEG (32-channel) | Motor imagery BCI for fully paralyzed patients |

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions carry **internal choice**: the paper will offer two alternatives; you attempt one. Each alternative has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Time budget ≈ 22 minutes per 14-mark question.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-layer hierarchical architecture** used in rehabilitation robot control. Describe the function of each layer and the typical time-scale at which it operates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **PD control law** for a single revolute joint of a rehabilitation robot, showing the **error dynamics** and the **Routh–Hurwitz stability conditions**. State the formula for critical damping in terms of $J_m$, $b$, and $K_p$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram:**

Refer to **§4.2** of these notes (the three-layer Mermaid diagram).

**Explanation of each layer:**

1. **Layer 1 — Task / Decision Layer:**
    - Inputs: patient's therapy protocol, ROM targets, repetition count.
    - Outputs: high-level commands such as "perform 10 reach-to-grasp cycles."
    - Time-scale: seconds to minutes.
    - Examples: rule-based AI, finite-state machines, therapist tablet interface.

2. **Layer 2 — Trajectory & Impedance Layer:**
    - Inputs: desired task, current patient state.
    - Outputs: desired joint trajectory $q_d(t)$, desired stiffness $K_d(t)$, damping $B_d(t)$, equilibrium pose.
    - Time-scale: 10–100 ms (real-time).
    - Examples: minimum-jerk trajectory generator, impedance profile interpolator.

3. **Layer 3 — Servo / Joint Layer:**
    - Inputs: desired $q_d$, $\dot{q}_d$ and measured $q$, $\dot{q}$.
    - Outputs: motor current / voltage commands.
    - Time-scale: 1–10 ms (kHz range).
    - Examples: PID, computed-torque, sliding-mode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marker expects:** the **time-scale hierarchy** in your answer — write it as a 3-row table to gain full 7 marks.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Plant Model:**

$$
J_m \ddot{\theta} + b \dot{\theta} = \tau_m - \tau_{dist}
$$

where $\tau_{dist}$ lumps gravity + patient interaction; assumed measured by F/T sensor and cancelled via feed-forward.

**Step 2 — Define Error:**

$$
e = \theta_d - \theta
$$

**Step 3 — PD Control Law:**

$$
\tau_m = K_p e - K_d \dot{\theta}
$$

**Step 4 — Closed-Loop Error Dynamics:**

$$
J_m \ddot{e} + (b + K_d)\dot{e} + K_p e = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Characteristic Equation:**

$$
J_m s^2 + (b + K_d)s + K_p = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Routh–Hurwitz Conditions:**

$$
J_m > 0, \quad b + K_d > 0, \quad K_p > 0
$$

Hence stability requires $K_p > 0$ and $K_d > -b$. Physically, choose $K_d \ge 0$.

**Step 7 — Critical Damping Formula:**

$$
K_d^{crit} = 2\sqrt{J_m K_p} - b
$$

**Step 8 — Natural Frequency:**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_p}{J_m}}
$$

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **impedance control** in rehabilitation robots. Derive the desired impedance relation and show how a programmer can vary the "feel" of the robot from soft (free-floating) to rigid (splint-like) by tuning a single parameter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Assist-as-Needed (AAN) control** with a state-machine diagram. Explain the role of the **tunnel width** in state-space and the clinical rationale for limiting assistance to encourage patient effort. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Concept of Impedance Control:**
Impedance control (Hogan, 1985) makes the robot's end-effector behave as a programmable **mass–spring–damper** to the patient. The desired relation between the patient's contact force $F_{ext}$ and the deviation from a virtual trajectory $(x - x_d)$ is:

$$
M_d(\ddot{x} - \ddot{x}_d) + B_d(\dot{x} - \dot{x}_d) + K_d(x - x_d) = -F_{ext}
$$

(The sign convention is chosen so that a positive force from the patient pushes the robot away from the desired path.)

**Step 2 — Solving for Acceleration (control objective):**

$$
\ddot{x} = \ddot{x}_d + M_d^{-1}\bigl[-F_{ext} - B_d(\dot{x} - \dot{x}_d) - K_d(x - x_d)\bigr]
$$

**Step 3 — Programming the "Feel":**

| $K_d$ value | Robot behavior | Clinical feel |
|---|---|---|
| $K_d = 0$ | No spring — patient free to deviate | Like therapist's loose hands (gravity-compensated) |
| $K_d$ small | Compliant, gentle guidance | Soft push toward target |
| $K_d$ large | Stiff, position-dominated | Like a rigid splint |
| $K_d \to \infty$ | Pure position control | Full trajectory enforcement |

**Step 4 — Jacobian Transform to Joint Space:**

$$
\tau = M(q)J^{-1}(\ddot{x}_{ref} - \dot{J}\dot{q}) + C\dot{q} + G + J^T F_{ext}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this answers "single parameter":** the **stiffness $K_d$** is *the* parameter a therapist adjusts on a tablet to change the robot's character without altering the underlying control code.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**AAN Control Philosophy:**
In traditional position control, the patient becomes a *passenger*. The literature shows this leads to **learned non-use** and slower recovery. AAN control provides torque **only when the patient needs it**, with the patient doing as much work as possible.

**State Machine** — refer to **§4.4** of these notes (5-state Mermaid diagram: STANDBY → DETECTING → WAITING → ASSISTING ↔ COASTING → SAFE_STOP).

**The "Tunnel" in State-Space:**

- Define a *tunnel* of width $w$ around the desired trajectory $(q_d, \dot{q}_d)$.
- If the patient's state $(q, \dot{q})$ **lies inside the tunnel** at time $t$, the controller applies **zero** assistive torque.
- If the state **exits the tunnel**, the controller applies torque proportional to the *deviation*, pulling the state back into the tunnel.

Mathematically, the assistance torque is:

$$
\tau_{AAN}(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{if } \vert q - q_d \vert \le w \\ -K_p(q - q_d) - K_d(\dot{q} - \dot{q}_d), & \text{if } \vert q - q_d \vert > w \end{cases}
$$

**Clinical Rationale:**

1. **Promotes neuroplasticity** — active movement drives cortical reorganization.
2. **Prevents slacking** — the patient cannot rely on the robot.
3. **Adapts to recovery** — early sessions: wide tunnel, much help; late sessions: narrow tunnel, little help.
4. **Motivational** — patient sees real progress attributable to *their* effort.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **not** write "impedance control = PID." They are *not* the same. PID acts on a single error signal; impedance control explicitly shapes the dynamic relationship between force and motion.
> - Do **not** confuse *admittance* and *impedance.* Use the mnemonic: **"I measure motion, give Force" → Impedance** (I-F); **"A measure force, give motion" → Admittance** (A-M).
> - In derivation questions, write the **error-dynamics equation** explicitly, or you will lose 2 marks.
> - For 14-mark questions, **always include a diagram.** A 7-mark sub-part without a diagram is capped at 5 marks.
> - State the **Routh–Hurwitz conditions** and the **critical-damping formula** by name; KTU 2024 examiners explicitly award 1 mark for naming the criterion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Robot Control**

- [ ] Robot control in rehabilitation = closed-loop regulation of *position, force, and impedance* to interact with a *human*.
- [ ] Three control layers: **Task → Trajectory/Impedance → Servo**, with time-scales **s, 10–100 ms, 1–10 ms**.
- [ ] **Position control** = patient passive; **Force control** = robot compliant; **Impedance control** = unified free/constrained motion; **Admittance** = inverse of impedance for stiff backbones.
- [ ] **PD law** $\tau = K_p e - K_d \dot\theta$ → closed-loop error dynamics $J_m \ddot e + (b+K_d)\dot e + K_p e = 0$.
- [ ] Stability: **$K_p > 0$, $K_d > -b$**; critical damping $K_d^{crit} = 2\sqrt{J_m K_p} - b$.
- [ ] Impedance law: $M_d \ddot{\tilde x} + B_d \dot{\tilde x} + K_d \tilde x = F_{ext}$ where $\tilde x = x - x_d$.
- [ ] Tuning $K_d$ alone transitions the robot from "soft hand" ($K_d = 0$) to "rigid splint" ($K_d \to \infty$).
- [ ] **Forward kinematics** $T_{ee} = \prod A_i$; **differential** $\dot x = J \dot q$; **static force** $\tau = J^T F$.
- [ ] **AAN control** = "tunnel" in state-space; assist only when error exceeds tunnel width; promotes neuroplasticity.
- [ ] Key sensors: **encoders (proprioceptive), F/T sensors + EMG + EEG (exteroceptive).**
- [ ] Key actuators: **BLDC + Harmonic Drive** (industrial-like), **SEA** (compliant), **Pneumatic Muscle** (soft orthoses).
- [ ] **Safety supervisor** runs in parallel to controller; cuts current in 1–3 ms on limit violation.
- [ ] Sampling-rate rule: $f_s \ge 10 f_{bw}$ to satisfy Nyquist with safety margin.
- [ ] When in doubt on KTU answer: **draw the block diagram, write the error-dynamics equation, name Routh–Hurwitz, finish with a clinical example.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robot Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Robot Sensors

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Sensors (KTU Definition):** A *robot sensor* is a transducer or measurement device that converts a physical, chemical, or biological quantity from the robot's internal state or external environment into an electrical signal that can be processed, interpreted, and used as feedback for intelligent control, decision-making, and task execution in a rehabilitation robot.

In the context of **Rehabilitation Engineering**, robot sensors are the *gateway between the patient, the assistive/therapeutic robot, and the supervising clinician*. They allow rehabilitation robots (e.g., exoskeletons, prosthetic limbs, gait trainers, upper-limb therapy devices, and assistive manipulators) to:

1. **Sense the patient** — joint angles, muscle activity (EMG), neural intent (EEG/ECoG), forces exerted, and physiological vitals.
2. **Sense the robot** — actuator position, torque, motor current, end-effector pose, and battery state.
3. **Sense the environment** — obstacles, terrain, object properties, ambient conditions.

Mathematically, a sensor is modelled as a mapping function:

$$
f: \mathbb{R}^{n}_{\text{physical}} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}^{m}_{\text{electrical}}
$$

where the input is a physical measurand and the output is a measurable electrical quantity (voltage, current, frequency, or digital code).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Human Senses vs Robot Sensors:**
> Just as humans use their **eyes** (vision), **ears** (hearing), **skin** (touch), **muscles** (proprioception), and **vestibular system** (balance) to interact with the world, a rehabilitation robot uses **electronic sensors** to "feel" its environment and the patient. Without sensors, a robot is essentially **blind and deaf** — it cannot detect that a stroke survivor's elbow is bending too far, that a prosthetic hand is slipping an object, or that an exoskeleton is approaching a wall. Sensors are the *nervous system* of every rehabilitation robot.

Think of an EMG-controlled prosthetic hand: when an amputee *thinks* about closing the hand, residual muscles generate tiny electrical signals (microvolts). The **EMG sensor** "hears" these signals, converts them to a control command, and tells the robotic fingers to close — much like a microphone captures your voice and a speaker plays it back louder.

## 1.3 Why Robot Sensors are Critical in Rehabilitation Engineering

- **Patient Safety** — prevent injury during robotic therapy.
- **Closed-Loop Control** — sensors provide feedback for PID, impedance, and adaptive control.
- **Intent Recognition** — detect user's motion intention (BCI, EMG, gaze).
- **Outcome Quantification** — measure therapy progress (range of motion, smoothness, strength).
- **Compliance with ISO 13482 / IEC 80601** — medical robot safety standards require redundant sensing.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics for Sensors (High-Yield Definitions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Sensitivity (S):** Ratio of output change to input change; units: V/unit of measurand.
> - **Accuracy:** Closeness of measured value to true value; expressed as ±% of full scale (FS).
> - **Precision / Repeatability:** Ability to produce the same output for repeated identical inputs.
> - **Resolution:** Smallest detectable change in the input.
> - **Linearity:** Degree to which output is a straight line versus input (ideal: $R^2 = 1$).
> - **Bandwidth (Hz):** Frequency range over which the sensor responds faithfully.
> - **Dynamic Range (dB):** $20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}}\right)$.
> - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Ratio of signal power to noise power, in dB.
> - **Drift:** Slow change of output with time or temperature.
> - **Hysteresis:** Difference in output for the same input approached from opposite directions.

## 1.5 High-Level Classification of Robot Sensors

Robot sensors are broadly classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Categories |
|------|-----------|
| **Source of signal** | Proprioceptive (internal) vs Exteroceptive (external) |
| **Energy conversion** | Active (require excitation) vs Passive (self-generating) |
| **Output type** | Analog vs Digital |
| **Contact** | Contact vs Non-contact |

> **Proprioceptive sensors** measure *internal* robot variables (joint angle, motor speed, torque).
> **Exteroceptive sensors** measure *external* variables (force from patient, distance to object, light, sound).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Categories of Robot Sensors in Rehabilitation

### 2.1.1 Position & Displacement Sensors

These measure joint angles, linear position, and orientation of the robot or the patient.

**A. Optical Encoders (Incremental & Absolute)**
- **Incremental encoder:** Produces two quadrature square waves (A, B) with phase difference of 90°. Resolution is given by the number of lines per revolution ($N$).
- **Absolute encoder:** Provides a unique digital code (e.g., Gray code) for every shaft position.

**B. Potentiometers**
- A resistive element with a sliding wiper. Output voltage is a linear (or logarithmic) function of wiper position.
- Output: $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{x}{x_{max}}$ (linear taper).

**C. Resolvers & Synchros**
- Electromagnetic devices with rotor (excited by AC) and two stator windings (sine and cosine).
- Output voltages: $V_s = V_0 \sin(\omega t)\sin(\theta)$, $V_c = V_0 \sin(\omega t)\cos(\theta)$.
- Angle recovered by: $\theta = \arctan(V_s / V_c)$.

**D. Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)**
- Transformer with one primary and two secondaries; core displacement changes mutual coupling.
- Differential output is linear with displacement over a specified range.

**E. Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**
- Combines **accelerometer**, **gyroscope**, and **magnetometer** to estimate orientation.
- Outputs: roll ($\phi$), pitch ($\theta$), yaw ($\psi$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Encoders Dominate Rehabilitation Robots:** They are digital, non-contact, have long life (>10⁸ revolutions), high resolution, and are immune to electrical noise — all critical for exoskeletons and prosthetics that undergo millions of cycles.

### 2.1.2 Velocity Sensors

- **Tachogenerator:** DC generator whose output voltage is proportional to rotational speed: $V = K \cdot \omega$.
- **Differentiating encoder signal:** $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$.
- **Doppler radar / optical:** Used in non-contact gait analysis.

### 2.1.3 Force / Torque / Pressure Sensors

**A. Strain-Gauge Based Force/Torque Sensor**
- Wheatstone bridge with four active strain gauges; output is proportional to applied force.
- Located typically at the robot's wrist or end-effector (e.g., ATI Mini40, JR3).

**B. Load Cells**
- Single or multi-axis force measurement using strain gauges.
- Force equation: $F = \dfrac{V_{out}}{G \cdot V_{ex}}$ where $G$ is gauge factor and $V_{ex}$ is excitation.

**C. Tactile / Pressure Sensor Arrays**
- Sensor skins with distributed sensing elements (capacitive, piezoresistive, optical).
- Used on prosthetic fingertips and assistive grippers.

**D. Series Elastic Actuator (SEA) Force Sensing**
- Spring deflection in series with motor gives force: $F = k \cdot \Delta x$.
- Used widely in rehabilitation exoskeletons (e.g., LOPES, BLEEX).

### 2.1.4 Bioelectric Sensors (Patient-Intent Sensing)

**A. Electromyography (EMG) Sensors**
- Surface EMG (sEMG) or intramuscular EMG (iEMG) measures electrical activity of muscles.
- Amplitude: 50 µV – 5 mV; Frequency content: 20 – 500 Hz.
- Used to control prosthetic limbs and exoskeletons by *decoding user intent*.

**B. Electroencephalography (EEG) Sensors**
- Brain–Computer Interface (BCI) electrodes measure cortical potentials.
- Used in assistive robots for severely paralyzed patients (e.g., ALS, locked-in syndrome).

**C. Electrooculography (EOG)**
- Measures eye-movement potentials; used in gaze-controlled wheelchairs.

### 2.1.5 Proximity, Range, and Vision Sensors

- **Ultrasonic / Sonar sensors:** Time-of-flight (ToF) measurement of distance.
- **Infrared / LiDAR:** Time-of-flight or triangulation; for obstacle avoidance.
- **RGB / Depth Cameras (Intel RealSense, Microsoft Kinect):** Used for markerless motion tracking of patient limbs.
- **Tactile proximity:** Capacitive sensors detect nearby objects without contact.

### 2.1.6 Environmental & Physiological Sensors

- **Temperature sensors (thermistors, RTDs)** — patient skin/limb temperature.
- **Heart rate / SpO₂ sensors** — pulse oximeters for therapy safety monitoring.
- **Sweat / GSR sensors** — detect emotional/physiological state during therapy.

## 2.2 Mathematical Model of a Sensor

A sensor can be represented as a **linear, time-invariant system** with the following ideal transfer function:

$$
y(t) = S \cdot x(t) + b
$$

where $y(t)$ is the output, $S$ is the sensitivity (gain), $x(t)$ is the measurand, and $b$ is the bias (offset).

In practice, real sensors exhibit **non-idealities** captured by:

$$
y(t) = S \cdot x(t) \cdot \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right] + b_0 + n(t) + h\bigl(x(t)\bigr)
$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $\alpha$ | Temperature coefficient of sensitivity (TCS) |
| $T$ | Operating temperature |
| $T_0$ | Reference temperature (usually 25 °C) |
| $b_0$ | Initial bias |
| $n(t)$ | Additive noise (thermal, flicker, shot) |
| $h(\cdot)$ | Non-linear distortion / hysteresis function |

## 2.3 Signal Conditioning Block

The raw sensor signal is almost always weak and noisy. A standard signal conditioning chain is:

```
Sensor  →  Amplifier  →  Filter  →  ADC  →  Microcontroller / DSP
```

- **Amplifier:** Instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620, INA128) for high CMRR > 100 dB.
- **Filter:** Anti-aliasing low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, plus a 50/60 Hz notch.
- **ADC:** Resolution $N$ bits → smallest detectable voltage $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TABLE 2.A — Essential Formulas for Robot Sensors**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Linear sensor output | $y = Sx + b$ | S in V/unit |
| 2 | Sensitivity (incremental) | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | V/unit |
| 3 | Sensitivity with temperature drift | $S_T = S_0 [1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$ | $S_0$ at $T_0$ |
| 4 | Gauge factor (strain gauge) | $G = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | Dimensionless; typical ≈ 2 |
| 5 | Resistance of strained gauge | $R = R_0 (1 + G\varepsilon)$ | $\varepsilon$ = strain |
| 6 | Strain-gauge bridge output | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon}{4}$ (full bridge) | Single active gauge: $/4$ |
| 7 | Wheatstone bridge balance | $R_1 / R_2 = R_3 / R_4$ | At zero strain |
| 8 | LVDT output (linear range) | $V_{diff} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$ | K in V/mm |
| 9 | Resolver angle | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_c}\right)$ | Quadrant logic needed |
| 10 | Encoder resolution (per revolution) | $\text{Res} = 4N$ (with ×4 quadrature decoding) | N = lines on disc |
| 11 | Tachogenerator output | $V = K_t \cdot \omega$ | $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 12 | SEA force from spring | $F = k \cdot \Delta x$ | $k$ = spring stiffness |
| 13 | EMG RMS amplitude | $A_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$ | Indicative of muscle activation |
| 14 | EMG-to-force (Hof's law) | $F \propto \int \vert EMG \vert^2 \, dt$ | Approximate relationship |
| 15 | Dynamic range | $DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{max}}{V_{min}}\right)$ | dB |
| 16 | SNR | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB |
| 17 | ADC resolution | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$ | Volts per LSB |
| 18 | Anti-aliasing filter cutoff | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz (Nyquist: $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$) |
| 19 | Accelerometer output | $V_{out} = V_0 + S_a \cdot a$ | $V_0$ = 0-g offset |
| 20 | Gyroscope output | $\omega = \dfrac{V_{out} - V_0}{S_g}$ | rad/s |
| 21 | LiDAR/Ultrasonic ToF distance | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | c = speed of wave |
| 22 | Skin-strain on prosthetic socket | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Must stay below skin-injury threshold |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a **rehabilitation exoskeleton** like BLEEX or ReWalk, the *primary* sensors are: (i) **encoders** at every joint for position, (ii) **load cells** at the foot for ground reaction force, and (iii) **EMG electrodes** on the patient's quadriceps for intent detection. The fusion of these three sensor streams (sensor fusion) gives the controller the information needed to walk the patient.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of the most important formulas a KTU 2024 student must master for the Robot Sensors topic. Every algebraic transition is explicitly written.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Incremental Optical Encoder Resolution with Quadrature Decoding

> **Problem:** An incremental optical encoder has $N$ slots (lines) on its disc. Determine the effective counts per revolution, the smallest angular displacement it can resolve, and the digital position after $k$ pulses.

### Step 1 — Define the disc geometry

A disc with $N$ equally spaced slots and $N$ transparent windows gives $N$ pulses per revolution on a single channel. A second channel, offset by one quarter-pitch (90° electrical), is provided for direction discrimination.

### Step 2 — Pulses per revolution (single channel)

$$
\text{PPR}_{\text{channel}} = N
$$

### Step 3 — Quadrature decoding (×4 multiplier)

By counting **rising and falling edges of both channels A and B**, the effective resolution becomes:

$$
\text{PPR}_{\text{effective}} = 4N
$$

### Step 4 — Angular resolution (radians per count)

One revolution is $2\pi$ radians, divided into $4N$ counts:

$$
\delta\theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{4N} = \dfrac{\pi}{2N} \text{ [rad/count]}
$$

### Step 5 — Position after $k$ counts

$$
\theta(k) = k \cdot \delta\theta = \dfrac{k\pi}{2N}
$$

### Step 6 — Worked example (KTU style)

For $N = 500$ lines, $k = 1200$ counts:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{1200 \cdot \pi}{2 \cdot 500} = \dfrac{1200 \pi}{1000} = 1.2\pi \text{ rad} = 216^{\circ}
$$

> **Cross-check:** $4N = 2000$ counts/rev; $1200/2000 = 0.6$ rev $= 216°$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Strain-Gauge Bridge Output Voltage

> **Problem:** Derive the output voltage of a **quarter, half, and full Wheatstone bridge** under applied strain.

### Step 1 — General Wheatstone bridge

The bridge has four arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ with excitation $V_{ex}$ across opposite nodes (A, C) and output $V_{out}$ across the other pair (B, D).

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)
$$

### Step 2 — Balanced condition (no strain)

When $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$, $V_{out} = 0$.

### Step 3 — One gauge changes due to strain

Let $R_1 \to R_1 + \Delta R$, with $\Delta R = G \varepsilon R_1$ where $G$ is gauge factor and $\varepsilon$ is strain.

For a **quarter bridge** (one active gauge, three fixed):

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{R_1 + \Delta R}{2R_1 + \Delta R} - \dfrac{1}{2} \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Linearization (small $\varepsilon$)

For $G\varepsilon \ll 1$, denominator $\approx 2R_1$, and using $\Delta R \ll R_1$:

$$
V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{4 R_1} = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon}{4}
$$

### Step 5 — Half bridge (two active gauges, opposite arms)

If $R_1$ increases by $\Delta R$ and $R_2$ decreases by $\Delta R$ (poisson configuration):

$$
V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon}{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Full bridge (four active gauges, two in tension, two in compression)

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon
$$

### Step 7 — Generalized formula

$$
V_{out} = n \cdot \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon}{4}, \quad n \in \{1, 2, 4\}
$$

for quarter, half, and full bridge respectively.

### Step 8 — Sensitivity comparison

The full bridge is **4× more sensitive** than the quarter bridge and also **automatically temperature compensated** (when configured with matched gauges on adjacent arms).

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Series Elastic Actuator (SEA) Force Sensing

> **Problem:** A rehabilitation exoskeleton joint uses a SEA with spring stiffness $k$ N/m. The motor-side encoder reads $\theta_m$ and the joint-side encoder reads $\theta_j$. Derive the joint torque.

### Step 1 — Spring deflection

$$
\Delta\theta = \theta_m - \theta_j
$$

### Step 2 — Torque from spring

$$
\tau = k \cdot \Delta\theta
$$

### Step 3 — Accounting for gear ratio

If the motor is geared by $n$ relative to the spring output:

$$
\tau = n^2 \cdot k \cdot (\theta_m - \theta_j) = n^2 \cdot k \cdot \Delta\theta
$$

### Step 4 — Resolution of torque measurement

If encoder resolutions are $\delta\theta_m$ and $\delta\theta_j$:

$$
\delta\tau = n^2 k \sqrt{\delta\theta_m^2 + \delta\theta_j^2}
$$

### Step 5 — Worked example

Given $n = 100$, $k = 1000$ N·m/rad, $\delta\theta_m = \delta\theta_j = 1.745 \times 10^{-5}$ rad (0.001°):

$$
\delta\tau = 100^2 \cdot 1000 \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot (1.745 \times 10^{-5})^2}
$$

$$
\delta\tau = 10^7 \cdot 1.745 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 174.5 \cdot 1.4142 \approx 246.8 \text{ N·m / LSB} \cdot 10^{-3}
$$

Re-evaluating with proper units:

$$
\delta\tau = 10^7 \times 1.745\times10^{-5} \times 1.4142 \approx 246.8 \text{ N·m}
$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** SEA enables **transparent, force-controlled interaction** with the patient — critical for safe robotic therapy.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — EMG Signal Acquisition & RMS Envelope

> **Problem:** The raw EMG signal $v(t)$ is band-pass filtered (20–500 Hz) and full-wave rectified. Derive the RMS envelope used as a control input for a prosthetic limb.

### Step 1 — Band-pass filter

The raw sEMG is first filtered to remove DC offset, motion artefact, and high-frequency noise:

$$
v_{bp}(t) = h_{bp}(t) * v(t), \quad H_{bp}(f) = 1 \text{ for } 20 \le f \le 500 \text{ Hz}
$$

### Step 2 — Full-wave rectification

$$
v_{rect}(t) = \vert v_{bp}(t) \vert
$$

### Step 3 — RMS envelope (sliding window of length $T$)

$$
v_{RMS}(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{t-T}^{t} v_{rect}^2(\tau)\, d\tau}
$$

### Step 4 — Approximation as control input

Many controllers use a simpler **Moving Average (MA)**:

$$
\bar{v}(t) = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{t-T}^{t} \vert v_{bp}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau
$$

### Step 5 — Decision rule for prosthetic grasp

$$
\text{Grasp state} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Open}, & v_{RMS}(t) < V_{th,low} \\
\text{Hold}, & V_{th,low} \le v_{RMS}(t) < V_{th,high} \\
\text{Close}, & v_{RMS}(t) \ge V_{th,high}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 6 — Relationship to force (Hof's law)

Hof (1981) showed that muscle force is approximately proportional to the **integrated squared** EMG:

$$
F(t) \approx a \int_0^t v_{RMS}^2(\tau)\, d\tau + b
$$

where $a, b$ are subject-specific calibration constants obtained during a **Maximum Voluntary Contraction (MVC)** test.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection for Common Rehabilitation Applications

| Application | Required Sensing | Sensor Choice | Justification |
|-------------|------------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Stroke upper-limb therapy robot (e.g., InMotion ARM)** | End-effector position, patient force | Optical encoders + 6-axis F/T sensor | Sub-mm accuracy, sub-N force resolution |
| **Lower-limb exoskeleton (ReWalk, Ekso)** | Joint angle, ground reaction force, posture | Encoders + foot pressure insoles + IMU | High reliability, real-time balance feedback |
| **Prosthetic hand (e.g., DEKA LUKE arm)** | Intent, grip force, slip | sEMG + tactile sensors + force sensors | Multi-modal intent decoding |
| **Gait trainer (Lokomat)** | Knee/hip angle, body weight support | Encoders + load cell | Therapy quantification |
| **Wheelchair (BCI-controlled)** | User intent | EEG (dry electrodes) + EOG | Severe paralysis, no EMG available |
| **Smart wheelchair with obstacle avoidance** | Distance to obstacles, line following | Ultrasonic + IR + IR line sensor | Low cost, reliable |
| **FES (Functional Electrical Stimulation)** integration | Muscle activity feedback | EMG + joint angle | Closed-loop FES control |
| **Soft rehabilitation glove** | Finger flexion, applied pressure | Flex sensors + piezoresistive fabric | Comfortable, conformal |

---

## 3.6 Sensor Fusion — Complementary Filter (IMU Orientation)

> **Problem:** Combine gyroscope and accelerometer data to estimate the pitch angle $\theta$ of a patient's trunk during walking.

### Step 1 — Gyroscope integration

$$
\theta_{gyro}(t) = \int_0^t \omega(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

Problem: integration drift.

### Step 2 — Accelerometer tilt

$$
\theta_{acc} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{a_y}{a_z}\right)
$$

Problem: noisy due to vibrations.

### Step 3 — Complementary filter (1st order)

$$
\theta(t) = \alpha \cdot \left[\theta(t-1) + \omega(t) \cdot \Delta t\right] + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \theta_{acc}(t)
$$

where $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ (typical: 0.95–0.98).

### Step 4 — Interpretation

- High $\alpha$ → trust gyroscope more (short term, no drift of high-pass part).
- Low $\alpha$ → trust accelerometer more (long term, low-pass part).
- Together: stable, drift-free, low-noise orientation estimate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> All diagrams use the **Mermaid flowchart** syntax and follow the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 *Node Identifier Alpha Rule* and *Label Formatting Restriction* (no markdown, no HTML, no reserved keywords).

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Robot Sensors in Rehabilitation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ROBOT SENSORS REHABILITATION] --> B[PROPRIOCEPTIVE INTERNAL]
    A --> C[EXTEROCEPTIVE EXTERNAL]
    A --> D[BIOELECTRIC PATIENT INTENT]

    B --> B1[Position Encoders]
    B --> B2[Potentiometers]
    B --> B3[Resolvers and LVDT]
    B --> B4[IMU Accel Gyro Mag]
    B --> B5[Motor Current Torque]
    B --> B6[Tachogenerators]

    C --> C1[Force Torque Sensors]
    C --> C2[Tactile Pressure Arrays]
    C --> C3[Ultrasonic Sonar]
    C --> C4[IR and LiDAR]
    C --> C5[RGB and Depth Camera]
    C --> C6[Load Cells]

    D --> D1[Surface EMG sEMG]
    D --> D2[Intramuscular EMG iEMG]
    D --> D3[EEG Brain Computer Interface]
    D --> D4[EOG Eye Tracking]
    D --> D5[ECG and Heart Rate]
    D --> D6[Skin Temperature and GSR]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sensor Signal Chain

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Physical Measurand] --> S[Sensor Element]
    S --> SC[Signal Conditioning]
    SC --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP --> FIL[Anti Aliasing Filter]
    FIL --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> DSP[Microcontroller and DSP]
    DSP --> FU[Sensor Fusion Algorithm]
    FU --> CT[Robot Controller and Actuator]
    CT --> ACT[Actuator Output to Patient]

    DSP --> FB[Feedback to Clinician HMI]
    DSP --> LOG[Data Logger Therapy Metrics]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Series Elastic Actuator (SEA) with Dual Encoders

```mermaid
graph TD
    M[Motor] --> G[Gearbox Ratio n]
    G --> SP[Spring Element Stiffness k]
    SP --> LK[Linkage to Patient Joint]
    LK --> PT[Patient Limb]

    M --> ENC1[Motor Encoder theta m]
    LK --> ENC2[Joint Encoder theta j]
    ENC1 --> DIFF[Compute Delta Theta]
    ENC2 --> DIFF
    DIFF --> TOR[Compute Torque tau equals n squared k times delta theta]
    TOR --> CTL[Force Controller]
    CTL --> M
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — EMG-Driven Prosthetic Hand Control

```mermaid
graph TD
    MU[Residual Muscle EMG Source] --> EL[Surface Electrodes Differential Pair]
    EL --> IA[Instrumentation Amplifier CMRR 100 dB]
    IA --> BPF[Band Pass 20 to 500 Hz]
    BPF --> REC[Full Wave Rectifier]
    REC --> EN[RMS Envelope Sliding Window]
    EN --> THR[Threshold Decision Logic]
    THR --> ST[State Open Hold Close]
    ST --> DRV[Motor Driver]
    DRV --> FH[Prosthetic Hand Fingers]
    FH --> FB[Tactile and Force Feedback]
    FB --> MU
```

## 4.5 Sensor Fusion — Complementary Filter for IMU Orientation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Accelerometer ax ay az] --> AT[Compute Tilt arctan ay over az]
    G[Gyroscope omega x y z] --> IN[Integrate to get Angle]
    AT --> MUX[Weighted Sum alpha times Gyro plus 1 minus alpha times Accel]
    IN --> MUX
    MUX --> OUT[Estimated Pitch Roll Yaw]
    OUT --> AP[Apply to Exoskeleton or Prosthetic]
```

## 4.6 Closed-Loop Rehabilitation Robot Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Patient] --> E[Environment and Task]
    P --> I[Intent EMG EEG EOG]
    I --> S1[Bioelectric Sensor]
    E --> S2[Exteroceptive Sensor Vision Force Proximity]
    S1 --> SF[Sensor Fusion and Intent Decoder]
    S2 --> SF
    SF --> CTL[Adaptive Impedance Controller]
    CTL --> ACT[Actuator with Proprioceptive Feedback Encoders Current]
    ACT --> P
    CTL --> HMI[Therapist Dashboard and Safety Monitor]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions are tagged with **simulated KTU past year style**, **marks**, and **Bloom's level**. Each Part B question is provided in **TRUE KTU ESE internal-choice format** (Q7A or Q7B).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` — **3 Marks** — *Bloom Level: Remember/Understand*

**Define a robot sensor. Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors with two rehabilitation examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **robot sensor** is a device that converts a physical, chemical, or biological quantity into an electrical signal which can be processed by the robot controller.
>
> | Property | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Source** | Internal state of robot/patient joint | External environment |
> | **Typical use** | Position, velocity, motor current | Force, vision, proximity |
> | **Rehab example 1** | Optical encoder on knee joint of exoskeleton | Force/torque sensor at hand of therapy robot |
> | **Rehab example 2** | IMU on patient's trunk for posture | RGB-D camera tracking reaching motion |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` — **3 Marks** — *Bloom Level: Understand*

**List the key performance specifications of a force sensor used in a rehabilitation robot. State one standard sensor for end-effector force measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

> Key specifications:
> 1. **Range** (N) — max measurable force.
> 2. **Resolution** (N) — smallest detectable change.
> 3. **Accuracy** (% FS) — deviation from true value.
> 4. **Bandwidth** (Hz) — frequency response.
> 5. **Cross-axis sensitivity** (%) — sensitivity to orthogonal axes.
> 6. **Non-linearity & hysteresis** (% FS).
> 7. **Temperature drift** (% FS/°C).
>
> **Standard sensor:** ATI Mini40 / JR3 6-axis F/T sensor (commonly used in MIT-Manus, InMotion ARM).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice*

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either Q7A or Q7B**.

---

### **Q7A. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]`** — **14 Marks** — *Bloom Level: Apply / Analyze*

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **optical incremental encoder** for joint position sensing in a rehabilitation exoskeleton. Derive the resolution for an encoder with $N$ lines per revolution, including ×4 quadrature decoding.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A **series elastic actuator (SEA)** is used in a knee exoskeleton. The motor encoder reads $\theta_m$ and joint encoder reads $\theta_j$. Spring stiffness is $k = 2000$ N·m/rad and gear ratio $n = 80$. If the motor rotates by $0.05$ rad more than the joint, calculate the **joint torque** transmitted to the patient. If encoder resolution is $0.001^\circ$ per count, calculate the **torque resolution** in N·m.

#### Model Solution — Q7A (a)

A **rotary optical incremental encoder** consists of:
- An **LED** (light source),
- A **code disc** with $N$ transparent and opaque radial lines,
- A **photodiode array** (typically two photodetectors offset by 90° electrical — channels A and B),
- Signal conditioning electronics.

**Working principle:** As the disc rotates with the motor shaft, light from the LED is alternately transmitted and blocked, producing two **quadrature square waves** A and B. A third index pulse Z gives one pulse per revolution (homing).

**Quadrature decoding (×4):** Every **edge** (rising and falling) of both A and B is counted, multiplying resolution by 4.

$$
\text{PPR}_{eff} = 4N
$$

**Angular resolution:**

$$
\delta\theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{4N} = \dfrac{\pi}{2N} \text{ rad/count}
$$

**Direction discrimination:** If B leads A → CW rotation; if A leads B → CCW. (For this, mark 1 separately for direction logic.)

**Rehabilitation application:** Used at every actuated joint (hip, knee, ankle) of an exoskeleton like ReWalk, providing real-time position feedback with sub-degree accuracy.

#### Model Solution — Q7A (b)

**Given:** $k = 2000$ N·m/rad, $n = 80$, $\Delta\theta = \theta_m - \theta_j = 0.05$ rad, $\delta\theta_{enc} = 0.001^\circ = 1.7453 \times 10^{-5}$ rad.

**Step 1 — Torque transmitted:**

$$
\tau = n^2 \cdot k \cdot \Delta\theta
$$

$$
\tau = (80)^2 \times 2000 \times 0.05
$$

$$
\tau = 6400 \times 2000 \times 0.05
$$

$$
\tau = 12{,}800{,}000 \times 0.05 = 640{,}000 \text{ N·m} ?? \text{ (recheck)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Magnitude Check:** A typical knee joint torque is ≤ 100 N·m for an adult. **640,000 N·m is physically impossible** — this would mean the motor-gearbox is over-rated. Students often forget that the **gear ratio $n$ reduces the spring deflection by a factor of $n$** at the joint (not amplifies it). The correct equation is $\tau = k \cdot \Delta\theta / n$ when the spring is on the joint side, OR the spring deflection $\Delta\theta$ in the formula must be **spring-side deflection, not motor-side**.

**Re-derivation of correct equation:**

If motor rotates by $\theta_m$ and joint by $\theta_j$, and the spring sits between them, then the *spring* deflection (in spring coordinates, after gearbox) is:

$$
\Delta\theta_{spring} = \dfrac{\theta_m}{n} - \theta_j
$$

So,

$$
\tau = k \cdot \left(\dfrac{\theta_m}{n} - \theta_j\right)
$$

Plugging values:

$$
\tau = 2000 \times \left(\dfrac{0.05}{80} - 0\right) = 2000 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} = 1.25 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Torque resolution:**

For independent encoders:

$$
\delta\tau = k \cdot \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\delta\theta_{enc}}{n}\right)^2 + \delta\theta_{enc}^2}
$$

$$
\delta\tau = 2000 \times \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{1.7453 \times 10^{-5}}{80}\right)^2 + (1.7453 \times 10^{-5})^2}
$$

$$
\delta\tau = 2000 \times \sqrt{(2.182 \times 10^{-7})^2 + (1.7453 \times 10^{-5})^2}
$$

$$
\delta\tau = 2000 \times \sqrt{4.76 \times 10^{-14} + 3.046 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
\delta\tau \approx 2000 \times \sqrt{3.046 \times 10^{-10}} = 2000 \times 1.745 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta\tau \approx 0.0349 \text{ N·m}}
$$

> **Final Answer Q7A(b):** $\tau = 1.25$ N·m; $\delta\tau \approx 0.035$ N·m.

---

### **Q7B. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`** — **14 Marks** — *Bloom Level: Apply / Analyze*

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a **surface EMG (sEMG) sensor** in controlling a prosthetic hand. With a block diagram, describe signal acquisition, conditioning, rectification, RMS envelope extraction, and threshold-based grasp decision.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Wheatstone bridge output voltage** for a full-bridge strain-gauge configuration. A full bridge with gauge factor $G = 2.1$, excitation $V_{ex} = 5$ V, and strain $\varepsilon = 500 \, \mu\varepsilon$ is bonded to a load-cell beam. Calculate (i) the change in resistance of each gauge, and (ii) the bridge output voltage.

#### Model Solution — Q7B (a)

**Surface EMG (sEMG)** measures the **summated electrical activity** of motor units in a muscle, picked up by skin-surface electrodes. The signal amplitude is typically **50 µV – 5 mV**, bandwidth **20 – 500 Hz**, with strong 50/60 Hz power-line interference and motion artefact.

**Block diagram flow (refer to §4.4):**

1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl)** in differential pair pick up muscle potential → $\text{CMRR} > 100$ dB.
2. **Instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., AD620) with gain $G_1 = 100$–$1000$.
3. **Band-pass filter** 20–500 Hz (active op-amp, 2nd order Butterworth) → removes DC, motion artefact, HF noise.
4. **Notch filter** at 50 Hz → removes mains interference.
5. **Full-wave rectifier** → $\vert v(t) \vert$.
6. **RMS envelope** (sliding window $T = 100$–$300$ ms) → gives smooth activation level.
7. **Threshold logic** → 3-state grasp (Open / Hold / Close) with hysteresis to prevent chatter.
8. **Motor driver** → drives prosthetic hand.

**Engineering use:** State-of-the-art multi-articulated hands (e.g., DEKA LUKE, bebionic, i-Limb) decode sEMG with **pattern recognition (LDA, SVM, or neural networks)** to control multiple degrees of freedom from 2–8 electrode channels.

#### Model Solution — Q7B (b)

**Derivation of full-bridge output (as in §3.2):**

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon
$$

**Given:** $G = 2.1$, $V_{ex} = 5$ V, $\varepsilon = 500 \, \mu\varepsilon = 500 \times 10^{-6}$.

**Step 1 — Strain in each gauge:**

In a full bridge, two gauges are in tension (+ε) and two in compression (−ε). Each gauge sees magnitude $\varepsilon$.

**Step 2 — Change in resistance of each gauge:**

$$
\Delta R = G \cdot \varepsilon \cdot R_0
$$

For a typical $R_0 = 350 \, \Omega$ gauge:

$$
\Delta R = 2.1 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} \times 350 = 2.1 \times 0.175 = 0.3675 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Bridge output voltage:**

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon = 5 \times 2.1 \times 500 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 5 \times 1.05 \times 10^{-3} = 5.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{out} = 5.25 \text{ mV}}
$$

> **Final Answer Q7B(b):** $\Delta R \approx 0.3675 \, \Omega$ per gauge; $V_{out} = 5.25$ mV.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places KTU students LOSE MARKS in Robot Sensors questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in formulas** — encoder resolution in rad/count, torque in N·m, sensitivity in V/unit. Always carry units. *(–1 to –2 marks)*
> 2. **Confusing gauge factor with Poisson's ratio** — $G = 2.1$ is the gauge factor (dimensionless), not Poisson's ratio.
> 3. **Missing the ×4 quadrature multiplication** in encoder resolution questions. *(–2 marks)*
> 4. **Ignoring the direction-discrimination logic** (A leads B vs B leads A) — examiner specifically looks for this in 7-mark answers. *(–1 to –2 marks)*
> 5. **Forgetting CMRR** in bioelectric sensor (EMG/EEG) amplifier chains. Always mention $\text{CMRR} > 100$ dB.
> 6. **Sign convention in Wheatstone bridge** — opposite arms must change in opposite directions for full sensitivity.
> 7. **Forgetting to linearize** the bridge equation with $\Delta R \ll R_0$ assumption.
> 8. **No mention of signal conditioning** (amplifier, filter, ADC) — examiners often allocate 2–3 marks specifically for this.
> 9. **Wrong current direction in F/T sensors** — always state the convention (tension positive, compression negative).
> 10. **Skipping safety/standards** (ISO 13482, IEC 60601) — even one line on "patient safety" can earn a boundary mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **MODULE 4 — ROBOT SENSORS | KTU 2024 RAPID RECAP**

- [ ] A **robot sensor** = transducer converting physical → electrical signal.
- [ ] **Two classes:** Proprioceptive (internal) vs Exteroceptive (external); Active vs Passive.
- [ ] **Encoders** — Incremental (×4 quadrature) gives $4N$ counts/rev, resolution $\pi/(2N)$ rad.
- [ ] **Potentiometers** — simple, cheap, but contact wear; $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot x/x_{max}$.
- [ ] **Resolver/LVDT** — robust, contactless, used in harsh environments.
- [ ] **Strain-gauge bridge:** $V_{out} = n \cdot V_{ex} G \varepsilon / 4$, $n = 1, 2, 4$ for quarter/half/full bridge.
- [ ] **Gauge factor** $G = (\Delta R/R)/\varepsilon \approx 2.0$–$2.1$ for constantan.
- [ ] **SEA torque:** $\tau = k \cdot \Delta\theta_{spring}$ (in spring coordinates).
- [ ] **EMG signal chain:** Electrodes → IA (CMRR > 100 dB) → BPF 20–500 Hz → Notch 50 Hz → Rectify → RMS envelope → Threshold.
- [ ] **EMG amplitude:** 50 µV – 5 mV, bandwidth 20 – 500 Hz.
- [ ] **IMU sensor fusion** uses complementary filter: $\theta = \alpha(\theta_{gyro}) + (1-\alpha)\theta_{acc}$, $\alpha \approx 0.95$–$0.98$.
- [ ] **Accelerometer output:** $V_{out} = V_0 + S_a \cdot a$ (with 0-g offset).
- [ ] **Gyroscope:** $\omega = (V_{out} - V_0)/S_g$.
- [ ] **LiDAR / Ultrasonic ToF distance:** $d = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$.
- [ ] **Dynamic Range (dB)** $= 20 \log(V_{max}/V_{min})$; **SNR (dB)** $= 20 \log(V_{signal}/V_{noise})$.
- [ ] **ADC LSB** $= V_{ref}/2^N$.
- [ ] **Anti-aliasing filter** cutoff $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$; Nyquist $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$.
- [ ] **Tactile sensors** on prosthetic fingertips detect grip force, slip, and texture.
- [ ] **EEG-based BCIs** are used when EMG is unavailable (severe paralysis, ALS).
- [ ] Always include **patient safety** and **redundant sensing** in rehabilitation robot design (ISO 13482).
- [ ] **State-of-the-art rehabilitation robots:** ReWalk, Ekso, Lokomat, InMotion ARM, BLEEX, ARTHuR.
- [ ] **Frequent KTU keywords to memorize:** proprioception, exteroception, sensitivity, gauge factor, quadrature decoding, SEA, RMS envelope, complementary filter, sensor fusion, CMRR.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Interfaces to Robotic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Human Interfaces to Robotic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human Interfaces to Robotic Systems (HIRS)** refers to the multidisciplinary domain encompassing the **sensors, transducers, control architectures, and communication channels** that enable bidirectional information exchange between a human operator (typically a person with a physical disability or impairment) and a rehabilitation/assistive robotic device. The interface must translate **human intent** (motor, cognitive, or physiological signals) into **machine commands** and concurrently relay **machine feedback** (visual, auditory, or haptic) to the human.

In the KTU 2024 Rehabilitation Engineering context, HIRS is recognized as the **"communication bridge"** that determines the clinical viability of any rehabilitation robot. The interface must satisfy three cardinal requirements: **Safety**, **Intuitiveness**, and **Low Cognitive Load**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Steering Wheel of a Car**
> Imagine you are driving a car. Your hands on the steering wheel, your eyes on the road, and your ears listening to the engine form a *human–vehicle interface*. If the steering is too heavy (high cognitive load), driving becomes exhausting. If the steering gives no feedback (poor haptic response), you lose control. If the steering is too sensitive (oversensitive), you crash.
>
> A **Human–Robot Interface (HRI)** works exactly the same way for a person using a robotic wheelchair, prosthetic arm, or exoskeleton. The interface is the "steering wheel" that converts the *user's intent* into *robot motion* and returns *robot state* as feedback. The better the interface, the more naturally the human and robot work as a **single cooperative system**.

## 1.3 Classification of Human Interfaces (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 categorizes Human Interfaces to Robotic Systems into **four primary classes**, each aligned with a specific human physiological signal channel:

| Interface Class | Human Signal Source | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Electromyographic (EMG)** | Muscle action potentials | Prosthetic arms, hand orthoses |
| **Electroencephalographic (EEG)** | Cortical brain activity | Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI) |
| **Electro-oculographic (EOG)** | Eye-movement potentials | Communication aids, wheelchair control |
| **Mechanical / Kinematic** | Residual limb motion, joint torque | Exoskeletons, body-powered prostheses |
| **Haptic / Vibrotactile** | Skin mechanoreceptors | Sensory substitution, feedback |

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects students to recall the following benchmark metrics for evaluating a human–robot interface:
> - **Throughput (bits/sec)**: Information transfer rate, benchmark $\geq$ **30 bits/min** for clinical viability.
> - **Latency (ms)**: Signal round-trip delay, must be **< 300 ms** for real-time control.
> - **Classification Accuracy (%)**: Correct intent recognition, must be **> 90%** for safe actuation.
> - **Cognitive Workload (NASA-TLX score)**: Subjective load, must remain **< 40/100**.

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in Rehabilitation Engineering

A rehabilitation robot is only as clinically effective as its interface. Even a perfectly engineered robotic arm becomes useless if a paralyzed patient cannot *tell* it what to do. The interface is therefore the **rate-limiting component** in the entire rehabilitation chain. KTU 2024 explicitly tests this topic because it unifies **biomedical signal processing, control theory, and human factors engineering** into a single clinically deployable system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Control Architecture for Human–Robot Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 emphasizes the **Three-Level Hierarchical Control Architecture** as the canonical framework for human interfaces to robotic systems. Every rehabilitation robot, from a simple prosthetic hand to a full-body exoskeleton, is decomposed into three control layers:

### Level 1 — High-Level Intent Layer (Human Side)
- Captures the **user's desired task** (e.g., "pick up the cup").
- Implemented via **BCI, voice, or residual EMG** signals.
- Output: A discrete symbolic command or a low-dimensional task vector.

### Level 2 — Shared/Traded Control Layer (Cooperative)
- **Shared Control**: Human and robot both contribute simultaneously to the motion (e.g., user steers, robot avoids obstacles).
- **Traded Control**: Control authority switches between human and robot (e.g., robot takes over during unsafe maneuvers).
- This layer arbitrates safety versus user autonomy.

### Level 3 — Low-Level Servo Layer (Robot Side)
- Executes **joint-level trajectories** using PID, impedance, or admittance controllers.
- Operates at **1 kHz** or higher, completely transparent to the user.
- Returns **force, position, and tactile** data up the hierarchy.

## 2.2 EMG-Based Interface — Signal Model

The surface EMG signal is modelled as a zero-mean stochastic process whose **Root Mean Square (RMS)** amplitude is proportional to muscle activation:

$$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i^2}$$

where $v_i$ is the $i$-th sampled voltage and $N$ is the number of samples in the analysis window. The RMS amplitude is then mapped to a desired joint torque via a static recruitment curve:

$$\tau_{desired} = f(V_{RMS}) \quad \text{where} \quad f(\cdot) \text{ is a nonlinear monotonic function}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RMS and not peak?** RMS captures the *power* of the EMG signal, which correlates better with the underlying muscle force than instantaneous peak voltage. Peaks are noisy and unreliable.

## 2.3 Brain–Computer Interface (BCI) — Information Theory

The performance of a BCI is quantified by the **Shannon–Hartley theorem**, which defines the maximum information transfer rate $B$ in bits per minute:

$$B = C \cdot \left[ \log_2 M + P \log_2 P + (1-P)\log_2\left(\frac{1-P}{M-1}\right) \right] \cdot 60$$

where:
- $C$ = number of commands per minute (selections/min)
- $M$ = number of possible choices
- $P$ = classifier accuracy (probability of correct selection)
- The expression inside brackets is **bits per selection**, derived from Shannon entropy.

For a clinical-grade P300 speller BCI, typical values are: $C = 5$/min, $M = 36$ (alphabet), $P = 0.90$, yielding $B \approx 25$ bits/min.

## 2.4 Haptic Interface — Impedance Control Law

A haptic interface renders virtual environment dynamics to the human hand. The generalized **impedance control** law is:

$$F_{rendered} = M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x - x_{eq})$$

where:
- $M$ = virtual mass (kg)
- $B$ = virtual damping (Ns/m)
- $K$ = virtual stiffness (N/m)
- $x$ = device position, $x_{eq}$ = equilibrium position

The operator feels as though their hand is interacting with a virtual spring–mass–damper system, even though the physical device may be a lightweight stylus.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic. All units and boundary conditions are exam-critical.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units | Clinical Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | EMG RMS Amplitude | $V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i^2}$ | $v_i$ = voltage sample, $N$ = window length | Volts (V) | $N \geq 200$ for stable RMS |
| 2 | EMG-Torque Mapping | $\tau = a \cdot V_{RMS} + b$ | $a,b$ = recruitment coefficients | Nm | Monotonic increase only |
| 3 | BCI Information Rate | $B = 60 C \cdot \left[\log_2 M + \mathcal{H}(P)\right]$ | $C$ = selections/min, $M$ = choices, $\mathcal{H}$ = binary entropy | bits/min | $B \geq 25$ for clinical use |
| 4 | Haptic Impedance | $F = M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x - x_{eq})$ | $M,B,K$ = virtual mass, damping, stiffness | N (force) | Stable only if $B^2 \geq 4MK$ |
| 5 | Fitts' Law (Target Selection) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{D}{W}\right)$ | $T$ = move time, $D$ = distance, $W$ = target width | seconds | Index of Difficulty $\leq 6$ bits |
| 6 | SNR of Bioelectric Signal | $SNR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | $V_{signal}$, $V_{noise}$ = RMS voltages | dB | $\geq 20$ dB for reliable control |
| 7 | Reaction Time Bound | $T_{latency} < 300$ ms | Total signal-processing + actuation delay | ms | Hard real-time limit |
| 8 | Classification Accuracy | $P = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | $TP,TN,FP,FN$ = confusion matrix entries | dimensionless | $P \geq 0.90$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: In the Fitts' Law formula, students frequently write $\log_2(2D/W)$ — this is **wrong**. The correct form is $\log_2(1 + D/W)$, also called the **Shannon form**. The $1+$ inside the log is what distinguishes Shannon's formulation from the older Mackenzie's form.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hierarchical interface architecture is the **backbone of every modern rehabilitation robot**:
- **DEKA LUKE Arm** (USA): Uses **EMG + IMU** at Level 1, **shared control** at Level 2, **impedance servo** at Level 3.
- **ReWalk Exoskeleton** (Israel): Uses **joystick + tilt sensor** at Level 1, **balance arbitration** at Level 2, **joint PID** at Level 3.
- **BrainGate2 BCI** (USA): Uses **intracortical electrodes** at Level 1, **decoded trajectory planning** at Level 2, **cursor/robot servo** at Level 3.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: EMG RMS-to-Torque Recruitment Curve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation shows how a raw noisy EMG signal is mathematically converted into a clinically usable torque command for a prosthetic or orthotic device.

### Step 1 — Acquisition and Rectification
The raw surface EMG $v(t)$ is bipolar and centered around zero. The first processing step is full-wave rectification, which folds the negative half-cycle above the axis:

$$v_{rect}(t) = \vert v(t) \vert$$

This step is required because torque is a **positive-definite** physical quantity — we cannot command a "negative muscle activation."

### Step 2 — Smoothing via Moving RMS
A moving window of length $N$ samples slides across the rectified signal, computing the RMS at each instant $k$:

$$V_{RMS}[k] = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} v_{rect}^2[k-i]}$$

This step extracts the **envelope** of muscle activation, which is directly proportional to the underlying motor unit recruitment and firing rate.

### Step 3 — Bias Removal
Because the rectified signal has a small DC offset due to amplifier noise, a baseline $V_0$ measured during voluntary rest is subtracted:

$$V_{active}[k] = V_{RMS}[k] - V_0$$

### Step 4 — Linear Recruitment Mapping
A linear recruitment function maps the cleaned EMG envelope to a desired joint torque. The two coefficients $a$ and $b$ are calibrated per-user during clinical fitting:

$$\tau_{desired}[k] = a \cdot V_{active}[k] + b$$

**Derivation of the calibration procedure:**
- Ask the user to produce **Maximum Voluntary Contraction (MVC)** and record $V_{MVC}$.
- Set $\tau_{max}$ to a safe clinical maximum (e.g., 80% of joint structural limit).
- Then $a = \dfrac{\tau_{max} - b}{V_{MVC}}$ and $b$ is chosen such that the dead-zone is eliminated (typically $b = 0.05 \cdot a \cdot V_{MVC}$).

### Step 5 — Saturation and Rate-Limiting
To prevent abrupt torque jumps that could harm the user, the command is clipped and rate-limited:

$$\tau_{cmd}[k] = \text{clip}\left(\tau_{desired}[k],\, 0,\, \tau_{max}\right)$$

$$\Delta\tau[k] = \tau_{cmd}[k] - \tau_{cmd}[k-1]$$

$$\tau_{final}[k] = \tau_{cmd}[k-1] + \text{clip}(\Delta\tau[k], -\Delta_{max}, +\Delta_{max})$$

where $\Delta_{max}$ is the maximum allowable torque change per control cycle, ensuring smooth and safe actuation.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — BCI Information Rate

**Problem (KTU-style):** A P300-based BCI speller has $M = 26$ choices (English alphabet), the user makes $C = 6$ selections per minute, and the classifier accuracy is $P = 0.88$. Calculate the information transfer rate in bits per minute.

### Step 1 — Compute the bits-per-selection term
Using the Shannon form:

$$\text{bits/selection} = \log_2 M + P\log_2 P + (1-P)\log_2\left(\frac{1-P}{M-1}\right)$$

Substitute $M = 26$ and $P = 0.88$:

$$\log_2 26 = 4.7004$$

$$0.88 \cdot \log_2 0.88 = 0.88 \cdot (-0.1844) = -0.1623$$

$$0.12 \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{0.12}{25}\right) = 0.12 \cdot \log_2(0.0048) = 0.12 \cdot (-7.7028) = -0.9243$$

Summing:

$$\text{bits/selection} = 4.7004 - 0.1623 - 0.9243 = 3.6138 \text{ bits/selection}$$

### Step 2 — Multiply by selections per minute

$$B = C \cdot \text{bits/selection} = 6 \cdot 3.6138 = 21.68 \text{ bits/min}$$

### Step 3 — Conclusion and Clinical Interpretation
The BCI achieves **21.68 bits/min**, which is **below the clinical viability threshold of 25 bits/min**. Therefore, this particular BCI configuration would **not** be suitable for a high-throughput communication aid; either $C$ or $P$ must be improved.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Students often forget to multiply by 60 inside the formula and report the answer in bits/second. KTU consistently asks for **bits per minute** in board examinations — read the units carefully.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Interface Selection Decision

> [!NOTE]
> The following decision matrix is a high-yield KTU topic. It maps **user impairment type** to the **optimal interface choice** and is frequently asked as a 14-mark design question.

| Impairment Profile | Residual Capability | Recommended Interface | Rationale | Example Device |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Transradial amputation | Forearm muscles intact | Multi-channel EMG | High bandwidth, intuitive | Ottobock Michelangelo Hand |
| Quadriplegia (C4–C5) | Limited shoulder shrug | Sip-and-puff + tongue switch | No upper-limb function available | Tongue Drive System |
| Locked-in syndrome | Eye movement only | EOG / Steady-State Visual Evoked Potential (SSVEP) BCI | Only oculomotor channel usable | Intendix SSVEP Speller |
| Stroke hemiparesis | Weak but present EMG | EMG + functional electrical stimulation hybrid | Voluntary effort triggers FES | Bioness H200 |
| Lower-limb paralysis | Upper body intact | Crutch force sensors + IMU | Body-weight support feedback | ReWalk Exoskeleton |
| Cognitive decline | Intact motor, impaired cognition | Single-switch scanning | Minimal cognitive load | Single-switch AAC device |

## 3.4 Numerical Stability Check for Haptic Impedance

For the haptic rendering law $F = M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x - x_{eq})$ to be **physically stable** (no self-oscillation), the system must be critically or overdamped. The stability condition for a second-order mass–spring–damper is:

$$B^2 \geq 4 M K$$

**Worked Check:** Suppose $M = 0.5$ kg, $K = 200$ N/m. Then $4MK = 400$. Choosing $B = 25$ Ns/m gives $B^2 = 625 \geq 400$ — **stable**. If a student had chosen $B = 10$ Ns/m, then $B^2 = 100 < 400$ — **unstable**, leading to dangerous oscillations of the haptic device. KTU examiners specifically test this calculation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Level Hierarchical Control Block Diagram

The diagram below maps the canonical control hierarchy from human intent down to motor actuation, and feedback flowing back to the human.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Human Operator Intent] --> B1[Level 1 High Level Intent Decoder]
    B1 --> C1[Symbolic Task Command e.g. pick cup]
    C1 --> D1[Level 2 Shared Traded Control Arbitrator]
    D1 --> E1[Level 3 Low Level Joint Servo Controller]
    E1 --> F1[Robot Actuator Motors]
    F1 --> G1[Physical Environment]
    G1 --> H1[Haptic Force Position Sensors]
    H1 --> E1
    E1 --> D1
    D1 --> I1[Visual Auditory Haptic Display]
    I1 --> A1
    D1 -. Safety Override .-> J1[Emergency Stop Layer]
    J1 -.-> F1
    styleA1 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleB1 fill:#06aed5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleC1 fill:#06aed5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleD1 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleE1 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleF1 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleG1 fill:#073b4c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    styleH1 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleI1 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleJ1 fill:#d62828,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The forward path (top to bottom) carries intent from human to environment. The feedback path (bottom to top) carries sensory information back to the human. The **red block** is the shared/traded control arbitrator, and the **dark red block** is the independent safety override that can halt the robot regardless of higher-level commands.

## 4.2 Multi-Modal Interface Selection Flowchart

This diagram guides a clinical engineer through the decision process of selecting the optimal interface for a given patient.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Patient Clinical Assessment] --> B2{Residual Motor Function Available}
    B2 -- Yes --> C2{Upper Limb EMG Detectable}
    B2 -- No --> D2{Eye Movement Preserved}
    C2 -- Yes --> E2[EMG Based Interface]
    C2 -- No --> F2{Mechanical Switch Sip Puff Available}
    D2 -- Yes --> G2[EOG or SSVEP BCI]
    D2 -- No --> H2[Cortical BCI Implant Electrodes]
    F2 -- Yes --> I2[Pneumatic Switch Interface]
    F2 -- No --> J2{Cognitive Function Intact}
    J2 -- Yes --> K2[Single Switch Scanning AAC]
    J2 -- No --> L2[Caregiver Operated Tilt Sensor]
    E2 --> M2[Prosthetic Hand or Exoskeleton]
    G2 --> M2
    H2 --> M2
    I2 --> M2
    K2 --> M2
    L2 --> M2
    styleA2 fill:#ffe066,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleB2 fill:#06aed5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleC2 fill:#06aed5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleD2 fill:#06aed5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleE2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleF2 fill:#06aed5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleG2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleH2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleI2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleJ2 fill:#06aed5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleK2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleL2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleM2 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical insight**: The flowchart enforces a **descending order of invasiveness** — start with the least invasive interface (external EMG) and only resort to implanted electrodes (invasive BCI) when no external channel works. This is the ethical and clinical standard expected in KTU 2024 answers.

## 4.3 EMG Signal-Processing Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Surface Electrodes on Skin] --> S2[Differential Amplifier Gain 1000]
    S2 --> S3[Bandpass Filter 20 to 500 Hz]
    S3 --> S4[Notch Filter 50 Hz Mains Rejection]
    S4 --> S5[Full Wave Rectifier]
    S5 --> S6[Moving RMS Envelope Detector]
    S6 --> S7[Bias Subtraction V0]
    S7 --> S8[Linear Recruitment Mapping]
    S8 --> S9[Saturation and Rate Limit]
    S9 --> S10[Torque Command to Robot Joint]
    styleS1 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleS2 fill:#06aed5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleS3 fill:#06aed5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleS4 fill:#06aed5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleS5 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleS6 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleS7 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleS8 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleS9 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
