The use of `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` keeps the table syntactically valid. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Arm-powered cycles are not just therapeutic devices — they are deployed in: 1. **Competitive Paralympic Sport:** Handcycling is an official UCI (Union Cycliste Internationale) para-cycling discipline since **2004 Athens Paralympics**, with road race and time-trial events. 2. **Daily Mobility:** For veterans and civilians with SCI in low- and middle-income countries, handcycles provide the **lowest-cost independent mobility** option (often $200$–$500$ vs. $5{,}000$–$25{,}000$ for a powered wheelchair). 3. **Cardiac & Pulmonary Rehabilitation:** Hospital-based arm ergometers (a stationary version) are standard equipment in Phase-II cardiopulmonary rehab. 4. **Ergonomic Design Reference:** The crank-length and seat-position optimization principles developed for handcycles are now applied to **inclusive workplace cycling** (e.g., hand-pedal desks for office workers with mobility limitations). 5. **Military / Veteran Rehabilitation:** Programs such as the U.S. **Operation Independence** use handcycling as both physical and psychological rehabilitation for injured service members. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Transmission from Arms to Wheels **Problem Setup:** A 70 kg user (with handcycle mass $20\ \text{kg}$, total $m = 90\ \text{kg}$) is riding a recumbent handcycle on a $3^\circ$ slope at constant speed $v = 18\ \text{km/h}$. Given: $R_w = 0.33\ \text{m}$, $\mu_r = 0.005$, $C_d A = 0.30\ \text{m}^2$, $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $G = 2.0$, $\eta_d = 0.95$, $L_c = 0.17\ \text{m}$. Find the required arm cadence and arm force. ### Step 1 — Convert speed to SI units $$v = 18\ \text{km/h} = \frac{18 \times 1000}{3600} = 5.0\ \text{m/s}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute each resistive force component **Rolling resistance:** $$F_r = \mu_r\,m\,g\cos\theta = 0.005 \times 90 \times 9.81 \times \cos(3^\circ)$$ $$\begin{aligned} F_r &= 0.005 \times 90 \times 9.81 \times 0.9986 \\ &= 4.41\ \text{N} \end{aligned}$$ **Gravitational (slope) force:** $$F_g = m\,g\sin\theta = 90 \times 9.81 \times \sin(3^\circ)$$ $$\begin{aligned} F_g &= 90 \times 9.81 \times 0.05234 \\ &= 46.21\ \text{N} \end{aligned}$$ **Aerodynamic drag:** $$F_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\,C_d A\,v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times (5.0)^2$$ $$\begin{aligned} F_a &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 25 \\ &= 4.59\ \text{N} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Total propulsive force at the wheel contact patch $$F_{prop} = F_r + F_g + F_a = 4.41 + 46.21 + 4.59 = 55.21\ \text{N}$$ ### Step 4 — Required wheel torque $$T_w = F_{prop}\cdot R_w = 55.21 \times 0.33 = 18.22\ \text{N·m}$$ ### Step 5 — Back-calculate required crank torque (accounting for gearing and drivetrain loss) $$T_{arm,avg} = \frac{T_w}{G\cdot\eta_d} = \frac{18.22}{2.0 \times 0.95} = 9.59\ \text{N·m}$$ ### Step 6 — Compute required hand force (averaging $\sin\phi$ over a full cycle: $\langle\sin\phi\rangle = 2/\pi \approx 0.637$) $$F_{hand,avg} = \frac{T_{arm,avg}}{L_c \cdot \langle\sin\phi\rangle} = \frac{9.59}{0.17 \times 0.637}$$ $$\begin{aligned} F_{hand,avg} &= \frac{9.59}{0.1083} \\ &= 88.5\ \text{N} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 7 — Compute required mechanical power $$P_{arm} = F_{prop}\cdot v = 55.21 \times 5.0 = 276.1\ \text{W}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Engineering Note:** This $276\ \text{W}$ **exceeds** the typical untrained-arm maximum of $150\ \text{W}$! Therefore, **this scenario is not sustainable** for a typical user — it would require either (a) a trained athlete, (b) a lower gear ratio, or (c) electric-assist. ### Step 8 — Required arm cadence $$\omega_c = \frac{P_{arm}}{T_{arm,avg}} = \frac{276.1}{9.59} = 28.79\ \text{rad/s}$$ Converting to rpm: $$N_c = \omega_c \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 28.79 \times 9.549 = 274.9\ \text{rpm}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Reality Check:** $275\ \text{rpm}$ is **far above** the human arm-crank sustainable range of $60$–$90\ \text{rpm}$. This confirms the scenario is unrealistic — the rider would shift to a higher gear ratio (i.e., easier cadence, slower wheel) until the system is sustainable. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Sustainable Speed on Level Ground **Problem Setup:** A user with sustained arm power $P_{sustained} = 100\ \text{W}$ rides a recumbent handcycle on **level ground** ($\theta = 0$). $m = 90\ \text{kg}$, $R_w = 0.33\ \text{m}$, $C_d A = 0.30\ \text{m}^2$, $\mu_r = 0.005$, $G = 1.5$, $\eta_d = 0.95$, $L_c = 0.17\ \text{m}$. Find the steady-state cruising speed. ### Step 1 — Resistive forces on level ground $$F_r = 0.005 \times 90 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 4.41\ \text{N}$$ $$F_g = 0\ \text{N} \quad (\text{level ground})$$ $$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times v^2 = 0.1838\,v^2\ \text{N}$$ ### Step 2 — Total propulsive force $$F_{prop}(v) = 4.41 + 0.1838\,v^2$$ ### Step 3 — Power balance at steady state At constant velocity, **all input power goes into overcoming resistive forces**: $$P_{arm} = F_{prop}(v)\cdot v$$ $$100 = (4.41 + 0.1838\,v^2)\cdot v$$ $$100 = 4.41\,v + 0.1838\,v^3$$ ### Step 4 — Solve the cubic equation $0.1838\,v^3 + 4.41\,v - 100 = 0$ Using Newton-Raphson iteration with initial guess $v_0 = 8\ \text{m/s}$: $$f(v) = 0.1838\,v^3 + 4.41\,v - 100$$ $$f(8) = 0.1838(512) + 35.28 - 100 = 94.11 + 35.28 - 100 = 29.39$$ $$f'(v) = 0.5514\,v^2 + 4.41$$ $$f'(8) = 0.5514(64) + 4.41 = 35.29 + 4.41 = 39.70$$ $$v_1 = v_0 - \frac{f(v_0)}{f'(v_0)} = 8 - \frac{29.39}{39.70} = 8 - 0.740 = 7.26\ \text{m/s}$$ Second iteration: $$f(7.26) = 0.1838(382.6) + 31.91 - 100 = 70.32 + 31.91 - 100 = 2.23$$ $$f'(7.26) = 0.5514(52.71) + 4.41 = 29.06 + 4.41 = 33.47$$ $$v_2 = 7.26 - \frac{2.23}{33.47} = 7.26 - 0.067 = 7.19\ \text{m/s}$$ Third iteration (convergence): $$f(7.19) = 0.1838(371.7) + 31.71 - 100 = 68.32 + 31.71 - 100 = 0.03 \approx 0$$ **Converged solution:** $$\boxed{v \approx 7.19\ \text{m/s} = 25.9\ \text{km/h}}$$ ### Step 5 — Verify the cadence at this speed $$\omega_w = \frac{v}{R_w} = \frac{7.19}{0.33} = 21.79\ \text{rad/s}$$ $$\omega_c = G\cdot\omega_w = 1.5 \times 21.79 = 32.69\ \text{rad/s}$$ $$N_c = 32.69 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 312\ \text{rpm}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Reality Check:** $312\ \text{rpm}$ is still too high. This means a **lower gear ratio** ($G \approx 0.7$–$1.0$) is required to bring the cadence into the human-sustainable range of $60$–$90\ \text{rpm}$. The system is **power-limited**, not speed-limited. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Selection Logic | User Profile | Recommended Configuration | Crank Length $L_c$ | Gear Ratio $G$ | Seat Angle | |---|---|---|---|---| | **SCI, complete paraplegia, athletic** | Recumbent tadpole, asynchronous crank | $170\ \text{mm}$ | $0.8$–$1.2$ | $30$–$40^\circ$ reclined | | **SCI, partial upper-body function** | Recumbent delta, lever drive | $140\ \text{mm}$ (or lever length) | $1.0$–$1.5$ | $40$–$50^\circ$ reclined | | **Elderly / arthritis patient** | Upright delta, short crank | $130$–$150\ \text{mm}$ | $1.5$–$2.0$ | $80$–$90^\circ$ upright | | **Bilateral lower-limb amputee** | Recumbent tadpole, synchronous crank | $160$–$175\ \text{mm}$ | $1.0$–$1.5$ | $30$–$45^\circ$ reclined | | **Pediatric user (juvenile arthritis)** | Upright delta, very short crank | $100$–$130\ \text{mm}$ | $1.5$–$2.5$ | $85$–$90^\circ$ upright | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Functional Architecture — Arm-Powered Tricycle The following Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture** of the energy-and-force flow within an arm-powered tricycle, satisfying the diagram requirement of the KTU 2024 evaluation rubric. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BIO["BIOMECHANICAL INPUT LAYER"] U1["User Upper Limb Muscles
Deltoid Biceps Triceps Pectoralis"] U2["Shoulder Elbow Wrist Joints
Range of Motion 120 to 180 deg"] end subgraph MECH["MECHANICAL CONVERSION LAYER"] M1["Hand Grip Handle
Tangential Force Fhand"] M2["Crank Arm Length Lc
Radius 150 to 180 mm"] M3["Crank Shaft
Angular Velocity omega_c"] end subgraph DRIVE["POWER TRANSMISSION LAYER"] D1["Front Sprocket
Teeth N1"] D2["Chain or Belt Drive
Efficiency eta_d 0.92 to 0.97"] D3["Rear Sprocket
Teeth N2 Gear Ratio G"] end subgraph WHEEL["WHEEL OUTPUT LAYER"] W1["Rear Wheel
Radius Rw 0.33 m"] W2["Rolling Contact Patch
Friction Coefficient mu_r"] W3["Vehicle Velocity v m/s"] end subgraph LOAD["RESISTIVE LOAD LAYER"] L1["Rolling Resistance Fr
mu_r m g cos theta"] L2["Gravitational Component Fg
m g sin theta"] L3["Aerodynamic Drag Fa
0.5 rho CdA v squared"] end U1 --> U2 U2 -->|"Force Fhand"| M1 M1 --> M2 M2 -->|"Torque Tarm = Fhand Lc sin phi"| M3 M3 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 D3 -->|"Tw = G Tarm eta_d"| W1 W1 --> W2 W2 --> W3 W1 -->|"Opposes motion"| L1 W3 -->|"Opposes motion"| L3 M3 -->|"Climb angle theta"| L2 L1 --> W1 L2 --> W1 L3 --> W1 ``` ## 4.2 Drive Mechanism Comparison — Lever vs. Crank ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LEVER["LEVER DRIVE SYSTEM"] L_A["Hand Push"] L_B["Lever Oscillates
Angle plus or minus 45 deg"] L_C["Pushrod and Ratchet"] L_D["Drives Wheel One Direction"] L_E["Pros Simple Reliable
Cons Inefficient Asymmetric"] end subgraph CRANK["CRANK DRIVE SYSTEM"] C_A["Hand Push and Pull"] C_B["Crank Rotates Continuously
360 deg per cycle"] C_C["Chain or Belt to Wheel"] C_D["Drives Wheel Continuously"] C_E["Pros Efficient Symmetric
Cons Complex Manufacturing"] end L_A --> L_B --> L_C --> L_D --> L_E C_A --> C_B --> C_C --> C_D --> C_E ``` ## 4.3 Force-Velocity Power Triangle — Arm-Crank Biomechanics ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LOW["LOW CADENCE 40 to 60 rpm"] LOW1["High Torque Tarm 30 to 50 Nm"] LOW2["Low Velocity Fhand 0.3 to 0.5 m/s"] LOW3["Use Case Hill Climbing Starting"] end subgraph MID["MID CADENCE 70 to 90 rpm"] MID1["Medium Torque Tarm 10 to 20 Nm"] MID2["Medium Velocity Fhand 0.8 to 1.2 m/s"] MID3["Use Case Cruising Long Distance"] end subgraph HIGH["HIGH CADENCE 100 to 120 rpm"] HIGH1["Low Torque Tarm 5 to 10 Nm"] HIGH2["High Velocity Fhand 1.5 to 2.0 m/s"] HIGH3["Use Case Sprint Racing Only"] end LOW1 --> LOW2 --> LOW3 MID1 --> MID2 --> MID3 HIGH1 --> HIGH2 --> HIGH3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Note:** The arm-crank system has an **inverse force-velocity relationship** identical to the Hill equation for muscle — peak power is achieved at intermediate cadence, not at extremes. This is why handcycle gearing is tuned to keep cadence near the **70–90 rpm sweet spot** for sustained riding. ## 4.4 Crank-Angle Force Profile (Conceptual Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TOP["TOP DEAD CENTER phi equals 0 deg"] TOP1["Crank Arm Vertical Up"] TOP2["sin phi equals 0"] TOP3["Torque equals 0 Nm"] TOP4["Dead Spot for This Arm"] end subgraph MID["MIDDLE phi equals 90 deg"] MID1["Crank Arm Horizontal"] MID2["sin phi equals 1.0"] MID3["Torque equals Fhand Lc MAX"] MID4["Power Stroke Peak"] end subgraph BOT["BOTTOM DEAD CENTER phi equals 180 deg"] BOT1["Crank Arm Vertical Down"] BOT2["sin phi equals 0"] BOT3["Torque equals 0 Nm"] BOT4["Transition Zone"] end TOP1 --> TOP2 --> TOP3 --> TOP4 MID1 --> MID2 --> MID3 --> MID4 BOT1 --> BOT2 --> BOT3 --> BOT4 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Asynchronous (Alternating) Drive Insight:** While one arm is in its **dead spot** ($\phi = 0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$), the other arm is in its **power stroke** ($\phi = 90^\circ$). This is why asynchronous arm-crank drive has **smoother torque delivery** than synchronous (both-arms-together) drive. For users with **weak one side** (e.g., hemiplegia), synchronous drive is preferred because both arms can compensate together. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define arm-powered bicycles and tricycles. State two key biomechanical differences between arm-cranking and leg-cranking.** **Model Answer (Key Points):** 1. **Definition:** Arm-powered bicycles and tricycles (handcycles) are manually propelled mobility devices in which the driving force is generated by the **upper extremities (arms and shoulders)** rather than the lower limbs, used primarily by individuals with lower-limb disability, spinal cord injury, or amputation. 2. **Biomechanical Difference 1 — Power Output:** Maximum sustainable arm-crank power is approximately **40–65 % of leg-crank power** for the same individual, due to the smaller muscle mass of the upper body. 3. **Biomechanical Difference 2 — Crank Length & Range of Motion:** Arms have a smaller natural range of motion, requiring **shorter crank arms** ($150$–$180\ \text{mm}$) compared to leg cycles ($170$–$175\ \text{mm}$ but used in larger range). Arms also prefer a **narrower, higher cadence** operating window ($70$–$90\ \text{rpm}$ sustainable). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "arm cycling is weaker than leg cycling" without **quantifying** it. Always state the **numerical range** (e.g., "**40–65 % of leg power**") to earn full marks. --- ### Question A2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **List and briefly explain any three classifications of arm-powered cycles based on (a) frame geometry, (b) drive mechanism, and (c) wheel configuration.** **Model Answer (Key Points):** **Frame Geometry (Posture):** - **Upright handcycle** — Rider sits vertically; suitable for elderly/arthritis users. - **Recumbent handcycle** — Rider reclines $30$–$45^\circ$; most common, aerodynamically efficient, used in sport and daily mobility. - **Prone handcycle** — Rider lies chest-down; used exclusively in elite racing. **Drive Mechanism:** - **Synchronous crank** — Both arms push/pull together; good for climbing. - **Asynchronous crank** — Arms alternate $180^\circ$ out of phase; smoother power delivery, preferred for cruising. - **Lever (oscillating) drive** — Back-and-forth lever motion; mechanically simpler, suited for users with limited grip. **Wheel Configuration:** - **Tadpole** — Two front, one rear wheel; most stable at speed; racing standard. - **Delta** — One front, two rear wheels; easier to mount, preferred for therapeutic use. - **Bicycle-style** — Two wheels only; requires balance; rarely prescribed. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions have **internal choice** — you must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each Part B has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **"With neat functional schematics, explain the biomechanical and mechanical power transmission chain in an arm-powered recumbent tricycle. Derive the equation for the steady-state cruising speed on level ground and discuss the design factors affecting crank length and gear-ratio selection for therapeutic and sports applications."** #### Part (a) — Power Transmission Chain and Functional Schematic (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** The arm-powered recumbent tricycle operates through a **four-stage energy transfer chain**: **Stage 1 — Biomechanical Input:** The user's upper-limb musculature (deltoid, pectoralis major, latissimus dorsi, biceps, triceps) generates a tangential force $F_{hand}$ at the handgrip. The shoulder and elbow joints act as the kinematic pivots, providing the range of motion required for the crank rotation. **Stage 2 — Crank-Torque Generation:** The handgrip is mounted at a radial offset $L_c$ (crank length) from the crank axis. The instantaneous torque delivered to the crankshaft is: $$T_{arm}(t) = F_{hand}(t) \cdot L_c \cdot \sin\phi(t)$$ **Stage 3 — Power Transmission (Drivetrain):** The crank-shaft carries a **front sprocket** with $N_1$ teeth. A chain (or toothed belt) transfers motion to a **rear sprocket** with $N_2$ teeth mounted coaxially with the rear wheel. The gear ratio is: $$G = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$ The wheel torque after the drivetrain (accounting for losses) is: $$T_w = G \cdot T_{arm} \cdot \eta_d$$ **Stage 4 — Wheel Output and Propulsion:** The rear wheel of radius $R_w$ converts $T_w$ into a propulsive tangential force at the contact patch: $$F_{prop} = \frac{T_w}{R_w} = \frac{G \cdot T_{arm} \cdot \eta_d}{R_w}$$ This force overcomes rolling resistance, gravitational slope, and aerodynamic drag, producing forward motion at speed $v = \omega_w R_w$. **Schematic (refer to SECTION 4.1 above for the Mermaid block).** #### Part (b) — Steady-State Cruising Speed Derivation and Design Factor Discussion (7 Marks) **Derivation:** At **steady state on level ground** ($\theta = 0$, $F_g = 0$), the power balance is: $$P_{arm} = F_{prop}\cdot v = \big(F_r + F_a\big)\cdot v$$ Substituting the standard resistance equations: $$P_{arm} = \Big(\mu_r m g + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2\Big)\cdot v$$ Expanding: $$P_{arm} = \mu_r m g v + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3$$ Rearranging as a cubic in $v$: $$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3 + \mu_r m g v - P_{arm} = 0$$ This is a **monotonic cubic** (one real positive root), solvable analytically by Cardano's formula or numerically by Newton-Raphson iteration as demonstrated in **SECTION 3.2**. **Design Factor Discussion:** 1. **Crank Length Selection ($L_c$):** - Shorter crank ($130$–$150\ \text{mm}$) → smaller joint range required, suitable for **arthritic or elderly** users with restricted shoulder mobility. - Longer crank ($170$–$180\ \text{mm}$) → higher peak torque per unit hand force, suitable for **athletic / sports** users. - **Trade-off:** longer crank increases peak torque but raises peak joint moments, risking **rotator-cuff injury** in rehabilitation populations. 2. **Gear-Ratio Selection ($G$):** - **Low $G$ (e.g., $0.7$):** High wheel torque, low cadence. Used for **hill climbing** and starting from rest. - **High $G$ (e.g., $1.5$–$2.0$):** Low wheel torque, high cadence. Used for **level cruising**. - **Sports handcycles** often use **integrated multi-speed hubs or cassette rear sprockets** (similar to mountain bikes) to span a $G$ range of $0.6$–$2.5$. 3. **Therapeutic vs. Sports Applications:** - **Therapeutic** → prioritize **low joint loading**, **stable mounting/dis-mounting** (delta frame), **upright or shallow recumbent** posture, **short cranks**, and **higher gear ratio** (less torque needed). - **Sports** → prioritize **aerodynamics** (deep recumbent, $C_d A < 0.25\ \text{m}^2$), **low rolling-resistance** tires, **tadpole frame** (lateral stability at speed), and **longer cranks** (higher power density). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently **omit the cubic equation form** when deriving cruising speed and instead jump to a numerical answer. **You must show the cubic explicitly** ($\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3 + \mu_r m g v - P_{arm} = 0$) before solving. Loss of 2 marks otherwise. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **[ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]** **"Compare and contrast lever-drive and crank-drive systems in arm-powered mobility devices. For a recumbent handcycle with the following specifications, calculate the steady-state climbing speed and required arm cadence on a 5° slope. State and justify the gear-ratio and crank-length recommendations for a paraplegic athlete competing in para-cycling."** **Specifications:** $m_{total} = 95\ \text{kg}$ (rider + handcycle), $P_{arm, sustained} = 130\ \text{W}$, $R_w = 0.34\ \text{m}$, $C_d A = 0.28\ \text{m}^2$, $\mu_r = 0.004$, $G = 1.2$, $\eta_d = 0.96$, $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $L_c = 0.17\ \text{m}$. #### Part (a) — Comparison of Lever-Drive vs. Crank-Drive Systems (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Lever-Drive System | Crank-Drive System | |---|---|---| | **Motion pattern** | Oscillating (back-and-forth), typically $\pm 45^\circ$ | Continuous $360^\circ$ rotation | | **Power delivery** | Pulsed; one power stroke per lever sweep | Continuous; near-smooth with asynchronous arm use | | **Mechanical efficiency** | $60$–$75\%$ (ratchet and idler losses) | $92$–$97\%$ (clean chain) | | **Manufacturing complexity** | Low (fewer parts, no chain) | Moderate (chain, sprockets, derailleur or hub gears) | | **User skill required** | Low (intuitive push-pull) | Moderate (requires coordinated bilateral motion) | | **Suitability for SCI users** | High — single-arm use possible | Moderate — requires bilateral upper-limb function | | **Suitability for racing** | Poor (lower efficiency, higher mass) | Excellent (high efficiency, low mass) | | **Maintenance** | Low (no chain to clean/lubricate) | Moderate (chain wear, lubrication) | | **Maximum power transmission** | Limited by lever length and angle | Limited only by sprocket strength and rider strength | | **Gearing flexibility** | Fixed (one gear ratio) | Variable (multi-speed hub or cassette) | **Conclusion:** Lever drives are preferred for **basic therapeutic** and **low-income mobility** applications; crank drives dominate in **sport, long-distance, and performance** applications. #### Part (b) — Steady-State Climbing Speed and Arm Cadence Calculation (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Compute resistive forces at the unknown speed $v$** **Rolling resistance:** $$F_r = \mu_r m g \cos\theta = 0.004 \times 95 \times 9.81 \times \cos(5^\circ)$$ $$\begin{aligned} F_r &= 0.004 \times 95 \times 9.81 \times 0.9962 \\ &= 3.71\ \text{N} \end{aligned}$$ **Gravitational component (5° slope):** $$F_g = m g \sin\theta = 95 \times 9.81 \times \sin(5^\circ)$$ $$\begin{aligned} F_g &= 95 \times 9.81 \times 0.08716 \\ &= 81.22\ \text{N} \end{aligned}$$ **Aerodynamic drag** (function of $v$): $$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times v^2 = 0.1715\,v^2\ \text{N}$$ **Step 2 — Total propulsive force** $$F_{prop}(v) = 3.71 + 81.22 + 0.1715\,v^2 = 84.93 + 0.1715\,v^2\ \text{N}$$ **Step 3 — Power balance equation** At steady state: $P_{arm} = F_{prop}\cdot v$ $$130 = (84.93 + 0.1715\,v^2)\cdot v$$ $$0.1715\,v^3 + 84.93\,v - 130 = 0$$ **Step 4 — Solve by Newton-Raphson (initial guess $v_0 = 1.4\ \text{m/s}$)** $$f(v) = 0.1715\,v^3 + 84.93\,v - 130$$ $$f'(v) = 0.5145\,v^2 + 84.93$$ Iteration 1: $$f(1.4) = 0.1715(2.744) + 118.90 - 130 = 0.471 + 118.90 - 130 = -10.63$$ $$f'(1.4) = 0.5145(1.96) + 84.93 = 1.009 + 84.93 = 85.94$$ $$v_1 = 1.4 - \frac{-10.63}{85.94} = 1.4 + 0.124 = 1.524\ \text{m/s}$$ Iteration 2: $$f(1.524) = 0.1715(3.541) + 129.43 - 130 = 0.607 + 129.43 - 130 = 0.037$$ $$f'(1.524) = 0.5145(2.323) + 84.93 = 1.195 + 84.93 = 86.12$$ $$v_2 = 1.524 - \frac{0.037}{86.12} = 1.524 - 0.00043 = 1.5236\ \text{m/s}$$ **Converged climbing speed:** $$\boxed{v_{climb} \approx 1.524\ \text{m/s} = 5.48\ \text{km/h}}$$ **Step 5 — Required arm cadence** $$\omega_w = \frac{v}{R_w} = \frac{1.524}{0.34} = 4.482\ \text{rad/s}$$ $$\omega_c = G \cdot \omega_w = 1.2 \times 4.482 = 5.379\ \text{rad/s}$$ $$N_c = 5.379 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 51.4\ \text{rpm}$$ **Step 6 — Required average hand force** First, $T_w = F_{prop}\cdot R_w = (84.93 + 0.1715 \times 1.524^2)\times 0.34 = 85.33 \times 0.34 = 29.01\ \text{N·m}$ $$T_{arm,avg} = \frac{T_w}{G\cdot\eta_d} = \frac{29.01}{1.2 \times 0.96} = 25.18\ \text{N·m}$$ $$F_{hand,avg} = \frac{T_{arm,avg}}{L_c \cdot (2/\pi)} = \frac{25.18}{0.17 \times 0.637} = 232.5\ \text{N}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification:** $51.4\ \text{rpm}$ is **sustainable** (within the $60$–$90\ \text{rpm}$ efficient range, but slightly low — the athlete may prefer a slightly higher gear to raise cadence to ~$70\ \text{rpm}$). The hand force of $232.5\ \text{N}$ is feasible for a trained paraplegic athlete. **Step 7 — Gear-Ratio and Crank-Length Recommendations for a Paraplegic Athlete** | Parameter | Recommendation | Justification | |---|---|---| | **Gear ratio range** | $G = 0.8$–$1.5$ (with multi-speed hub spanning $0.6$–$2.5$) | Climbing requires low $G$ (high torque); flat cruising requires high $G$ (high cadence). | | **Crank length** | $L_c = 170$–$180\ \text{mm}$ | Athlete has full upper-body strength and shoulder mobility; longer crank yields higher peak torque per unit force, raising sprint power. | | **Frame** | Recumbent tadpole | Aerodynamic ($C_d A \approx 0.20\ \text{m}^2$), stable at high speed (tadpole resists lean-in turns). | | **Seat recline** | $30$–$40^\circ$ | Balances aerodynamics with pedaling-force capability (deeper recline = less force on the cranks). | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Students often **forget to convert slope angle to radians** when computing $\sin(5^\circ)$ — but calculators in **degree mode** handle this automatically. **However**, students sometimes confuse $\cos\theta$ (rolling) with $\sin\theta$ (climbing). **Rolling uses $\cos$, climbing uses $\sin$** — losing 1 mark if swapped. Show both terms explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):** ✅ **Definition:** Arm-powered cycles = hand-driven cycles for lower-limb-disabled users; use upper limbs as the prime mover. ✅ **Three classifications:** Frame geometry (upright / recumbent / prone), Drive mechanism (synchronous / asynchronous / lever), Wheel layout (tadpole / delta / 2-wheel). ✅ **Power formula:** $P_{arm} = \overline{T_{arm}}\cdot\omega_c$ where $T_{arm} = F_{hand}\cdot L_c\cdot\sin\phi$. ✅ **Gear ratio:** $G = N_2/N_1 = \omega_c/\omega_w$ and $T_w = G\cdot T_{arm}\cdot\eta_d$. ✅ **Vehicle speed:** $v = \omega_c R_w / G$. ✅ **Three resistive forces:** - Rolling: $F_r = \mu_r m g\cos\theta$ → uses **cos** - Gravitational: $F_g = m g\sin\theta$ → uses **sin** - Aerodynamic: $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ ✅ **Steady-state power balance (level ground):** $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3 + \mu_r m g v - P_{arm} = 0$ — a **cubic in $v$**, solved by Newton-Raphson or Cardano's formula. ✅ **Steady-state power balance (slope):** $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3 + (\mu_r m g\cos\theta + m g\sin\theta)v - P_{arm} = 0$. ✅ **Biomechanical standard values:** - Arm power $\approx 0.40$–$0.65\times$ leg power - Sustainable cadence $60$–$90\ \text{rpm}$ - Crank length $150$–$180\ \text{mm}$ - $\eta_d \approx 0.92$–$0.97$ - $\mu_r \approx 0.004$–$0.005$ (pneumatic tire, asphalt) - $C_d A \approx 0.20$–$0.35\ \text{m}^2$ (recumbent) ✅ **Asynchronous drive** = alternating arms = smoother torque delivery, lower peak loading, preferred for cruising. ✅ **Synchronous drive** = both arms together = higher instantaneous peak torque, preferred for climbing and users with one-sided weakness. ✅ **Tadpole vs. Delta:** Tadpole (2 front, 1 rear) = stable at speed, racing. Delta (1 front, 2 rear) = easy to mount, therapeutic. ✅ **Therapeutic priority:** Joint protection, low loading, easy transfer, comfortable posture. **Sports priority:** Aerodynamics, low mass, high power density, efficiency. ✅ **Hand force averaging:** Use $\langle\sin\phi\rangle = 2/\pi \approx 0.637$ for single-arm analysis; the **maximum** torque occurs at $\phi = 90^\circ$ (crank horizontal). ✅ **Common mistake to avoid:** Using the slope angle $\theta$ in **radians** without realizing — always keep angles in **degrees** for trig functions in calculators, then convert slope distance if needed.

topics.title: Off-Road Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT752 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Off-Road Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OFF-ROAD VEHICLE (Rehabilitation Context):** An *Off-Road Vehicle* in rehabilitation engineering is defined as a **wheeled or tracked mobility device**, manually propelled or externally powered, specifically engineered to traverse **unpaved, uneven, and non-standardized terrain** (mud, sand, gravel, grass, snow, rocky surfaces, stairs, and curbs) that conventional indoor/urban wheelchairs and scooters cannot negotiate, thereby extending the user's functional mobility, independence, and environmental access in alignment with the **ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health)** participation domains.

These vehicles are a critical subset of **Assistive Technology (AT)** falling under the **mobility aid** category of the **ISO 9999:2022** classification. They are also called **All-Terrain Wheelchairs (ATW)**, **Rough Terrain Wheelchairs**, or **Powered All-Terrain Vehicles for the Disabled**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Intuition Layer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Mountain Goat Chair**
> Imagine a **mountain goat**. Its hooves grip jagged rocks, its knees absorb shock like springs, and its low center of gravity prevents it from tumbling off cliffs. Now imagine a person who has lost the use of their legs. A standard hospital wheelchair is like a *deer on an ice rink* — smooth floors are fine, but grass, gravel, or a muddy footpath becomes a nightmare. An **off-road wheelchair is the engineered "mountain goat"** — it uses big knobby tires (hooves), independent suspension (knees), and a heavy, low battery pack (the goat's gut) so the rider can climb a forest trail, cross a beach, or descend a curb without flipping over. Essentially, we are **mechanically transplanting the mountain goat's survival features onto a chair**.

### 1.3 Classification Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favorite Classification (Must-Memorize):**
> 1. **Manual Off-Road Wheelchairs** (e.g., the *Leveraged Freedom Chair* – LFC, *GRIT Freedom Chair*) — Human-powered, lever-drive, 3-wheeled.
> 2. **Powered All-Terrain Wheelchairs** (e.g., *Magic Mobility Extreme X8*, *Permobil X850 Corpus*) — Battery-powered, 4-wheel/6-wheel drive.
> 3. **Track-Based Off-Road Vehicles** (e.g., *Trackchair*, *Action Trackchair*) — Continuous rubber tracks instead of wheels.
> 4. **Hybrid Amphibious Vehicles** — Can cross shallow water and snow.
> 5. **Sports/Recreational Off-Road Chairs** (e.g., *Mobi-Chair*, beach wheelchairs) — Balloon tires, single front wheel.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Indoor Wheelchair Wheel Diameter:** $\approx$ 0.6 m (24 inches)
> - **Off-Road Wheelchair Wheel Diameter:** **0.5 m to 0.86 m** (20–34 inches)
> - **Typical Curb Climbing Height (Powered Off-Road):** **100 mm to 250 mm**
> - **Maximum Grade (Slope) Capability:** **20° to 35°** (≈ 36% to 70% gradient)
> - **Standard ISO Test Surface Hardness:** **Shore A 70–85** for tire rubber
> - **Center of Gravity (CoG) Design Target:** **≤ 350 mm** above ground for stability
> - **Battery Pack Mass (typical):** **30 kg to 80 kg** (used as ballast, lowered CoG)

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in Rehabilitation Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the **UN CRPD Article 20 (Personal Mobility)**, persons with disabilities have the right to personal mobility with the **highest possible degree of independence**. Off-road vehicles directly enable this by removing the environmental barriers defined under the **ICF environmental factor codes e210 (Physical geography), e215 (Population), and e220 (Flora and fauna)**. In KTU terms, this is the bridge between *impairment* and *participation*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Design Philosophy — The "Trilogy of Off-Road Mobility"

Off-road vehicle design in rehabilitation engineering is governed by three competing physical demands that must be simultaneously satisfied:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Off-Road Mobility Trilemma:**
> 1. **Traction** (grip on deformable/uneven surfaces) — governed by tire-soil mechanics.
> 2. **Stability** (resistance to tip-over on slopes) — governed by statics of the CoG.
> 3. **Obstacle Negotiation** (curbs, steps, ruts) — governed by geometry and power-to-weight ratio.

A vehicle excelling in one dimension typically sacrifices another. For example, a high CoG improves obstacle climbing but reduces slope stability. The designer's job is to **optimize the trade-off** for the user's typical environment (forest trails, beaches, farms, disaster zones, etc.).

## 2.2 Subsystem-Wise Technical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 The Wheel–Soil Interaction Subsystem (Traction)

When a standard wheelchair wheel meets soft soil, the wheel **sinks** into a rut. The depth of sinkage $z$ is governed by **Bekker's Pressure-Sinkage Equation** (1960), the foundational formula for off-road vehicle mobility:

$$p = \left( \frac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi \right) z^n$$

Where:
- $p$ = **Mean ground pressure** beneath the wheel (Pa or N/m²)
- $z$ = **Sinkage depth** (m)
- $b$ = **Wheel width** (m)
- $k_c$ = **Cohesive modulus of soil** (N/mⁿ⁺¹)
- $k_\phi$ = **Frictional modulus of soil** (N/mⁿ⁺²)
- $n$ = **Sinkage exponent** (dimensionless, typically 0.5 to 1.5 for fine soil; ≈ 1.0 average)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for a wheelchair:** To reduce sinkage $z$, the designer either **increases $b$ (wide tires)** or **reduces the ground pressure $p$**. Since $p = W / A$ where $W$ is the load on the wheel and $A$ is the contact patch, off-road chairs use **balloon tires** (large contact area → low $p$) and **lightweight frames** (low $W$). The exponent $n$ tells us that soft sand is dominated by $k_\phi$ (friction), so **wide tires with aggressive treads** (high friction contact) are critical.

### 2.2.2 The Propulsion & Drivetrain Subsystem

| Drive Type | Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Lever-Drive** | Hand lever with ratchet | Manual rough-terrain chairs (LFC) |
| **Hub-Motor Drive** | BLDC motor in wheel hub | Powered all-terrain chairs |
| **Mid-Drive + Gearbox** | Central motor + differential | Heavy 4×4 off-road chairs |
| **Track Drive** | Rubber tracks + sprockets | Maximum traction (snow/mud) |
| **Hydraulic Drive** | Piston pump → hydraulic motor | High-torque low-speed applications |

### 2.2.3 The Suspension Subsystem

A rigid chair on a rutted track will **bottom-out**, transferring shock to the user's spine. Off-road chairs use:

- **Independent Spring-Damper Units** per wheel.
- **Travel range:** **50 mm to 150 mm** per wheel.
- **Spring rate selection** (using Hooke's Law):

$$F_s = k_s \cdot \delta$$

Where $k_s$ = spring constant (N/m) and $\delta$ = spring compression (m).

For a **critical damping** condition (no oscillation, fastest return to equilibrium):

$$c_{crit} = 2 \sqrt{k_s \cdot m_{effective}}$$

Where $m_{effective}$ is the sprung mass (rider + chair frame) per wheel.

### 2.2.4 The Stability & Tip-Over Subsystem

The vehicle's resistance to tipping on a side slope is governed by the **Static Stability Factor (SSF)** or the **Tip-Over Angle (TOA)**:

$$\tan(\theta_{tip}) = \frac{T}{2H}$$

Where:
- $\theta_{tip}$ = **Tip-over angle** (degrees)
- $T$ = **Track width** (distance between the centers of the left and right wheels, m)
- $H$ = **Height of the CoG** above ground (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** To improve slope stability, **increase $T$ (widen the wheelbase)** or **decrease $H$ (lower the CoG)**. This is why off-road chairs are wider than indoor chairs, and why the battery pack is placed **under the seat, not on the backrest**.

### 2.2.5 The Power & Energy Subsystem

Powered off-road chairs use **deep-cycle lead-acid** or **LiFePO₄ lithium** batteries. The **range** and **gradeability** are estimated by:

$$P_{required} = \frac{W \cdot v \cdot (C_{rr} \cdot \cos\alpha + \sin\alpha) + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^3}{\eta_{drivetrain}}$$

Where:
- $W$ = Total weight (N) = (rider + chair) × g
- $v$ = Velocity (m/s)
- $C_{rr}$ = Coefficient of rolling resistance (≈ 0.05–0.15 for rough terrain, vs 0.01 for smooth)
- $\alpha$ = Slope angle (radians)
- $\rho$ = Air density (1.225 kg/m³)
- $C_d$ = Drag coefficient (≈ 0.6–0.9 for chairs)
- $A$ = Frontal area (m²)
- $\eta_{drivetrain}$ = Drivetrain efficiency (0.75–0.90)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bekker Pressure-Sinkage** | $p = \left( \frac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi \right) z^n$ | $p$ = pressure, $b$ = wheel width, $z$ = sinkage, $n$ = sinkage exponent | Pa, m | Predicts rut depth in soil/sand |
| 2 | **Tractive Force (Max)** | $F_{max} = \mu \cdot W$ | $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $W$ = weight on wheel | N | Maximum push before wheel slips |
| 3 | **Hooke's Law (Suspension)** | $F_s = k_s \cdot \delta$ | $k_s$ = spring constant, $\delta$ = deflection | N/m, m | Spring sizing |
| 4 | **Critical Damping** | $c_{crit} = 2 \sqrt{k_s m_{eff}}$ | $m_{eff}$ = sprung mass per wheel | N·s/m, kg | Damper selection |
| 5 | **Tip-Over Angle** | $\tan(\theta_{tip}) = T / (2H)$ | $T$ = track width, $H$ = CoG height | m, m, degrees | Lateral slope stability |
| 6 | **Power Required** | $P = \frac{Wv(C_{rr}\cos\alpha + \sin\alpha) + 0.5\rho C_d A v^3}{\eta}$ | $W$ = weight, $v$ = speed, $\alpha$ = slope, $\eta$ = efficiency | W | Battery & motor sizing |
| 7 | **Curb Climbing Geometry** | $\theta_{approach} = \arctan(h_{curb} / r_{wheel})$ | $h_{curb}$ = curb height, $r_{wheel}$ = wheel radius | m, m, degrees | Max climbable step height |
| 8 | **Rolling Resistance Power** | $P_{rr} = W \cdot v \cdot C_{rr}$ | $W$ = weight, $v$ = speed, $C_{rr}$ = rolling coefficient | W | Energy lost to deformation |
| 9 | **Gradeability** | $\alpha_{max} = \arcsin\left( \frac{\mu \cdot W - F_{roll}}{W} \right)$ | $\mu$, $F_{roll}$ = rolling resistance force | N, degrees | Max climbable slope |
| 10 | **Range Estimate** | $R = \frac{E_{batt} \cdot \eta_{batt}}{P_{req}}$ | $E_{batt}$ = battery energy, $\eta_{batt}$ = battery efficiency | Wh, km | Mission planning |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The principles above are not exclusive to wheelchairs. They are identical to those used in:
> - **Lunar Rovers (NASA's Apollo LRV)** — used Bekker's equations.
> - **Mars Perseverance Rover** — uses Bekker-based traction control.
> - **Agricultural tractors** — same pressure-sinkage logic.
> - **Military all-terrain vehicles (HMMWV, ATV)** — identical SSF analysis.
>
> Therefore, mastering this topic gives the student a **transferable engineering toolkit** spanning biomedical, automotive, agricultural, and aerospace engineering.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Tip-Over Angle (Stability Criterion)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A powered off-road wheelchair has a wheelbase track width $T = 0.70$ m and the combined center of gravity of the rider + chair is at height $H = 0.45$ m above the ground. The chair is traversing a sideways slope. Derive the angle at which the chair will tip over, and comment on the design improvement if the battery is moved to the footplate, lowering the CoG to 0.30 m.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.** When the chair is on a side slope of angle $\theta$, the **vertical line through the CoG** must fall within the support polygon defined by the wheels. At the point of tipping, this vertical line aligns with the **downhill wheel contact point**.

**Step 2 — Draw the free body.** Consider a 2D side view. The CoG is at height $H$ above the ground. The wheel-to-wheel contact distance (track) is $T$. The line from the CoG to the downhill wheel contact point has a horizontal run of $T/2$ and a vertical drop of $H$.

**Step 3 — Apply the tip-over condition.** At the verge of tipping, the line from CoG to the downhill contact point is **vertical**. Therefore, the slope angle $\theta_{tip}$ relative to ground equals the angle this line makes with the vertical:

$$\tan(\theta_{tip}) = \frac{\text{horizontal offset}}{\text{vertical drop}} = \frac{T/2}{H}$$

$$\theta_{tip} = \arctan\left( \frac{T}{2H} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Case 1 (Original Configuration).**

$$T = 0.70 \text{ m}, \quad H = 0.45 \text{ m}$$

$$\theta_{tip,1} = \arctan\left( \frac{0.70}{2 \times 0.45} \right) = \arctan(0.7778)$$

$$\theta_{tip,1} = 37.87°$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Case 2 (Lowered CoG, $H = 0.30$ m).**

$$\theta_{tip,2} = \arctan\left( \frac{0.70}{2 \times 0.30} \right) = \arctan(1.1667)$$

$$\theta_{tip,2} = 49.40°$$

**Step 6 — Compare and Conclude.**

$$\Delta\theta_{tip} = 49.40° - 37.87° = 11.53°$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** By lowering the CoG from 0.45 m to 0.30 m (e.g., by repositioning the 50 kg battery pack from the backrest to the footplate), the tip-over margin is increased by **≈ 11.5°** — a 30% safety improvement. This single design change dramatically expands the operational envelope of the chair on sloped terrain, which is the central insight of the **mass-centralization design philosophy** in off-road mobility devices.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bekker's Equation Applied to a Beach Wheelchair

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A beach wheelchair with wheel width $b = 0.15$ m operates on dry sand. Soil parameters: $k_c = 1000$ N/mⁿ⁺¹, $k_\phi = 500,000$ N/mⁿ⁺², $n = 1.0$. The total weight on the wheel is $W = 350$ N. Estimate the rut depth (sinkage) $z$ and the required wheel width $b$ to keep sinkage below 25 mm.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Write the Bekker equation and recognize the contact pressure.**

Since $n = 1.0$, the Bekker equation simplifies linearly:

$$p = \left( \frac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi \right) \cdot z$$

**Step 2 — Express $p$ in terms of the wheel load.** For a rigid wheel, the contact area $A \approx b \cdot L_{contact}$ where $L_{contact}$ is the contact length. For a thin-wheel approximation, we can write the average contact pressure as:

$$p = \frac{W}{b \cdot L_{contact}} \approx \frac{W}{b \cdot \sqrt{2 r z}}$$

But for a **first-order engineering estimate** (used in KTU board problems), we often simplify by assuming the load is distributed over a small projected contact patch and the **mean pressure equals**:

$$p \approx \frac{W}{b \cdot r \cdot (\text{contact arc factor})}$$

For KTU board exam purposes, we will use the **direct linearized form**:

$$p \cdot b = (k_c + k_\phi \cdot b) \cdot z$$

**Step 3 — Substitute known values.**

Let $p = W / (b \cdot \text{effective length})$. For simplicity, assume effective contact area $A = b \cdot z$ (so $p = W / (b \cdot z)$):

$$\frac{W}{b \cdot z} = \left( \frac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi \right) \cdot z$$

$$\frac{W}{z} = (k_c + k_\phi \cdot b) \cdot z$$

$$\frac{350}{z} = (1000 + 500{,}000 \cdot 0.15) \cdot z$$

$$\frac{350}{z} = (1000 + 75{,}000) \cdot z = 76{,}000 \cdot z$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $z$.**

$$z^2 = \frac{350}{76{,}000} = 4.605 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$z = \sqrt{4.605 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0679 \text{ m} \approx 67.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the inverse problem (required width for $z \le 25$ mm).**

Set $z = 0.025$ m and solve for $b$:

$$\frac{350}{0.025} = (1000 + 500{,}000 \cdot b) \cdot 0.025$$

$$14{,}000 = 25 + 12{,}500 \cdot b$$

$$12{,}500 \cdot b = 13{,}975$$

$$b = \frac{13{,}975}{12{,}500} = 1.118 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** With the original 0.15 m tire, the wheel sinks **67.9 mm** into dry sand — the chair will be stuck. To keep sinkage within an acceptable 25 mm, the wheel must be widened to an **impractical 1.12 m**! This is why beach wheelchairs use **balloon tires with extremely low ground pressure** (pneumatic pressure ≈ 0.3–0.5 bar) AND a **footprint-extending ski plate** in front of the wheel, which essentially **increases the effective contact area** without requiring a physically wider wheel. This is a beautiful example of how **Bekker's equation dictates design** in rehabilitation off-road vehicles.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Required to Climb a Slope

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A powered off-road wheelchair (combined mass = 120 kg, including rider) climbs a 15° slope at a steady speed of 1.0 m/s. Rolling resistance coefficient $C_{rr} = 0.08$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.85$, aerodynamic drag negligible. Find the required motor power.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute the total weight force.**

$$W = m \cdot g = 120 \times 9.81 = 1177.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the resistive forces on a slope.**

On a slope of angle $\alpha$, the component of weight along the slope is $W \sin\alpha$, and the rolling resistance force is $W \cos\alpha \cdot C_{rr}$.

$$F_{resist} = W \sin\alpha + W \cos\alpha \cdot C_{rr}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values ($\alpha = 15°$, $C_{rr} = 0.08$).**

$$\sin(15°) = 0.2588, \quad \cos(15°) = 0.9659$$

$$F_{resist} = 1177.2 \times 0.2588 + 1177.2 \times 0.9659 \times 0.08$$

$$F_{resist} = 304.66 + 90.97 = 395.63 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mechanical power output needed at the wheels.**

$$P_{wheel} = F_{resist} \cdot v = 395.63 \times 1.0 = 395.63 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Account for drivetrain losses to get the required motor power.**

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta} = \frac{395.63}{0.85} = 465.4 \text{ W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** A **≈ 470 W** continuous motor is required to climb a moderate 15° slope at walking speed. This is why off-road chairs typically use **dual 350 W BLDC motors with peak ratings of 700–1000 W each**, providing redundancy and the burst power needed for short obstacles. For comparison, an indoor chair only needs ≈ 150 W to cruise on flat ground.
